topics.title: Introduction to Vehicle Performance Parameters: Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Vehicle Performance Parameters: Speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Definition of Vehicle Speed

In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing**, *speed* is one of the **primary performance parameters** of an automobile. It is defined as the **rate of change of displacement of the vehicle with respect to time**, measured along its direction of motion. From a performance testing standpoint, it is not merely a kinematic quantity but a *figure of merit* that reflects the integrated capability of the powerplant, transmission, chassis, aerodynamics, and rolling mechanism working in unison.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** *Vehicle Performance Parameters* broadly include **maximum speed, acceleration, gradeability, braking performance, fuel economy, and handling**. *Speed* is the most fundamental and is the reference parameter against which all other performance metrics are plotted.

Mathematically, the instantaneous speed of the vehicle along its longitudinal axis is given by:

$$
v(t) \;=\; \frac{dx(t)}{dt}
$$

where $x(t)$ is the linear displacement of the vehicle's center of gravity in meters and $t$ is time in seconds. The standard SI unit of vehicle speed is **m/s**, but for automotive engineering conventions it is reported in **km/h** (1 m/s = 3.6 km/h) or **mph** in certain international markets.

## 1.2 Types / Categories of Vehicle Speed (KTU Classification)

KTU examiners expect students to clearly differentiate between the following sub-classifications of vehicle speed:

| # | Speed Category | Operational Meaning | Typical Usage |
|---|----------------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Maximum Speed ($V_{max}$)** | The highest sustainable speed under specified road, load, and ambient conditions. | Top-line marketing specification. |
| 2 | **Cruising Speed ($V_c$)** | The speed at which the vehicle can run continuously without straining the engine. | Highway driving economy. |
| 3 | **Design Speed ($V_d$)** | A theoretical speed chosen as the basis for geometric design of highways. | Highway/road design. |
| 4 | **Sustainable / Continuous Speed** | Speed that can be maintained indefinitely on a given grade. | Heavy commercial vehicle spec. |
| 5 | **Instantaneous Speed** | Speed at any given instant, equal to $dx/dt$. | Telematics and ADAS sensors. |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Speed is the Outcome of a Tug-of-War"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine a horse (the engine's available tractive force) pulling a loaded sled (the vehicle) through mud (road resistance) against a headwind (aerodynamic drag) on an incline (gradient). The *horse's pulling force* represents the **available tractive effort at the wheels**, while the *mud, headwind, and incline* represent **resistive forces**. The instantaneous speed of the sled is simply a measure of *how fast the horse is winning this tug-of-war at any given instant*.

If the horse pulls harder than the resistance → vehicle **accelerates**.
If pull = resistance → vehicle cruises at **constant speed**.
If resistance > pull → vehicle **decelerates**.

Thus, *speed is not a property of the engine alone*; it is the **equilibrium outcome** of available tractive force and total resistive force.

## 1.4 Why Speed is a "Performance Parameter" and Not Just a Number

A vehicle could be capable of $V_{max}$ = 200 km/h on paper, but unable to climb a 6 % grade at 80 km/h — this represents a *performance deficiency*. Therefore, KTU considers speed as a *parameter* only when reported alongside **the conditions under which it is achieved** (level road, percentage grade, payload, ambient temperature, wind speed, etc.). The standard reference conditions used by SAE J816 and ISO are:

- **Level, dry, smooth asphalt road** (rolling resistance coefficient $f \approx 0.013$ for passenger car).
- **No wind** ($V_{air} = 0$).
- **Standard atmospheric pressure** $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa.
- **Standard temperature** $T = 288.15$ K → Air density $\rho = 1.225$ **kg/m³**.

## 1.5 Factors Influencing Vehicle Speed — The "Big Five"

The maximum achievable speed of any vehicle is governed by the interplay of:

1. **Available Power at the Wheels** — limited by engine power, transmission efficiency, and final drive ratio.
2. **Rolling Resistance** — function of tire type, inflation pressure, road surface, vehicle weight.
3. **Aerodynamic Drag** — function of vehicle frontal area, drag coefficient, and the square of vehicle speed.
4. **Gradient / Grade** — gravitational component along the road.
5. **Drivetrain Efficiency** — losses in transmission, differential, and bearings.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** Because aerodynamic drag scales with $V^2$ and the power required to overcome drag scales with $V^3$, **at very high speeds, aerodynamic drag is the dominant resistance**. This is why supercars obsess over drag coefficients ($C_d$) and sports cars with low power often cannot exceed 150 km/h.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fundamental Force Balance (Newton's Second Law on a Vehicle)

The longitudinal dynamics of a road vehicle can be completely described by:

$$
\sum F_{x} \;=\; m_{eff}\cdot\frac{dv}{dt}
$$

Expanding all longitudinal forces acting on a vehicle moving up a grade of angle $\theta$:

$$
F_{tractive} \;-\; F_{rolling} \;-\; F_{aerodynamic} \;-\; F_{grade} \;-\; F_{inertia} \;=\; 0 \quad \text{(steady state)}
$$

For the steady-state **constant-speed condition**, the equation reduces to:

$$
F_{t} \;=\; F_{r} \;+\; F_{a} \;+\; F_{g}
$$

where each force is a function of speed $V$ (or constants like vehicle weight and grade angle).

## 2.2 Detailed Description of Each Force

### (a) Tractive Force at the Wheels
The maximum tractive force a driven wheel can deliver without slipping is bounded by:

$$
F_{t,max} \;=\; \mu \cdot W_{driven}
$$

where $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction** between tire and road and $W_{driven}$ is the normal load on the driven wheels.

### (b) Rolling Resistance Force
For low speeds and small slip angles, rolling resistance is modelled as:

$$
F_{r} \;=\; f \cdot W \cdot \cos\theta
$$

where $f$ is the **dimensionless rolling resistance coefficient** (typically 0.008 to 0.018 for radial-ply tires on smooth asphalt) and $W = m \cdot g$ is the total vehicle weight. Note: rolling resistance is **practically independent of speed** for conventional tires below 130 km/h.

### (c) Aerodynamic Drag Force
A vehicle moving through still air experiences a drag force:

$$
F_{a} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_{d} \cdot A_{f} \cdot V^{2}
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density (**1.225 kg/m³** at sea level, ISA conditions)
- $C_d$ = drag coefficient (dimensionless; 0.25 for modern EVs, 0.30–0.35 for sedans, 0.40+ for trucks)
- $A_f$ = frontal area in m²
- $V$ = vehicle speed relative to air in m/s

### (d) Grade Resistance Force
On a road inclined at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal:

$$
F_{g} \;=\; W \cdot \sin\theta
$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta \approx G$ (grade in fraction). For a **% grade** $p$, we have $G = p/100$.

### (e) Inertia / Acceleration Resistance
This is the effective mass term accounting for translational mass plus rotating inertias:

$$
F_{i} \;=\; m_{eff}\cdot\frac{dv}{dt} \;=\; \left(m \;+\; \frac{I_{w}}{r^{2}} \;+\; \frac{I_{e}\,i_{g}^{2}\,i_{0}^{2}}{r^{2}}\right)\cdot a
$$

For most KTU problems, an **equivalent mass factor** $\delta$ is used, giving:

$$
F_{i} \;=\; \delta \cdot m \cdot a
$$

where $\delta \approx 1.04$ to $1.08$ for passenger cars with manual transmission.

## 2.3 Power Required to Overcome Resistances

Multiplying each force by vehicle speed $V$ gives the corresponding power:

$$
P_{r} \;=\; F_{r} \cdot V, \qquad P_{a} \;=\; F_{a}\cdot V, \qquad P_{g} \;=\; F_{g}\cdot V, \qquad P_{i} \;=\; F_{i}\cdot V
$$

Substituting the equations and noting that $V$ in m/s must be converted appropriately:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{F \cdot V}{1000} \quad \text{[kW, when } V \text{ is in m/s and } F \text{ in N]}
$$

Equivalently, if speed is in km/h:

$$
P \;=\; \frac{F \cdot V}{3600} \quad \text{[kW]}
$$

## 2.4 The Maximum Speed Equation — Derivation Logic

At **maximum speed on a level road**, the available power at the wheels exactly equals the sum of resistance powers:

$$
P_{wheels} \;=\; \frac{1}{\eta_t}\cdot\left[F_{r}\,V_{max} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_{max}^{3}\right]
$$

Because $F_r$ is constant and the $V^3$ aerodynamic term dominates at high $V$, this equation becomes:

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_{max}^{3} \;+\; f\cdot W\cdot V_{max} \;-\; \frac{P_{engine}\cdot\eta_t}{1} \;=\; 0
$$

Solving this cubic in $V_{max}$ yields the **maximum speed**. Because $f\cdot W\cdot V_{max}$ is small relative to the cubic term at high speeds, the simplified engineering approximation is:

$$
V_{max} \;\approx\; \left[\dfrac{2\cdot P_{wheels}}{\rho\,C_d\,A_f}\right]^{1/3}
$$

This explains why **doubling engine power increases top speed by only a factor of $\sqrt[3]{2} \approx 1.26$** — a classic KTU-level conceptual question.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formula Sheet — Commit to Memory Before Examinations**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units / Conditions |
|---|-----------|---------|--------------------|
| 1 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^2$ | $V$ in m/s; $\rho$ in kg/m³ |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_r = f\cdot W\cdot\cos\theta$ | $W = m\cdot g$ in N |
| 3 | Grade Resistance Force | $F_g = W\cdot\sin\theta \approx W\cdot G$ | $G$ = grade as decimal |
| 4 | Total Resistance (steady) | $F_R = F_r + F_a + F_g$ | Level road: $F_g = 0$ |
| 5 | Power to overcome $F$ | $P = \dfrac{F\cdot V}{3600}$ | $V$ in km/h → $P$ in kW |
| 6 | Power to overcome Drag | $P_a = \dfrac{\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{3}}{2\times 3600\times 1000}$ | $V$ in km/h → kW |
| 7 | Max Speed (level, approximate) | $V_{max} \approx \left[\dfrac{2P_w}{\rho\,C_d\,A_f}\right]^{1/3}$ | $V$ in m/s |
| 8 | Max Speed (level, with rolling) | Cubic in $V$: solve numerically | Use Newton-Raphson |
| 9 | Acceleration Time (0 → V) | $t = \dfrac{\delta\,m}{P_w/V \,-\, F_R/V}\int dV$ | Integrate $dt = \dfrac{\delta m\,dV}{F_{net}}$ |
| 10 | Speed-RPM relation | $V = \dfrac{2\pi\,r\,N}{60\,i_g\,i_0}\times 3.6$ | $N$ in rpm → $V$ in km/h |

## 2.6 Practical Utility in Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in industry:** Vehicle manufacturers use these exact relationships to:
> - **Set gear ratios** that allow the engine to operate near its peak power RPM at $V_{max}$.
> - **Reduce $C_d \cdot A_f$** as the primary lever to increase top speed (this is why Formula 1 cars run on low-downforce wings at Monza).
> - **Estimate fuel consumption** at highway cruising speeds (since $P_a \propto V^3$, fuel use grows rapidly with speed).
> - **Design EV range** — at constant 120 km/h cruising, **over 60 %** of battery energy is spent overcoming aerodynamic drag.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Maximum Speed on a Level Road (Complete)

We wish to derive the closed-form expression for $V_{max}$ assuming rolling resistance and aerodynamic drag only.

### Step 1: State the Equilibrium at Maximum Speed

At maximum speed, acceleration $a = 0$. Newton's second law reduces to:

$$
F_{tractive} \;=\; F_{rolling} \;+\; F_{aerodynamic}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Resistance Equations

$$
F_{t} \;=\; f\cdot W \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{2}
$$

### Step 3: Convert Force to Available Power at the Wheels

The engine produces brake power $P_b$ at the flywheel. After drivetrain losses, the available power at the wheels is:

$$
P_{wheels} \;=\; P_b \cdot \eta_t
$$

where $\eta_t \approx 0.88$–$0.92$ for modern manual transmissions. The relationship between force and power is:

$$
F_t \;=\; \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V}
$$

(Valid in SI when $V$ is in m/s and $P$ in W.)

### Step 4: Equate Tractive Power to Resistive Power

$$
\dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V} \;=\; f\cdot W \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{2}
$$

Multiply both sides by $V$:

$$
P_{wheels} \;=\; f\cdot W\cdot V \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{3}
$$

### Step 5: Rearrange into a Cubic Polynomial in $V$

Let $k_1 = f\cdot W$ (constant, in N) and $k_2 = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f$ (constant, in kg/m).

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{3} \;+\; f\cdot W\cdot V \;-\; P_{wheels} \;=\; 0
$$

$$
k_2\,V^{3} \;+\; k_1\,V \;-\; P_{wheels} \;=\; 0
$$

### Step 6: Solve the Cubic (Engineering Approximation)

For $V_{max} > 60$ km/h, $k_2 V^3 \gg k_1 V$. Neglecting the rolling term:

$$
k_2\,V_{max}^{3} \;\approx\; P_{wheels}
$$

$$
V_{max}^{3} \;\approx\; \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{k_2} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f}
$$

$$
V_{max} \;\approx\; \left[\dfrac{2\,P_{wheels}}{\rho\,C_d\,A_f}\right]^{1/3}
$$

### Step 7: Convert to Engineering Units (km/h)

Since $V$ was solved in m/s, multiply by 3.6:

$$
V_{max,\,km/h} \;=\; 3.6 \times \left[\dfrac{2\,P_{wheels}}{\rho\,C_d\,A_f}\right]^{1/3}
$$

### Step 8: Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Given:** A passenger car with $P_b = 110$ kW at 5500 rpm, $\eta_t = 0.90$, $m = 1500$ kg, $C_d = 0.32$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $f = 0.015$, $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, level road.

**Step 8a — Compute $P_{wheels}$:**

$$
P_{wheels} \;=\; 110 \times 0.90 \;=\; 99 \text{ kW} \;=\; 99{,}000 \text{ W}
$$

**Step 8b — Compute $k_1$ and $k_2$:**

$$
k_1 \;=\; f\cdot W \;=\; 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 \;=\; 220.7 \text{ N}
$$

$$
k_2 \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.32 \times 2.2 \;=\; 0.4312 \text{ kg/m}
$$

**Step 8c — Use Approximation:**

$$
V_{max}^{3} \;\approx\; \dfrac{99{,}000}{0.4312} \;=\; 229{,}592 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}^3
$$

$$
V_{max} \;\approx\; \sqrt[3]{229{,}592} \;\approx\; 61.30 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 8d — Convert to km/h:**

$$
V_{max} \;\approx\; 61.30 \times 3.6 \;\approx\; 220.7 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Step 8e — Refine with Rolling Term (Newton-Raphson refinement, optional):**

The exact cubic is $0.4312 V^3 + 220.7 V - 99{,}000 = 0$. Plugging $V = 61.3$:

$$
0.4312(61.3)^3 + 220.7(61.3) - 99{,}000 \;\approx\; 99{,}043 + 13{,}529 - 99{,}000 \;\approx\; 13{,}572
$$

This positive residual shows $V$ must increase slightly. Iterating:

$$
V \;\approx\; 61.5 \text{ m/s} \;\approx\; 221.4 \text{ km/h}
$$

The rolling correction is only ~0.3 %, confirming that **rolling resistance is negligible compared to aerodynamic drag at $V_{max}$**.

## 3.2 Derivation — Maximum Grade at a Given Speed

### Step 1: Set Up Equilibrium on a Grade

For constant-speed climb at speed $V_c$:

$$
F_{tractive} \;=\; F_r \;+\; F_a \;+\; F_g
$$

$$
\dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V_c} \;=\; f\cdot W\cos\theta \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_c^{2} \;+\; W\sin\theta
$$

### Step 2: Solve for $\sin\theta$

$$
W\sin\theta \;=\; \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V_c} \;-\; f\cdot W\cos\theta \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_c^{2}
$$

$$
\sin\theta \;=\; \dfrac{1}{W}\left[\dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V_c} \;-\; f\cdot W\cos\theta \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_c^{2}\right]
$$

For small grades, $\cos\theta \approx 1$ and $G \approx \sin\theta$, giving:

$$
G_{max} \;\approx\; \dfrac{1}{W}\left[\dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V_c} \;-\; f\cdot W \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_c^{2}\right]
$$

This is the **gradeability** at cruising speed — a critical performance specification.

## 3.3 Derivation — Time to Accelerate from 0 to 100 km/h (0–100 s benchmark)

### Step 1: General Acceleration Equation

$$
\delta\cdot m\cdot\dfrac{dV}{dt} \;=\; \dfrac{P_{wheels}}{V} \;-\; F_r \;-\; F_a
$$

### Step 2: Separate Variables

$$
dt \;=\; \dfrac{\delta\, m\cdot V\, dV}{P_{wheels} \;-\; F_r V \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{3}}
$$

### Step 3: Integrate from $V_1$ to $V_2$

$$
t_{1\to 2} \;=\; \int_{V_1}^{V_2}\dfrac{\delta\, m\cdot V\, dV}{P_{wheels} \;-\; F_r V \;-\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{3}}
$$

Because the denominator contains $V^3$, this integral **does not have a closed-form elementary solution** and is evaluated numerically in industry-grade simulation tools (e.g., MATLAB/Simulink, AVL CRUISE, CarSim). However, neglecting rolling and aerodynamic drag (valid for low-speed initial acceleration), it simplifies to:

$$
t_{0\to V} \;\approx\; \dfrac{\delta\, m\, V^2}{2\,P_{wheels}}
$$

For our example car: $t \approx \dfrac{1.05 \times 1500 \times (27.78)^2}{2 \times 99{,}000} \approx 6.14$ s. This under-estimates the real 0–100 km/h time (~7.5 s) because aerodynamic drag is being neglected.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Speed versus Dominant Resistance Regime

| Speed Regime | Dominant Resistance | Scaling of $P_{req}$ with $V$ | Engineering Lever |
|--------------|---------------------|-------------------------------|--------------------|
| $V < 30$ km/h | Inertia / Acceleration | $P_{req} \propto V$ (constant force) | Reduce mass, increase torque |
| 30–80 km/h | Rolling Resistance | $P_{req} \propto V$ | Tire pressure, low-$f$ tires |
| 80–150 km/h | Mixed (rolling + aero) | $P_{req} \propto V^{1.5}$ to $V^{2.5}$ | Streamlining |
| $V > 150$ km/h | Aerodynamic Drag dominant | $P_{req} \propto V^{3}$ | $C_d$ reduction, $A_f$ reduction |
| $V = V_{max}$ | Aero Drag = 100 % | $P_{req} \propto V^{3}$ | Engine power scaling |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Vehicle Longitudinal Dynamics — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block represents the *Block-Level Functional Architecture* mapping how available engine power is transformed into vehicle speed through the drivetrain and finally balanced against the resistive forces.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Engine Block] -->|Brake Power Pb| B[Clutch Transmission]
    B -->|Gear Ratio ig| C[Propeller Shaft]
    C -->|Final Drive i0| D[Differential Half Shafts]
    D -->|Tractive Force Ft| E[Wheels Tyres]
    E -->|Reaction Force Fr| F[Road Surface]
    F -->|Rolling Resistance Fr| G[Resistance Summation Node]
    H[Aerodynamic Drag Fa] --> G
    I[Grade Resistance Fg] --> G
    J[Inertia Resistance Fi] --> G
    G -->|Total Resistance Ftot| K[Net Force Comparator]
    E -->|Tractive Effort Ft| K
    K -->|Net Force Fnet| L[Newtons Second Law Integration]
    L -->|Integration over time| M[Vehicle Speed V t]
    L -->|Integration again| N[Distance Travelled x t]
```

## 4.2 Power Flow at the Wheels — Sankey-Style Block View

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Engine Brake Power Pb kW] --> P2[Transmission Loss]
    P2 -->|Power to Wheels Pw = Pb x eta_t| P3[Wheel Power Available]
    P3 --> R1[Power to Rolling Resistance f W V]
    P3 --> R2[Power to Aerodynamic Drag 0.5 rho Cd Af V cubed]
    P3 --> R3[Power to Grade Climb W sin theta V]
    P3 --> R4[Power to Acceleration delta m a V]
    R4 --> S1[Net Vehicle Acceleration]
    R1 --> S2[Vehicle Steady State Speed]
    R2 --> S2
    R3 --> S2
    S1 --> S3[Speed Performance Curve V vs t]
    S2 --> S3
```

## 4.3 Speed-Resistance Map — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT PARAMETERS"]
        I1[Vehicle Mass m]
        I2[Frontal Area Af]
        I3[Drag Coefficient Cd]
        I4[Engine Power Pb]
        I5[Rolling Coeff f]
        I6[Grade Angle theta]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE["COMPUTATION MODULE"]
        C1[Fr = f W cos theta]
        C2[Fa = 0.5 rho Cd Af V squared]
        C3[Fg = W sin theta]
        C4[Ft available = Pb eta_t / V]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["PERFORMANCE METRICS"]
        O1[Maximum Speed Vmax]
        O2[Gradeability at Vc]
        O3[Acceleration Time 0 to 100]
        O4[Speed vs Time Curve]
    end
    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C2
    I3 --> C2
    I4 --> C4
    I5 --> C1
    I6 --> C1
    I6 --> C3
    I1 --> C3
    C1 --> O1
    C2 --> O1
    C4 --> O1
    C1 --> O2
    C2 --> O2
    C3 --> O2
    C4 --> O2
    C1 --> O3
    C2 --> O3
    C4 --> O3
    C1 --> O4
    C2 --> O4
    C3 --> O4
    C4 --> O4
```

## 4.4 Gear-Ratio Effect on Vehicle Speed

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E[Engine RPM Ne] -->|Engaged Gear Ratio ig| S1[Transmission Output]
    S1 -->|Final Drive Ratio i0| S2[Wheel Rotational Speed Nw]
    Nw -->|Tyre Rolling Radius r| V[Vehicle Speed V km/h]
    V -->|Resistance Forces| R[Total Resistance Ftot]
    R -->|Limits Maximum Speed| Vmax[Reaches Vmax when Pw = Ftot V]
```

The vehicle speed at any given engine RPM is computed using:

$$
V \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi\,r\,N_e}{60\,i_g\,i_0}\times 3.6 \quad \text{km/h}
$$

This means that for the same engine RPM, **lower gear ratios produce lower vehicle speed but higher tractive force**, while **higher gear ratios yield higher speed but reduced tractive force** — the fundamental trade-off solved by multi-speed transmissions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper Set A]**
> **Define the term "maximum speed of a vehicle" and list any four factors on which it depends.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

*Definition (1 Mark):* **Maximum speed** is the highest steady speed that a vehicle can attain on a level road under standard ambient conditions (no acceleration, no grade), when the available tractive power at the wheels is exactly balanced by the total resistive power demand.

*Four factors (4 × 0.5 = 2 Marks):*
1. **Engine brake power and drivetrain efficiency.**
2. **Aerodynamic drag** — function of $C_d$, frontal area, air density.
3. **Rolling resistance coefficient** — function of tire and road surface.
4. **Vehicle weight and grade angle.**

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Exam]**
> **Explain why aerodynamic drag becomes the dominant resistance at high vehicle speeds. State the relationship between drag power and speed.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Aerodynamic drag force is proportional to $V^2$, so the power required to overcome it is proportional to **$V^3$** (since $P = F \cdot V$). Rolling resistance, in contrast, is practically independent of speed. Hence, as speed increases, the $V^3$ term grows far more rapidly than the constant rolling term, making aerodynamic drag dominant above approximately 80–100 km/h for typical passenger cars. **(3 Marks)**

Mathematical statement:

$$
P_{aero} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^{3} \;\propto\; V^{3}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation]**
>
> **Question A (14 Marks):**
> A passenger car has the following data:
> - Curb mass = 1400 kg, frontal area $A_f$ = 2.1 m², drag coefficient $C_d$ = 0.30, rolling resistance coefficient $f$ = 0.014.
> - Engine maximum power = 95 kW at 5800 rpm, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t$ = 0.90.
> - Tyre rolling radius = 0.31 m.
> - Air density $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, g = 9.81 m/s².
>
> **(a)** Derive the equation governing the maximum speed of the vehicle on a level road and determine the maximum speed in km/h. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** If the same vehicle climbs a uniform 4 % grade, what is the highest constant speed it can maintain? Comment on the dominant resistance at this speed. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Maximum Speed Derivation and Calculation:**

*Step 1 — Force balance at $V_{max}$, level road (3 Marks):*

$$
F_t \;=\; F_r \;+\; F_a
$$

*Step 2 — Substitute each term (2 Marks):*

$$
\dfrac{P_w}{V_{max}} \;=\; f\cdot m\cdot g \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_{max}^{2}
$$

Multiplying by $V_{max}$:

$$
P_w \;=\; f\cdot m\cdot g\cdot V_{max} \;+\; \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_{max}^{3}
$$

*Step 3 — Compute numerical constants (1 Mark):*

$$
P_w = 95\times 0.90 = 85.5 \text{ kW} = 85{,}500 \text{ W}
$$

$$
f\cdot m\cdot g = 0.014 \times 1400 \times 9.81 = 192.28 \text{ N}
$$

$$
\tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.30 \times 2.1 = 0.3859 \text{ kg/m}
$$

*Step 4 — Solve cubic in $V_{max}$ (1 Mark):*

$$
0.3859\,V^{3} \;+\; 192.28\,V \;-\; 85{,}500 \;=\; 0
$$

Using approximation (since aero term dominates):

$$
V_{max}^{3} \approx \dfrac{85{,}500}{0.3859} = 221{,}560 \;\Rightarrow\; V_{max} \approx 60.40 \text{ m/s}
$$

Convert to km/h:

$$
\boxed{V_{max} \;\approx\; 217.4 \text{ km/h}}
$$

**(b) Maximum Speed on a 4 % Grade:**

*Step 1 — Add grade force (2 Marks):*

$$
F_g = W \cdot G = 1400 \times 9.81 \times 0.04 = 549.36 \text{ N}
$$

*Step 2 — Force balance (2 Marks):*

$$
\dfrac{85{,}500}{V_c} \;=\; 192.28 \;+\; 0.3859\,V_c^{2} \;+\; 549.36
$$

$$
\dfrac{85{,}500}{V_c} \;=\; 741.64 \;+\; 0.3859\,V_c^{2}
$$

*Step 3 — Solve iteratively (2 Marks):*

Try $V_c = 100$ km/h = 27.78 m/s:

RHS: $741.64 + 0.3859 \times (27.78)^2 = 741.64 + 297.81 = 1039.45$
LHS: $85{,}500/27.78 = 3078.5$ — too high, so $V_c > 100$ km/h.

Try $V_c = 160$ km/h = 44.44 m/s:
RHS: $741.64 + 0.3859 \times (44.44)^2 = 741.64 + 762.27 = 1503.91$
LHS: $85{,}500/44.44 = 1923.7$ — still higher.

Try $V_c = 180$ km/h = 50 m/s:
RHS: $741.64 + 0.3859 \times 2500 = 741.64 + 964.75 = 1706.39$
LHS: $85{,}500/50 = 1710$ — almost equal.

Try $V_c = 180.2$ km/h:
RHS ≈ 1710, LHS ≈ 1710 ✓

$$
\boxed{V_c \;\approx\; 180 \text{ km/h on a 4 \% grade}}
$$

*Step 4 — Dominant resistance comment (1 Mark):*

At 180 km/h, the **aerodynamic drag is the dominant resistance**, contributing ≈ 56 % of total resistance. Grade contributes ≈ 32 %, rolling ≈ 12 %.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative choice:**
> With reference to the same vehicle in Question A, plot (qualitatively) the variation of power required with vehicle speed for (i) rolling resistance, (ii) aerodynamic drag, and (iii) total resistance. Mark the intersection point with available power and explain why the intersection gives maximum speed. Discuss the effect on $V_{max}$ if the drag coefficient is reduced from 0.30 to 0.25 with all other parameters unchanged. **(14 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i)–(iii) Power Curves (7 Marks):**

The three power curves are:

- **$P_r$ vs $V$:** A straight line passing through origin with slope $f\cdot W$. At $V = 0$, $P_r = 0$. At 200 km/h, $P_r \approx 9.6$ kW.
- **$P_a$ vs $V$:** A cubic curve starting at origin. At 200 km/h, $P_a \approx 119$ kW. Hence the cubic dominates.
- **$P_{total}$ vs $V$:** Sum of the two — almost cubic-shaped above 80 km/h.

The **available wheel power** $P_w$ is plotted as a horizontal line at 85.5 kW (for level, constant-speed running).

**Intersection = $V_{max}$ (3 Marks):**

Maximum speed is reached when available power exactly equals the total resistance power:

$$
P_w \;=\; P_r \;+\; P_a
$$

Beyond this intersection, the resistance power exceeds the available power → the vehicle cannot accelerate further. Hence, the intersection abscissa is $V_{max} \approx 217$ km/h.

**Effect of reducing $C_d$ from 0.30 to 0.25 (4 Marks):**

New $k_2 = 0.5 \times 1.225 \times 0.25 \times 2.1 = 0.3216$ kg/m.

$$
V_{max,\,new}^{3} \approx \dfrac{85{,}500}{0.3216} = 265{,}857 \;\Rightarrow\; V_{max,\,new} \approx 64.36 \text{ m/s} \approx 231.7 \text{ km/h}
$$

Thus, a 16.7 % reduction in $C_d$ yields a **6.6 % increase in $V_{max}$** (from 217.4 to 231.7 km/h). This illustrates the **diminishing returns** of aerodynamic refinement on top speed.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks (Top Three Pitfalls):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s** when computing drag force $F_a$ — KTU examiners deduct a full 1 mark for unit inconsistency. Always remember: $V_{m/s} = V_{km/h}/3.6$.
> 2. **Confusing brake power (at flywheel) with wheel power** — $P_w = P_b \times \eta_t$. Several students forget this and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Neglecting rolling resistance entirely** at low speeds. At $V < 60$ km/h, rolling resistance is comparable to aerodynamic drag and must not be neglected. KTU values include 1 mark for properly identifying the dominant resistance regime.
> 4. **Plugging values into $V_{max}$ cube-root formula without verifying units of $P_w$** — must be in Watts when $V$ is in m/s.
> 5. **Writing $\sin\theta = \tan\theta$ for grades steeper than 15 %** — this approximation fails. Use exact trigonometric identity for steeper grades.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Vehicle **speed** = rate of change of displacement along motion direction, in **m/s** (SI) or **km/h** (industry).
- [ ] Three sub-categories: **Maximum, Cruising, Design speed**.
- [ ] Standard reference: level road, no wind, $\rho$ = 1.225 kg/m³, $P_{atm}$ = 101.325 kPa.
- [ ] Longitudinal force balance: $F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i$.
- [ ] **Rolling Resistance:** $F_r = f\cdot W\cdot\cos\theta$, **independent of speed**.
- [ ] **Aerodynamic Drag:** $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V^2$, $\propto V^2$.
- [ ] **Drag Power:** $P_a \propto V^3$ — grows rapidly with speed.
- [ ] **Maximum Speed Equation:** $P_w = f\cdot W\cdot V_{max} + \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,C_d\,A_f\,V_{max}^3$.
- [ ] **Engineering Approximation:** $V_{max} \approx \left[\dfrac{2P_w}{\rho\,C_d\,A_f}\right]^{1/3}$.
- [ ] **Doubling engine power → top speed up by only 26 %** ($2^{1/3} \approx 1.26$).
- [ ] **Grade Resistance:** $F_g = W\cdot\sin\theta$; for small angles use $G = \tan\theta \approx \sin\theta$.
- [ ] **Power Conversion:** $P\,[\text{kW}] = F\,[\text{N}]\times V\,[\text{km/h}]/3600$.
- [ ] **Speed-RPM relation:** $V = \dfrac{2\pi r N}{60 i_g i_0}\times 3.6$ km/h.
- [ ] **Gradeability at $V_c$:** $G_{max} = \dfrac{1}{W}\left[\dfrac{P_w}{V_c} - fW - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f V_c^2\right]$.
- [ ] **Acceleration 0–100 km/h:** $t \approx \dfrac{\delta m V^2}{2 P_w}$ (neglecting drag).
- [ ] **Equivalent mass factor** $\delta \approx 1.04$–$1.08$ for passenger cars.
- [ ] **At $V_{max}$**, aerodynamic drag is **>90 %** of total resistance.
- [ ] **At low speeds (< 30 km/h)**, inertia and rolling dominate; aero is negligible.
- [ ] **Tyre pressure** directly affects $f$ → higher pressure → lower rolling resistance → higher $V_{max}$.
- [ ] **Altitude** lowers $\rho$ → reduces drag → slightly increases $V_{max}$ but reduces engine power (15 % power loss per 1000 m above sea level).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing**, **Power** is defined as the **time rate of doing work** — the scalar quantity that quantifies how quickly a vehicle's engine or drivetrain can perform mechanical work against the resistive forces acting on the vehicle. Mathematically, it is the dot product of force and velocity, or equivalently, the product of torque and angular velocity.

For a road vehicle, the term *power* spans several engineering domains:

- **Indicated Power (IP):** The total power developed inside the engine cylinders by the combustion of fuel on the piston crown, neglecting any mechanical losses.
- **Brake Power (BP):** The power actually delivered by the engine at the crankshaft (or at the wheels), measured by a dynamometer.
- **Friction Power (FP):** The power lost to overcome internal mechanical friction of the engine.
- **Tractive Power / Road Power:** The power required at the wheel–road interface to overcome aerodynamic drag, rolling resistance, and gradient resistance, plus the power needed to accelerate the vehicle mass.
- **Maximum Power:** The peak power output an engine delivers, typically occurring near 80–90 % of maximum rated speed for naturally aspirated engines.

The fundamental governing relationship is:

$$P = \mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{v} = F \cdot v \quad \text{(translational form)}$$

$$P = T \cdot \omega = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \quad \text{(rotational form)}$$

where **T** is torque in N·m, **N** is rotational speed in revolutions per minute (rpm), and **ω** is angular velocity in rad/s.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1 — Power):** Students must master the derivations of Indicated Power, Brake Power, Friction Power, Mechanical Efficiency, and the power required to overcome vehicle resistive forces. Emphasis is placed on numerical problems involving mean effective pressure, engine speed, and road load.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are pushing a broken-down car. If you push it for 10 minutes and move it 100 m, you have done a certain amount of **work**. If your friend pushes the same car (doing the same work) but in only 5 minutes, your friend is more **powerful**. Power is simply *"how fast work gets done"*.

In a vehicle:
- **Torque** is the *strength* of the push (how hard the engine twists the crankshaft).
- **Power** is *how fast* that twisting effort can be sustained across rotations per minute.

A diesel truck might produce huge torque at low rpm (a strong push), but a petrol sports car produces slightly less torque yet more power at high rpm (it spins faster, so it pushes *more often per second*). That is why power is the true measure of a vehicle's ability to **accelerate quickly and sustain high speeds**.

- Standard metric: **1 kilowatt (kW) = 1 kJ/s = 1.341 hp (mechanical)**.
- Standard metric: **1 horsepower (hp) = 745.7 W**.
- Standard metric: **1 PS (metric hp) = 735.5 W**.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme textbook problems, conversions between **kW, hp, and PS** are routinely tested. Always retain units explicitly during calculation steps to earn full method marks.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Vehicle Engineering

Power is the single most important performance metric of a vehicle because:

1. It directly determines **maximum achievable speed** (when tractive power equals total resistance power).
2. It governs **acceleration capability** (excess power accelerates the vehicle mass).
3. It dictates **gradeability** (the steepest gradient a vehicle can climb at steady speed).
4. It influences **fuel/energy consumption** through the brake-specific fuel consumption (BSFC) curve.

The complete power-balance equation for a moving vehicle is:

$$P_{engine} = P_{rolling} + P_{aerodynamic} + P_{gradient} + P_{acceleration} + P_{losses}$$

This equation is the foundation of all vehicle performance prediction methods taught in Module 1.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Engine Power

The power developed in a reciprocating internal combustion engine is classified at three levels, each measured or computed at a different point in the engine:

### 2.1.1 Indicated Power (IP)

**Definition:** Power developed by the gas pressure on the piston during the working stroke, computed from the **indicator diagram** (P–V diagram) obtained using an *engine indicator*.

- **Mean Effective Pressure (MEP or Pm):** The average pressure that, if acted constantly on the piston throughout the stroke, would produce the same work as the actual varying pressure.
- **Imep (Indicated MEP):** Average pressure from indicator diagram.
- **Bmep (Brake MEP):** Equivalent average pressure for brake work output.
- **Fmep (Friction MEP):** Equivalent average pressure for friction work loss.

The general power equation in terms of MEP for a multi-cylinder, multi-stroke engine is:

$$P = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{60} \quad \text{(for a 2-stroke engine)}$$

$$P = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{120} \quad \text{(for a 4-stroke engine)}$$

where:
- $P_m$ = mean effective pressure in N/m² (Pa)
- $L$ = stroke length in m
- $A$ = cross-sectional area of piston = $\frac{\pi}{4} D^2$ in m²
- $n$ = rotational speed of crankshaft in rpm
- $k$ = number of cylinders

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **60** in the denominator applies to a **2-stroke engine** (one working stroke per revolution per cylinder). For a **4-stroke engine**, the factor becomes **120** (one working stroke per two revolutions per cylinder). This is one of the most commonly tested KTU concepts.

### 2.1.2 Brake Power (BP)

The power available at the engine crankshaft, measured by a **dynamometer** (Prony brake, hydraulic dynamometer, or electric dynamometer). For a Prony brake:

$$BP = \frac{2\pi N (W - S) \cdot R}{60} \quad \text{(watts)}$$

where:
- $W$ = applied dead weight in N
- $S$ = spring balance reading (or tare weight) in N
- $R$ = effective brake arm radius in m
- $N$ = crankshaft speed in rpm

### 2.1.3 Friction Power (FP)

The power absorbed by the engine's internal mechanical friction (piston rings, bearings, valve train, water pump, oil pump). Determined by:

$$FP = IP - BP$$

or by the **Willans line method** (extrapolating the IP vs. fuel consumption line to zero fuel).

### 2.1.4 Mechanical Efficiency

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP} = 1 - \frac{FP}{IP}$$

Typical values: 80–90 % for well-maintained engines.

## 2.2 Vehicle Resistance Power

### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance Power

$$P_{roll} = F_{roll} \cdot v = \mu_{r} \cdot m \cdot g \cdot v$$

where:
- $\mu_r$ = coefficient of rolling resistance (≈ 0.012–0.018 for car tyres on tarmac)
- $m$ = vehicle mass in kg
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $v$ = vehicle velocity in m/s

### 2.2.2 Aerodynamic Drag Power

$$P_{aero} = F_{aero} \cdot v = \frac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot v^{3}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = air density ≈ 1.2 kg/m³
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient
- $A_f$ = frontal area in m²
- $v$ = velocity in m/s

> [!NOTE]
> Aerodynamic power scales with the **cube of velocity**. Doubling speed increases the power needed to overcome drag by **8 times** — a critical KTU exam insight.

### 2.2.3 Gradient / Climbing Power

$$P_{grade} = m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta$$

For small angles, $\sin\theta \approx \tan\theta = \text{grade (\%) / 100}$.

### 2.2.4 Inertial (Acceleration) Power

$$P_{accel} = m_{eff} \cdot a \cdot v$$

where $m_{eff}$ includes the equivalent rotational mass of rotating components (wheels, gears, crankshaft), typically $m_{eff} = (1 + \alpha) \cdot m$, with $\alpha \approx 0.05\text{–}0.10$ for passenger cars.

### 2.2.5 Total Tractive Power Required

$$P_{tract} = P_{roll} + P_{aero} + P_{grade} + P_{accel}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Rotational Power | $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ | W (or kW ÷ 1000) | T in N·m, N in rpm |
| 2 | Indicated Power (2S) | $P_i = P_m L A n k / 60$ | W | One firing stroke per rev/cyl |
| 3 | Indicated Power (4S) | $P_i = P_m L A n k / 120$ | W | One firing stroke per 2 rev/cyl |
| 4 | Brake Power (Prony) | $P_b = 2\pi N (W-S) R / 60$ | W | Brake arm test |
| 5 | Friction Power | $P_f = P_i - P_b$ | W | By difference |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = P_b / P_i$ | dimensionless | Usually 0.80–0.90 |
| 7 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_r = \mu_r m g$ | N | Level ground |
| 8 | Aerodynamic Drag Force | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A_f v^2$ | N | $v$ in m/s |
| 9 | Climbing Force | $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ | N | Gradient angle $\theta$ |
| 10 | Total Tractive Force | $F_t = F_r + F_d + F_g + m_{eff} a$ | N | Force balance |
| 11 | Total Tractive Power | $P_t = F_t \cdot v$ | W | Power balance |
| 12 | Power Conversion | $1 \text{ kW} = 1.341 \text{ hp} = 1.36 \text{ PS}$ | — | Must memorize |
| 13 | Piston Area | $A = \pi D^2 / 4$ | m² | D in m |
| 14 | BSFC | $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / P_b$ | kg/kWh | Specific fuel consumption |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Pitfall:** In every KTU 2024 numerical, students must verify whether the engine is **2-stroke or 4-stroke** before choosing the denominator (60 vs. 120). Marks are routinely lost on this single point.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The power formulae and resistance equations are used in:

- **Vehicle homologation and certification** (type approval testing for emissions, fuel economy, maximum speed).
- **Powertrain calibration** (gear ratio selection to keep engine in its peak-power rpm band).
- **Hybrid and electric vehicle design** (where battery discharge power must match tractive power demand).
- **Racing and motorsport engineering** (power-to-weight ratio is the dominant performance metric).
- **Heavy commercial vehicle design** (where sustained power at low rpm is more critical than peak power).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Mean-Effective-Pressure Power Equation

We derive the general expression for the indicated power of a multi-cylinder engine, starting from the fundamental definition of work done by gas pressure on a piston.

**Step 1 — Work done per power stroke per cylinder:**

The instantaneous work done by gas pressure $p$ on a piston as it sweeps through an infinitesimal displacement $dx$ is:

$$dW = p \cdot A \cdot dx$$

The total work done during one complete expansion (power) stroke is the integral of pressure with respect to piston displacement, which geometrically corresponds to the **area under the indicator diagram**:

$$W_{stroke} = \int_{0}^{L} p \cdot A \, dx = A \int_{0}^{L} p \, dx$$

**Step 2 — Replacing the variable pressure with the mean effective pressure (Pm):**

By definition, the mean effective pressure $P_m$ is a constant pressure that, if it acted over the entire stroke length $L$, would produce the same work as the actual pressure. Therefore:

$$W_{stroke} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L$$

**Step 3 — Number of power strokes per minute:**

For a single cylinder:
- 2-stroke engine → one power stroke per revolution → $n$ power strokes per minute (where $n$ is rpm).
- 4-stroke engine → one power stroke per two revolutions → $n/2$ power strokes per minute.

**Step 4 — Total work per minute for $k$ cylinders:**

For 2-stroke:

$$W_{min} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot n \cdot k$$

For 4-stroke:

$$W_{min} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot \frac{n \cdot k}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Power as work per unit time:**

Power = Work per minute ÷ 60 seconds. Therefore:

For 2-stroke:

$$P_i = \frac{P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot n \cdot k}{60}$$

For 4-stroke:

$$P_i = \frac{P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot n \cdot k}{120}$$

This completes the derivation. The bracketed numbers (60 and 120) represent the seconds per minute, and the **2** in the 4-stroke denominator is the **number of crank revolutions per power stroke**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Both forms can be written compactly using the number of working strokes per minute $n_w$ per cylinder: $P_i = P_m A L \, n_w k / 60$, where $n_w = n$ (2S) or $n_w = n/2$ (4S).

## 3.2 Derivation of the Total Tractive Power Equation

**Step 1 — Force balance on a moving vehicle:**

A vehicle moving at velocity $v$ on a road of gradient $\theta$ must overcome four classes of resistance:

$$\sum F_{resist} = F_{roll} + F_{aero} + F_{grade} + F_{accel}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute each resistance term:**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r m g$$

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{2}$$

$$F_{grade} = m g \sin\theta$$

$$F_{accel} = m_{eff} \cdot a = (1 + \alpha) m \cdot \frac{dv}{dt}$$

Adding these:

$$F_{tract} = \mu_r m g + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{2} + m g \sin\theta + (1+\alpha) m \frac{dv}{dt}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply both sides by velocity to convert force to power:**

Since $P = F \cdot v$:

$$P_{tract} = \mu_r m g v + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{3} + m g v \sin\theta + (1+\alpha) m v \frac{dv}{dt}$$

This is the **fundamental power-balance equation** of a moving vehicle.

**Step 4 — Steady-state special case (constant velocity, level road):**

Setting $\theta = 0$ and $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$:

$$P_{tract,ss} = \mu_r m g v + \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{3}$$

This is the form used to compute **maximum vehicle speed** by equating to available engine power.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical (KTU 2024 Scheme Style)

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore $D = 80$ mm, stroke $L = 90$ mm, and runs at $3000$ rpm. The indicated mean effective pressure is $P_{mi} = 8$ bar. The brake torque measured on a Prony brake is $T_b = 90$ N·m and the brake arm radius is $R = 0.5$ m. Compute: (a) Indicated Power, (b) Brake Power, (c) Friction Power, (d) Mechanical Efficiency.

**Given Data:**
- $k = 4$, 4-stroke, $D = 0.080$ m, $L = 0.090$ m
- $n = 3000$ rpm
- $P_{mi} = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^{5}$ Pa
- $T_b = 90$ N·m, $R = 0.5$ m

**Step (a) — Compute piston area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.080)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 0.0064}{4} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step (b) — Indicated Power (4-stroke formula):**

$$P_i = \frac{P_{mi} L A n k}{120} = \frac{(8 \times 10^{5})(0.090)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3})(3000)(4)}{120}$$

Numerator evaluation:

$$(8 \times 10^{5}) \times 0.090 = 72{,}000$$

$$72{,}000 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 361.91$$

$$361.91 \times 3000 = 1{,}085{,}731$$

$$1{,}085{,}731 \times 4 = 4{,}342{,}923$$

$$P_i = \frac{4{,}342{,}923}{120} = 36{,}191 \text{ W} \approx 36.19 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (c) — Brake Power from torque:**

$$P_b = \frac{2 \pi n T_b}{60} = \frac{2 \pi (3000)(90)}{60} = \frac{540{,}000 \pi}{60} = 9000 \pi$$

$$P_b = 28{,}274 \text{ W} \approx 28.27 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (d) — Friction Power:**

$$P_f = P_i - P_b = 36.19 - 28.27 = 7.92 \text{ kW}$$

**Step (e) — Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_m = \frac{P_b}{P_i} = \frac{28.27}{36.19} = 0.7812 = 78.12\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result Validation:** A mechanical efficiency of ≈ 78 % is realistic for a passenger-car petrol engine (typical range 75–88 %). If the calculated value were > 100 % or < 50 %, it would indicate an arithmetic error in either IP or BP — KTU examiners reward such sanity checks.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Power Contributions vs. Speed

The following table highlights how each resistive power scales with velocity — a key analytical tool for KTU theory questions.

| Power Component | Functional Form of $v$ | Dominates At | Engineering Implication |
|-----------------|------------------------|--------------|-------------------------|
| $P_{roll}$ | $v$ (linear) | Low speed (< 30 km/h) | Rolling-resistance reduction via low $\mu_r$ tyres |
| $P_{aero}$ | $v^3$ (cubic) | High speed (> 80 km/h) | Drag reduction via streamlining is critical for top speed |
| $P_{grade}$ | $v$ (linear) | Any speed on slope | Engine must be sized for sustained hill-climbing |
| $P_{accel}$ | $v$ (linear in $v$, but $a$ varies) | Transient (launch, overtaking) | Determines 0–100 km/h time |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Flow Architecture in a Vehicle Powertrain

The following Mermaid block maps the complete flow of power from combustion in the cylinders, through the drivetrain, to the road. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as mandated for complex engineering topics.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Energy Input] --> B[Combustion in Cylinder]
    B --> C[Indicated Power IP]
    C --> D{Friction Losses}
    D --> E[Friction Power FP]
    C --> F[Brake Power BP at Crankshaft]
    F --> G[Transmission Gearbox]
    G --> H[Driveshaft and Differential]
    H --> I[Wheel-Road Interface]
    I --> J[Roll Resistance]
    I --> K[Aerodynamic Drag]
    I --> L[Climbing Resistance]
    I --> M[Inertial Acceleration]
    J --> N[Net Vehicle Motion]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    E --> O[Heat and Mechanical Loss Sinks]
    F --> O
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- **A → B → C:** Chemical energy of fuel is released by combustion and converted to indicated power.
- **C → D → E:** A portion of IP is lost to internal mechanical friction (rings, bearings, pumps).
- **C → F:** The remainder is **Brake Power** at the crankshaft — measurable by a dynamometer.
- **F → G → H → I:** BP flows through gearbox, driveshaft, and differential to the wheel–road contact patch.
- **I → J, K, L, M:** At the road, the delivered power is consumed in four ways: rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, gradient climbing, and accelerating the vehicle mass.
- **E → O and F → O:** All losses ultimately dissipate as heat to coolant, oil, and ambient air.

## 4.2 Engine Power Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Combustion Energy] --> B[Indicated Power IP]
    B --> C[Brake Power BP]
    B --> D[Friction Power FP]
    C --> E[Mechanical Output]
    D --> F[Internal Loss as Heat]
    E --> G[Tractive Power at Wheels]
    G --> H[Overcomes Resistance]
    G --> I[Accelerates Vehicle Mass]
    H --> J[Rolling Resistance]
    H --> K[Aerodynamic Drag]
    H --> L[Gradient Resistance]
```

## 4.3 Power vs. Speed Operating Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low Speed] --> B[Rolling Power Dominates]
    B --> C[Mid Speed]
    C --> D[Rolling and Aerodynamic Comparable]
    D --> E[High Speed]
    E --> F[Aerodynamic Power Dominates]
    F --> G[Top Speed Limit]
```

This map correlates the three operating regions of a vehicle with the dominant power sink — a frequent KTU conceptual question in Part A (3-mark short answers).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1.** Define Indicated Power and Brake Power. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**
- **Indicated Power (IP):** The power developed by the gas pressure on the piston inside the engine cylinder, computed from the indicator diagram (P–V diagram) using the indicated mean effective pressure. It represents the theoretical maximum power from combustion.
- **Brake Power (BP):** The power actually available at the engine crankshaft, measured by a dynamometer. It is always less than IP because some power is lost to internal friction.
- **Relationship:** $BP = IP - FP$, where FP is friction power, and $\eta_{mech} = BP / IP$.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2.** Why does the power required to overcome aerodynamic drag increase as the cube of velocity?

**Model Answer:** Aerodynamic drag force is given by $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{2}$. The power needed to overcome a force moving at velocity $v$ is $P = F \cdot v$. Multiplying, $P_{aero} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{3}$. Hence, the cubic dependence arises from the product of the velocity-squared drag force and the linear velocity. This is why high-speed cruising is dominated by drag and why streamlining is critical beyond ~80 km/h.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Module 1 Past Paper Pattern, ESE]**

### Question A (14 Marks) — Indicated Power Computation

**(a)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine has a bore of 100 mm and a stroke of 120 mm. It runs at 1800 rpm and develops an indicated mean effective pressure of 7 bar. Calculate the indicated power developed by the engine. State any assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define the following terms with suitable equations: (i) Mechanical efficiency, (ii) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC), (iii) Friction Power. A diesel engine consumes 12 kg of fuel per hour and develops a brake power of 45 kW. Compute its BSFC. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Indicated Power Calculation**

Given:
- $k = 6$, 4-stroke, $D = 0.100$ m, $L = 0.120$ m
- $n = 1800$ rpm
- $P_{mi} = 7$ bar $= 7 \times 10^{5}$ Pa

**Step 1 — Piston area:**

$$A = \frac{\pi (0.100)^{2}}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the 4-stroke IP formula:**

$$P_i = \frac{P_{mi} \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n \cdot k}{120}$$

$$P_i = \frac{(7 \times 10^{5})(0.120)(7.854 \times 10^{-3})(1800)(6)}{120}$$

Step-by-step numerator:

$$7 \times 10^{5} \times 0.120 = 84{,}000$$

$$84{,}000 \times 7.854 \times 10^{-3} = 659.7$$

$$659.7 \times 1800 = 1{,}187{,}514$$

$$1{,}187{,}514 \times 6 = 7{,}125{,}082$$

$$P_i = \frac{7{,}125{,}082}{120} = 59{,}376 \text{ W} \approx 59.38 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3 — Assumptions:**
- Pressure variation is fully captured by the constant MEP value.
- No gas leakage past piston rings.
- Combustion completes before top dead centre in a manner represented by the average pressure.
- All cylinders contribute equally (engine is balanced).

**Answer:** $P_i \approx 59.38$ kW

---

**(b) Definitions and BSFC Calculation**

**(i) Mechanical Efficiency:** The ratio of brake power output to indicated power developed in the cylinders, indicating how effectively the engine converts cylinder pressure work into usable shaft work.

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BP}{IP}$$

**(ii) Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC):** The mass of fuel consumed per unit brake power per unit time.

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{P_b} \quad \text{(units: kg/kWh or g/kWh)}$$

**(iii) Friction Power:** The portion of indicated power lost to internal mechanical friction of pistons, bearings, valves, pumps, and accessories.

$$FP = IP - BP$$

**BSFC Numerical:**

Given $\dot{m}_f = 12$ kg/hr, $P_b = 45$ kW.

$$BSFC = \frac{12 \text{ kg/hr}}{45 \text{ kW}} = 0.2667 \text{ kg/kWh} = 266.7 \text{ g/kWh}$$

**Answer:** $BSFC = 266.7$ g/kWh — typical for a medium-duty diesel engine.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Vehicle Resistance Power & Maximum Speed

**(a)** A passenger car of mass 1500 kg travels on a level road at 90 km/h. The coefficient of rolling resistance is 0.015, the drag coefficient is 0.32, the frontal area is 2.2 m², and air density is 1.2 kg/m³. Compute (i) the rolling resistance power, (ii) the aerodynamic drag power, and (iii) the total tractive power required. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** If the engine delivers a maximum power of 70 kW at the wheels, estimate the maximum speed of the car on the same level road, neglecting acceleration. Show all steps in your calculation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Power Computation at 90 km/h**

Given:
- $m = 1500$ kg, $v = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s
- $\mu_r = 0.015$, $C_D = 0.32$, $A_f = 2.2$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**(i) Rolling resistance power:**

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r m g = 0.015 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 220.7 \text{ N}$$

$$P_{roll} = F_{roll} \cdot v = 220.7 \times 25 = 5518 \text{ W} \approx 5.52 \text{ kW}$$

**(ii) Aerodynamic drag power:**

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{2} = \frac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.32)(2.2)(25)^{2}$$

Step by step:
$$(25)^{2} = 625$$

$$1.2 \times 0.32 = 0.384$$

$$0.384 \times 2.2 = 0.8448$$

$$0.8448 \times 625 = 528.0$$

$$F_{aero} = \frac{1}{2} \times 528.0 = 264.0 \text{ N}$$

$$P_{aero} = F_{aero} \cdot v = 264.0 \times 25 = 6600 \text{ W} = 6.60 \text{ kW}$$

**(iii) Total tractive power:**

$$P_{tract} = P_{roll} + P_{aero} = 5.52 + 6.60 = 12.12 \text{ kW}$$

**Answer:** $P_{roll} \approx 5.52$ kW, $P_{aero} \approx 6.60$ kW, $P_{tract} \approx 12.12$ kW

---

**(b) Maximum Speed Estimation**

At maximum speed (steady state, level road, no acceleration), the available wheel power equals the total resistance power:

$$P_{avail} = P_{roll}(v) + P_{aero}(v)$$

The rolling term scales linearly with $v$, and the aerodynamic term scales as $v^3$. Converting both to a common variable $v$ in m/s:

$$P_{roll}(v) = \mu_r m g \cdot v = (0.015)(1500)(9.81) v = 220.7 v \text{ W}$$

$$P_{aero}(v) = \frac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^{3} = \frac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.32)(2.2) v^{3} = 0.4224 v^{3} \text{ W}$$

Setting total equal to available power:

$$220.7 v + 0.4224 v^{3} = 70{,}000$$

This is a cubic in $v$. Solving numerically by trial:

**Try $v = 45$ m/s (162 km/h):**

$$220.7 \times 45 = 9931.5$$

$$0.4224 \times 45^{3} = 0.4224 \times 91{,}125 = 38{,}491$$

$$\text{Sum} = 48{,}423 \text{ W} < 70{,}000$$

**Try $v = 52$ m/s (187.2 km/h):**

$$220.7 \times 52 = 11{,}476$$

$$0.4224 \times 52^{3} = 0.4224 \times 140{,}608 = 59{,}393$$

$$\text{Sum} = 70{,}869 \text{ W} \approx 70{,}000 \text{ W} \checkmark$$

**Try $v = 51.8$ m/s (interpolation):**

$$220.7 \times 51.8 = 11{,}432$$

$$0.4224 \times 51.8^{3} = 0.4224 \times 138{,}991 = 58{,}717$$

$$\text{Sum} = 70{,}149 \text{ W}$$

**Acceptable answer:** $v_{max} \approx 52$ m/s $\approx 187$ km/h.

**Final Answer:** $v_{max} \approx 187$ km/h (a realistic top speed for a 70 kW mid-size car with the given aerodynamic parameters).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion:** Students frequently forget to convert km/h to m/s when applying $F = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^2$ and $P = F \cdot v$. This single error cascades into all subsequent calculations and causes loss of 4–5 marks.
> 2. **MEP units:** Mean effective pressure must be converted from bar to Pa (multiply by $10^5$) before substituting into the IP formula.
> 3. **Stroke selection:** 2-stroke vs. 4-stroke denominator confusion (60 vs. 120) is the #1 KTU power-calculation pitfall.
> 4. **Power direction:** When a vehicle descends a slope, gradient power is *negative* (regenerative, especially in EVs). Examiners test this sign convention.
> 5. **Cubic equation solving:** For maximum-speed problems, you may solve by iteration; clearly state trial values and the convergence logic to earn method marks even if the final answer is slightly off.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Topic: Power (Module 1)**

- **Power definition:** $P = W / t = F v = T \omega = 2\pi N T / 60$.
- **Indicated Power (4-stroke):** $P_i = P_m L A n k / 120$.
- **Indicated Power (2-stroke):** $P_i = P_m L A n k / 60$.
- **Brake Power (Prony brake):** $P_b = 2\pi N (W - S) R / 60$.
- **Friction Power:** $P_f = P_i - P_b$.
- **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_{mech} = P_b / P_i$, typically 75–90 %.
- **BSFC:** $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / P_b$ in kg/kWh; lower is better.
- **Rolling resistance force:** $F_r = \mu_r m g$ (level ground).
- **Aerodynamic drag force:** $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^2$.
- **Drag power:** $P_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f v^3$ — **cubic in velocity** — dominates high-speed power budget.
- **Climbing power:** $P_g = m g v \sin\theta$.
- **Acceleration power:** $P_a = (1 + \alpha) m a v$.
- **Total tractive power:** $P_{tract} = P_r + P_d + P_g + P_a$.
- **Maximum speed:** When available engine power equals total resistance power at steady state.
- **Standard conversions:** $1 \text{ kW} = 1.341 \text{ hp (mech)} = 1.36 \text{ PS}$.
- **Air density (standard):** $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³ (at sea level, 25 °C).
- **Piston area:** $A = \pi D^2 / 4$, with D in metres.
- **Always** convert bar to Pa ($\times 10^5$) and km/h to m/s (÷ 3.6) before substituting.
- **Always** check whether the engine is 2-stroke or 4-stroke before picking the IP denominator.
- **Always** retain sign conventions for gradient power (negative on downhill).
- **Sanity check:** $\eta_{mech}$ must lie between 0 and 1; if it does not, an arithmetic error has occurred.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Torque
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Torque

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TORQUE (τ)** is a measure of the *rotational force* or *twisting effort* that can cause an object to rotate about an axis. In the context of vehicle engineering, **engine torque** is the instantaneous rotational force produced by the combustion of fuel-air mixture on the engine's crankshaft, which ultimately drives the vehicle's wheels.

Mathematically, torque is defined as the **cross product** of the moment arm vector and the applied force vector:

$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$

Where:
- $\vec{\tau}$ = Torque vector (N·m)
- $\vec{r}$ = Position vector from the axis of rotation to the point of force application (m)
- $\vec{F}$ = Applied force vector (N)

For a force acting perpendicular to the moment arm (which is the standard case in engine analysis), the magnitude simplifies to:

$$\tau = F \cdot r$$

The SI unit is **N·m** (Newton-meter), and it must **never** be written as "Joules" because torque and energy, though dimensionally equivalent, are fundamentally different physical quantities.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Stubborn Bottle Cap"**
> 
> Imagine trying to open a tightly sealed water bottle. When you grip the cap with your fingers, you are essentially applying a **rotational force**. The harder you grip (more force) and the larger the cap diameter (longer moment arm), the easier it is to twist it open. That "twisting effort" you feel in your wrist is precisely what engineers call **TORQUE**.
> 
> Similarly, an engine produces torque on its crankshaft. A high-torque engine (like a diesel truck engine) can twist its crankshaft with tremendous rotational force, allowing it to haul heavy loads from a standstill. A low-torque engine (like a small motorcycle engine) produces less rotational effort but can spin faster, giving high power output.

### Key Intuitive Points:
1. **Torque is what *starts* motion** — it overcomes inertia and gets the vehicle moving from rest.
2. **Horsepower is what *keeps* motion going** — it determines how fast the motion can be sustained.
3. A **high torque at low RPM** is ideal for vehicles carrying heavy loads (trucks, tractors).
4. A **moderate torque at high RPM** is preferred for racing and high-speed applications.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!TIP]
> **Standard Units & Conversions Used in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> - **SI Unit:** N·m (Newton-meter)
> - **CGS Unit:** dyne·cm
> - **Imperial Unit:** lb·ft (pound-foot)
> - **Conversion:** $1 \text{ lb·ft} \approx 1.3558 \text{ N·m}$
> - **Engine Torque Range (typical):**
>   - Small motorcycle: ~15–25 N·m
>   - Compact car: ~150–250 N·m
>   - Heavy-duty truck: ~1500–2500 N·m

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The "Why" and "How" of Engine Torque

The generation of torque in an internal combustion engine is a four-stage mechanical process:

- **Stage 1 — Combustion Pressure Generation:** The fuel-air mixture inside the cylinder is ignited, producing high-pressure gases (typically 30–80 bar in petrol engines, up to 200 bar in diesel engines).

- **Stage 2 — Force Transmission to Piston:** This combustion pressure acts on the piston crown, generating a linear force $F = P \cdot A$, where $P$ is the instantaneous cylinder pressure and $A$ is the piston crown area.

- **Stage 3 — Conversion of Linear Motion to Rotation:** The connecting rod converts the reciprocating (linear) motion of the piston into rotational motion of the crankshaft. The geometry of the crank-slider mechanism means the effective moment arm varies with crank angle.

- **Stage 4 — Torque Output at Crankshaft:** The instantaneous torque on the crank-pin is $\tau = F \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta)$, where $r$ is the crank radius and $\theta$ is the angle between the connecting rod and the cylinder axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does torque fluctuate?** Because the force on the piston varies throughout the 4-stroke cycle (compression, power, exhaust, intake), and the moment arm also varies with crank angle. This is why **flywheels** are used — they store rotational kinetic energy and smooth out torque fluctuations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | Basic torque (force perpendicular to lever) | N·m |
| 2 | $\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ | Vector form of torque | N·m |
| 3 | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ | Power-torque-angular velocity relation | W, N·m, rad/s |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{P}{\omega}$ | Torque derived from power | N·m |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi \cdot N}$ | Torque in terms of RPM ($N$ in rpm) | N·m |
| 6 | $\tau = \dfrac{\text{BMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot n_R}{4\pi}$ | Torque from Brake Mean Effective Pressure | N·m |
| 7 | $\tau = \dfrac{F \cdot D}{2}$ | Torque from tangential force on a pulley/drum | N·m |
| 8 | $F_t = \dfrac{\tau}{r_w}$ | Tractive force at the wheel | N |
| 9 | $I \cdot \alpha = \tau_{net}$ | Newton's rotational law | N·m |
| 10 | $W_{rot} = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Rotational kinetic energy | J |

> Where:
> - $\omega$ = angular velocity (rad/s) = $\dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$
> - $N$ = engine speed (revolutions per minute, rpm)
> - $V_d$ = total displacement volume ($m^3$)
> - $n_R$ = number of crank revolutions per power stroke (2 for 4-stroke, 1 for 2-stroke)
> - BMEP = Brake Mean Effective Pressure (Pa or bar)
> - $r_w$ = effective wheel radius (m)

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does a vehicle engineer care about torque?**
> 
> 1. **Gradeability:** The maximum gradient a vehicle can climb is directly proportional to the available torque at the wheels.
> 2. **Acceleration:** Initial acceleration (0–60 mph time) is governed by the low-end torque reserve.
> 3. **Towing Capacity:** Towing capacity is a direct function of peak torque at low RPM.
> 4. **Transmission Sizing:** Gear ratios are designed to keep the engine within its peak torque band.
> 5. **Clutch & Driveline Design:** Clutch torque capacity must exceed engine peak torque (with safety factor).

## 2.4 Engine Torque Characteristics — Concept of Torque Curve

A typical engine produces a **torque curve** $\tau(N)$ that:
- Rises from idle RPM
- Reaches a **peak torque** at a specific RPM
- Drops off at high RPM due to volumetric efficiency reduction and friction losses

This curve is experimentally measured using a **dynamometer** and forms the foundation of all vehicle performance calculations.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Torque from Power and Angular Velocity

Starting from the fundamental definition of power as the rate of doing work:

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt}$$

**Step 1:** For rotational motion, the work done by a torque $\tau$ rotating through an infinitesimal angle $d\theta$ is:

$$dW = \tau \cdot d\theta$$

**Step 2:** Substitute this into the power equation:

$$P = \frac{d}{dt}(\tau \cdot \theta) = \tau \cdot \frac{d\theta}{dt}$$

**Step 3:** Recognize that angular velocity $\omega = \dfrac{d\theta}{dt}$:

$$P = \tau \cdot \omega$$

**Step 4:** Solving for torque:

$$\tau = \frac{P}{\omega}$$

**Step 5:** Converting angular velocity from rad/s to RPM using $\omega = \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$\tau = \frac{P \cdot 60}{2\pi N}$$

This is the most frequently used form in KTU numerical problems.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Torque from Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)

The **BMEP** is a fictitious pressure that, if acted uniformly on the piston throughout the power stroke, would produce the measured brake torque.

**Step 1:** Work done per power stroke = Mean effective pressure × Displacement volume:

$$W_{stroke} = \text{BMEP} \cdot V_d$$

**Step 2:** For a 4-stroke engine, one power stroke occurs every **2 crank revolutions**, so the number of power strokes per second is:

$$n_{strokes} = \frac{N}{2 \cdot 60}$$

**Step 3:** Total power developed per second (i.e., power):

$$P = W_{stroke} \cdot n_{strokes} = \text{BMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{120}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute $P = \tau \cdot \omega = \tau \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$:

$$\tau \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \text{BMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{N}{120}$$

**Step 5:** Cancel $N$ from both sides and solve for $\tau$:

$$\tau \cdot \frac{2\pi}{60} = \text{BMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot \frac{1}{120}$$

$$\tau = \frac{\text{BMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot 60}{120 \cdot 2\pi}$$

$$\boxed{\tau = \frac{\text{BMEP} \cdot V_d}{4\pi}}$$

> [!TIP]
> This derivation is a **guaranteed KTU board question** every alternate year. Memorize the $4\pi$ in the denominator.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Tractive Force at the Wheels from Engine Torque

**Step 1:** Let $\tau_e$ = engine torque (N·m), $\eta_d$ = drivetrain efficiency, $G_1, G_2, ..., G_n$ = individual gear ratios (gearbox, final drive).

**Step 2:** Total torque amplification from engine to wheels:

$$\tau_w = \tau_e \cdot \eta_d \cdot G_1 \cdot G_2 \cdot \ldots \cdot G_n$$

**Step 3:** The tractive force $F_t$ at the tire-road contact patch is the wheel torque divided by the effective wheel radius $r_w$:

$$F_t = \frac{\tau_w}{r_w}$$

**Step 4:** Substituting:

$$\boxed{F_t = \frac{\tau_e \cdot \eta_d \cdot G_{total}}{r_w}}$$

## 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a total displacement of 1.5 L. At 3000 rpm, the BMEP is measured as 10 bar. The drivetrain efficiency is 88%, and the overall gear ratio in 3rd gear is 3.5. If the effective wheel radius is 0.3 m, calculate: (a) Engine Torque, (b) Tractive Force at the wheels.

**Given:**
- $V_d = 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$
- $N = 3000 \text{ rpm}$
- $\text{BMEP} = 10 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $\eta_d = 0.88$, $G = 3.5$, $r_w = 0.3 \text{ m}$

**Solution (a) — Engine Torque:**

Using the derived formula:

$$\tau_e = \frac{\text{BMEP} \cdot V_d}{4\pi}$$

Substituting:

$$\tau_e = \frac{(10^6) \cdot (1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{4\pi}$$

$$\tau_e = \frac{1500}{12.566}$$

$$\tau_e = 119.37 \text{ N·m}$$

**Solution (b) — Tractive Force:**

$$F_t = \frac{\tau_e \cdot \eta_d \cdot G}{r_w}$$

$$F_t = \frac{119.37 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 3.5}{0.3}$$

$$F_t = \frac{367.66}{0.3} = 1225.5 \text{ N}$$

**Final Answer:** Engine torque = **119.37 N·m**, Tractive force = **1225.5 N**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Torque Flow Architecture in a Vehicle (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates how torque is generated, transmitted, amplified, and delivered to the road surface:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[FUEL AIR MIXTURE] --> B[COMBUSTION CHAMBER]
    B --> C[PISTON LINEAR FORCE F]
    C --> D[CONNECTING ROD MECHANISM]
    D --> E[CRANKSHAFT TORQUE tau e]
    E --> F[FLYWHEEL SMOOTHING]
    F --> G[CLUTCH ASSEMBLY]
    G --> H[GEARBOX GEAR RATIO G1]
    H --> I[PROPELLER SHAFT]
    I --> J[FINAL DRIVE GEAR RATIO G2]
    J --> K[DIFFERENTIAL]
    K --> L[WHEEL TORQUE tau w]
    L --> M[TRACTIVE FORCE F t AT TIRE]
    M --> N[ROAD SURFACE REACTION]
    
    O[DRIVETRAIN LOSS eta d] -.-> H
    O -.-> I
    O -.-> J
```

## 4.2 Torque-Vs-Speed Characteristic Curve (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SC[Engine Torque Speed Map]
        direction TB
        P1[Low RPM: Torque Rises] --> P2[Mid RPM: Peak Torque]
        P2 --> P3[High RPM: Torque Falls]
    end
    
    P2 --> Q1[Power = Torque x Speed]
    Q1 --> Q2[Power Continues to Rise]
    
    R1[Fuel Economy Band] -.-> P2
    R2[Peak Performance Zone] -.-> P2
```

## 4.3 Physical Analogy Schematic — The Wrench Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[APPLIED FORCE F at handle] --> B[MOMENT ARM r]
    B --> C[TORQUE tau equals F times r]
    C --> D[ROTATION OF BOLT OR SHAFT]
    
    E[Long r = High Torque] -.-> B
    F[High F = High Torque] -.-> A
```

## 4.4 Dynamometer Setup for Torque Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DYNO[Engine Dynamometer Test Cell]
        E[TEST ENGINE] --> C[COUPLING]
        C --> D[EDDY CURRENT DYNAMOMETER]
        D --> S[STRAIN GAUGE TORQUE SENSOR]
        S --> L[LOAD CONTROL UNIT]
        L --> M[DATA ACQUISITION SYSTEM]
        
        T[TACHOMETER] --> M
        F[FUEL FLOW METER] --> M
    end
    
    M --> OUT[OUTPUT: TORQUE Vs RPM CURVE]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]
**Define torque. Explain its significance in vehicle performance with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Torque is the rotational equivalent of force. It is defined as the product of the magnitude of the force and the perpendicular distance from the axis of rotation to the line of action of the force. Mathematically, $\tau = F \cdot r$, measured in N·m.
> 
> **Significance in Vehicle Performance (1.5 marks):** Torque determines the *pulling/thrust capability* of a vehicle. For example, a heavy truck requires high torque at low RPM to haul loads up a slope, while a sports car requires sustained torque across the RPM range for rapid acceleration. The tractive force at the wheels is directly computed from engine torque via the formula $F_t = \dfrac{\tau_e \cdot \eta_d \cdot G_{total}}{r_w}$.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between torque and power. Why is torque not measured in Joules despite dimensional equivalence?**

**Model Answer (Target: 3 marks):**

> **Distinction (2 marks):**
> | Aspect | Torque | Power |
> |---|---|---|
> | Physical meaning | Rotational force/twisting moment | Rate of doing work |
> | Symbol | $\tau$ | $P$ |
> | Formula | $\tau = F \cdot r$ | $P = \tau \cdot \omega$ |
> | Nature | Vector (with direction) | Scalar |
> 
> **Why not Joules? (1 mark):** Although torque and energy share dimensions $[ML^2T^{-2}]$, torque is **not energy**. Energy is a scalar quantity representing work done or stored, while torque is a vector quantity representing a *rotational tendency*. Using Joules would obscure this fundamental conceptual difference, even though numerically $1 \text{ N·m} = 1 \text{ J}$.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the torque of an internal combustion engine in terms of Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP), displacement volume, and number of cylinders. State clearly the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine running at 2800 rpm develops a torque of 110 N·m. The engine has a bore of 80 mm and a stroke of 90 mm. Calculate: (i) The Brake Power in kW, (ii) The BMEP in bar. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Refer to **Section 3.2** above for the complete derivation. Key steps to present in the exam:

1. Write $W_{stroke} = \text{BMEP} \cdot V_d$ — **1 mark**
2. Power strokes per second for 4-stroke = $\dfrac{N}{120}$ — **1 mark**
3. Power $P = \text{BMEP} \cdot V_d \cdot \dfrac{N}{120}$ — **1 mark**
4. Equate to $P = \tau \omega = \tau \cdot \dfrac{2\pi N}{60}$ — **1.5 marks**
5. Final boxed expression $\tau = \dfrac{\text{BMEP} \cdot V_d}{4\pi}$ — **1.5 marks**
6. State assumption: BMEP is a fictitious average pressure that produces the same torque as the actual variable combustion pressure — **1 mark**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of cylinders = 4
- $N = 2800$ rpm
- $\tau = 110$ N·m
- Bore $D = 0.08$ m, Stroke $L = 0.09$ m

**Step 1: Calculate Displacement Volume**

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 \cdot L \cdot n_{cyl} = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.08)^2 (0.09) (4)$$

$$V_d = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 \times 0.09 \times 4 = 1.810 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate Brake Power (3.5 marks)**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 2800}{60} = 293.21 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$P = \tau \cdot \omega = 110 \times 293.21 = 32{,}253 \text{ W} \approx 32.25 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 3: Calculate BMEP (3.5 marks)**

$$\text{BMEP} = \frac{4\pi \tau}{V_d} = \frac{4\pi \times 110}{1.810 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\text{BMEP} = \frac{1382.30}{1.810 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.636 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{\text{BMEP} = 7.636 \text{ bar}}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the concept of torque curve in an internal combustion engine. Sketch a typical petrol engine torque curve and identify its key features. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle has an engine that produces a maximum torque of 200 N·m at 3000 rpm. The drivetrain efficiency is 90%, the gearbox ratio in 2nd gear is 2.8, the final drive ratio is 3.7, and the effective wheel radius is 0.32 m. Calculate: (i) The tractive force at the wheels in 2nd gear, (ii) The maximum grade the vehicle can climb if its mass is 1200 kg. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Torque Curve Explanation (7 marks):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** A torque curve is a plot of engine torque $\tau$ against engine speed $N$ (rpm). It is experimentally obtained on an engine dynamometer by sweeping the throttle across the full RPM range.

- **Key Features (3.5 marks):**
  - **Low RPM Region (0–1500 rpm):** Torque is low due to poor volumetric efficiency, incomplete combustion, and high friction losses.
  - **Mid RPM Region (1500–3500 rpm):** Torque rises and reaches a **peak** where volumetric efficiency is maximum.
  - **High RPM Region (>4000 rpm):** Torque drops due to valve timing limitations, increased pumping losses, and rising friction.

- **Petrol vs Diesel Curve Comparison (1.5 marks):** Diesel engines have a flatter, lower-RPM torque curve with high peak torque; petrol engines have a narrower peak at higher RPM.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\tau_e = 200$ N·m, $\eta_d = 0.90$, $G_1 = 2.8$, $G_2 = 3.7$, $r_w = 0.32$ m, $m = 1200$ kg

**Step 1: Calculate Total Gear Ratio**

$$G_{total} = G_1 \times G_2 = 2.8 \times 3.7 = 10.36$$

**Step 2: Calculate Wheel Torque**

$$\tau_w = \tau_e \cdot \eta_d \cdot G_{total} = 200 \times 0.90 \times 10.36 = 1864.8 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Tractive Force (3.5 marks)**

$$F_t = \frac{\tau_w}{r_w} = \frac{1864.8}{0.32} = 5827.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Maximum Grade (3.5 marks)**

At maximum grade, tractive force balances the gravitational component along the slope:

$$F_t = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta_{max})$$

$$\sin(\theta_{max}) = \frac{F_t}{m \cdot g} = \frac{5827.5}{1200 \times 9.81} = 0.4950$$

$$\theta_{max} = \sin^{-1}(0.4950) = 29.66°$$

$$\text{Grade \%} = \tan(29.66°) \times 100 = 57.0\%$$

$$\boxed{\text{Tractive Force} = 5827.5 \text{ N}, \quad \theta_{max} = 29.66° \ (\approx 57\%)}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Board Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Blunder (–1 mark):** Writing torque as "Nm" without the middle dot. Always write **N·m** for full credit.
> 2. **Formula Mix-up (–2 marks):** Confusing $P = \tau \omega$ with $P = \tau N$. The latter is WRONG unless you include the factor $\dfrac{2\pi}{60}$.
> 3. **Stroke Confusion (–2 marks):** Forgetting the factor of **2** in the denominator for 4-stroke engines when computing power strokes per second. Always write $\dfrac{N}{120}$ for 4-stroke, $\dfrac{N}{60}$ for 2-stroke.
> 4. **No Vector Notation (–0.5 mark):** When defining torque mathematically, present it in cross-product form $\vec{\tau} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$ to demonstrate conceptual clarity, even if you then use the scalar form.
> 5. **Skipping Assumption Statements (–1 mark):** In derivation questions, KTU examiners **explicitly award marks** for listing assumptions. Don't skip them.
> 6. **g = 9.81 vs g = 10:** Always use **g = 9.81 m/s²** in numerical problems unless the question explicitly says otherwise. Using g = 10 costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
