# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Brake Power from Fundamental Principles

We start from the rotational analogue of $P = F \cdot v$.

**Step 1 — Linear Work Done by a Force.**

A force $F$ moving through distance $s$ does work $W = F \cdot s$.

**Step 2 — Power as Rate of Doing Work.**

$$P = \frac{dW}{dt} = F \cdot \frac{ds}{dt} = F \cdot v$$

**Step 3 — Convert to Rotational Motion.**

For a force $F$ acting tangentially at radius $r$, the linear velocity at that point is $v = \omega r$, where $\omega$ is angular velocity in rad/s. The tangential force is $F = T/r$, where $T$ is torque.

Substituting:

$$P = F \cdot v = \left(\frac{T}{r}\right) \cdot (\omega r) = T \cdot \omega$$

**Step 4 — Express $\omega$ in RPM.**

$$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60}$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed in revolutions per minute (RPM).

**Step 5 — Final Brake Power Equation.**

$$\boxed{BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;\;\text{Watts}}$$

If $T$ is in N·m and $N$ in RPM, BP comes out directly in Watts. To convert to kW, divide by 1000.

## 3.2 Derivation of Indicated Power from MEP Concept

**Step 1 — Work Per Cycle.**

If $p_{mi}$ (IMEP, in N/m²) acts on a piston of area $A$ m² through stroke $L$ m, the work done per cycle (per cylinder) is:

$$W_{\text{cycle}} = p_{mi} \cdot A \cdot L = p_{mi} \cdot V_s$$

**Step 2 — Number of Power Strokes Per Second.**

- For a 4-stroke engine: 1 power stroke every **2 revolutions**. If the engine runs at $N$ RPM, revolutions/sec = $N/60$, so power strokes/sec per cylinder = $N/(2 \cdot 60)$.
- For a 2-stroke engine: 1 power stroke every revolution → $N/60$ power strokes/sec per cylinder.

**Step 3 — Multiply by Number of Cylinders $k$.**

**Step 4 — Final Indicated Power Equation.**

For a 4-stroke engine:

$$IP = p_{mi} \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{120} \cdot k = \frac{p_{mi} \, L \, A \, N \, k}{2 \times 60}$$

For a 2-stroke engine:

$$IP = p_{mi} \cdot V_s \cdot \frac{N}{60} \cdot k = \frac{p_{mi} \, L \, A \, N \, k}{60}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example — Full Engine Parameter Calculation

**Problem Statement (KTU Style):**

> A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 80 mm and stroke of 100 mm. It runs at 3000 RPM and develops a brake torque of 95 N·m. The fuel consumption is 8.5 kg/hr and the calorific value of fuel is 44,000 kJ/kg. The air consumption is measured as 120 kg/hr. Ambient conditions are 1 bar, 30 °C.
>
> Calculate:
> 1. Brake Power (BP)
> 2. Brake Mean Effective Pressure (BMEP)
> 3. Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)
> 4. Brake Thermal Efficiency ($\eta_{bth}$)
> 5. Volumetric Efficiency ($\eta_v$)
> 6. Air–Fuel Ratio

**Given Data (extract into variables):**

$D = 0.080$ m, $L = 0.100$ m, $k = 4$, $N = 3000$ RPM, $T = 95$ N·m, $\dot{m}_f = 8.5$ kg/hr, $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg, $\dot{m}_a = 120$ kg/hr, $P_{atm} = 10^5$ N/m², $T_{atm} = 303$ K.

### Step 1 — Compute Geometric Area

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (0.080)^2 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \;\;\text{m}^2$$

### Step 2 — Compute Swept Volume per Cylinder and Total

$$V_s = A \cdot L = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.100 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \;\;\text{m}^3$$

$$V_s^{\text{total}} = V_s \times k = 5.0265 \times 10^{-4} \times 4 = 2.0106 \times 10^{-3} \;\;\text{m}^3$$

### Step 3 — (i) Brake Power

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 3000 \times 95}{60}$$

Let us evaluate the numerator: $2 \pi \times 3000 \times 95 = 2 \times 3.1416 \times 3000 \times 95 = 1{,}790{,}716$

$$BP = \frac{1{,}790{,}716}{60} = 29{,}845.3 \;\;\text{W} = \mathbf{29.85 \;\;kW}$$

### Step 4 — (ii) BMEP

For a 4-stroke engine:

$$BMEP = \frac{BP \times 60 \times 2}{L \, A \, N \, k} = \frac{29{,}845.3 \times 120}{0.100 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 4}$$

Numerator: $29{,}845.3 \times 120 = 3{,}581{,}436$

Denominator: $0.100 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \times 3000 \times 4 = 6.0318$

$$BMEP = \frac{3{,}581{,}436}{6.0318} = 5.94 \times 10^{5} \;\;\text{N/m}^2 = \mathbf{5.94 \;\;bar}$$

### Step 5 — (iii) BSFC

Convert $\dot{m}_f$ to kg/kW·h first:

$$\dot{m}_f = 8.5 \;\;\text{kg/hr}, \quad BP = 29.85 \;\;\text{kW}$$

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{8.5}{29.85} = \mathbf{0.2847 \;\;kg/kW\!\cdot\!h}$$

### Step 6 — (iv) Brake Thermal Efficiency

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{BP \times 3600}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}$$

Note: $BP$ must be in kW (= kJ/s) and $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/s.

$\dot{m}_f = 8.5 / 3600 = 2.361 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/s

$$BP \text{ in kW} = 29.85 \;\;\text{kJ/s}$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{29.85 \times 3600}{(2.361 \times 10^{-3}) \times 44{,}000 \times 1000}$$

Numerator: $29.85 \times 3600 = 107{,}460$

Denominator: $2.361 \times 10^{-3} \times 44{,}000{,}000 = 103{,}884$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{107{,}460}{103{,}884} = 1.034 \;\;\text{(fraction)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Numerical Verification Pitfall:** $\eta_{bth}$ cannot exceed 1.000 in real engines. Our value of 1.034 indicates we have made a unit error. The correct procedure: keep BP in **Watts** when CV is in **J/kg**, OR convert both to compatible kW/kJ. Re-evaluating with $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg and BP in kW·h equivalent: heat input/hr = $8.5 \times 44{,}000 = 374{,}000$ kJ/hr. BP in kJ/hr = $29.85 \times 3600 = 107{,}460$ kJ/hr. $\eta_{bth} = 107{,}460 / 374{,}000 = \mathbf{28.7\%}$ ✓ (realistic).

### Step 7 — (v) Volumetric Efficiency

Air density at 30 °C and 1 bar:

$$\rho_a = \frac{P}{R T} = \frac{10^5}{287 \times 303} = 1.150 \;\;\text{kg/m}^3$$

For 4-stroke: actual air mass inducted per second = $\dot{m}_a = 120 / 3600 = 0.0333$ kg/s.

Theoretical air mass flow (swept volume flow rate × density):

$$\dot{V}_{\text{swept}} = V_s^{\text{total}} \times \frac{N}{2 \times 60} = 2.0106 \times 10^{-3} \times 25 = 5.027 \times 10^{-2} \;\;\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\eta_v = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\rho_a \times \dot{V}_{\text{swept}}} = \frac{0.0333}{1.150 \times 5.027 \times 10^{-2}} = \mathbf{0.576 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 57.6\%}$$

### Step 8 — (vi) Air–Fuel Ratio

$$A/F = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{\dot{m}_f} = \frac{120}{8.5} = \mathbf{14.12}$$

(Close to stoichiometric for gasoline ≈ 14.7, confirming near-stoichiometric operation.)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity-Check Matrix for Every Engine Parameter Problem:**
> - $\eta_{bth}$ should be 20–35 % for SI engines, 30–42 % for CI engines.
> - $\eta_v$ should be 60–90 % for naturally aspirated engines, 100–150 % for turbocharged.
> - BMEP for petrol engines: 8–12 bar typical; for diesels: 10–20 bar.
> - BSFC for petrol: 0.30–0.40 kg/kW·h; for diesel: 0.22–0.30 kg/kW·h.

## 3.4 Analytical Heat Balance Sheet — Format for KTU Problems

Heat input per hour: $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$ (kJ/hr)
Heat converted to BP: $Q_{bp} = BP \times 3600$ (kJ/hr)
Heat to cooling water: $Q_w = \dot{m}_w c_{pw} (T_{wo} - T_{wi}) \times 3600$ (kJ/hr)
Heat to exhaust: $Q_e = \dot{m}_e c_{pe} (T_e - T_a) \times 3600$ (kJ/hr)
Unaccounted: $Q_u = Q_{in} - (Q_{bp} + Q_w + Q_e)$

Express each as a **percentage of $Q_{in}$** for the standard heat balance table.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define Indicated Mean Effective Pressure (IMEP). Why is it considered a more fundamental performance indicator than Indicated Power alone?**

**Model Answer:**

IMEP is defined as the constant (hypothetical) pressure that, if it acted on the piston throughout the entire power stroke, would produce the same net indicated work as the actual variable in-cylinder pressure during the cycle. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$p_{mi} = \frac{W_{\text{indicated, per cycle}}}{V_s}$$

IMEP is considered more fundamental than Indicated Power alone because IP depends on engine size (displacement), number of cylinders, and speed, whereas IMEP normalizes the in-cylinder work per unit swept volume. This makes IMEP a **size-independent** performance metric, enabling direct comparison of engines of different displacements and cylinder counts. Engines with higher IMEP for the same speed are thermodynamically superior in extracting work from combustion.

---

### Question A.2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between BSFC and ISFC. Which is larger in value, and why?**

**Model Answer:**

- **BSFC (Brake Specific Fuel Consumption)** is the fuel mass flow rate per unit **brake** power output: $BSFC = \dot{m}_f / BP$.
- **ISFC (Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption)** is the fuel mass flow rate per unit **indicated** power: $ISFC = \dot{m}_f / IP$.

Since the same $\dot{m}_f$ is divided by BP and IP respectively, and IP > BP always (because IP includes friction losses that BP excludes), it follows that **ISFC < BSFC** numerically. BSFC is always larger than ISFC by an amount that reflects the fuel energy "wasted" in overcoming friction. For a healthy engine, BSFC / ISFC ≈ 1 / $\eta_m$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question B (Choice A) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the Brake Power of an IC engine from first principles, stating clearly the units involved and the assumptions made. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A four-cylinder, four-stroke petrol engine has a bore of 75 mm and stroke of 90 mm. At a test speed of 2800 RPM, it produces a brake torque of 80 N·m. The fuel consumption is 7.2 kg/hr, CV of fuel is 44,000 kJ/kg, and the ambient conditions are 1 bar and 27 °C. Compute: **(i)** BP in kW, **(ii)** BMEP in bar, **(iii)** BSFC, and **(iv)** $\eta_{bth}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

The derivation is reproduced from §3.1 of these notes. Key steps to present in the answer:

1. State $P = F \cdot v$ for linear motion. **(1 mark)**
2. For rotation, $v = \omega r$ and $F = T/r$. **(1 mark)**
3. Multiply to get $P = T \cdot \omega$. **(1 mark)**
4. Substitute $\omega = 2\pi N/60$ to obtain $BP = 2\pi N T / 60$. **(2 marks)**
5. State units clearly (T in N·m, N in RPM → P in Watts; divide by 1000 for kW). Mention that this assumes shaft is rigid and torque measurement includes all mechanical losses up to the dynamometer coupling. **(2 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $k = 4$, 4-stroke, $D = 0.075$ m, $L = 0.090$ m, $N = 2800$ RPM, $T = 80$ N·m, $\dot{m}_f = 7.2$ kg/hr, $CV = 44{,}000$ kJ/kg.

**(i) Brake Power:**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 2800 \times 80}{60} = 23{,}452 \;\;\text{W} = \mathbf{23.45 \;\;kW}$$

**(ii) BMEP:**

$A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3}$ m²

For 4-stroke:

$$BMEP = \frac{BP \times 120}{L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k} = \frac{23{,}452 \times 120}{0.090 \times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \times 2800 \times 4}$$

Numerator: $2{,}814{,}240$
Denominator: $0.4456$

$$BMEP = 6.316 \times 10^{5} \;\;\text{Pa} = \mathbf{6.32 \;\;bar}$$

**(iii) BSFC:**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BP} = \frac{7.2}{23.45} = \mathbf{0.307 \;\;kg/kW\!\cdot\!h}$$

**(iv) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

Heat input rate (kJ/hr) = $7.2 \times 44{,}000 = 316{,}800$ kJ/hr
Brake work rate (kJ/hr) = $23.45 \times 3600 = 84{,}420$ kJ/hr

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{84{,}420}{316{,}800} = \mathbf{0.2665 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 26.65\%}$$

(Realistic for an SI engine — sanity check passed ✓)

---

### Question B (Choice B) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Define volumetric efficiency. List the major factors that affect the volumetric efficiency of a 4-stroke SI engine. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** During a test on a single-cylinder, 4-stroke diesel engine, the following observations were recorded: bore = 12 cm, stroke = 15 cm, fuel consumption = 2.4 kg/hr, speed = 1500 RPM, brake torque = 60 N·m, mechanical efficiency = 80 %, and CV of fuel = 42,000 kJ/kg. Calculate: **(i)** BP, **(ii)** IP, **(iii)** BSFC, **(iv)** $\eta_{bth}$, **(v)** $\eta_{ith}$. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Theory (7 Marks):**

**Definition (3 marks):** Volumetric efficiency is the ratio of the actual mass of air inducted into the cylinder per cycle to the theoretical mass of air that would occupy the swept volume at ambient conditions.

$$\eta_v = \frac{m_{a,\text{actual}}}{\rho_a \cdot V_s}$$

**Factors affecting $\eta_v$ (4 marks — list any four with brief explanation):**

1. **Engine speed** — At very high RPM, valve duration is too short to fully fill the cylinder; at low RPM, $\eta_v$ is higher (in naturally aspirated engines).
2. **Intake manifold design and length** — Ram-effect tuning can boost $\eta_v$ at specific speeds.
3. **Valve timing (opening and closing angles)** — Early/late intake valve closing can exploit inertia to trap more charge.
4. **Intake air temperature** — Cooler air is denser → higher $\eta_v$ (intercoolers help turbocharged engines).
5. **Throttle position** — At part-throttle, pumping losses reduce $\eta_v$ significantly.
6. **Back pressure in the exhaust** — High back pressure reduces scavenging.
7. **Friction in the air-cleaner and manifold** — Reduces pressure differential.

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):**

Given: $k = 1$, 4-stroke, $D = 0.12$ m, $L = 0.15$ m, $\dot{m}_f = 2.4$ kg/hr, $N = 1500$ RPM, $T = 60$ N·m, $\eta_m = 0.80$, $CV = 42{,}000$ kJ/kg.

**(i) BP:**

$$BP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1500 \times 60}{60} = 3000 \pi = \mathbf{9424.78 \;\;W = 9.425 \;\;kW}$$

**(ii) IP (using mechanical efficiency):**

$$IP = \frac{BP}{\eta_m} = \frac{9.425}{0.80} = \mathbf{11.78 \;\;kW}$$

**(iii) BSFC:**

$$BSFC = \frac{2.4}{9.425} = \mathbf{0.2547 \;\;kg/kW\!\cdot\!h}$$

(Typical for diesel — sanity check ✓)

**(iv) Brake Thermal Efficiency:**

Heat input/hr = $2.4 \times 42{,}000 = 100{,}800$ kJ/hr
Brake energy/hr = $9.425 \times 3600 = 33{,}930$ kJ/hr

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{33{,}930}{100{,}800} = \mathbf{0.3366 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 33.66\%}$$

**(v) Indicated Thermal Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{ith} = \frac{\eta_{bth}}{\eta_m} = \frac{0.3366}{0.80} = \mathbf{0.4207 \;\; \text{or} \;\; 42.07\%}$$

Or directly: $IP \times 3600 / (\dot{m}_f \times CV) = (11.78 \times 3600) / 100{,}800 = 42{,}408 / 100{,}800 = 42.07\%$ ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Losing Mistakes in Basic Engine Parameter Problems:**
>
> 1. **Wrong power-stroke count:** Mixing 2-stroke (N strokes/min) with 4-stroke (N/2 strokes/min) formulas is the #1 error. Read the question stem twice.
> 2. **Unit mismatch:** Using CV in kJ/kg with $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/s but BP in Watts (not kW). Always ensure units are coherent (kW ↔ kJ/s ↔ kJ/kg).
> 3. **Forgetting $\times 3600$:** SFC demands per-hour basis, so $\dot{m}_f$ in kg/hr goes directly; but $\eta$ demands energy units, so multiply BP (kW = kJ/s) by 3600 to get kJ/hr.
> 4. **Confusing BSFC with air consumption:** A/F ratio is dimensionless; BSFC has units kg/kW·h.
> 5. **Failing to convert mm/cm to m:** Bore D and stroke L **must** be in metres before substituting into equations. A common silent error that costs 1 full mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Basic Engine Parameters**

- **BP** = $2\pi NT/60$ — derived from rotational mechanics.
- **IP** = $p_{mi} L A N k / (2 \times 60)$ for 4-stroke; $p_{mi} L A N k / 60$ for 2-stroke.
- **FP** = IP − BP; **$\eta_m$** = BP/IP; always 0 < $\eta_m$ < 1.
- **BMEP** is the BMEP formula inverted: it is **not** equal to IMEP × $\eta_m$ exactly (it is only true when the power-stroke cycles are equal — true here).
- **$\eta_v$** = actual inducted air mass / (swept volume flow × ambient air density). For 4-stroke: swept volume flow = $V_s^{\text{tot}} \times N / (2 \times 60)$.
- **$\eta_{bth}$** < **$\eta_{ith}$** always; both < 100 %.
- **BSFC** > **ISFC** always; BSFC = ISFC / $\eta_m$.
- **Air-Fuel Ratio:** stoichiometric for gasoline ≈ 14.7, for diesel ≈ 14.5; lean mixtures have higher A/F.
- **Heat Balance:** $Q_{bp} + Q_w + Q_e + Q_u = Q_{in}$. Express each as % of $Q_{in}$.
- **Sanity-check values:** $\eta_{bth}$: 20–35 % SI, 30–42 % CI; $\eta_v$: 60–90 % NA, 100–150 % TC; BMEP: 8–20 bar; BSFC: 0.22–0.40 kg/kW·h.
- **Always** convert mm/cm to m before plugging D and L into any formula.
- **Always** keep $N$ in RPM throughout; do not convert to rad/s mid-calculation.
- The IP > BP > 0 strict inequality is a **universal rule** for all real IC engines.
- For heat balance closure, $Q_u$ must be **positive**; if negative, your measurements have errors.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of BHP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Measurement of BHP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Brake Horse Power (BHP)

**Brake Horse Power (BHP)** is defined as the actual usable power output delivered by an engine at the output shaft (crankshaft or flywheel) and measured by applying a controlled load (braking action) on the engine using a device called a **dynamometer**.

$$BHP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60}$$

Where,
- **BHP** = Brake Horse Power (in Watts or kW)
- **N** = Rotational speed of the crankshaft in **RPM**
- **T** = Net braking torque applied by the dynamometer in **N·m**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The term **"Brake"** in BHP does NOT refer to the vehicle's service brakes. Instead, it refers to the mechanical **braking (loading) action** applied by the dynamometer to absorb the engine's output power. The power is "braked" — i.e., absorbed and dissipated as heat — so that it can be measured.

## 1.2 Classification of Engine Power

In KTU 2024 syllabus terms, the total power developed inside the engine cylinder is called **Indicated Horse Power (IHP)**. After accounting for mechanical losses (friction in piston, bearings, water pump, oil pump, etc.), the remaining power available at the crankshaft is **Brake Horse Power (BHP)**. The lost power is called **Friction Horse Power (FHP)**.

$$IHP = BHP + FHP$$

**Mechanical Efficiency:**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100\%$$

| Parameter | Meaning | Where Measured |
|---|---|---|
| **IHP** | Power developed by gas pressure on piston | Inside cylinder (using indicator) |
| **BHP** | Power available at crankshaft | At output shaft (using dynamometer) |
| **FHP** | Power lost to friction | By difference (IHP − BHP) |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Pedal Generator

Imagine a bicycle rider pedaling hard against a **stationary roller (like a home trainer)**. The roller resists the pedaling action. The harder the rider pedals, the more heat the roller generates.

- The **rider's legs** = the engine.
- The **stationary roller (trainer)** = the dynamometer (brake).
- The **heat produced in the roller** = absorbed power (BHP).
- The **resistance felt by the rider** = the braking torque T.

Just as the roller measures how much power the rider is generating by the resistance it offers, a dynamometer measures how much power an engine produces by the load (torque) it must absorb at a given RPM. This is the entire philosophy behind **BHP measurement**.

## 1.4 Why BHP is the Most Important Performance Metric

In KTU Vehicle Performance studies, BHP is the *starting point* for almost every calculation:

> [!NOTE]
> - **Tractive Effort** of a vehicle is computed from BHP (along with gear ratio and wheel radius).
> - **Acceleration performance** uses BHP vs. speed curves.
> - **Gradeability**, **climbing ability**, and **top speed** all depend on the BHP–RPM characteristic curve of the engine.
> - **Specific fuel consumption (BSFC)** is reported against BHP, not IHP.

Therefore, accurate measurement of BHP is the foundation of all vehicle performance evaluation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Dynamometer — The Core Device for BHP Measurement

A **dynamometer** (or "dyno") is a precision instrument that applies a controlled, measurable load to a rotating shaft and simultaneously measures the resulting torque and rotational speed. From these two values, BHP is computed.

**Dynamometers are classified into two broad families:**

1. **Absorption (Power-absorbing) Dynamometers** — absorb the engine's power and dissipate it as heat (friction, water, or electrical eddy currents).
2. **Transmission (Driven) Dynamometers** — the engine drives a generator or another motor; the electrical output is measured.

For BHP measurement of engines in KTU laboratory and theory, **Absorption dynamometers** are the standard.

## 2.2 Types of Absorption Dynamometers

### A. Prony Brake Dynamometer
- **Principle:** Mechanical friction brake using wooden blocks pressed against a rotating drum mounted on the engine shaft.
- **Construction:** A lever arm rests on friction blocks; spring balances read the force; cooling water prevents overheating.
- **Use:** Small to medium engines; educational labs.
- **Limitation:** High wear; limited to low/medium speed; not suitable for high-power engines.

### B. Rope Brake Dynamometer
- **Principle:** A rope (hemp or cotton) wound around the flywheel, with weights and spring balance at the two ends to measure tension difference.
- **Construction:** Rope passes over the flywheel rim; one end attached to a fixed support (spring balance), other end to a hanging weight.
- **Use:** Very simple; used for field tests and small engines.
- **Limitation:** Cannot handle high speeds; cooling is a concern.

### C. Hydraulic (Froude / Water-Brake) Dynamometer
- **Principle:** Engine drives a rotor inside a stator filled with water; water resistance provides braking torque.
- **Construction:** Concentric rotor and stator chambers; water inlet and outlet control loading.
- **Use:** Medium to large engines; automotive testing stations.
- **Advantage:** Smooth, stepless control; handles high power; water absorbs and carries away heat.

### D. Eddy Current Dynamometer
- **Principle:** A metallic disc rotates through a magnetic field; induced eddy currents oppose motion, creating braking torque.
- **Construction:** Stationary field coils with DC excitation; rotating aluminium/copper disc.
- **Use:** Modern engine dynamometers; high precision.
- **Advantage:** No physical contact; smooth; wide speed range; easily controlled electrically.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table. Every KTU exam question on BHP measurement uses one of these equations.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power (general) | $P = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ | **W (Watts)** | N in RPM, T in N·m |
| 2 | Power in kW | $P_{kW} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60000}$ | **kW** | $1\,\text{kW} = 1000\,\text{W}$ |
| 3 | Power in Metric HP | $P_{HP} = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60000 \times 0.7355}$ | **HP** | $1\,\text{HP} = 0.7355\,\text{kW}$ |
| 4 | Prony Brake Torque | $T = (W - S) \cdot L \cdot g$ | **N·m** | W = weight, S = spring balance, L = arm length |
| 5 | Rope Brake Torque | $T = (W - S) \cdot R \cdot g$ | **N·m** | R = effective flywheel radius |
| 6 | Mechanical Efficiency | $\eta_m = \dfrac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100$ | **%** | Always < 100% |
| 7 | Friction Power | $FHP = IHP - BHP$ | **kW or HP** | Difference method |
| 8 | Specific Fuel Consumption | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BHP}$ | **kg/kWh** | $m_f$ = fuel mass per hour |
| 9 | Prony Brake Power | $BHP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W-S) L}{60}$ | **W** | Direct formula |
| 10 | Rope Brake Power | $BHP = \dfrac{2 \pi N (W-S) R}{60}$ | **W** | Direct formula |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant to remember:**
> $1\,\text{metric HP} = 735.5\,\text{W} = 0.7355\,\text{kW}$
> $1\,\text{mechanical HP} = 745.7\,\text{W} = 0.7457\,\text{kW}$
> KTU problems use **metric HP** unless stated otherwise.

## 2.4 Why These Equations Matter in Real Engineering

- **Engine manufacturers** plot BHP vs. RPM curves on engine test beds using these exact relations.
- **Automotive R&D** uses hydraulic and eddy current dynamometers because they can run endurance tests for thousands of hours while precisely controlling load and speed.
- **Emission testing** requires simultaneous measurement of BHP and exhaust gases — the dynamometer provides the loading and the load cell the torque.
- **Field tuning workshops** use rope brake dynamometers because they are inexpensive and portable.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Fundamental Power–Torque–Speed Relation

Let an engine rotate at **N revolutions per minute**.

**Step 1:** In one revolution, the shaft sweeps an angle of $2\pi$ radians.

**Step 2:** Work done per revolution = Force × distance, but in rotational terms:
$$W_{rev} = T \times 2\pi \quad \text{(Joules)}$$

Where T is the torque in N·m applied against the load.

**Step 3:** Number of revolutions per second = $\dfrac{N}{60}$.

**Step 4:** Work done per second = Power.

$$P = W_{rev} \times \text{revs/sec} = T \cdot 2\pi \cdot \frac{N}{60}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60} \;\text{Watts}}$$

**Step 5:** Conversion to kilowatts:

Since $1\,\text{kW} = 1000\,\text{W}$,

$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60000} \;\text{kW}$$

**Step 6:** Conversion to metric HP:

Since $1\,\text{HP} = 735.5\,\text{W}$,

$$P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 735.5} \;\text{HP}$$

> Every BHP measurement problem in KTU begins with this single relation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Prony Brake Dynamometer Torque Equation

**Setup:** A lever arm of effective length **L** (metres) rests on friction blocks pressed against the engine's brake drum. A dead weight **W** (kgf or N) acts downward at the arm end, while a spring balance reads force **S** at a calibrated distance.

**Step 1:** The friction blocks exert a horizontal friction force **F** on the drum.

**Step 2:** By the principle of moments about the drum centre, the net tangential force × radius = torque, AND the equivalent moment about the pivot equals the difference in weights times the arm length.

**Step 3:** Taking moments of forces about the pivot of the lever:

The downward dead weight W produces a clockwise moment; the spring balance pull S produces a counter-clockwise moment. The friction reaction creates a moment equal to the net effective load.

**Step 4:** Equating moments:

$$F \times r = (W - S) \times L$$

Where r is the effective drum radius. Substituting $F \times r = T$:

$$\boxed{T = (W - S) \times L \quad \text{(kgf·m)}}$$

**Step 5:** Converting to SI units (multiply by g = 9.81 m/s²):

$$T_{N \cdot m} = (W - S) \times L \times g$$

**Step 6:** Substituting into the power equation:

$$BHP = \frac{2\pi N (W-S) L g}{60} \;\text{Watts} = \frac{2\pi N (W-S) L g}{60000} \;\text{kW}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In many KTU problems, **W and S are in kgf** and the formula uses $g$ explicitly. If W and S are already in Newtons, drop the g.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Rope Brake Dynamometer Torque Equation

**Setup:** A rope is wound around the engine flywheel of effective radius **R** (metres). One end of the rope is attached to a spring balance (reading **S**), the other end carries a dead weight **W**.

**Step 1:** When the flywheel rotates, friction between rope and flywheel creates a tangential force.

**Step 2:** The difference in tensions is $(W - S)$. The friction force at the rim is proportional to this tension difference.

**Step 3:** The braking torque is:

$$T = (W - S) \times R \quad \text{(kgf·m)}$$

**Step 4:** In SI units:

$$T = (W - S) \times R \times g \;\text{N·m}$$

**Step 5:** Brake power:

$$\boxed{BHP = \frac{2\pi N (W-S) R g}{60} \;\text{W}}$$

> Water is dripped on the rope during testing to carry away the heat generated by friction. The cooling water absorbs the entire BHP as sensible heat (and some evaporation).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical

**Problem (KTU Model):**
> A Prony brake test on a single-cylinder engine gives the following readings:
> - Spring balance reading with engine running = **14 kgf**
> - Spring balance reading with engine shut off = **2 kgf** (no-load zero)
> - Effective length of brake arm = **0.5 m**
> - Engine speed = **1000 RPM**
>
> Calculate: (a) Brake Torque, (b) BHP in kW.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**Given Data:**
- No-load spring balance: $S_0 = 2\,\text{kgf}$
- Loaded spring balance: $S = 14\,\text{kgf}$
- Effective arm length: $L = 0.5\,\text{m}$
- Speed: $N = 1000\,\text{RPM}$
- $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Net effective load on the arm**

$$W_{net} = S - S_0 = 14 - 2 = 12\,\text{kgf}$$

**Step 2: Brake Torque in kgf·m**

$$T = W_{net} \times L = 12 \times 0.5 = 6\,\text{kgf·m}$$

**Step 3: Convert to N·m**

$$T = 6 \times 9.81 = 58.86\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4: Apply the BHP formula**

$$BHP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$

$$BHP = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1000 \times 58.86}{60}$$

$$BHP = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 1000 \times 58.86}{60}$$

$$BHP = \frac{369821.6}{60}$$

$$BHP = 6163.7\,\text{W}$$

**Step 5: Convert to kW**

$$BHP = \frac{6163.7}{1000} = 6.164\,\text{kW}$$

**Final Answers:**
- **(a)** Brake Torque $T = 58.86\,\text{N·m}$
- **(b)** BHP $= 6.164\,\text{kW}$

**Verification using alternate path (using kgf·m directly):**

$$BHP = \frac{2\pi N T_{kgf \cdot m}}{60 \times 75} = \frac{2\pi \times 1000 \times 6}{60 \times 75} = 8.378\,\text{HP}$$

Converting: $8.378 \times 0.7355 = 6.162\,\text{kW}$ ✓ (matches within rounding).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Dynamometers

| Feature | Prony Brake | Rope Brake | Hydraulic | Eddy Current |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Friction (block-on-drum) | Friction (rope-on-rim) | Water shear | Eddy currents |
| **Max Speed** | Low–Medium | Low | Medium–High | High |
| **Max Power** | Small engines | Very small | Large | Very large |
| **Loading** | Step (limited) | Step | Continuous | Smooth, electrical |
| **Cooling** | Water drip | Water drip | Water (internal) | Self (heat in disc) |
| **Torque Measurement** | Lever + spring balance | Spring balance | Lever + scale | Load cell (electronic) |
| **Cost** | Very low | Very low | High | Very high |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Low | High | Very high |
| **Use in KTU Lab** | Yes (common) | Yes (basic) | Industrial | Industrial |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Architecture — BHP Measurement System

The following block diagram shows the complete signal/energy flow in a typical BHP measurement setup using an absorption dynamometer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ENG[ENGINE UNDER TEST] --> SH[CRANKSHAFT OUTPUT]
    SH --> COUP[DYNAMOMETER COUPLING]
    COUP --> DYNO[ABSORPTION DYNAMOMETER]
    DYNO --> LOAD[CONTROLLED LOAD APPLIED]
    LOAD --> TORQ[TORQUE TRANSDUCER]
    LOAD --> SPD[SPEED SENSOR TACHOMETER]
    TORQ --> AMP1[AMPLIFIER SIGNAL CONDITIONING]
    SPD --> AMP2[FREQUENCY TO VOLTAGE CONVERTER]
    AMP1 --> DSP[DIGITAL DATA ACQUISITION UNIT]
    AMP2 --> DSP
    DSP --> CALC[POWER CALCULATION MODULE]
    CALC --> FORM[P EQUAL 2 PI N T BY 60]
    FORM --> DISP[DISPLAY BHP IN KW OR HP]
    DISP --> PLOT[BHP VERSUS RPM CURVE PLOT]
    DISP --> LOG[DATA LOGGING FOR ANALYSIS]
```

---

## 4.2 Classification Tree — Dynamometers Used for BHP Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DYN[DYNAMOMETERS FOR BHP MEASUREMENT]
    DYN --> ABS[ABSORPTION TYPE]
    DYN --> TRAN[TRANSMISSION TYPE]
    ABS --> P1[PRONY BRAKE]
    ABS --> P2[ROPE BRAKE]
    ABS --> P3[HYDRAULIC FRoude]
    ABS --> P4[EDDY CURRENT]
    ABS --> P5[AIR FAN TYPE]
    TRAN --> T1[GENERATOR DYNAMO]
    TRAN --> T2[MOTOR GENERATOR SET]
    P1 --> U1[USED FOR SMALL ENGINES]
    P2 --> U2[USED FOR FIELD TESTS]
    P3 --> U3[USED FOR MEDIUM TO LARGE ENGINES]
    P4 --> U4[USED FOR MODERN AUTOMOTIVE TESTING]
```

---

## 4.3 Functional Flow — Prony Brake Dynamometer (Sectional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ROTATING_ASSEMBLY
        SHF[ENGINE SHAFT] --> DRUM[BRAKE DRUM]
    end
    DRUM --> FB[FRICTION BLOCKS WOODEN]
    FB --> ARM[LEVER ARM L]
    ARM --> W[DEAD WEIGHT W]
    ARM --> SB[SPRING BALANCE S]
    SB --> SCALE[CALIBRATED SCALE NEWTONS]
    FB --> COOL[COOLING WATER SPRAY]
    COOL --> DRAIN[WATER DRAIN AND HEAT OUT]
    SHF --> RPM[TACHOMETER READS N]
    W --> MOM[NET MOMENT EQUALS W MINUS S TIMES L]
    SB --> MOM
    MOM --> TORQ_OUT[BRAKE TORQUE T]
    RPM --> CALC[POWER CALCULATION]
    TORQ_OUT --> CALC
    CALC --> BHP_OUT[FINAL BHP VALUE]
```

---

## 4.4 Power Flow Through the Engine (IHP → BHP → FHP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    FUEL[FUEL CHEMICAL ENERGY] --> COMB[COMBUSTION IN CYLINDER]
    COMB --> IHP1[INDICATED HORSE POWER IHP]
    IHP1 --> LOSS[FRICTION LOSSES]
    LOSS --> FHP1[FRICTION HORSE POWER FHP]
    IHP1 --> SUB[IHP MINUS FHP]
    LOSS --> SUB
    SUB --> BHP1[BRAKE HORSE POWER BHP]
    BHP1 --> TRANS[TRANSMISSION LOSSES]
    TRANS --> WHP[WHEEL HORSE POWER WHP]
    BHP1 --> DYNO_M[BHP MEASURED AT DYNAMOMETER]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 frequently tests whether students know the distinction between **BHP, IHP, FHP, and WHP**. The block above should be committed to memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define Brake Horse Power (BHP). Mention its SI unit and write the formula used to compute it from torque and speed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Brake Horse Power is the actual power output available at the engine crankshaft (output shaft), measured by absorbing the engine's output using a dynamometer which applies a controlled braking torque. The power is "braked" — i.e., absorbed and dissipated as heat — allowing it to be quantified.

**Unit (0.5 mark):**
SI unit: **Watts (W)**; commonly expressed in **kW** or **metric HP**.

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$BHP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} \;\text{Watts}$$

where N is the crankshaft speed in RPM and T is the net braking torque in N·m.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Prony brake and Rope brake dynamometers (any three points).**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per point):**

| Aspect | Prony Brake | Rope Brake |
|---|---|---|
| **Friction Element** | Wooden/metallic friction blocks pressed against a drum | Hemp/cotton rope wound around the flywheel |
| **Construction** | Lever arm with calibrated length L; uses spring balance + dead weight | Simple rope with weight at one end, spring balance at the other |
| **Cooling** | Water dripped externally on drum | Water dripped on rope |
| **Power Range** | Small to medium engines | Very small engines only |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | Low |
| **Speed Range** | Limited to medium speeds | Limited to low speeds |

---

## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]

**A. (a)** Explain with a neat sketch the working of a **Prony brake dynamometer**. Derive the expression for brake power in kW. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction and Working (4 marks):**
The Prony brake consists of a brake drum rigidly mounted on the engine crankshaft. Two wooden or fibrous friction blocks are placed on either side of the drum, held in a yoke (lever arm) of effective length L. The yoke is pivoted at one end and rests on a knife-edge fulcrum. A dead weight W is suspended at the arm end on one side, and a spring balance S is attached on the other side. Cooling water is dripped continuously onto the drum to remove the heat generated by friction.

When the engine runs, friction between the blocks and the rotating drum exerts a tangential force on the drum. This creates a reaction force on the lever arm. The dead weight W tries to rotate the arm downward, while the spring balance S resists it. At equilibrium, the difference in readings is proportional to the friction torque.

**Free-Body Diagram Concept (1 mark):**

```
        [Dead Weight W ↓]
              |
              |
   ___________|____________
   ↑S    ←  ARM length L  → ↓W
   |
 [Spring Balance]
         |
     [Pivot]
   _________________________
        (BRAKE DRUM)
```

**Derivation of Brake Power (2 marks):**

Taking moments about the pivot:

$$T = (W - S) \times L \times g \;\text{N·m}$$

(where W and S are in kgf and g = 9.81 m/s²)

Brake power:

$$BHP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2 \pi N (W-S) L g}{60000} \;\text{kW}$$

---

**A. (b)** A Prony brake test on a 4-stroke, 4-cylinder petrol engine gave the following data:
- Dead weight on arm: $W = 30\,\text{kgf}$
- Spring balance reading: $S = 6\,\text{kgf}$
- Effective arm length: $L = 0.75\,\text{m}$
- Engine speed: $N = 1500\,\text{RPM}$
- Fuel consumption: $7.5\,\text{kg/hr}$
- Calorific value of fuel: $43{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg}$

**Calculate:**
1. Brake Torque in N·m
2. BHP in kW and metric HP
3. Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (BSFC)
4. Brake Thermal Efficiency **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $W = 30\,\text{kgf}$, $S = 6\,\text{kgf}$, $L = 0.75\,\text{m}$, $N = 1500\,\text{RPM}$
- $\dot{m}_f = 7.5\,\text{kg/hr}$, $CV = 43{,}000\,\text{kJ/kg}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$

**Step 1: Net effective load (0.5 mark)**

$$W_{net} = W - S = 30 - 6 = 24\,\text{kgf}$$

**Step 2: Brake Torque (1 mark)**

$$T = 24 \times 0.75 = 18\,\text{kgf·m}$$

$$T = 18 \times 9.81 = 176.58\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step 3: BHP in Watts and kW (1 mark)**

$$BHP = \frac{2 \pi N T}{60} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 1500 \times 176.58}{60}$$

$$BHP = \frac{1{,}664{,}126}{60} = 27{,}735.4\,\text{W} = 27.735\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 4: BHP in metric HP (1 mark)**

$$BHP = \frac{27.735}{0.7355} = 37.71\,\text{HP}$$

**Step 5: BSFC (1 mark)**

$$BSFC = \frac{\dot{m}_f}{BHP_{kW}} = \frac{7.5}{27.735} = 0.2704\,\text{kg/kWh}$$

**Step 6: Brake Thermal Efficiency (1.5 marks)**

Heat input per hour:

$$Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 7.5 \times 43{,}000 = 322{,}500\,\text{kJ/hr}$$

Convert BHP to kJ/hr:

$$W_{out} = BHP_{kW} \times 3600 = 27.735 \times 3600 = 99{,}846\,\text{kJ/hr}$$

$$\eta_{bth} = \frac{W_{out}}{Q_{in}} \times 100 = \frac{99{,}846}{322{,}500} \times 100 = 30.96\%$$

**Final Answers:**
- $T = 176.58\,\text{N·m}$
- $BHP = 27.735\,\text{kW} = 37.71\,\text{HP}$
- $BSFC = 0.2704\,\text{kg/kWh}$
- $\eta_{bth} = 30.96\%$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**B. (a)** Explain with a sketch the working of a **Hydraulic (Froude) dynamometer**. State any four advantages over a Prony brake. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction and Working (4 marks):**
A Froude hydraulic dynamometer consists of two main parts: a **rotor** keyed to the engine shaft and a **stator (casing)** held in bearings but free to rotate slightly. The rotor and stator have closely-fitting concentric chambers (or pockets) filled with water. As the rotor spins, water is dragged with it; the impact and shear against the stator create a braking torque.

The stator is restrained from rotating by a lever arm resting on a weighing platform (or load cell). The reaction torque is measured by the force on the platform multiplied by the lever arm length.

The amount of water in the chambers controls the braking torque: more water → more resistance → more torque. A water inlet and outlet regulate this loading continuously.

**Sketch concept (1 mark):**

```
   [ENGINE SHAFT]
        |
      [ROTOR] <--- rotates with engine
      [WATER FILLED GAP]
      [STATOR] <--- tries to rotate; held by lever
        |
     [LEVER ARM]
        |
     [WEIGH PLATFORM / LOAD CELL]
```

**Advantages over Prony Brake (2 marks — any four):**

1. **Smooth, stepless loading** — torque is controlled continuously by water level, not in steps.
2. **High power absorption capacity** — can handle large automotive engines easily.
3. **Better cooling** — water circulates through the dynamometer continuously, removing heat efficiently.
4. **No friction contact** — no wearing parts; longer life and consistent accuracy.
5. **Suitable for high-speed operation** — Prony brakes cannot withstand high RPM.
6. **Stable operation** — no stick-slip behaviour; uniform torque.

---

**B. (b)** In a rope brake dynamometer test on a diesel engine, the following observations were recorded:
- Effective flywheel diameter: $D = 1.2\,\text{m}$
- Weight on one side of rope: $W = 50\,\text{kgf}$
- Spring balance reading on other side: $S = 5\,\text{kgf}$
- Engine speed: $N = 800\,\text{RPM}$
- Diameter of rope: $d = 25\,\text{mm}$

**Calculate:**
1. Effective radius of the rope (Rope diameter correction)
2. Brake Torque in N·m
3. BHP in kW
4. If the engine is rated 12 kW IHP, find the Mechanical Efficiency **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given Data:**
- $D = 1.2\,\text{m}$, $d = 0.025\,\text{m}$, $W = 50\,\text{kgf}$, $S = 5\,\text{kgf}$, $N = 800\,\text{RPM}$, $IHP = 12\,\text{kW}$

**Step 1: Effective radius of rope (1 mark)**

$$R_{eff} = \frac{D + d}{2} = \frac{1.2 + 0.025}{2} = \frac{1.225}{2} = 0.6125\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2: Net load (0.5 mark)**

$$W_{net} = W - S = 50 - 5 = 45\,\text{kgf}$$

**Step 3: Brake Torque (1.5 marks)**

$$T = W_{net} \times R_{eff} = 45 \times 0.6125 = 27.5625\,\text{kgf·m}$$

$$T = 27.5625 \times 9.81 = 270.39\,\text{N·m}$$

**Step 4: BHP in kW (2 marks)**

$$BHP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60 \times 1000} = \frac{2 \times \pi \times 800 \times 270.39}{60000}$$

$$BHP = \frac{1{,}359{,}471}{60{,}000} = 22.658\,\text{kW}$$

**Step 5: Mechanical Efficiency (2 marks)**

$$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100 = \frac{22.658}{12} \times 100$$

$$\eta_{mech} = 188.8\%$$

**Result discussion:** This value is **physically impossible** (efficiency cannot exceed 100%). This indicates that the IHP was likely under-reported or the dynamometer had errors. In a properly conducted test, $\eta_{mech} \approx 80\% \text{ to } 90\%$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to subtract the no-load spring balance reading** in Prony brake — costs 1–2 marks easily.
> 2. **Mixing up units**: using kgf·m without multiplying by g = 9.81 to convert to N·m. Always state units explicitly.
> 3. **Forgetting the rope diameter correction** in rope brake problems — examiners frequently give this to test attention to detail.
> 4. **Dividing by 60 vs. 60000** — wrong divisor means BHP comes out 1000× too large or too small. Remember: Watts = $2\pi NT/60$; kW = divide by 1000 more.
> 5. **HP conversion factor** — Indian KTU uses metric HP = 735.5 W. Using 746 W is the British mechanical HP and is wrong here.
> 6. **Not converting RPM to rev/s** — students forget the 60 in denominator.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Measurement of BHP**

- ✅ **BHP** = Brake Horse Power = usable power at the crankshaft, measured using a **dynamometer**.
- ✅ **Master formula:** $BHP = \dfrac{2 \pi N T}{60}$ (Watts), where N is RPM and T is N·m.
- ✅ **Mechanical Efficiency:** $\eta_m = \dfrac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100\%$
- ✅ **Prony Brake:** uses friction blocks + lever arm. Torque $T = (W - S) \times L \times g$ N·m.
- ✅ **Rope Brake:** uses rope on flywheel. Torque $T = (W - S) \times R_{eff} \times g$ N·m, where $R_{eff} = (D+d)/2$.
- ✅ **Hydraulic (Froude) Dynamometer:** water-filled rotor–stator; stepless, smooth loading.
- ✅ **Eddy Current Dynamometer:** electrical loading via eddy currents; no friction, high precision.
- ✅ Always convert **kgf → N** by multiplying with $g = 9.81$ before using the SI formula.
- ✅ **Metric HP** = 735.5 W = 0.7355 kW (KTU standard).
- ✅ Cooling water in rope/Prony brake **absorbs the entire BHP** as heat.
- ✅ Difference **BHP − WHP** gives transmission loss; **IHP − BHP** gives FHP.
- ✅ Modern automotive test beds predominantly use **eddy current and AC dynamometers**.
- ✅ In exam problems, **state the no-load correction** explicitly if a spring balance is used.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IHP - Engine Testing on Dynamometers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** IHP - Engine Testing on Dynamometers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Indicated Horsepower (IHP) – Formal Definition **Indicated Horsepower (IHP)** is the power actually developed by the working fluid (combustion gases) on the piston face inside the engine cylinder, calculated from the **pressure–volume (P-V) diagram** or **indicator diagram** recorded during one complete working cycle. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, IHP represents the **gross power output** of an engine measured at the cylinder level — *before* any mechanical losses (friction, pumping, accessories) are subtracted. It is the theoretical maximum power the thermodynamic cycle can deliver to the piston. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** IHP is determined from the **indicated mean effective pressure (imep)** obtained from an indicator diagram, **NOT** from a dynamometer. The dynamometer is used to measure **Brake Horsepower (BHP)**, and the difference (IHP − BHP) gives **Friction Horsepower (FHP)**. ## 1.2 Dynamometer – Formal Definition A **dynamometer (dyno)** is a device used to **measure, absorb, and apply load** to the rotating output shaft of an engine or motor, allowing the direct determination of **Brake Horsepower (BHP)** and **Torque**. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition Imagine you are **pumping water from a deep well using a hand pump**: - The energy your arms put into the piston (inside the cylinder) is the **IHP** — it never leaks out. - Some of that energy is lost in friction of the pump mechanism itself (**FHP**). - The water that finally gushes out the spout is the **useful BHP**. > **So:** IHP = Energy developed inside | FHP = Energy lost in friction | BHP = Energy delivered at the output shaft (brake/dynamometer) For a dynamometer, think of it as a **controllable brake for the engine's output shaft**: - An engine cannot simply "spin freely" and tell you its power — you must **resist** its rotation and measure how much it can push. - A dynamometer is that "resistor" with built-in measurement. - It absorbs the engine's output as **heat (or electricity)**, but first *measures* the resisting torque and rotational speed — from which BHP is calculated. > [!NOTE] > **Why measure IHP separately?** Because a dynamometer CANNOT see what happens *inside* the cylinder. To know the true thermodynamic power, we use an **engine indicator** (mechanical, electrical, or optical) that traces the P-V diagram. Combining both methods gives the complete engine performance picture. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | |---|---|---| | Indicated Horsepower | IHP | **kW** (SI) or hp | | Brake Horsepower | BHP | **kW** (SI) or hp | | Friction Horsepower | FHP | **kW** or hp | | Mechanical Efficiency | η_mech | **dimensionless (%)** | | Indicated Mean Effective Pressure | imep (P_m) | **kPa** or **bar** (N/m²) | | Stroke length | L | **metres (m)** | | Cylinder bore | D | **metres (m)** | | Engine speed | N | **rpm** | | Number of cylinders | k | dimensionless | | Torque | T | **N·m** | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Powers of an Engine – Conceptual Flow The thermodynamic energy path inside an IC engine is: ``` Chemical Energy in Fuel ↓ (Combustion) Gas Pressure on Piston = IHP (Indicated Power) ↓ (Piston, crank, bearings, gears friction) Rotational Power at Crankshaft = BHP (Brake Power) ↓ (Dynamometer absorbs it) Heat / Electrical Output (measured as BHP) ``` The **gap** between IHP and BHP is **FHP**: $$FHP = IHP - BHP$$ and the fraction that reaches the shaft is the **Mechanical Efficiency**: $$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100\%$$ ## 2.2 Deriving the IHP Formula – The Logic 1. Each cylinder has a **piston area** A = (π/4)·D², swept by stroke **L** in one revolution. 2. The average pressure pushing the piston down usefully is the **imep** (P_m). 3. **Work per cycle per cylinder** = P_m × A × L (Joules) 4. For a **4-stroke engine**, one working cycle takes **2 revolutions**. - Number of power strokes per second per cylinder = N/(2×60) 5. For a **2-stroke engine**, one working cycle takes **1 revolution**. - Number of power strokes per second per cylinder = N/60 6. Multiplying by the number of cylinders **k** gives total IHP in Watts. 7. Dividing by 1000 converts to kW. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conversion:** 1 hp = 745.7 W ≈ 0.7457 kW. KTU problems may use either SI (kW) or metric hp — always check! | # | Formula | Description / Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$ | Piston cross-sectional area (m²) | | 2 | $W_{cycle} = P_m \cdot A \cdot L$ | Work per cycle per cylinder (J) | | 3 | $IHP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot n}{60}$ | IHP in Watts for 4-stroke (n = N/2) | | 4 | $IHP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{60}$ | IHP (W) for **2-stroke** engine, k cylinders | | 5 | $IHP = \dfrac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{2 \times 60}$ | IHP (W) for **4-stroke** engine, k cylinders | | 6 | $BHP = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Brake Power in Watts from torque (N·m) and rpm | | 7 | $BHP = \dfrac{(W - S)\pi D_r N}{60}$ | Rope-brake dynamometer (W,S in Newtons, D_r in m) | | 8 | $FHP = IHP - BHP$ | Friction Horsepower | | 9 | $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BHP}{IHP}$ | Mechanical efficiency | | 10 | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{mech} \times \eta_{thermal}$ | Overall efficiency | | 11 | $BHP_{drop} = n \cdot [BHP_n - BHP_{n-1}]$ | **Morse Test:** FHP = n × (drop per cylinder) | | 12 | $BSFC = \dfrac{m_f}{BHP}$ | Brake Specific Fuel Consumption (kg/kWh) | | 13 | $ISFC = \dfrac{m_f}{IHP}$ | Indicated Specific Fuel Consumption (kg/kWh) | | 14 | $\eta_{thermal} = \dfrac{IHP \times 3600}{m_f \times CV}$ | Indicated thermal efficiency | > Where: P_m = imep in N/m², L in m, A in m², N = rpm, k = number of cylinders, T = torque in N·m, n = power strokes/sec. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Automotive R&D:** Comparing different engine designs (e.g., 2-stroke vs 4-stroke) by isolating the thermodynamic power. - **Friction reduction validation:** Quantifies how much power is lost to mechanical friction (helps in choosing low-viscosity oils, roller bearings, DLC coatings). - **Emission testing:** Mechanical efficiency affects BSFC, which directly relates to CO₂ emissions per kWh. - **Two-wheeler / scooter industries:** IHP is critical for small 2-stroke engines (e.g., older mopeds) where friction loss is a large fraction. - **Heavy-duty diesel engines (trucks, ships):** IHP defines the work done per cycle — basis for specific output (kW/litre). ## 2.5 Types of Dynamometers (Brief – for context) | Type | Principle | Absorption | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | **Rope Brake** | Friction of rope on drum | Heat (friction) | Simple lab tests (small engines) | | **Hydraulic (water brake)** | Fluid shear on rotor | Heat (water) | Medium engines, tractors | | **Eddy Current** | Eddy currents in copper/iron | Heat + electricity | Automotive, locomotive | | **Electrical (AC/DC)** | Generator loading | Electricity | Large stationary, EV motor testing | | **Fan/Air Brake** | Air drag on fan | Heat (air) | Small petrol engines | | **Prony Brake** | Friction pad on drum | Heat | Educational / vintage | > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Focus:** Rope brake, hydraulic dynamometer, eddy-current dynamometer, and **Morse test** are the most-tested items. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Detailed Derivation of IHP from First Principles ### Step 1 – Work done per cycle Consider one cylinder. The piston moves through stroke **L** against an average effective pressure **P_m** (imep). $$W_{cycle} = P_m \times A \times L \quad \text{[Joules per cycle per cylinder]}$$ > *Logic:* Pressure (N/m²) × Area (m²) = Force (N). Force × Distance (m) = Work (J). ### Step 2 – Number of power strokes per second For a **4-stroke** engine, one power stroke occurs every **2 revolutions**. $$n = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} \quad \text{[power strokes/sec/cylinder]}$$ For a **2-stroke** engine, one power stroke occurs every **1 revolution**: $$n = \frac{N}{60} \quad \text{[power strokes/sec/cylinder]}$$ ### Step 3 – Power per cylinder Power = Work per cycle × cycles per second: $$P_{cyl} = W_{cycle} \times n = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot n \quad \text{[Watts]}$$ ### Step 4 – Multi-cylinder engine Multiply by number of cylinders **k**: $$IHP = k \cdot P_m \cdot A \cdot L \cdot n \quad \text{[Watts]}$$ ### Step 5 – Final compact form (4-stroke) $$\boxed{IHP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{120} \quad \text{Watts (4-stroke)}}$$ ### Step 6 – Final compact form (2-stroke) $$\boxed{IHP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{60} \quad \text{Watts (2-stroke)}}$$ > **Memory trick:** 4-stroke = divide by 120, 2-stroke = divide by 60. The factor is always (60 × strokes-per-cycle). ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation of BHP from a Rope Brake Dynamometer ### Step 1 – Net force on the brake The rope exerts a tight-side load **W** (N) and a slack-side load **S** (N) on the brake drum. The effective tangential force is: $$F_{net} = (W - S) \quad \text{[Newtons]}$$ ### Step 2 – Torque on the drum With effective drum diameter **D_r** (m), the torque is: $$T = F_{net} \times \frac{D_r}{2} = (W - S) \cdot \frac{D_r}{2} \quad \text{[N·m]}$$ ### Step 3 – Power (Watts) $$BHP = \frac{2\pi N T}{60}$$ Substituting T: $$BHP = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \cdot (W - S) \cdot \frac{D_r}{2}$$ Simplifying: $$\boxed{BHP = \frac{\pi N D_r (W - S)}{60} \quad \text{Watts}}$$ To convert to kW, divide by 1000. > If W and S are given in **kgf**, multiply by **g = 9.81** to get Newtons before substituting. ## 3.3 Detailed Derivation – Mechanical Efficiency Mechanical efficiency is the ratio of power delivered at the shaft (useful) to the power developed in the cylinder (gross). $$\eta_{mech} = \frac{\text{Output (shaft) Power}}{\text{Input (cylinder) Power}} = \frac{BHP}{IHP}$$ Since BHP < IHP always, $\eta_{mech}$ < 100%. Typical values: - Small 2-stroke petrol: **70 – 80 %** - Modern 4-stroke petrol: **80 – 90 %** - Heavy diesel engines: **85 – 92 %** ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Style) > **[Worked Problem 1]** > A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore **D = 80 mm**, stroke **L = 100 mm**, runs at **N = 3000 rpm**, and has an **imep = 8 bar**. The rope brake gives **W = 80 N, S = 10 N** with effective drum diameter **D_r = 0.6 m**. Find: (a) IHP, (b) BHP, (c) FHP, (d) Mechanical efficiency. ### Solution: **Step A — Piston area:** $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.080)^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 0.0064 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step B — Convert imep:** $$P_m = 8 \text{ bar} = 8 \times 10^5 \text{ N/m}^2$$ **Step C — IHP (4-stroke formula, k = 4):** $$IHP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{120} = \frac{(8 \times 10^5)(0.100)(5.0265 \times 10^{-3})(3000)(4)}{120}$$ Computing numerator step-wise: - $8 \times 10^5 \times 0.100 = 8 \times 10^4$ - $8 \times 10^4 \times 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} = 402.12$ - $402.12 \times 3000 = 1{,}206{,}365$ - $1{,}206{,}365 \times 4 = 4{,}825{,}462$ $$IHP = \frac{4{,}825{,}462}{120} = 40{,}212 \text{ W} \approx 40.21 \text{ kW}$$ **Step D — BHP (rope brake):** $$BHP = \frac{\pi N D_r (W - S)}{60} = \frac{\pi (3000)(0.6)(80 - 10)}{60}$$ - $3000 \times 0.6 = 1800$ - $1800 \times 70 = 126{,}000$ - $\pi \times 126{,}000 = 395{,}840$ $$BHP = \frac{395{,}840}{60} = 6{,}597 \text{ W} \approx 6.60 \text{ kW}$$ **Step E — FHP:** $$FHP = IHP - BHP = 40.21 - 6.60 = 33.61 \text{ kW}$$ **Step F — Mechanical efficiency:** $$\eta_{mech} = \frac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100\% = \frac{6.60}{40.21} \times 100\% \approx 16.4\%$$ > [!NOTE] > This unusually low mechanical efficiency (16.4%) suggests the rope brake loading is light (W − S = 70 N is small for a 40 kW engine). In a real lab, **W − S** would be increased until the engine speed drops to rated value. ## 3.5 Detailed Derivation – The Morse Test The **Morse Test** determines FHP experimentally by progressively cutting out cylinders (by shorting the spark plug or cutting off fuel) and observing the drop in BHP. ### Step-by-step: 1. Run engine at full load with **all n cylinders firing**. Measure BHP₁. 2. Cut out one cylinder (no combustion in that cylinder). Measure BHP₂. 3. Cut out a second cylinder. Measure BHP₃. Continue until 1 cylinder remains. 4. For each test, the **indicated power of the cut-out cylinder is lost**, while FHP remains constant (assumed). 5. Plot BHP vs. number of cylinders firing. **Extrapolate to zero cylinders firing** — the intercept is the FHP. Mathematically: $$FHP = n \cdot [BHP_n - BHP_{(n-1)}]$$ In practice, the average drop per cylinder is multiplied by n: $$FHP = n \cdot \overline{\Delta BHP}$$ > The IHP of one cylinder is the drop in BHP when only that cylinder is cut out. So: $$IHP_{one\,cyl} = BHP_{all\,firing} - BHP_{(n-1)\,firing}$$ > And the total IHP = n × IHP_per_cylinder (for identical cylinders). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Assumption in Morse Test:** FHP is assumed **constant** irrespective of which cylinder is cut out. This is an approximation because cutting out cylinders changes the load and cooling conditions. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Power-Flow Block Diagram (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Chemical Energy]:::start --> B[Combustion in Cylinder] B --> C[Gas Pressure on Piston] C --> D["IHP - Indicated Power"] D --> E["FHP - Friction Losses (Piston Rings, Bearings, Gears, Oil Pump)"] E --> F["BHP - Brake Power at Crankshaft"] F --> G["Dynamometer - Absorbs and Measures"] G --> H["Heat / Electrical Output - Measured BHP Value"] H --> I["Calculation: eta_mech = BHP / IHP"] D --> I classDef start fill:#FFD580,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef power fill:#B8E0FF,stroke:#0066CC,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef loss fill:#FFB3B3,stroke:#CC0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Rope-Brake Dynamometer Schematic ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Engine ENG[Engine Crankshaft] end subgraph Brake DRUM[Brake Drum
Diameter Dr] ROPE[Rope wrapped around drum
W - tight side
S - slack side] end subgraph Measurement WLOAD[Weight W - kgf] SLOAD[Weight S - kgf - light] SCALE[Spring Balance] end ENG --- DRUM DRUM --- ROPE ROPE --> WLOAD ROPE --> SCALE SCALE --> SLOAD DRUM --> T["Torque T = W-S x Dr/2"] ENG --> N["Speed N - rpm"] T --> CALC[BHP Calculation Block] N --> CALC CALC --> RES["BHP = pi x N x Dr x W-S / 60 W"] ``` ## 4.3 Morse Test – Cylinder Cut-Out Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: All n cylinders firing] --> B[Measure BHP1] B --> C[Cut out Cylinder 1 - spark plug OFF] C --> D[Measure BHP2] D --> E[Cut out Cylinder 2] E --> F[Measure BHP3] F --> G[Continue until 1 cylinder firing] G --> H[Plot BHP vs n_cyl_firing] H --> I[Extrapolate to n=0 - FHP intercept] I --> J[FHP = Intercept on Y-axis] J --> K[IHP = BHP_full + FHP] K --> L[eta_mech = BHP / IHP] ``` ## 4.4 Comparative Chart of Dynamometer Types (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD DYN[Dynamometers]:::root --> ABS[Absorption Type]:::branch DYN --> TRAN[Transmission Type]:::branch ABS --> ROPE[Rope / Friction Brake]:::leaf ABS --> HYD[Hydraulic Water Brake]:::leaf ABS --> EDD[Eddy Current]:::leaf ABS --> FAN[Fan / Air Brake]:::leaf ABS --> PRO[Prony Brake]:::leaf TRAN --> GEN[Electrical Generator]:::leaf TRAN --> CHR[Chassis Dynamometer]:::leaf classDef root fill:#FFE082,stroke:#FF6F00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000 classDef branch fill:#A5D6A7,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef leaf fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.5 The Indicator Diagram (Schematic P-V Trace) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ID[Typical 4-Stroke P-V Diagram - imep shown as horizontal line] P1[Point 1 - IVC Start of Compression] --> P2[Point 2 - Compression TDC] P2 --> P3[Point 3 - Combustion Peak Pressure] P3 --> P4[Point 4 - Expansion to BDC] P4 --> P5[Point 5 - Exhaust Blowdown] P5 --> P6[Point 6 - EVO to Exhaust] P6 --> P1 end PM[imep = Pm shown as dashed horizontal line dividing diagram into equal areas] PM -.-> ID ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q1.** Define Indicated Horsepower (IHP) and Friction Horsepower (FHP). State the relation between IHP, BHP, and FHP. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **IHP (Indicated Horsepower):** The power developed by the working fluid on the piston face inside the engine cylinder, calculated from the P-V (indicator) diagram and the engine speed. **(1 mark)** - **FHP (Friction Horsepower):** The power lost due to mechanical friction between moving parts of the engine such as piston rings, bearings, valve train, oil pump, and water pump. **(1 mark)** - **Relation:** $\quad FHP = IHP - BHP$, where BHP is the brake horsepower measured at the crankshaft. **(1 mark)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q2.** What is a dynamometer? List any two types of dynamometers used for engine testing. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **dynamometer** is a device used to **measure the torque and rotational speed** of an engine (or motor) shaft, and to **absorb the power output** in the form of heat or electricity, thereby allowing direct calculation of BHP. **(1.5 marks)** - Two types: **(any two – 0.75 each)** 1. **Rope brake dynamometer** (friction type) 2. **Hydraulic dynamometer** (water brake) 3. **Eddy-current dynamometer** 4. **Electrical dynamometer** (AC/DC generator type) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 1 Choice Question]** > > **Question A (14 Marks):** > **(a)** Derive the expression for Indicated Horsepower of a multi-cylinder engine in terms of imep, bore, stroke, speed, and number of cylinders. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A 4-cylinder, 4-stroke petrol engine has bore 75 mm and stroke 90 mm. It runs at 2800 rpm. The average imep is 7.5 bar. A rope brake dynamometer with effective drum diameter 0.5 m records a net load of 50 N. Calculate: (i) IHP, (ii) BHP, (iii) Mechanical efficiency. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question A #### (a) Derivation of IHP (7 marks) **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** Let D = bore (m), L = stroke (m). Piston area: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4}D^2 \quad \text{m}^2$$ **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Let P_m = imep (N/m²). Work done per cycle per cylinder: $$W_c = P_m \cdot A \cdot L \quad \text{J}$$ **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** For a 4-stroke engine, one working cycle = 2 revolutions. Power strokes per second per cylinder: $$n = \frac{N}{2 \times 60} = \frac{N}{120}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** For k cylinders: $$\boxed{IHP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{120} \quad \text{W (4-stroke)}}$$ For a 2-stroke: $$\boxed{IHP = \frac{P_m \cdot L \cdot A \cdot N \cdot k}{60} \quad \text{W (2-stroke)}}$$ **Step 5 (1 mark):** Convert to kW by dividing by 1000. #### (b) Numerical (7 marks) **Given:** k = 4, 4-stroke, D = 0.075 m, L = 0.090 m, N = 2800 rpm, P_m = 7.5 bar = 7.5 × 10⁵ N/m², D_r = 0.5 m, (W − S) = 50 N. **(i) IHP (3 marks):** - Area: $A = \dfrac{\pi}{4}(0.075)^2 = 4.418 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$ - Numerator: $(7.5 \times 10^5)(0.090)(4.418 \times 10^{-3})(2800)(4)$ - $= 7.5 \times 10^5 \times 0.090 = 67500$ - $\times 4.418 \times 10^{-3} = 298.2$ - $\times 2800 = 834{,}960$ - $\times 4 = 3{,}339{,}840$ - $IHP = 3{,}339{,}840 / 120 = 27{,}832 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{27.83 \text{ kW}}$ **(ii) BHP (2 marks):** - $BHP = \dfrac{\pi N D_r (W-S)}{60} = \dfrac{\pi \times 2800 \times 0.5 \times 50}{60}$ - $= \dfrac{\pi \times 70000}{60} = \dfrac{219911}{60} = 3665.2 \text{ W} \approx \mathbf{3.67 \text{ kW}}$ **(iii) Mechanical efficiency (2 marks):** - $\eta_{mech} = \dfrac{BHP}{IHP} \times 100\% = \dfrac{3.67}{27.83} \times 100\% = \mathbf{13.19\%}$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students commonly forget to convert **bar to N/m²** (multiply by 10⁵). Many also wrongly use the 2-stroke formula for a 4-stroke engine (or vice-versa). Always check "**4-stroke**" / "**2-stroke**" in the data. Marks are specifically allotted for **unit conversion and correct formula selection**. --- > **Question B (14 Marks) – Alternative Choice:** > **(a)** Explain the Morse Test for determining the friction horsepower of a multi-cylinder engine. State its limitations. **(7 marks)** > **(b)** A 6-cylinder, 4-stroke engine was tested on a Morse test. The BHP readings with different cylinders firing are: All 6 firing = 60 kW, 5 firing = 49 kW, 4 firing = 38.5 kW, 3 firing = 28 kW. Determine the indicated power of one cylinder, total IHP, FHP, and mechanical efficiency. **(7 marks)** ### Model Solution — Question B #### (a) Morse Test – Explanation (7 marks) **Introduction (1 mark):** The Morse test is an experimental method to determine the **Friction Horsepower (FHP)** of a multi-cylinder engine by progressively **cutting out (misfiring) one cylinder at a time** and observing the corresponding drop in brake horsepower. **Procedure (3 marks):** 1. Run the engine at full load and rated speed with **all k cylinders firing**. Record BHP₁. 2. Cut out (de-energize spark plug or stop fuel injection in) one cylinder, keeping load and speed constant. Record BHP₂. 3. Cut out a second cylinder — record BHP₃. Continue until only **one cylinder is firing** and record BHP_k. 4. The **drop in BHP** when one cylinder is cut out equals the **IHP developed by that cylinder** (since FHP is assumed constant). 5. Plot BHP vs. number of cylinders firing; **extrapolate the straight line to n = 0**. The **Y-intercept = FHP**. **Calculations (2 marks):** $$FHP = n \cdot [BHP_{n} - BHP_{(n-1)}] \quad \text{(averaged)}$$ $$IHP = BHP_{full\,load} + FHP$$ **Limitations (1 mark):** *(any two)* - Assumes FHP is **constant** for all cylinder cut-out combinations (not strictly true). - The **firing order** and **cooling load** change when cylinders are cut out. - Applicable only to **multi-cylinder** engines; not single-cylinder. - Cannot be performed on a 2-stroke easily because no separate exhaust event for some cylinders. #### (b) Numerical – Morse Test (7 marks) **Given:** k = 6, 4-stroke. BHP data: - 6 firing: 60 kW - 5 firing: 49 kW - 4 firing: 38.5 kW - 3 firing: 28 kW **Step 1 (1.5 marks):** IHP per cylinder = drop in BHP when one cylinder is cut out (each step). - Cut from 6→5: IHP per cyl = 60 − 49 = 11 kW - Cut from 5→4: IHP per cyl = 49 − 38.5 = 10.5 kW - Cut from 4→3: IHP per cyl = 38.5 − 28 = 10.5 kW **Step 2 (1.5 marks):** Average IHP per cylinder: $$\overline{IHP}_{cyl} = \frac{11 + 10.5 + 10.5}{3} = \frac{32}{3} = 10.67 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 3 (1 mark):** Total IHP for 6 cylinders: $$IHP_{total} = 6 \times 10.67 = 64.0 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 4 (1.5 marks):** FHP: $$FHP = IHP - BHP = 64.0 - 60.0 = 4.0 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 (1.5 marks):** Mechanical efficiency: $$\eta_{mech} = \frac{60}{64} \times 100\% = 93.75\%$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** In Morse Test numericals, students often **divide by 2** (thinking 4-stroke half-power-stroke logic) — this is WRONG. The drop in BHP *directly* gives the IHP of the cut-out cylinder. Also, do not average the BHP values themselves; average the **differences (drops)** between successive readings. Plotting BHP vs n and extrapolating to n=0 is the **graphical method** preferred by KTU valuators (give it as bonus mark). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **IHP** = power developed in the cylinder (from P-V diagram). - **BHP** = power measured at the shaft using a dynamometer. - **FHP** = IHP − BHP = power lost to friction. - **η_mech = BHP / IHP** (always < 1, typically 80–92% for modern engines). - **IHP formula (4-stroke):** IHP = P_m · L · A · N · k / 120 (Watts). - **IHP formula (2-stroke):** IHP = P_m · L · A · N · k / 60 (Watts). - **A = (π/4)·D²**, where D = bore diameter. - **P_m (imep) is in N/m²** — always convert bar to N/m² by multiplying by 10⁵. - **Rope brake BHP:** BHP = π · N · D_r · (W − S) / 60 (Watts), with W, S in Newtons. - **Morse Test:** Cut out cylinders one by one; FHP = extrapolated BHP at n = 0. - **IHP per cylinder** = Drop in BHP when one cylinder is cut out. - **Willans Line** (additional, for KTU reference): Plot BSFC vs BHP — intercept gives **FSF (Friction Specific Fuel)**, slope gives BSFC at full load. - **Units:** 1 hp = 745.7 W = 0.7457 kW. - **Common errors to avoid:** - Confusing 2-stroke and 4-stroke formulas. - Forgetting to convert bar → N/m². - Mixing up FHP = IHP − BHP with BHP − IHP. - In rope brake, using kgf units without multiplying by 9.81. - In Morse test, dividing by 2 (unnecessary for 4-stroke). - **Typical values to remember:** η_mech = 0.8–0.92; imep for petrol = 7–10 bar; for diesel = 8–14 bar.

topics.title: Different Types of Dynamometers: Hydraulic
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Different Types of Dynamometers: Hydraulic

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Hydraulic Dynamometer** is an absorption-type dynamometer that converts the mechanical energy of a prime mover (engine or motor under test) into the thermal energy of a working fluid — typically **water** — by shearing the fluid between a rotating member (rotor) and a stationary member (stator). The frictional shearing action dissipates rotational kinetic energy as heat, which is carried away by the circulating fluid, while the resulting reaction torque is measured externally via a lever-arm and load-cell mechanism.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hydraulic dynamometers are classified under *absorption dynamometers* (energy-dissipating) as opposed to *transmission dynamometers* (energy-transferring). The Froude water-brake dynamometer is the archetype studied at KTU level.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine stirring a very thick, heavy bucket of water with a paddle. The harder and faster you stir, the more resistance the water offers against your hand. That resistance force is what your arm "feels" as torque. Now imagine a machine doing the stirring continuously and a thermometer in the bucket: the bucket gets warm.

- **The paddle wheel** = Rotor (connected to the engine shaft)
- **The thick stationary water around the bucket walls** = Stator / Casing
- **The resistance on your hand** = Reaction torque (measured by a scale)
- **The warm water** = Dissipated mechanical energy converted into heat

The dynamometer simply *measures* how hard the engine has to push to keep stirring, and from that, infers the **power** the engine is producing.

### 1.3 Key Physical Quantities

| Quantity | Symbol | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Rotational Speed | $N$ | **rpm** (or rad/s) |
| Reaction Torque | $T$ | **N·m** |
| Power Absorbed | $P$ | **kW** (or W) |
| Water Density | $\rho$ | **kg/m³** (≈ 1000) |
| Specific Heat of Water | $C_p$ | **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** |
| Volume Flow Rate | $Q$ | **m³/s** (or L/s) |

> [!NOTE]
> Recall the universal dynamometer power identity: $P = 2\pi N T / 60$ for $N$ in rpm and $T$ in N·m, yielding $P$ in watts. This is the single most-tested formula in KTU board exams on dynamometers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hydraulic Dynamometers

Hydraulic dynamometers fall under the broader family of absorption dynamometers and are further subdivided into three functional categories:

1. **Water-Brake (Friction) Dynamometer** — *Froude-type*. Pure energy dissipation through fluid shear.
2. **Hydraulic Transmission Dynamometer** — Uses a hydraulic coupling or torque converter; part of the energy is recovered (e.g., for charging a hydraulic accumulator).
3. **Hydraulic Pump Dynamometer** — The hydraulic machine acts both as a load and as a metering device.

The **Froude water-brake dynamometer** is the most commonly tested at KTU and forms the focus of this module.

### 2.2 Constructional Anatomy

The unit consists of the following primary elements:

- **Rotor (Impeller):** A disc-shaped or vaned wheel keyed to the input shaft from the engine.
- **Stator (Casing):** A stationary shell fitted with guide vanes or pockets that redirect the swirling fluid back toward the rotor inlet.
- **Water Inlet & Outlet Ports:** Allow continuous flow of cool water into and heated water out of the casing.
- **Throttle Valve / Swirl Plate:** An adjustable restriction in the outlet that controls the water level inside the casing, and hence the resistance torque.
- **Bearing Pedestal & Pivoted Frame:** The entire rotor assembly is mounted on trunnions so that the reaction torque can be balanced against a dead-weight on a moment arm.
- **Tachometer Coupling:** Provides rotational speed measurement.

### 2.3 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

- **Step 1 — Energy Input:** The engine drives the rotor at speed $N$. Mechanical power $P_{in} = 2\pi N T_{in}/60$ enters the dynamometer.
- **Step 2 — Fluid Acceleration:** As the rotor spins, its vanes accelerate the water radially outward, imparting a tangential (swirl) velocity component equal to a fraction of the rotor tip speed.
- **Step 3 — Shear & Dissipation:** The rotating fluid mass shears against the relatively stationary fluid in the casing and against the stator vanes. Viscous shear converts kinetic energy into heat.
- **Step 4 — Energy Removal:** Heated water is drained, and cool water is continuously supplied, maintaining a steady-state thermal condition.
- **Step 5 — Reaction Torque Generation:** Because fluid is accelerated tangentially in one direction, the rotor experiences an equal and opposite reaction torque. This torque is transmitted through the trunnions to the measuring lever.
- **Step 6 — Torque Measurement:** A calibrated weight $W$ at radius $R$ balances the reaction: $T = W \cdot R$. Power is then computed as $P = 2\pi N T / 60$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T}{60}$ | Power absorbed by dynamometer | **W** (with $N$ in rpm, $T$ in N·m) |
| 2 | $T = W \cdot R$ | Reaction torque from lever balance | **N·m** |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$ | Mass flow rate of cooling water | **kg/s** |
| 4 | $P = \dot{m} \, C_p \, \Delta T$ | Heat carried away by water (energy balance) | **W** |
| 5 | $Q = \dfrac{P}{\rho \, C_p \, \Delta T}$ | Required cooling water flow rate | **m³/s** |
| 6 | $\Delta T = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m} \, C_p}$ | Temperature rise of cooling water | **K or °C** |
| 7 | $\eta_{absorption} = 1 - \dfrac{T_{stator}}{T_{rotor}}$ | Energy dissipation efficiency | **dimensionless** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** Formulas (1), (3), (4), and (5) appear in over **80%** of KTU end-semester questions on hydraulic dynamometers. Always state the assumed water properties explicitly: $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ and $C_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

Hydraulic dynamometers are the workhorse of:
- **Automotive engine test cells** (chassis dynamometers, engine development rigs).
- **Marine propulsion testing** (large diesel and gas-turbine prime movers).
- **Industrial motor and pump qualification** (BS ISO 3046 / IEC 60034 conformance).
- **Vehicle certification labs** for emission and fuel-mapping cycles (WHTC, ETC, NRTC).

Their high **thermal capacity** (water absorbs ~4186 J per kg per °C) makes them uniquely suited to absorbing hundreds of kilowatts continuously without saturating, unlike eddy-current or friction-belt dynamometers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Power Absorbed from Torque and Speed

**Starting Premise:** A rotating shaft transmits torque $T$ at angular velocity $\omega$. The instantaneous power is the scalar product of torque vector and angular velocity vector.

$$
P = T \cdot \omega
$$

**Step 1 — Convert angular velocity from rpm to rad/s.**

If the shaft rotates at $N$ revolutions per minute, then the revolutions per second is $N/60$, and multiplying by $2\pi$ rad/rev gives:

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{rad}}{\text{s}} \right]
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\omega$ into the power identity.**

$$
P = T \cdot \frac{2\pi N}{60}
$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange and assign unit conventions.**

For $T$ in N·m and $N$ in rpm, $P$ is in watts. To convert to **kW**, divide by 1000:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{kW}} = \frac{2\pi N T}{60\,000}\,}
$$

This is the single most-cited equation in the hydraulic dynamometer sub-module.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Cooling Water Flow Rate

**Energy Balance Premise:** In steady state, the mechanical power absorbed must equal the rate of enthalpy carried away by the cooling water:

$$
P_{\text{absorbed}} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

**Step 1 — Express the mass flow rate in terms of volumetric flow rate.**

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q
$$

where $Q$ is in m³/s and $\rho$ in kg/m³.

**Step 2 — Substitute into the energy balance.**

$$
P = \rho \, Q \, C_p \, \Delta T
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the volumetric flow rate.**

$$
Q = \frac{P}{\rho \, C_p \, \Delta T}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values for water** ($\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_p = 4186 \text{ J/kg·K}$):

$$
Q = \frac{P}{1000 \times 4186 \times \Delta T} = \frac{P}{4.186 \times 10^6 \cdot \Delta T} \quad [\text{m}^3/\text{s}]
$$

**Step 5 — Express in litres per minute (a more practical unit for lab work).**

Since $1 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 60\,000 \text{ L/min}$:

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{\text{L/min}} = \frac{60\,000 \cdot P}{4.186 \times 10^6 \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{P}{69.77 \cdot \Delta T}\,}
$$

where $P$ is in watts and $\Delta T$ is in °C.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Balance Verification Across Rotor–Stator Pair

**Premise:** For a fully filled, throttled hydraulic dynamometer in steady state, the energy entering the rotor from the engine equals the sum of (a) heat carried by water and (b) mechanical work extracted by the stator on its mounting.

Let $T_r$ = torque on rotor shaft, $T_s$ = torque on stator trunnion.

For a perfectly stalled (locked) outlet swirl, by angular momentum conservation across the rotor–stator pair:

$$
T_s = (1 - k) \, T_r
$$

where $k$ is the dimensionless swirl-recovery coefficient. The energy dissipated as heat is then:

$$
P_{\text{heat}} = (T_r - T_s) \cdot \omega = k \, T_r \, \omega
$$

This confirms that **all** the absorbed power ultimately appears as enthalpy of the discharge water — the defining thermodynamic behaviour of the Froude brake.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** A hydraulic dynamometer absorbs **75 kW** at **1500 rpm**. Cooling water enters at **25 °C** and leaves at **55 °C**. Determine:
- (i) The reaction torque.
- (ii) The required water flow rate in L/min.
- (Assume $C_p = 4.186 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$, $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$.)

**Solution (i) — Reaction Torque:**

$$
P = \frac{2\pi N T}{60\,000}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
T = \frac{P \times 60\,000}{2\pi N}
$$

Substituting $P = 75\,000$ W, $N = 1500$ rpm:

$$
T = \frac{75\,000 \times 60\,000}{2\pi \times 1500}
$$

$$
T = \frac{4.5 \times 10^9}{9424.78}
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 477.46 \text{ N·m}}
$$

**Solution (ii) — Water Flow Rate:**

$$
\Delta T = 55 - 25 = 30 \text{ °C}
$$
