Since $10.47 > 5$, the vehicle **FAILS** the side-slip test. **Corrective action required:** shorten the tie-rod to reduce toe-in until per-wheel side slip ≤ 5 m/km (i.e. toe ≤ 0° 17').

---

### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** Derive the fundamental relationship between toe angle $\theta_t$ (radians) and side slip $S$ (m/km) for a single wheel. Clearly state the small-angle approximation and the boundary under which it is valid. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a periodic fitness test, a heavy commercial vehicle records a side slip of 7.5 m/km outward on its front-right wheel and 2.3 m/km inward on its front-left wheel. The lane tolerance for this vehicle category is $\pm 7$ m/km per wheel. (i) Determine whether each wheel independently passes. (ii) Compute the *net* side slip of the axle (algebraic sum). (iii) If the net reading fails, suggest two practical corrections the workshop should perform. **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks)

**Setup (1 mark):** Let the wheel plane be inclined at angle $\theta_t$ (toe angle) to the vehicle longitudinal axis. As the wheel rolls forward by arc length $s$ without slipping, decompose the displacement into:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x = s \cos(\theta_t) \quad & \text{(forward component)} \\
y = s \sin(\theta_t) \quad & \text{(lateral component)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Eliminate $s$ (2 marks):**

$$
\frac{y}{x} = \tan(\theta_t)
$$

**Plate relation (1 mark):** Over the plate length $L_p$ (forward travel $x = L_p$):

$$
\Delta y = L_p \tan(\theta_t)
$$

**Definition of side slip (2 marks):** Side slip $S$ is lateral displacement per kilometer of forward travel:

$$
S = \frac{\Delta y}{L_p} \cdot 1000 = 1000 \tan(\theta_t) \;\; \text{m/km}
$$

**Small-angle statement (1 mark):** For $\theta_t < 1°$ (= 0.01745 rad), $\tan(\theta_t) \approx \theta_t$, so:

$$
\boxed{S \approx 1000 \, \theta_t \;\; \text{m/km}, \quad \theta_t \text{ in rad}}
$$

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Front-right wheel: $S_{RH} = +7.5$ m/km (outward = toe-in convention, positive)
- Front-left wheel: $S_{LH} = -2.3$ m/km (inward = toe-out convention, negative)
- Per-wheel tolerance for heavy vehicle: $\pm 7$ m/km

**Step (i) — Independent per-wheel check (3 marks):**

| Wheel | Reading (m/km) | Limit | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Right | +7.5 | ±7 | ❌ FAIL (exceeds +7) |
| Left | −2.3 | ±7 | ✅ PASS (within limit) |

**Step (ii) — Net side slip of axle (2 marks):**

$$
S_{\text{net}} = S_{LH} + S_{RH} = (-2.3) + (+7.5) = +5.2 \;\; \text{m/km}
$$

**Step (iii) — If net fails, two corrective actions (2 marks):**
*(Note: net +5.2 m/km is within ±7, so technically the net passes. However, since the right wheel individually failed, the workshop must still act.)*
1. **Tie-rod adjustment** on the right wheel to bring its toe back within ±7 m/km (i.e. reduce toe-in by ≈ 0.5 m/km).
2. **Steering arm / king-pin inspection** for bent components, followed by a full four-wheel alignment on a laser or 3D aligner.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on side-slip problems:**
> 1. **Unit mix-up** — writing $\theta_t$ in degrees inside the formula $1000 \tan(\theta_t)$. The formula *requires radians for the small-angle version* OR you must convert to degrees first and use the degree-form explicitly. Mixing units is the single most common 2-mark deduction.
> 2. **Sign confusion** — forgetting that toe-in and toe-out have *opposite* signs. The algebraic sum of the two wheels can cancel partially; students who blindly add magnitudes get the wrong net.
> 3. **Forgetting the small-angle warning** — examiners often award only 1/2 marks for using $\tan(\theta_t)$ when $\theta_t > 5°$ without acknowledging the approximation's validity.
> 4. **Confusing *per-wheel* and *axle total*** — always read the question stem carefully; CMVR specifies the limit *per vehicle* (= algebraic sum), not per wheel, although some lanes apply per-wheel limits. State your assumption.
> 5. **Skipping the conversion of minutes/seconds** — a toe of "0° 18'" must first be converted to 0.3° before $\tan$ is applied; otherwise calculators error out.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Side slip** is the lateral scrub of a steered wheel, expressed in **m/km**.
- ✅ It is caused predominantly by **toe angle**; small contributions from camber and tire conicity.
- ✅ **Acceptance limit (CMVR/ARAI):** $\vert S \vert \le 5$ m/km for passenger cars; up to $\pm 7$ m/km for heavy vehicles.
- ✅ **Fundamental equation:** $S = 1000 \tan(\theta_t)$; small-angle: $S \approx 1000 \cdot \theta_t$ (rad).
- ✅ **Inverse relation:** $\theta_t = \arctan(S/1000) \approx S/1000$ rad.
- ✅ **Two main tester types:** (i) mechanical sliding-plate with dial gauge, (ii) electronic LVDT/potentiometer with digital readout.
- ✅ **Test procedure:** roll vehicle at 4–6 km/h over a flush-mounted sliding plate of length ≈ 1 m, with steering released.
- ✅ **Outputs:** per-wheel reading, axle net reading, PASS/FAIL indication, data log to lane computer.
- ✅ **1 m/km ≈ 0.0573°** of toe angle (conversion constant worth memorising).
- ✅ **Indicative readings:** 1° toe ⇒ 17.46 m/km (severe); 0° 10' toe ⇒ 2.91 m/km (acceptable); 0° 17' toe ⇒ 4.94 m/km (borderline PASS).
- ✅ **Common faults causing high S:** bent tie-rod, worn ball-joint, bent steering knuckle, incorrect ride height, manufacturing defect.
- ✅ **Standards to cite in KTU answers:** IS 9211 (test lane), SAE J1570 (side-slip test), CMVR Rule 62 (fitness).
- ✅ Always state the **sign convention** (toe-in positive / toe-out negative) before solving any problem.
- ✅ The tester measures **displacement**, not force — it is a *kinematic* test, not a *force* test like a wheel-alignment load cell.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brake Testers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Brake Testers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Brake Tester** is a precision diagnostic instrument used in automotive service stations, vehicle inspection bays, and homologation laboratories to quantitatively measure the **braking efficiency**, **brake force**, **brake imbalance**, and **deceleration capability** of a vehicle's service brake system. Brake testers translate the frictional retarding torque generated at the wheel–brake interface into measurable physical quantities (force, torque, or deceleration) without requiring the vehicle to perform a full road test.

According to the **Bureau of Indian Standards (IS 11852)** and the **ECE R13 / R13-H** regulations adopted under the **Central Motor Vehicle Rules (CMVR)**, brake testers form an integral part of the **Automated Vehicle Inspection & Certification (AVIC)** ecosystem. They are categorized based on their measurement principle: **Roller-Type (Dynamometric)**, **Plate-Type**, **Decelerometer-Type**, and **Inertia-Type** testers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on the *Roller-Type Brake Tester* as the industry standard for periodic vehicle inspection. Students must master the calculation of **brake efficiency**, **imbalance**, and **ovality** from tester readings.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are trying to determine how strong a person is by simply asking them to push against a fixed wall. The harder they push, the more force they generate, and you can measure that push with a scale placed between them and the wall. A **brake tester works on exactly the same principle**. Instead of pushing, the wheel's brake pushes back against a spinning roller (the "wall"), and a load cell (the "scale") measures the resulting reaction force.

- **Why the wheel doesn't move during the test:** The roller is motor-driven and spins at road-equivalent speed, so the wheel remains essentially stationary while the brake applies its full clamping force.
- **Why this is safer than a road test:** The vehicle is stationary, so there's no risk of skidding, traffic interference, or driver error — making it ideal for crowded inspection lanes.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics

The following **bold** metrics form the standard vocabulary of brake tester analysis:

- **Brake Force (F)**: The tangential retarding force generated at the tire–roller interface, measured in **Newton (N)** or **kgf**.
- **Brake Efficiency (η or BE)**: The ratio of total brake force to the gross vehicle weight (or per-axle weight), expressed as a **percentage (%)**.
- **Brake Imbalance**: The percentage difference in braking force between the left and right wheels on the same axle.
- **Ovality**: The deviation of brake drum/disc from a perfect circle, measured as a percentage.
- **Coefficient of Friction (μ)**: The dimensionless ratio of brake force to normal axle load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Threshold (CMVR / IS 11852):** A vehicle is deemed to have passed the brake test if the **service brake efficiency ≥ 50 %** for the **service brake** and **≥ 25 %** for the **parking brake** (older CMVR norms), with modern norms tightening to **60 %** for service brakes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Brake Testers

Brake testers are classified into **four major families** based on their operating principle:

1. **Roller-Type (Dynamometric) Brake Tester**
   - The vehicle's drive wheel is placed on a pair of contra-rotating rollers.
   - The brake is applied, and the reactive force on the roller shaft is measured by a **load cell** or **strain gauge bridge**.
   - Roller diameter and surface friction are calibrated to simulate road conditions.
   - **Application:** Most widely used in authorized service stations and inspection centers.

2. **Plate-Type Brake Tester**
   - A low-friction plate is placed under each wheel, and the vehicle is driven onto it.
   - During braking, the plate slides forward against a **dynamometer** that records the longitudinal reaction force.
   - **Application:** Heavy commercial vehicles, buses, and multi-axle trucks.

3. **Decelerometer-Type Brake Tester**
   - The vehicle is driven at a fixed speed (typically 30–40 km/h), and the brakes are fully applied.
   - An onboard **decelerometer** (pendulum or piezoelectric accelerometer) records the peak deceleration.
   - Brake efficiency is calculated from deceleration and wheel reaction forces.
   - **Application:** Road tests, fleet management, ADR-compliant audits.

4. **Inertia-Type (Flywheel) Brake Tester**
   - A **flywheel** simulates the vehicle's kinetic energy.
   - The brake assembly is mounted and spun to a calculated speed, then applied.
   - The deceleration of the flywheel yields the brake's torque–speed–energy absorption characteristics.
   - **Application:** Laboratory R&D, brake component development (pads, discs, drums).

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Roller-Type Brake Tester

The following bullet structure mirrors the **KTU marker's expected answer flow** for a 7-mark question:

- **Step 1 — Vehicle Positioning:** The vehicle is guided onto the test lane so that each wheel rests on its dedicated roller pair. Axle load is either measured by built-in load cells or assumed from the registration document.
- **Step 2 — Roller Spin-Up:** Electric motors drive the rollers to a calibrated surface speed (typically 2.5–5 km/h for static brake tests, or up to 100 km/h for high-speed chassis dynamometers).
- **Step 3 — Brake Application:** The driver applies the brake pedal at a controlled rate until maximum pedal force is reached (or until anti-lock systems intervene).
- **Step 4 — Force Measurement:** Strain gauges bonded to the roller support arms produce a voltage proportional to the tangential brake force $F_b$.
- **Step 5 — Data Acquisition:** The controller converts the analog signal to digital, applies calibration constants, and displays brake force per wheel, per axle, and total.
- **Step 6 — Result Calculation:** The system computes brake efficiency, imbalance, and ovality (if drum-brake measurement is selected) using embedded algorithms.

> [!TIP]
> **Why use a roller pair instead of a single roller?** Two contra-rotating rollers prevent the tire from being **dragged sideways** and ensure that the **slip ratio** remains constant, giving a stable force reading.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Parameter** | **Symbol** | **Formula** | **Unit** | **Notes** |
| :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Brake Force (per wheel) | $F_b$ | $F_b = \mu \times N$ | N (or kgf) | $N$ = normal reaction at wheel |
| Total Brake Force (vehicle) | $F_{total}$ | $F_{total} = \sum F_{b,i}$ | N | Sum of all wheel forces |
| Brake Efficiency (Service) | $\eta_s$ | $\eta_s = \dfrac{F_{total}}{W_{g}} \times 100$ | % | $W_g$ = gross vehicle weight |
| Brake Efficiency (Parking) | $\eta_p$ | $\eta_p = \dfrac{F_{parking}}{W_{g}} \times 100$ | % | Parking brake only |
| Brake Imbalance | $\Delta F$ | $\Delta F = \dfrac{\vert F_{L} - F_{R} \vert}{F_{L} + F_{R}} \times 100$ | % | Per axle; ECE limit $\leq 30$ % |
| Drum Ovality | $O$ | $O = \dfrac{D_{max} - D_{min}}{D_{max}} \times 100$ | % | Measured with dial gauge |
| Equivalent Deceleration | $a$ | $a = \dfrac{F_{total}}{m}$ | m/s² | $m$ = vehicle mass |
| Stopping Distance | $s$ | $s = \dfrac{v^{2}}{2 a}$ | m | From kinematic equation |
| Kinetic Energy (Inertia Tester) | $KE$ | $KE = \dfrac{1}{2} I \omega^{2}$ | J | $I$ = flywheel inertia, $\omega$ = angular velocity |
| Friction Torque (Disc) | $T_f$ | $T_f = \mu \times F_c \times r_{eff} \times n$ | N·m | $n$ = number of friction surfaces |
| Friction Torque (Drum) | $T_f$ | $T_f = \mu \times F_c \times r_{eff}$ | N·m | Single friction surface |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When calculating brake efficiency, always use the **dynamic wheel load** during braking (load transfer to the front axle occurs) — but for **statutory testing under CMVR**, the static gross vehicle weight is the denominator. Mixing these two will cost 1–2 marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Brake testers are the **gatekeepers of road safety**. Modern **Intelligent Transport Systems (ITS)** integrate brake tester data into **Vehicle Inspection & Certification (VIC)** databases, enabling governments to identify vehicles with substandard braking before they cause accidents. In the **commercial vehicle sector**, fleet operators use roller brake testers for **pre-trip inspections** to comply with **AIS-153** and **ADR** norms, reducing liability and insurance costs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Brake Efficiency from Roller Tester Readings

A two-axle vehicle is tested on a roller brake tester. The front axle gross load is $W_f = 8$ kN and the rear axle gross load is $W_r = 6$ kN. The recorded brake forces are:

- Front Left: $F_{FL} = 3200$ N, Front Right: $F_{FR} = 3000$ N
- Rear Left: $F_{RL} = 2200$ N, Rear Right: $F_{RR} = 2000$ N

Find: (a) Total brake force, (b) Brake efficiency, (c) Front and rear axle imbalance.

**Step 1 — Total Brake Force**

We sum the brake forces from all four wheels. The unit is in Newtons.

$$
F_{total} = F_{FL} + F_{FR} + F_{RL} + F_{RR}
$$

Substituting the given numerical values into the summation expression.

$$
F_{total} = 3200 + 3000 + 2200 + 2000
$$

Performing the arithmetic addition from left to right.

$$
F_{total} = 10400 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2 — Gross Vehicle Weight**

The gross vehicle weight is the sum of the front and rear axle static weights.

$$
W_{g} = W_f + W_r
$$

Substituting the axle weights.

$$
W_{g} = 8000 + 6000 = 14000 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Brake Efficiency**

Brake efficiency is the ratio of total brake force to gross vehicle weight, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta = \frac{F_{total}}{W_{g}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the previously calculated values.

$$
\eta = \frac{10400}{14000} \times 100
$$

Performing the division and multiplication.

$$
\eta = 0.7428 \times 100 = 74.28 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4 — Front Axle Imbalance**

Imbalance is the absolute difference between left and right forces divided by their sum, multiplied by 100.

$$
\Delta F_{front} = \frac{\vert F_{FL} - F_{FR} \vert}{F_{FL} + F_{FR}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the front axle values.

$$
\Delta F_{front} = \frac{\vert 3200 - 3000 \vert}{3200 + 3000} \times 100
$$

Computing the numerator and denominator.

$$
\Delta F_{front} = \frac{200}{6200} \times 100 = 3.23 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 5 — Rear Axle Imbalance**

Following the same formula for the rear axle.

$$
\Delta F_{rear} = \frac{\vert F_{RL} - F_{RR} \vert}{F_{RL} + F_{RR}} \times 100
$$

Substituting the rear axle values.

$$
\Delta F_{rear} = \frac{\vert 2200 - 2000 \vert}{2200 + 2000} \times 100 = \frac{200}{4200} \times 100
$$

Performing the final computation.

$$
\Delta F_{rear} = 4.76 \text{ \%}
$$

**Result Summary**

The vehicle generates a **total brake force of 10400 N**, achieves a **brake efficiency of 74.28 %** (which exceeds the 50 % CMVR threshold), and shows **imbalance values well below the 30 % ECE limit**, indicating that the vehicle passes the brake test.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Drum Ovality from Tester Measurements

A brake drum is measured at four angular positions 90° apart using a dial gauge. The maximum reading is $D_{max} = 252.40$ mm and the minimum reading is $D_{min} = 251.20$ mm. Determine the ovality percentage.

**Step 1 — Recall Ovality Formula**

The ovality is defined as the ratio of the difference between maximum and minimum diameters to the maximum diameter, expressed as a percentage.

$$
O = \frac{D_{max} - D_{min}}{D_{max}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Values**

Inserting the measured diameter values.

$$
O = \frac{252.40 - 251.20}{252.40} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Numerator**

Subtracting the minimum from the maximum.

$$
O = \frac{1.20}{252.40} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Final Calculation**

Dividing and converting to percentage.

$$
O = 0.00475 \times 100 = 0.475 \text{ \%}
$$

**Interpretation:** A drum ovality under 0.5 % is generally acceptable; values above 1 % require re-machining or replacement.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Equivalent Vehicle Deceleration from Brake Force

Given the same vehicle from Derivation 1 with mass $m = 1428$ kg (i.e., $W_g = 14$ kN / $9.81$ m/s²), find the equivalent deceleration and stopping distance from 60 km/h.

**Step 1 — Compute Equivalent Deceleration**

By Newton's second law, the equivalent deceleration is the total brake force divided by the vehicle mass.

$$
a = \frac{F_{total}}{m}
$$

Substituting the known values.

$$
a = \frac{10400}{1428}
$$

Performing the division.

$$
a = 7.28 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Convert Speed to m/s**

The initial speed in SI units is obtained by multiplying km/h by 5/18.

$$
v = 60 \times \frac{5}{18} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Kinematic Equation**

Using the third equation of motion, where final velocity is zero.

$$
v^{2} = u^{2} + 2 a s
$$

Solving for stopping distance $s$ by setting $v = 0$ and rearranging.

$$
0 = u^{2} - 2 a s \quad \Rightarrow \quad s = \frac{u^{2}}{2 a}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and Compute**

Substituting the numerical values.

$$
s = \frac{(16.67)^{2}}{2 \times 7.28}
$$

Computing the numerator and denominator.

$$
s = \frac{277.89}{14.56} = 19.08 \text{ m}
$$

**Result Summary**

The vehicle decelerates at **7.28 m/s²** (approximately **0.74 g**) and stops from 60 km/h in **19.08 m**, which is well within typical safety margins (MVD norms require stopping within ~22 m from 60 km/h for M1 category vehicles).

> [!NOTE]
> **Analytical Insight:** Notice that the **decelerometer-type brake tester** would yield *exactly* this $a$ value, and from it, the brake efficiency is back-calculated as $\eta = a \times m / W_g \times 100$. This dual-pathway is why both tester types produce legally equivalent results.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Roller-Type Brake Tester

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** and **signal flow** of a typical dynamometric roller brake tester. This mapping covers the physical, electromechanical, and computational subsystems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Wheel Mounted on Roller Pair] --> B[Motor Driven Contra Rotating Rollers]
    B --> C[Brake Pedal Applied by Driver]
    C --> D[Brake Calliper Clamps on Disc or Drum]
    D --> E[Retarding Torque Transmitted to Roller Shaft]
    E --> F[Strain Gauge Bridge on Roller Arm]
    F --> G[Analog Signal Amplifier and Filter]
    G --> H[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    H --> I[Microprocessor Based Data Acquisition Unit]
    I --> J[Brake Force Calculation Engine]
    I --> K[Brake Imbalance Computation Module]
    I --> L[Ovality Measurement Subsystem]
    J --> M[Digital Display Unit and Printer]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Result Storage and Cloud Upload to VIC Database]
    N --> O[Pass or Fail Decision as per CMVR Threshold]
```

### 4.2 Comparison Flow: Selection Logic for Brake Tester Type

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Vehicle Arrives at Inspection Bay] --> B{Vehicle Category}
    B -->|Two Wheeler| C[Roller Tester Single Wheel]
    B -->|Passenger Car LMV| D[Rear Roller Tester with Pit]
    B -->|Heavy Commercial Vehicle| E[Plate Type Tester]
    B -->|Laboratory R and D| F[Inertia Flywheel Tester]
    B -->|Fleet Road Audit| G[Decelerometer Method]
    C --> H[Generate Test Report]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Compliance Check}
    I -->|Meets CMVR| J[Issue Fitness Certificate]
    I -->|Fails| K[Rectify and Retest]
```

### 4.3 Signal Conditioning & Calibration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Strain Gauge Output mV] --> A2[Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000]
    A2 --> A3[Low Pass Filter 50 Hz Cutoff]
    A3 --> A4[16 Bit ADC Sampling at 1 kHz]
    A4 --> A5[Digital Filtering Moving Average]
    A5 --> A6[Calibration Constant Applied kN per V]
    A6 --> A7[Brake Force Reading kN]
    A7 --> A8[Compare with Threshold and Flag Result]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation Note for Students:** The strain gauge signal is in **millivolts (mV)** — three orders of magnitude below the ADC input range. The instrumentation amplifier provides the gain, and the calibration constant converts voltage to force. Understanding this signal chain is essential for **KTU instrumentation-based viva questions**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Define a roller-type brake tester and list its main components.*

**Model Answer:**
A roller-type brake tester is a stationary diagnostic device used to measure the braking force of a vehicle's wheels by placing them on motor-driven, contra-rotating rollers. Its main components are:
(i) **Roller pair** (driven by an electric motor),
(ii) **Load cell / strain gauge assembly** to measure the reactive force,
(iii) **Signal conditioner and ADC** for data acquisition,
(iv) **Microprocessor / controller** for computation, and
(v) **Digital display and printer** for output. The system enables measurement of brake force, efficiency, and imbalance without a road test.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*What is brake imbalance? What is the permissible limit as per ECE R13?*

**Model Answer:**
Brake imbalance is the percentage difference in braking force between the left and right wheels on the same axle, calculated as $\Delta F = \vert F_L - F_R \vert / (F_L + F_R) \times 100$. As per **ECE R13**, the permissible imbalance limit is **30 %** for the service brake on any axle. Excessive imbalance causes **vehicle pull to one side** during braking, increasing accident risk.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Roller-Type Brake Tester Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 (Expected Pattern)]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the construction and working of a **roller-type brake tester**. List the parameters it can measure. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A four-wheeled passenger car is tested on a roller brake tester. The recorded brake forces are: FL = 2.8 kN, FR = 2.6 kN, RL = 1.8 kN, RR = 2.0 kN. The gross vehicle weight is 10 kN. Calculate: (i) Total brake force, (ii) Brake efficiency, (iii) Front axle imbalance, (iv) Rear axle imbalance. Comment on whether the vehicle passes the CMVR test. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

The roller-type brake tester consists of a **roller pair** for each wheel, driven by an electric motor through a gear train. The vehicle is positioned such that the test wheel rests on the rollers. When the brake is applied, the brake friction torque opposes the rotation of the rollers. This opposition is transmitted to the **strain gauges** bonded on the roller support arms. The strain gauge output is amplified, digitized, and processed by a microcontroller. The tangential force $F_b$ is computed as $F_b = T / r_{roller}$, where $T$ is the measured torque and $r_{roller}$ is the roller radius.

The parameters measured are: **(i) Brake force per wheel, (ii) Total brake force, (iii) Brake efficiency, (iv) Brake imbalance per axle, (v) Drum ovality, and (vi) Drag force (residual brake friction when pedal is released).** The tester also features a digital display, printer, and PC interface for record-keeping.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Total Brake Force**

We sum the brake forces from all four wheels. The unit is converted to Newtons.

$$
F_{total} = 2800 + 2600 + 1800 + 2000 = 9200 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Brake Efficiency**

Brake efficiency is the ratio of total brake force to gross vehicle weight, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta = \frac{9200}{10000} \times 100 = 92 \text{ \%}
$$

**(iii) Front Axle Imbalance**

We compute the imbalance using the absolute difference between left and right forces divided by their sum.

$$
\Delta F_{front} = \frac{\vert 2800 - 2600 \vert}{2800 + 2600} \times 100 = \frac{200}{5400} \times 100 = 3.70 \text{ \%}
$$

**(iv) Rear Axle Imbalance**

We apply the same formula to the rear axle.

$$
\Delta F_{rear} = \frac{\vert 1800 - 2000 \vert}{1800 + 2000} \times 100 = \frac{200}{3800} \times 100 = 5.26 \text{ \%}
$$

**Conclusion:** The brake efficiency is **92 %**, far exceeding the 50 % CMVR threshold. Both front and rear axle imbalances (3.70 % and 5.26 %) are well below the 30 % ECE R13 limit. **The vehicle passes the brake test comfortably.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice: Comparative Tester Analysis**

**(a)** Compare **roller-type**, **plate-type**, and **decelerometer-type** brake testers. State two advantages and two limitations of each. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A deceleration test on a vehicle of mass 1500 kg shows a peak deceleration of 6.5 m/s² from a speed of 50 km/h. Calculate: (i) Equivalent brake force, (ii) Brake efficiency, (iii) Stopping distance, (iv) Kinetic energy dissipated. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Roller-Type Tester:** Advantages — (1) Highly accurate, (2) Can measure per-wheel force. Limitations — (1) Expensive, (2) Requires precise alignment.

**Plate-Type Tester:** Advantages — (1) Suitable for heavy multi-axle vehicles, (2) Quick test cycle. Limitations — (1) Measures only longitudinal force, (2) Surface friction affects results.

**Decelerometer Tester:** Advantages — (1) Simple and portable, (2) Reflects real-road conditions. Limitations — (1) Driver-skill dependent, (2) No per-wheel data.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Equivalent Brake Force**

By Newton's second law, the equivalent brake force equals mass times deceleration.

$$
F = m \times a = 1500 \times 6.5 = 9750 \text{ N}
$$

**(ii) Brake Efficiency**

The vehicle weight is mass times gravitational acceleration.

$$
W = 1500 \times 9.81 = 14715 \text{ N}
$$

Brake efficiency is the ratio of brake force to weight expressed as a percentage.

$$
\eta = \frac{9750}{14715} \times 100 = 66.26 \text{ \%}
$$

**(iii) Stopping Distance**

First, the initial speed is converted to SI units.

$$
v = 50 \times \frac{5}{18} = 13.89 \text{ m/s}
$$

Using the third equation of motion, the stopping distance is computed.

$$
s = \frac{v^{2}}{2 a} = \frac{(13.89)^{2}}{2 \times 6.5} = \frac{192.93}{13.00} = 14.84 \text{ m}
$$

**(iv) Kinetic Energy Dissipated**

The kinetic energy at the start equals the work done by brakes.

$$
KE = \frac{1}{2} m v^{2} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times (13.89)^{2}
$$

$$
KE = 750 \times 192.93 = 1.447 \times 10^{5} \text{ J} = 144.7 \text{ kJ}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert kN to N** before summing brake forces — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing static and dynamic weights** — CMVR uses static gross weight; using dynamic transfer gives a wrong (higher) efficiency.
> 3. **Imbalance formula sign error** — always use the **absolute value** in the numerator.
> 4. **Mixing up roller radius and tire radius** — the force formula $F = T / r$ uses the **roller radius**, not the wheel radius.
> 5. **Not stating units explicitly** — KTU deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit in numerical answers.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Brake Tester Definition:** A diagnostic device that measures vehicle braking performance statically (roller/plate) or dynamically (decelerometer/inertia).
- **Roller-Type Tester** is the **industry standard** for periodic inspection under CMVR and IS 11852.
- **Brake Force Formula:** $F_b = \mu \times N$ (per wheel) and $F_{total} = \sum F_{b,i}$ (vehicle).
- **Brake Efficiency:** $\eta = (F_{total} / W_g) \times 100$ — threshold ≥ 50 % under CMVR.
- **Brake Imbalance:** $\Delta F = \vert F_L - F_R \vert / (F_L + F_R) \times 100$ — limit ≤ 30 % (ECE R13).
- **Ovality:** $O = (D_{max} - D_{min}) / D_{max} \times 100$ — acceptable below 0.5 %, machinable up to 1 %.
- **Strain Gauge Output** is in millivolts; an **instrumentation amplifier** is essential.
- **Decelerometer Method** yields equivalent deceleration $a = F/m$ and can back-calculate efficiency.
- **Inertia Flywheel Testers** measure $KE = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ for component-level brake R&D.
- **Plate-Type Testers** dominate the heavy commercial vehicle segment due to high axle loads.
- **Always convert km/h to m/s** (multiply by 5/18) before using kinematic equations.
- **Static GVW** (not dynamic) is the legal denominator for CMVR brake efficiency.
- **Signal chain order:** Strain Gauge → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → Microcontroller → Display.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Head Light Alignment Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Head Light Alignment Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Head Light Alignment Testing** is a standardized photometric and geometric evaluation procedure mandated by automotive homologation regulations (AIS-008 / ECE R112) to verify that the headlamp of a motor vehicle emits light of the correct **luminous intensity (candela)**, **spatial distribution (beam pattern)**, and **geometric aim direction** without causing unacceptable glare to oncoming traffic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT633 Module 1), this topic covers the testing of **Driving Beam (High Beam)** and **Passing Beam (Low Beam)** headlamps, the aim preparation on a vertical aiming screen, the interpretation of the **cut-off line**, and the measurement of illumination in **lux** at specified reference points on a 25 m or 10 m test screen.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine standing in a dark room holding a torch (flashlight). If the torch is pointed straight at a wall 10 meters away, you see a bright circle (the *hot spot*). Now imagine you are driving a car at night. The **hot spot** on the road in front of you must fall at the **right spot** — not too far (you can't see far ahead), not too near (you outrun your own light), and not too high (you blind oncoming drivers).

**Headlight alignment testing** is essentially:
1. **Shooting a known light pattern** at a calibrated wall,
2. **Measuring where the brightest patch lands**, and
3. **Checking how bright (in lux)** it is at specific grid points.

The wall is called an **Aiming Screen**, and the bright patch is called the **Hot Spot**. The whole exercise ensures that the **car's "eyes" are pointed exactly where they should be** — far enough ahead, slightly down and to the right (for right-hand-drive countries like India), so the road is lit but other drivers are not dazzled.

### 1.3 Governing Standards and Indian Regulation Context

| Standard | Scope |
| :--- | :--- |
| **AIS-008 (Rev. 1)** | Indian standard for headlamp performance, equivalent to ECE R112. |
| **ECE R112** | UN regulation for asymmetric passing beam and driving beam headlamps. |
| **FMVSS 108** | US Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard (referenced for comparison). |
| **AIS-010** | Provisions concerning approval of headlamps with gas-discharge (HID) sources. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Photometric Units You Must Memorize:**
> - **Candela (cd):** SI unit of *luminous intensity* — light power per solid angle.
> - **Lumen (lm):** Total light *flux* emitted by a source.
> - **Lux (lx):** *Illuminance* — lumens per square metre ($\text{lm/m}^2$).

### 1.4 Test Environment Parameters

For a valid test under AIS-008:
- **Test distance:** $d = 25 \text{ m}$ (reference) or $d = 10 \text{ m}$ (scaled aiming).
- **Dark room:** Ambient illumination $< 1 \text{ lux}$.
- **Supply voltage:** $13.2 \text{ V}$ (for 12 V systems) ± 0.1 V.
- **Stabilisation time:** Lamps lit for **30 minutes** before measurement.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Headlamp Beams

A modern vehicle is fitted with **two distinct beams**, each tested separately:

1. **Driving Beam (High / Main Beam):**
   - Symmetrical, narrow, far-reaching.
   - Hot spot centred directly ahead on the H-H axis.
   - Used on open highways with no oncoming traffic.

2. **Passing Beam (Dipped / Low Beam):**
   - Asymmetrical with a horizontal **cut-off line**.
   - Right side of the beam (in RHD countries) is thrown farther; left side is depressed to avoid glare.
   - Contains a characteristic **"elbow"** at the cut-off.

### 2.2 Key Beam Geometry Terminology

| Term | Definition |
| :--- | :--- |
| **H–V Line** | Horizontal line at the **same height** as the centre of the headlamp lens (reference axis origin). |
| **H–H Line** | Horizontal reference line, usually **0.85 m** above the ground (or at lamp centre height $h$ above ground). |
| **V–V Line** | Vertical line passing through the headlamp centre. |
| **Cut-off Line** | Sharp horizontal boundary between the bright lower zone and dark upper zone of the passing beam. |
| **Elbow Point** | Bend in the cut-off line, typically $15^\circ$ from the V–V axis (asymmetric passing beam). |
| **Hot Spot** | Point of maximum illuminance (peak lux) within the beam pattern. |

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Logic of the Aiming Operation

The aiming logic proceeds in five linked logical steps:

- **Step 1 — Reference Datum:** The lamp is set so its reference axis is horizontal and parallel to the vehicle longitudinal axis when the vehicle is on a level floor with specified tyre pressure and one person (75 kg) on the driver's seat.

- **Step 2 — Screen Setup:** A vertical aiming screen is placed at the reference distance (10 m or 25 m). The **H-line** on the screen is drawn at the same height as the lamp centre.

- **Step 3 — Vertical Aim of Driving Beam:** The hot spot of the **driving beam** must lie at the **H–V intersection** (i.e., dead centre of the aiming screen).

- **Step 4 — Vertical Aim of Passing Beam:** The **cut-off line** of the passing beam must lie:
  - **0.4 m below** the H-line (at 25 m screen), OR
  - Equivalent scaled depression at 10 m screen: **0.16 m below H-line**.
  - This corresponds to a depression angle of approximately **$0.91^\circ$** below horizontal.

- **Step 5 — Lateral Aim:** The elbow of the cut-off must lie on the **V-line** for symmetric systems, or be displaced to the right by the specified amount for asymmetric systems.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are **examinable** in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE.

$$
\boxed{\,E \,=\, \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}\,}
$$

The **inverse square law of illumination**, where $E$ = illuminance in lux, $I$ = luminous intensity in candela, $d$ = distance in metres.

$$
\boxed{\,I_{\text{reference}} \,=\, E_{\text{measured}} \times d^{2}\,}
$$

Re-arranged form used when measuring lux on screen and computing the effective source intensity.

$$
\boxed{\,\theta_{\text{depression}} \,=\, \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{h_{\text{lamp}} - h_{\text{cut-off}}}{D}\right)\,}
$$

Depression angle of the passing beam, where $h_{\text{lamp}}$ = mounting height, $h_{\text{cut-off}}$ = cut-off height on screen, $D$ = screen distance.

$$
\boxed{\,E_{10}\,=\,E_{25}\times\!\left(\dfrac{25}{10}\right)^{2}\,=\,6.25\times E_{25}\,}
$$

Conversion between 10 m screen illuminance and 25 m screen illuminance (since intensity is constant, distance squared scales the illuminance).

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Typical Value |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Driving beam reference intensity | $I_{\max}$ | $\text{cd}$ | $\leq 215{,}000$ |
| Driving beam max illuminance at HV | $E_{HV}$ | $\text{lx}$ | $\geq 32$ (at 25 m) |
| Passing beam max in zone B50L | $E_{B50L}$ | $\text{lx}$ | $\leq 0.4$ (anti-glare) |
| Lamp mounting height | $h$ | $\text{m}$ | $0.5 - 1.2$ |
| Test distance | $D$ | $\text{m}$ | 10 or 25 |
| Depression angle | $\theta$ | $\text{deg}$ | $0.5 - 1.0$ |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Headlight alignment is critical for:
- **Road safety:** Misaligned beams cause ~3% of night-time accidents (NHTSA data).
- **Legal compliance:** Vehicles failing aim test are rejected at fitness centres.
- **Driver comfort:** Proper aim reduces eye fatigue on long drives.
- **ADAS calibration:** Modern matrix-LED systems require sub-degree accuracy.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between 10 m and 25 m Test Screens

The headlamp emits a fixed **luminous intensity $I$** (in candela) in any given direction — this is a property of the lamp and is independent of distance. However, the **illuminance $E$** measured on a screen depends on distance via the inverse square law.

**Starting equation (photometric inverse square law):**

$$
E \,=\, \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}
$$

**Step 1 — Write illuminance at 10 m screen:**

$$
E_{10} \,=\, \dfrac{I}{10^{2}} \,=\, \dfrac{I}{100}
$$

**Step 2 — Write illuminance at 25 m screen (same lamp, same direction):**

$$
E_{25} \,=\, \dfrac{I}{25^{2}} \,=\, \dfrac{I}{625}
$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio $E_{10} / E_{25}$:**

$$
\dfrac{E_{10}}{E_{25}} \,=\, \dfrac{I/100}{I/625}
$$

**Step 4 — Cancel the common intensity $I$:**

$$
\dfrac{E_{10}}{E_{25}} \,=\, \dfrac{625}{100}
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the fraction:**

$$
\dfrac{E_{10}}{E_{25}} \,=\, 6.25
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $E_{10}$ in terms of $E_{25}$:**

$$
\boxed{\,E_{10} \,=\, 6.25 \times E_{25}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** A lux reading taken on a 10 m screen will be **6.25 times brighter** than the same beam read at 25 m, because the screen is closer to the source. Therefore, all regulatory limits specified at 25 m must be **multiplied by 6.25** when comparing with 10 m measurements.

### 3.2 Derivation: Required Cut-Off Depression on the Aiming Screen

For a vehicle with **lamp centre height $h$** above the ground, the test regulation requires the passing beam's cut-off to fall **0.4 m below the H-line on a 25 m screen**. We derive the equivalent depression angle.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.** The lamp is at height $h$ above the ground. The H-line on the screen is drawn at the **same height as the lamp centre**. The cut-off on the screen lies **0.4 m below H-line**, so vertically it is at height $(h - 0.4)$ above the ground.

**Step 2 — Horizontal distance to the screen.**

$$
D \,=\, 25 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 3 — Vertical drop from H-line to cut-off position.**

$$
\Delta h \,=\, 0.4 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the depression angle (small-angle approximation valid because $\Delta h \ll D$):**

$$
\tan\theta \,=\, \dfrac{\Delta h}{D} \,=\, \dfrac{0.4}{25}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute numerical value:**

$$
\tan\theta \,=\, 0.016
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to degrees:**

$$
\theta \,=\, \tan^{-1}(0.016) \,=\, 0.9167^\circ \approx 0.92^\circ
$$

**Step 7 — Final result:**

$$
\boxed{\,\theta \,\approx\, 0.92^\circ \text{ below horizontal}\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Equivalent depression at 10 m screen (proportional scaling):**

$$
\Delta h_{10} \,=\, 0.4 \times \dfrac{10}{25} \,=\, 0.16 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\theta_{10} \,=\, \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{0.16}{10}\right) \,=\, 0.92^\circ \text{ (same angle, as expected)}
$$

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A headlamp is tested at 10 m from an aiming screen. The lux meter placed at the **H-V point** reads $E_{HV} = 200 \text{ lux}$. Determine: (a) The luminous intensity of the driving beam at that point. (b) The equivalent reading if the same test were done at 25 m.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Intensity at H-V:**

Using the inverse square law:

$$
I_{HV} \,=\, E_{HV} \times d^{2}
$$

$$
I_{HV} \,=\, 200 \times 10^{2} \,=\, 200 \times 100
$$

$$
\boxed{\,I_{HV} \,=\, 20{,}000 \text{ cd}\,}
$$

This is within the typical driving beam range of **15,000 – 215,000 cd**, so the lamp passes.

**Part (b) — Equivalent 25 m reading:**

Using the conversion relation derived above:

$$
E_{25} \,=\, \dfrac{E_{10}}{6.25} \,=\, \dfrac{200}{6.25}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{25} \,=\, 32 \text{ lux}\,}
$$

This matches the **ECE R112 minimum of 32 lx** at HV for driving beam — confirming the lamp is regulation-compliant at standard distance.

### 3.4 Analytical Compliance Matrix (Key Test Points)

| Test Point | Location on Screen | Limit (25 m) | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **HV** | Centre | $\geq 32 \text{ lx}$ | Hot spot of driving beam |
| **B50L** | 50 m equivalent left | $\leq 0.4 \text{ lx}$ | Anti-glare for oncoming driver (LHD oncoming = our right in RHD) |
| **75R** | 75 m right of HV | $\geq 12 \text{ lx}$ | Road illumination ahead-right |
| **50R** | 50 m right of HV | $\geq 12 \text{ lx}$ | Lane marking visibility |
| **50V** | 50 m below HV | $\geq 6 \text{ lx}$ | Close-range road illumination |
| **25L** | 25 m left of HV | $\geq 2 \text{ lx}$ | Pedestrian / shoulder visibility |

> [!NOTE]
> The reference numbers (e.g., **B50L**, **75R**, **50V**) correspond to **real road distances** when projected from the headlamp through the test geometry — not screen distances.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Test Setup Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Vehicle on Level Test Track] --> B[Apply Spec Tyre Pressure 0.2 MPa]
    B --> C[Place 75 kg Driver Load]
    C --> D[Position Aiming Screen at 10 m or 25 m]
    D --> E[Mark HV Reference Point on Screen]
    E --> F[Stabilise Lamps for 30 min at 13.2 V]
    F --> G[Switch ON Driving Beam]
    G --> H[Measure Lux at HV Point]
    H --> I{Is HV >= 32 lx at 25 m?}
    I -- Yes --> J[Driving Beam Pass]
    I -- No --> K[Driving Beam Fail]
    J --> L[Switch OFF Driving Beam]
    L --> M[Switch ON Passing Beam]
    M --> N[Locate Cut-Off Line]
    N --> O{Meets Depression of 0.92 deg?}
    O -- Yes --> P[Locate Elbow Point]
    O -- No --> Q[Adjust Headlamp Screws]
    Q --> M
    P --> R{Meets Zone B50L <= 0.4 lx?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Passing Beam Pass]
    R -- No --> T[Passing Beam Fail]
```

### 4.2 Aiming Screen Reference Layout (Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    block:ScreenTop["Screen Zone Top Row"]
        A1["HV 0 lx ref"] A2["HV ref cross"] A3["Symmetry check"]
    end
    block:ScreenMid["Screen Zone Mid Row"]
        B1["75R >= 12 lx"] B2["50R >= 12 lx"] B3["25L >= 2 lx"]
    end
    block:ScreenBot["Screen Zone Bot Row"]
        C1["50V >= 6 lx"] C2["B50L <= 0.4 lx"] C3["Elbow on VV line"]
    end
```

### 4.3 Beam Pattern Schematic (Asymmetric Passing Beam)

```
            V-V line  H-H line
                |       |
                |   *   |    <-- Hot Spot (H-V intersection)
                |       |
   <-- LEFT     |       |    RIGHT -->
                |_______|
   ___________/         \___________
  /  DARK ZONE          LIT ZONE    \
 /   (anti-glare)       (road)        \
/____________________________________  \
       Cut-off line (with elbow at VV)
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **horizontal cut-off line** lies **0.4 m below H-H line** at 25 m screen.
- The **elbow point** lies on the **V-V vertical line**.
- The **right side** (passing beam throws farther right) is the **bright zone** that illuminates the driver's own lane.
- The **left side** is depressed to avoid dazzling oncoming traffic.

### 4.4 Beam Type Comparison Diagram (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DB["Driving Beam High Beam"]
        DB1["Symmetrical cone"]
        DB2["Hot spot at HV"]
        DB3["Range 100 m plus"]
    end
    subgraph PB["Passing Beam Low Beam"]
        PB1["Asymmetric L shape"]
        PB2["Cut-off with elbow"]
        PB3["Range 60 to 80 m ahead"]
    end
    SW[Headlamp Switch] --> DB
    SW --> PB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> When studying for KTU ESE, the **most commonly drawn diagram** is the aiming screen with the **cut-off line** clearly marked below the H-H line and the **elbow** on the V-V line. Practise drawing this — it carries easy marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define "cut-off line" and "elbow point" in the context of a passing beam headlamp. Why is the elbow placed on the V-V vertical axis of the aiming screen?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Cut-off line (1 mark):** The sharp horizontal boundary separating the illuminated lower zone from the dark upper zone of an asymmetric passing beam pattern.
- **Elbow point (1 mark):** The bend in the cut-off line where the horizontal portion transitions into the $15^\circ$ upward inclined segment toward the driving lane.
- **Reason for placement on V-V axis (1 mark):** It ensures the passing beam throws maximum light to the driver's own lane (right side in RHD countries) while keeping the oncoming driver's lane depressed, preventing glare. The elbow position defines the lateral aim of the lamp.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is the headlamp aim test performed with a stabilised voltage of 13.2 V rather than the nominal 12 V battery voltage?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- During operation, the **alternator charges the battery** and supplies a steady voltage of approximately **13.2 – 14.4 V** (1 mark).
- The light output of a tungsten-halogen bulb is highly sensitive to voltage — a 10% increase in voltage roughly doubles the luminous flux (1 mark).
- Therefore, testing at the **actual operating voltage (13.2 V)** simulates real driving conditions and ensures compliance is verified under realistic flux output (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students commonly lose 2–3 marks by:
> - Confusing **driving beam aim (hot spot at HV)** with **passing beam aim (cut-off below HH)**.
> - Forgetting the **6.25× conversion factor** between 10 m and 25 m screens.
> - Drawing the **cut-off above** the H-H line instead of **below** it.
> - Skipping units in final numerical answers.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **headlamp aiming screen test procedure** as per AIS-008 for verifying the passing beam pattern. Explain the significance of test points **B50L**, **75R**, and **50R** on the screen.

**(b) [7 Marks]** During a headlamp test at **10 m screen distance**, the lux meter reads:
- At point HV: **180 lux**
- At point B50L: **0.7 lux**
- At point 75R: **70 lux**

Determine, with calculations, whether the headlamp **complies** with AIS-008 / ECE R112 regulations (use the 6.25 conversion factor and the limits given in Section 3.4).

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Aiming Screen Test Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Test Preparation (2 marks):**
   - Vehicle placed on level floor; tyre pressure set to manufacturer's specification (typically 0.2 MPa).
   - Driver's seat loaded with 75 kg (or per regulation).
   - Voltage stabilised at 13.2 V; lamps run for 30 min before measurement.
   - Dark room or darkened aiming screen tunnel.

2. **Screen Setup and Aim (2 marks):**
   - Aiming screen placed at 10 m or 25 m perpendicular to vehicle longitudinal axis.
   - V-V vertical line drawn through headlamp centre.
   - H-H horizontal line drawn at the same height as the lamp centre.
   - For **passing beam**, the cut-off line should lie **0.4 m below H-H line** at 25 m (or 0.16 m at 10 m).

3. **Significance of Test Points (3 marks):**
   - **B50L (50 m left of V-V at eye height of oncoming driver):** Must read $\leq 0.4$ lux to **prevent glare** to oncoming LHD traffic. In RHD, this is the driver's lane side but still kept low for safety in the case of wrong-side driving.
   - **75R (75 m ahead on the right lane):** Must read $\geq 12$ lux to ensure **adequate road illumination** for distant obstacles, signs, and lane markings on the driver's own lane.
   - **50R (50 m ahead on the right):** Must read $\geq 12$ lux to ensure **mid-range visibility** of road curvature and pedestrians.

*Sketch (to be drawn by student):*
```
   H-H ---------------------------------------------------
       |          |                |
       |   75R    |       50R      |     25L
       |          |                |
   0.4 m drop below H-H:
       |          |                |
   ---CUT-OFF -- elbow ----- CUT-OFF CONTINUES ----------- (asymmetric, droops left)
       |   50V    |     B50L       |
       |          |                |
       V-V
```

**Part (b) — Numerical Compliance Check (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert all measured lux to 25 m equivalent** using $E_{25} = E_{10} / 6.25$ (1 mark).

**At HV:**
$$
E_{HV,25} = \dfrac{180}{6.25} = 28.8 \text{ lx}
$$
**Limit:** $\geq 32 \text{ lx}$ → **FAILS** (28.8 < 32) — *Not compliant at HV.* (1 mark)

**At B50L:**
$$
E_{B50L,25} = \dfrac{0.7}{6.25} = 0.112 \text{ lx}
$$
**Limit:** $\leq 0.4 \text{ lx}$ → **PASSES** (0.112 < 0.4) — *Glare limit OK.* (1 mark)

**At 75R:**
$$
E_{75R,25} = \dfrac{70}{6.25} = 11.2 \text{ lx}
$$
**Limit:** $\geq 12 \text{ lx}$ → **FAILS** (11.2 < 12) — *Not compliant at 75R.* (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Tabulated Result (2 marks):**

| Test Point | Measured at 10 m (lx) | Equivalent at 25 m (lx) | Regulation Limit | Result |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| HV | 180 | 28.8 | $\geq 32$ | **FAIL** |
| B50L | 0.7 | 0.112 | $\leq 0.4$ | **PASS** |
| 75R | 70 | 11.2 | $\geq 12$ | **FAIL** |

**Step 3 — Conclusion (1 mark):**
The headlamp **does not comply** with AIS-008 because it fails on **two out of three** test points (HV and 75R). The lamp's driving beam intensity is **insufficient** and requires re-aiming or replacement.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **inverse square law of illumination**. Derive the conversion factor between a **10 m test screen** and a **25 m test screen** for headlamp photometric measurements. Why is this conversion necessary?

**(b) [7 Marks]** A vehicle has headlamps mounted at a height of **0.75 m** above the road. During a passing beam test on a **25 m screen**, the cut-off line falls **0.4 m below the H-H line**. Calculate:
1. The depression angle of the passing beam.
2. The expected cut-off drop (in mm) on a **10 m screen** for the same beam.
3. Comment on whether the aim is correct as per AIS-008.

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Inverse Square Law (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Statement of the Law (1 mark):**
The illuminance $E$ produced on a surface by a point source of luminous intensity $I$ is **inversely proportional to the square of the distance** $d$ between the source and the surface.

**Step 2 — Mathematical Form (1 mark):**
$$
E = \dfrac{I}{d^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Derivation of the 6.25 conversion factor (3 marks):** *(Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for full derivation.)* The ratio $E_{10}/E_{25} = (25/10)^2 = 6.25$. Therefore, $E_{10} = 6.25 \times E_{25}$.

**Step 4 — Necessity of Conversion (2 marks):**
- The AIS-008 / ECE R112 specifies lux limits at the **reference distance of 25 m**.
- Many testing stations use a shorter **10 m screen** for practical reasons (space, cost).
- Because illuminance varies with distance, raw 10 m readings must be mathematically scaled to 25 m equivalent values before comparison with regulatory limits.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**(i) Depression angle (3 marks):**

$$
\tan\theta = \dfrac{0.4}{25} = 0.016
$$

$$
\theta = \tan^{-1}(0.016) = 0.917^\circ \approx 0.92^\circ
$$

*(Note: The lamp height of 0.75 m is irrelevant here because the H-H line is drawn at the lamp's own centre height — the depression is measured from the H-H reference, not the ground.)*

**(ii) Expected cut-off drop on 10 m screen (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta h_{10} = 0.4 \times \dfrac{10}{25} = 0.16 \text{ m} = 160 \text{ mm}
$$

**(iii) Comment on aim (2 marks):**
The depression of **0.92°** matches the AIS-008 specification of approximately **0.91°** below horizontal. Hence, the **vertical aim of the passing beam is correct**. The aim complies with the regulation.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE:**

- ☐ **Photometric units:** Candela (cd) = intensity; Lux (lx) = illuminance = $\text{lm/m}^2$; Lumen (lm) = flux.
- ☐ **Inverse square law:** $E = I/d^2$ — always the first equation to write.
- ☐ **10 m ↔ 25 m conversion:** Multiply by **6.25** (or divide by 6.25 for reverse).
- ☐ **Driving beam aim:** Hot spot at **H-V intersection**; $\geq 32$ lx at 25 m.
- ☐ **Passing beam aim:** Cut-off **0.4 m below H-H line** at 25 m screen (= 160 mm at 10 m screen).
- ☐ **Depression angle:** Approximately **$0.92^\circ$** below horizontal.
- ☐ **Test voltage:** $13.2$ V stabilised; lamp warm-up $30$ minutes.
- ☐ **Test distance:** Reference $25$ m (regulatory); practical $10$ m (with conversion).
- ☐ **Cut-off line:** Horizontal sharp boundary in passing beam with **elbow on V-V axis**.
- ☐ **B50L:** Critical anti-glare point; must read $\leq 0.4$ lx (often fails in real testing).
- ☐ **Asymmetric beam:** Right side (driver's lane) is brighter and thrown farther.
- ☐ **Vehicle prep:** Tyre pressure, 75 kg driver load, level floor — all affect aim.
- ☐ **Elbow angle:** Standard $15^\circ$ inclination from V-V line on the cut-off.

**Mnemonic for beam-test points: "Big Beams Love 75 Road-marking 50 Vision":**
- **B**50L — Big anti-glare zone (left)
- **B**eam — HV — central hot spot
- **L**ong range — 75R
- **R**oad marking — 50R
- **V**ision — 50V

This completes the KTU 2024 Scheme study notes for **Head Light Alignment Testing** under Module 1 of PEMUT633.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Brake Testing — The Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing (PEMUT633)**, the **Air and Hydraulic Brake Test** refers to the standardized experimental procedures employed to evaluate the **braking performance** of a road vehicle, specifically quantifying the **brake efficiency**, **stopping distance**, **deceleration**, **brake fade**, and **brake balance** of vehicles equipped with either **pneumatic (air) braking systems** or **hydraulic braking systems**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT633 – Module 1):**
> Brake testing is a measurement procedure conducted to determine the ability of a vehicle's braking system to reduce speed or bring the vehicle to a halt under specified test conditions, in accordance with standards such as **IS 11852**, **AIS-052**, and **ECE R13 / R13-H**.

The two principal categories studied are:

| Braking System | Energy Transmission Medium | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Hydraulic Brake** | Incompressible fluid (DOT 3/4 brake fluid) | Passenger cars, two-wheelers, LCVs |
| **Air (Pneumatic) Brake** | Compressed air (typically 6–8 bar) | Heavy commercial vehicles (HCVs), buses, trucks |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bicycle Hand Brake" Intuition

Imagine you are riding a bicycle down a hill. The brake lever (your hand) squeezes a cable (or fluid column in a car) which transmits the squeezing force to a friction pad pressed against the wheel rim. The harder you squeeze, the more friction is generated, and the faster you slow down.

- In a **hydraulic system**, the "cable" is replaced by an **incompressible fluid** in a sealed pipe — like pressing one end of a filled water syringe and watching the other end pop out. The fluid cannot be compressed, so the force is transmitted almost instantly.
- In an **air brake system**, the "cable" is replaced by **compressed air** stored in a tank. When you press the brake pedal, a valve opens, sending pressurized air to diaphragm chambers that push the brake shoes against the drum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two systems?**
> Hydraulic brakes are **compact, precise, and self-energizing** — ideal for light vehicles. Air brakes are **powerful, fail-safe, and capable of braking multi-tonne trucks** — where hydraulic fluid leakage would be catastrophic. The fundamental physics of the **brake test** (efficiency, stopping distance, deceleration) is identical for both — only the actuation medium differs.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Test Metrics

The following constants and standards govern the analysis:

- **Standard test speed:** 60 km/h (typical), or 80 km/h for high-speed vehicles
- **Maximum acceptable stopping distance @ 60 km/h:** ≤ **18 m** (as per AIS norms)
- **Mean Fully Developed Deceleration (MFDD):** ≥ **5.0 m/s²** (mandatory ECE R13 limit)
- **Air pressure for HCV brake test:** 6.0 – 8.0 bar (≈ 600 – 800 kPa)
- **Hydraulic fluid pressure:** 4.0 – 10.0 MPa (depending on master cylinder bore)
- **Coefficient of friction (µ) for brake lining:** 0.30 – 0.45 (typical)
- **Dynamic loaded radius of tire (r_d):** used in speed-distance conversion

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** KTU questions frequently ask the student to compute the **braking efficiency** as a ratio of **actual deceleration to maximum possible deceleration** ($\mu \cdot g$). Memorize the formula in Section 2.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Objectives of an Air / Hydraulic Brake Test

The principal goals of conducting a brake test on a vehicle are:

1. **Quantification of Braking Force (F_b):** The tangential friction force generated at the tire-road contact patch.
2. **Determination of Braking Efficiency ($\eta_b$):** Ratio of actual to theoretical maximum braking performance.
3. **Measurement of Stopping Distance (S):** The distance traveled between brake application and complete halt.
4. **Evaluation of Brake Fade:** Loss of braking effectiveness due to heat build-up during repeated or prolonged braking.
5. **Assessment of Brake Balance:** Verification that braking effort is distributed correctly between front and rear axles.
6. **Compliance Verification:** Ensuring the vehicle meets statutory standards (AIS-052, ECE R13).

## 2.2 The Physics of Braking — Core Logic Steps

The braking process converts the **kinetic energy** of the moving vehicle into **thermal energy** (heat) through friction at the brake interface. The analytical chain is:

**Step 1 — Kinetic Energy of the Vehicle:**
The vehicle of mass $m$ moving at velocity $u$ possesses kinetic energy $KE = \frac{1}{2} m u^2$.

**Step 2 — Work Done by the Braking Force:**
Over a stopping distance $S$, the constant braking force $F_b$ does work $W = F_b \cdot S$.

**Step 3 — Energy Equivalence (Work-Energy Theorem):**
By conservation, $F_b \cdot S = \frac{1}{2} m u^2$, yielding $F_b = \dfrac{m u^2}{2 S}$.

**Step 4 — Deceleration Computation:**
From Newton's second law, $a = \dfrac{F_b}{m} = \dfrac{u^2}{2S}$.

**Step 5 — Braking Efficiency:**
The theoretical maximum deceleration is $\mu g$ (where $\mu$ is the tire-road friction coefficient). Hence:
$$\eta_b = \dfrac{a_{actual}}{\mu g} = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S \mu g}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?**
> The brake efficiency value tells a designer whether the brake hardware is being utilized to its theoretical maximum. Values **< 70%** indicate poor brake design, worn linings, or insufficient actuation force. Values **> 100%** indicate measurement error or that the vehicle is operating above the tire-road friction limit (which is physically impossible — the tires would lock up).

## 2.3 Air Brake System — Operational Logic

A typical **pneumatic (air) brake system** for heavy commercial vehicles uses a **dual-circuit** layout with the following components:

- **Air Compressor** — driven by the engine; compresses atmospheric air to ~10 bar.
- **Reservoir Tanks** — store compressed air at 6–8 bar working pressure.
- **Brake Pedal Valve (Foot Valve)** — modulates air delivery to brake chambers upon driver input.
- **Brake Chambers (Actuators)** — diaphragm-type cylinders that convert air pressure into mechanical linear force.
- **Brake Drums / Discs with Linings** — convert linear actuator force into friction torque.

**The Air Brake Actuation Equation:**
$$F_{push} = P_{air} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$$

where $P_{air}$ is the supply pressure and $A_{diaphragm}$ is the effective diaphragm area (typically 80 – 130 cm² for service brake chambers).

The mechanical advantage of the brake chamber and slack adjuster then multiplies this push force into the **clamping force** $F_c$ on the brake lining.

## 2.4 Hydraulic Brake System — Operational Logic

A typical **hydraulic brake system** operates on **Pascal's Law** — pressure applied to a confined fluid is transmitted undiminished in all directions.

- **Master Cylinder** — converts mechanical pedal force into hydraulic pressure.
- **Brake Lines / Hoses** — transmit pressurized fluid (DOT 3/4/5.1).
- **Wheel Cylinders (Drum) or Calipers (Disc)** — convert hydraulic pressure back into mechanical clamping force.

**Pascal's Law Application:**
$$P_{hydraulic} = \dfrac{F_{pedal}}{A_{master}} = \dfrac{F_{wheel}}{A_{wheel}}$$

Hence the **force multiplication** between pedal and wheel cylinder is:
$$F_{wheel} = F_{pedal} \cdot \left( \dfrac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}} \right)$$

For a tandem master cylinder with a bore ratio of 1:1 to 1:2, mechanical advantage of 4–6× is common.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Description | Standard Unit | Applicability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F_b = \dfrac{m u^2}{2 S}$ | Braking force from stopping distance | **N** | Both systems |
| 2 | $a = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S}$ | Vehicle deceleration | **m/s²** | Both systems |
| 3 | $\eta_b = \dfrac{a}{\mu g} = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S \mu g}$ | Braking efficiency | **dimensionless (%)** | Both systems |
| 4 | $P_{hyd} = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Hydraulic pressure (Pascal's Law) | **Pa** or **bar** | Hydraulic brakes |
| 5 | $F_{wheel} = F_{pedal} \cdot \dfrac{A_{wheel}}{A_{master}}$ | Hydraulic force multiplication | **N** | Hydraulic brakes |
| 6 | $F_{push} = P_{air} \cdot A_{diaphragm}$ | Air brake chamber push force | **N** | Air brakes |
| 7 | $MFDD = \dfrac{u_b^2 - u_e^2}{25.92 (S_e - S_b)}$ | Mean Fully Developed Deceleration | **m/s²** | ECE R13 standard |
| 8 | $\tau_{brake} = \mu \cdot F_c \cdot r_{drum}$ | Brake torque (drum brake) | **N·m** | Both systems |
| 9 | $P_{lin} = \dfrac{F_c \mu V}{A_{lin}}$ | Lining pressure (fade check) | **Pa** | Both systems |
| 10 | $S = \dfrac{u^2}{2 \mu g \eta_b}$ | Stopping distance (rearranged) | **m** | Both systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion reminder for exam:** $1 \text{ km/h} = \dfrac{5}{18} \text{ m/s}$. Speed in km/h must be converted to m/s **before** substituting into any of the above formulas.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Vehicle Design Validation:** Brake test data is used to validate FEA-simulated brake torque and thermal models.
- **Homologation & Certification:** Mandatory for type-approval under Indian AIS-052 / ECE R13 frameworks.
- **Maintenance & Diagnostics:** Periodic brake tests detect **brake fade**, **uneven wear**, and **hydraulic leaks** in service.
- **Active Safety Systems:** Modern ABS (Anti-lock Braking System) and EBD (Electronic Brake-force Distribution) tuning is benchmarked against baseline brake test results.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Stopping Distance from First Principles

**Problem Setup:** A vehicle of mass $m$ kg traveling at initial speed $u$ m/s is brought to rest over a distance $S$ m by a constant braking force $F_b$ N. Derive an expression for $F_b$, $S$, and the deceleration $a$.

**Step 1 — Initial and Final States:**
Let initial velocity $= u$ m/s, final velocity $v = 0$ m/s (vehicle halted).
The displacement during braking $= S$ m.

**Step 2 — Apply the Work-Energy Theorem:**
The work done by the net retarding force equals the change in kinetic energy:
$$W_{net} = \Delta KE$$
$$-F_b \cdot S = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 - \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2$$

**Step 3 — Substitute Final Condition $v = 0$:**
$$-F_b \cdot S = 0 - \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2$$
$$-F_b \cdot S = -\tfrac{1}{2} m u^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Braking Force:**
$$F_b \cdot S = \tfrac{1}{2} m u^2$$
$$\boxed{F_b = \dfrac{m u^2}{2 S}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law to obtain Deceleration:**
The net force on the vehicle is $-F_b$ (decelerating), so:
$$m a = -F_b \quad \Rightarrow \quad a = -\dfrac{F_b}{m} = -\dfrac{u^2}{2 S}$$

The magnitude of deceleration is:
$$\boxed{\vert a \vert = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S}}$$

**Step 6 — Express Stopping Distance in Terms of Initial Speed:**
Rearranging the deceleration equation:
$$\boxed{S = \dfrac{u^2}{2 a} = \dfrac{u^2}{2 \mu g \eta_b}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Notice that **stopping distance scales with the SQUARE of speed**. Doubling the speed quadruples the stopping distance — a critical safety insight in highway speed regulation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Braking Efficiency from Energy & Friction Limits

**Problem Setup:** Show that the braking efficiency is the ratio of the actual deceleration to the maximum achievable deceleration ($\mu g$).

**Step 1 — Identify the Friction Limit:**
The maximum friction force available at the tire-road interface is:
$$F_{friction,max} = \mu \cdot N = \mu \cdot m g$$
where $N = mg$ is the normal reaction on a level road.

**Step 2 — Maximum Possible Deceleration:**
From Newton's second law:
$$a_{max} = \dfrac{F_{friction,max}}{m} = \mu g$$

**Step 3 — Define Braking Efficiency as the Ratio:**
$$\eta_b = \dfrac{a_{actual}}{a_{max}} = \dfrac{a_{actual}}{\mu g}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Expression for Actual Deceleration:**
Using the result from Derivation 1, $a_{actual} = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S}$:
$$\eta_b = \dfrac{u^2 / 2S}{\mu g} = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S \mu g}$$

**Step 5 — Express in Percentage Form:**
$$\boxed{\eta_b \,(\%) = \dfrac{u^2}{2 S \mu g} \times 100}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hydraulic Force Multiplication in a Master-Slave Cylinder System

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic brake master cylinder has bore diameter $d_m$ and the wheel cylinder has bore diameter $d_w$. A pedal force $F_p$ is applied. Derive the wheel cylinder clamping force $F_w$.

**Step 1 — Master Cylinder Pressure from Pascal's Law:**
The pressure generated at the master cylinder outlet is:
$$P = \dfrac{F_p}{A_m} = \dfrac{4 F_p}{\pi d_m^2}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Pascal's Law to Wheel Cylinder:**
The same pressure acts on the wheel cylinder piston (incompressible fluid, no losses):
$$P = \dfrac{F_w}{A_w} = \dfrac{4 F_w}{\pi d_w^2}$$

**Step 3 — Equate and Eliminate Pressure:**
$$\dfrac{4 F_p}{\pi d_m^2} = \dfrac{4 F_w}{\pi d_w^2}$$
$$F_p \cdot d_w^2 = F_w \cdot d_m^2$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Wheel Cylinder Force:**
$$\boxed{F_w = F_p \cdot \left( \dfrac{d_w}{d_m} \right)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Mechanical Advantage Interpretation:**
If $d_w = 2 d_m$, then $F_w = 4 F_p$ — a 4× mechanical advantage, independent of fluid type.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Air Brake Chamber Push Force

**Step 1 — Pressure Multiplication at Compressor:**
A governor cuts off the compressor when reservoir pressure reaches 8 bar; cut-in is at 6 bar. The working pressure at the brake chamber is $P_{air}$.

**Step 2 — Push Rod Force Calculation:**
The effective area of a typical Type-20 brake chamber is $A_{dia}$:
$$F_{push} = P_{air} \cdot A_{dia}$$

For a Type-30 chamber (larger), $A_{dia} \approx 130 \text{ cm}^2 = 130 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$ and $P_{air} = 6 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$:
$$F_{push} = 6 \times 10^5 \times 130 \times 10^{-4} = 7800 \text{ N} \approx 7.8 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Total Wheel Clamping Force (with Slack Adjuster Leverage):**
The push rod acts on a lever (slack adjuster) of length $L_s$, which rotates the S-cam and applies clamping force $F_c$:
$$F_c = F_{push} \cdot \dfrac{L_s}{r_{cam}}$$
where $r_{cam}$ is the effective S-cam radius.

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — A Typical KTU Problem

**Problem:** A car of mass 1200 kg is braked from 90 km/h to rest over a distance of 38 m on a level road. The tire-road friction coefficient is 0.8. Compute: (a) the braking force, (b) the deceleration, and (c) the braking efficiency.

**Solution:**

**Given:** $m = 1200$ kg, $u = 90$ km/h, $S = 38$ m, $\mu = 0.8$, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units:**
$$u = 90 \times \dfrac{5}{18} = 25 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Braking Force (Part a):**
$$F_b = \dfrac{m u^2}{2 S} = \dfrac{1200 \times 25^2}{2 \times 38} = \dfrac{1200 \times 625}{76} = \dfrac{750000}{76}$$
$$\boxed{F_b \approx 9868.4 \text{ N} \approx 9.87 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Deceleration (Part b):**
$$a = \dfrac{u^2}{2S} = \dfrac{625}{76}$$
$$\boxed{a \approx 8.22 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**Step 4 — Braking Efficiency (Part c):**
$$\eta_b = \dfrac{a}{\mu g} = \dfrac{8.22}{0.8 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{8.22}{7.848}$$
$$\boxed{\eta_b \approx 1.0475 \text{ or } 104.75\%}$$

**Interpretation:** An efficiency > 100% is physically unrealistic. This indicates that the assumed $\mu = 0.8$ is too low for the actual road-tire interface, OR measurement error exists. The true $\mu$ can be back-calculated as $\mu = 8.22/9.81 \approx 0.838$.

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Air Brake System Test Rig

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the functional architecture of an air brake test setup, showing the path of compressed air from the compressor through the storage, regulation, and actuation stages, with telemetry feedback to the data acquisition system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Driven Compressor] --> B[Air Dryer and Governor]
    B --> C[Reservoir Tank 1 Four Bar]
    B --> D[Reservoir Tank 2 Six Bar]
    D --> E[Brake Pedal Foot Valve]
    C --> F[Spring Brake Parking Chamber]
    E --> G[Service Brake Chamber Front]
    E --> H[Service Brake Chamber Rear]
    G --> I[Front Brake Drum and Lining]
    H --> J[Rear Brake Drum and Lining]
    I --> K[Wheel Speed Sensor]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Data Acquisition System]
    F --> L
    L --> M[Brake Efficiency Report]
    L --> N[Stopping Distance Log]
    L --> O[Deceleration Time Curve]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The compressor charges the reservoirs; the foot valve modulates air to the service chambers; the spring brake chamber acts as the **parking / emergency** brake using stored spring force (fail-safe principle). Wheel speed sensors feed the data acquisition system to compute MFDD.

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Hydraulic Brake Test Setup

The following Mermaid diagram traces the hydraulic fluid path and signal flow for a typical hydraulic brake test on a passenger car dynamometer or road test.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brake Pedal Input] --> B[Vacuum Booster Servo]
    B --> C[Tandem Master Cylinder]
    C --> D[Brake Fluid Reservoir]
    C --> E[Front Left Wheel Caliper]
    C --> F[Front Right Wheel Caliper]
    C --> G[Rear Left Wheel Cylinder]
    C --> H[Rear Right Wheel Cylinder]
    E --> I[Front Disc Rotor]
    F --> I
    G --> J[Rear Drum Assembly]
    H --> J
    I --> K[Brake Torque Transducer]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Brake Efficiency Calculator]
    L --> M[Hydraulic Pressure Sensor Readout]
    L --> N[Stop Watch and Distance Marker]
    L --> O[Final Test Report]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pedal feel and servo action:** The vacuum booster multiplies the driver's foot force by 3–6× before it reaches the master cylinder. This is critical because modern disc brakes require clamping forces of 10–20 kN per wheel, which the human leg cannot directly produce.

## 4.3 Test Procedure Flowchart — Sequential Logic of a Brake Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Vehicle Preparation and Safety Check] --> B[Weigh Vehicle and Record Axle Load]
    B --> C[Install Speed Sensor and Decelerometer]
    C --> D[Warm Up Brakes to Operating Temperature]
    D --> E[Accelerate to Test Speed Sixty km per h]
    E --> F[Apply Brakes at Marked Trigger Point]
    F --> G[Record Stopping Distance S]
    G --> H[Record Deceleration Profile a of t]
    H --> I[Compute Brake Efficiency Eta]
    I --> J{Brake Efficiency Greater Than Seventy Percent?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Test Passes]
    J -->|No| L[Test Fails Inspect Brake System]
    L --> M[Rectify Fault and Retest]
    M --> E
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Viva Tip:** Examiners love to ask, *"Why is the brake warmed up before the test?"* Answer: To bring the brake linings to their **operating temperature** (~100–200 °C), ensuring that the friction coefficient used in the calculation corresponds to real-world hot-braking conditions, not cold ambient data.

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic — Air vs Hydraulic Brake Actuation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph AIR[Air Brake System]
        A1[Driver Pedal] --> A2[Foot Valve]
        A2 --> A3[Air Reservoir Six Bar]
        A3 --> A4[Brake Chamber]
        A4 --> A5[Slack Adjuster and S Cam]
        A5 --> A6[Brake Shoe and Drum]
    end
    subgraph HYD[Hydraulic Brake System]
        H1[Driver Pedal] --> H2[Vacuum Booster]
        H2 --> H3[Master Cylinder]
        H3 --> H4[Brake Lines]
        H4 --> H5[Wheel Caliper or Cylinder]
        H5 --> H6[Disc Pad or Drum Shoe]
    end
    A6 --> COMPARE[Brake Friction Torque Output]
    H6 --> COMPARE
    COMPARE --> OUT[Vehicle Deceleration and Stop]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key contrast:** The air system uses **gas compressibility** to store energy and a **mechanical lever** (slack adjuster + S-cam) to amplify force. The hydraulic system uses an **incompressible fluid** and **area ratio** to amplify force. Both deliver friction torque at the wheel, which is the *measured quantity* in a brake test.
