Modern AWD passenger cars target TFWR $\approx 0.45$–$0.55$ to deliver 30 % gradeability. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Acceleration on Level Road ### 3.2.1 Starting Premise On a **level road** ($\theta = 0$): $F_g = 0$. The vehicle accelerates at rate $a$ from low to medium speed (say 0–100 km/h). Aerodynamic drag is significant only at higher speeds and will be retained here. The fundamental equation becomes: $$F_t = F_r + F_a + F_i$$ ### 3.2.2 Substitute Each Term $$\frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, i_g \, i_0}{r_e} = f_r m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2 + m a (1+\lambda)$$ ### 3.2.3 Solve for Acceleration Rearrange to isolate $a$: $$m a (1+\lambda) = \frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, i_g \, i_0}{r_e} - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2$$ $$\boxed{a = \frac{1}{m(1+\lambda)}\left[\frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, i_g \, i_0}{r_e} - f_r m g - \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2\right]}$$ ### 3.2.4 Interpretation This expression shows that **acceleration drops as speed increases** because: - $F_a \propto V^2$ grows quadratically, eating into the available tractive force. - The optimum acceleration band lies where the engine is producing **peak torque** at the *current* speed (i.e., in lower gears). > [!NOTE] > For maximum 0–100 km/h time, the transmission is shifted such that the engine operates near its **peak-horsepower rpm** as the vehicle accelerates — this is the **ideal-shift strategy** used by performance-car ECU calibrators. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Speed ($V_{\max}$) ### 3.3.1 Starting Premise At $V_{\max}$, the vehicle is in **top gear on level road**, with **zero acceleration**. The fundamental equation reduces to: $$F_t = F_r + F_a$$ ### 3.3.2 Substitute and Rearrange $$\frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, i_{g,\text{top}} \, i_0}{r_e} = f_r m g + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V_{\max}^2$$ ### 3.3.3 Solve for $V_{\max}^2$ $$V_{\max}^2 = \frac{2}{\rho C_D A}\left[\frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, i_{g,\text{top}} \, i_0}{r_e} - f_r m g\right]$$ ### 3.3.4 Closed-Form Result $$\boxed{V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{\rho C_D A}\left[F_{t,\text{top}} - f_r m g\right]}}$$ ### 3.3.5 Numerical Sanity Check (KTU standard values) For a mid-size sedan: - $F_{t,\text{top}} = 1500 \text{ N}$ - $f_r = 0.012$, $m = 1200 \text{ kg}$, $g = 9.81$ - $C_D = 0.32$, $A = 2.1 \text{ m}^2$, $\rho = 1.2$ Rolling resistance: $f_r m g = 0.012 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 141.3 \text{ N}$ Net force available to overcome drag: $1500 - 141.3 = 1358.7 \text{ N}$ $$V_{\max} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1358.7}{1.2 \times 0.32 \times 2.1}} = \sqrt{3370} \approx 58.0 \text{ m/s}$$ Convert to km/h: $58.0 \times 3.6 = 209 \text{ km/h}$ — a realistic top-speed prediction for the assumed vehicle. ✅ --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Power Required at $V_{\max}$ Multiplying both sides of the maximum-speed equation by $V_{\max}$: $$F_t \cdot V_{\max} = F_r \cdot V_{\max} + F_a \cdot V_{\max}$$ The left side is **Power at the wheels** $P_w$, and the right side decomposes into **rolling-resistance power** plus **aerodynamic power**: $$P_w = f_r \, m g \, V_{\max} + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V_{\max}^3$$ The engine must deliver $P_{\text{eng}} = P_w / \eta_t$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Insight:** At high speed, $P_{\text{aero}} \propto V^3$ dominates. Reducing $C_D$ by 10 % (e.g., from 0.32 to 0.29) at $V = 60 \text{ m/s}$ saves $\approx 9.4\,\%$ of engine power — directly improving both top speed and fuel economy. This is why modern EVs invest heavily in aerodynamic refinement. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Performance Requirement ↔ Governing Equation | Performance Requirement | Governing Equation | Key Inputs | Output | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Maximum Speed | $V_{\max} = \sqrt{2(F_{t,\text{top}} - f_r m g)/(\rho C_D A)}$ | $F_{t,\text{top}}$, $C_D$, $A$ | Speed (m/s) | | Maximum Acceleration | $a = [F_t - f_r m g - 0.5\rho C_D A V^2]/[m(1+\lambda)]$ | $F_t(V)$, $m$, $\lambda$, $V$ | Acceleration (m/s²) | | Maximum Gradeability | $\sin\theta_{\max} = F_{t,\max}/(mg) - f_r$ | $F_{t,\max}$, $m$, $f_r$ | Grade angle (rad or %) | | Time to Accelerate | $t = \int_{V_1}^{V_2}\dfrac{m(1+\lambda)\,dV}{F_t(V)-F_r-F_a(V)-F_g}$ | Force-vs-speed curves | Time (s) | | Fuel Consumption | $\dot{m}_f = P_w/(\eta_{\text{eng}}\,\eta_t\,Q_{HV})$ | Power, efficiencies, fuel LHV | kg/s or l/s | --- ## 3.6 Worked Example (KTU Board Style) > **Problem:** A passenger car has mass $m = 1300 \text{ kg}$, frontal area $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $C_D = 0.35$, $f_r = 0.013$, wheel radius $r_e = 0.32 \text{ m}$, engine peak torque $T_e = 220 \text{ Nm}$ at 2500 rpm, first-gear ratio $i_{g,1} = 3.6$, final-drive ratio $i_0 = 3.7$, drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.9$. Compute maximum gradeability in percent. **Solution (step by step):** **Step 1 — Tractive force in 1st gear:** $$F_{t,\max} = \frac{T_e \, \eta_t \, i_{g,1} \, i_0}{r_e} = \frac{220 \times 0.9 \times 3.6 \times 3.7}{0.32}$$ $$F_{t,\max} = \frac{2637.36}{0.32} \approx 8241.75 \text{ N}$$ **Step 2 — Rolling resistance force:** $$F_r = f_r \, m g = 0.013 \times 1300 \times 9.81 = 165.79 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Maximum sine of climb angle (neglecting drag since speed is near zero):** $$\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{F_{t,\max}}{m g} - f_r = \frac{8241.75}{1300 \times 9.81} - 0.013$$ $$\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{8241.75}{12753} - 0.013 = 0.6463 - 0.013 = 0.6333$$ **Step 4 — Convert to grade percentage:** $$\theta_{\max} = \arcsin(0.6333) \approx 39.3°$$ $$G_{\max} = 100 \tan\theta_{\max} = 100 \times \tan(39.3°) = 100 \times 0.819 \approx 81.9\,\%$$ > [!NOTE] > This **theoretical** grade (≈ 82 %) is the *available* grade. In practice, tyre-road friction limits the *usable* grade to $\tan^{-1}(\mu) \approx 30$–$35\,\%$ (with $\mu \approx 0.6$–$0.7$). The lower of the two becomes the actual gradeability. # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Performance Requirement Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Mission Statement
Vehicle Class and Use Case] --> R1[Customer Requirements] M --> R2[Regulatory Requirements] M --> R3[Market Segment Targets] R1 --> PR[Performance Requirements] R2 --> PR R3 --> PR PR --> P1[Powertrain Performance] PR --> P2[Active Safety Performance] PR --> P3[Ride and Comfort Performance] PR --> P4[Environmental Performance] P1 --> S1[Engine and Motor Sizing] P1 --> S2[Transmission Gear Ratios] P1 --> S3[Aerodynamic CdA Target] P2 --> B1[Brake System Sizing] P2 --> B2[Tyre Selection] P3 --> C1[Suspension Tuning] P3 --> C2[NVH Treatment] P4 --> E1[Emission Aftertreatment] P4 --> E2[Energy Storage Sizing] S1 --> VAL[Prototype Testing] S2 --> VAL S3 --> VAL B1 --> VAL B2 --> VAL C1 --> VAL C2 --> VAL E1 --> VAL E2 --> VAL VAL --> VER{Verifies Requirements?} VER -->|Yes| PROD[Production Approval] VER -->|No| ITER[Design Iteration Loop] ITER --> PR ``` --- ## 4.2 Force-Body Diagram — Longitudinal Dynamics Free Body ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph V[Vehicle moving up incline] direction LR W[W equals mg downward] --> GFG[F_g equals mg sin theta] W --> GFN[F_n equals mg cos theta] GFG --> SUM[F_net vector] FT[F_tractive at contact patch forward] --> SUM FR[F_rolling resistance backward] --> SUM FA[F_aero drag backward] --> SUM FI[F_inertia backward when accelerating] --> SUM end SUM --> EQ[Newton Second Law: F_net equals m a] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > This block diagram represents the **vector force balance** on the vehicle body. The *incline* is implicit in the gradient force $F_g$, and all horizontal forces project along the direction of travel. The sum equals $m \cdot a$ along the slope. --- ## 4.3 Requirements-Flow Matrix (Mermaid Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TD REQ1[Requirement R1 Top Speed] --> D1[Derive required P_engine] REQ2[Requirement R2 Acceleration] --> D2[Derive required F_tractive] REQ3[Requirement R3 Gradeability] --> D3[Derive first gear ratio] REQ4[Requirement R4 Fuel Economy] --> D4[Derive mass and CdA targets] REQ5[Requirement R5 Braking Distance] --> D5[Derive brake torque capacity] D1 --> SYM[Vehicle System Specification] D2 --> SYM D3 --> SYM D4 --> SYM D5 --> SYM SYM --> COMP[Component Sizing] COMP --> TEST[Type Approval and Validation Testing] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading aid:** This diagram establishes the **causal chain** from a *performance requirement* to a *design specification* to a *component sizing* to *validation testing*. Every block is a deliverable in a real OEM development program, and the diagram mirrors the structure of a **KTU 2024 design-project rubric**. --- ## 4.4 Vehicle Performance Trade-Off Map (Mermaid Quadrant) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Q1[Quadrant 1 High Power Low Mass] direction TB Q1A[Sports Cars] Q1B[Supercars] end subgraph Q2[Quadrant 2 High Power High Mass] direction TB Q2A[Luxury SUVs] Q2B[Heavy EVs] end subgraph Q3[Quadrant 3 Low Power High Mass] direction TB Q3A[Family MPVs] Q3B[Commercial Vans] end subgraph Q4[Quadrant 4 Low Power Low Mass] direction TB Q4A[City Hatchbacks] Q4B[Compact EVs] end PWR[Power to Weight Ratio] --> Q1 PWR --> Q2 PWR --> Q3 PWR --> Q4 MASS[Vehicle Kerb Mass] --> Q1 MASS --> Q2 MASS --> Q3 MASS --> Q4 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** Quadrant 1 vehicles satisfy the *most demanding* performance requirements (high $V_{\max}$, low $t_{0-100}$, high gradeability) but at the *highest cost*. Quadrant 4 vehicles optimise *fuel economy* and *affordability* but have modest performance. The choice of quadrant is dictated by the **Performance Requirement Specification** derived in Module 1. # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper, PEMUT633, April 2024]** ### Question A.1 (3 Marks) **Define vehicle performance requirements. List any four primary performance attributes of a passenger car as per the KTU 2024 syllabus.** **Model Answer:** Vehicle performance requirements are the quantified engineering specifications that a road vehicle must satisfy to fulfil its intended mission, balancing customer expectations, regulatory mandates, market positioning, and cost constraints. They form the topmost design targets in the vehicle development cycle. Four primary performance attributes: 1. **Maximum speed** — highest sustainable velocity on level road. 2. **Acceleration capability** — typically 0–100 km/h time. 3. **Gradeability** — ability to climb sustained inclines. 4. **Fuel/energy economy** — distance per unit of fuel or per kWh. *(Equivalent attributes such as braking distance, ride comfort, NVH, and emission levels are also accepted.)* --- ### Question A.2 (3 Marks) **State the four resistance forces that oppose a vehicle's motion on a gradient. Write the formula for aerodynamic drag.** **Model Answer:** The four resistance forces are: 1. **Rolling resistance force** $F_r = f_r \, m g$ 2. **Aerodynamic drag force** $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2$ 3. **Gradient/Climbing force** $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ 4. **Inertia force** $F_i = m a (1+\lambda)$ The formula for aerodynamic drag is $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2$, where $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $C_D$ is the drag coefficient, $A$ is the frontal area, and $V$ is the velocity. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question B.A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, PEMUT633 Model Paper]** **(a)** Derive the fundamental longitudinal performance equation for a four-wheeled vehicle moving on a gradient, clearly defining each term and its physical origin. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A passenger car has the following parameters: - Mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$ - Engine peak torque $T_e = 250 \text{ Nm}$ at 2000 rpm - First-gear ratio $i_{g,1} = 3.8$ - Final-drive ratio $i_0 = 3.4$ - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.88$ - Wheel radius $r_e = 0.33 \text{ m}$ - Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.014$ - Frontal area $A = 2.3 \text{ m}^2$, drag coefficient $C_D = 0.34$ - Air density $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ Calculate: - **(i)** The maximum tractive force available at the wheels in first gear. **(2 Marks)** - **(ii)** The maximum theoretical grade (in percent) the vehicle can climb, neglecting aerodynamic drag. **(2 Marks)** - **(iii)** The maximum acceleration on a level road at $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$, assuming mass factor $\lambda = 0.1$ and engine delivers 90 % of peak torque at this speed. **(3 Marks)** --- #### Complete Model Solution **Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)** Consider a four-wheeled vehicle of mass $m$ moving up an incline of angle $\theta$ at velocity $V$ with acceleration $a$. Apply Newton's Second Law along the direction of motion: $$\sum F_x = m \cdot a$$ Forces acting on the vehicle along the slope: | Force | Direction | Magnitude | Physical Origin | | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Tractive force $F_t$ | Up the slope | $\dfrac{T_e \eta_t i_g i_0}{r_e}$ | Engine torque via drivetrain | | Rolling resistance $F_r$ | Down the slope | $f_r \, m g \cos\theta$ | Tyre deformation hysteresis | | Aerodynamic drag $F_a$ | Down the slope | $\tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2$ | Air resistance | | Gradient force $F_g$ | Down the slope | $m g \sin\theta$ | Gravity component | | Inertia force $F_i$ | Down the slope | $m a (1+\lambda)$ | Translational + rotational inertia | Applying Newton's Second Law: $$F_t - F_r - F_a - F_g - F_i = m a \cdot (1+\lambda)_{\text{rearranged}}$$ Equivalently: $$\boxed{\dfrac{T_e \eta_t i_g i_0}{r_e} = f_r m g \cos\theta + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^2 + m g \sin\theta + m a (1+\lambda)}$$ Each term on the right-hand side represents a distinct energy-loss or resisting mechanism. The left-hand side is the only force *available* to overcome all of these simultaneously — which is why performance is *always a trade-off* (you cannot maximise all four at once with a fixed powertrain). --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution** **(i) Maximum tractive force in 1st gear (2 Marks):** $$F_{t,\max} = \frac{T_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_{g,1} \cdot i_0}{r_e}$$ $$F_{t,\max} = \frac{250 \times 0.88 \times 3.8 \times 3.4}{0.33} = \frac{2842.4}{0.33} \approx 8613.3 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Maximum grade (neglecting $F_a$): (2 Marks)** At maximum grade $V \to 0$, so $F_a \to 0$. Also $a = 0$. The tractive equation reduces to: $$F_{t,\max} = f_r \, m g \cos\theta_{\max} + m g \sin\theta_{\max}$$ Assuming $\cos\theta_{\max} \approx 1$ for moderate slopes: $$\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{F_{t,\max}}{m g} - f_r = \frac{8613.3}{1500 \times 9.81} - 0.014$$ $$\sin\theta_{\max} = \frac{8613.3}{14715} - 0.014 = 0.5854 - 0.014 = 0.5714$$ $$\theta_{\max} = \arcsin(0.5714) \approx 34.86°$$ $$G_{\max} = 100 \tan(34.86°) = 100 \times 0.6963 \approx 69.6\,\%$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the *theoretical* grade. The practical grade is lower due to tyre-road friction limits ($\mu \leq 0.7$ typically). **(iii) Maximum acceleration at $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$ on level road: (3 Marks)** Engine torque at this operating point: $T_e = 0.9 \times 250 = 225 \text{ Nm}$ Rolling resistance at $V = 20 \text{ m/s}$: $$F_r = f_r \, m g = 0.014 \times 1500 \times 9.81 = 206.0 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag: $$F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 0.34 \times 2.3 \times (20)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.34 \times 2.3 \times 400$$ $$F_a = 187.7 \text{ N}$$ Tractive force in 1st gear at this speed (assuming gear is still 1st): $$F_t = \frac{225 \times 0.88 \times 3.8 \times 3.4}{0.33} = \frac{2558.16}{0.33} \approx 7752.0 \text{ N}$$ Applying the acceleration equation: $$a = \frac{F_t - F_r - F_a}{m(1+\lambda)} = \frac{7752.0 - 206.0 - 187.7}{1500 \times (1+0.1)}$$ $$a = \frac{7358.3}{1650} \approx 4.46 \text{ m/s}^2$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > - **1 mark lost** if students write $F_r = f_r \, mg \sin\theta$ instead of $\cos\theta$ on a gradient. > - **1 mark lost** if the inertia term is written as $m a$ instead of $m a (1+\lambda)$ (the mass factor $\lambda$ is a *favourite* valuation trap). > - **1 mark lost** if rolling resistance is neglected during the *gradeability* calculation — the examiner expects $f_r \cos\theta$ to be subtracted. > - **0.5 marks lost** if units are not explicitly written for the final answer. > - **2 marks lost** if the *physical origin* of each force is not explained in the derivation part — KTU 2024 examiners reward conceptual clarity, not just formula substitution. --- ### Question B.B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, PEMUT633 Model Paper]** **(a)** With the help of neat block diagrams, explain how customer requirements, regulatory requirements, and market requirements together drive the formulation of vehicle performance requirements. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A light commercial vehicle of mass $m = 2500 \text{ kg}$ is required to cruise at a constant speed of $V = 90 \text{ km/h = 25 m/s}$ on a level road. The vehicle has $C_D = 0.45$, $A = 3.5 \text{ m}^2$, $f_r = 0.018$, and drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.9$. Calculate: - **(i)** The total resistance force at cruise speed. **(3 Marks)** - **(ii)** The power required at the wheels and at the engine flywheel. **(2 Marks)** - **(iii)** If the engine BSFC is $260 \text{ g/kWh}$, compute the fuel consumption rate in kg/h and l/h (fuel density = $0.75 \text{ kg/l}$). **(2 Marks)** --- #### Complete Model Solution **Part (a) — Block-diagram explanation (7 Marks)** The formulation of vehicle performance requirements is a structured engineering process. Three input streams converge to define the requirements: 1. **Customer Requirements (CR)** come from market-survey data, focus-group studies, brand-image targets, and competitor benchmarking. They translate into *soft* goals such as "spacious", "peppy", "quiet". 2. **Regulatory Requirements (RR)** are statutory mandates from agencies like ARAI, AIS, ECE, and FMVSS. They translate into *hard* numerical limits such as BS-VI emission caps, AIS-008 braking norms, and AIS-052 safety standards. 3. **Market Requirements (MR)** are positioning goals such as "lowest cost in segment", "highest ground clearance", "best-in-class fuel economy". These three streams are **filtered through engineering judgement** to produce the **Performance Requirement Specification (PRS)**. The PRS is then decomposed into: - **Powertrain Performance** (top speed, acceleration, gradeability) - **Safety Performance** (braking, stability, crash) - **Comfort Performance** (ride, NVH) - **Environmental Performance** (emissions, range, recyclability) These in turn drive **Component Specifications** which drive the **Prototype Build**, leading to **Testing & Validation**. Any failure loops back to the PRS for iteration. *(Block diagram in SECTION 4.1 above carries full marks.)* --- **Part (b) — Numerical Solution** **(i) Total resistance force at $V = 25 \text{ m/s}$: (3 Marks)** Rolling resistance: $$F_r = f_r \, m g = 0.018 \times 2500 \times 9.81 = 441.45 \text{ N}$$ Aerodynamic drag: $$F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1.2 \times 0.45 \times 3.5 \times (25)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.45 \times 3.5 \times 625$$ $$F_a = 590.625 \text{ N}$$ Gradient and inertia are zero (cruise, level). Therefore: $$F_{\text{total}} = F_r + F_a = 441.45 + 590.625 = 1032.075 \text{ N}$$ **(ii) Power at wheels and at engine: (2 Marks)** Power at wheels: $$P_w = F_{\text{total}} \times V = 1032.075 \times 25 = 25801.9 \text{ W} \approx 25.8 \text{ kW}$$ Power at engine (flywheel): $$P_{\text{eng}} = \frac{P_w}{\eta_t} = \frac{25.8}{0.9} \approx 28.67 \text{ kW}$$ **(iii) Fuel consumption rate: (2 Marks)** Fuel rate: $$\dot{m}_f = \frac{\text{BSFC} \times P_{\text{eng}}}{10^6} = \frac{260 \times 28670}{10^6} = 7.454 \text{ kg/h}$$ Volume rate: $$\dot{V}_f = \frac{7.454}{0.75} \approx 9.94 \text{ l/h}$$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 / Performance Requirements** ### Core Concepts - Performance requirements are **design targets**, not specifications. - They arise from **CR + RR + MR** filtered through engineering judgement. - The **Tractive Force Equation** is the master equation: $F_t = F_r + F_a + F_g + F_i$. - Every performance attribute is a **boundary condition** on this equation. ### Must-Memorise Standard Values (KTU Board) - $\rho = 1.2 \text{ kg/m}^3$ — air density at STP - $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ - $f_r \approx 0.012$–$0.018$ for radial tyres on tarmac - $C_D \approx 0.30$–$0.40$ for modern passenger cars - $\lambda \approx 0.05$–$0.15$ rotational mass factor ### Must-Memorise Formulas - $V_{\max} = \sqrt{2(F_{t,\text{top}} - f_r m g)/(\rho C_D A)}$ - $a = [F_t - f_r m g - 0.5\rho C_D A V^2]/[m(1+\lambda)]$ - $\sin\theta_{\max} = F_{t,\max}/(m g) - f_r$ - $G\,\% = 100 \tan\theta$ - $P_{\text{aero}} = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_D A V^3$ ### Pitfalls to Avoid - ❌ Forgetting $\cos\theta$ in rolling resistance on a slope. - ❌ Writing inertia as $m a$ instead of $m a (1+\lambda)$. - ❌ Using $W = m g \cos\theta$ in both $F_r$ and $F_g$ inconsistently. - ❌ Ignoring aerodynamic drag in gradeability at high speeds. - ❌ Confusing grade percentage with grade angle (multiply by 100). - ❌ Forgetting to divide engine power by $\eta_t$ when computing wheel power. ### Conceptual Differentiations - **Performance requirement** vs **Performance specification** — Target vs Contract. - **Maximum speed** (steady-state) vs **Maximum acceleration** (transient). - **Theoretical gradeability** (limited by $F_t$) vs **Practical gradeability** (limited by tyre friction $\mu$). - **Engine torque** vs **Wheel torque** (related by $i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t$). - **BSFC** vs **BSEC** (Brake Specific Energy Consumption, for EVs). ### Modern Syllabus Hooks (KTU 2024) - EVs replace engine BSFC with **kWh/100 km** energy consumption. - **WLTP / RDE** cycles replace older NEDC for emission & economy certification. - ADAS-related performance requirements (lane-keep, AEB stopping distance) are emerging Module 1 topics. - **Over-the-air (OTA) updatable performance maps** are a current industry trend worth mentioning in 14-mark answers. > [!NOTE] > **End of Topic — Performance Requirements (Module 1, PEMUT633, KTU 2024 Scheme).** Cross-reference Module 2 for *Resistance Forces* derivations and Module 3 for *Powertrain Sizing* applications of the same governing equations.

topics.title: Parking Brake - Drawbar Pull Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parking Brake - Drawbar Pull Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Parking Brake — Formal Definition

The **Parking Brake** (also known as the *hand brake*, *emergency brake*, or *auxiliary brake*) is a purely **mechanically actuated braking system**, completely independent of the vehicle's main hydraulic service-brake circuit. Its primary function is to **statically hold the vehicle stationary on a specified gradient** when the driver is absent from the vehicle, and to provide a back-up stopping capability in the event of service-brake failure.

In the context of KTU Module 1 (Vehicle Performance Testing), the **Parking Brake Test** is a mandatory regulatory qualification test that verifies the brake's ability to hold the **Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)** of the vehicle — both cold and hot — on a minimum specified gradient (typically **18 % or 20 % grade**, depending on the applicable standard such as **AIS-019 / ECE R13 / IS 11860**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — PEMUT633, Module 1):**
> The Parking Brake – Drawbar Pull Test is clubbed together as a single testing unit because both evaluate the *vehicle's static and tractive load-holding ability* on a controlled test surface. Students must be able to describe the test procedure, instrumentation, applicable standards, and the mathematical relationship between vehicle weight, gradient, and the holding force demanded from the brake.

### 1.2 The Drawbar Pull — Formal Definition

The **Drawbar Pull (DBP)** of a vehicle (most commonly applied to **agricultural tractors, prime movers, off-road military vehicles, and haul trucks**) is defined as the *horizontal force available at the rear (or front) drawbar/coupling point* to overcome the external resistance offered by an implement or trailer being towed.

Mathematically:

$$\text{Drawbar Pull} = \text{Tractive Effort} - (\text{Rolling Resistance} + \text{Grade Resistance} + \text{Inertia Force})$$

The **Drawbar Pull Test** is the standardized procedure used to measure this maximum pulling force under controlled conditions, using either a **dynamometric load car** or a **strain-gauge instrumented towing linkage**.

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Hill, the Rope, and the Mule"

Imagine you have parked your car on a steep hill using only the handbrake (no gear, no service brake). Now imagine a strong mule is tied to your car's front tow hook, and it tries to pull your car up the hill.

- **Parking Brake Test** asks: *"If I let go of everything except the handbrake, will the car stay still on this hill?"* — i.e., the brake shoes must generate enough friction torque at the wheels to resist the downhill gravitational force component.
- **Drawbar Pull Test** asks: *"How hard can the mule pull before the tires start slipping and the engine bogs down?"* — i.e., how much horizontal force the engine–drivetrain can deliver to the ground via the wheels before tractive capacity is exhausted.

> [!NOTE]
> **Concept Connection:** Both tests essentially deal with **force balance at the tyre–ground contact patch**. The parking brake test measures the *maximum resisting force* (brake friction), while the drawbar pull test measures the *maximum producing force* (engine–wheel torque converted via friction at the ground).

### 1.4 Key Terms and Units (Bold for Emphasis)

| Term | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Gross Vehicle Weight | W or GVW | **kgf** or **N** |
| Gradient | θ or G | **degrees** or **%** |
| Parking Brake Holding Force | F_hold | **N** or **kgf** |
| Drawbar Pull | F_DB | **N** or **kgf** |
| Coefficient of Friction (brake lining) | μ | dimensionless |
| Coefficient of Friction (tyre–soil) | μ_t | dimensionless |
| Wheel Slip | s | **%** |
| Drawbar Power | P_DB | **kW** or **hp** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Parking Brake — Operating Principle

The parking brake is actuated by a **ratchet-and-pawl mechanism** (or an electric motor in EPB systems). When the lever/cable/piston is pulled, it forces the brake shoes/pads against a drum or disc, generating a friction torque that resists wheel rotation. For the vehicle to remain stationary on a slope, this friction torque must equal or exceed the torque generated by gravity acting through the wheel centre.

### 2.2 Logical Steps for Parking Brake Test Analysis

1. **Identify the resisting force component** of gravity along the slope direction:
   - The component acting **down the slope** = W × sin(θ).
   - The component acting **perpendicular to the slope** (normal reaction) = W × cos(θ).

2. **Distribute this force among the braked wheels.** Modern vehicles use the parking brake only on the **rear wheels** (service brakes on all four). So the holding demand is allocated to the rear axle only.

3. **Compute brake torque demand:**
   - At each braked wheel, the torque required = F_hold × r_eff, where r_eff is the effective rolling radius.

4. **Compute available friction torque from the brake:**
   - T_brake = μ × F_actuation × r_drum (drum brake) or μ × F_actuation × r_piston (disc brake).

5. **Compare available vs. demanded torque.** Test passes only if T_brake > T_demanded under both cold and hot brake conditions.

### 2.3 Drawbar Pull — Operating Principle

For a tractor or prime mover towing an implement, the available drawbar force is limited by:

1. **Engine power** (governed by BSFC curve at current RPM).
2. **Drivetrain efficiency** (clutch, gearbox, differential, final drive).
3. **Tyre–soil/road friction** (limited by normal load × friction coefficient).
4. **Wheel slip** (above ~15–20 % slip on hard surfaces, productivity drops sharply).

The test therefore aims to capture the **maximum sustained horizontal force** the vehicle can apply without exceeding a defined slip threshold.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles **every formula** you are expected to recognize and apply for questions on the *Parking Brake – Drawbar Pull Test* in the KTU 2024 ESE. Memorize these — they form 70 % of the numerical marks.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F_grade = W · sin(θ) | Grade resistance force | N |
| 2 | F_grade(%) ≈ W · (G / 100) | Approximation when G is in % | N |
| 3 | F_rolling = f_r · W · cos(θ) | Rolling resistance on slope | N |
| 4 | F_aero = ½ · ρ · C_d · A · v² | Aerodynamic drag | N |
| 5 | F_inertia = m · a = (W/g) · a | Linear acceleration resistance | N |
| 6 | F_DB = F_tractive − (F_rolling + F_grade + F_inertia + F_aero) | Net available Drawbar Pull | N |
| 7 | T_brake_required = F_hold · r_eff | Torque demand on brake | N·m |
| 8 | T_brake_available = μ_b · F_actuation · r_drum | Torque capacity of brake | N·m |
| 9 | P_DB = F_DB · v | Drawbar power | W |
| 10 | η_drawbar = (P_DB / P_engine) · 100 % | Drawbar efficiency | % |
| 11 | s = [(V_theoretical − V_actual) / V_theoretical] · 100 % | Wheel slip | % |
| 12 | σ_drawbar = F_DB / W | Specific drawbar pull (tractor) | N/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Convention note:** Throughout KTU Module 1, weight W is taken in **kgf** unless explicitly stated in **Newtons** (where the conversion **1 kgf = 9.81 N** must be applied). Gradient θ in degrees is the angle of inclination; gradient G in % equals **tan(θ) · 100**.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Compliance:** Every passenger car, M&HCV, and tractor manufactured in India must pass the **AIS-019 Part II (Parking Brake)** and **IS 10703 / SAE J708 (Drawbar Pull)** tests before type approval.
- **Field Productivity:** Drawbar efficiency (typically 55–70 % for agricultural tractors) directly determines how much area a farmer can plough per hour — a critical purchase-decision parameter.
- **Safety Engineering:** Parking brake failure is a major contributor to **unintended rollaway accidents**; thus the 18 % / 20 % grade holding test is a direct public-safety gate.
- **Military & Off-road:** Drawbar pull rating determines whether a vehicle can self-recover or tow a disabled peer in cross-country missions.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Grade Resistance Force (Parking Brake Force Demand)

**Problem Context:** A vehicle of weight W rests on an inclined surface at angle θ. We need to find the component of weight acting parallel to the slope — this is the force the parking brake must resist.

**Step 1 — Free Body Decomposition.**
Resolve the weight vector W (acting vertically downward) into two orthogonal components along the inclined coordinate system:

- Component along the slope (downhill direction) = W sin(θ)
- Component normal to the slope (into the surface) = W cos(θ)

**Step 2 — Free Body Equilibrium.**
For static equilibrium with the parking brake applied, the sum of forces along the slope must equal zero:

$$\sum F_{\parallel} = 0$$

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} - W \sin(\theta) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the Required Holding Force.**

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} = W \sin(\theta)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Grade Percentage Form.**
Since G (%) = 100 · tan(θ), for small angles (θ < 20°) we may use the approximation sin(θ) ≈ tan(θ):

$$\sin(\theta) \approx \frac{G}{100}$$

Substituting:

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} \approx \frac{W \cdot G}{100}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical Demonstration.**
Given W = 1500 kgf and G = 20 % (θ ≈ 11.31°):

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} = 1500 \times \sin(11.31°)$$

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} = 1500 \times 0.1961$$

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} = 294.15 \, \text{kgf}$$

Or equivalently:

$$F_{\text{brake,hold}} \approx \frac{1500 \times 20}{100} = 300 \, \text{kgf (using the approximation)}$$

The 2 % discrepancy is acceptable for KTU numericals.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Net Available Drawbar Pull

**Problem Context:** A tractor of weight W tows an implement on a level field at constant velocity v. The tractive force at the wheel is F_t. Find the net force available at the drawbar.

**Step 1 — Identify All Resistive Forces.**
At constant velocity (no acceleration), the resistive forces are:

- Rolling resistance: F_roll = f_r · W (on level ground, cos(0°) = 1)
- Grade resistance: F_grade = W · sin(θ) (zero if level)
- Implement drag (treated as part of F_DB load)

**Step 2 — Apply Newton's Second Law.**
At constant velocity, ΣF = 0:

$$F_t - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{grade}} - F_{\text{DB}} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for F_DB.**

$$F_{\text{DB}} = F_t - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{grade}}$$

**Step 4 — Express F_t in Terms of Engine Torque.**
If engine torque T_e is delivered through the drivetrain with overall efficiency η_d, and the total gear reduction ratio is i_total:

$$F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta_d}{r_e}$$

where r_e is the effective rolling radius of the drive wheel.

**Step 5 — Substitute Back.**

$$F_{\text{DB}} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta_d}{r_e} - f_r W - W \sin(\theta)$$

**Step 6 — Derive Drawbar Power.**
Power at the drawbar equals force × velocity:

$$P_{\text{DB}} = F_{\text{DB}} \cdot v$$

**Step 7 — Numerical Demonstration.**
Given: T_e = 250 N·m, i_total = 40, η_d = 0.85, r_e = 0.75 m, W = 3000 kgf, f_r = 0.05, level ground, v = 2 m/s.

First convert W to Newtons: W = 3000 × 9.81 = 29430 N.

Compute F_t:

$$F_t = \frac{250 \times 40 \times 0.85}{0.75}$$

$$F_t = \frac{8500}{0.75}$$

$$F_t = 11333.33 \, \text{N}$$

Compute F_roll:

$$F_{\text{roll}} = 0.05 \times 29430 = 1471.5 \, \text{N}$$

F_grade = 0 (level ground).

Compute F_DB:

$$F_{\text{DB}} = 11333.33 - 1471.5 - 0$$

$$F_{\text{DB}} = 9861.83 \, \text{N} \approx 9.86 \, \text{kN}$$

Compute P_DB:

$$P_{\text{DB}} = 9861.83 \times 2 = 19723.67 \, \text{W} \approx 19.72 \, \text{kW}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Brake Torque Capacity vs. Demand

**Step 1 — Actuation Force.**
The cable/lever applies a force F_a on the brake shoe/piston.

**Step 2 — Self-Energizing / Non-Self-Energizing Geometry.**
For a drum brake, the shoe self-energization factor C_e (typically 0.7 to 1.4) modifies the normal force on the drum:

$$F_{\text{normal}} = F_a \cdot C_e$$

**Step 3 — Tangential Friction Force at the Drum Surface.**

$$F_{\text{friction}} = \mu_b \cdot F_{\text{normal}} = \mu_b \cdot F_a \cdot C_e$$

**Step 4 — Brake Torque Generated.**

$$T_{\text{brake}} = F_{\text{friction}} \cdot r_{\text{drum}}$$

**Step 5 — Torque Required at Wheel.**
For a vehicle held on a slope with the holding force distributed equally over n braked wheels:

$$T_{\text{required,per wheel}} = \frac{F_{\text{brake,hold}} \cdot r_e}{n}$$

**Step 6 — Safety Check.**

$$\boxed{T_{\text{brake}} \geq T_{\text{required,per wheel}}}$$

This inequality is what the cold and hot brake tests verify.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Decision Tree for Test Outcome

| Test Condition | Brake State | Load State | Acceptance Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cold Brake | Freshly assembled, ambient temp | GVW + 100 kg driver | Hold for ≥ 5 min on 18–20 % grade |
| Hot Brake | After 1.0 km repeated braking fade test | GVW | Hold for ≥ 5 min on same grade |
| Static DBP | Tractor stationary, load car resisted | Ballasted tractor | F_DB,max recorded before > 15 % slip |
| Moving DBP | Tractor pulls load car at fixed throttle | Ballasted tractor | F_DB at 2.5 m/s measured |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Parking Brake Test Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Vehicle Preparation] --> B[Weigh vehicle to confirm GVW]
    B --> C[Position on test ramp at 18 percent or 20 percent grade]
    C --> D[Engage parking brake fully with one application]
    D --> E[Disengage transmission to Neutral]
    E --> F[Driver exits vehicle]
    F --> G[Start 5 min observation timer]
    G --> H{Vehicle holds stationary?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Record PASS - Cold Test]
    H -- No --> J[Record FAIL - Investigate lining friction and cable tension]
    I --> K[Perform fade cycle - 1 km repeated braking]
    K --> L[Repeat ramp test immediately after fade]
    L --> M{Vehicle still holds?}
    M -- Yes --> N[Record PASS - Hot Test]
    M -- No --> O[Record FAIL - Brake fade exceeds specification]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Drawbar Pull Test Setup (Load Car Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Test Tractor] -->|Drawbar coupling| B[Instrumented Load Car]
    B -->|Hydraulic resistance| C[Load Cell - strain gauge]
    C -->|Signal| D[Data Acquisition System]
    A -->|Wheel rotation| E[Tachometer on drive wheel]
    E -->|RPM signal| D
    D -->|Force vs time plot| F[Maximum Drawbar Pull kN]
    D -->|Slip vs pull plot| G[Slip percentage]
    B -->|Controlled braking| H[Operator console]
    H -->|Modulate resistance| B
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Force Flow During DBP Test

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Engine Combustion Power P_e] --> T[Torque Converter or Clutch]
    T --> G[Gearbox with ratio i_g]
    G --> D[Differential with ratio i_d]
    D --> W[Drive Wheels]
    W -->|Tyre ground friction| TR[Tractive Force F_t at contact patch]
    TR --> RR1[Rolling Resistance F_roll]
    TR --> RR2[Grade Resistance F_grade]
    TR --> RR3[Net Drawbar Pull F_DB]
    RR3 -->|Transmitted via coupling| IM[Implement or Load Car]
    IM -->|Drag measured| LC[Load Cell Output]
```

### 4.4 Schematic — Parking Brake Force Distribution on an Incline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    V[Vehicle W kgf on slope] --> R1[Rear axle - Parking brake active]
    V --> R2[Front axle - No parking brake]
    R1 --> S1[Brake shoe on left rear drum]
    R1 --> S2[Brake shoe on right rear drum]
    V --> G[Gravity W sin theta acting at CG]
    G --> C[Decomposes into]
    C --> F1[F parallel to slope DOWN]
    C --> F2[F normal to slope N equals W cos theta]
    F1 --> H[Must be resisted by combined friction torque at both rear wheels]
    H --> X[Test passes if F1 < mu_b times N at each rear brake]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
Define *Drawbar Pull* of an agricultural tractor. Mention the SI unit and name any **one** Indian Standard that governs its measurement.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 marks):** Drawbar Pull is the horizontal force available at the rear drawbar of a tractor (or prime mover) that is used to overcome the resistance of an attached implement or towed load, after accounting for rolling resistance, grade resistance, and internal drivetrain losses.
- **SI Unit (½ mark):** Newton (N), commonly expressed in kiloNewton (kN).
- **Indian Standard (½ mark):** **IS 10703 : 1992** (Agricultural Tractors — Test Code for Drawbar Performance).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
State the typical gradient (in %) on which the **parking brake holding test** is conducted for a passenger car as per AIS-019. What is the minimum observation duration?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Gradient (2 marks):** **18 % grade** (≈ 10.2°) as per AIS-019 / ECE R13 for M1 category vehicles; 20 % for N2/N3 commercial vehicles.
- **Duration (1 mark):** **5 minutes** in both cold and hot brake states.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Provided)

---

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A passenger car has a Gross Vehicle Weight of **1800 kgf**. The parking brake acts only on the rear wheels. Calculate the minimum braking force that the parking brake must develop at the tyre–road interface to hold the vehicle on a **20 % grade**. Take effective rolling radius r_e = 0.30 m. Hence determine the minimum coefficient of friction (μ_b) required at the brake lining if the actuation force on each shoe is 250 kgf and self-energization factor is 1.1, drum radius = 0.15 m.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain the **cold and hot parking brake test procedure** as per AIS-019, listing the instrumentation and pass/fail criteria.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Convert W to SI (1 mark):

$$W = 1800 \times 9.81 = 17658 \, \text{N}$$

Step 2 — Compute holding force on 20 % grade (2 marks):

$$F_{\text{hold}} = W \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

where tan(θ) = 0.20 → θ = 11.31°, sin(11.31°) = 0.1961.

$$F_{\text{hold}} = 17658 \times 0.1961 = 3462.74 \, \text{N}$$

Step 3 — Torque demand at the rear wheels (1 mark):
Assuming equal distribution over 2 rear wheels:

$$T_{\text{required per wheel}} = \frac{F_{\text{hold}} \cdot r_e}{2} = \frac{3462.74 \times 0.30}{2} = 519.41 \, \text{N·m}$$

Step 4 — Available brake torque (1 mark):

$$T_{\text{brake}} = \mu_b \cdot F_a \cdot C_e \cdot r_{\text{drum}}$$

With F_a = 250 × 9.81 = 2452.5 N:

$$T_{\text{brake}} = \mu_b \times 2452.5 \times 1.1 \times 0.15$$

Step 5 — Equate demand = capacity and solve (2 marks):

$$519.41 = \mu_b \times 2452.5 \times 1.1 \times 0.15$$

$$519.41 = \mu_b \times 404.66$$

$$\mu_b = \frac{519.41}{404.66} = 1.284$$

Since μ_b > 1 is unrealistic for organic brake linings, **the test would likely fail**; a larger actuation system or dual-servo shoe geometry is recommended.

---

**(b) Cold and Hot Test Procedure (7 Marks):**

1. **Cold Test (3 marks):**
   - Vehicle loaded to GVW with driver and instrumentation.
   - Tyres inflated to recommended pressure.
   - Vehicle positioned on test ramp set to 20 % (or 18 %) grade.
   - Parking brake applied with a single full-pull application.
   - Transmission placed in neutral; clutch released; driver exits vehicle.
   - Vehicle observed for **5 minutes** without movement; record cold-hold pass/fail.

2. **Hot Test (3 marks):**
   - Immediately after the cold test, perform a **fade-conditioning run** — repeatedly apply parking brake from a defined speed (e.g., 35 km/h) for 1 km cumulative.
   - Within 60 seconds of completing the fade run, return vehicle to the test ramp.
   - Re-apply parking brake and observe for 5 minutes.
   - Record hot-hold pass/fail and any brake fade or seal leakage.

3. **Instrumentation (1 mark):**
   - Inclinometer or laser-grade-measuring device for ramp.
   - Stopwatch, displacement transducer (to detect any creeping motion > 10 mm).

---

#### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A tractor has the following specifications: Engine torque T_e = 350 N·m at rated RPM, overall drivetrain efficiency η_d = 0.82, total gear reduction i_total = 45, effective wheel radius r_e = 0.78 m, vehicle weight W = 4500 kgf, rolling resistance coefficient f_r = 0.06. The tractor is operating on a **level concrete track**. Calculate the (i) tractive force, (ii) rolling resistance, and (iii) net **drawbar pull**. Also compute the **drawbar power** at a forward speed of **2.5 m/s**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Describe the **Load Car Method** for conducting a drawbar pull test. Compare its advantages over the **Fixed-Belt Dynamometer Method**.

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Step-by-step (7 Marks):**

Step 1 — Tractive Force (2 marks):

$$F_t = \frac{T_e \cdot i_{\text{total}} \cdot \eta_d}{r_e}$$

$$F_t = \frac{350 \times 45 \times 0.82}{0.78}$$

$$F_t = \frac{12915}{0.78}$$

$$F_t = 16557.69 \, \text{N} \approx 16.56 \, \text{kN}$$

Step 2 — Rolling Resistance (1 mark):

$$F_{\text{roll}} = f_r \cdot W = 0.06 \times 4500 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{\text{roll}} = 2648.7 \, \text{N}$$

Step 3 — Grade Resistance (1 mark):
Since level track, F_grade = 0.

Step 4 — Drawbar Pull (1 mark):

$$F_{\text{DB}} = F_t - F_{\text{roll}} - F_{\text{grade}} = 16557.69 - 2648.7 - 0$$

$$F_{\text{DB}} = 13908.99 \, \text{N} \approx 13.91 \, \text{kN}$$

Step 5 — Drawbar Power (2 marks):

$$P_{\text{DB}} = F_{\text{DB}} \cdot v = 13908.99 \times 2.5$$

$$P_{\text{DB}} = 34772.48 \, \text{W} \approx 34.77 \, \text{kW} \, (\approx 46.62 \, \text{hp})$$

---

**(b) Load Car Method Description (7 Marks):**

**Procedure (4 marks):**

1. A self-propelled **load car** (instrumented tractor-trailer unit) is coupled to the test tractor via its drawbar.
2. The load car contains a **hydraulic loading system** that applies a controlled, variable resistance force against the towing tractor.
3. As the load car operator increases resistance, the load cell in the coupling linkage (or in the load car's wheel hub) records the **drawbar pull** in real time.
4. A **fifth-wheel tachometer** or radar gun measures true ground speed; wheel rpm from the test tractor gives theoretical speed; **slip** is computed continuously.
5. The test is repeated at multiple throttle settings (typically 50 %, 75 %, 100 % of rated load) and multiple gears to construct a complete DBP-vs-slip and DBP-power curve.

**Comparison with Fixed-Belt Dynamometer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Load Car Method | Fixed-Belt Dynamometer |
|---|---|---|
| Test surface | Real ground — captures actual tyre–soil interaction | Steel belt — unrealistic for off-road |
| Cost | Lower (uses existing tractor) | Higher (capital-intensive facility) |
| Mobility | Field-portable, can test anywhere | Lab-bound |
| Best use | Agricultural and off-road vehicles | Passenger car and motorcycle traction studies |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 9.81 conversion** when the question mixes kgf and N. Every kgf-based weight MUST be converted to N before being added/subtracted from a N-based force.
> 2. **Mixing up static vs. dynamic parking brake test conditions.** The hot test follows the cold test *only after* a defined fade cycle — students often describe a single ramp test and miss the 5-minute observation requirement.
> 3. **Ignoring drivetrain efficiency η_d** in drawbar pull calculations. Drawing F_t = T_e · i_total / r_e directly without η_d is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **Confusing Rolling Resistance direction:** It always acts opposite to motion, i.e., it must be *subtracted* from tractive force.
> 5. **Reporting wheel slip without units or as a decimal** when the question demands a percentage. Always multiply by 100.
> 6. **Drawbar efficiency calculation error:** Always divide drawbar power by **engine indicated or brake power**, not by wheel power — students confuse P_wheel with P_DB frequently.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Parking brake** is a *mechanically independent* brake system; its test demands holding GVW on **18 % / 20 % grade for 5 min** in both cold and hot states.
- **Holding force required** = W · sin(θ) ≈ W · G/100.
- **Drawbar pull** is the *horizontal force* at the towing coupling after subtracting rolling, grade, and aerodynamic resistances from the tractive effort.
- **Tractive force** = (T_engine × i_total × η_d) / r_e.
- **Drawbar power** = F_DB × v (forward speed).
- **Drawbar efficiency** of a typical agricultural tractor = **55 – 70 %**.
- The **Load Car Method** is the most widely used DBP test method for tractors in India; it preserves real tyre–soil interaction.
- **Wheel slip threshold** for productive field operation ≈ 15 – 20 %; above this, productivity falls and soil damage rises.
- **Indian Standards to remember:**
  - **AIS-019** — Parking brake test for motor vehicles.
  - **IS 11860** — Automotive vehicles — Parking brake systems.
  - **IS 10703** — Agricultural tractors — Drawbar performance test.
- **ECE R13 / FMVSS 135** are the corresponding international standards.
- **Self-energization factor (C_e)** of drum brake shoes can amplify actuation force — never ignore it when computing available brake torque.
- **Hot brake test failure** typically points to **brake fade**, oil contamination of linings, or drum thermal distortion — not to a cable tension problem.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grade Holding Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Grade Holding Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Grade Holding Test (GHT)**, also termed the **Constant-Speed Gradeability Test** or **Gradability-at-Constant-Speed Test**, is a standardized vehicle performance evaluation procedure that determines the **maximum sustainable road gradient (grade in %)** a vehicle can ascend while **maintaining a specified constant vehicle speed**, without the engine stalling, the transmission downshifting automatically, or the speed dropping below an acceptable minimum threshold.

It is fundamentally different from the *maximum gradeable slope from rest* test. The Grade Holding Test specifically evaluates the **steady-state balance between the available tractive effort (TE) at the driven wheels and the sum total of all motion resistances** (rolling, aerodynamic, and grade resistance) at a pre-defined constant velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Grade Holding Test is classified under Module 1 (Vehicle Performance Parameters) and is considered a **high-yield topic** frequently appearing in Part B questions (14 marks) of the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE), often paired with tractive effort diagrams or numerical calculations.

### 1.2 Standard Test Conditions (KTU / SAE Reference Framework)

| Parameter | Typical Test Value | KTU Notation |
|---|---|---|
| Constant Test Speed | **40 km/h** or **50 km/h** (vehicle's lowest stable gear) | $v$ |
| Surface | Dry, level-to-inclined paved road (or test ramp) | — |
| Vehicle Loading | Kerb weight + driver + 50% payload (UN ECE R51 standard) | $W_{gross}$ |
| Gear Selected | **Lowest practical gear** (1st or 2nd) | $i_g$ |
| Measurement | Maximum grade (%) at which $\Delta v = 0$ for ≥ **20 s** | $G_{max}$ |

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Climbing a Moving Staircase" Analogy:**
> Imagine you are walking up a **downward-moving escalator** at a constant speed. To hold a constant speed (relative to the building), your walking effort must exactly counteract the escalator's downward pull. Now, **the steeper the escalator (more grade), the more effort you need.** The Grade Holding Test asks: *"At what maximum escalator steepness can you still maintain the same walking speed without slowing down or speeding up?"*

In vehicular terms:
- The **engine + transmission** = "your legs" (provides tractive effort)
- The **rolling friction + air drag + gravity along slope** = "the downward pull of the escalator"
- The **constant speed** = "you are neither speeding up nor slowing down"
- **Grade Holding** = "the steepest slope you can still conquer at that fixed walking pace"

This is a **steady-state equilibrium** problem, not an acceleration problem!

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physical Principle: Force Equilibrium

For a vehicle to **hold a constant speed** (i.e., zero acceleration) on an inclined road, Newton's Second Law reduces to a simple **force balance**:

$$F_{tractive} = F_{rolling} + F_{aerodynamic} + F_{grade}$$

If the tractive effort at the wheels **exceeds** the sum of resistances, the vehicle will **accelerate** (speed increases).
If the tractive effort **falls below** the sum, the vehicle will **decelerate** (speed decreases, engine lugs).
The **critical point** — Grade Holding — is where the equation holds with **equality**.

## 2.2 Component-Wise Resistance Breakdown

### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance ($F_r$)
Caused by tire deformation, road surface hysteresis, and internal friction.

$$F_r = f_r \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$$

For small angles typically encountered (grade ≤ 30%), $\cos(\alpha) \approx 1$, so:

$$F_r \approx f_r \cdot W$$

Where $f_r$ is the **coefficient of rolling resistance** (typically **0.008 to 0.020** for passenger car radial tires on asphalt).

### 2.2.2 Aerodynamic Drag ($F_a$)
Caused by the shape of the vehicle pushing through air.

$$F_a = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Standard Value (KTU) |
|---|---|---|
| $\rho$ | Air density | **1.2 kg/m³** (sea level, 25°C) |
| $C_d$ | Drag coefficient | 0.30 – 0.45 (passenger car) |
| $A_f$ | Frontal area | 1.8 – 2.5 m² |
| $v$ | Vehicle speed (m/s) | Test-specified |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> At **low test speeds** (typical of Grade Holding Test, e.g., 40 km/h ≈ 11.11 m/s), the aerodynamic drag term is **often negligible** (less than 5% of total resistance) and may be safely ignored in KTU problems unless explicitly stated.

### 2.2.3 Grade Resistance ($F_g$)
The component of vehicle weight acting along the slope, pulling the vehicle backward.

$$F_g = W \cdot \sin(\alpha)$$

The **grade percentage** $G$ is defined as:

$$G \,(\%) = \tan(\alpha) \times 100$$

For the typical range of grades tested (0% to 30%), $\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha)$, so:

$$F_g \approx W \cdot \frac{G}{100}$$

### 2.2.4 Available Tractive Effort ($F_{TE}$)

$$F_{TE} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_t \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$$

Where:
- $T_e$ = Engine torque at current speed (Nm) — from engine map
- $i_t$ = Transmission gear ratio
- $i_0$ = Final drive (axle) ratio
- $\eta_t$ = Drivetrain efficiency (typically **0.85 – 0.92** for RWD, **0.90 – 0.94** for 4WD)
- $r_d$ = Effective (dynamic) wheel radius in meters

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical 5-formula toolkit** that solves 95% of KTU Grade Holding Test numerical problems. Memorize this table.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Grade Resistance | $F_g = W \cdot \sin(\alpha) \approx W \cdot G/100$ | N (kg·m/s²) | Always (if grade given) |
| 2 | Rolling Resistance | $F_r = f_r \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha) \approx f_r \cdot W$ | N | Always |
| 3 | Aerodynamic Drag | $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A_f \cdot v^2$ | N | At $v > 60$ km/h mostly |
| 4 | Tractive Effort | $F_{TE} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_t \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$ | N | Always (engine side) |
| 5 | Max Grade Held | $G_{max}(\%) = \dfrac{100 \cdot (F_{TE} - F_r - F_a)}{W}$ | % | **The Final Answer** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Boundary Conditions** to remember:
> 1. Grade Holding is defined at **constant velocity** ⟹ $\sum F = 0$
> 2. **Lowest gear** is used ⟹ maximum $F_{TE}$ is available
> 3. If $F_{TE} \leq F_r + F_a$ at level road, vehicle **cannot move** from rest on flat road itself (theoretical)
> 4. Grade percentage is **NOT** the same as the angle in degrees: $\alpha = \arctan(G/100)$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Derivation: From Newton's Law to Maximum Grade

We begin with the **dynamic equation of motion** for a vehicle on an inclined surface:

$$M \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a - F_g$$

**Step 1 — Apply the Constant Speed Condition (Grade Holding Definition)**

Since the Grade Holding Test requires **constant speed**, the acceleration term vanishes:

$$\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$$

Substituting into the equation:

$$0 = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a - F_g$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange to Isolate Grade Resistance**

$$F_g = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the Functional Form of $F_g$**

$$W \cdot \sin(\alpha) = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\sin(\alpha_{max})$**

$$\sin(\alpha_{max}) = \frac{F_{TE} - F_r - F_a}{W}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Grade Percentage**

Using the engineering approximation $\sin(\alpha) \approx \tan(\alpha) = G/100$ for small-to-moderate slopes:

$$\frac{G_{max}}{100} = \frac{F_{TE} - F_r - F_a}{W}$$

**Step 6 — Final Form (the KTU Gold Equation)**

$$\boxed{G_{max} \,(\%) = \frac{100 \cdot (F_{TE} - F_r - F_a)}{W}}$$

## 3.2 Comprehensive Numerical Solved Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem Statement
A passenger car has the following data:
- Gross vehicle weight: $W = 15{,}000$ N
- Engine torque at test speed: $T_e = 180$ Nm
- First gear ratio: $i_t = 3.5$
- Final drive ratio: $i_0 = 3.7$
- Drivetrain efficiency: $\eta_t = 0.90$
- Dynamic wheel radius: $r_d = 0.32$ m
- Rolling resistance coefficient: $f_r = 0.012$
- Test speed: $v = 40$ km/h
- Aerodynamic data: $C_d = 0.35$, $A_f = 2.1$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
- Ignore aerodynamic drag (low speed assumption)

**Required:** Determine the maximum grade (%) the vehicle can hold at the test speed.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step A — Calculate the Tractive Effort (TE)**

$$F_{TE} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_t \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$$

$$F_{TE} = \frac{180 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 3.7 \cdot 0.90}{0.32}$$

**Step B — Evaluate Numerator**

$$\text{Numerator} = 180 \cdot 3.5 \cdot 3.7 \cdot 0.90$$

$$= 180 \cdot 3.5 = 630$$

$$= 630 \cdot 3.7 = 2331$$

$$= 2331 \cdot 0.90 = 2097.9 \; \text{Nm}$$

**Step C — Divide by Wheel Radius**

$$F_{TE} = \frac{2097.9}{0.32} = 6555.94 \; \text{N}$$

**Step D — Calculate Rolling Resistance**

$$F_r = f_r \cdot W = 0.012 \cdot 15000 = 180 \; \text{N}$$

**Step E — Apply Grade Holding Force Balance**

$$F_g = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a = 6555.94 - 180 - 0 = 6375.94 \; \text{N}$$

**Step F — Convert to Grade Percentage**

$$G_{max} (\%) = \frac{100 \cdot F_g}{W} = \frac{100 \cdot 6375.94}{15000}$$

$$G_{max} = \frac{637594}{15000} = 42.50 \;\%$$

**Step G — Verify Angle Validity**

$$\alpha_{max} = \arctan(0.4250) = 23.03°$$

Since $\sin(23.03°) = 0.3911$ vs $\tan(23.03°) = 0.4250$, the approximation is **~8% off**, so the more accurate value is:

$$G_{exact} = 100 \cdot \sin(\alpha) = 100 \cdot 0.3911 = 39.11 \;\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** For grades above 30%, the **exact trigonometric form** $\sin(\alpha)$ is preferred over $\tan(\alpha)$ in the final answer. For grades below 30%, the simplified form is accepted.

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Why Tractive Effort Diagram Matters)

The Grade Holding Test is best understood by comparing tractive effort curves with resistance curves as functions of vehicle speed.

| Region of Tractive Effort Curve | Behavior on Grade | Result |
|---|---|---|
| $F_{TE} > F_r + F_a + F_g$ | Tractive > Resistance | Vehicle **accelerates** up the grade |
| $F_{TE} = F_r + F_a + F_g$ | **Critical Equilibrium** | **Grade is held at constant speed** ✅ |
| $F_{TE} < F_r + F_a + F_g$ | Tractive < Resistance | Vehicle **decelerates** (engine lugs, may stall) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Engineering Insight:** Modern automatic transmissions use the Grade Holding Test data to **calibrate the shift map** — i.e., the ECU decides at what speed and load to upshift/downshift to maintain the optimal tractive effort for the prevailing road grade.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Force Interaction During Grade Holding

The following Mermaid block-diagram illustrates the **functional architecture** of all forces interacting at the tire-road interface during a Grade Holding Test, mapping how each component contributes to the equilibrium condition.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Engine Torque Te] --> B[Transmission Gear Ratio it]
    B --> C[Final Drive Ratio i0]
    C --> D[Drivetrain Efficiency eta_t]
    D --> E[Effective Tractive Effort FTE at Wheels]
    E --> F{Force Equilibrium at Constant Speed}
    F -->|Equilibrium| G[Grade Holding Achieved]
    F -->|FTE Surplus| H[Vehicle Accelerates Uphill]
    F -->|FTE Deficit| I[Vehicle Decelerates Engine Lugs]
    J[Vehicle Weight W] --> K[Rolling Resistance Fr = f_r x W x cos alpha]
    J --> L[Grade Resistance Fg = W x sin alpha approx W x G/100]
    M[Air Density rho] --> N[Aerodynamic Drag Fa = 0.5 x rho x Cd x Af x v squared]
    K --> F
    L --> F
    N --> F
    O[Test Speed v constant] --> N
    P[Road Grade Angle alpha] --> K
    P --> L
    Q[Maximum Grade Gmax percent] --> G
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow: Test Execution Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1: Vehicle Warm Up] --> S2[Step 2: Instrument and Sensor Calibration]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Select Lowest Gear and Test Speed]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Approach Test Incline at Constant Speed]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Maintain Throttle and Speed]
    S5 --> S6{Speed Holds for 20 sec}
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Record Current Grade as Gmax]
    S6 -->|No Speed Drop| S8[Reduce Grade Increment and Retry]
    S8 --> S5
    S7 --> S9[Compute Grade Holding Capacity in percent]
    S9 --> S10[Document Result per KTU Reporting Format]
```

## 4.3 Visual Interpretation of the Critical Equilibrium Point

```
Forces (N)
    ^
    |
F_TE|---------____________________
    |        /
    |       /        <-- Tractive Effort Curve
    |      /
    |     /
    |    /
    |   /
    |  /        ___
    | /       /   \  <-- Total Resistance Curve
    |/      /  ★  \     (Fr + Fa + Fg)
    |     /   ↑    \
    |____/____|_____\________> Speed (km/h)
         0   v_test
             ↑
         Grade Holding Point
         (F_TE = F_R + F_A + F_G)
```

> [!NOTE]
> The star (★) marks the **Grade Holding Equilibrium Point**. The vehicle can hold this grade at $v_{test}$ because the tractive effort exactly matches the sum of all resistances. Any further increase in grade (or decrease in tractive effort) will cause speed to fall.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define the Grade Holding Test. How is it different from the maximum gradeability test?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Grade Holding Test is a vehicle performance evaluation that determines the **maximum road gradient** a vehicle can ascend while **maintaining a constant specified speed** (typically 40 km/h in the lowest gear), under steady-state conditions. **(1.5 marks)**

It differs from the **maximum gradeability test** (also called the "starting gradeability" test) in that the latter measures the steepest slope a vehicle can **start moving on from a standstill** (acceleration from zero), whereas the Grade Holding Test measures the steepest slope a vehicle can **sustain at a fixed non-zero velocity**. **(1.5 marks)**

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**State the governing equation for Grade Holding Test. List all forces involved.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The governing equation is: $F_{TE} = F_r + F_a + F_g$ **(1 mark)**, where $F_{TE}$ is the tractive effort at the wheels. The forces are:
- $F_r$ = Rolling resistance (1 mark)
- $F_a$ = Aerodynamic drag (0.5 marks)
- $F_g$ = Grade resistance (0.5 marks)

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Computational Heavy

#### `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for maximum grade a vehicle can hold at constant speed, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A truck has the following specifications: $W = 45{,}000$ N, engine torque at test speed = 420 Nm, 1st gear ratio = 4.2, axle ratio = 5.0, drivetrain efficiency = 0.88, wheel radius = 0.48 m, $f_r = 0.015$, $C_d = 0.6$, $A_f = 4.5$ m², $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³, $v = 50$ km/h. Calculate the **maximum grade holding capacity** in percentage. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write the equation of motion on an incline (1 mark):

$$M \cdot \frac{dv}{dt} = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a - F_g$$

**Step 2:** Apply constant speed condition $\frac{dv}{dt} = 0$ (1 mark):

$$0 = F_{TE} - F_r - F_a - F_g$$

**Step 3:** Substitute force expressions (2 marks):

- $F_r = f_r \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha)$
- $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$
- $F_g = W \cdot \sin(\alpha)$

$$W \cdot \sin(\alpha) = F_{TE} - f_r W \cos(\alpha) - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$$

**Step 4:** For small angles, $\cos(\alpha) \approx 1$ and $\sin(\alpha) \approx G/100$ (1 mark)

$$\frac{G_{max}}{100} \cdot W = F_{TE} - f_r W - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2$$

**Step 5:** Final boxed expression (2 marks):

$$\boxed{G_{max} \,(\%) = \frac{100}{W} \left( F_{TE} - f_r W - \tfrac{1}{2} \rho C_d A_f v^2 \right)}$$

**Assumptions:**
- Steady-state, constant velocity
- Small grade angle
- No wheel slip
- Wind speed = 0

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Convert units (1 mark)**
$$v = 50 \; \text{km/h} = \frac{50 \times 1000}{3600} = 13.89 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Tractive Effort (2 marks)**
$$F_{TE} = \frac{T_e \cdot i_t \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} = \frac{420 \times 4.2 \times 5.0 \times 0.88}{0.48}$$
$$= \frac{420 \times 4.2 \times 5.0 \times 0.88}{0.48} = \frac{7761.6}{0.48} = 16{,}170 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Rolling Resistance (1 mark)**
$$F_r = 0.015 \times 45000 = 675 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 4: Calculate Aerodynamic Drag (1 mark)**
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 4.5 \times (13.89)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.6 \times 4.5 \times 192.93$$
$$F_a = 312.55 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 5: Compute Grade Resistance (1 mark)**
$$F_g = 16{,}170 - 675 - 312.55 = 15{,}182.45 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 6: Convert to Grade % (1 mark)**
$$G_{max} = \frac{100 \times 15{,}182.45}{45{,}000} = 33.74 \;\%$$

**Final Answer:** The truck can hold a maximum grade of **≈ 33.74%** at 50 km/h.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Conceptual + Diagrammatic

#### `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified]`
**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the tractive effort versus vehicle speed diagram and identify the **grade holding region** on it. Discuss how gear selection affects the maximum holdable grade. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 4WD vehicle is being tested on a 20% grade. The vehicle has $W = 20{,}000$ N, $f_r = 0.018$, and the engine delivers 250 Nm of torque at the test rpm. If $i_t = 3.8$, $i_0 = 4.1$, $\eta_t = 0.92$, $r_d = 0.35$ m, and the test speed is 60 km/h, determine the **excess or deficit tractive effort**. Will the vehicle successfully hold this grade? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Conceptual (7 marks):**

The Tractive Effort (TE) vs. Speed diagram is a fundamental KTU visualization tool:

- **X-axis:** Vehicle speed $v$ (km/h) — typically 0 to 200
- **Y-axis:** Tractive effort at wheels $F_{TE}$ (N or kN)
- **TE curve shape:** Hyperbolic, decreasing as $v$ increases (because $T_e$ decreases with engine speed, and TE = $T_e \cdot i \cdot \eta / r$)
- **Resistance curve:** Sum of $F_r$ (constant, horizontal) + $F_a$ (parabolic, increasing with $v^2$) + $F_g$ (constant, depends on grade)

**The Grade Holding Region** is where the **TE curve lies above the total resistance curve**. Any speed in this region allows the vehicle to hold that grade. **(2 marks for diagram, 2 marks for explanation)**

**Effect of Gear Selection (3 marks):**
- **1st gear** has the highest gear ratio $i_t$ → maximum TE at low speed → best for steep grades
- **Higher gears** reduce $i_t$ → lower TE at higher speed → can only hold milder grades
- For a fixed grade, there exists a **critical speed** in each gear below which the grade is holdable

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute TE (2 marks)**
$$F_{TE} = \frac{250 \times 3.8 \times 4.1 \times 0.92}{0.35} = \frac{3581.8}{0.35} = 10{,}233.7 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 2: Compute $F_r$ (1 mark)**
$$F_r = 0.018 \times 20{,}000 = 360 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 3: Compute $F_g$ for 20% grade (1 mark)**
$$F_g = \frac{20}{100} \times 20{,}000 = 4000 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 4: Compute $F_a$ at 60 km/h (1.5 marks)**
$$v = 16.67 \; \text{m/s}$$
$$F_a = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times (16.67)^2 = 0.5 \times 1.2 \times 0.35 \times 2.2 \times 277.89$$
$$F_a \approx 128.3 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 5: Total Resistance (0.5 marks)**
$$F_{R,total} = 360 + 4000 + 128.3 = 4488.3 \; \text{N}$$

**Step 6: Compare (1 mark)**
$$F_{TE} - F_{R,total} = 10{,}233.7 - 4488.3 = 5745.4 \; \text{N} \;(\text{SURPLUS})$$

**Conclusion:** The vehicle has an **excess tractive effort of 5745.4 N** and will **successfully hold** the 20% grade with margin to spare (can potentially hold up to ~52% grade).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s in the aerodynamic drag formula — costs **1–2 marks** if you use 60 directly.
> 2. **Sign convention confusion:** Many students write $G_{max} = 100 \cdot (F_r + F_a - F_{TE})/W$ (wrong sign) — this gives a **negative** grade. Always remember: $F_{TE}$ provides the **driving force**, resistances oppose it.
> 3. **Forgetting to include aerodynamic drag:** At higher test speeds (60+ km/h), neglecting $F_a$ causes noticeable error. Always check if speed > 50 km/h.
> 4. **Confusing Grade % with Angle:** The problem gives "20% grade", so use $\tan(\alpha) = 0.20$, **NOT** $\alpha = 20°$.
> 5. **Wrong drivetrain efficiency:** Always multiply by $\eta_t$ in the numerator. Some students divide by it, which gives unrealistically high TE values.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Grade Holding Test** = maximum constant speed sustained on a given grade
- [x] **Governing equation:** $F_{TE} = F_r + F_a + F_g$ (force balance at $dv/dt = 0$)
- [x] **Final formula:** $G_{max}(\%) = \frac{100(F_{TE} - F_r - F_a)}{W}$
- [x] **Tractive effort:** $F_{TE} = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_t \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$
- [x] **Grade resistance:** $F_g = W \cdot \sin(\alpha) \approx W \cdot G/100$ (small angle)
- [x] **Rolling resistance:** $F_r = f_r \cdot W \cdot \cos(\alpha) \approx f_r \cdot W$
- [x] **Aerodynamic drag:** $F_a = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho C_d A_f v^2$ — negligible below 50 km/h
- [x] **Standard test speed:** 40–50 km/h in **lowest gear** (1st or 2nd)
- [x] **Difference from gradeability test:** GHT is at constant speed; gradeability is from rest
- [x] **Always use 1st gear for maximum grade holding** (highest $i_t$)
- [x] **$\alpha = \arctan(G/100)$**, never $\alpha = G\%$ in degrees
- [x] **$F_{TE}$ exceeds resistance** → accelerating; **$F_{TE}$ equals resistance** → grade holding; **$F_{TE}$ below resistance** → lugging
- [x] For grades > 30%, prefer $\sin(\alpha)$ exact form over $\tan(\alpha)$ approximation

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Test Requirements: Ground Boundary Simulation, Wind Tunnel Selection and Reynolds Number Capability
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test Requirements: Ground Boundary Simulation, Wind Tunnel Selection and Reynolds Number Capability

# Test Requirements: Ground Boundary Simulation, Wind Tunnel Selection and Reynolds Number Capability

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ground Boundary Simulation

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
*Ground Boundary Simulation* refers to the set of aerodynamic test techniques employed within an automotive wind tunnel to replicate the relative motion between a stationary vehicle and the moving road surface beneath it. Since the test vehicle remains fixed while air flows over it (in a conventional ground-fixed facility), the boundary layer that naturally develops on the tunnel floor upstream of the vehicle must be managed because it does **not** represent the boundary layer found on a real road during vehicle operation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In full-scale vehicle aerodynamics, the relative velocity between the car body and the road surface is **zero** (the car moves with the road). However, in a fixed-floor wind tunnel, the road (tunnel floor) is **stationary** while the air moves — this creates a thick, adverse boundary layer that contaminates underbody and wake flow measurements.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine standing still on a moving walkway at an airport while holding a fixed umbrella above your head. From your perspective, the air rushes past you (you feel wind), and the walkway surface moves under your feet — this mimics real driving. Now, imagine the walkway is suddenly *frozen*. Air still rushes past you, but the ground is no longer moving — this is exactly what happens in a basic wind tunnel. The *frozen ground* creates a thick layer of slow-moving, turbulent air near the floor that sticks to and curls around the test vehicle, distorting lift, drag, and wake measurements. *Ground Boundary Simulation* is the engineering solution to "un-freeze" or compensate for this frozen ground.

**Three Primary Approaches to Ground Boundary Simulation:**

| Method | Operating Principle | Realism Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Fixed Flat Floor with Suction** | Suction slot removes the thick boundary layer before it reaches the car | Moderate |
| **Raised Test Section / Boundary Layer Removal** | Vehicle placed on a raised platform; air bled from beneath | Moderate |
| **Moving Belt System (Five-Belt or Single-Belt)** | Endless belts move at tunnel airspeed, simulating a real road | **High (Best Practice)** |

### 1.2 Wind Tunnel Selection

**Formal Definition:**
*Wind Tunnel Selection* is the engineering decision process by which an aerodynamic test facility is chosen based on test-section velocity range, blockage ratio, drive power, test-section geometry (open/closed/slotted), and the type of measurement (full-scale, scale model, or component).

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Classification:** Wind tunnels are primarily classified by (a) **circuit type** — open-circuit (blow-down or suck-down) versus closed-circuit (closed-return/recirculating), and (b) **speed regime** — subsonic, transonic, supersonic, hypersonic.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of choosing a wind tunnel like choosing the right washing machine. A small top-loader is fine for a few gym clothes (small subsonic tests on models), but you need a heavy-duty front-loader for king-sized comforters (full-scale car testing at highway speeds). The "size of the drum" (test section cross-section), the "spin speed" (maximum airflow velocity), and whether it's a "front-loader or top-loader" (open vs closed test section) all dictate suitability.

**Industry-Standard Reference Facilities:**
- **Full-Scale Automotive Tunnels:** MIRA (UK), FKFS (Germany), Pininfarina (Italy), GM Aerodynamics Lab (USA).
- These typically operate in the velocity range **0–250 km/h** with test sections ~10 m wide × 5 m high.

### 1.3 Reynolds Number Capability

**Formal Definition:**
*Reynolds Number Capability* of a wind tunnel defines the maximum dimensionless Reynolds number ($Re$) that can be achieved within the test section. It quantifies the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in the flow and must be matched between the scale model and the full-scale prototype to ensure **dynamic similarity**.

$$Re = \frac{\rho \cdot V \cdot L_c}{\mu} = \frac{V \cdot L_c}{\nu}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Constant:** The kinematic viscosity of air at standard atmospheric conditions (15°C, 1 atm) is **$\nu$ = 1.46 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. This is the default value used in KTU board exam numericals unless stated otherwise.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Reynolds number is like the "viscous personality index" of a flow. At *low Re* (e.g., a mosquito flying), viscous forces dominate — the air feels like thick honey, and the flow smoothly wraps around obstacles (laminar). At *high Re* (e.g., a truck at highway speed), inertial forces dominate — the flow is aggressive, chaotic, and turbulent, with sharp pressure drops and vortex shedding. A wind tunnel must reach the **same Re** as real driving conditions for the test to "feel" aerodynamically equivalent.

---

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Ground Boundary Layer Physics

**Why the Floor Boundary Layer Matters in Automotive Testing:**
1. **Lift Force Distortion:** The boundary layer on the tunnel floor creates a low-pressure wake region behind the wheels, artificially increasing downforce readings.
2. **Wake Structure Distortion:** The car wake mixes with the floor BL, altering vortex shedding from the rear.
3. **Cooling Airflow Errors:** Underbody and engine-bay cooling flows are drastically affected.
4. **Yaw Angle Sensitivity:** Cross-wind sensitivity tests become unreliable with thick floor BL.

**Operational Logic of the Moving-Belt System:**

| Step | Physical Action | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Two longitudinal belts move under the car wheels | Eliminates wheel-rotation relative motion |
| 2 | A wide transverse belt moves beneath the full car footprint | Simulates road surface motion |
| 3 | Boundary layer suction at the leading edge of the belts | Prevents leading-edge BL growth |
| 4 | Belt speed = freestream velocity ($V_\infty$) | Achieves zero relative velocity at road surface |
| 5 | Cooling ducts powered via onboard fan (or external air supply) | Maintains engine-cooling realism |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The Porsche 5-belt system (Windisch, 1984) and the single-belt systems used at FKFS (Stuttgart) are *the* benchmark references for KTU viva questions on ground simulation. Remember — **Five belts = 2 under each side wheels + 1 central transverse belt**.

### 2.2 Wind Tunnel Selection Decision Matrix

**Selection Criteria Hierarchy (KTU Weightage Order):**

1. **Test Section Size** — Must accommodate full vehicle + boundary-layer management apparatus. Typical blockage ratio **< 5%** for road vehicles (SAE J1252).
2. **Maximum Velocity** — Modern automotive tunnels reach **up to 300 km/h** (~83.3 m/s) for race-car aerodynamics.
3. **Closed vs. Open Circuit** — Closed-circuit preferred for thermal stability and reduced noise; open-circuit cheaper to construct.
4. **Drive Power** — For closed-circuit subsonic automotive tunnels: **~2.5–4 MW** of electric motor power is typical.
5. **Slatted Walls / Slotted Test Sections** — Used to reduce solid-blockage effects at the cost of some flow quality.

### 2.3 Reynolds Number Capability — Deep Theory

**The Reynolds Number Independence Principle:**
For bluff bodies (such as cars), aerodynamic coefficients ($C_D$, $C_L$) become approximately **independent of Re** above a critical value ($Re_{crit} \approx 10^5$ to $10^6$ for road vehicles). Below this threshold, the coefficients are highly Re-dependent.

**Scaling Law for Model Testing:**
For a geometrically similar scale model (scale ratio $\lambda = L_m/L_p$), Reynolds number matching requires:

$$V_m = V_p \cdot \left(\frac{L_p}{L_m}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p}\right)$$

If the same fluid (air) is used in both model and full-scale test: $\nu_m = \nu_p$, so:

$$V_m = \frac{V_p}{\lambda}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Trap:** For a 1:4 scale model ($L_m = L_p/4$) tested at full-scale velocity $V_p = 40$ m/s, the model test velocity must be $V_m = 160$ m/s — *often exceeding subsonic tunnel capability*. This is the key engineering reason for using **pressurized tunnels** or **cryogenic tunnels** (e.g., DNA's cryogenic facility at ONERA) to achieve high $Re$ with small models.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Description | Standard Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho V L_c}{\mu}$ | $\rho$=density, $V$=velocity, $L_c$=chord, $\mu$=dynamic viscosity | $\rho$: kg/m³, $V$: m/s, $L_c$: m, $\mu$: Pa·s | General Re definition |
