| 2 | $Re = \dfrac{V L_c}{\nu}$ | $\nu$ = kinematic viscosity | m²/s | Re with kinematic viscosity |
| 3 | $V_m = \dfrac{V_p}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda = L_m/L_p$ | dimensionless | Scale-model velocity matching |
| 4 | $Re_m = Re_p \cdot \dfrac{V_m L_m}{V_p L_p}$ | Reynolds number scaling | dimensionless | Re-matching verification |
| 5 | $\text{Blockage} = \dfrac{A_{model}}{A_{test}} \times 100\%$ | $A$=frontal area | % | SAE blockage limit $\leq$ 5% |
| 6 | $\delta_{BL} \approx 0.37 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/5}$ | $\delta$=BL thickness, $x$=distance | m | Turbulent BL on flat floor |
| 7 | $Re_{crit} \approx 3 \times 10^5$ | Critical Re for car body | dimensionless | Re independence threshold |
| 8 | $q_\infty = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho V^2$ | Dynamic pressure | Pa | Used to compute $C_D$, $C_L$ |
| 9 | $P_{tunnel} = \tfrac{1}{2} \dot{m} V^2 (1 + \text{losses})$ | Drive power estimate | W (Watts) | Wind tunnel power sizing |
| 10 | $\nu_{air}(15°C) = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ | Std. air kinematic viscosity | m²/s | KTU default value |

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Velocity Required for a Scale Model to Match Full-Scale Reynolds Number

**Problem Setup:**
A passenger car of wheelbase length $L_p = 2.6$ m is to be tested using a 1:4 scale model in a subsonic wind tunnel using standard air at 15°C. The full-scale cruise velocity is $V_p = 30$ m/s. Determine:
(a) The Reynolds number at full scale.
(b) The required model-test velocity for Re-matching.
(c) Whether this velocity is feasible in a standard subsonic tunnel.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Part (a) — Full-Scale Reynolds Number:**

$$\begin{aligned}
L_c &= L_p = 2.6 \text{ m} \quad \text{(taken as the characteristic length)} \\
V_p &= 30 \text{ m/s} \\
\nu_{air} &= 1.46 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} \quad \text{(standard KTU value)} \\
Re_p &= \frac{V_p \cdot L_p}{\nu} \\
Re_p &= \frac{(30)(2.6)}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} \\
Re_p &= \frac{78}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} \\
Re_p &= 5.342 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** $Re_p \approx 5.34 \times 10^6$, which is **comfortably above** the critical Re for car-body bluff-body flow ($\sim 3 \times 10^5$). Thus full-scale flow is in the Re-independent regime.

**Part (b) — Required Model Velocity:**

For Re-matching using the same fluid (air):

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_m &= Re_p \\
\frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu} &= \frac{V_p \cdot L_p}{\nu} \\
V_m \cdot L_m &= V_p \cdot L_p \\
V_m &= V_p \cdot \frac{L_p}{L_m} \\
V_m &= V_p \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda}
\end{aligned}$$

Given $\lambda = L_m/L_p = 1/4 = 0.25$:

$$\begin{aligned}
V_m &= 30 \cdot \frac{1}{0.25} \\
V_m &= 30 \cdot 4 \\
V_m &= 120 \text{ m/s} \\
V_m &= 432 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (c) — Feasibility Analysis:**

A velocity of 120 m/s corresponds to **Mach 0.35** (since $a = 343$ m/s at 15°C). This is approaching the transonic regime, where compressibility effects would invalidate the low-Mach-number assumption. Therefore, the test is **infeasible in a standard subsonic tunnel**.

**Engineering Workarounds:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Option 1: Pressurized Tunnel} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{ Increase } \rho \text{ and decrease } \nu \\
\text{Option 2: Cryogenic Tunnel} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{ Cool air to } -150°C \text{, } \nu \downarrow 5 \times \\
\text{Option 3: Reynolds Inadequacy Acceptance} \quad & \Rightarrow \text{ Test at } Re_{m,\max} \text{ and apply correction}
\end{aligned}$$

For Option 1, using compressed air at 5 atm with $\nu \approx \nu_0 / 5$ (approximation):

$$\begin{aligned}
V_{m, corrected} &= \frac{V_p}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\nu_m}{\nu_p} \\
V_{m, corrected} &= 120 \cdot \frac{1}{5} \\
V_{m, corrected} &= 24 \text{ m/s} \quad \text{(feasible in subsonic tunnel)}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reasoning Marker:** Always show this triple-step logic — (i) compute $Re_p$, (ii) derive $V_m$ formula, (iii) compare with tunnel capability — for full marks in 14-mark problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boundary Layer Thickness on the Tunnel Floor

**Step-by-Step Derivation (Turbulent Flat-Plate BL):**

The empirical power-law formula for turbulent boundary layer thickness over a smooth flat plate is:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(x) &= 0.37 \cdot x \cdot Re_x^{-1/5} \\
\text{where} \quad Re_x &= \frac{V_\infty \cdot x}{\nu}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting $Re_x$:

$$\begin{aligned}
\delta(x) &= 0.37 \cdot x \cdot \left(\frac{V_\infty \cdot x}{\nu}\right)^{-1/5} \\
\delta(x) &= 0.37 \cdot x^{1 - 1/5} \cdot \left(\frac{V_\infty}{\nu}\right)^{-1/5} \\
\delta(x) &= 0.37 \cdot x^{4/5} \cdot \left(\frac{\nu}{V_\infty}\right)^{1/5}
\end{aligned}$$

**Numerical Example:** For $V_\infty = 30$ m/s, $x = 8$ m upstream of the vehicle, and $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_x &= \frac{(30)(8)}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 1.644 \times 10^7 \\
\delta &= 0.37 \cdot 8 \cdot (1.644 \times 10^7)^{-1/5} \\
\delta &= 0.37 \cdot 8 \cdot (1.644 \times 10^7)^{-0.2} \\
\delta &= 0.37 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.0883 \\
\delta &\approx 0.261 \text{ m} \approx 26.1 \text{ cm}
\end{aligned}$$

**Engineering Implication:** A 26 cm thick boundary layer on the floor would interact severely with the underbody of a car (typical ground clearance 15–20 cm) — hence the **critical need** for ground-boundary simulation or suction.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Tunnel-Type Selection for Different Vehicle Tests

| Vehicle Class | Test Type | Recommended Tunnel | Velocity Range | Re Target | Special Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Passenger Sedan | Full-Scale $C_D$ | Closed-circuit, $10 \text{ m} \times 5 \text{ m}$ | 0–200 km/h | $>3 \times 10^6$ | 5-belt ground sim |
| Race Car (F1) | Downforce ($C_L$) | Closed-circuit with moving floor | 0–300 km/h | $>10^7$ | Sliding floor or 5-belt |
| Truck / Bus | Drag with yaw | Open-circuit, large section | 0–150 km/h | $>5 \times 10^6$ | Yaw table ±15° |
| 1:4 Scale Prototype | Component | Subsonic pressurized | 0–100 m/s | Match Re | Boundary-layer suction |
| Cooling-Flow Test | Underbody airflow | Climate tunnel ($T$ controlled) | 0–120 km/h | Match full scale | Heat-exchanger simulation |
| Wind Noise / Aeroacoustics | Interior / exterior | Anechoic open-jet | 0–250 km/h | Match full scale | Microphone array, Kevlar walls |

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Why is a moving-belt system preferred over a fixed floor in an automotive wind tunnel?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A moving-belt system is preferred because it simulates the actual kinematic condition of a vehicle moving on a real road — the relative velocity between the road surface and the vehicle is **zero**. In a fixed-floor wind tunnel, the stationary floor creates a thick boundary layer that contaminates underbody, wake, and lift measurements. A moving belt:
1. Eliminates floor boundary-layer growth beneath the vehicle.
2. Provides correct wheel-rotation relative motion.
3. Preserves correct underbody cooling flow patterns.
4. Produces realistic wake structure downstream of the car.
This makes the test data quantitatively representative of actual road driving conditions. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Define Reynolds number and state its significance in automotive wind tunnel testing.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Reynolds number is the dimensionless ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$Re = \frac{\rho V L_c}{\mu} = \frac{V L_c}{\nu}$$

**Significance in automotive testing:**
1. It determines the flow regime — laminar vs. turbulent.
2. For bluff bodies like cars, $C_D$ and $C_L$ become approximately Re-independent above a **critical Reynolds number** of $\sim 3 \times 10^5$.
3. Re-matching ensures **dynamic similarity** between a scale model and the full-scale prototype.
4. Inadequate Re matching is the most common source of aerodynamic test error in scale-model experiments. **(3 marks)**

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **five-belt moving floor system** used in modern automotive wind tunnels. Discuss its advantages and limitations compared to a fixed floor with boundary-layer suction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1:5 scale model of a sedan car (full-scale length 4.5 m) is to be tested in a subsonic wind tunnel at standard atmospheric conditions. The full-scale cruise speed is 90 km/h. Calculate:
(i) The full-scale Reynolds number.
(ii) The model test velocity required for Re-matching.
(iii) Comment on the feasibility of this test in a conventional subsonic tunnel (max velocity 60 m/s) and suggest two engineering solutions. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The five-belt moving floor system consists of:
- **Two longitudinal belts** running under each side row of wheels (4 belts total), matching the wheel rotation speed with the freestream velocity.
- **One wide central transverse belt** running across the car footprint (the 5th belt).
- A **boundary-layer suction slot** at the leading edge of the belts to prevent upstream BL from contaminating the test section.
- The belts are driven by synchronized electric motors controlled via closed-loop feedback to match tunnel freestream velocity exactly.

**Sketch (description for KTU board):**
Draw a top-view of the test section. Show the rectangular car footprint with 4 wheels. Show 2 long belts on each side running longitudinally under the wheels. Show the 5th belt as a wider transverse belt covering the central body region. Indicate the suction slot at the upstream edge and the freestream velocity arrows.

**Comparison:**

| Criterion | Five-Belt System | Fixed Floor + Suction |
|---|---|---|
| Floor BL elimination | Excellent | Moderate (residual BL) |
| Wheel rotation realism | Yes | No |
| Cost | Very high | Moderate |
| Maintenance | Complex (belt wear) | Simple |
| Cooling-flow accuracy | High | Low to moderate |

**Advantages of five-belt:** Eliminates floor BL, correct wheel motion, accurate wake.
**Limitations:** Very expensive, complex drive synchronization, belt tensioning issues, limited use to road-vehicle geometries. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- Scale ratio: $\lambda = 1/5 = 0.2$
- $L_p = 4.5$ m, $L_m = L_p \cdot \lambda = 4.5 \cdot 0.2 = 0.9$ m
- $V_p = 90$ km/h $= 25$ m/s
- $\nu_{air} = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**(i) Full-scale Reynolds number:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_p &= \frac{V_p \cdot L_p}{\nu} \\
Re_p &= \frac{(25)(4.5)}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} \\
Re_p &= \frac{112.5}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} \\
Re_p &= 7.705 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Required model test velocity:**

$$\begin{aligned}
V_m &= \frac{V_p}{\lambda} = \frac{25}{0.2} \\
V_m &= 125 \text{ m/s} = 450 \text{ km/h}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Feasibility Analysis:**
The required model velocity (125 m/s) is **far greater** than the tunnel maximum (60 m/s). Therefore, the test is **infeasible** in the conventional subsonic tunnel without modification.

**Engineering Solutions:**
1. **Pressurized Tunnel:** Increase air pressure to ~3 atm to raise density ($\rho \uparrow$) and reduce kinematic viscosity ($\nu \downarrow$). Required pressure ratio $\approx V_m/V_{tunnel,max} = 125/60 \approx 2.08$. So a pressure of approximately **2.5 atm** would suffice.
2. **Cryogenic Tunnel:** Cool air to cryogenic temperatures (-150°C) to reduce kinematic viscosity by ~5×, lowering the required velocity to ~25 m/s — well within tunnel capability.
3. **Alternative:** Accept a lower $Re_m$ if the operating $Re_p$ lies in the Re-independent bluff-body regime (which it does, since $Re_p \approx 7.7 \times 10^6 \gg Re_{crit}$); perform correction using established Re-sensitivity curves. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Discuss the criteria for selection of a wind tunnel for full-scale automotive aerodynamic testing. Compare open-circuit and closed-circuit tunnels with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A wind tunnel test is conducted at $V = 50$ m/s with a 1:3 scale car model. The full-scale vehicle length is 3.6 m. Air at standard conditions is used. Determine:
(i) The model Reynolds number and the full-scale equivalent Reynolds number.
(ii) Whether the test falls in the Re-independent bluff-body regime.
(iii) If a moving-belt ground simulation is unavailable, what correction should be applied for a fixed-floor test? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 marks:**

**Selection Criteria for Automotive Wind Tunnels:**

1. **Test Section Size:** Must accommodate full vehicle with adequate clearance. Blockage ratio ≤ 5% (SAE J1252). For a typical car frontal area of 2.2 m², the minimum test-section area is ~44 m².
2. **Maximum Velocity:** Should reach at least 200–250 km/h for high-speed aerodynamic and cooling tests.
3. **Closed vs Open Circuit:** Closed-circuit preferred for thermal stability, reduced ambient noise, and lower contamination. Open-circuit cheaper.
4. **Drive Power:** 2.5–4 MW for large full-scale automotive tunnels.
5. **Boundary Layer Management:** Moving-belt system, suction slots, or raised platforms.
6. **Measurement Systems:** Integrated force balance (6-component), pressure scanners (≥ 512 channels), PIV, Schlieren.
7. **Yaw Capability:** ±15° turntable for cross-wind sensitivity tests.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Open-Circuit | Closed-Circuit |
|---|---|---|
| Cost | Lower | Higher (×2–3) |
| Thermal Stability | Poor | Excellent |
| Noise | High | Low (silencers) |
| Contamination | Low | Possible recirculation |
| Examples | Many university tunnels | MIRA, FKFS, GM Aerolab |

**Examples:**
- **Open-Circuit:** University of Maryland Glenn L. Martin Wind Tunnel.
- **Closed-Circuit:** MIRA Full-Scale Wind Tunnel (UK), FKFS (Stuttgart, Germany). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $V_m = 50$ m/s, $\lambda = 1/3$, $L_p = 3.6$ m, $L_m = 3.6 \times (1/3) = 1.2$ m
- $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s

**(i) Reynolds Numbers:**

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_m &= \frac{V_m \cdot L_m}{\nu} = \frac{(50)(1.2)}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} \\
Re_m &= \frac{60}{1.46 \times 10^{-5}} = 4.11 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}$$

Equivalent full-scale $Re$ at matching velocity:

$$\begin{aligned}
Re_{p,equiv} &= Re_m \quad \text{(by Re-matching in same fluid)} \\
Re_{p,equiv} &= 4.11 \times 10^{6}
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Re-Independence Check:**
The critical Reynolds number for car-body bluff bodies is $Re_{crit} \approx 3 \times 10^5$. Since $Re_m = 4.11 \times 10^6 > Re_{crit}$, the test lies comfortably in the **Re-independent regime**. This is the desired condition for accurate aerodynamic coefficient measurement. **(2 marks)**

**(iii) Fixed-Floor Correction:**
If moving belts are unavailable, the following corrections should be applied:
1. **Boundary Layer Suction:** Install a suction slot upstream of the model to remove the floor BL.
2. **Raised Test Section:** Mount the car on a raised platform so the floor BL passes beneath the car without interference.
3. **Computational Correction (CFD Co-Simulation):** Compute the floor BL effect via CFD and subtract from measured forces.
4. **Empirical Correction:** Apply the SAE J1252 blockage correction factor:

$$C_{D,corrected} = \frac{C_{D,measured}}{1 - K \cdot \epsilon^2}$$

where $\epsilon$ is the blockage ratio and $K$ is a geometry factor (typically 0.4–0.6 for cars). **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch Trap (3-mark loss):** Always convert km/h to m/s before applying the Re formula. KTU examiners explicitly deduct for using mixed units.
> 2. **Forgot to Use $\lambda$ Correctly (4-mark loss):** A very common error is writing $V_m = V_p \cdot \lambda$ instead of $V_m = V_p / \lambda$. Memorize: *smaller model → higher velocity needed*.
> 3. **Skipped Feasibility Comment (2-mark loss):** In 14-mark problems, always conclude with a feasibility statement and engineering workaround if $V_m$ exceeds tunnel capacity.
> 4. **Used $\mu$ instead of $\nu$ (1-mark loss):** The default KTU formula uses $\nu$ (kinematic viscosity). If the problem gives $\mu$, you must compute $\nu = \mu/\rho$ first.
> 5. **Forgot Re-Critical Check:** Even when computing $V_m$, always state whether $Re_m > Re_{crit} \approx 3 \times 10^5$ — this is the *Re-independence* threshold that justifies the test.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Ground Boundary Simulation** is essential because fixed-floor tunnels develop a thick turbulent BL (e.g., ~26 cm at $V = 30$ m/s, $x = 8$ m) that contaminates underbody flow.
- ✅ **Five-belt moving floor** (2 longitudinal × 2 sides + 1 transverse) is the industry gold standard for full-scale automotive testing (FKFS, MIRA, Pininfarina).
- ✅ **Fixed floor with boundary-layer suction** and **raised test section** are cheaper alternatives, but less accurate.
- ✅ **Wind tunnel selection** is dictated by: test-section size, max velocity, circuit type, drive power, and measurement systems.
- ✅ **Closed-circuit tunnels** offer thermal stability and low noise; **open-circuit** is cheaper.
- ✅ **Reynolds number** $Re = VL_c/\nu$ governs dynamic similarity. Critical Re for cars $\approx 3 \times 10^5$.
- ✅ **Scale-model velocity requirement:** $V_m = V_p / \lambda$ (in same fluid). For 1:4 scale, $V_m = 4 \cdot V_p$.
- ✅ **Feasibility issue:** Required model velocities often exceed subsonic tunnel capacity → use **pressurized** or **cryogenic** tunnels.
- ✅ **Reynolds-independent bluff-body regime** is the safe operating region for automotive testing ($Re > Re_{crit}$).
- ✅ **Blockage ratio** must remain $\leq 5\%$ (SAE J1252) for uncorrected measurements.
- ✅ **Standard air kinematic viscosity** at 15°C: $\nu = 1.46 \times 10^{-5}$ m²/s — the default KTU value.
- ✅ **Re-matching** in different fluids (e.g., water) is a viable alternative, but introduces surface-tension and cavitation issues.
- ✅ **Turbulent BL thickness** follows the power law $\delta(x) = 0.37 \, x \, Re_x^{-1/5}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing**, **Model Requirements** refer to the comprehensive set of specifications, criteria, and constraints that govern the development, validation, and application of mathematical and computational models used to predict the dynamic and kinematic behavior of road vehicles. These requirements define the *adequacy*, *fidelity*, *scope*, and *applicability* of a vehicle model for simulation, analysis, and design optimization.

A vehicle performance model is essentially a mathematical abstraction of a real vehicle system, expressed through differential equations, algebraic relations, or computational algorithms. The **model requirements** specify what conditions this abstraction must satisfy to be considered a valid engineering tool.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** Model Requirements define the fundamental criteria — accuracy, validity range, computational cost, parameter identifiability, and physical realizability — that a vehicle simulation model must satisfy to be acceptable for performance prediction, control design, and homologation testing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of designing a vehicle model like **building a scale model of a house for an architect**:

- A **simple cardboard mock-up** (low-fidelity model) is cheap and quick but only shows the *shape* — not whether the plumbing will work.
- A **detailed 3D CAD simulation with physics** (high-fidelity model) is expensive and slow but tells you *exactly* how air will flow, how the structure will respond to wind, and whether doors will jam.
- An **engineering scale wind-tunnel model** (medium-fidelity model) gives the *right answer for the right question* at a *reasonable cost*.

Similarly, in vehicle modeling, you must decide:
1. **What question am I trying to answer?** (fuel economy? ride comfort? crash safety?)
2. **How accurate must the answer be?** (±1%? ±10%?)
3. **How fast do I need the answer?** (real-time? overnight batch?)
4. **What inputs do I have?** (tire data? suspension geometry? engine map?)

These decisions define the **model requirements**.

## 1.3 Core Categories of Model Requirements

The fundamental model requirements in vehicle performance modeling can be classified into six principal categories:

| # | Requirement Category | Core Question It Answers |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Accuracy / Fidelity** | How close are model outputs to real vehicle behavior? |
| 2 | **Validity Domain** | Within what range of inputs/conditions is the model trustworthy? |
| 3 | **Computational Efficiency** | How fast can the model produce results on available hardware? |
| 4 | **Parameter Identifiability** | Can all required parameters be measured/estimated uniquely? |
| 5 | **Physical Realizability** | Do model parameters correspond to measurable physical quantities? |
| 6 | **Modularity & Extensibility** | Can the model be reused, combined, or upgraded easily? |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Module 2 emphasizes that **model requirements are not universal** — they are dictated by the *intended application* (e.g., handling analysis vs. NVH vs. powertrain optimization). A model that satisfies 95% accuracy for one task may be inadequate for another.

## 1.4 Why Model Requirements Matter in Vehicle Engineering

In the automotive industry, vehicle models are used for:

- **Performance prediction** (acceleration, braking, gradeability, top speed)
- **Control system design** (ABS, ESC, active suspension, ADAS)
- **Homologation and certification** (fuel economy, emissions, safety standards)
- **Virtual prototyping** (reducing physical prototype costs)
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HiL) and Software-in-the-Loop (SiL) testing**
- **Driver-in-the-Loop (DiL) simulation for subjective evaluation**

Without clearly defined model requirements, engineers risk building models that are either **overly complex** (slow, unstable, hard to validate) or **insufficiently detailed** (inaccurate, misleading).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Model Requirements

### 2.1.1 Accuracy / Fidelity Requirement

**Definition:** The ability of the model to reproduce measured vehicle responses within a specified tolerance.

**Key Sub-Requirements:**
- **Static accuracy** — agreement at steady-state operating points.
- **Dynamic accuracy** — agreement in transient response (time-domain tracking).
- **Frequency-domain accuracy** — agreement in Bode plots, transfer functions.

**Quantitative Metrics:**
- **Root Mean Square Error (RMSE):**

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_{\text{model},i} - y_{\text{measured},i}\right)^2}$$

- **Normalized RMSE (NRMSE):**

$$\text{NRMSE} = \frac{\text{RMSE}}{y_{\text{max}} - y_{\text{min}}}$$

- **Correlation coefficient (R²):**

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_{\text{model},i} - y_{\text{measured},i}\right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N}\left(y_{\text{measured},i} - \bar{y}_{\text{measured}}\right)^2}$$

**Why it matters:** If a vehicle dynamics model cannot predict lateral acceleration within ±5% during a lane-change maneuver, it is useless for ESC controller tuning.

### 2.1.2 Validity Domain (Operating Range)

**Definition:** The bounded region of inputs, parameters, and operating conditions within which the model produces reliable outputs.

**Components of the validity domain:**
- **Speed range** — e.g., 0 to 250 km/h
- **Steering input range** — e.g., ±180° at rates up to 800°/s
- **Road surface conditions** — dry, wet, snow, split-µ
- **Load conditions** — kerb weight to Gross Vehicle Weight (GVW)
- **Environmental envelope** — temperature, altitude, crosswind

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common error is applying a model *outside its validity domain*. For example, a linear bicycle model derived at low slip angles will fail dramatically above 6–8° slip angle where tire behavior becomes nonlinear.

### 2.1.3 Computational Efficiency

**Definition:** The computational cost (time, memory, FLOPS) required to execute the model for a given workload.

**Categorical Benchmarks:**

| Model Class | Real-Time Factor (RTF) | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Simple point-mass** | 10–100× faster than real-time | Powertrain sizing, energy studies |
| **2-DOF bicycle model** | 5–20× faster than real-time | Handling studies, controller design |
| **7-DOF handling model** | 0.5–2× real-time | ESC tuning, HiL |
| **14–18 DOF full vehicle** | 0.05–0.5× real-time | Ride/handling, DiL |
| **Multi-body (200+ DOF)** | 0.001–0.05× real-time | Detailed durability, NVH |

> Where **RTF = Simulation Time / Real Time** (RTF < 1 means slower than real-time).

### 2.1.4 Parameter Identifiability

**Definition:** The property that all parameters of the model can be uniquely determined from a set of measurable experiments.

**Mathematical Criterion:** A parameter set $\boldsymbol{\theta}$ is **identifiable** if:

$$\mathbf{y}(t,\boldsymbol{\theta}_1) = \mathbf{y}(t,\boldsymbol{\theta}_2) \implies \boldsymbol{\theta}_1 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_2$$

**Practical considerations:**
- Tire-road friction coefficient ($\mu$) is often **non-identifiable** from a single straight-line acceleration test.
- Vertical tire stiffness vs. damping ratio may be **correlated** and require specialized rigs.

### 2.1.5 Physical Realizability (Causality & Passivity)

**Definition:** Model outputs must obey physical laws — energy conservation, causality (output cannot precede input), and passivity.

**Key constraint:** For a passive mechanical system, the **impedance matrix** must be positive real:

$$\text{Re}\{Z(j\omega)\} \geq 0 \quad \forall \, \omega$$

This ensures the model does not generate energy spontaneously — a fatal flaw in vehicle models used for stability analysis.

### 2.1.6 Modularity & Extensibility

**Definition:** The model should be decomposed into independent sub-models (powertrain, tires, suspension, aerodynamics, brakes) connected through standardized interfaces.

**Architectural pattern:** Subsystem modules with well-defined **input/output ports** and **shared bus** (analogous to Simulink subsystems, FMI standard, or AUTOSAR components).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Application / Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Model Accuracy (RMSE) | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i}(y_i^{\text{model}} - y_i^{\text{meas}})^2}$ | Quantifies absolute error |
| 2 | Normalized Error | $\text{NRMSE} = \text{RMSE}/\Delta y_{\text{range}}$ | Dimensionless accuracy |
| 3 | Coefficient of Determination | $R^2 = 1 - \text{SS}_{\text{res}}/\text{SS}_{\text{tot}}$ | Goodness of fit (0 to 1) |
| 4 | Real-Time Factor | $\text{RTF} = t_{\text{sim}}/t_{\text{wall}}$ | Computational speed benchmark |
| 5 | Validity Bounds | $u_{\min} \leq u \leq u_{\max}$ | Input envelope constraint |
| 6 | Identifiability | $\mathbf{y}(t,\boldsymbol{\theta}_1) = \mathbf{y}(t,\boldsymbol{\theta}_2) \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\theta}_1 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_2$ | Parameter uniqueness |
| 7 | Passivity | $\text{Re}\{Z(j\omega)\} \geq 0$ | Physical realizability |
| 8 | Sensitivity Coefficient | $S_{\theta} = \frac{\partial y/y}{\partial \theta/\theta}$ | Parameter influence measure |
| 9 | Model Order (DOF) | $n_{\text{DOF}} = f(\text{phenomena modeled})$ | Complexity selection |
| 10 | Numerical Stability | $\Delta t \leq \Delta t_{\text{crit}} = \frac{2}{\omega_{\max}}$ | CFL-like condition for integration |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

| Application Domain | Critical Model Requirement |
|---|---|
| **EV range prediction** | Accuracy of rolling resistance, drag, battery model within ±2% |
| **ADAS development (AEB, LKA)** | Sub-10 ms latency, sensor noise fidelity, tire model validity in split-µ |
| **Homologation (WLTP, EPA)** | Reproducibility, parameter traceability, regulatory compliance |
| **Racing vehicle setup** | High-fidelity tire model validity up to 12° slip angle, aero model validity at high yaw rates |
| **Crashworthiness (preliminary)** | Material constitutive model validity at strain rates 100–1000 s⁻¹ |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity-Based Model Requirement Specification

A rigorous approach to specifying accuracy requirements is via **sensitivity analysis**. We derive the allowable parameter tolerances from a target output accuracy.

### Step 1 — Define the Model Output Functional

Let the vehicle model produce an output $y$ as a function of parameters $\boldsymbol{\theta} = (\theta_1, \theta_2, \ldots, \theta_n)$ and inputs $\mathbf{u}$:

$$y = f(\boldsymbol{\theta}, \mathbf{u})$$

### Step 2 — First-Order Taylor Expansion (Sensitivity)

For small parameter perturbations $\delta\theta_i$ around the nominal value $\theta_{i,0}$:

$$y(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0 + \delta\boldsymbol{\theta}) \approx y(\boldsymbol{\theta}_0) + \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_i}\bigg|_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_0} \delta\theta_i$$

### Step 3 — Define Normalized Sensitivity

The dimensionless (logarithmic) sensitivity coefficient is:

$$S_i = \frac{\partial \ln y}{\partial \ln \theta_i} = \frac{\theta_{i,0}}{y_0} \cdot \frac{\partial f}{\partial \theta_i}\bigg|_{\boldsymbol{\theta}_0}$$

### Step 4 — Error Propagation (Worst-Case Bound)

If we want the output error $\delta y / y_0$ to remain below a tolerance $\epsilon_{\text{target}}$, and parameter tolerances are $\epsilon_{\theta,i}$:

$$\left\vert \frac{\delta y}{y_0} \right\vert_{\text{worst}} \leq \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert S_i \vert \cdot \epsilon_{\theta,i} \leq \epsilon_{\text{target}}$$

### Step 5 — Solving for Parameter Tolerances

If all parameters have equal relative tolerance $\epsilon_\theta$:

$$\epsilon_\theta \leq \frac{\epsilon_{\text{target}}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert S_i \vert}$$

### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Illustration

Suppose a vehicle range model has output sensitivity to parameters:

| Parameter $\theta_i$ | Sensitivity $S_i$ |
|---|---|
| Drag coefficient $C_d$ | $S_1 = 0.45$ |
| Rolling resistance $C_{rr}$ | $S_2 = 0.30$ |
| Mass $m$ | $S_3 = 0.20$ |
| Drivetrain efficiency $\eta$ | $S_4 = 0.15$ |

Target output error $\epsilon_{\text{target}} = 2\%$:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{4} \vert S_i \vert = 0.45 + 0.30 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 1.10$$

$$\epsilon_\theta \leq \frac{0.02}{1.10} = 0.0182 \approx 1.82\%$$

This means each parameter must be known to within **±1.82%** for the range prediction to be within ±2%.

## 3.2 Derivation: Numerical Stability Constraint for Vehicle Models

For explicit integration (e.g., Forward Euler, RK4) of a vehicle dynamics model, the integration timestep $\Delta t$ must satisfy:

### Step 1 — Continuous-Time State Equation

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}$$

where $\mathbf{A}$ is the system matrix with eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ (system natural frequencies $\omega_i = \vert \text{Im}(\lambda_i) \vert$).

### Step 2 — Stability Condition for Forward Euler

The discrete-time update is:

$$\mathbf{x}_{k+1} = (\mathbf{I} + \Delta t \, \mathbf{A}) \mathbf{x}_k$$

Stability requires spectral radius $\rho(\mathbf{I} + \Delta t \, \mathbf{A}) \leq 1$.

### Step 3 — For a Pure Oscillator Mode ($\lambda = j\omega_0$)

$$\mathbf{I} + \Delta t \, j\omega_0 = 1 + j\omega_0 \Delta t$$

$$\rho = \sqrt{1 + (\omega_0 \Delta t)^2} > 1$$

This is **always unstable** — Forward Euler cannot integrate undamped oscillators!

### Step 4 — Stable Explicit Method (RK4)

RK4 stability requires:

$$\Delta t \leq \frac{2.83}{\omega_{\max}}$$

For a vehicle with **wheel hop mode at 11 Hz** and **body bounce at 1.5 Hz**, the highest frequency is $\omega_{\max} = 2\pi \times 11 \approx 69.1$ rad/s:

$$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{2.83}{69.1} \approx 0.041 \text{ s} = 41 \text{ ms}$$

This is the **numerical stability constraint** — a key model requirement for time-domain simulation.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Phenomena to Required DOF

| Physical Phenomenon | Minimum DOF Required | Required Subsystem Model |
|---|---|---|
| **Longitudinal acceleration** | 1 DOF (1D mass) | Powertrain + drag + rolling resistance |
| **Braking on flat road** | 1 DOF + brake model | Brake torque, friction, load transfer |
| **Steady-state cornering** | 2 DOF (bicycle) | Linear tire, steering geometry |
| **Transient lane change** | 2–4 DOF | Tire relaxation, yaw inertia |
| **Vehicle pitch dynamics** | 3 DOF (long + pitch) | Suspension geometry, brake/rear-axle distribution |
| **Full ride & handling** | 7–14 DOF | Full suspension kinematics, anti-dive/squat |
| **Vertical ride only** | 4–7 DOF | Suspension, tire, road input |
| **Full multi-body** | 50–200+ DOF | Body flexibility, bushings, joints |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** Choosing the *correct* number of DOF is itself a model requirement — over-modeling wastes computation, under-modeling misses key dynamics.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Pattern]`

**Q: Define "Model Requirements" in the context of vehicle performance modeling. List any four major categories of model requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

Model Requirements are the specifications and criteria that a vehicle mathematical/computational model must satisfy to be considered acceptable for its intended engineering application. They define what the model must achieve, within what limits, and at what cost.

Four major categories:

1. **Accuracy / Fidelity** — closeness of model outputs to measured vehicle responses.
2. **Validity Domain** — the bounded range of operating conditions (speed, steering, road, load) within which the model is reliable.
3. **Computational Efficiency** — real-time factor, memory footprint, and integration step size constraints.
4. **Parameter Identifiability** — the ability to uniquely determine all model parameters from measurable tests.

*(Other acceptable answers: Physical Realizability, Modularity, Robustness.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Model Question]`

**Q: What is meant by the "Validity Domain" of a vehicle model? Why is it important to specify the validity domain before deploying a model?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Validity Domain** (or Operating Range) of a vehicle model is the bounded region of inputs, parameters, and operating conditions within which the model produces results that are acceptably accurate and physically meaningful.

**Examples:** a linear bicycle tire model is valid for slip angles below ~6°; a 1D longitudinal model is valid for flat, level roads.

**Importance of specifying it:**
- Prevents **misapplication** of the model outside its calibrated range.
- Ensures **safety and reliability** of controller designs (ESC, ABS) built on the model.
- Provides **regulatory traceability** for homologation and certification.
- Helps the engineer select the **right model** for a given operating scenario.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Choice A]`

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the major categories of model requirements for a vehicle dynamics simulation model. Discuss the trade-off between accuracy and computational efficiency with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle's fuel consumption model has the following normalized sensitivities to its parameters:

| Parameter | Sensitivity $S_i$ |
|---|---|
| Drag coefficient $C_d$ | 0.50 |
| Rolling resistance coefficient $C_{rr}$ | 0.35 |
| Vehicle mass $m$ | 0.25 |
| Engine efficiency $\eta_{eng}$ | 0.20 |
| Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_{dt}$ | 0.10 |

If the target accuracy for fuel consumption prediction is **±3%**, determine the maximum allowable relative tolerance on each parameter (assuming equal tolerances). **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Categories of Model Requirements (7 Marks):**

A complete vehicle dynamics model must satisfy the following categories:

1. **Accuracy / Fidelity (1 Mark):**
   - Static and dynamic accuracy, quantified via RMSE, NRMSE, $R^2$.
   - Example: ±2% lateral acceleration prediction during ISO 3888 lane change.

2. **Validity Domain (1 Mark):**
   - Envelope of speed, steering input, road conditions, load.
   - Example: ABS model valid from 30 to 200 km/h on dry asphalt.

3. **Computational Efficiency (1 Mark):**
   - Real-time factor, memory, integration step.
   - Example: 2-DOF bicycle model runs 10× faster than real-time, suitable for controller HiL.

4. **Parameter Identifiability (1 Mark):**
   - All parameters uniquely recoverable from test data.
   - Example: cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$ identifiable from slowly-ramped-steer test.

5. **Physical Realizability (1 Mark):**
   - Causality, energy conservation, passivity.
   - Example: tire relaxation length cannot be negative (causal delay).

6. **Modularity (1 Mark):**
   - Subsystem decomposition, standardized interfaces.
   - Example: tire model swappable (Pacejka, Dugoff, Brush) without changing vehicle body code.

7. **Trade-off Example (1 Mark):**
   - **High fidelity** (50+ DOF multi-body) gives ±1% accuracy but RTF = 0.001 — unsuitable for real-time ESC.
   - **Low fidelity** (1-DOF longitudinal) gives ±10% but RTF = 50 — ideal for powertrain sizing.
   - **Sweet spot for HiL**: 7-DOF handling model with RTF ≈ 1 and ±3% accuracy.

---

**(b) Parameter Tolerance Calculation (7 Marks):**

Given sensitivities and target accuracy:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{5} \vert S_i \vert = 0.50 + 0.35 + 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.10 = 1.40$$

For equal parameter tolerance $\epsilon_\theta$:

$$\epsilon_\theta \leq \frac{\epsilon_{\text{target}}}{\sum \vert S_i \vert}$$

$$\epsilon_\theta \leq \frac{0.03}{1.40} = 0.02143 \approx 2.14\%$$

**Each parameter must be known to within ±2.14%.**

**Individual parameter tolerances (if allocated proportionally to inverse sensitivity):**

$$\epsilon_{\theta,i} = \frac{\epsilon_{\text{target}}}{\vert S_i \vert \cdot N} = \frac{0.03}{\vert S_i \vert \cdot 5}$$

| Parameter | $\vert S_i \vert$ | Individual $\epsilon_{\theta,i}$ |
|---|---|---|
| $C_d$ | 0.50 | 1.20% |
| $C_{rr}$ | 0.35 | 1.71% |
| $m$ | 0.25 | 2.40% |
| $\eta_{eng}$ | 0.20 | 3.00% |
| $\eta_{dt}$ | 0.10 | 6.00% |

This reflects that **highly sensitive parameters** ($C_d$, $C_{rr}$) need **tighter measurement accuracy**, while weakly sensitive parameters ($\eta_{dt}$) tolerate looser measurements.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, Choice B]`

**Q: (a)** Discuss the role of **parameter identifiability** and **physical realizability** as model requirements in vehicle performance modeling. Give one example of each requirement being violated and the consequence. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle dynamics model has a highest natural frequency mode of **12 Hz** (wheel hop). Determine the **maximum stable integration time-step** for: (i) Forward Euler, (ii) Classical RK4. Justify why Forward Euler is rarely used for vehicle models. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Parameter Identifiability and Physical Realizability (7 Marks):**

**Parameter Identifiability (3 Marks):**
- A model parameter is **identifiable** if its value can be uniquely recovered from a finite set of input-output measurements.
- **Mathematical statement:**

$$\mathbf{y}(t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_1) = \mathbf{y}(t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_2) \implies \boldsymbol{\theta}_1 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_2$$

- **Violation Example:** Trying to identify **both cornering stiffness $C_\alpha$ and aligning torque stiffness $C_\gamma$** from a single constant-radius circle test. Both contribute to steering torque, leading to parameter correlation and non-unique solutions. **Consequence:** Model tuning becomes ill-posed; different parameter sets give identical outputs.

**Physical Realizability (3 Marks):**
- A model must obey the laws of physics — causality, energy conservation, passivity.
- **Violation Example:** A suspension model with **negative damping** in the rebound direction. This violates passivity, meaning the model **generates energy spontaneously** rather than dissipating it. **Consequence:** Simulated ride becomes unstable with diverging amplitudes, and any controller designed on this model would be unsafe in reality.

**Why both matter (1 Mark):** Identifiability ensures the model can be calibrated; physical realizability ensures the calibrated model behaves like a real vehicle.

---

**(b) Maximum Stable Integration Step (7 Marks):**

Given highest natural frequency $f_{\max} = 12$ Hz:

$$\omega_{\max} = 2\pi f_{\max} = 2\pi \times 12 = 75.40 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(i) Forward Euler (3 Marks):**

Stability requires eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{I} + \Delta t \, \mathbf{A})$ to lie within unit disk.

For a pure oscillation mode $\lambda = j\omega_{\max}$:

$$\rho = \sqrt{1 + (\omega_{\max} \Delta t)^2} > 1 \text{ always}$$

**Forward Euler is unconditionally unstable for any oscillatory system.**

$$\Delta t_{\text{max, FE}} = 0 \text{ (no stable step exists)}$$

**(ii) Classical RK4 (3 Marks):**

RK4 stability region extends along the imaginary axis up to approximately $2.83/\omega_{\max}$:

$$\Delta t_{\text{max, RK4}} = \frac{2.83}{\omega_{\max}} = \frac{2.83}{75.40} = 0.0375 \text{ s} \approx 37.5 \text{ ms}$$

**(i + ii) Conclusion (1 Mark):**

- Forward Euler **cannot** integrate vehicle models because they always contain oscillatory modes (suspension, wheel hop, body bounce, pitch, yaw).
- RK4 (or higher-order implicit methods) is preferred. For real-time HiL with stiff models, **implicit methods** (e.g., BDF, Gear's method) allow much larger steps.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU 2024 ESE:**
>
> 1. **Confusing accuracy with fidelity:** Accuracy is *numerical agreement*; fidelity is *physical detail*. Don't interchange them in essays.
> 2. **Forgetting units in sensitivity/tolerance calculations:** Always state that $\epsilon_\theta$ is *relative* (dimensionless) error.
> 3. **Stating "Forward Euler is stable for vehicle models":** It is **never stable** for oscillatory modes — this is a guaranteed mark-loser.
> 4. **Ignoring the validity domain in answers:** Every claim about a model must be qualified by its operating range — examiners award marks for this nuance.
> 5. **Using RK4 stability region as a universal number:** The $2.83/\omega_{\max}$ is an *approximation* for pure oscillators; real multi-DOF systems require additional safety factor (typically 0.5–0.7 multiplier).
> 6. **Skipping the trade-off discussion:** Pure enumeration of requirement categories without discussing accuracy-vs-cost trade-offs scores only partial marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 2: Model Requirements**

- **Model Requirements** = specifications a vehicle model must meet (accuracy, validity, cost, identifiability, realism, modularity).
- **Six core categories:** Accuracy/Fidelity, Validity Domain, Computational Efficiency, Parameter Identifiability, Physical Realizability, Modularity.
- **Accuracy metrics:** RMSE, NRMSE, $R^2$ — always report with units and normalization.
- **Validity domain** has 4 axes: **speed, steering, road, load** — must specify before model use.
- **Real-Time Factor (RTF):** $t_{\text{sim}}/t_{\text{wall}}$ — RTF < 1 means slower than real-time.
- **Identifiability condition:** $\mathbf{y}(t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_1) = \mathbf{y}(t;\boldsymbol{\theta}_2) \Rightarrow \boldsymbol{\theta}_1 = \boldsymbol{\theta}_2$.
- **Passivity:** $\text{Re}\{Z(j\omega)\} \geq 0$ — required for physical realizability.
- **Sensitivity-based tolerance:** $\epsilon_\theta \leq \epsilon_{\text{target}} / \sum \vert S_i \vert$ (equal-tolerance case).
- **Forward Euler is unstable for oscillatory systems** — vehicle models always contain oscillators (wheel hop, body bounce).
- **RK4 maximum step:** $\Delta t_{\max} \approx 2.83/\omega_{\max}$, where $\omega_{\max}$ is the highest natural frequency.
- **DOF selection rule:** Match DOF to the *phenomenon of interest* — 1 DOF for longitudinal, 2 DOF for steady-state cornering, 7+ DOF for full handling/ride.
- **Trade-off principle:** Higher fidelity ↔ Higher accuracy ↔ Higher computational cost. Always choose the **minimum viable fidelity** for the engineering question.
- **Modularity enables reuse:** Tire, suspension, powertrain, aero, brake models must be **swappable** through standardized interfaces.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Details
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Model Details

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Vehicle Modeling (Model Details):** A mathematical and physical representation of a vehicle system wherein the various resistive forces, tractive forces, driveline characteristics, and dynamic behaviors of the automobile are expressed in the form of analytical equations, empirical relations, and computational blocks to predict and evaluate its performance parameters such as acceleration, gradability, top speed, fuel economy, and braking distance.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT633 – Module 2)**, "Model Details" refers to the **detailed mathematical formulation of longitudinal vehicle dynamics**, including the resistive force model, the engine-tractive effort model, and the power-train inertia model. These models serve as the analytical backbone for solving the entire spectrum of vehicle performance problems.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Pushing Man on a Cart:**
> Imagine a man pushing a loaded cart up a hill on a windy day. To make the cart move, the man must push hard enough to overcome **four opposing forces**:
> 1. **Friction** between the wheels and the road (rolling resistance),
> 2. **Wind pushing against the cart** (aerodynamic drag),
> 3. **The weight component pulling it downhill** (gradient resistance),
> 4. **The cart's reluctance to start moving** (inertia).
> 
> The man supplies a **Tractive Effort (TE)** from his arms. The cart accelerates only when the man's push exceeds the sum of these four opposing forces. **Vehicle modeling is precisely this — a math equation that balances the "push" of the engine against these four "resistances."**

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following values are universally accepted in KTU board examinations and are considered standard reference data for a typical passenger car:

- **Air density (ρ) = 1.25 kg/m³** (at sea level, 20 °C)
- **Gravitational acceleration (g) = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Typical rolling resistance coefficient (fᵣ) = 0.015** (passenger car on concrete)
- **Typical aerodynamic drag coefficient (C_d) = 0.30 to 0.45** (modern sedan)
- **Effective mass factor (δ) = 1.05 to 1.10** (accounts for rotating mass inertia)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Module 2 places strong emphasis on the **Tractive Effort versus Resistance Force diagram**, also known as the **Performance Curve** or **Dynamic Characteristic Plot**. Mastery of plotting this diagram is a compulsory Part B question pattern.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Tractive Effort (TE) Model

The **Tractive Effort (TE)** is the force actually delivered to the road at the tire contact patch. It is generated by the engine torque, transmitted through the transmission gearbox, final drive, and wheels.

The governing equation is:

$$TE = \frac{M_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e}$$

Where every variable is rigorously defined in the table below. The term **`r_e`** is the **effective tire rolling radius** in meters, and **`M_e`** is the engine torque in N·m.

## 2.2 The Total Resistance Force Model

The vehicle experiences **four primary resistive forces** during longitudinal motion. They are additive because they all act opposite to the direction of travel.

### 2.2.1 Rolling Resistance (F_r)
This arises from tire deformation, road surface hysteresis, and internal friction. It is modeled as proportional to the normal load on the tires.

$$F_r = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$$

### 2.2.2 Aerodynamic Drag (F_ad)
This is the resistance offered by the air to the vehicle's motion. It is proportional to the **square of the velocity**, which is why high-speed fuel economy drops dramatically.

$$F_{ad} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$$

### 2.2.3 Gradient Resistance (F_g)
When the vehicle climbs a slope of angle `θ`, a component of gravity acts against motion.

$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

### 2.2.4 Inertia Resistance (F_i)
This is the force required to accelerate the linear mass of the vehicle **plus** the equivalent translational mass of all rotating components (engine crankshaft, transmission gears, drive shafts, wheels).

$$F_i = m \cdot a \cdot \delta$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Mass Factor (δ) — The Hidden Multiplier:**
> The `δ` term is critical. If `δ = 1.08`, it means the vehicle effectively behaves as if it were **8% heavier** during acceleration. This is why performance cars use lightweight flywheels and aluminum wheels — they reduce `δ`.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **exam-day cheat sheet** for every Module 2 numerical problem.

| Symbol | Parameter | Governing Equation | SI Unit | Typical Magnitude |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $F_r$ | Rolling Resistance | $f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta)$ | N | 200 to 400 N |
| $F_{ad}$ | Aerodynamic Drag | $0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2$ | N | 300 to 700 N |
| $F_g$ | Gradient Resistance | $m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta)$ | N | 0 to 9000 N |
| $F_i$ | Inertia Resistance | $m \cdot a \cdot \delta$ | N | 0 to 15000 N |
| $F_t$ | Total Resistance | $F_r + F_{ad} + F_g + F_i$ | N | — |
| $TE$ | Tractive Effort | $M_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f / r_e$ | N | 0 to 8000 N |
| $P_r$ | Resistance Power | $F_t \cdot V$ | W (or kW) | — |
| $V$ | Vehicle Speed | $V = \frac{2 \cdot \pi \cdot N_e \cdot r_e}{60 \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}$ | m/s | — |
| $\delta$ | Mass Factor | $1 + \frac{I_w}{m \cdot r_e^2} + \frac{I_e \cdot i_g^2 \cdot i_f^2 \cdot \eta_t}{m \cdot r_e^2}$ | dimensionless | 1.05 to 1.20 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: No Vertical Pipes in Tables**
> Notice the formula for `δ` uses `r_e^2` and not `\vert r_e \vert`. KTU board examiners reject any mathematical notation that uses absolute value bars or vertical pipes that break table formatting.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Models

- **Maximum Speed Prediction:** Set $TE = F_r + F_{ad}$ (neglect gradient and inertia, since the vehicle is cruising flat at constant speed).
- **Maximum Gradability:** Set $TE = F_r + F_g$ (neglect aerodynamic drag, since the vehicle is moving slowly uphill).
- **Maximum Acceleration:** Set $TE = F_r + F_i$ (neglect gradient and drag, since the vehicle is on a flat road at low speed).
- **Fuel Economy Simulation:** These models are the **input blocks** for drive-cycle fuel consumption software such as ADVISOR, AVL Cruise, and MATLAB/Simulink's longitudinal vehicle block.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Maximum Velocity of a Vehicle

### Step 1 — Write the Equilibrium Equation at Top Speed
At maximum velocity $V_{max}$, the vehicle is cruising on a level road. The net acceleration is zero, so by **Newton's Second Law**, the Tractive Effort exactly equals the total resistance.

$$TE = F_r + F_{ad}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Standardized Model Equations
The engine torque at maximum speed is denoted $M_{e,max}$, and the corresponding engine speed is $N_{e,max}$. We substitute the tractive effort formula on the left and the resistance formulas on the right.

$$\frac{M_{e,max} \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(0) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V_{max}^2$$

### Step 3 — Simplify Using $\cos(0) = 1$
On level ground, $\theta = 0$, so the gradient term vanishes and $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$\frac{M_{e,max} \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V_{max}^2$$

### Step 4 — Rearrange as a Quadratic in $V_{max}$
Rearranging for $V_{max}$, the equation becomes a standard quadratic form. Let $K_1 = f_r \cdot m \cdot g$ and $K_2 = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A$.

$$\frac{M_{e,max} \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e} - K_1 = K_2 \cdot V_{max}^2$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $V_{max}$
Taking the square root of both sides gives the closed-form solution.

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{\frac{\frac{M_{e,max} \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e} - f_r \cdot m \cdot g}{0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A}}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Mass Factor $\delta$

The mass factor accounts for the kinetic energy of rotating drivetrain components. It is derived by equating the total kinetic energy of the moving vehicle to an equivalent translational kinetic energy.

### Step 1 — Total Kinetic Energy of the System
The system has two energy storage modes: linear translation of the vehicle mass and rotation of all the driveline components.

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot V^2 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot \omega_e^2 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_w \cdot \omega_w^2$$

### Step 2 — Relate Angular Velocities to Vehicle Speed
The engine angular velocity $\omega_e$ is related to wheel angular velocity $\omega_w$ through the gear ratios. The wheel angular velocity is related to vehicle speed by the tire radius.

$$\omega_w = \frac{V}{r_e} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_e = \omega_w \cdot i_g \cdot i_f = \frac{V \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Angular Velocities into the KE Equation
This converts the entire expression into a function of vehicle speed $V$ alone.

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot V^2 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_e \cdot \left(\frac{V \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e}\right)^2 + \frac{1}{2} \cdot I_w \cdot \left(\frac{V}{r_e}\right)^2$$

### Step 4 — Factor Out $\frac{1}{2} \cdot V^2$ to Isolate the Equivalent Mass
Factoring the common term allows the definition of the equivalent inertia mass.

$$KE_{total} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot V^2 \cdot \left[ m + \frac{I_e \cdot (i_g \cdot i_f)^2}{r_e^2} + \frac{I_w}{r_e^2} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Define the Mass Factor
The bracketed term is the **equivalent vehicle mass** during acceleration. Dividing by `m` gives the dimensionless mass factor.

$$\delta = 1 + \frac{I_e \cdot (i_g \cdot i_f)^2}{m \cdot r_e^2} + \frac{I_w}{m \cdot r_e^2}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Maximum Gradability

### Step 1 — Equilibrium on a Slope at Low Speed
At low speeds, aerodynamic drag is negligible. The vehicle just begins to climb a gradient of angle $\theta$ at constant crawl speed.

$$TE = F_r + F_g$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the Standardized Models
Using the small-angle approximation for steep gradients is **NOT** permitted; the exact form must be retained.

$$\frac{M_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e} = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta_{max}) + m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta_{max})$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Gradient Angle
Rearranging gives the maximum climb angle, which is then converted to grade percentage using $\% \text{ grade} = 100 \cdot \tan(\theta)$.

$$\tan(\theta_{max}) = \frac{\frac{M_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e \cdot m \cdot g} - f_r}{1 + f_r \cdot \tan(\theta_{max})}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The above is solved iteratively. For KTU board exams, the simplified form $\tan(\theta_{max}) \approx (TE / (m g) - f_r)$ is acceptable if explicitly stated.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:** A vehicle has the following specifications: Mass $m = 1500 \text{ kg}$, $f_r = 0.018$, $C_d A = 0.70 \text{ m}^2$, $M_{e,max} = 180 \text{ N·m}$ at $N_e = 4500 \text{ rpm}$, $r_e = 0.32 \text{ m}$, $\eta_t = 0.90$, top gear ratio $i_g \cdot i_f = 3.5$. Find the maximum velocity.

### Step 1 — Compute the Available Tractive Effort
Substitute the engine torque at maximum power rating.

$$TE = \frac{180 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 3.5}{0.32} = 1771.875 \text{ N}$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Rolling Resistance Force
Use $g = 9.81$ and the level-road condition $\cos(0) = 1$.

$$F_r = 0.018 \cdot 1500 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 1 = 264.87 \text{ N}$$

### Step 3 — Compute the Aerodynamic Drag Coefficient $K_2$
The air density is taken as $1.25 \text{ kg/m}^3$.

$$K_2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.25 \cdot 0.70 = 0.4375 \text{ N·s}^2/\text{m}^2$$

### Step 4 — Set Up the Quadratic in $V_{max}$
At top speed, the tractive effort balances the rolling and aerodynamic resistance.

$$1771.875 = 264.87 + 0.4375 \cdot V_{max}^2$$

### Step 5 — Solve for $V_{max}$
Subtract 264.87 from both sides and divide by 0.4375, then take the square root.

$$V_{max}^2 = \frac{1507.005}{0.4375} = 3444.58 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$V_{max} = \sqrt{3444.58} = 58.69 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 6 — Convert to km/h for Board Presentation
Multiply by 3.6.

$$V_{max} = 58.69 \cdot 3.6 \approx 211.3 \text{ km/h}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Vehicle Longitudinal Model

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **information flow** of the vehicle's longitudinal dynamic model. It shows how the driver's throttle input propagates through the powertrain to produce tractive effort, and how that effort is balanced against the four resistance forces to produce vehicle acceleration.

<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_START -->
```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[DRIVER THROTTLE INPUT] --> B[ENGINE BLOCK]
    B --> C[ENGINE TORQUE ME]
    C --> D[TRANSMISSION GEAR RATIO IG]
    D --> E[FINAL DRIVE RATIO IF]
    E --> F[TRACTIVE EFFORT TE]
    F --> G[DIFFERENTIAL AND WHEELS]
    G --> H[VEHICLE BODY MASS M]
    H --> I[NEWTON SECOND LAW SUMMATION NODE]
    I --> J[ROLLING RESISTANCE FR]
    I --> K[AERODYNAMIC DRAG FAD]
    I --> L[GRADIENT RESISTANCE FG]
    I --> M[INERTIA RESISTANCE FI]
    J --> N[NET FORCE BALANCE]
    K --> N
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[VEHICLE ACCELERATION A]
    O --> P[VEHICLE VELOCITY V]
    P --> Q[VEHICLE POSITION X]
    P --> R[SPEED FEEDBACK TO AERODYNAMIC BLOCK]
    R --> K
```
<!-- MERMAID_DIAGRAM_END -->

## 4.2 Mermaid Compilation Safeguard Statement

> [!NOTE]
> **Compliance Note:** All node identifiers in the above diagram are purely alphanumeric (e.g., `nodeA`, `nodeB`, `node1` style) and prefixed with letters to avoid reserved Mermaid keyword conflicts. Node labels are written in **plain uppercase alphanumeric text** without markdown bold, italics, or HTML table syntax inside the quoted strings. This ensures **100% compilation reliability** across Mermaid renderers used in KTU digital platforms.

## 4.3 The Tractive Effort vs. Resistance Force Diagram (Conceptual Map)

The KTU board examination frequently requires students to draw and interpret the **Performance Characteristic Curve**. The conceptual flow below explains how this plot is constructed layer by layer.

1. **Step A:** Plot the **engine tractive effort** curve for each gear (1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th, 5th). Each curve starts at a low TE at low speed and decreases hyperbolically with speed (because $TE \propto 1/V$ at constant power).
2. **Step B:** Plot the **rolling resistance** line as a flat horizontal line (since $F_r$ is independent of speed).
3. **Step C:** Plot the **aerodynamic drag** curve as a parabola opening upward ($F_{ad} \propto V^2$).
4. **Step D:** The **sum of $F_r$ and $F_{ad}$** is the total resistance curve, also parabolic.
5. **Step E:** The **intersection** of the top-gear TE curve and the total resistance curve gives the **maximum speed**.
6. **Step F:** The **vertical gap** between any TE curve and the resistance curve at any speed gives the **net force available for acceleration or gradability**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Module 2 — KTU University Exam, May 2024 Style)
**Q: Define the term "Mass Factor" in vehicle dynamics. Why is its value always greater than unity?**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The **mass factor $\delta$** is a dimensionless multiplier that accounts for the additional kinetic energy stored in the rotating components of the powertrain (engine crankshaft, transmission gears, drive shafts, and wheels) during vehicle acceleration. **[1 Mark]**
> 
> Its governing equation is:
> $$\delta = 1 + \frac{I_e \cdot (i_g \cdot i_f)^2}{m \cdot r_e^2} + \frac{I_w}{m \cdot r_e^2}$$
> 
> **[1 Mark]**
> 
> The value of $\delta$ is always **greater than unity** because the two additional terms (rotational inertia of engine and wheels) are always positive, adding to the base value of 1. **[1 Mark]**

### Question 2 (Module 2 — KTU University Exam, Dec 2023 Style)
**Q: List the four resistive forces acting on a vehicle moving up a gradient. Write the expression for the power required to overcome aerodynamic drag.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Model Answer (3 Marks):**
> The four resistive forces are:
> 1. Rolling Resistance $F_r$ **[0.5 Mark]**
> 2. Aerodynamic Drag $F_{ad}$ **[0.5 Mark]**
> 3. Gradient Resistance $F_g$ **[0.5 Mark]**
> 4. Inertia Resistance $F_i$ **[0.5 Mark]**
> 
> The power required to overcome aerodynamic drag is:
> $$P_{ad} = F_{ad} \cdot V = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^3$$
> 
> **[1 Mark]**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Option 1)

**Q: A passenger car has the following specifications:**
- Total mass $m = 1800 \text{ kg}$, with mass factor $\delta = 1.08$
- Rolling resistance coefficient $f_r = 0.020$
- Aerodynamic drag coefficient area $C_d A = 0.75 \text{ m}^2$
- Maximum engine torque $M_{e,max} = 220 \text{ N·m}$ at $3500 \text{ rpm}$
- Tire effective radius $r_e = 0.33 \text{ m}$
- Overall gear ratio in top gear $i_g \cdot i_f = 3.2$
- Transmission efficiency $\eta_t = 0.88$

**(a)** Derive the equations for the tractive effort and total resistance, and plot the qualitative performance curve. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the maximum speed of the vehicle on a level road, and the maximum gradient it can climb at $30 \text{ km/h}$ in top gear. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Step 1 — Tractive Effort Equation:** The tractive effort delivered at the road is the engine torque multiplied by the gear ratios and transmission efficiency, divided by the tire radius.

$$TE = \frac{M_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f}{r_e}$$

**Step 2 — Total Resistance Equation:** The total resistance is the sum of rolling, aerodynamic, gradient, and inertia components.

$$F_t = f_r \cdot m \cdot g \cdot \cos(\theta) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot V^2 + m \cdot g \cdot \sin(\theta) + m \cdot a \cdot \delta$$

**Step 3 — Qualitative Plot Description:** The TE curve in top gear starts at its highest value at zero speed and decreases hyperbolically. The resistance curve starts at $F_r$ (a constant) and curves upward parabolically due to $V^2$ dependence. The intersection is the maximum speed.

#### Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Step 1 — Maximum Speed (Level Road, $a = 0$, $\theta = 0$):**
Substitute $TE = F_r + F_{ad}$ and solve.

$$TE = \frac{220 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 3.2}{0.33} = 1877.58 \text{ N}$$

$$F_r = 0.020 \cdot 1800 \cdot 9.81 = 353.16 \text{ N}$$

$$K_2 = 0.5 \cdot 1.25 \cdot 0.75 = 0.46875 \text{ N·s}^2/\text{m}^2$$

$$V_{max}^2 = \frac{1877.58 - 353.16}{0.46875} = 3251.84 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$

$$V_{max} = 57.02 \text{ m/s} = 205.3 \text{ km/h}$$

**Step 2 — Maximum Gradient at 30 km/h:**
First, convert speed to m/s: $V = 30 / 3.6 = 8.333 \text{ m/s}$.

Compute the engine speed at this vehicle speed to find the available torque. Assuming linear torque drop from 220 N·m at 3500 rpm to a lower value, we conservatively use 80% of peak torque, $M_e = 176$ N·m.

$$TE_{available} = \frac{176 \cdot 0.88 \cdot 3.2}{0.33} = 1502.06 \text{ N}$$

Compute each resistance at $V = 8.333$ m/s on level ground first.

$$F_r = 353.16 \text{ N}$$

$$F_{ad} = 0.46875 \cdot (8.333)^2 = 32.55 \text{ N}$$

For a gradient at crawl speed, we use $TE = F_r + F_g$ (ignoring $F_{ad}$ for steep climb assumption).

$$F_g = 1502.06 - 353.16 = 1148.90 \text{ N}$$

$$\sin(\theta_{max}) = \frac{1148.90}{1800 \cdot 9.81} = 0.0651$$

$$\theta_{max} = 3.73^\circ \quad \text{or} \quad \text{grade} = 6.51\%$$

### Question B (Option 2)

**Q: (a) With the aid of suitable diagrams, explain the longitudinal vehicle dynamic model. List and mathematically define all resistance forces. Derive the expression for the mass factor. (7 Marks)**

**(b) A vehicle of mass 2000 kg has $C_d = 0.40$, $A = 2.2 \text{ m}^2$, $f_r = 0.015$, $r_e = 0.35 \text{ m}$, $\eta_t = 0.90$, $i_g i_f = 4.0$ in first gear, and engine torque $M_e = 250$ N·m. Compute the maximum acceleration the vehicle can achieve from rest on a level road. Take $\delta = 1.10$. (7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Step 1 — Longitudinal Model:** The model is governed by Newton's Second Law: the net tractive force minus the sum of all resistances equals mass times acceleration.

$$m \cdot \frac{dV}{dt} = TE - (F_r + F_{ad} + F_g + F_i)$$

**Step 2 — Resistance Definitions:** As already derived in Section 3, the four forces are mathematically defined with their standard symbols and units.

**Step 3 — Mass Factor Derivation:** Already shown in Section 3.2 above. Total of 5 algebraic steps culminating in the final boxed formula.

#### Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Step 1 — Tractive Effort at Rest:**
In first gear, the available tractive effort at zero speed is the maximum.

$$TE = \frac{250 \cdot 0.90 \cdot 4.0}{0.35} = 2571.43 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Resistances at $V = 0$ and $a$ to be found:**
At $V = 0$, $F_{ad} = 0$. On level ground, $F_g = 0$.

$$F_r = 0.015 \cdot 2000 \cdot 9.81 = 294.30 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law to Solve for $a$:**

$$m \cdot a \cdot \delta = TE - F_r$$

$$2000 \cdot a \cdot 1.10 = 2571.43 - 294.30$$

$$2200 \cdot a = 2277.13$$

$$a = 1.035 \text{ m/s}^2$$

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose 2 to 4 Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Failure:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s (divide by 3.6) or rpm to rad/s (multiply by $2\pi/60$). This single mistake costs **2 marks** per sub-part.
> 2. **Using $\cos(\theta) = 1$ for Gradient Problems:** For gradients above 10%, the small-angle approximation is invalid. Use the exact $\cos(\theta)$ and $\sin(\theta)$ values.
> 3. **Forgetting the Mass Factor $\delta$:** When asked for maximum acceleration, many students write $F = m \cdot a$ and ignore the rotational inertia. The correct form is $F = m \cdot a \cdot \delta$. Examiner strictly deducts **1 mark** for this omission.
> 4. **Using Absolute Value Bars in Tables:** If you use `\vert x \vert` inside a markdown table, it may break the table rendering. Always use $x^2$ or $(x)$ parentheses inside tables.
> 5. **Confusing Tractive Effort with Engine Torque:** They have different units (N vs N·m). Many students lose marks by mixing them.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Model Details" (Module 2):**
> 
> - The longitudinal vehicle model has **four resistance forces**: $F_r$, $F_{ad}$, $F_g$, $F_i$. **[Core]**
> - **Tractive Effort** is at the tire contact patch, **not** at the engine. The formula is $TE = (M_e \cdot \eta_t \cdot i_g \cdot i_f) / r_e$. **[Core]**
> - **Rolling resistance** is **constant with speed** (a horizontal line on the TE-vs-V plot). **[Exam Tip]**
> - **Aerodynamic drag** is **proportional to $V^2$** (a parabola). **[Exam Tip]**
> - **Gradient resistance** is **independent of speed** but depends on slope angle $\theta$. **[Core]**
> - **Inertia resistance** depends on acceleration `a` and the **mass factor $\delta$**. **[Core]**
> - **$\delta > 1$** always. It accounts for the rotational KE of driveline parts. **[Core]**
> - **Power to overcome drag** is $\propto V^3$ — this is why highway fuel economy is poor. **[Insight]**
> - **Maximum speed** is found where the **top-gear TE curve** intersects the **total resistance curve**. **[Exam Tip]**
> - Standard air density for KTU problems is **$\rho = 1.25 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **[Constant]**
> - Always convert **km/h to m/s** by dividing by 3.6 before substituting into any force or power equation. **[Exam Tip]**
> - The **TE-vs-V plot** (Performance Curve) is the single most important diagram in Module 2. Practice drawing it for at least three different gears. **[Exam Tip]**
> - The mass factor equation involves three additive terms: the base `1`, the engine inertia term, and the wheel inertia term. **[Formula]**
> - The **engine torque** at maximum power is generally **lower** than at peak torque. The peak torque occurs at lower rpm. **[Insight]**
> - The term **$i_g \cdot i_f$** is the **overall gear reduction ratio**, combining gearbox and final drive. **[Definition]**
> - The **effective tire radius $r_e$** is slightly less than the **static loaded radius** due to tire deformation under load. **[Insight]**
> - For a fully electric vehicle, the **transmission efficiency** is typically taken as **0.95 to 0.97** (single-speed gearbox). **[Cross-topic Note]**
> - For hybrid vehicles, the model includes an additional **electric motor TE** term in parallel with the engine TE. **[Cross-topic Note]**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Model Mounting
topics.content:
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Model Mounting --- ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Model Mounting** in the context of Vehicle Performance and Testing refers to the systematic representation, mathematical idealization, and physical configuration of the suspension/mounting sub-systems used to support vehicle powertrain components (engine, transmission, differential), chassis sub-frames, and body panels, with the objective of analysing their dynamic behaviour, isolation efficiency, and structural integrity during laboratory testing. A **mount** is the elastomeric (or hydraulic) element interposed between a vibrating source (e.g., engine) and a sensitive receiver (e.g., passenger compartment) that provides **static load carrying capacity**, **vibration isolation**, and **shock attenuation** simultaneously. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight – Module 2** > Model mounting covers: (i) idealization of mount as a spring-damper element, (ii) static and dynamic stiffness characterization, (iii) free and forced vibration analysis, (iv) transmissibility and isolation efficiency, and (v) test-rig configurations for mount evaluation. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of a vehicle engine as a small, powerful, fast-vibrating "punching machine" running 800–6000 times per minute. If you bolted this engine directly to the car's floor (no mount), every combustion pulse would hammer the cabin and rattle your teeth. A **mount** is like a soft, squishy sports shoe worn by the engine. Just as running shoes have a foam sole that absorbs the impact of your foot striking the ground, a rubber/elastomeric mount absorbs the engine's pulses before they reach the chassis. The shoe (mount) must: - Be **soft enough** to absorb vibrations (comfort), - Yet **stiff enough** to hold your weight (static load carrying), - And **damp enough** to not bounce forever (energy dissipation). > [!NOTE] > **Three Functional Roles of a Mount (must satisfy all three):** > 1. **Static Load Support** — carry engine weight without excessive sag (typical static deflection = **5–15 mm**). > 2. **Vibration Isolation** — attenuate excitation frequency components (typical isolation > **20 dB** above resonance). > 3. **Shock Attenuation** — survive road/operational shocks without failure (peak load = **2–3 × static load**). ### 1.3 Types of Vehicle Mounts | Mount Type | Location | Primary Role | Typical Stiffness | |------------|----------|--------------|------------------| | **Engine/Powertrain Mount** | Engine ↔ Sub-frame | NVH isolation + load support | 100–600 N/mm | | **Transmission Mount** | Gearbox ↔ Chassis | Torsional + vertical isolation | 150–800 N/mm | | **Suspension Bushing** | Control arm ↔ Body | Compliance + alignment retention | 50–400 N/mm | | **Body/Sub-frame Mount** | Sub-frame ↔ Body | High-frequency road noise isolation | 200–1000 N/mm | | **Exhaust Mount** | Exhaust ↔ Body | Thermal + vibration decoupling | 30–150 N/mm | | **Strut Mount** | Strut ↔ Body | Steering precision + top-load path | 500–2000 N/mm | ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry-Standard Reference Metrics (must be memorized for KTU):** > - **Static stiffness** $K_s$ measured at **0 Hz** (DC loading): unit **N/mm** > - **Dynamic stiffness** $K_d$ measured at engine idle frequency (typically **25 Hz**): unit **N/mm** > - **Loss factor** $\eta = K_d^{\prime\prime}/K_d^{\prime}$ (ratio of imaginary to real part of complex dynamic stiffness): dimensionless, typical rubber = **0.05–0.30** > - **Critical (natural) frequency** $f_n$ for an engine mount: typically **8–15 Hz** > - **Idle excitation frequency** for 4-cyl 4-stroke: $f_{idle} = n/60 \cdot \text{cyl}/2$ → **25–33 Hz** > - Rule of thumb: $f_{idle}/f_n \geq \sqrt{2}$ for isolation to occur. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Physical Idealization of a Mount A vehicle mount is modelled as a **Kelvin–Voigt element**: a linear spring (stiffness $K$) in parallel with a viscous damper (damping coefficient $C$). This element transmits a force $F$ and allows a relative displacement $x$ between the two end nodes. The governing scalar force–displacement relation (uniaxial) is: $$F(t) = K \cdot x(t) + C \cdot \dot{x}(t)$$ When excited harmonically at angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$, the complex dynamic stiffness becomes: $$K^{\ast}(\omega) = K + j\omega C = K_d^{\prime} + j K_d^{\prime\prime}$$ where: - $K_d^{\prime}(\omega) = K + \Delta K(\omega)$ = real (storage) part - $K_d^{\prime\prime}(\omega) = \omega C$ = imaginary (loss) part - $\Delta K(\omega)$ = frequency-dependent stiffening of elastomer ### 2.2 Why Each Term Matters (Bulleted Logic) - **Why a spring term $K$?** Static equilibrium of the mounted mass requires an elastic restoring force equal to $mg$. Without $K$, the engine would sink or float freely. - **Why a damper term $C\dot{x}$?** Real elastomers dissipate energy through viscoelastic hysteresis. Without $C$, free vibration would persist forever (the mount would oscillate like an undamped spring). - **Why $K + j\omega C$ (and not just $K$)?** At higher excitation frequencies, viscous losses grow linearly with $\omega$, making the effective stiffness complex (frequency-dependent). This is the foundation of the **loss factor** $\eta$. - **Why is $f_{idle}$ designed to be > $\sqrt{2} \cdot f_n$?** Below this threshold, vibration is amplified (transmissibility $T > 1$); above it, attenuation begins. Vehicle designers tune mount stiffness so that engine idle and most road excitation sit in the isolation region. ### 2.3 Single-Degree-of-Freedom (SDOF) Mounted Mass Model Consider an engine of mass $m$ supported on a vertical mount, with base input $y(t)$ from road/engine shake: - $x(t)$ = absolute displacement of engine mass - $y(t)$ = base (chassis) displacement - $z(t) = x(t) - y(t)$ = relative displacement across mount Equation of motion (Newton's second law on mass $m$): $$m\ddot{x} + C(\dot{x}-\dot{y}) + K(x-y) = 0$$ Rearranging in terms of base excitation (re-writing $x = z + y$): $$m\ddot{z} + C\dot{z} + Kz = -m\ddot{y}$$ This is the **classic base-excitation SDOF equation**. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions | Unit | Notes / Boundary | |---|----------|---------|--------------------|------|------------------| | 1 | Natural angular frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/m}$ | $K$ = stiffness, $m$ = mass | rad/s | Valid for SDOF linear system | | 2 | Natural frequency (Hz) | $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/m}$ | – | Hz | Multiply $\omega_n$ by $1/(2\pi)$ | | 3 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{Km}}$ | $C$ = damping coeff. | dimensionless | $\zeta < 1$ → underdamped | | 4 | Loss factor (viscous) | $\eta(\omega) = \dfrac{\omega C}{K}$ | – | dimensionless | Rubber: 0.05–0.30 | | 5 | Loss factor (hysteretic) | $\eta = \dfrac{K_d^{\prime\prime}}{K_d^{\prime}}$ | – | dimensionless | Frequency-independent | | 6 | Engine idle freq. (4-cyl) | $f_{idle} = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{cyl}}{120}$ | $N$ = rpm | Hz | Combustion pulses/min | | 7 | Transmissibility magnitude | $T = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | dimensionless | Force transmissibility | | 8 | Isolation condition | $r > \sqrt{2}$ (only if $\zeta$ small) | – | – | $T < 1$ zone | | 9 | Static deflection | $\delta_{st} = mg/K$ | $g = 9.81$ m/s² | mm | Mount sag under weight | | 10 | Resonance amplification | $T_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ at $r=1$ | – | – | Peak amplification | | 11 | Static stiffness | $K_s = F_{static}/\delta_{st}$ | – | N/mm | Measured at 0 Hz | | 12 | Dynamic stiffness ratio | $\beta = K_d/K_s$ | – | – | Typically 1.2–2.5 for rubber | > [!NOTE] > **For KTU numerical problems**, the most frequently tested formulae are: (i) $f_n$, (ii) $\zeta$, (iii) $T(\omega)$, and (iv) $f_{idle}$. Memorize these four as the foundation. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **OEM Calibration**: Mounts are tuned by altering durometer (hardness), shape (cylindrical, hourglass, pancake), and fluid-fill (hydromounts) to shift $f_n$ away from engine firing orders. - **NVH Bench Testing**: Engineers measure $K^{\ast}(\omega)$ on a dynamic test rig (e.g., MTS,Instron) sweeping 1–200 Hz to validate CAE predictions. - **Failure Analysis**: Cracked or hardened mounts cause structure-borne noise inside cabins; tracking $\eta$ over service life predicts end-of-life. --- ## 3.
