Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Natural Frequency from SDOF Equation of Motion **Statement:** Derive the natural (resonant) frequency of an engine of mass $m$ mounted on a linear spring of stiffness $K$. **Starting point** (free body diagram, vertical direction, no damping for clarity): $$m\ddot{x} + Kx = 0$$ **Step 1 — Assume harmonic solution:** $$x(t) = X\sin(\omega t + \phi)$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate twice:** $$\dot{x}(t) = X\omega\cos(\omega t + \phi)$$ $$\ddot{x}(t) = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t + \phi) = -\omega^2 x(t)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into equation of motion:** $$m(-\omega^2 x) + Kx = 0$$ **Step 4 — Factor out $x$ (non-trivial solution requires $x \neq 0$):** $$x(K - m\omega^2) = 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; K - m\omega^2 = 0$$ **Step 5 — Solve for $\omega$:** $$\omega^2 = \dfrac{K}{m} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$$ **Step 6 — Convert to Hz:** $$f_n = \dfrac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}}$$ This is the natural frequency that every mounted powertrain system possesses. If the engine's firing frequency $f_{idle}$ coincides with $f_n$, severe vibration amplification occurs. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Force Transmissibility with Viscous Damping **Statement:** For a SDOF system with base excitation $y(t) = Y\sin\omega t$, derive the force transmissibility $T = F_T/F_0$ where $F_T$ is the force transmitted to the base and $F_0$ is the excitation force amplitude. **Step 1 — Write the EOM with base excitation (relative displacement $z = x - y$):** $$m\ddot{z} + C\dot{z} + Kz = -m\ddot{y}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute harmonic base $y(t) = Y\sin\omega t$ → $\ddot{y} = -Y\omega^2\sin\omega t$:** $$m\ddot{z} + C\dot{z} + Kz = mY\omega^2 \sin\omega t$$ **Step 3 — Assume steady-state solution of the form:** $$z(t) = Z\sin(\omega t - \theta)$$ Then $\dot{z} = Z\omega\cos(\omega t - \theta)$ and $\ddot{z} = -Z\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \theta)$. **Step 4 — Substitute into the EOM and group coefficients of $\sin$ and $\cos$:** The amplitude $Z$ is obtained by equating coefficients: $$Z = \dfrac{mY\omega^2}{\sqrt{(K-m\omega^2)^2 + (C\omega)^2}}$$ **Step 5 — Express in terms of $r = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\zeta$:** Divide numerator and denominator by $K$ and use $K = m\omega_n^2$, $C = 2\zeta m\omega_n$: $$Z = \dfrac{Y\,r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **Step 6 — Force transmitted to the base through the mount:** The transmitted force equals the spring + damper reaction: $$F_T(t) = Kz + C\dot{z}$$ Its amplitude is: $$F_T = Z\sqrt{K^2 + (C\omega)^2}$$ **Step 7 — Substitute $Z$ and simplify using $K = m\omega_n^2$ and $C = 2\zeta m\omega_n\omega/\omega$ (careful algebra):** $$F_T = m\omega_n^2 \cdot Y\,r^2 \cdot \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ **Step 8 — Compare with excitation force amplitude $F_0 = mY\omega^2 = m\omega_n^2 Y r^2$:** $$\boxed{\;T(\omega) = \dfrac{F_T}{F_0} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;}$$ **Step 9 — Limiting cases (verify physically):** - At $r \to 0$ (very low frequency): $T \to \sqrt{1/1} = 1$ → no isolation, full force transmitted. ✓ - At $r = 1$ (resonance): $T = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + 4\zeta^2}}{2\zeta} \to \infty$ as $\zeta \to 0$. ✓ - At $r \to \infty$: $T \to \dfrac{2\zeta r}{r^2} = \dfrac{2\zeta}{r} \to 0$ → perfect isolation. ✓ - Setting $T = 1$ and solving yields $r = \sqrt{2}$ (independent of $\zeta$ to first order). ✓ --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Engine Idle Frequency for a 4-Cylinder 4-Stroke Engine **Statement:** Derive the dominant excitation frequency for a 4-cylinder, 4-stroke gasoline engine running at $N$ rpm. **Step 1 — Number of combustion events per revolution:** In a 4-stroke engine, each cylinder fires once every **2 revolutions**. With 4 cylinders, total firings per revolution = $4/2 = 2$. **Step 2 — Firings per minute:** $$\text{Firings/min} = N \cdot 2 = 2N$$ **Step 3 — Convert to Hz (firings per second):** $$f_{idle} = \dfrac{2N}{60} = \dfrac{N}{30}\;\text{Hz}$$ **Step 4 — Generalize to $k$ cylinders, 4-stroke:** $$f_{idle} = \dfrac{kN}{120}\;\text{Hz}$$ For 6-cylinder 4-stroke at 3000 rpm: $f = 6 \times 3000 / 120 = \mathbf{150\;Hz}$. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Critical Numerical Comparison Table | $r = \omega/\omega_n$ | $\zeta = 0.05$ | $\zeta = 0.10$ | $\zeta = 0.20$ | $\zeta = 0.40$ | |:--------------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------:| | 0.1 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | 1.00 | | 0.5 | 1.42 | 1.32 | 1.13 | 0.92 | | 0.7 | 2.34 | 1.89 | 1.39 | 1.04 | | **1.0** | **9.95** | **5.03** | **2.55** | **1.31** | | $\sqrt{2}$ ≈ 1.414 | 1.04 | 1.02 | 1.00 | 1.00 | | 2.0 | 0.53 | 0.53 | 0.55 | 0.62 | | 4.0 | 0.25 | 0.25 | 0.26 | 0.29 | | 10.0 | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.10 | **Key Insight from Table:** - At $r=1$, low damping gives $T \approx 10$; high damping caps it near 1.3 — but high damping degrades high-frequency isolation (compare $r=4$: all $\zeta$ similar). - The **isolation region $r > \sqrt{2}$** is the working regime of an engine mount. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a Vehicle Mounting Model ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Excitation Source
Road Input or Engine Firing] --> B[Base Node
Chassis or Sub-frame y of t] B --> C[Mount Element
Kelvin Voigt K and C] C --> D[Mounted Mass
Engine or Powertrain m] D --> E[Measured Response
Acceleration or Displacement] C --> C1[Spring Branch K of x] C --> C2[Damper Branch C dx by dt] C1 --> C3[Parallel Coupling] C2 --> C3 D --> F[Sensor: Accelerometer] D --> G[Sensor: LVDT for Static Deflection] E --> H[FFT Analyzer Frequency Domain] F --> H G --> H H --> I[Output Transmissibility Curve T vs Frequency] H --> J[Output Natural Frequency f sub n] H --> K[Output Damping Ratio Zeta] ``` **Block-Level Interpretation:** - **Excitation Source** drives the base node, which represents the chassis or sub-frame input. - **Mount Element** is the dynamic kernel — modelled as a parallel spring-damper (Kelvin–Voigt). - **Mounted Mass** is the engine/aggregate whose vibration we want to control. - **Sensors** capture static (LVDT) and dynamic (accelerometer) responses. - **FFT analyzer** transforms time data to frequency domain, yielding $T(f)$, $f_n$, and $\zeta$. ### 4.2 Mermaid Free-Vibration Decay Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Impact Load Initial Displacement x0] --> P2[Mass m begins Oscillation] P2 --> P3[Spring K stores Energy] P2 --> P4[Damper C dissipates Energy] P3 --> P5[Amplitude decays Envelope exp minus zeta omega n t] P4 --> P5 P5 --> P6[Logarithmic Decrement delta] P6 --> P7[Damping Ratio zeta from Log Decrement] ``` **Engineering meaning:** Logarithmic decrement is measured experimentally by tapping the mount and recording the free-decay oscillation. From the ratio of successive peaks $x_n/x_{n+1}$: $$\delta = \ln\left(\dfrac{x_n}{x_{n+1}}\right) = \dfrac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$ Solving for small $\zeta$ ($\zeta < 0.3$): $\zeta \approx \delta/(2\pi)$. ### 4.3 Schematic of Test-Rig Configuration (Block Form) ```mermaid flowchart TD R1[Hydraulic Shaker Actuator] --> R2[Mount Specimen under Test] R2 --> R3[Load Cell measures Transmitted Force F sub T] R2 --> R4[Accelerometer measures Mounted Mass Response] R1 --> R5[LVDT measures Base Input y of t] R3 --> R6[Data Acquisition DAQ System] R4 --> R6 R5 --> R6 R6 --> R7[Controller Sweeps Frequency 1 to 200 Hz] R7 --> R1 R6 --> R8[Output T of omega and K star of omega] ``` This represents the **standardized dynamic stiffness test** (per ISO 1726 and SAE J1502) used by every automotive OEM to characterize mounts. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]`** **Define "model mounting" in the context of vehicle testing. State any two functional requirements of a vehicle mount.** **Model Answer (3 marks — definition 2M, requirements 1M):** > **Model mounting** refers to the mathematical idealization (typically as a spring–dashpot element) and physical configuration of the elastomeric/pneumatic elements used to support powertrain or chassis components on a vehicle, enabling analysis of their static load-bearing and dynamic vibration-isolation behaviour under laboratory testing conditions. **Two functional requirements (any two, ½ mark each):** 1. Static load carrying: must support engine weight with controlled sag (≤ 15 mm). 2. Vibration isolation: transmissibility $T < 1$ above the natural frequency. 3. Shock durability: survive 2–3× static load transient events without failure. 4. Environmental resistance: thermal, oil, ozone stability. --- **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]`** **A vehicle engine mount has a stiffness of $K = 250$ N/mm and supports an engine mass of $m = 120$ kg. Calculate the natural frequency in Hz.** **Model Answer (3 marks — formula 1M, substitution 1M, answer 1M):** Given: $K = 250$ N/mm = $250{,}000$ N/m, $m = 120$ kg. Using $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/m}$: $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{250{,}000}{120}}$$ $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{2083.33} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}(45.64)$$ $$\boxed{f_n \approx 7.26 \text{ Hz}}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2]` **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **idealized SDOF Kelvin–Voigt model** of an engine mounting system. Define stiffness, damping coefficient, natural frequency, and damping ratio with units. **(7 marks)** **(b)** An engine of mass 150 kg is supported on a mount of stiffness $K = 180$ N/mm and damping coefficient $C = 800$ N·s/m. The base is excited by an engine firing frequency of 30 Hz. Calculate: (i) Natural frequency $f_n$ (ii) Damping ratio $\zeta$ (iii) Frequency ratio $r$ (iv) Force transmissibility $T$ **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A: **(a) Theoretical explanation (7 marks):** 1. **Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a mass $m$ on top of a parallel spring $K$ and damper $C$, with base excitation $y(t)$ and absolute mass response $x(t)$. (See Section 4.1.) 2. **Equation of motion (2 marks):** $$m\ddot{x} + C(\dot{x}-\dot{y}) + K(x-y) = 0$$ 3. **Definitions (3 marks):** - **Stiffness $K$:** slope of force–deflection curve at static equilibrium; unit N/m or N/mm. - **Damping coefficient $C$:** viscous force per unit relative velocity; unit N·s/m. - **Natural frequency $\omega_n = \sqrt{K/m}$, $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/m}$**: unit rad/s or Hz. - **Damping ratio $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{Km}}$**: dimensionless. --- **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks — 1.5M + 1.5M + 1M + 3M):** **Given:** $m = 150$ kg, $K = 180$ N/mm = $180{,}000$ N/m, $C = 800$ N·s/m, excitation frequency $f = 30$ Hz. **(i) Natural frequency:** $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{180{,}000}{150}}$$ $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{1200} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}(34.64)$$ $$\boxed{f_n \approx 5.51 \text{ Hz}} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$ **(ii) Damping ratio:** $$\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{Km}} = \dfrac{800}{2\sqrt{180{,}000 \times 150}}$$ $$\zeta = \dfrac{800}{2\sqrt{27{,}000{,}000}} = \dfrac{800}{2 \times 5196.15} = \dfrac{800}{10{,}392.3}$$ $$\boxed{\zeta \approx 0.077} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$ **(iii) Frequency ratio:** $$r = \dfrac{f}{f_n} = \dfrac{30}{5.51} \approx 5.44$$ $$\boxed{r \approx 5.44} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$ **(iv) Force transmissibility:** $$T = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$ Calculate components: - $2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.077 \times 5.44 = 0.838$ - $(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.702$ - $r^2 = 29.59$, $1 - r^2 = -28.59$, $(1-r^2)^2 = 817.4$ $$T = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + 0.702}}{\sqrt{817.4 + 0.702}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{1.702}}{\sqrt{818.1}}$$ $$T = \dfrac{1.305}{28.60} \approx 0.0456$$ $$\boxed{T \approx 0.046 \text{ or } 4.6\%} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$ **Interpretation:** Only ~4.6% of the excitation force reaches the chassis — excellent isolation, as expected since $r = 5.44 \gg \sqrt{2}$. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]` **(a)** Discuss the different types of vehicle mounts (engine, transmission, suspension, body) and the materials used. Explain the concept of **static vs dynamic stiffness** with reference to the **loss factor**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 4-cylinder 4-stroke gasoline engine runs at 3000 rpm. Each mount has a stiffness $K = 200$ N/mm and supports 50 kg. If the engine block is excited harmonically with an amplitude of 1 mm at the firing frequency: (i) Compute the firing frequency $f_{idle}$. (ii) Compute the natural frequency $f_n$. (iii) Determine whether the system is in the isolation region or amplification region. **(7 marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B: **(a) Theoretical discussion (7 marks):** 1. **Types of mounts (3 marks):** Engine mounts (NVH + load), transmission mounts (torsional isolation), suspension bushings (compliance + alignment), body/sub-frame mounts (high-frequency isolation), strut mounts (steering precision). 2. **Materials (2 marks):** Natural rubber (NR), styrene–butadiene rubber (SBR), EPDM, polyurethane (PU), silicone, hydraulic fluid-filled elastomer (hydromounts). Choose based on temperature range, oil resistance, durability. 3. **Static vs dynamic stiffness + loss factor (2 marks):** - **Static stiffness $K_s$:** force per unit deflection measured quasi-statically (e.g., 0.01 Hz loading). Reflects mounting sag. - **Dynamic stiffness $K_d(\omega)$:** force per unit harmonic deflection at frequency $\omega$. Includes viscous and inertial stiffening effects. - **Loss factor $\eta = K_d^{\prime\prime}/K_d^{\prime}$:** ratio of imaginary (energy loss) to real (energy storage) stiffness. Rubber $\eta \approx 0.1$; higher $\eta$ means better damping but reduced high-frequency isolation. --- **(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):** **(i) Firing frequency:** For 4-cylinder, 4-stroke at $N = 3000$ rpm: $$f_{idle} = \dfrac{kN}{120} = \dfrac{4 \times 3000}{120} = \boxed{100 \text{ Hz}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(ii) Natural frequency:** $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{m}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{200{,}000}{50}}$$ $$f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{4000} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}(63.25)$$ $$\boxed{f_n \approx 10.06 \text{ Hz}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$ **(iii) Isolation or amplification?** $$r = \dfrac{f_{idle}}{f_n} = \dfrac{100}{10.06} \approx 9.94$$ Since $r = 9.94 \gg \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, **the system is deep in the isolation region**. Transmission to chassis will be very small. **(3 marks)** Even without computing $T$, the student should justify using $r > \sqrt{2}$ criterion. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Mounting Problems:** > 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Stiffness is often given in N/mm but $K/m$ in formula requires N/m. Always convert: **multiply N/mm by 1000**. > 2. **Forgetting to convert $\omega$ to $f$:** $T(\omega)$ uses $\omega$ in rad/s; $f_n$ in Hz requires division by $2\pi$. > 3. **Wrong idle frequency formula:** Students use $f = N/60$ (single cylinder firing) instead of $f = kN/120$. For a 4-cyl 4-stroke at 3000 rpm, answer is **100 Hz, not 50 Hz**. > 4. **Ignoring damping when stated:** If $\zeta$ is given in the problem, it **must** appear in the transmissibility equation. Omitting it loses 2–3 marks. > 5. **Sign error in $1-r^2$:** Transmissibility formula uses $(1-r^2)^2$, so the negative sign disappears by squaring. Do not mistakenly write $r^2 - 1$. > 6. **Forgetting internal choice:** KTU ESE always provides Module internal choice — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B, not both. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Mount = Kelvin–Voigt element** = parallel spring ($K$) + damper ($C$). - **Three roles**: load support, vibration isolation, shock attenuation. - **Governing formula**: $m\ddot{x} + C(\dot{x}-\dot{y}) + K(x-y) = 0$. - **Natural frequency**: $f_n = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{K/m}$ — memorize with unit care. - **Damping ratio**: $\zeta = \dfrac{C}{2\sqrt{Km}}$ — dimensionless. - **Frequency ratio**: $r = \omega/\omega_n = f/f_n$. - **Transmissibility**: $T = \dfrac{\sqrt{1+(2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2+(2\zeta r)^2}}$. - **Isolation criterion**: $r > \sqrt{2}$ AND $\zeta$ small → $T < 1$. - **Resonance peak**: $T_{max} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\zeta}$ (for small $\zeta$). - **Engine idle frequency**: $f_{idle} = \dfrac{kN}{120}$ Hz (for 4-stroke, $k$ cylinders). - **Loss factor**: $\eta = K_d^{\prime\prime}/K_d^{\prime}$ — characterizes elastomer damping. - **Static stiffness** $K_s$ at 0 Hz; **dynamic stiffness** $K_d$ at operational frequency. - **Test rig**: shaker + load cell + accelerometer + DAQ → sweep 1–200 Hz → compute $T(\omega)$. - **Typical values**: engine mount $K$ = 100–600 N/mm, $\zeta$ = 0.05–0.15, $f_n$ = 8–15 Hz. - **Rule of thumb**: design $f_n$ such that $f_{idle}/f_n \geq 3$ for comfortable isolation. ---

topics.title: Test Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Test Procedure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle Test Procedure** refers to the systematically engineered, standardised sequence of preparatory, executional, and post-processing operations conducted on a production-intent or prototype vehicle to objectively quantify, validate, and document its performance characteristics — including *acceleration, braking, top speed, fuel economy, gradeability, coast-down resistance, and emissions* — under rigorously controlled ambient, road-surface, and instrumentation conditions prescribed by recognised national and international standards.

The governing documents typically include:

| Standard Body | Document | Scope |
|---------------|----------|-------|
| **SAE International** | SAE J1346, J2188, J1491 | Acceleration, Coast-down, Braking |
| **ISO** | ISO 362-1, ISO 10521 | Sound & Coast-down |
| **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** | IS 14654, IS 11852 | Indian road-test procedure |
| **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards)** | AIS-051, AIS-052, AIS-053 | Indian type approval |
| **ECE / EEC** | ECE R13, R15, R117 | Braking, Emissions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Emphasis:** Examiners *expect* students to recall the standard atmospheric reference conditions used for correction of test results. The **standard reference temperature is 25 °C** and **standard barometric pressure is 100 kPa** as per SAE J1346 / IS 14654.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Annual Health Check-Up"

Imagine a doctor examining a patient. Before declaring the patient fit, the doctor insists on **standardised conditions**: the patient must be at rest (vehicle: warmed up), in a fasting state (vehicle: cool ambient), and specific tests must follow a strict order — blood pressure, then ECG, then blood report (vehicle: brake test, then acceleration, then fuel-consumption test).

Likewise, a vehicle undergoing a **test procedure** must:

1. Be in a *defined state* (run-in completed, fuel topped, tyre pressure set).
2. Be tested in *defined ambient conditions* (temperature, humidity, wind speed < 3 m/s).
3. Be tested by *defined driver inputs* (full-throttle, controlled braking).
4. Be measured using *calibrated, traceable instruments*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why standardise?** Two different testers measuring the same car on the same day under different conditions can get results differing by **up to 15 %** for fuel economy and **up to 20 %** for coast-down coefficient. Standardisation removes this ambiguity.

### 1.3 Classification of Test Procedures

Tests are broadly classified into:

- **Road (Chassis) Dynamometer Tests** — performed on a chassis dynamometer (indoor, climate-controlled).
- **Track / Road Tests** — performed on a test track (high-speed oval, straight, gradient ramp).
- **Component / Bench Tests** — engine on dyno, transmission on test bench.

---

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Pre-Test Setup Checklist (KTU Board Favourite):**
> 1. Vehicle mass in **kerb** or **loaded** condition as specified.
> 2. Tyre pressure to manufacturer specification (cold).
> 3. Fuel tank filled to **90 % capacity** for whole-vehicle tests.
> 4. Engine, transmission, brakes run-in complete (minimum **1500 km**).
> 5. Ambient temperature: **5 °C – 40 °C**.
> 6. Wind speed **< 3 m/s** for outdoor tests.
> 7. Track surface: dry, level (±1 %), clean asphalt/concrete.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Phase-Wise Logical Breakdown of a Standard Test Procedure

A complete vehicle performance test is partitioned into **four ordered phases**. Deviating from this order invalidates the data because earlier tests can pre-condition the vehicle (e.g., a brake test heats the brakes, biasing subsequent coast-down results).

**Phase A — Pre-Test Verification**
- Vehicle inspection and photograph documentation.
- Verification of odometer reading (must exceed run-in mileage).
- Tyre pressure, brake-fluid level, engine-oil level check.
- Instrumentation mounting (GPS antenna, fifth-wheel, OBD logger, accelerometers, fuel-flow meter).
- Calibration: all sensors zeroed with vehicle on level ground.

**Phase B — Warm-Up & Conditioning**
- Vehicle driven at 60 % of max speed for a minimum of **30 km** to bring engine, transmission, tyres to operating temperature.
- Brakes bedded-in by a series of controlled snubs.
- This phase ensures **repeatability** of measurements.

**Phase C — Performance Tests (in mandated order)**
1. **Coast-Down Test** (resistance coefficients) — must be first; affected least by fuel/load.
2. **Maximum Speed Test**.
3. **Acceleration Tests** (0–100, 400 m, 1 km standing start).
4. **Gradeability Test**.
5. **Brake Test** (cold + hot).
6. **Fuel-Consumption Test** (constant-speed + simulated urban cycle).
7. **Emission Test** (optional, separate vehicle).

**Phase D — Post-Processing & Report Generation**
- Raw data correction using standard temperature/pressure formulae.
- Statistical averaging across **a minimum of three runs** in each direction (to cancel wind & gradient effects).
- Generation of consolidated test report.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2 = \dfrac{F_D}{m g}$ | Coast-down resistance coefficient form | dimensionless |
| 2 | $a = \dfrac{v_i^2 - v_f^2}{2 \, s}$ | Mean deceleration from speed $v_i$ to $v_f$ over distance $s$ | m/s² |
| 3 | $t = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \dfrac{m \, \delta}{\left(F_t - F_R\right)} \, dv$ | Acceleration time between speeds (Newton's 2nd law) | s |
| 4 | $\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{F_t - F_R - F_A}{m \, g}$ | Maximum sustainable gradient | – |
| 5 | $\text{FC} = \dfrac{V_f}{D} \times 3600$ | Volumetric fuel consumption (instantaneous) | L/h |
| 6 | $\text{Mileage} = \dfrac{D \times 1000}{V_f}$ | Distance per litre fuel economy | km/L |
| 7 | $D_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum D_i}{n}$ | Average of $n$ coast-down runs | m |
| 8 | $C_D = \dfrac{2 \, F_{D,air}}{\rho \, A_f \, v^2}$ | Drag coefficient from aerodynamic force | – |
| 9 | $\text{Correction Factor} = \dfrac{T_{ref}}{T_{amb}}$ | Temperature correction for fuel consumption | – |
| 10 | $t_{0-100} = \text{measured}$ time with $v_1=0, v_2=27.78$ m/s | Standard 0–100 km/h time | s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation used:**
> - $F_t$ = Tractive force at tyre–road interface (N)
> - $F_R = m g (f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2)$ = Total resistance force (N)
> - $f_0, f_1, f_2$ = Rolling, speed-linear, aerodynamic resistance coefficients
> - $\delta$ = Rotational inertia factor (≈ 1.05–1.10 for passenger car)
> - $\rho$ = Air density (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at STP)
> - $A_f$ = Frontal area (m²)

### 2.3 Real-World Utility of Standardised Test Procedures

- **Type Approval & Homologation:** Every vehicle sold in India must pass **AIS-053** (CMVR) emission and **AIS-051** brake tests before registration.
- **Manufacturer R&D:** Engineers objectively compare two design iterations; without standard procedures, "feel-based" judgements fail.
- **Consumer Transparency:** **ARAI-certified** fuel-economy figures (e.g., the mileage printed in brochures) are generated under exactly these procedures.
- **Insurance & Forensics:** Accident reconstruction relies on the *standard* braking-distance data.
- **Legal Compliance:** Court-admissible evidence requires traceable, standard test data.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coast-Down Test Resistance Coefficients

The **coast-down test** is the foundation of all subsequent performance tests because it provides the resistance coefficients used in acceleration and gradeability equations.

**Conceptual Setup:** A vehicle is accelerated to a speed slightly above the desired top speed, placed in neutral gear, and allowed to decelerate naturally due to rolling resistance, aerodynamic drag, and driveline friction. Time or distance is recorded as a function of instantaneous speed.

**Step 1 — Force Balance in Coast-Down**

Newton's second law for the coasting vehicle (no tractive force):

$$
m \, \frac{dv}{dt} = -\left[F_{R0} + F_{R1}\,v + F_{R2}\,v^2\right]
$$

where $F_{R0}, F_{R1}, F_{R2}$ are the constant, linear-in-speed, and quadratic-in-speed resistance coefficients respectively.

**Step 2 — Resisting Torque Components**

The total resistance torque on the vehicle comprises rolling resistance, driveline friction, and aerodynamic drag.

$$
F_{R} = m g \, f_0 + N \, c_{dr} \, v + \tfrac{1}{2} \rho \, C_D \, A_f \, v^2
$$

where $N$ is the rotational speed of the driveline (proportional to vehicle speed $v$).

**Step 3 — Non-dimensionalising by Weight**

Dividing both sides by $mg$ gives the dimensionless form:

$$
\frac{1}{g}\frac{dv}{dt} = -\left[f_0 + f_1\, v + f_2\, v^2\right]
$$

This is the canonical **SAE J2188** form, where:

- $f_0$ ≈ rolling-only term (≈ **0.013–0.018** for passenger car)
- $f_1$ ≈ driveline-friction term (≈ **0** in modern cars with low-friction bearings)
- $f_2$ ≈ aerodynamic term (≈ **0.00003–0.00006** for typical sedan)

**Step 4 — Speed-Distance Relation**

Using the kinematic identity $\dfrac{dv}{dt} = v \, \dfrac{dv}{ds}$:

$$
v \, \frac{dv}{ds} = -g\left[f_0 + f_1\, v + f_2\, v^2\right]
$$

Rearranging for displacement $s$ between speeds $v_1$ and $v_2$:

$$
ds = -\frac{v \, dv}{g\left[f_0 + f_1\, v + f_2\, v^2\right]}
$$

Integrating from $v_1$ (initial) to $v_2$ (final):

$$
s = \frac{1}{2 g f_2}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{f_0 + f_1 v_1 + f_2 v_1^2}{f_0 + f_1 v_2 + f_2 v_2^2}\right] - \frac{f_1}{2 g f_2}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{v_1\,(f_0 + f_1 v_2 + f_2 v_2^2)}{v_2\,(f_0 + f_1 v_1 + f_2 v_1^2)}\right] \cdot \tfrac{1}{\text{(combined)}} 
$$

In **simplified two-coefficient form** ($f_1 \approx 0$):

$$
s = \frac{1}{2 g f_2}\,\ln\!\left[\frac{f_0 + f_2 v_1^2}{f_0 + f_2 v_2^2}\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineer's quick rule:** During coast-down, drag dominates at high speeds ($> 80$ km/h) and rolling resistance dominates at low speeds ($< 40$ km/h). The transition point is approximately at the speed where the two resistances are equal.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Deceleration in a Brake Test

**Step 1 — Apply kinematic equation for constant deceleration:**

$$
v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2 \, a_{avg} \, s
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $a_{avg}$:**

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{v_i^2 - v_f^2}{2\,s}
$$

**Step 3 — Typical Numerical Example (KTU-style problem):**

A vehicle travelling at $v_i = 100$ km/h = $27.78$ m/s brakes to a halt ($v_f = 0$) over a distance $s = 50$ m. Calculate mean deceleration.

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{(27.78)^2 - 0^2}{2 \times 50}
$$

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{771.73}{100} = 7.717 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to g-units** (for examiner-friendly reporting):

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{7.717}{9.81} \approx 0.787 \, g
$$

This corresponds to a "Good" brake rating per typical KTU marking schemes (≥ 0.8 g = Excellent; 0.6–0.8 g = Good; < 0.6 g = Poor).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Acceleration Time (Time to Accelerate from $v_1$ to $v_2$)

**Step 1 — Newton's Second Law for an accelerating vehicle:**

$$
m\,\delta\,\frac{dv}{dt} = F_t - F_R
$$

where $\delta$ accounts for rotational inertia of engine, wheels, transmission.

**Step 2 — Rearranging for time:**

$$
dt = \frac{m\,\delta\,dv}{F_t(v) - m g \left(f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2\right)}
$$

**Step 3 — Integrate between $v_1$ and $v_2$:**

$$
t_{1\to 2} = \int_{v_1}^{v_2} \frac{m\,\delta}{F_t(v) - m g \left(f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2\right)} \, dv
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Trapezoidal Approximation (since $F_t(v)$ is non-analytical from engine map):**

$$
t_{1\to 2} \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{m\,\delta\,\Delta v}{F_t(v_i) - F_R(v_i)}
$$

Practically, KTU board problems use **gear-shift logic**:

| Gear | Speed Range | Force Equation |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| 1st | 0 → ~25 km/h | $F_t = \eta_g \cdot T_{e,max} \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 / r_d$ |
| 2nd | ~25 → ~50 km/h | (similar with 2nd gear ratio) |
| 3rd | ~50 → ~80 km/h | (similar with 3rd gear ratio) |
| 4th/5th | > 80 km/h | (similar with top gear ratio) |

Total $t_{0-100} = t_{0-25} + t_{25-50} + t_{50-80} + t_{80-100}$ (with optimal shift points).

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Gradeability at Steady Speed

**Step 1 — Force balance on an incline of angle $\theta$ at constant speed:**

$$
F_t = F_R + m g \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $\theta_{max}$:**

$$
\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{F_{t,max} - m g \left(f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2\right)}{m g}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to grade percentage:**

$$
\% \text{ grade} = \tan\theta_{max} \times 100 \approx \sin\theta_{max} \times 100 \text{ (for small angles)}
$$

A **30 % grade** is considered a steep mountain road; commercial trucks must achieve **≥ 25 %** at rated load.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Standard Vehicle Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["START: Vehicle Receipt"] --> nodeB["Pre-Test Inspection"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Instrumentation Mount"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Calibration Check"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Warm-Up Phase 30 km"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Phase 1: Coast-Down Test"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Phase 2: Max Speed Test"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["Phase 3: Acceleration 0 to 100"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["Phase 4: Gradeability Test"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["Phase 5: Brake Cold and Hot"]
    nodeJ --> nodeK["Phase 6: Fuel Economy"]
    nodeK --> nodeL["Phase 7: Emission if applicable"]
    nodeL --> nodeM["Data Logging and Correction"]
    nodeM --> nodeN["Statistical Averaging of 3 plus runs"]
    nodeN --> nodeO["Report Generation"]
    nodeO --> nodeP["END: Certified Report Issued"]
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow for Test Acceptance / Rejection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeDA["Raw Test Data"] --> nodeDB["Apply Temperature Correction"]
    nodeDB --> nodeDC["Apply Barometric Correction"]
    nodeDC --> nodeDD["Apply Wind Correction"]
    nodeDD --> nodeDE{"Coefficient of Variation less than 3 percent"}
    nodeDE -- YES --> nodeDF["ACCEPT: Average Result"]
    nodeDE -- NO --> nodeDG["REPEAT Test Run"]
    nodeDG --> nodeDA
```

### 4.3 Instrument Layout Block Diagram (Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1["Engine"] --> nodeS2["Transmission"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS3["Wheels and Tyres"]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS4["Vehicle Body"]
    nodeS4 --> nodeS5["Frontal Air Flow"]
    nodeS5 --> nodeS6["Fifth Wheel Sensor"]
    nodeS6 --> nodeS7["Data Acquisition Unit DAU"]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS7
    nodeS4 --> nodeS8["Inertial Measurement Unit"]
    nodeS8 --> nodeS7
    nodeS4 --> nodeS9["Fuel Flow Meter"]
    nodeS9 --> nodeS7
    nodeS7 --> nodeS10["Onboard Computer"]
    nodeS10 --> nodeS11["Final Report Output"]
```

### 4.4 Test-Track Layout (Schematic — Top-Down Functional)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeT1["Coast-Down Lane Length 1 km"] --> nodeT2["Brake Test Lane Length 500 m"]
    nodeT2 --> nodeT3["Acceleration Lane Length 2 km"]
    nodeT3 --> nodeT4["Maximum Speed Oval"]
    nodeT4 --> nodeT5["Gradient Ramp 30 percent"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Track Requirements (IS 11852 / SAE J2188):**
> - Coast-down lane: **straight, level (gradient < 0.5 %), at least 1 km** long.
> - Wind speed during coast-down: **< 5 m/s**, perpendicular component **< 3 m/s**.
> - Surface texture consistent throughout lane; no puddles or loose debris.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
*List the standard reference atmospheric conditions used for correction of vehicle test results as per SAE J1346 / IS 14654.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribution below):**

The standard reference atmospheric conditions prescribed for vehicle test corrections are:

1. **Reference Temperature (T_ref):** $T_{ref} = 25\,^\circ\text{C} = 298.15\,\text{K}$ **(1 mark)**
2. **Reference Barometric Pressure (P_ref):** $P_{ref} = 100\,\text{kPa} = 1\,\text{bar}$ **(1 mark)**
3. **Reference Air Density:** $\rho_{ref} = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (derived from T and P above) **(1 mark)**

These values are used to correct measured fuel consumption, power, and coast-down data to a common reference so that tests performed on different days at different altitudes can be compared objectively.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
*Define the 'coast-down test'. State the standard test speeds between which coast-down data are typically recorded for a passenger car.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** The coast-down test is a controlled deceleration test in which a vehicle, accelerated to a speed slightly above the upper test speed, is placed in neutral and allowed to coast freely to a speed below the lower test speed. During coasting, the resistance forces (rolling, driveline friction, aerodynamic drag) decelerate the vehicle, and the deceleration profile is recorded to determine the resistance coefficients $f_0$, $f_1$, and $f_2$.

**Standard Test Speeds (1 mark):** As per **SAE J2188** and **IS 14654**, coast-down data for a passenger car are recorded between:
- **Lower speed:** $v_L = 16\,\text{km/h}$ (≈ 4.44 m/s)
- **Upper speed:** $v_U = 120\,\text{km/h}$ (≈ 33.33 m/s) — or **maximum achievable speed − 5 km/h**, whichever is lower.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model QP]**
**(a)** Describe, with a flowchart, the standard sequence of test procedures to be followed for a passenger-car performance evaluation. Justify why the **coast-down test is performed before the brake test**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A passenger car of mass $m = 1500\,\text{kg}$ is subjected to a brake test from an initial speed $v_i = 90\,\text{km/h}$ to a final speed $v_f = 0$. The measured stopping distance is $s = 42\,\text{m}$. Determine:
   (i) The mean deceleration in m/s² and in g.
   (ii) The braking efficiency (assumed theoretical maximum deceleration = $\mu g$ with $\mu = 0.85$).
   (iii) Comment on the brake performance rating. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Flowchart (4 marks):** Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes. The student must reproduce (in text or diagram form) the sequence:
*Pre-Test Inspection → Instrumentation → Calibration → Warm-up → Coast-Down → Max Speed → Acceleration → Gradeability → Brake → Fuel Economy → Report.*

**Justification of Coast-Down before Brake Test (3 marks):**
1. The coast-down test determines the **resistance coefficients** which are subsequently required for analytical predictions of acceleration, gradeability, and top speed.
2. Performing brake tests first would **elevate brake-disc temperature**, biasing subsequent coast-down results (since brake drag at temperature ≠ brake drag when cold).
3. Coast-down is **non-destructive** and only mildly heats the driveline; brake tests are **highly thermal** and must therefore be done later when their thermal disturbance will not contaminate other measurements.
4. The standard **SAE J2188** explicitly mandates this order.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$m = 1500\,\text{kg}$, $v_i = 90\,\text{km/h} = 25\,\text{m/s}$, $v_f = 0$, $s = 42\,\text{m}$, $\mu = 0.85$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**(i) Mean deceleration (3 marks):**

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{v_i^2 - v_f^2}{2 s}
$$

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{(25)^2 - 0^2}{2 \times 42} = \frac{625}{84}
$$

$$
a_{avg} \approx 7.440 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

Converting to g-units:

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{7.440}{9.81} \approx 0.758 \, g
$$

**(ii) Braking efficiency (2 marks):**

Theoretical maximum deceleration on a dry surface:

$$
a_{max} = \mu \cdot g = 0.85 \times 9.81 = 8.339 \, \text{m/s}^2
$$

Braking efficiency:

$$
\eta_{brake} = \frac{a_{avg}}{a_{max}} \times 100 \%
$$

$$
\eta_{brake} = \frac{7.440}{8.339} \times 100 \approx 89.22 \, \%
$$

**(iii) Performance rating (2 marks):**

A braking efficiency of **~89 %** and mean deceleration of **0.758 g** fall in the **"Good"** category per typical industry rating scales:

| Deceleration | Rating |
|--------------|--------|
| ≥ 0.8 g | Excellent |
| 0.6 – 0.8 g | Good |
| < 0.6 g | Poor |

**Verdict:** The vehicle exhibits **good braking performance**, suitable for normal passenger-car service.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 / Model QP]**
**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the **coast-down test procedure** in detail. Derive the expression for the deceleration of the vehicle in terms of the resistance coefficients $f_0, f_1, f_2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle of mass $1800\,\text{kg}$ has resistance coefficients $f_0 = 0.015$, $f_1 = 0$, $f_2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$. Calculate:
   (i) The total resistance force at $v = 100\,\text{km/h}$.
   (ii) The tractive force required to maintain $v = 100\,\text{km/h}$ on a 5 % gradient.
   (iii) The maximum sustainable gradient (in %) at $v = 60\,\text{km/h}$ if the engine can deliver a maximum tractive force of $8500\,\text{N}$ at this speed. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

**Procedure (3 marks):**
1. The test track is a straight, level lane of length ≥ 1 km.
2. Vehicle accelerated to $v_{max} + 5\,\text{km/h}$, gear placed in **neutral**, clutch released.
3. A fifth-wheel sensor records instantaneous vehicle speed; data sampled at ≥ **10 Hz**.
4. Wind speed and direction recorded at start, mid, and end of run.
5. The run is repeated **at least 3 times in each direction** (to cancel wind & gradient effects).
6. Resistance coefficients extracted by curve-fitting the deceleration profile.

**Derivation (4 marks):** Refer to **Derivation 1, Section 3.1** of these notes.

Final coast-down equation (canonical form):

$$
\frac{1}{g}\frac{dv}{dt} = -\left[f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2\right]
$$

where $f_0$ is rolling, $f_1 v$ is driveline friction, and $f_2 v^2$ is aerodynamic drag (non-dimensionalised by vehicle weight $mg$).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Given:**
$m = 1800\,\text{kg}$, $f_0 = 0.015$, $f_1 = 0$, $f_2 = 4.5 \times 10^{-5}$, $g = 9.81\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**(i) Resistance force at $v = 100\,\text{km/h} = 27.78\,\text{m/s}$ (2 marks):**

$$
F_R = m g \left[f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2\right]
$$

$$
F_R = 1800 \times 9.81 \times \left[0.015 + 0 + 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \times (27.78)^2\right]
$$

$$
F_R = 17658 \times \left[0.015 + 0.03472\right]
$$

$$
F_R = 17658 \times 0.04972 \approx 877.93 \, \text{N}
$$

**(ii) Tractive force on 5 % gradient (2 marks):**

5 % grade → $\sin\theta \approx 0.05$ (for small angles).

$$
F_t = F_R + m g \sin\theta
$$

$$
F_t = 877.93 + 1800 \times 9.81 \times 0.05
$$

$$
F_t = 877.93 + 882.9
$$

$$
F_t \approx 1760.83 \, \text{N}
$$

**(iii) Maximum gradient at $v = 60\,\text{km/h} = 16.67\,\text{m/s}$ with $F_t = 8500\,\text{N}$ (3 marks):**

Resistance at 60 km/h:

$$
F_R = 1800 \times 9.81 \times \left[0.015 + 4.5 \times 10^{-5} \times (16.67)^2\right]
$$

$$
F_R = 17658 \times \left[0.015 + 0.01250\right]
$$

$$
F_R = 17658 \times 0.02750 \approx 485.60 \, \text{N}
$$

Now, from $F_t = F_R + m g \sin\theta_{max}$:

$$
\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{F_t - F_R}{m g} = \frac{8500 - 485.60}{17658}
$$

$$
\sin\theta_{max} = \frac{8014.4}{17658} \approx 0.4539
$$

$$
\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.4539) \approx 27.0^\circ
$$

Converting to **percentage grade**:

$$
\% \text{ grade} = \tan(27.0^\circ) \times 100 \approx 0.5095 \times 100 \approx 50.95 \%
$$

**Result:** The vehicle can sustain a grade of approximately **51 %** at 60 km/h, which is **excellent** — suitable for heavy-duty mountain operation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion error:** Failing to convert km/h → m/s before substituting into formulas (loses 1–2 marks instantly).
> 2. **Order-of-test confusion:** Writing that brake test is performed before coast-down (loses 1 mark in part (a) reasoning).
> 3. **Confusing % grade with angle:** % grade uses $\tan\theta$, not $\sin\theta$. For small angles they are similar, but for grades > 30 % the distinction matters.
> 4. **Forgetting the rotational inertia factor $\delta$** in acceleration derivations. Even if the problem gives no value, *mentioning* $\delta$ and assuming $\delta \approx 1.05$ earns full credit.
> 5. **Not stating standard test conditions:** Many 3-mark questions require you to *list* the standard reference temperature and pressure (25 °C, 100 kPa). Omitting this is a 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [ ] **Standard reference conditions:** $T_{ref} = 25\,^\circ\text{C}$, $P_{ref} = 100\,\text{kPa}$, $\rho_{ref} = 1.2\,\text{kg/m}^3$.
- [ ] **Test order is mandatory:** Coast-Down → Max Speed → Acceleration → Gradeability → Brake → Fuel → Emission.
- [ ] **Coast-down form (canonical):** $\dfrac{1}{g}\dfrac{dv}{dt} = -(f_0 + f_1 v + f_2 v^2)$.
- [ ] **Brake test deceleration formula:** $a_{avg} = \dfrac{v_i^2 - v_f^2}{2s}$ — always convert km/h to m/s first.
- [ ] **Typical good brake deceleration:** ≥ 0.8 g (Excellent), 0.6–0.8 g (Good).
- [ ] **Fuel economy:** $\text{Mileage (km/L)} = \dfrac{D \times 1000}{V_f}$.
- [ ] **Track requirements:** straight, level (gradient < 0.5 %), wind speed < 5 m/s perpendicular component.
- [ ] **Instrumentation:** Fifth wheel, IMU, fuel-flow meter, OBD logger, GPS — all calibrated to traceable standards.
- [ ] **Statistical acceptance:** Coefficient of variation of repeated runs must be < 3 % for acceptance.
- [ ] **Indian standards to remember:** IS 14654 (performance), AIS-051 (brakes), AIS-053 (emissions), AIS-052 (hydrogen vehicles).
- [ ] **International standards to remember:** SAE J1346, SAE J2188, ISO 362-1, ECE R13/R15/R117.
- [ ] **Run-in mileage before test:** ≥ 1500 km.
- [ ] **Wind component limit:** perpendicular < 3 m/s during outdoor tests.
- [ ] **Gradeability conversion:** % grade = $\tan\theta \times 100 \approx \sin\theta \times 100$ for small angles.
- [ ] **Tractive force on incline:** $F_t = F_R + m g \sin\theta$ at steady speed.
- [ ] **Pre-test fuel level:** 90 % of tank capacity for whole-vehicle tests.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human Testing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Human Testing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Human Testing** in vehicle performance engineering refers to a category of subjective and objective evaluation procedures wherein trained human subjects (drivers, evaluators, or test engineers) interact with the vehicle to assess its dynamic performance, comfort, ergonomics, and overall driving quality. It encompasses the systematic measurement and rating of human physiological and psychological responses to vehicle-induced stimuli such as vibration, noise, acceleration, and handling demands.

According to KTU 2024 PEMUT633 Module 3, human testing is classified under **Subjective Vehicle Evaluation Methods** and includes ride comfort assessment, handling quality rating, NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) perceptual evaluation, driver fatigue analysis, and ergonomics testing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Distinction:** Human Testing ≠ Objective Instrumental Testing. While objective tests use sensors, accelerometers, and data loggers, human testing relies on **perceptual responses** of trained evaluators, often correlated with simultaneous instrument measurements (a technique called **objective–subjective correlation**).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Imagine tasting food at a restaurant. You can measure the exact sugar content (objective), temperature (objective), and acidity (objective) using laboratory equipment — but ultimately, your *enjoyment* of the meal is **subjective**. A Michelin-star chef may rate the same dish a "9/10" while an average diner rates it "6/10" because the chef has a **trained palate** (calibrated sensory system).

Human testing in vehicles works identically:
- **Objective data**: Accelerometer reading 0.35 g vertical acceleration at the driver's seat.
- **Subjective perception**: The same driver *feels* the ride as "comfortable" or "jarring."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is Human Testing Irreplaceable?** The ultimate "customer" of a vehicle is a human being. No matter how sophisticated the instrumentation, the final acceptance criterion is the **human perceptual response**. Therefore, OEM (Original Equipment Manufacturer) validation teams always include a human testing component in their vehicle development cycle.

### 1.3 Standardized Metrics and Constants

The following standard metrics are universally used in human vehicle testing:

- **ISO 2631** — Mechanical vibration and shock evaluation of human exposure.
- **SAE J1060** — Subjective rating scale for vehicle ride comfort.
- **VDV (Vibration Dose Value)** — Cumulative vibration exposure metric (m/s^1.75).
- **RMS acceleration (a_w)** — Weighted root-mean-square acceleration (m/s²).
- **CRF (Crew Rest Factor)** — Driver fatigue quantification.
- **Cooper-Harper Scale** — Standardized handling quality rating (1–10).
- **Borg CR-10 Scale** — Perceived exertion rating for driver workload.
- **Mean Opinion Score (MOS)** — 5-point rating scale for NVH assessment.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote the relevant **ISO/SAE standard number** alongside the metric in your answer. Examiners reward awareness of industry standards heavily.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Human Testing Methods

Human testing procedures are classified into the following categories based on what is being evaluated:

#### A. **Ride Comfort Testing**
The evaluation of vertical, longitudinal, and lateral vibrations transmitted to the driver/passenger through the seat, steering wheel, and floor.

**Procedure:**
1. Multiple test drivers drive the vehicle over standardized road profiles (smooth, rough, Belgian pave, cobblestone).
2. Drivers rate the comfort on a numerical scale (typically 1–10).
3. Simultaneously, accelerometers record vibration at the seat–occupant interface.
4. Data is statistically averaged across drivers and runs.

#### B. **Handling Quality Testing**
Evaluation of the vehicle's response to steering, braking, and lateral acceleration inputs in maneuvers like lane change, slalom, and steady-state cornering.

**Procedure:**
1. Drivers perform standardized maneuvers (e.g., ISO 3888 lane change test).
2. Drivers rate handling using the **Cooper-Harper Scale** or **NATOPS** scale.
3. Objective parameters (yaw rate, sideslip, lateral g) are recorded.
4. **Handling Quality Rating (HQR)** is derived from driver commentary.

#### C. **NVH Subjective Evaluation**
The perceived assessment of noise, vibration, and harshness characteristics.

**Procedure:**
1. Trained "golden ears" evaluators assess interior noise during cruising, acceleration, and braking.
2. Subjective ratings are paired with sound pressure level (SPL) measurements.
3. The **Mean Opinion Score (MOS)** methodology is applied.

#### D. **Driver Fatigue and Workload Analysis**
Assessment of cognitive and physical load on the driver during prolonged driving.

**Procedure:**
1. Heart rate variability (HRV), EEG, or eye-tracking data is recorded.
2. Subjective workload is rated using **NASA-TLX** (Task Load Index) or **Borg CR-10**.
3. Driving duration until subjective fatigue onset is noted.

#### E. **Ergonomics and Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Testing**
Evaluation of reach, visibility, control force, and seat comfort.

**Procedure:**
1. Anthropometric data of test population is collected.
2. Standard tasks (e.g., reaching all controls, mirror adjustment) are timed.
3. **RULA (Rapid Upper Limb Assessment)** and **REBA** scores are computed.

### 2.2 Why "Trained" Evaluators?

The credibility of human testing hinges on the **calibration** of evaluators. KTU expects students to know that:

- **Naive raters** introduce bias and high variance.
- **Trained raters** undergo **anchoring sessions** where reference stimuli are presented to stabilize internal scales.
- **Inter-rater reliability** is quantified using **Cohen's Kappa coefficient (κ)**.
- A κ value > **0.7** indicates acceptable agreement between raters.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In automotive OEM practice, "golden ears" for NVH evaluation undergo **at least 200 hours of structured training** before being certified to rate production vehicles. This ensures low inter-rater variance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Weighted RMS Acceleration | $a_w = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T a_w^2(t) \, dt}$ | m/s² | Ride comfort, ISO 2631-1 |
| 2 | Vibration Dose Value (VDV) | $VDV = \left[ \int_0^T a_w^4(t) \, dt \right]^{1/4}$ | m/s^1.75 | Transient/shock vibration |
| 3 | Crest Factor | $CF = \frac{a_{peak}}{a_{RMS}}$ | dimensionless | Distinguishes shock from continuous |
| 4 | Run Mean Square (RMS) | $a_{RMS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T a^2(t) \, dt}$ | m/s² | Steady-state vibration |
| 5 | Mean Opinion Score (MOS) | $MOS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$ | dimensionless (1–5) | NVH subjective rating |
| 6 | Cohen's Kappa (Inter-rater) | $\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$ | dimensionless (0–1) | Rater agreement; $\kappa > 0.7$ acceptable |
| 7 | Cooper-Harper Rating | $CHR$ = 1 (excellent) to 10 (worst) | dimensionless | Handling quality |
| 8 | NASA-TLX Score | $TLX = \sum_{i=1}^{6} w_i \cdot r_i$ | 0–100 | Driver workload |
| 9 | Likert Mean Score | $\bar{L} = \frac{\sum f_i \cdot x_i}{\sum f_i}$ | dimensionless | Discrete rating aggregation |
| 10 | Standard Error of Mean | $SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{n}}$ | same as variable | Confidence in subjective mean |
| 11 | Statistical Power | $1 - \beta = P(\text{reject } H_0 \mid H_1 \text{ true})$ | dimensionless | Sample size for raters |
| 12 | Crew Rest Factor (CRF) | $CRF = \frac{T_{rest}}{T_{drive}}$ | dimensionless | Fatigue ratio |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **VDV** (fourth-power integral, transient) with **RMS** (second-power, steady-state). The **fourth power** in VDV makes it highly sensitive to peaks and shocks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Vehicle Development:** Human testing validates that objective chassis tuning translates to perceived comfort before launch.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** ISO 2631-1 vibration exposure limits must be satisfied for occupational health.
- **Comparative Benchmarking:** Competing vehicles are rated on the same scale for market positioning.
- **Active Suspension Calibration:** Subjective feedback is used to fine-tune Skyhook/Groundhook logic.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Acceptance:** Public trust in autonomous systems is fundamentally a *human acceptance* metric.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Weighted RMS Acceleration (a_w) from ISO 2631-1

The **Weighted RMS acceleration** is the fundamental metric for human vibration exposure. It is derived from the raw acceleration signal as follows:

**Step 1: Acquire raw acceleration time history.**

Let $a(t)$ be the instantaneous acceleration recorded at the seat–occupant interface (typically measured at the seat pan using a tri-axial accelerometer). The signal is acquired over observation time $T$.

**Step 2: Apply frequency-weighting filters.**

ISO 2631-1 defines frequency-weighting factors $W_k$ for each axis (vertical: $W_z$, fore-aft: $W_x$, lateral: $W_y$) to account for human body sensitivity. The weighted signal is:

$$a_w(t) = W_k \cdot a(t)$$

where $W_k$ is implemented as a digital filter (e.g., Butterworth band-pass 0.5–80 Hz for $W_z$).

**Step 3: Square the weighted signal.**

$$a_w^2(t) = [W_k \cdot a(t)]^2$$

This step ensures that positive and negative accelerations contribute equally to the energy metric.

**Step 4: Integrate over the observation period.**

The time-averaged squared acceleration is:

$$\overline{a_w^2} = \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T a_w^2(t) \, dt$$

**Step 5: Take the square root.**

$$a_w = \sqrt{\overline{a_w^2}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T a_w^2(t) \, dt}$$

**Step 6: Apply the vector sum (for multi-axis).**

For whole-body vibration with components along all three axes, the total weighted RMS is:

$$a_{wv} = \sqrt{(1.4 \cdot a_{wx})^2 + (1.4 \cdot a_{wy})^2 + a_{wz}^2}$$

where the factor **1.4** accounts for the higher sensitivity of humans to lateral and fore-aft vibration relative to vertical.

**Final Expression (Vector Sum Form):**

$$\boxed{a_{wv} = \sqrt{1.96 \, a_{wx}^2 + 1.96 \, a_{wy}^2 + a_{wz}^2}}$$

This $a_{wv}$ value is then compared to ISO 2631 health guidance caution zones:
- $a_{wv} < 0.315$ m/s² → **No health effect expected**
- $0.315 \le a_{wv} < 0.63$ m/s² → **Caution zone**
- $a_{wv} \ge 0.63$ m/s² → **Health risk zone**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the 1.4 multiplier?** Empirical biodynamic studies (Griffin, 1990) showed that fore-aft and lateral vibrations are perceived ~1.4× more strongly than vertical at the same magnitude, hence the axial equivalence factor.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Vibration Dose Value (VDV)

The VDV is the **fourth-power moment** of the weighted acceleration, making it especially sensitive to transient peaks. It is derived as follows:

**Step 1: Recognize the limitation of RMS for transients.**

RMS treats all accelerations equally over time. However, occasional shocks (e.g., pothole impacts) cause disproportionate discomfort. VDV resolves this by using the **fourth power** instead of the second power.

**Step 2: Formulate the fourth-power integral.**

$$\int_0^T a_w^4(t) \, dt$$

The fourth power amplifies large transients 16× relative to small values (since $2^4 / 1^4 = 16$).

**Step 3: Normalize using the fourth root.**

The fourth root is applied so that VDV has the same dimensional units as $a_w$ raised to 0.75:

$$VDV = \left[ \int_0^T a_w^4(t) \, dt \right]^{1/4}$$

**Dimensional Check:**

$$[VDV] = \left[ (m/s^2)^4 \cdot s \right]^{1/4} = m \cdot s^{-1.75}$$

So VDV has units of **m/s^1.75**.

**Step 4: Relationship to RMS for stationary signals.**

For a stationary (constant RMS) signal of duration $T$:

$$VDV = a_{w,RMS} \cdot T^{1/4}$$

This shows that for the same RMS, longer exposure yields a higher VDV — capturing the **time-dependence** of vibration dose.

**Numerical Example (Worked):**

A driver experiences a 2-hour commute (T = 7200 s) with $a_w = 0.4$ m/s² RMS.

$$VDV = 0.4 \times 7200^{0.25}$$

Calculate: $7200^{0.25} = (7200)^{1/4}$

$$7200^{1/4} = \sqrt{\sqrt{7200}} = \sqrt{84.85} \approx 9.21$$

Therefore:

$$VDV \approx 0.4 \times 9.21 = 3.68 \text{ m/s}^{1.75}$$

Compare to ISO 2631 VDV health guidance:
- VDV < 9.1 m/s^1.75 → Low health risk
- 9.1 ≤ VDV < 21 m/s^1.75 → Moderate risk
- VDV ≥ 21 m/s^1.75 → High risk

Our result (3.68) indicates **low health risk**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Cohen's Kappa for Rater Agreement

When multiple human evaluators rate the same vehicle, the agreement between them must be quantified. Simple percent agreement is misleading because it includes chance agreement. Cohen's Kappa corrects for this.

**Step 1: Observed agreement.**

$$p_o = \frac{\text{Number of agreements}}{\text{Total ratings}}$$

**Step 2: Expected agreement by chance.**

For a 2-rater scenario with $k$ categories:

$$p_e = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_{i,1} \cdot p_{i,2}$$

where $p_{i,1}$ is the proportion of ratings in category $i$ by Rater 1, and similarly for Rater 2.

**Step 3: Compute Kappa.**

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e}$$

**Numerical Example:**

Two NVH evaluators rate 50 vehicles on a 3-point scale (Quiet / Moderate / Loud).

| Rater 1 ↓ \ Rater 2 → | Quiet | Moderate | Loud | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Quiet | 20 | 5 | 0 | 25 |
| Moderate | 3 | 12 | 2 | 17 |
| Loud | 1 | 2 | 5 | 8 |
| **Total** | 24 | 19 | 7 | 50 |

**Step 3a: Observed agreement.**

Agreements (diagonal): 20 + 12 + 5 = 37

$$p_o = 37/50 = 0.74$$

**Step 3b: Expected agreement.**

$$p_e = \frac{25}{50} \cdot \frac{24}{50} + \frac{17}{50} \cdot \frac{19}{50} + \frac{8}{50} \cdot \frac{7}{50}$$

$$p_e = 0.5 \cdot 0.48 + 0.34 \cdot 0.38 + 0.16 \cdot 0.14$$

$$p_e = 0.24 + 0.1292 + 0.0224 = 0.3916$$

**Step 3c: Kappa.**

$$\kappa = \frac{0.74 - 0.3916}{1 - 0.3916} = \frac{0.3484}{0.6084} \approx 0.573$$

**Interpretation:** $\kappa = 0.573$ indicates **moderate agreement** (Landis & Koch scale: 0.41–0.60 = moderate). This is below the 0.7 threshold, suggesting the raters need additional calibration training.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of Mean Opinion Score (MOS) Confidence Interval

The MOS is a subjective rating. To report it with statistical confidence, the **standard error of the mean (SE)** is used.

**Step 1: Compute mean MOS.**

$$MOS = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} R_i$$

**Step 2: Compute standard deviation.**

$$\sigma = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (R_i - MOS)^2}$$

**Step 3: Standard error.**

$$SE = \frac{\sigma}{\sqrt{N}}$$

**Step 4: 95% Confidence Interval (assuming normal distribution).**

$$CI_{95\%} = MOS \pm 1.96 \cdot SE$$

**Worked Example:**

10 drivers rate interior noise on a 1–5 scale. Ratings: 4, 5, 3, 4, 4, 5, 3, 4, 4, 5.

Mean:

$$MOS = \frac{4+5+3+4+4+5+3+4+4+5}{10} = \frac{41}{10} = 4.1$$

Variance:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{\sum (R_i - 4.1)^2}{9}$$

Compute deviations: -0.1, 0.9, -1.1, -0.1, -0.1, 0.9, -1.1, -0.1, -0.1, 0.9
Squared: 0.01, 0.81, 1.21, 0.01, 0.01, 0.81, 1.21, 0.01, 0.01, 0.81
Sum: 4.90

$$\sigma^2 = 4.90 / 9 = 0.544 \Rightarrow \sigma = 0.738$$

Standard error:

$$SE = 0.738 / \sqrt{10} = 0.738 / 3.162 = 0.233$$

95% CI:

$$CI_{95\%} = 4.1 \pm 1.96 \cdot 0.233 = 4.1 \pm 0.457$$

So the MOS is reported as **4.1 ± 0.46 at 95% confidence**.

> [!TIP]
> **For 95% CI, the multiplier is 1.96. For 99% CI, it is 2.576. For 90% CI, it is 1.645.** KTU examiners frequently ask this.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix: When to Use Which Method

| Scenario | Recommended Human Test | Key Metric | ISO/SAE Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Long-distance bus ride comfort | Whole-body vibration, ride comfort | VDV, $a_{wv}$ | ISO 2631-1 |
| Sports car handling on track | Handling quality evaluation | Cooper-Harper Rating | SAE J2180 |
| EV interior quietness at highway | NVH subjective + objective | MOS, SPL (dBA) | ISO 5128 |
| Truck driver fatigue on long haul | Workload + physiological | NASA-TLX, HRV | ISO 10075 |
| Seat comfort for office workers | Localized seat vibration | Seat Effective Amplitude Transmissibility (SEAT) | ISO 2631-1 |
| Off-road military vehicle | Shock exposure | VDV, SED (Shock Exposure Duration) | ISO 2631-5 |
| Ergonomic control reach | Anthropometric analysis | RULA, REBA | ISO 9241 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Human Testing Workflow (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Define Test Objective] --> B0[Select Test Standard ISO/SAE]
    B0 --> C0[Recruit Trained Evaluators]
    C0 --> D0[Anchor Calibration Session]
    D0 --> E0[Define Test Matrix Road Profile Maneuver Speed]
    E0 --> F0[Mount Instrumentation Accelerometers Microphones GPS]
    F0 --> G0[Conduct Test Runs Multiple Drivers Multiple Repetitions]
    G0 --> H0[Record Subjective Ratings Likert MOS Cooper-Harper]
    H0 --> I0[Record Objective Data Vibration SPL Yaw Rate]
    I0 --> J0[Statistical Analysis Mean SE CI Kappa]
    J0 --> K0[Objective Subjective Correlation Pearson r]
    K0 --> L0[Final Vehicle Performance Report]
    L0 --> M0[Acceptance Decision Pass Fail Revise]

    style A0 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style B0 fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style C0 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style D0 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style E0 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style F0 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G0 fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style H0 fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style I0 fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style J0 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style K0 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style L0 fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style M0 fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.2 Classification Tree of Human Testing Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    HT[Human Testing] --> RC[Ride Comfort]
    HT --> HQ[Handling Quality]
    HT --> NV[NVH Evaluation]
    HT --> DF[Driver Fatigue]
    HT --> ER[Ergonomics HMI]

    RC --> RC1[Whole Body Vibration]
    RC --> RC2[Local Seat Vibration]
    RC --> RC3[Postural Stability]

    HQ --> HQ1[Steering Response]
    HQ --> HQ2[Braking Response]
    HQ --> HQ3[Lane Change Maneuver]
    HQ --> HQ4[Slalom Test]

    NV --> NV1[Engine Noise]
    NV --> NV2[Wind Noise]
    NV --> NV3[Tire Road Noise]
    NV --> NV4[NVH Buzz Squeak Rattle BSR]

    DF --> DF1[Heart Rate Variability]
    DF --> DF2[EEG Brain Activity]
    DF --> DF3[Eye Tracking]
    DF --> DF4[Subjective Workload NASA TLX]

    ER --> ER1[Control Reach]
    ER --> ER2[Visibility Analysis]
    ER --> ER3[Seat Comfort]
    ER --> ER4[Display Readability]

    style HT fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style RC fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style HQ fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style NV fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style DF fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style ER fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.3 ISO 2631 Vibration Health Zones (Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Driver Exposure] --> B[Weighted RMS aw]
    A --> C[VDV]
    A --> D[Crest Factor CF]

    B --> E{Aw Value}
    E -->|Below 0.315 m/s2| F[Comfortable Safe]
    E -->|0.315 to 0.63| G[Caution Fatigue Possible]
    E -->|Above 0.63| H[Health Risk Zone]

    C --> I{VDV Value}
    I -->|Below 9.1| J[Low Risk]
    I -->|9.1 to 21| K[Moderate Risk]
    I -->|Above 21| L[High Risk Reduce Exposure]

    D --> M{CF Value}
    M -->|Below 9| N[Stationary Vibration Use RMS]
    M -->|Above 9| O[Transient Shocks Use VDV]

    style A fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style J fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style K fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style L fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style O fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

### 4.4 Subjective–Objective Correlation Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OBJ[Objective Measurement] --> ACC[Accelerometer]
    OBJ --> MIC[Microphone SPL]
    OBJ --> GPS[GPS Speed Position]
    OBJ --> EYE[Eye Tracker]

    SUB[Subjective Rating] --> LIK[Likert 1 to 10]
    SUB --> MOS[MOS 1 to 5]
    SUB --> CH[Cooper Harper 1 to 10]

    ACC --> CORR[Correlation Engine Pearson r Kendall tau]
    MIC --> CORR
    GPS --> CORR
    EYE --> CORR

    LIK --> CORR
    MOS --> CORR
    CH --> CORR

    CORR --> OUT{Decision}
    OUT -->|r above 0.7| PASS[Valid Model Objective Predicts Subjective]
    OUT -->|r below 0.7| FAIL[Reassess Rating Scale or Re Calibrate Raters]

    style OBJ fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style SUB fill:#a16207,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style CORR fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style PASS fill:#14532d,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
    style FAIL fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000,color:#ffffff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Vibration Dose Value (VDV)" and explain why it is preferred over RMS acceleration for evaluating transient vibration exposure in human testing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)** is defined as the fourth root of the integral of the fourth power of the frequency-weighted acceleration over the exposure period:

$$VDV = \left[ \int_0^T a_w^4(t) \, dt \right]^{1/4}$$

It has units of **m/s^1.75**. VDV is preferred over RMS for transient/shock exposure because the **fourth power** weighting amplifies the contribution of peaks and shocks disproportionately, making it more sensitive to occasional high-amplitude events (such as pothole impacts) that RMS tends to mask. For a stationary signal, VDV = $a_{w,RMS} \cdot T^{1/4}$, showing that VDV also captures cumulative time-dependence of vibration dose, which RMS does not.

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1) + Formula (1) + Justification (1)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the Cooper-Harper Scale? How is it used in vehicle handling evaluation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Cooper-Harper Scale** is a standardized 10-point subjective rating scale developed for evaluating aircraft handling qualities and later adapted for vehicle handling evaluation. The scale ranges from **1 (excellent — highly desirable, no improvement needed)** to **10 (worst — vehicle is uncontrollable/improvement mandatory)**.

In vehicle handling evaluation, trained drivers perform standardized maneuvers (e.g., lane change, slalom, steady-state cornering) and rate the vehicle's controllability, precision, and workload. The rating is given in a **decision-tree** manner: first, the driver determines whether the desired performance is achievable; second, what level of workload is required; third, whether compensation is needed.

The Cooper-Harper Rating (CHR) is correlated with objective metrics like **sideslip angle, yaw rate response time, and steering sensitivity** to develop handling quality criteria.

> **Marking Scheme:** Scale definition (1) + Range/explanation (1) + Application (1)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the methodology of **subjective ride comfort evaluation** in vehicles. Discuss the role of ISO 2631-1 in this context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vehicle is tested for ride comfort over a 30-minute drive. The frequency-weighted vertical RMS acceleration measured at the seat pan is 0.5 m/s² and the fore-aft weighted RMS is 0.3 m/s². Calculate:
(i) The total weighted vibration total value $a_{wv}$.
(ii) The Vibration Dose Value (VDV), assuming a stationary signal.
(iii) Comment on the health risk using ISO 2631-1 zones. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Subjective Ride Comfort Evaluation Methodology:**

1. **Test Setup:** A panel of trained evaluators (typically 5–10) is selected. They undergo calibration using reference stimuli.

2. **Test Conditions:** The vehicle is driven over standardized road profiles:
   - Smooth asphalt
   - Washboard
   - Belgian pave
   - Cobblestone
   - Random rough road

3. **Speed Protocol:** Multiple speeds (e.g., 40, 60, 80, 100 km/h) are tested.

4. **Subjective Rating:** Each evaluator rates the ride comfort on a numerical scale (commonly **1 = very uncomfortable to 10 = very comfortable**, or SAE J1060 1–10 scale).

5. **Instrumentation:** Tri-axial accelerometers are placed at:
   - Seat pan (buttock interface)
   - Seat back
   - Floor
   - Steering wheel

6. **Data Reduction:** ISO 2631-1 frequency weightings are applied. The weighted RMS acceleration $a_w$ in each axis is computed and combined using the vector sum formula:

$$a_{wv} = \sqrt{(1.4 a_{wx})^2 + (1.4 a_{wy})^2 + a_{wz}^2}$$

7. **Correlation:** The subjective ratings are correlated with $a_{wv}$ and VDV using **Pearson's correlation coefficient (r)**. A high correlation (r > 0.7) validates the model.

8. **Health Assessment:** ISO 2631-1 health guidance caution zones are used to flag potential health risks for the driver.

> **Marking Scheme:** Methodology steps (5) + Role of ISO 2631 (2)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $a_{wz} = 0.5$ m/s² (vertical)
- $a_{wx} = 0.3$ m/s² (fore-aft, assume lateral $a_{wy} = 0$)
- $T = 30$ min = 1800 s

**(i) Total weighted vibration total value $a_{wv}$:**

$$a_{wv} = \sqrt{(1.4 \cdot 0.3)^2 + 0^2 + (0.5)^2}$$

$$a_{wv} = \sqrt{(0.42)^2 + (0.5)^2}$$

$$a_{wv} = \sqrt{0.1764 + 0.25}$$

$$a_{wv} = \sqrt{0.4264}$$

$$\boxed{a_{wv} \approx 0.653 \text{ m/s}^2}$$

**(ii) Vibration Dose Value (VDV) for stationary signal:**

$$VDV = a_w \cdot T^{1/4}$$

For combined axis, use $a_{wv}$:

$$VDV = 0.653 \times 1800^{0.25}$$

Compute $1800^{0.25}$:

$$1800^{0.5} = 42.43$$

$$1800^{0.25} = \sqrt{42.43} \approx 6.514$$

$$VDV = 0.653 \times 6.514 \approx 4.254 \text{ m/s}^{1.75}$$

$$\boxed{VDV \approx 4.25 \text{ m/s}^{1.75}}$$

**(iii) Health risk assessment (ISO 2631-1):**

For $a_{wv} = 0.653$ m/s²:
- ISO zones: < 0.315 = safe, 0.315–0.63 = caution, > 0.63 = health risk.
- 0.653 m/s² falls **slightly into the health risk zone** ($a_{wv} > 0.63$ m/s²).

For VDV = 4.25 m/s^1.75:
- ISO VDV zones: < 9.1 = low risk, 9.1–21 = moderate, > 21 = high.
- 4.25 m/s^1.75 is in the **low health risk** category.

**Conclusion:** While the RMS-based metric marginally exceeds the caution threshold, the VDV indicates low cumulative dose. The vehicle is acceptable for short-duration use but should be re-evaluated for prolonged driving conditions. **Recommend suspension retuning** to bring $a_{wv}$ below 0.5 m/s².

> **Marking Scheme:** (i) Formula + substitution + answer (3) | (ii) Formula + calculation + answer (2) | (iii) Zone identification + commentary (2)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Module 3]**

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **complete workflow of human testing** in vehicle performance evaluation. List at least three ISO/SAE standards used in this process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two expert NVH evaluators independently rate 100 vehicles on a 4-point interior noise scale (1 = Very Quiet, 2 = Quiet, 3 = Noisy, 4 = Very Noisy). The agreement matrix is as follows:

| Rater 1 ↓ / Rater 2 → | Very Quiet | Quiet | Noisy | Very Noisy | Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Very Quiet | 30 | 5 | 1 | 0 | 36 |
| Quiet | 4 | 25 | 6 | 1 | 36 |
| Noisy | 0 | 5 | 18 | 3 | 26 |
| Very Noisy | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 2 |
| **Total** | 34 | 35 | 26 | 5 | 100 |

Calculate **Cohen's Kappa** and interpret the rater agreement. Is further calibration training required? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

*(Flowchart has been provided in Section 4.1 — refer to the diagram.)*

**Three ISO/SAE standards used in human testing:**

1. **ISO 2631-1** — Mechanical vibration and shock: Evaluation of human exposure to whole-body vibration. Defines the frequency weighting $W_k$, the vector sum formula, and health guidance caution zones.

2. **SAE J1060** — Subjective rating scale for vehicle ride comfort and handling. Provides a standardized 1–10 scale with verbal anchors.

3. **ISO 5128** — Acoustics — Measurement of noise inside motor vehicles. Specifies microphone positions, speed conditions, and SPL measurement protocols for subjective–objective correlation.

4. **(Optional) ISO 10075** — Ergonomic principles related to mental workload. Defines terms and procedures for assessing driver cognitive load.

> **Marking Scheme:** Flowchart narrative (3) + List of 3 standards with purpose (3) + ISO/SAE number correctness (1)

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1: Observed agreement $p_o$:**

Sum of diagonal entries:

$$p_o = \frac{30 + 25 + 18 + 1}{100} = \frac{74}{100} = 0.74$$

**Step 2: Expected agreement $p_e$:**

Compute marginal proportions:

- Rater 1: 36/100, 36/100, 26/100, 2/100
- Rater 2: 34/100, 35/100, 26/100, 5/100

$$p_e = (0.36 \cdot 0.34) + (0.36 \cdot 0.35) + (0.26 \cdot 0.26) + (0.02 \cdot 0.05)$$

$$p_e = 0.1224 + 0.1260 + 0.0676 + 0.0010$$

$$p_e = 0.3170$$

**Step 3: Cohen's Kappa:**

$$\kappa = \frac{p_o - p_e}{1 - p_e} = \frac{0.74 - 0.3170}{1 - 0.3170} = \frac{0.4230}{0.6830}$$

$$\boxed{\kappa \approx 0.619}$$

**Step 4: Interpretation (Landis & Koch scale):**

| $\kappa$ Range | Strength of Agreement |
|---|---|
| < 0.00 | Poor |
| 0.00 – 0.20 | Slight |
| 0.21 – 0.40 | Fair |
| 0.41 – 0.60 | Moderate |
| 0.61 – 0.80 | **Substantial** ← Our case |
| 0.81 – 1.00 | Almost Perfect |

Our $\kappa = 0.619$ indicates **substantial agreement**.

**Step 5: Decision on calibration training:**

The threshold for acceptable inter-rater reliability in OEM practice is generally **$\kappa \ge 0.70$**. Since $0.619 < 0.70$:

- **YES**, additional calibration training is recommended.
- Specific focus should be on the **"Noisy" vs "Very Noisy"** boundary (3 disagreements) and **"Quiet" vs "Noisy"** boundary (6 + 5 = 11 disagreements) — these are the largest sources of disagreement.
- Anchoring sessions using reference audio stimuli should be conducted to refine the internal scale of both raters.

> **Marking Scheme:** (1) $p_o$ computation (2) | (2) $p_e$ computation (2) | (3) Kappa calculation (1) | (4) Interpretation + decision (2)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (and where marks are lost):**
>
> 1. **Confusing VDV units** — VDV is **m/s^1.75**, NOT m/s². Writing the wrong unit costs 1 mark.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the 1.4 axial multiplier** in the $a_{wv}$ vector sum formula. This factor is mandatory per ISO 2631-1.
>
> 3. **Using Cohen's Kappa without subtracting chance agreement** — Students often report $p_o$ as the "agreement" and skip Kappa. Kappa is required for chance-corrected agreement.
>
> 4. **Mixing up Cooper-Harper scale direction** — Lower number = better handling, higher = worse. Reverse interpretation is a common error.
>
> 5. **Omitting ISO/SAE standard numbers** — Always cite the standard (e.g., "ISO 2631-1", "SAE J1060") in your answer. Examiners deduct marks for vague references.
>
> 6. **Skipping the "Stationary vs Transient" justification** for choosing RMS vs VDV. Always state *why* one metric is preferred.
>
> 7. **Ignoring the time-dependence of VDV** — Show the $T^{1/4}$ relationship explicitly for stationary signals.
>
> 8. **Not mentioning the Landis & Koch interpretation scale** for Cohen's Kappa — a bare number without interpretation loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Human testing** = subjective + objective evaluation of vehicle performance using trained human evaluators.
- **ISO 2631-1** is the cornerstone standard for whole-body vibration.
- **Weighted RMS acceleration** $a_w$ — used for steady-state vibration. Formula: $a_w = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T} \int_0^T a_w^2(t)\, dt}$.
- **VDV** = $\left[ \int_0^T a_w^4(t) \, dt \right]^{1/4}$, units = **m/s^1.75**. Sensitive to transients.
- **Vector sum formula** (multi-axis): $a_{wv} = \sqrt{(1.4 a_{wx})^2 + (1.4 a_{wy})^2 + a_{wz}^2}$.
- **Cooper-Harper Scale** = 1 (best) to 10 (worst) for handling.
- **MOS** = 1 to 5 scale for NVH subjective rating.
- **NASA-TLX** = 0 to 100 for driver workload.
- **Crest Factor** = $a_{peak} / a_{RMS}$. If CF > 9, use VDV instead of RMS.
- **Cohen's Kappa** $\kappa > 0.7$ = acceptable inter-rater agreement; $\kappa = 0.41$–$0.60$ = moderate.
- **Landis & Koch interpretation** for $\kappa$: < 0 poor, 0.0–0.2 slight, 0.21–0.4 fair, 0.41–0.6 moderate, 0.61–0.8 substantial, 0.81–1.0 almost perfect.
- **Standard error** $SE = \sigma / \sqrt{N}$; **95% CI** uses multiplier **1.96**.
- **Health risk zones** for $a_{wv}$: < 0.315 m/s² safe, 0.315–0.63 caution, > 0.63 risk.
- **VDV health zones**: < 9.1 low, 9.1–21 moderate, > 21 high.
- **Trained evaluators** are essential — naive raters introduce high variance and bias.
- **Pearson correlation** r > 0.7 validates objective–subjective model.
- **Anthropometric data** must be representative of the target population for ergonomics tests.
- **Inter-rater reliability** is mandatory statistical validation in any human testing protocol.
- **Repeatability** = same driver, same vehicle, same conditions, multiple runs.
- **Reproducibility** = different drivers, same vehicle, different days.
- **Golden ears** for NVH undergo ≥ 200 hours of training before certification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line summary for KTU exam:** Human Testing in vehicles combines trained evaluator perception ratings (subjective) with instrumented measurements (objective) to assess ride comfort, handling, NVH, and ergonomics, with the **ISO 2631-1 weighted RMS / VDV** framework serving as the quantitative backbone and **statistical methods** (Cohen's Kappa, Pearson correlation) ensuring reliability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dummies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dummies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a "Dummy" in Vehicle Testing?

In the context of automotive safety engineering, a **Dummy** refers to an **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** — a high-precision, instrumented mechanical surrogate designed to mimic the human body's geometry, mass distribution, joint articulation, and dynamic response under crash-loading conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (PEMUT633):** An *Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)* is a biofidelic manikin equipped with embedded transducers (accelerometers, load cells, angular rate sensors, potentiometers) used to quantify occupant kinematics and injury potential during controlled vehicle crash tests mandated by safety regulations such as FMVSS, Euro NCAP, ECE, and AIS.

**Key Purpose:** Dummies are the **"instrumented eyewitnesses"** of a crash test. They do not prevent injury — they **measure** the injury potential of a real human occupant in a real-world collision.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Crash-Avatar

Imagine a **stunt double** in a Hollywood action film. The stunt double (Dummy) steps in to perform the dangerous scene (crash) so the actor (a real human) doesn't get hurt.
