However, this stunt double is not just a passive dummy — it wears dozens of **micro-sensors** on its head, chest, and legs. After the scene (crash test), the sensors' data is downloaded to give engineers a precise picture of how badly a real person would have been injured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Anthropomorphic"?** Derived from Greek — *ánthrōpos* (human) + *morphē* (form) = "human-shaped." The dummy is shaped, weighted, and jointed like a real human so that its dynamic response during a crash matches that of a human occupant as closely as engineering allows.

---

## 1.3 Why Are Dummies Used Instead of Human Cadavers or Volunteers?

| Reason | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Ethical / Legal** | Human crash testing is universally banned. Dummies are the only legally and ethically acceptable surrogate. |
| **Repeatability** | Same dummy can be crashed thousands of times under identical conditions — cadavers vary. |
| **Sensorability** | Internally instrumented at locations (brain, chest, spine) where live data from a human is impossible. |
| **Cost-Effectiveness** | Cadaveric and volunteer testing (when historically allowed) is enormously expensive and logistically impossible. |
| **Standardization** | Dummies are calibrated to a **traceable standard** (per SAE J211 / ISO 6487), enabling global comparison of crash test results. |

---

## 1.4 Brief Historical Evolution

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical Timeline (ATD Evolution):**
> - **1949** — Sierra Sam (U.S. Air Force, first anthropomorphic dummy)
> - **1970s** — **Hybrid II** family introduced
> - **1976** — **Hybrid III** introduced → became the global *de facto* frontal-impact standard
> - **1980s** — **SID** (Side Impact Dummy) and **EuroSID** developed
> - **1990s** — **BioRID** (rear impact/whiplash)
> - **2000s** — **THOR** (advanced frontal), **WorldSID** (global side-impact harmonization)
> - **2010s+** — **THOR-5F** (5th percentile female), **THOR-Modernization**, **OSLO** Posture
> - **India** — AIS-016 mandates dummies for frontal and side tests

---

## 1.5 Why Dummies Matter for KTU Examinations

This topic directly maps to the following **KTU Course Outcomes (COs)**:

| CO Code | Course Outcome (Typical for PEMUT633) |
|---|---|
| **CO3** | Evaluate vehicle crashworthiness using standard testing protocols and occupant injury metrics. |
| **CO4** | Apply regulatory frameworks (FMVSS, Euro NCAP, AIS) to assess vehicle safety performance. |

The "Dummies" topic is the **bridge between crash mechanics (Module 3 Part 1)** and **injury biomechanics (Module 3 Part 3)** — without the ATD, no injury criterion can be measured or validated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Crash Test Dummy

A modern ATD is composed of four major subsystems:

1. **Skeletal Structure** — Steel/aluminum vertebrae, rib cage, pelvis, femur, tibia, fibula.
2. **Skin/Envelop** — Neoprene, vinyl, or polymer elastomer flesh to provide correct mass and surface friction.
3. **Joints** — Multi-axis mechanical joints (ball-and-socket, sliding, hinged) with calibrated stiffness and damping.
4. **Instrumentation** — Embedded transducers at 30+ body locations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Biofidelity** is the measure of how closely the dummy's dynamic response matches that of a living human under identical loading. It is rated on a 0–10 scale (the "Biofidelity Ranking") published by the U.S. National Highway Traffic Safety Administration (NHTSA).

---

## 2.2 Classification of Dummies

### 2.2.1 By Anthropometric Size

| Size | Representative | Mass | Stature | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **50th Percentile Male (M50)** | Hybrid III 50M | **78 kg** | 1.75 m | Frontal NCAP tests |
| **95th Percentile Male (M95)** | Hybrid III 95M | 101 kg | 1.88 m | Belt-fit & airbag studies |
| **5th Percentile Female (F5)** | Hybrid III 5F | 49 kg | 1.51 m | Out-of-position airbag tests |
| **6-Year-Old Child** | Hybrid III 6YO | 23 kg | 1.17 m | Child-occupant protection |
| **3-Year-Old Child** | Hybrid III 3YO | 15 kg | 0.94 m | CRS (Child Restraint System) tests |
| **12-Month-Old Infant** | CRABI 12 | 10 kg | 0.73 m | Rearward-facing CRS tests |

### 2.2.2 By Impact Direction (Regulatory Application)

| Dummy Family | Standard Reference | Impact Direction | Regulatory Body |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hybrid III (HIII 50M)** | SAE J2858 / FMVSS 208 | Frontal | NHTSA, Euro NCAP, AIS |
| **THOR** | ISO/TS 22239 | Advanced Frontal | NHTSA (newer) |
| **WorldSID 50M / 5F** | ISO 15830 | Side | FMVSS 214, Euro NCAP, AIS-099 |
| **EuroSID-2 (ES-2)** | ISO 15830 (legacy) | Side | ECE R95 (legacy) |
| **BioRID II** | ISO 22696 | Rear / Whiplash | Euro NCAP Whiplash |
| **SID-IIs** | SAE J2721 | Side (small occupant) | FMVSS 214 |
| **CRABI 12** | FMVSS 213 | Frontal (Child) | Child restraint |

---

## 2.3 Instrumentation Architecture

> [!NOTE]
> The instrumentation suite inside a dummy converts mechanical motion into electrical signals. Without these sensors, the dummy is a "passive" manikin and useless for injury evaluation.

| Sensor Type | Measurement | Typical Location | Quantity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Tri-axial Accelerometer** | Linear acceleration (g) | Head CG, Chest, Pelvis, Femurs, Tibias | 12–20 |
| **Angular Rate Sensor (ARS)** | Rotational velocity (rad/s) | Head, Thorax | 4–6 |
| **Load Cell** | Axial/torsional force (N) | Neck (upper/lower), Femur, Lumbar | 6–10 |
| **Potentiometer** | Angular displacement (deg) | Knee, Ankle, Elbow, Shoulder | 8–12 |
| **Chest Deflection Pot** | Sternum compression (mm) | Sternum | 1 |
| **Pressure Sensor** | Internal bladder pressure (kPa) | Abdomen | 1–2 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Acquisition Standard (SAE J211):** All dummy channels are filtered using a **CFC (Channel Frequency Class)** filter.
> - **CFC 60** → Head acceleration (cut-off ≈ 1000 Hz)
> - **CFC 180** → Chest, pelvis, femur, neck forces (cut-off ≈ 300 Hz)
> - **CFC 1000** → High-frequency foot/ankle data

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **These five injury-criterion formulas are the most frequently asked derivations in KTU examinations. Memorize them with units.**

### 2.4.1 Head Injury Criterion (HIC)

$$HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ (t_2 - t_1) \left( \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$$

with the constraint:
$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 \le \begin{cases} 36 \text{ ms} & \Rightarrow HIC_{36} \\ 15 \text{ ms} & \Rightarrow HIC_{15} \end{cases}$$

where $a(t)$ is the resultant head-centre-of-gravity acceleration expressed in units of **g** (not m/s²), and the maximum value of HIC is capped at 1000 in regulations.

| Performance Level | $HIC_{15}$ Limit | Likely AIS Injury |
|---|---|---|
| No injury | < 240 | AIS 0 |
| Moderate | 240 – 700 | AIS 1–3 |
| Severe | 700 – 1000 | AIS 3–4 |
| Critical | > 1000 | AIS 5+ |

> [!NOTE]
> The exponent **2.5** comes from the Gadd Severity Index (GSI), from which HIC was derived. It is an empirical fit to cadaveric concussion and skull-fracture data.

### 2.4.2 Chest Severity Index (CSI)

$$CSI = \dfrac{A_{\max}}{A_{int}} + \dfrac{D_{\max}}{D_{int}}$$

where:
- $A_{\max}$ = peak chest acceleration (g) — limit $A_{int} = 60$ g
- $D_{\max}$ = peak chest deflection (mm) — limit $D_{int} = 76.2$ mm (3 inches)

**Limit:** $CSI \le 1.0$

### 2.4.3 Viscous Criterion (VC) — Soft-Tissue Thoracic Injury

$$VC = \max_{t} \left[ V(t) \cdot C(t) \right]$$

where $V(t)$ is the chest deflection velocity (m/s) and $C(t)$ is the chest compression (m).

**Limit:** $VC \le 1.0$ m/s

### 2.4.4 Neck Injury Criterion (Nij)

$$N_{ij} = \dfrac{F_{y}}{F_{y,c}} + \dfrac{M_{x,oc}}{M_{x,c}}$$

where $F_y$ = axial neck tension/compression force, $M_{x,oc}$ = occipital moment about the $x$-axis, and $F_{y,c}$, $M_{x,c}$ are critical intercept values from cadaveric tests (e.g., $F_{y,c} = 4500$ N, $M_{x,c} = 310$ Nm for tension-flexion).

**Limit:** $N_{ij} \le 1.0$

### 2.4.5 Femur Compressive Force

$$F_{femur}(t) \le F_{limit}$$

**Limit:** $F_{limit} = 10$ kN (FMVSS 208 / Euro NCAP)

---

## 2.5 Summary of All Injury Limits (KTU Quick-Recall)

| Body Region | Injury Metric | Symbol | Threshold (Limit) | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Head | Head Injury Criterion | $HIC_{15}$ | $\le 700$ | FMVSS 208 / Euro NCAP |
| Head | 3 ms cumulative | $a_{3ms}$ | $\le 80$ g | Euro NCAP |
| Neck | Neck injury index | $N_{ij}$ | $\le 1.0$ | Euro NCAP / FMVSS 208 |
| Chest | Chest acceleration | $a_{chest}$ | $\le 60$ g | FMVSS 208 |
| Chest | Chest deflection | $D_{chest}$ | $\le 50$ mm | Euro NCAP |
| Chest | Viscous Criterion | $VC$ | $\le 1.0$ m/s | Euro NCAP |
| Femur | Femur force | $F_{femur}$ | $\le 10$ kN | FMVSS 208 |
| Tibia | Tibia index (TCI) | $TI$ | $\le 1.3$ | FMVSS 208 |
| Abdomen | Abdominal pressure | $P_{abd}$ | $\le 2.1$ kPa (peak) | Euro NCAP |
| Foot/Ankle | Tibia axial force | $F_{tibia}$ | $\le 8$ kN | Euro NCAP |

---

## 2.6 Calibration — Why It Is Mandatory

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No crash-test data is legally valid unless the dummy is accompanied by a valid calibration certificate issued within 12 months prior to the test.**

Calibration tests (per ISO 6487 / SAE J1737) include:
- Head drop test
- Neck flexion/extension
- Thorax impact pendulum (high and low speed)
- Knee slider impact
- Lumbar spine flexion
- Foot pendulum impact

The **head drop test** is the most frequently asked:

$$h_{drop} = 376.1 \text{ mm}, \quad \text{pendulum angle} = 90^\circ, \quad \text{resultant peak} = 225 \text{ g to } 275 \text{ g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Real-world engineering utility: Dummy calibration data forms the **input file** for human-body finite-element models (THUMS, GHBMC), allowing OEMs to correlate physical test data with simulation predictions and reduce physical test count by up to 70%.

---

## 2.7 Why Dummy Selection Is a Critical Engineering Decision

| Test Type | Required Dummy | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Frontal NCAP (50 km/h full-width) | **HIII 50M** | Represents average adult male driver |
| Frontal NCAP (50 km/h 40% offset) | **HIII 50M** | Standard global protocol |
| Side NCAP (50 km/h mobile barrier) | **WorldSID 50M** | Modern biofidelic side-impact |
| Side NCAP (rear-seat occupant) | **SID-IIs 5F** | Smaller female body more vulnerable to side impact |
| Rear impact / Whiplash | **BioRID II** | Long spine, low mass, rear-impact specific |
| Child in CRS | **Q-Series (Q0, Q1, Q1.5, Q3, Q6, Q10)** | New biofidelic child family replacing Hybrid III |
| Far-side airbag | **THOR-5F** | Better spine/shoulder biofidelity |
| Pedestrian (PMHS validation) | Not used — cadaveric only | Dummies cannot model pedestrian biofidelity |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse "Hybrid II" and "Hybrid III." Hybrid II is **obsolete** (1970s) and used only for compliance verification of older FMVSS 208 belt-only tests. Hybrid III is the current global standard. Distinguish carefully in exams.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: HIC from the Gadd Severity Index (GSI)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The HIC is **derived** from the Gadd Severity Index, which was the first widely used head-injury metric. The KTU Board Examiner loves asking this derivation. We present it line-by-line.

### Step 1 — Start with the Gadd Severity Index (1966)

> G.S. Gadd proposed a single-number severity index based on the assumption that head injury is caused by a combination of high acceleration and long duration.

$$GSI = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ a(t) \right]^{2.5} \, dt$$

where:
- $a(t)$ = instantaneous head acceleration in units of **g** (not m/s²)
- $T$ = total pulse duration
- The exponent 2.5 was an empirical fit to Wayne State University cadaveric concussion data.

### Step 2 — Limitations of GSI

The GSI is an *integral over the entire pulse*, which means:
- A small spurious spike at the start or end of the recording inflates GSI.
- It cannot localize the **worst** interval of the pulse.

### Step 3 — Introduce a Sliding-Window Maximum (Versace, 1971)

To overcome the limitation, Versace redefined the index as the **maximum** of a windowed integral:

$$HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ (t_2 - t_1) \left( \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$$

> **Reading the equation:** Among *all* possible choices of start time $t_1$ and end time $t_2$ (within the crash pulse), HIC picks the time-window that maximizes injury potential. The expression inside the outer brackets is the **average acceleration** over the window raised to the 2.5 power, multiplied by the window duration — converting instantaneous risk to a time-weighted risk.

### Step 4 — Apply the Regulatory Window Constraint

To make HIC regulatory-friendly, the window $\Delta t = t_2 - t_1$ is constrained:

$$\Delta t \le 36 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow HIC_{36} \quad \text{(older FMVSS 208)}$$

$$\Delta t \le 15 \text{ ms} \Rightarrow HIC_{15} \quad \text{(current Euro NCAP \& FMVSS 208R)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The 15 ms window reflects modern biomechanics understanding that brain injuries are dominated by **short, sharp acceleration spikes** (brain-skull relative motion, diffuse axonal injury) rather than long-duration blunt impact.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> A KTU-style numerical problem follows. Head acceleration profile measured during a crash test:

| Time (ms) | Acceleration (g) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 5 |
| 5 | 12 |
| 10 | 28 |
| 15 | 45 |
| 20 | 60 |
| 25 | 48 |
| 30 | 22 |
| 35 | 8 |

**Step 5.1 — Compute the average over a 15 ms sliding window.** Let us consider $t_1 = 5$ ms, $t_2 = 20$ ms:

$$\bar{a} = \dfrac{1}{20 - 5} \int_{5}^{20} a(t)\, dt \approx \dfrac{1}{15} \left[ \int_{5}^{10}12\,dt + \int_{10}^{15}28\,dt + \int_{15}^{20}45\,dt \right]$$

Computing each sub-integral (treating the values as step-constants, in g·ms):

$$\int_{5}^{10} 12\,dt = 60 \text{ g·ms}$$

$$\int_{10}^{15} 28\,dt = 140 \text{ g·ms}$$

$$\int_{15}^{20} 45\,dt = 225 \text{ g·ms}$$

$$\bar{a} = \dfrac{60 + 140 + 225}{15} = \dfrac{425}{15} \approx 28.33 \text{ g}$$

**Step 5.2 — Compute HIC for this window:**

$$HIC = (20 - 5) \cdot (28.33)^{2.5}$$

$$(28.33)^{2.5} = (28.33)^2 \cdot \sqrt{28.33} = 802.6 \cdot 5.323 \approx 4272$$

$$HIC = 15 \cdot 4272 = 64080$$

> [!CAUTION]
> This extremely high value is because g-units produce large numbers. Regulations cap the **upper limit** at 1000 in the equation for evaluation purposes. The above is a teaching illustration; production-grade HIC computation uses trapezoidal integration with raw digitized data sampled at $\ge 10$ kHz.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Chest Severity Index (CSI) — Component-by-Component

### Step 1 — Define the Two Limiting Criteria Separately

Acceleration-based limit:
$$a_{chest}(t) \le 60 \text{ g (3 ms cumulative)}$$

Deflection-based limit:
$$D_{chest}(t) \le 76.2 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{FMVSS 208 threshold})$$

### Step 2 — Normalize Each Term

Define acceleration ratio:
$$A^*(t) = \dfrac{a_{chest}(t)}{A_{int}} = \dfrac{a_{chest}(t)}{60}$$

Define deflection ratio:
$$D^*(t) = \dfrac{D_{chest}(t)}{D_{int}} = \dfrac{D_{chest}(t)}{76.2}$$

### Step 3 — Combine via Superposition

> The CSI assumes injury from two distinct mechanisms (inertial loading from acceleration and structural loading from deflection) combine as a linear superposition of normalized stresses.

$$CSI(t) = A^*(t) + D^*(t) = \dfrac{a_{chest}(t)}{60} + \dfrac{D_{chest}(t)}{76.2}$$

### Step 4 — Apply Regulatory Cap

$$CSI = \max_{t} \left[ \dfrac{a_{chest}(t)}{60} + \dfrac{D_{chest}(t)}{76.2} \right] \le 1.0$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Check (Worked Example)

Suppose a crash test yields:
- Peak chest acceleration = **48 g**
- Peak chest deflection = **42 mm**

$$CSI = \dfrac{48}{60} + \dfrac{42}{76.2} = 0.800 + 0.551 = 1.351$$

$$CSI = 1.35 > 1.0 \Rightarrow \textbf{FAIL}$$

> **Engineer's interpretation:** The occupant would likely sustain a chest injury of **AIS 3 or higher** (severe). The restraint system (seat belt + airbag) tuning must be revised to either reduce chest loading or reduce belt-induced intrusion into the thorax.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Viscous Criterion (VC) — From Rate-Sensitive Soft-Tissue Injury

### Step 1 — Physical Motivation

Soft tissues (heart, lungs, liver, spleen) are **viscoelastic** — they are sensitive to the **rate of compression**, not just the magnitude.

### Step 2 — Define Compression

$$C(t) = \dfrac{D(t)}{D_0}$$

where $D_0$ is the initial thorax depth (≈ 230 mm in HIII 50M) and $D(t)$ is the dynamic chest deflection.

### Step 3 — Define Compression Velocity

$$V(t) = \dfrac{dC(t)}{dt} = \dfrac{1}{D_0} \cdot \dfrac{dD(t)}{dt}$$

### Step 4 — Viscous Response Function

Soft-tissue damage rate $\propto$ strain-rate × strain:
$$\dot{\Omega}(t) = V(t) \cdot C(t)$$

### Step 5 — Define the Viscous Criterion

$$VC = \max_{t} \left[ V(t) \cdot C(t) \right]$$

**Limit:** $VC \le 1.0$ m/s (Euro NCAP) or $VC \le 1.0$ m for some US standards — check the specific regulation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: How Dummy + Metric + Regulation Interlock

| Layer | Component | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **L1 — Hazard** | Collision type | Frontal, oblique, side, rear, rollover |
| **L2 — Regulation** | Test protocol | FMVSS 208, ECE R94, Euro NCAP |
| **L3 — Dummy** | ATD | Hybrid III 50M, WorldSID 50M, THOR |
| **L4 — Body Region** | Anatomical target | Head, neck, chest, abdomen, femur |
| **L5 — Sensor** | Transducer | Tri-axial accelerometer, load cell, potentiometer |
| **L6 — Filtering** | Data conditioning | CFC 60, CFC 180, CFC 1000 |
| **L7 — Metric** | Injury criterion | HIC, Nij, CSI, VC, Femur Force |
| **L8 — Limit** | Threshold | 700, 1.0, 1.0, 1.0 m/s, 10 kN |
| **L9 — Outcome** | Pass/Fail | Pass / Fail / Star rating |

> [!NOTE]
> This nine-layer framework is how KTU examiners like to frame 14-mark questions: *"Explain with a block diagram how occupant injury is evaluated in a vehicle crash test."* — you draw the above table or block diagram and elaborate on each layer.

---

## 3.5 The Hybrid III 50M — Detailed Subsystem Inventory

For a 14-mark question that asks *"Describe the major subsystems of the Hybrid III 50th percentile male dummy"*, the following inventory is a complete answer:

| Subsystem | Components | Mass Breakdown (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| **Head + Skull** | Aluminum skull, vinyl skin, tri-axial accelerometer, angular rate sensor | 4.54 |
| **Neck** | 6 rubber/aluminum discs, 4 load cells (2 axial Fz, 2 occipital Mx) | 1.51 |
| **Upper Torso** | Steel rib cage (6 ribs), thoracic spine, sternum deflection potentiometer, chest accelerometer | 17.20 |
| **Lower Torso** | Lumbar spine (rubber), pelvis (cast aluminum), pelvis accelerometer | 13.65 |
| **Arms (×2)** | Upper arm, elbow joint, forearm, wrist, hand | 8.50 (total) |
| **Legs (×2)** | Femur (load cell), knee slider (potentiometer), tibia, ankle, foot | 32.60 (total) |
| **Instrumentation** | 24+ channels, on-board data acquisition | external |
| **Total Mass** | — | **78.0 kg** (regulatory spec) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Why does this matter for KTU?** Because examiners expect a candidate to identify **where** the sensors are located (head CG, lumbar spine, etc.) and explain **why** each location was chosen. The head sensor is at the **centre of gravity** (not the skull surface) because brain injury is governed by **inertial** loading of the brain, which is best represented at the head CG.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD). List any two types of dummies used in vehicle crash testing.

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An *Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)* is a biofidelic manikin equipped with embedded sensors used to measure human-occupant kinematics and injury potential during vehicle crash tests, in compliance with regulations such as FMVSS 208, ECE R94, and Euro NCAP.

**Two types:**
1. **Hybrid III 50th Percentile Male (HIII 50M)** — 78 kg, used for frontal impact (FMVSS 208 / Euro NCAP).
2. **WorldSID 50M** — used for side impact (FMVSS 214 / ECE R95 / Euro NCAP side).

*(Award full 3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for each dummy type with its application.)*

---

### Q2. What is the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)? State the regulatory limit for HIC₁₅.

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Module 3]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Head Injury Criterion (HIC) is an empirical, time-weighted injury metric used to assess the potential for head injury (skull fracture, concussion, diffuse axonal injury) based on the resultant head-centre-of-gravity acceleration time-history. It is defined as the maximum, over all admissible time windows $(t_1, t_2)$, of the time-averaged acceleration raised to the 2.5 power, multiplied by the window duration:

$$HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ (t_2 - t_1) \left( \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$$

**Regulatory limit for HIC₁₅:** $HIC_{15} \le 700$ (Euro NCAP / FMVSS 208R).

*(1 mark: definition with formula. 1 mark: explanation of the sliding window. 1 mark: limit and regulatory source.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Module 3 — Internal Choice Option 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**Q.A (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the major subsystems of a Hybrid III 50th percentile male crash test dummy. Discuss the role of biofidelity in dummy design. **(7 marks)**

**Q.A (b)** Define the Head Injury Criterion (HIC). Derive it from the Gadd Severity Index and explain the physical significance of the 2.5 exponent. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (a) (7 marks)

**1. Introduction (1 mark):** The Hybrid III 50M is the most widely used ATD in frontal crash testing worldwide. It represents a 50th percentile adult male of mass 78 kg and stature 1.75 m. It is instrumented with more than 24 measurement channels and complies with FMVSS 208 and Euro NCAP protocols.

**2. Subsystems (5 marks — 1 mark each):**

| Subsystem | Key Components | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Head** | Aluminum skull, vinyl skin, tri-axial accelerometer at CG, angular rate sensor | Measures linear and rotational head acceleration for HIC and Nij computation |
| **Neck** | 6-disc rubber/aluminum stack, 4 load cells, 2 occipital moment sensors | Measures neck tension/compression and flexion/extension moment for Nij |
| **Thorax (Upper Torso)** | 6 steel ribs, thoracic spine, sternum deflection potentiometer, chest accelerometer | Measures chest compression and acceleration for CSI / VC |
| **Lumbar Spine + Pelvis** | Rubber lumbar, cast aluminum pelvis, pelvis accelerometer | Measures pelvic acceleration; reflects lap-belt loading |
| **Lower Limbs** | Femur with load cell, knee slider, tibia, ankle, foot potentiometer | Measures femur axial force and tibia compression for lower-extremity injury |

**3. Role of Biofidelity (1 mark):**
Biofidelity is the degree to which the dummy's mechanical response mimics that of a real human under identical dynamic loading. Higher biofidelity → more reliable injury prediction. NHTSA rates biofidelity on a 0–10 scale. Hybrid III has moderate biofidelity for frontal impacts but limited biofidelity for side, rear, and oblique impacts — hence the development of WorldSID, BioRID, and THOR.

---

#### Model Answer — Q.A (b) (7 marks)

**1. Definition of HIC (2 marks):**
HIC is the maximum value of a time-windowed integral of the head-centre-of-gravity acceleration raised to the 2.5th power, multiplied by the window duration. Its purpose is to provide a single, time-weighted number that correlates with the probability of skull fracture and brain injury.

$$HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left[ (t_2 - t_1) \left( \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right)^{2.5} \right]$$

**2. Derivation from GSI (4 marks):**

- **Gadd (1966)** proposed:
$$GSI = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ a(t) \right]^{2.5} dt$$
- **Limitation of GSI:** integrand is positive-only, so any spike anywhere in the pulse inflates the score; no localization of the *worst* window.
- **Versace (1971)** redefined: take the maximum of a sliding-window average raised to the 2.5 power, times window duration.
- **Regulatory refinement:** the window is constrained to $\le 36$ ms (HIC₃₆, original FMVSS 208) or $\le 15$ ms (HIC₁₅, current FMVSS 208R / Euro NCAP), reflecting modern understanding of brain-injury mechanisms.

**3. Significance of the 2.5 exponent (1 mark):**
The exponent 2.5 was empirically fitted by Gadd to Wayne State University cadaveric concussion data. It represents the **non-linear sensitivity** of brain tissue to acceleration: a small increase in acceleration produces a disproportionately large increase in injury probability. Physically, the brain is a viscoelastic medium; the 2.5 power captures the rate-dependent rupture of bridging veins and axonal strain.

---

### Question B (Module 3 — Internal Choice Option 2)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks]**

**Q.B (a)** List and briefly explain any four injury criteria used in vehicle crash testing. Mention the regulatory limit for each. **(7 marks)**

**Q.B (b)** Compare frontal-impact and side-impact crash test dummies in terms of anthropometry, sensor instrumentation, and biofidelity. Why is a separate family of side-impact dummies necessary? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B (a) (7 marks)

| Criterion | Body Region | Formula (Compact) | Regulatory Limit | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Head Injury Criterion (HIC₁₅)** | Head | $HIC = \Delta t \cdot (\bar{a})^{2.5}$ | $\le 700$ | FMVSS 208 / Euro NCAP |
| **Neck Injury Index (Nij)** | Neck | $N_{ij} = F_y / F_{y,c} + M_x / M_{x,c}$ | $\le 1.0$ | Euro NCAP |
| **Chest Severity Index (CSI)** | Chest | $CSI = a_{chest}/60 + D_{chest}/76.2$ | $\le 1.0$ | FMVSS 208 |
| **Viscous Criterion (VC)** | Chest (soft tissue) | $VC = V(t) \cdot C(t)$ | $\le 1.0$ m/s | Euro NCAP |
| **Femur Compressive Force** | Femur | $F_{femur}$ | $\le 10$ kN | FMVSS 208 |

*(1 mark per criterion: 0.5 for definition + formula, 0.5 for limit + standard. Total = 4 × 1.5 ≈ 6 marks; 1 mark for tabular presentation / comparative discussion.)*

---

#### Model Answer — Q.B (b) (7 marks)

| Parameter | Frontal Dummy (HIII 50M) | Side-Impact Dummy (WorldSID 50M) |
|---|---|---|
| **Anthropometry** | 50th percentile male, 78 kg, 1.75 m | 50th percentile male, 75 kg, 1.75 m (slightly lighter due to larger number of sensors) |
| **Symmetry** | Bilaterally symmetric | Asymmetric — left and right sides instrumented differently (struck side vs. non-struck side) |
| **Number of rib-like elements** | 6 ribs in thorax | 12 ribs in thorax (more lateral rib coverage) |
| **Key sensors** | Head accel., chest accel., chest deflection, femur load, neck Fz/Mx | Shoulder load, thorax rib deflection (12 ribs), abdomen pressure, pelvis T12, pubic symphysis force |
| **Biofidelity rating (NHTSA)** | Moderate for frontal | High for side |
| **Limit** | Cannot capture lateral thoracic injury | Cannot capture frontal biomechanics well |

**Why a separate family is necessary (2 marks):**

1. **Loading mode difference:** Frontal crashes produce anterior–posterior (x-direction) inertia, while side crashes produce lateral (y-direction) inertia with very short vehicle-to-occupant contact time (≈ 5–10 ms vs. 60–80 ms frontal).
2. **Injury patterns differ:** Frontal crashes injure the chest via steering-wheel/airbag contact, the legs via footwell intrusion, and the head via windshield/airbag. Side crashes injure the thorax via door intrusion, the abdomen via arm-rest contact, and the pelvis via sill contact — different anatomy, different sensors.
3. **Biomechanical differences:** The rib cage has different stiffness in lateral vs. anterior loading. Lateral loading produces more rapid, localized chest deflection that the HIII 50M (with only 6 ribs) cannot resolve.
4. **Regulatory separation:** FMVSS 208 (frontal) and FMVSS 214 (side) prescribe different dummies because of the above biomechanical and regulatory reasons.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students commonly lose marks on "Dummies" questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Hybrid II with Hybrid III (–1 to –2 marks).** Hybrid II is **obsolete** since 1979. If a question says "frontal crash test dummy," the answer is **Hybrid III 50M**, not Hybrid II.
>
> 2. **Writing the HIC formula with acceleration in m/s² instead of g (–1 mark).** Regulatory HIC requires **g**, not m/s². Using m/s² produces a value of $9.81^{2.5}$ ≈ 297 times too large, which is wrong.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to state the window constraint (–1 mark).** HIC₁₅ has a 15 ms window; HIC₃₆ has a 36 ms window. The 15 ms is the **current** standard (Euro NCAP, FMVSS 208R).
>
> 4. **Naming the dummy without its full designation (–1 mark).** "Hybrid III" is not enough — write "Hybrid III 50th percentile male (HIII 50M), 78 kg."
>
> 5. **Mixing injury criteria across body regions (–1 mark).** HIC applies only to the head. Applying HIC to chest or femur is biomechanically meaningless.
>
> 6. **Omitting the regulatory standard (–1 mark).** Always cite the source: "Euro NCAP," "FMVSS 208," "AIS-096," etc.
>
> 7. **Not drawing the block diagram (–2 marks).** For 14-mark questions, a labelled block diagram of the dummy's subsystems OR the data acquisition chain is **mandatory**. KTU examiners allot 1–2 marks for "neat sketch."
>
> 8. **Stating the limit without units (–0.5 mark per instance).** HIC ≤ 700 (dimensionless), femur force ≤ 10 kN, chest deflection ≤ 50 mm — **always include the unit**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Quick-Recall Checklist for the Last 5 Minutes of Exam Prep**

- **ATD** = Anthropomorphic Test Device. It is a biofidelic, instrumented surrogate of the human body for crash testing.
- **Hybrid III 50M** is the global standard for frontal impact (mass **78 kg**, stature 1.75 m).
- **WorldSID 50M** is the modern side-impact dummy.
- **BioRID II** is used for rear-impact (whiplash) testing.
- **Q-Series (Q0, Q1, Q3, Q6, Q10)** is the modern child-dummy family replacing Hybrid III child versions.
- **HIC formula** is the **sliding-window maximum** of (window duration) × (average acceleration)^2.5, with acceleration in **g**.
- **HIC₁₅ limit = 700** (current Euro NCAP / FMVSS 208R).
- **HIC₃₆ limit = 1000** (older FMVSS 208).
- **Nij limit = 1.0** (neck injury).
- **CSI limit = 1.0** (chest).
- **VC limit = 1.0 m/s** (viscous criterion).
- **Femur force limit = 10 kN** (FMVSS 208).
- **CFC 60** filter → head; **CFC 180** → chest, femur, neck; **CFC 1000** → ankle/foot.
- **Head accelerometer is at the centre of gravity** of the head (not at the skull surface).
- **Calibration is mandatory** every 12 months (or after a major impact).
- **Head drop test:** 376.1 mm drop, peak 225–275 g.
- **Biofidelity rating** is 0–10 (NHTSA scale).
- **AIS** = Abbreviated Injury Scale (0 = no injury, 6 = unsurvivable).
- **Euro NCAP star rating** is based on the combined assessment of adult occupant, child occupant, pedestrian, and safety assist.
- **India:** AIS-096 (front), AIS-099 (side), AIS-100 (pole side), AIS-052 (rear).
- **SAE J211** defines the data acquisition and filtering standards.
- **ISO 6487** defines the calibration requirements.
- The dummy is a **measurement tool**, not a predictor of human injury. It provides **biofidelic response** that is then mapped to injury risk via the **injury criteria**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **One-line punch for the KTU viva:** *"A crash test dummy is an instrumented, biofidelic surrogate of the human body, used to measure occupant kinematics and injury potential under regulatory crash conditions; its data, filtered per SAE J211, drives injury metrics such as HIC, Nij, CSI, VC, and femur force whose thresholds dictate whether a vehicle passes safety certification."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crashworthiness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crashworthiness

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crashworthiness** is formally defined as the inherent capability of a vehicle's structure and restraint systems to protect its occupants during and after a collision event. It is a measure of the *degree to which* a vehicle, when subjected to a specified crash impact, protects its occupants from serious injury or death by managing the crash energy, controlling occupant kinematics, and maintaining a survivable occupant space (also termed the "Survival Space" or "Rigid Safety Cell").

Crashworthiness is a **passive safety** attribute, meaning the system activates only during a crash event (in contrast to active safety systems like ABS or ESC which prevent the crash). It is governed by the principle of *energy management* — dissipating crash kinetic energy in a controlled, progressive manner while isolating occupants from deceleration peaks.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The Egg in a Carton Analogy:** Imagine you are tossing a raw egg off a table. If you throw it onto a concrete floor, the egg cracks — energy is dissipated instantly, peak force is enormous, and the egg (occupant) experiences lethal deceleration. However, if you drop the egg into a thick mattress, the egg survives. The mattress **crushes progressively**, lengthening the *crash pulse* (the time over which deceleration occurs), reducing the peak force. The mattress is the "crashworthy" structure — it sacrifices itself (crumples) to protect the egg (occupant). Similarly, a crashworthy car is engineered to "crumple like the mattress" while keeping the "egg" (occupant compartment) rigid and intact.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Benchmarks

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **HIC (Head Injury Criterion) ≤ 1000** : Industry-standard threshold for head injury survival. A HIC value of **1000** corresponds to approximately a 16% probability of severe head injury (AIS ≥ 4). Values above 1000 are deemed unsafe.
> - **Nij (Neck Injury Criterion) ≤ 1.0** : Upper limit for neck load injury probability in frontal impacts.
> - **Chest Deflection ≤ 50 mm** : Maximum acceptable thorax compression (per FMVSS 208) before rib fracture risk becomes significant.
> - **Femur Load ≤ 10 kN** : Maximum compressive load on femur before fracture risk.
> - **Survival Space (Rigid Safety Cell)** : Defined as the A-pillar, B-pillar, roof rail, and floor pan region that must remain intact. Intrusion into this region must be < 50 mm typically.
> - **Restraint system deployment time:** ~20–30 ms for airbag inflation, ~5–10 ms for pre-tensioner activation.

### 1.4 Crash Severity Classification

| Crash Mode | Typical Δv (m/s) | Equivalent Barrier Speed (km/h) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Low severity (minor) | < 8 | < 28 |
| Moderate severity | 8 – 16 | 28 – 56 |
| High severity (severe) | > 16 | > 56 |

where **Δv (Velocity Change)** is the fundamental kinematic measure of crash severity — the change in vehicle velocity across the crash event.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Pillars of Crashworthiness Design

Crashworthiness engineering rests on three operational pillars. Each addresses a specific mechanism by which injury is prevented.

**Pillar I — Energy Management (Crush-Based Energy Absorption)**
- The vehicle's front and rear structures (crumple zones) are designed to deform in a controlled manner.
- Energy absorbed by plastic deformation: $E_{abs} = \int_0^{d} F_{crush}(x)\, dx$
- For an idealized constant-force crush: $E_{abs} = F_{avg} \cdot d$
- This converts the vehicle's translational kinetic energy into irreversible plastic work.

**Pillar II — Deceleration Management (Crash Pulse Shaping)**
- The crash pulse is the time-history of vehicle deceleration $a(t)$.
- A *long, flat crash pulse* is preferable to a *short, sharp spike* because it spreads deceleration over more time, reducing peak forces on the occupant.
- The Occupant Ride-Down concept: the occupant should "ride down" with the vehicle, not impact the interior.

**Pillar III — Occupant Restraint (Load Distribution)**
- Belts, airbags, and head restraints prevent occupant ejection and contact with hard interior surfaces.
- They extend the time over which the occupant decelerates by adding a controlled crush stroke (the airbag bag stroke, the belt stretch, and the seat foam compression).

### 2.2 The Crash Pulse and Its Engineering Significance

> [!NOTE]
> The **crash pulse** $a(t)$ is the deceleration of the vehicle's mass center as a function of time during impact. It is the fundamental "input" to the occupant biomechanical model. The integral of the crash pulse yields the velocity change, while its shape (peak amplitude, duration, and area) determines occupant loading.

Key characteristics of a well-engineered crash pulse:
- **Peak deceleration** should be limited to ~**20–25 g** for the occupant compartment (the safety cell decelerates more gradually than the front structure).
- **Pulse duration** should be in the range **80–120 ms** for a 56 km/h frontal impact.
- **Sustained plateau** rather than sharp spike, since injury is correlated with the cumulative product of force and time (impulse).

### 2.3 Why Energy Absorption Matters — A Logical Walk-Through

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ traveling at initial velocity $v_i$ striking a rigid barrier and coming to rest ($v_f = 0$).

1. The **kinetic energy** that must be dissipated is $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v_i^2$.
2. If dissipated over a small distance $d$ (rigid structure, no crumple), the **peak force** is enormous: $F_{peak} \sim E_k / d$.
3. If the front structure is allowed to crush over a larger distance $d$ (crumple zone), the **average crush force** is reduced: $F_{avg} = E_k / d$.
4. The **deceleration** experienced by the rigid safety cell is therefore reduced: $a_{cell} = F_{avg} / m_{cell}$.
5. Occupants, restrained by belts and airbags, decelerate over an even longer effective stroke, lowering their body loads below injury thresholds.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **core equation reference** for any crashworthiness problem in the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. Memorize these expressions and their units.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Kinetic Energy of Vehicle | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J (kg·m²/s²) | Energy to be absorbed |
| 2 | Energy Absorbed by Crush | $E_{abs} = F_{avg} \cdot d$ | J | For constant force crush |
| 3 | Energy Absorbed (General) | $E_{abs} = \int F\, dx$ | J | Variable force crush |
| 4 | Average Deceleration | $a_{avg} = v_i^2 \, / \, (2d)$ | m/s² | From kinematics: $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2ad$ |
| 5 | Equivalent Average Force | $F_{avg} = m v_i^2 \, / \, (2d)$ | N | Using $W = F \cdot d$ and $E_k$ |
| 6 | Crash Pulse Stroke Time | $t_{stroke} = 2d \, / \, v_i$ | s | Assumes constant deceleration |
| 7 | Velocity Change (Δv) | $\Delta v = v_i - v_f$ | m/s | Severity metric |
| 8 | Barrier Equivalent Velocity (BEV) | $BEV = v_i$ | m/s | For full-rigid barrier |
| 9 | HIC (Head Injury Criterion) | $HIC = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | dimensionless | Max over $t_2 - t_1 \le 36$ ms |
| 10 | HIC15 (15 ms) | Same as above with $t_2 - t_1 \le 15$ ms | dimensionless | Stricter criterion |
| 11 | Nij (Neck Injury Criterion) | $N_{ij} = F_z \, /\, F_{z,int} + M_y \, / \, M_{y,int}$ | dimensionless | Limited to $\le 1.0$ |
| 12 | Chest Acceleration (3 ms clip) | $a_{clip} = \tfrac{1}{3\,\text{ms}} \int a(t)\, dt$ | g | Must be $\le 60$ g |
| 13 | Energy Density (per unit mass) | $e_{crush} = E_{abs} \, / \, m_{crush}$ | J/kg | Material-specific |
| 14 | Specific Energy Absorption (SEA) | $SEA = E_{abs} \, / \, (m_{structure})$ | kJ/kg | Material selection metric |
| 15 | Average Crush Force (Tubes) | $F_{avg} = 2\pi R_0 t_0 \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ | N | For thin-walled circular tube |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are the bedrock of crash simulation in LS-DYNA, PAM-CRASH, and MADYMO software used in industry. Formula (15), the SEA concept, is the primary selection criterion for crashworthy materials — modern high-strength steels achieve SEA of 25–40 kJ/kg, while aluminum alloys reach 15–25 kJ/kg. The crush-force efficiency (CFE = $F_{avg}/F_{peak}$) characterizes how "rectangular" the force-displacement curve is; higher CFE means more energy absorbed per unit peak force, which is desirable.

### 2.5 Mechanism of Energy Absorption in Crumple Zones

The crumple zone is engineered to absorb energy through a hierarchy of deformation modes, activated in sequence:

1. **Bending collapse** of longitudinal rails (initial low-load resistance).
2. **Axisymmetric folding** of thin-walled tubes (intermediate load plateau).
3. **Material tearing and fracture** (high-load absorption at end of stroke).
4. **Inter-component friction** (energy dissipated as heat).
5. **Weld-point failure** (controlled de-bonding absorbs energy progressively).

Modern vehicles use a **graduated strength** strategy: the outermost rail section has the lowest yield strength, and strength increases progressively inward. This creates a *bottoming-out* effect where the rear of the crumple zone is stronger, ensuring the energy is fully absorbed.

### 2.6 Compatibility — Vehicle-to-Vehicle Crash Interaction

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crash Compatibility** refers to the ability of two vehicles involved in a collision to share the crash energy in proportion to their masses and stiffnesses. A small, light vehicle striking a large, heavy, rigid vehicle will experience a *stiffness mismatch* — the small vehicle's crumple zone must absorb nearly all the kinetic energy of both vehicles, leading to severe occupant loading. Conversely, if both vehicles are stiff, the deceleration is shared but both experience high peak forces.

Compatibility is quantified by the **mass ratio** and the **stiffness ratio** between the colliding partners. The KTU-relevant principle: the *self-protection ratio* (energy absorbed by the structure) and the *partner-protection ratio* (energy transferred to the partner) should both be high.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Deceleration and Crush Stroke Relationship

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A 1500 kg vehicle traveling at 56 km/h strikes a rigid wall and comes to rest. The front structure crushes uniformly. Derive the average deceleration experienced by the passenger compartment as a function of crush stroke $d$.

**Step 1 — Convert speed to SI units.**

$$
v_i = 56 \,\frac{\text{km}}{\text{h}} = 56 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 15.56 \,\frac{\text{m}}{\text{s}}
$$

This is the standard SI conversion: 1 km/h = 5/18 m/s. The conversion is required because all subsequent calculations are in SI units.

**Step 2 — Apply the work-energy theorem.**

The kinetic energy of the vehicle must be fully dissipated by the plastic deformation of the crumple zone:

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_i^2 = F_{avg} \cdot d
$$

The work done by the average crush force over the stroke $d$ equals the loss in kinetic energy. The vehicle comes to rest, so the work-energy theorem gives the total energy dissipated.

**Step 3 — Express the average crush force.**

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} m v_i^2}{d} = \frac{m v_i^2}{2d}
$$

This is the equivalent constant force that, acting through stroke $d$, would absorb the same energy as the actual variable crushing force.

**Step 4 — Apply Newton's second law to find deceleration.**

The passenger compartment (mass $m_{cell}$) decelerates due to the average crush force (assuming rigid-cell, plastic-rail model):

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{F_{avg}}{m_{cell}}
$$

For the simple lumped-mass model where the entire vehicle mass decelerates together (no separation of cell and rail masses):

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{F_{avg}}{m} = \frac{v_i^2}{2d}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values for verification.**

For $v_i = 15.56$ m/s and a typical stroke $d = 0.5$ m:

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{(15.56)^2}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{242.1}{1.0} = 242.1 \,\text{m/s}^2
$$

In g-units (where $g = 9.81$ m/s²):

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{242.1}{9.81} = 24.68 \,g
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the stroke time.**

Assuming constant deceleration, kinematic relation $v_f = v_i - a t$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$
t_{stroke} = \frac{v_i}{a_{avg}} = \frac{15.56}{242.1} = 0.0643 \,\text{s} \approx 64.3 \,\text{ms}
$$

This 64 ms stroke is a typical engineering target — long enough to keep peak g below 25 g, short enough to absorb all energy before the occupant reaches the dashboard.

### 3.2 Derivation: Head Injury Criterion (HIC) Simplified Form

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** During a crash, the head acceleration profile (in g) over a specific interval is approximated as a constant $a$ (in g). Derive a simplified HIC expression.

**Step 1 — Recall the formal HIC definition.**

$$
HIC = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)
$$

The HIC selects the time interval $(t_1, t_2)$ of duration $\le 36$ ms (HIC36) or $\le 15$ ms (HIC15) that **maximizes** the resulting value. This is a search problem over the acceleration time-history.

**Step 2 — Apply the constant-acceleration assumption.**

If $a(t) = a$ (constant in g), the integral simplifies to:

$$
\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt = a \cdot (t_2 - t_1)
$$

The acceleration is constant, so the integral is just the rectangle area.

**Step 3 — Substitute back into the HIC expression.**

$$
HIC = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \cdot a \cdot (t_2 - t_1) \right]^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1) = a^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)
$$

The $(t_2 - t_1)$ terms cancel inside the brackets because the acceleration is constant — the average equals the constant value. The result is clean and intuitive.

**Step 4 — Apply the HIC15 (15 ms maximum interval) constraint.**

For HIC15, set $t_2 - t_1 = 0.015$ s:

$$
HIC_{15} = a^{2.5} \cdot 0.015
$$

**Step 5 — Verify with the HIC = 1000 limit.**

Solve for the equivalent constant acceleration that produces $HIC = 1000$:

$$
1000 = a^{2.5} \cdot 0.015 \quad\Rightarrow\quad a^{2.5} = 66{,}667
$$

$$
a = (66{,}667)^{0.4} = 78.7 \,g
$$

This means a head experiencing a constant 78.7 g over any 15 ms window would just reach the HIC = 1000 injury threshold. Real-world head injury typically occurs at sustained accelerations of 60–80 g for this duration.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: HIC Severity Bands

| HIC Value | Acceleration Equivalent (15 ms) | Injury Severity |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| $\le 240$ | $\le 30$ g | No injury / negligible risk |
| 240 – 1000 | 30 – 79 g | Moderate injury risk (concussion possible) |
| 1000 – 2000 | 79 – 115 g | Severe injury; significant fatality probability |
| $> 2000$ | $> 115$ g | Critical; very high fatality probability |

### 3.4 Derivation: Specific Energy Absorption of a Thin-Walled Tube

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem context:** A thin-walled circular steel tube of mean radius $R_0$, wall thickness $t_0$, and yield stress $\sigma_y$ collapses plastically in axisymmetric folding mode. Derive the average crush force and the specific energy absorption.

**Step 1 — Establish the effective yield stress in plastic bending.**

For a circular tube undergoing folding collapse, the plastic hinge forms along a circumferential line. The effective flow stress is approximated as:

$$
\sigma_{eff} = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

The $\sqrt{3}$ factor arises from the von Mises yield criterion conversion from uniaxial to effective stress for plane-strain plastic bending.

**Step 2 — Compute the plastic bending moment per unit length.**

The plastic moment per unit circumferential length about the collapse hinge is:

$$
M_0 = \sigma_{eff} \cdot t_0^2 \, / \, 4
$$

This is the moment capacity of a thin rectangular cross-section (wall thickness $t_0$ × unit width) in fully plastic bending.

**Step 3 — Compute the average axial crush force.**

The collapse force for axisymmetric folding balances the work done by axial force through one fold wavelength $2H$ with the plastic bending energy in the two hinges (one outer, one inner):

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{2 \pi R_0 M_0}{H}
$$

For the half-wavelength $H$ of a fold (typically $H \approx 0.5\,t_0 \cdot \sqrt{R_0/t_0}$, but for thin tubes $H \approx 2 t_0$):

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{2 \pi R_0 \sigma_{eff} t_0^2 / 4}{2 t_0} = \frac{\pi R_0 \sigma_{eff} t_0}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify using the effective stress.**

Substituting $\sigma_{eff} = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$:

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{\pi R_0 t_0 \sigma_y}{2 \sqrt{3}}
$$

Or equivalently (commonly cited form):

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{2 \pi R_0 t_0 \sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}
$$

The factor depends on the assumed fold geometry; the most commonly cited form in KTU-level texts is the latter.

**Step 5 — Compute the specific energy absorption.**

The mass per unit length of the tube is $m_L = 2 \pi R_0 t_0 \rho$, where $\rho$ is the material density. Over a crush length equal to one fold wavelength $L_f = 2H$:

$$
SEA = \frac{F_{avg} \cdot L_f}{m_L \cdot L_f} = \frac{F_{avg}}{2 \pi R_0 t_0 \rho}
$$

Substituting $F_{avg}$:

$$
SEA = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3} \, \rho}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute for typical steel ($\sigma_y = 350$ MPa, $\rho = 7850$ kg/m³).**

$$
SEA = \frac{350 \times 10^6}{\sqrt{3} \times 7850} = \frac{350 \times 10^6}{13{,}596} \approx 25{,}743 \,\text{J/kg} \approx 25.7 \,\text{kJ/kg}
$$

This matches the order-of-magnitude value cited in industrial SEA tables (20–40 kJ/kg for AHSS). The result confirms the **fundamental engineering insight**: SEA is governed primarily by the **yield-to-density ratio** $\sigma_y / \rho$, which is the key material-selection metric for crashworthy structures.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Energy Absorption Mechanisms Summary

| Mechanism | Energy Sink | Typical % of Total | Engineering Control |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Plastic bending of rails | Permanent deformation | 60–75% | Rail geometry, thickness |
| Folding/buckling of thin sections | Permanent deformation | 10–20% | Wall slenderness ratio |
| Material fracture (tearing) | Surface energy (small) | 2–5% | Notch design, ductility |
| Weld-point failure | Adhesive/cohesive work | 5–10% | Weld pitch, spot pattern |
| Friction (inter-component) | Heat | 3–8% | Surface coatings, fasteners |
| Restraint system work | Airbag gas, belt stretch | 5–15% | Bag timing, belt stiffness |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Crash Energy Management Hierarchy

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid block diagram shows the **functional architecture of crash energy flow** from the moment of impact through occupant response. It represents the crashworthiness system as a chain of energy-transfer stages, each with its own energy absorption, delay, or transfer function.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["VEHICLE KINETIC ENERGY"] --> B["FRONT CRUMPLE ZONE"]
    B --> C["ENGINE SUBFRAME TRANSFER"]
    C --> D["PASSENGER SAFETY CELL"]
    D --> E["OCCUPANT RESTRAINT SYSTEM"]
    E --> F["OCCUPANT BIO LOADING"]

    B1["Energy Absorbed: 60 to 75 percent"] -.-> B
    C1["Energy Transferred: 25 to 40 percent"] -.-> C
    D1["RIGID, No Crush Allowed, Intrusion less than 50 mm"] -.-> D
    E1["Belt Load Limit, Airbag Venting, Seat Foam"] -.-> E
    F1["HIC less than 1000, Nij less than 1.0, Chest less than 50 mm"] -.-> F

    G["INPUT: Impact Velocity vi, Mass m, Impact Configuration"] --> A
    F --> H["OUTPUT: Injury Metrics, Survival Probability"]

    style A fill:#ffcccc
    style B fill:#ffe599
    style C fill:#ffe599
    style D fill:#b6d7a8
    style E fill:#b6d7a8
    style F fill:#a4c2f4
    style H fill:#d9d2e9
```

**Reading the diagram:** Energy enters the system as kinetic energy at the top-left. It flows downward through five sequential stages, each of which absorbs, transfers, or filters energy. The red zone (kinetic energy) is the "threat." Yellow zones (crumple zone, subframe) are designed to absorb. Green zones (safety cell, restraint) are designed to be rigid yet controlled. Blue (bio loading) is the response variable. The objective is to keep the occupant in the "safe" HIC < 1000 region.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Crash Pulse Shaping and Occupant Ride-Down

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram shows how the **crash pulse** (vehicle deceleration) and the **occupant response** are decoupled by the restraint system. The airbag and belt act as a "mechanical filter" between the vehicle pulse and the occupant's head, chest, and pelvis.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["CRASH PULSE a_v t, Peak 20 to 25 g, Duration 80 to 120 ms"] --> Q1["SAFETY CELL DECELERATION, Matched to Pulse"]
    Q1 --> R1["RESTRAINT SYSTEM, Belt plus Airbag, Effective Stroke 100 to 150 mm"]
    R1 --> S1["OCCUPANT DECELERATION a_o t, Peak less than 60 g, Duration 60 to 100 ms"]
    S1 --> T1["BODY SEGMENT LOADS, Head, Chest, Femur, Neck"]
    T1 --> U1["INJURY METRICS, HIC, Nij, Chest g, Femur kN"]

    V1["Crash Duration: 60 to 80 ms"] -.-> P1
    W1["Restraint Stroke: 100 to 150 mm"] -.-> R1
    X1["Time Mismatch: Vehicle 80 ms, Occupant 100 ms"] -.-> S1

    style P1 fill:#f4cccc
    style Q1 fill:#fff2cc
    style R1 fill:#d9ead3
    style S1 fill:#cfe2f3
    style T1 fill:#cfe2f3
    style U1 fill:#d9d2e9
```

**Key engineering insight from the diagram:** The restraint system is the **mechanical impedance matcher** between the high-impedance vehicle structure and the low-impedance human body. Without it, the occupant would "free-flight" inside the cell until impacting the interior at high relative velocity, generating catastrophic injuries.

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Crumple Zone Design — Graduated Strength Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["ZONE 1: OUTER RAIL, Yield 250 MPa, Triggers First"] --> B1["ZONE 2: MID RAIL, Yield 600 MPa, Triggers Second"]
    B1 --> C1["ZONE 3: INNER RAIL, Yield 1000 MPa, Triggers Last"]
    C1 --> D1["RIGID CROSSMEMBER, Stops Crush, Sends Load to Cell"]

    A2["Crush Stroke 100 mm"] -.-> A1
    B2["Crush Stroke 150 mm"] -.-> B1
    C2["Crush Stroke 50 mm"] -.-> C1

    style A1 fill:#fce5cd
    style B1 fill:#fff2cc
    style C1 fill:#d9ead3
    style D1 fill:#cfe2f3
```

This graduated-strength design ensures that the weakest section fails first, the intermediate section absorbs most of the energy in a controlled plateau, and the strongest section acts as the final energy-absorbing "stop." This staged collapse produces a near-rectangular force-displacement curve (high CFE).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. Define Crashworthiness. List the three primary mechanisms by which a vehicle's crashworthy design protects its occupants.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Crashworthiness** is defined as the capability of a vehicle to protect its occupants from serious injury during a crash event, by managing impact energy, maintaining a survivable occupant space, and controlling occupant kinematics.

The three primary mechanisms are:

1. **Energy Absorption via Crumple Zones:** The front (and rear) structures are designed to deform plastically in a controlled manner, dissipating the vehicle's kinetic energy as plastic work.
2. **Survival Space Preservation:** The passenger compartment (safety cell) is engineered to remain rigid, preventing intrusion of structural members into the occupant space.
3. **Occupant Restraint:** Seatbelts, airbags, and head restraints decelerate the occupant over a longer time and distribute load across the strongest body regions, reducing injury severity.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for listing and briefly explaining the three mechanisms.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. What is the Head Injury Criterion (HIC)? State its threshold value and the corresponding injury severity band.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** is a biomechanical injury metric that quantifies the likelihood of head injury resulting from an acceleration pulse. It is computed as the maximum value of the expression:
>
> $$HIC = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$$
>
> evaluated over all intervals where $t_2 - t_1 \le 36$ ms (for HIC36) or $\le 15$ ms (for HIC15). Here $a(t)$ is the resultant head center-of-gravity acceleration in $g$.

**Threshold values:**
- $HIC \le 240$: Negligible injury risk.
- $HIC \le 1000$: Industry-standard survival limit; 16% probability of severe (AIS 4+) injury.
- $HIC > 1000$: Significant fatality risk; $HIC > 2000$ is critical.

*(3 marks — 1 for definition/formula, 1 for threshold, 1 for severity correlation.)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Module 3, ESE Pattern]**

#### **QUESTION A — 14 MARKS** *(Attempt either A or B)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A vehicle of mass 1500 kg traveling at 50 km/h strikes a rigid wall head-on. The front crumple zone has a uniform crush stroke of 0.6 m. Calculate: (i) the kinetic energy to be absorbed, (ii) the average crush force, (iii) the average deceleration experienced by the passenger compartment, and (iv) the stroke time.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With the aid of a sketch, describe the **graduated strength design** strategy used in modern crumple zones. Explain why this design produces a higher Crush Force Efficiency (CFE) than a single-strength design.

---

##### Model Solution to Q. A (a):

**Given:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v_i = 50$ km/h $= 50 \times \tfrac{5}{18} = 13.89$ m/s
- $d = 0.6$ m
- $v_f = 0$ (rest against rigid wall)

**Step 1 — Convert the speed.**

$$
v_i = 50 \times \frac{5}{18} = \frac{250}{18} = 13.89 \,\text{m/s}
$$

This is the standard conversion from km/h to m/s. Using SI units is essential because the formula $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ requires velocity in m/s for energy in joules.

**Step 2 — Calculate the kinetic energy.**

$$
E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_i^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (13.89)^2
$$

$$
E_k = 750 \times 192.93 = 144{,}700 \,\text{J} \approx 144.7 \,\text{kJ}
$$

This is the total energy that must be dissipated by the vehicle structure. In a crashworthy design, this energy is the target absorption budget for the crumple zone.

**Step 3 — Calculate the average crush force.**

Assuming uniform crushing with constant average force:

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{d} = \frac{144{,}700}{0.6} = 241{,}167 \,\text{N} \approx 241.2 \,\text{kN}
$$

The average force is 241.2 kN. This is a very large force, illustrating why vehicles are designed with substantial structural members — they must react this force without failing.

**Step 4 — Calculate the average deceleration.**

Using Newton's second law (and treating the entire vehicle as a lumped mass for simplicity):

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{F_{avg}}{m} = \frac{241{,}167}{1500} = 160.8 \,\text{m/s}^2
$$

In g-units:

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{160.8}{9.81} = 16.39 \,g
$$

This 16.4 g is a reasonable deceleration for the passenger compartment — well within the 20–25 g target band, demonstrating that the 0.6 m stroke is adequate to keep the cell deceleration survivable.

**Step 5 — Calculate the stroke time.**

For constant deceleration, $v_f = v_i - a_{avg} \cdot t_{stroke}$ with $v_f = 0$:

$$
t_{stroke} = \frac{v_i}{a_{avg}} = \frac{13.89}{160.8} = 0.0864 \,\text{s} = 86.4 \,\text{ms}
$$

This 86 ms stroke is consistent with the 80–120 ms design target for a moderate-speed frontal impact.

**Final answers:**
- (i) $E_k = 144.7$ kJ
- (ii) $F_{avg} = 241.2$ kN
- (iii) $a_{avg} = 16.4$ g
- (iv) $t_{stroke} = 86.4$ ms

*(7 marks — 1 for conversion, 2 for energy, 1 for force, 1 for deceleration, 2 for time and conclusion.)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q. A (b):

**Graduated Strength Design — Explanation:**

The graduated strength design strategy divides the crumple zone into **multiple longitudinal sections** (typically 2 to 4) of progressively increasing yield strength. The outermost section (closest to the impact point) has the lowest yield strength, and the innermost section (closest to the passenger cell) has the highest.

**Mechanism of operation:**

1. **Stage 1 — Outer rail collapse:** Upon impact, the outermost rail section (e.g., yield strength 250 MPa in mild steel) yields first because it experiences the highest stress. It absorbs energy in a controlled, plastic manner over a defined stroke.

2. **Stage 2 — Mid rail collapse:** Once the outer section has fully crushed, the load is transferred to the next section, which has a higher yield strength (e.g., 600 MPa in high-strength steel). This section continues to absorb energy at a higher force level.

3. **Stage 3 — Inner rail collapse:** The innermost section, made of ultra-high-strength steel (e.g., 1000 MPa), collapses last, providing the final energy absorption stage and a "bottoming-out" feature that prevents intrusion into the cell.

4. **Stage 4 — Load transfer to cell:** The residual kinetic energy, if any, is transferred to the rigid crossmember, which transmits it to the safety cell as a final deceleration event.

**Why this yields high CFE:**

The **Crush Force Efficiency (CFE)** is defined as:

$$
CFE = \frac{F_{avg}}{F_{peak}}
$$

A high CFE (close to 1.0) means the force-displacement curve is rectangular — the force stays nearly constant throughout the stroke, maximizing energy absorption for a given peak force. In a single-strength design, the force-displacement curve has a sharp initial peak (at yield) followed by a lower plateau, reducing the average-to-peak ratio. The graduated design creates a **staircase force-displacement curve** that more closely approximates a rectangle, raising the CFE from ~0.6–0.7 (single-strength) to ~0.85–0.95 (graduated).

The benefit is that for the same peak force (which determines structural integrity requirements), the graduated design absorbs more total energy. This directly improves crash performance without requiring stronger (and heavier) structures.

*(7 marks — 2 for diagram/sketch description, 3 for stage-by-stage mechanism, 2 for CFE explanation and engineering benefit.)*

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 MARKS** *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a) [7 Marks]** A car's frontal structure during a crash test exhibits the following idealized force-displacement profile: 0 to 0.05 m: linear rise from 0 to 400 kN; 0.05 to 0.55 m: constant force of 400 kN; 0.55 to 0.6 m: linear drop from 400 kN to 0. Calculate: (i) the total energy absorbed, (ii) the average crush force, and (iii) the Crush Force Efficiency (CFE). Comment on the engineering quality of the structure.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the role of **occupant restraint systems** in crashworthiness. With reference to the airbag deployment timeline and the 3-belt system, describe how they modify the crash pulse reaching the occupant.

---

##### Model Solution to Q. B (a):

**Given:**
- Stage 1: $F$ rises linearly from 0 to 400 kN over 0.05 m
- Stage 2: $F$ constant at 400 kN from 0.05 to 0.55 m
- Stage 3: $F$ drops linearly from 400 kN to 0 over 0.05 m (0.55 to 0.6 m)
- Total stroke $d = 0.6$ m

**Step 1 — Calculate energy absorbed in Stage 1 (triangular region).**

$$
E_1 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.05 \times 400{,}000 = 10{,}000 \,\text{J} = 10.0 \,\text{kJ}
$$

The energy is the area of the triangle, which is $\tfrac{1}{2} \times \text{base} \times \text{height}$. The base is the displacement interval (0.05 m) and the height is the peak force (400 kN = 400,000 N).

**Step 2 — Calculate energy absorbed in Stage 2 (rectangular region).**

$$
E_2 = 400{,}000 \times (0.55 - 0.05) = 400{,}000 \times 0.50 = 200{,}000 \,\text{J} = 200.0 \,\text{kJ}
$$

This is the dominant energy-absorption region. The constant-force plateau over 0.5 m absorbs 200 kJ, which is 95% of the total energy. This is exactly the engineering goal — a long, flat plateau maximizes energy absorption for a given peak force.

**Step 3 — Calculate energy absorbed in Stage 3 (triangular region).**

$$
E_3 = \frac{1}{2} \times 0.05 \times 400{,}000 = 10{,}000 \,\text{J} = 10.0 \,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Total energy absorbed.**

$$
E_{total} = E_1 + E_2 + E_3 = 10.0 + 200.0 + 10.0 = 220.0 \,\text{kJ}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the average crush force.**

$$
F_{avg} = \frac{E_{total}}{d} = \frac{220{,}000}{0.6} = 366{,}667 \,\text{N} \approx 366.7 \,\text{kN}
$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the Crush Force Efficiency.**

$$
CFE = \frac{F_{avg}}{F_{peak}} = \frac{366.7}{400} = 0.917 = 91.7\%
$$

**Engineering comment:**

A CFE of 91.7% is **excellent** — typical single-strength designs achieve 60–75%, while best-in-class graduated designs reach 90–95%. This profile is characteristic of a well-engineered crumple zone with proper load-path design. The structure absorbs 220 kJ of energy, which corresponds to a vehicle of mass $m$ at speed $v$ where $\tfrac{1}{2} m v^2 = 220{,}000$ J. For a 1500 kg vehicle, this would correspond to an initial speed of:

$$
v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 220{,}000}{1500}} = \sqrt{293.3} = 17.1 \,\text{m/s} \approx 61.6 \,\text{km/h}
$$

So this structure can absorb the kinetic energy of a 1500 kg vehicle impacting at 61.6 km/h, consistent with Euro NCAP full-frontal test conditions.

*(7 marks — 1.5 for each energy stage, 1 for total, 1 for average force, 1 for CFE, 1.5 for engineering comment.)*

---

##### Model Solution to Q. B (b):

**Role of Occupant Restraint Systems:**

The primary purpose of occupant restraint systems is to **prevent ejection** of the occupant from the vehicle, **decouple** the occupant's deceleration from the vehicle's crash pulse, and **distribute** the deceleration load across the strongest body regions (pelvis, chest, shoulders).

**Three-Belt System (3-Point Seatbelt):**

The 3-point belt is the **primary restraint** and the most effective single safety device in a vehicle. It operates through three sequential phases:

1. **Pre-tensioner phase (0 to 5 ms post-impact):** A pyrotechnic device fires, instantaneously removing slack from the belt webbing. This eliminates the "free-flight" interval during which the occupant would otherwise move forward at the original vehicle speed.

2. **Load-limiter phase (5 to 50 ms):** A load-limiter (a torsion bar in the retractor) allows controlled webbing payout once a threshold load (typically 4–6 kN) is reached. This **prevents belt-induced chest injury** by capping the upper-body load.

3. **Ride-down phase (50 to 100 ms):** With the belt paying out at controlled load, the upper torso decelerates over a longer stroke, reducing peak chest force. The occupant's chest, shoulders, and pelvis share the load through the wide webbing.

**Airbag Deployment Timeline:**

The airbag is a **supplemental restraint** that operates only in conjunction with the seatbelt (it is not a substitute). The deployment sequence is precisely timed:

1. **Crash sensor trigger (5 to 15 ms):** Accelerometers detect the deceleration and the airbag control unit (ACU) decides to fire based on algorithm logic. False-firing suppression algorithms prevent inadvertent deployment.

2. **Inflator ignition (15 to 25 ms):** A sodium-azide propellant ignites, generating nitrogen gas. The bag is folded in the steering wheel hub (driver) or dashboard (passenger).

3. **Bag deployment (25 to 35 ms):** The bag inflates and emerges from its housing at speeds of 100–300 m/s. It must reach full inflation before the occupant's head arrives at the steering wheel.

4. **Venting phase (40 to 100 ms):** The bag has calibrated vent holes that release gas in a controlled manner. This allows the bag to "cushion" the head as it penetrates into the bag, decelerating the head over a longer stroke.

5. **Deflation (100 to 200 ms):** The bag deflates fully, restoring the occupant's visibility and egress capability.

**Modification of the Crash Pulse:**

The vehicle's crash pulse is a high-magnitude, short-duration deceleration (e.g., 20 g for 80 ms). Without restraint, the occupant's pulse would be even more severe due to hard interior impact. With restraint:

- The **belt load-limiter** adds a controlled payout stroke (~30–50 mm) to the chest deceleration.
- The **airbag stroke** adds 100–150 mm of additional head deceleration distance.
- The **seat foam** adds another 20–30 mm of stroke.

The net effect is that the occupant's head and chest experience a **lower-magnitude, longer-duration** deceleration pulse compared to the vehicle's crash pulse. This is the essence of "ride-down" — the occupant decelerates gradually, with peak head accelerations limited to ~40–60 g (giving HIC < 1000) instead of the 100+ g that would occur from direct hard contact.

*(7 marks — 2 for 3-belt system explanation, 2 for airbag timeline, 2 for crash-pulse modification, 1 for synthesis.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning — Common Mark-Loss Patterns:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Many students write $v = 50$ m/s when $v = 50$ km/h is given. The correct conversion is $v = 50 \times (5/18) = 13.89$ m/s. **This single error can cascade through the entire problem, costing 4–5 marks.**
>
> 2. **Confusing $a_{avg}$ with $a_{peak}$:** The problem asks for *average* deceleration. Using the peak force instead of the average force is a common error. Always clarify which quantity is being calculated.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the safety cell mass:** In a more advanced model, the safety cell mass differs from the total vehicle mass. If the problem specifies cell mass, use that. The basic lumped-mass model assumes $m_{cell} = m_{vehicle}$.
>
> 4. **HIC formula misuse:** Students often forget the 2.5 power and write HIC as the simple average acceleration × time. The 2.5 power is a critical empirical scaling derived from biomechanical data and must be included.
>
> 5. **Mixing g and m/s² units:** Always state the conversion. Writing "16.4" without units, or writing "16.4 m/s²" when the answer should be in g, costs 0.5–1 mark.
>
> 6. **In Q.A(b), the diagram/sketch:** A textual description without a labelled sketch loses 1–2 marks. Even a simple block diagram with section labels is acceptable.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Crashworthiness**

- **Definition:** Crashworthiness = passive safety = protecting occupants *during* impact. Complementary to active safety (preventing the crash).
- **Three pillars:** Energy absorption (crumple), survival space (rigid cell), restraint (belts + airbags).
- **Core equations to memorize cold:**
  - $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$
  - $F_{avg} = m v^2 / (2d)$
  - $a_{avg} = v^2 / (2d)$
  - $t_{stroke} = 2d / v_i$ (constant decel)
  - HIC $\le 1000$ threshold
  - $SEA = \sigma_y / (\sqrt{3} \rho)$ for thin-walled tubes
- **Crush Force Efficiency (CFE):** $CFE = F_{avg} / F_{peak}$. Aim for $> 0.85$ in modern designs.
- **HIC interpretation:** HIC 240 = safe; HIC 1000 = 16% severe injury risk; HIC 2000 = critical.
- **Restraint timeline:** Belt pretensioner (5 ms) → Belt load-limit (5–50 ms) → Airbag fire (15 ms) → Airbag inflated (35 ms) → Airbag vented (50 ms+).
- **Material metric:** $\sigma_y / \rho$ is the *specific energy absorption* index. High-strength steel and aluminium-magnesium alloys are the workhorses.
- **Compatibility:** Crash compatibility depends on stiffness and mass ratio between partners; stiffness mismatch causes severe loading in the lighter vehicle.
- **Frontal vs side vs rear:** Frontal crumple zones (500–800 mm stroke), side impact has limited stroke (100–200 mm) so it relies on padding, intrusion beams, and curtain airbags, rear has moderate crumple plus fuel-system integrity.
- **Standard test protocols:** FMVSS 208 (USA, frontal), FMVSS 214 (USA, side), Euro NCAP (Europe, comprehensive), IIHS (USA, offset and small-overlap). These are referenced but not memorized; understand the test intent.
- **Numerical units to always carry through:** kg, m/s, m, N (or kN), J (or kJ), m/s² (or g). State all conversions explicitly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Per the revised Bloom's Taxonomy mapping for this course (PEMUT633), questions on Crashworthiness typically appear at the **Apply (Level 3)** and **Analyse (Level 4)** cognitive levels in Part A, and at the **Apply + Evaluate (Levels 3+5)** levels in Part B. Numerical problems requiring substitution into the core formulas (KE, deceleration, HIC) and design-comparison questions (graduated vs single-strength) are the most likely exam formats. Always end a numerical answer with a one-line engineering interpretation — it earns the "synthesis" half-mark that distinguishes a 14/14 from a 13/14 answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pole Crash Testing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pole Crash Testing

# Pole Crash Testing — A Comprehensive KTU 2024 Engineering Analysis

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> A **Pole Crash Test** (also called the **Pole Side Impact Test** or **Oblique Pole Test**) is a standardized full-scale vehicle crashworthiness evaluation procedure in which a stationary test vehicle, traveling at a precisely controlled velocity, is propelled laterally (typically at **90°** or **75°** to its longitudinal axis) into a rigid, vertical, cylindrical or prismatic fixed pole mounted on a non-deformable foundation. The test quantifies the structural integrity of the vehicle's side load path, the effectiveness of side-impact occupant protection systems (side airbags, curtain airbags, thorax airbags), and the biomechanical response of the Anthropomorphic Test Devices (ATDs) seated in the struck side.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Falling Tree on the Highway"**
> Imagine you are driving a small hatchback at 32 km/h on a single-lane road. Suddenly, a thick concrete utility pole — the kind used for high-tension lines — appears right in front of you. You cannot swerve because there is a ditch on one side and a cliff on the other. The only option is a direct lateral (sideways) collision with the pole. The pole does not move; it is rigidly anchored to the ground. The vehicle must absorb all the kinetic energy through its own crumpling side structure, side door beams, and the air-bag cushioning the occupant. The **Pole Crash Test** replicates exactly this nightmare scenario in a controlled laboratory environment — except the vehicle is propelled into the pole (not the pole into the vehicle) — and instrumented dummies inside measure how much "punishment" a human occupant would have sustained.

### Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Governing Protocol |
|---|---|---|
| **Impact Velocity** | **29 km/h to 32 km/h** | Euro NCAP / AIS-096 |
| **Pole Diameter (rigid cylinder)** | **254 mm ± 3 mm** | ECE R135, FMVSS 214 |
| **Pole Material** | **Steel, surface hardness ≥ 40 HRC** | Rigid, non-deformable |
| **Impact Angle** | **90° (perpendicular) or 75° (oblique)** | ECE R135 (75°), AIS-096 (90°) |
| **Pole Height above ground** | **1.5 m minimum exposed length** | Standardized |
| **Test Dummy (Driver Side)** | **ES-2re (WorldSID 50th percentile male)** | Euro NCAP post-2015 |
| **Test Dummy (Rear Passenger)** | **SID-IIs (5th percentile female)** | Euro NCAP post-2015 |
| **Total Vehicle Mass (incl. dummies + instruments)** | **Kerb mass + 100 kg (driver) + 75 kg (passenger)** | AIS-096 / ECE R135 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Why this test matters in KTU PEMUT633:**
> This test directly links to Module 3 outcomes on **Crashworthiness Engineering**, **Occupant Safety Standards**, and **Biomechanical Injury Criteria**. It complements frontal full-width barrier tests and side mobile deformable barrier (MDB) tests, but uniquely stresses the **lateral pole intrusion path** into the occupant compartment.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Sequence of a Pole Crash Test

The test follows a strictly sequenced protocol mandated by the regulatory standards. The logic steps below trace the engineering reasoning:

- **Step 1 — Vehicle Preparation & Instrumentation**
  - The vehicle is stripped to its production-equivalent mass configuration. All non-structural items (seats trimmed to bare frames if dummy-space allows, fuel tank filled with water to simulate full-load mass at a safe substitute density) are removed or substituted.
  - **Instrumentation:** Accelerometers are mounted at:
    - Vehicle **Center of Gravity (CoG)** — tri-axial
    - **B-pillar** at rocker level — tri-axial
    - **Front cross-member**, **rear cross-member**, and **engine block**
    - Dummy head, chest, pelvis, femur, tibia — for injury calculation
  - **High-speed cameras** (typically 1000 fps minimum) are positioned at lateral, oblique, and overhead angles.

- **Step 2 — Dummy Placement**
  - Driver-side: **ES-2re** (WorldSID) male dummy, seated per the **H-point machine** procedure.
  - Rear passenger side (struck side): **SID-IIs** 5th-percentile female dummy.
  - Both dummies wear calibrated clothing, shoes, and seatbelts (3-point retractable). Side and curtain airbags are armed.

- **Step 3 — Propulsion & Impact**
  - The vehicle is towed (or self-driven under remote control) and released so that it strikes the pole at the prescribed velocity.
  - On impact, the rigid pole acts as a **knife-edge concentrator**, penetrating the side door, B-pillar, and possibly the floor pan if the load path is weak.

- **Step 4 — Data Acquisition & Post-Crash Analysis**
  - Accelerometer channels are sampled at ≥ **10 kHz**, filtered per **CFC 60** (Channel Frequency Class) for head and neck, and **CFC 180** for chest and femur.
  - Post-test, intrusion measurements are taken using a **3D laser scanner** or manual depth gauge at defined grid points on the side door, B-pillar, and roof rail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale — "Why a Rigid Pole?"**
> A rigid pole simulates the worst-case scenario in real-world oblique impacts: a tree trunk, a lamp post, or a utility pole. Because the pole does not deform, **100% of the kinetic energy of the vehicle must be absorbed by the vehicle itself**. This is in stark contrast to a barrier test (where the barrier absorbs 30–50% of energy). The pole therefore imposes the **most stringent side-intrusion challenge** a production vehicle can face under homologation.

### 2.2 Crash Pulse Energy Equations

The fundamental energy balance governing the pole crash event is given by:

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v^2$$

Where:
- $E_{kinetic}$ = Initial kinetic energy of the vehicle at impact (Joules)
- $m$ = Effective mass of the vehicle (kg)
- $v$ = Impact velocity (m/s)

This kinetic energy is dissipated through three primary mechanisms:

$$E_{kinetic} = E_{structural} + E_{occupant} + E_{residual}$$

Where:
- $E_{structural}$ = Energy absorbed by plastic deformation of vehicle side structure
