- $E_{occupant}$ = Energy transferred to the dummies (must be minimized to reduce injury)
- $E_{residual}$ = Residual kinetic energy after rebound

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Kinetic Energy | $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Joules (J) | Initial impact energy budget |
| 2 | Average Crush Force | $F_{avg} = \dfrac{E_k}{d_{crush}}$ | Newtons (N) | Where $d_{crush}$ is total intrusion depth |
| 3 | Average Crash Deceleration | $a_{avg} = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot d_{crush}}$ | m/s² | Used to size energy absorbers |
| 4 | Crash Pulse Duration | $t_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot d_{crush}}{v}$ | seconds (s) | Typical: 50–80 ms for pole impact |
| 5 | Peak Intrusion Velocity | $v_{int} = v_0 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{m_{effective}}{m_{vehicle}}}$ | m/s | Effective mass method |
| 6 | HIC (Head Injury Criterion) | $HIC = \left[ \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | dimensionless | Limits: HIC₁₅ ≤ 700; HIC₃₆ ≤ 1000 |
| 7 | Nij (Neck Injury Criterion) | $N_{ij} = \dfrac{F_z}{F_{zc}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{yc}}$ | dimensionless | Limits: ≤ 1.0 at any time |
| 8 | Chest Deflection Limit | $\delta_{chest}$ | mm | ≤ 42 mm (ES-2re male, WorldSID) |
| 9 | Pelvis Acceleration | $a_{pelvis}$ | g | 3 ms exceedance ≤ 82 g (SID-IIs) |
| 10 | Viscous Criterion (VC) | $VC = v \cdot C$ | m/s | Thorax soft-tissue tolerance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Engineering Insight:** The pole crash pulse is characterized by an extremely high initial peak (often **30–50 g** within the first 10–15 ms) because the load is concentrated at a small contact area. This contrasts with the side MDB test, where the pulse is more distributed and the peak is typically **20–30 g** but sustained longer. **Designing a side structure that handles BOTH scenarios simultaneously** is one of the central challenges in modern crashworthiness engineering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Crash Pulse Decay and Intrusion Depth Relationship

**Given:**
- Vehicle effective mass $m = 1500$ kg (typical B-segment SUV)
- Impact velocity $v_0 = 32$ km/h $= 8.889$ m/s
- Pole is rigid; vehicle structure acts as a **plastic-elastic energy absorber** modeled as a bilinear spring.

**Assumed Bilinear Force-Displacement Characteristic:**

The side structure exhibits an initial elastic stiffness $k_1$ up to a yield displacement $x_y$, followed by plastic stiffness $k_2$ (typically $k_2 \ll k_1$). This bilinear model is the industry-standard simplification for thin-walled steel side rails.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Compute Initial Kinetic Energy:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot m \cdot v_0^2$$

Substituting values:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (8.889)^2$$

$$E_k = 750 \cdot 79.01 = 59{,}257.5 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Energy Balance in the Elastic Phase:**

The energy stored elastically (recoverable) until yield point:

$$E_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_1 \cdot x_y^2$$

For a typical side rail: $k_1 \approx 5 \times 10^5$ N/m, $x_y \approx 0.02$ m

$$E_{elastic} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 5 \times 10^5 \cdot (0.02)^2 = 100 \text{ J}$$

This is negligible compared to total kinetic energy, confirming that **elastic energy storage is minimal** in a pole crash.

**Step 3 — Energy Balance in the Plastic Phase:**

The plastic dissipation equals the area under the plastic portion of the F-x curve:

$$E_{plastic} = F_y \cdot (x_{total} - x_y) + \frac{1}{2} \cdot k_2 \cdot (x_{total} - x_y)^2$$

For simplicity, assuming rigid-perfectly-plastic ($k_2 = 0$):

$$E_{plastic} \approx F_y \cdot x_{total}$$

**Step 4 — Equating Energy:**

$$F_y \cdot x_{total} = E_k - E_{elastic}$$

$$x_{total} = \frac{E_k - E_{elastic}}{F_y}$$

Assuming side rail average crush force $F_y = 250{,}000$ N (typical for B-pillar + door beam combined):

$$x_{total} = \frac{59{,}257.5 - 100}{250{,}000} = 0.237 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5 — Intrusion Depth Limit Check:**

Standard intrusion limit at dummy H-point: **≤ 150 mm** (ECE R135). Our computed 237 mm **EXCEEDS** the limit, indicating the side structure is undersized — design remediation is required (add side airbag, thicker B-pillar, or foam insert).

### 3.2 Derivation: Head Injury Criterion (HIC) for a Triangular Pulse

**Given:** A dummy head acceleration history approximated as a triangular pulse with peak $a_{peak} = 80$ g (785 m/s²) at $t = 30$ ms, returning to zero at $t = 60$ ms.

**Step 1 — Acceleration Function:**

For $0 \leq t \leq 30$ ms:
$$a(t) = \frac{785}{0.030} \cdot t = 26{,}167 \cdot t \text{ m/s}^2$$

For $30 \leq t \leq 60$ ms:
$$a(t) = 785 - \frac{785}{0.030} (t - 0.030) = 26{,}167 \cdot (0.060 - t)$$

**Step 2 — Compute Time Integral Over Window:**

Take HIC₁₅ window: $t_1 = 0$, $t_2 = 0.060$ s (60 ms).

$$\int_0^{0.060} a(t)\, dt = \int_0^{0.030} 26{,}167 \cdot t\, dt + \int_{0.030}^{0.060} 26{,}167 (0.060 - t)\, dt$$

**Compute first integral:**

$$\int_0^{0.030} 26{,}167 \cdot t\, dt = 26{,}167 \cdot \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_0^{0.030} = 26{,}167 \cdot \frac{(0.030)^2}{2} = 11.775 \text{ m/s}$$

**Compute second integral (by symmetry, equals the first):**

$$\int_{0.030}^{0.060} 26{,}167 (0.060 - t)\, dt = 11.775 \text{ m/s}$$

**Total integral:**

$$\int_0^{0.060} a(t)\, dt = 23.55 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Average Acceleration Over Window:**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \cdot \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt = \frac{23.55}{0.060} = 392.5 \text{ m/s}^2$$

**Step 4 — Compute HIC:**

$$HIC = (\bar{a})^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)$$

$$HIC = (392.5)^{2.5} \cdot 0.060$$

Compute $(392.5)^{2.5}$:

$$(392.5)^2 = 154{,}056.25$$

$$(392.5)^{0.5} = 19.81$$

$$(392.5)^{2.5} = 154{,}056.25 \cdot 19.81 = 3{,}051{,}855$$

Therefore:

$$HIC = 3{,}051{,}855 \cdot 0.060 = 183{,}111$$

This dramatically exceeds the limit of **HIC₁₅ ≤ 700**, confirming catastrophic head injury risk — the airbag/curtain system MUST deploy and cushion the head before t = 30 ms contact.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Identification

| Failure Mode | Root Cause | Diagnostic Metric | Remediation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Excessive B-pillar intrusion | Undersized B-pillar section modulus | Intrusion at rocker level > 150 mm | Use ultra-high-strength steel (UHSS) hot-stamped B-pillar |
| Door beam collapse | Door beam too thin or wrong grade | Door mid-span intrusion > 200 mm | Increase beam wall thickness from 1.6 mm to 2.0 mm, use boron steel |
| Curtain airbag tear | Bag material porosity / vent blockage | High-pressure spike (> 50 kPa) | Add pressure relief vents, use silicone-coated nylon |
| Thorax compression | Inadequate thorax padding in door trim | Chest deflection > 42 mm | Add EVA foam + side airbag |
| Neck hyperflexion | Seatbelt pretensioner failure | Nij > 1.0 | Enable pyrotechnic pretensioner trigger |
| Floor pan buckling | Weak rocker panel | Vertical floor acceleration > 60 g | Reinforce rocker with tubular insert |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Pole Crash Test Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Pre-Test Setup] --> nodeB[Vehicle Preparation]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Pole Foundation Rig]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Dummy Calibration]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Instrumentation Mounting]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Propulsion System]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Seat and Belt Installation]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Test Execution]
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Impact at 32 km/h]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Data Acquisition System]
    nodeI --> nodeK[High Speed Cameras]
    nodeJ --> nodeL[Post Test Analysis]
    nodeK --> nodeL
    nodeL --> nodeM[Injury Criteria Evaluation]
    nodeL --> nodeN[Intrusion Mapping]
    nodeL --> nodeO[Structural Assessment]
    nodeM --> nodeP[Compliance Report]
    nodeN --> nodeP
    nodeO --> nodeP
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Regulatory Submission]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Energy Flow During Pole Impact

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeR[Vehicle Initial Kinetic Energy 59257 J] --> nodeS[Side Structure Plastic Deformation]
    nodeR --> nodeT[Door Beam Buckling]
    nodeR --> nodeU[B-Pillar Bending]
    nodeR --> nodeV[Roof Rail Compression]
    nodeR --> nodeW[Floor Pan Torsional Twist]
    nodeS --> nodeX[Occupant Compartment Intrusion]
    nodeT --> nodeX
    nodeU --> nodeX
    nodeV --> nodeX
    nodeW --> nodeX
    nodeX --> nodeY[Airbag Deployment Energy Absorption]
    nodeX --> nodeZ[Seatbelt Pretensioner Energy]
    nodeX --> nodeAA[Dummy Restraint Coupling]
    nodeY --> nodeAB[Final Deceleration Pulse]
    nodeZ --> nodeAB
    nodeAA --> nodeAB
    nodeAB --> nodeAC[Biomechanical Injury Output]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Test Execution Timeline

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T0 as Test Director
    participant T1 as Propulsion Motor
    participant T2 as Vehicle
    participant T3 as Pole Rig
    participant T4 as Data Logger
    participant T5 as Camera Array
    T0->>T1: Trigger Release Command
    T1->>T2: Release at Predefined Speed
    T2->>T3: Impact Event
    T3-->>T2: Reaction Force Pulse
    T2->>T4: Accelerometer Channels
    T2->>T5: Visual Capture
    T4->>T0: Acquired Waveforms
    T5->>T0: Video Frames
    T0->>T0: Process Injury Criteria
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The pole acts as a **kinematic constraint**; once contact occurs, the vehicle's CoG must rotate around the pole, transferring lateral velocity into angular momentum. This rotation is one reason why occupant kinematics in a pole crash differ substantially from those in a flat-barrier side impact — the dummy's head trajectory is both lateral AND vertical (downward into the door panel), increasing the risk of head-to-door contact if the curtain airbag does not deploy in time.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is a pole crash test, and how does it differ from a side mobile deformable barrier (MDB) test?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A pole crash test is a full-scale vehicle safety evaluation where the test vehicle is propelled laterally into a fixed, rigid cylindrical pole, simulating impacts with roadside trees or utility poles. It differs from the side MDB test in three key aspects:
1. The pole is **rigid and non-deformable**, whereas the MDB has a deformable aluminum honeycomb face — meaning the vehicle must absorb **100% of the kinetic energy** in a pole test.
2. The **load path is highly concentrated** along a small contact line in the pole test, causing severe local intrusion, versus a distributed load in the MDB test.
3. The pole test induces significant **vehicle yaw rotation** post-impact, which the MDB test does not. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List the standard impact velocity and pole diameter specified by AIS-096 for the pole side impact test.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
According to AIS-096 (Automotive Industry Standards, India, aligned with ECE R135):
- **Impact Velocity:** 32 km/h (8.889 m/s) ± 0.5 km/h **(1 mark)**
- **Pole Diameter:** 254 mm ± 3 mm **(1 mark)**
- **Pole Material:** Rigid steel, surface hardness not less than 40 HRC, mounted on a non-deformable foundation. **(1 mark)**

---

### Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

**Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the test setup, instrumentation, and procedure of a pole crash test as per ECE R135. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A test vehicle of mass 1400 kg strikes a rigid pole at 32 km/h. If the side structure behaves as a rigid-perfectly-plastic energy absorber with a mean crush force of 220 kN, calculate (i) initial kinetic energy, (ii) total crush distance, and (iii) average deceleration experienced by the vehicle. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
The test setup consists of a rigid cylindrical steel pole of **diameter 254 mm**, mounted vertically on a non-deformable reinforced concrete foundation such that at least **1.5 m** of pole height is exposed above the test surface. The test vehicle is propelled by a tow cable or self-propelled rig and released to impact the pole at a velocity of **32 ± 0.5 km/h** such that the **centerline of the pole strikes the driver's seating position** (H-point). Instrumentation includes: tri-axial accelerometers on the vehicle CoG, B-pillar, and engine block; load cells on the seatbelt; and pressure transducers in the airbags. **ES-2re** (driver) and **SID-IIs** (rear passenger) dummies are positioned per the H-point machine procedure. High-speed cameras at 1000 fps record the event. Post-test, intrusion is measured at standard grid points. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $m = 1400$ kg
- $v_0 = 32$ km/h $= 8.889$ m/s
- $F_y = 220$ kN $= 220{,}000$ N

**(i) Initial Kinetic Energy:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 1400 \cdot (8.889)^2$$

$$E_k = 700 \cdot 79.01 = 55{,}307 \text{ J}$$

**(ii) Total Crush Distance:**

Using $E_k = F_y \cdot x_{crush}$:

$$x_{crush} = \frac{E_k}{F_y} = \frac{55{,}307}{220{,}000} = 0.2514 \text{ m} = 251.4 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Average Deceleration:**

$$a_{avg} = \frac{v_0^2}{2 \cdot x_{crush}} = \frac{(8.889)^2}{2 \cdot 0.2514} = \frac{79.01}{0.5028} = 157.14 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Converting to g: $a_{avg} = 157.14 / 9.81 = 16.02$ g

**Results:**
- $E_k = 55.31$ kJ **(2 marks)**
- $x_{crush} = 251.4$ mm **(2 marks)**
- $a_{avg} = 16.02$ g **(3 marks)**

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the role of side airbags, curtain airbags, and seatbelt pretensioners in reducing occupant injuries during a pole crash test. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A dummy's head strikes the inflated curtain airbag with a relative velocity of 7 m/s. If the airbag compresses uniformly over a distance of 90 mm to bring the head to rest, calculate the average deceleration and the corresponding HIC₃₆ value, assuming a triangular acceleration profile. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (a):**
During a pole crash, the lateral intrusion of the side door and B-pillar into the occupant compartment creates direct impact risks for the head, thorax, and pelvis of the struck-side occupant. **Curtain airbags** deploy from the roof rail within 15–25 ms and provide a cushioned barrier between the head and the intruding structure, reducing the risk of skull fracture and traumatic brain injury (TBI). **Side thorax airbags** deploy from the seat or door and spread the load on the rib cage, reducing chest deflection below the 42 mm WorldSID limit. **Seatbelt pretensioners** use pyrotechnic charges to instantly retract slack in the lap and shoulder belt, preventing the occupant's torso from "submarining" under the belt or moving too far laterally before airbag deployment. Together, these systems form a **layered protection strategy** that reduces the HIC, chest deflection, and Nij values to within regulatory limits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- $v = 7$ m/s
- $d = 90$ mm $= 0.090$ m
- Triangular acceleration profile
- Compute over HIC₃₆ window of 36 ms

**(i) Average Deceleration:**

$$a_{avg} = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot d} = \frac{(7)^2}{2 \cdot 0.090} = \frac{49}{0.180} = 272.22 \text{ m/s}^2$$

In g: $a_{avg} = 272.22 / 9.81 = 27.75$ g

**(ii) Crash Pulse Duration:**

$$t_c = \frac{2 \cdot d}{v} = \frac{2 \cdot 0.090}{7} = 0.0257 \text{ s} = 25.7 \text{ ms}$$

Since $t_c = 25.7$ ms < 36 ms, the full pulse fits within the HIC₃₆ window.

**(iii) HIC₃₆ Calculation:**

For a triangular pulse of peak $a_{peak} = 2 \cdot a_{avg} = 544.44$ m/s² (since triangular profile has peak equal to twice the average for the duration considered):

$$\bar{a} = a_{avg} = 272.22 \text{ m/s}^2$$

$$\Delta t = 0.0257 \text{ s}$$

$$HIC = (\bar{a})^{2.5} \cdot \Delta t = (272.22)^{2.5} \cdot 0.0257$$

Compute $(272.22)^{2.5}$:

$$(272.22)^2 = 74{,}103.7$$
$$(272.22)^{0.5} = 16.50$$
$$(272.22)^{2.5} = 74{,}103.7 \cdot 16.50 = 1{,}222{,}711$$

$$HIC = 1{,}222{,}711 \cdot 0.0257 = 31{,}424$$

**Result:** HIC = **31,424**, which dramatically exceeds HIC₃₆ = 1000 limit. This shows that even with airbag cushioning at 90 mm compression, head injury risk remains — the airbag must have multiple venting stages or the cushion distance must be increased to ≥ 200 mm. **(7 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout**
> **Common mistakes students make in pole crash questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversions:** Always convert km/h to m/s before any energy calculation. $v$ (m/s) $= v$ (km/h) $\times \frac{5}{18}$.
> 2. **Confusing pole test with frontal test:** The pole test does NOT involve engine block absorption; the side structure (B-pillar, door beam, rocker) does the work. Writing "crumple zone in the front" will lose marks.
> 3. **Using wrong dummy names:** ES-2re and SID-IIs are the modern names. Older references used EUROSID-1 or BIOSID — these are obsolete and will be penalized.
> 4. **Ignoring HIC window:** Always specify HIC₁₅ (15 ms) or HIC₃₆ (36 ms) and justify which one applies based on the crash pulse duration.
> 5. **Forgetting to mention the rigid foundation of the pole:** Examiners look for this — the pole is NOT just a standalone cylinder; it is bolted to a massive non-deformable concrete plinth.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Pole Crash Test Definition:** Lateral impact of a moving vehicle into a fixed, rigid cylindrical pole — simulates tree/pole impacts. **(Definition must include "rigid, vertical, cylindrical").**
- **Standard Velocity:** 32 km/h (AIS-096 / ECE R135); Euro NCAP uses 29 km/h.
- **Pole Diameter:** 254 mm ± 3 mm.
- **Impact Angle:** 75° (ECE R135) or 90° (AIS-096).
- **Dummies:** Driver = ES-2re (WorldSID 50th male); Rear passenger = SID-IIs (5th female).
- **Key Energy Equation:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$.
- **Intrusion Limit:** ≤ 150 mm at dummy H-point (ECE R135); ≤ 250 mm at door mid-span (some AIS variants).
- **HIC Limit:** HIC₁₅ ≤ 700; HIC₃₆ ≤ 1000.
- **Chest Deflection Limit:** ≤ 42 mm (ES-2re); ≤ 38 mm (SID-IIs).
- **Neck Nij Limit:** ≤ 1.0 at all times.
- **Energy Absorption Path:** Door → B-pillar → Rocker → Roof rail (the "ladder frame" of side load path).
- **Key Remediations:** Hot-stamped B-pillars, boron-steel door beams, foam-in-door trim, curtain + thorax airbags, pretensioners.
- **Distinguishing Feature:** Pole test = rigid obstacle, concentrated load, vehicle yaws post-impact; MDB test = deformable barrier, distributed load, no significant yaw.
- **Biomechanism Concern:** Head trajectory is **lateral + downward** in pole test — curtain airbag must cover the upper door AND roof rail.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Quick-Recall Mnemonic — "P-O-L-E":**
> **P** = Pole is rigid (254 mm cylinder)
> **O** = Occupant protection must handle 100% energy
> **L** = Load path is B-pillar + rocker + roof rail
> **E** = ES-2re driver dummy, SID-IIs rear dummy

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Near Crash Testing
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Near Crash Testing

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Near Crash Testing

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> *Near Crash Testing* is a category of vehicle safety evaluation that replicates real-world traffic scenarios where a vehicle comes perilously close to a collision with another vehicle, pedestrian, cyclist, or fixed obstacle, but either **avoids the crash entirely** through active intervention (braking, steering, or both) or experiences a **very low-severity contact**. It is primarily used to validate the performance of **Active Safety Systems** and **Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS)** such as Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB), Forward Collision Warning (FCW), Lane Keep Assist (LKA), and Electronic Stability Control (ESC).

### 1.1 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of it this way (The "Soccer Goalkeeper" Analogy):**
> Imagine a soccer goalkeeper facing a penalty kick. Crash testing is like the moment the ball *hits* the net — the impact has already happened. **Near Crash Testing**, however, is the milliseconds *before* the strike — the dramatic dive, the sudden lateral motion, the precise positioning of the gloves. We measure *how well the goalkeeper reacted, how quickly they moved, and how close the ball came to scoring*.
> In vehicles, near-crash tests measure how the car's "reflexes" (sensors, controllers, brakes, steering actuators) perform in the *split second before* an accident could have occurred. It is the science of measuring **near-misses**.

### 1.2 Why Near Crash Testing Matters

| Aspect | Conventional Crash Test | Near Crash Test |
|---|---|---|
| **Severity** | Full impact (high kinetic energy) | Pre-impact or sub-injury-threshold contact |
| **Primary Focus** | Passive safety (crumple zones, airbags) | Active safety (AEB, FCW, ESC) |
| **Measured Outcome** | Injury metrics (HIC, Nij) | Intervention timing, minimum clearance, deceleration onset |
| **Dummies Used** | Anthropomorphic (Hybrid III, THOR) | Surrogate targets (soft, articulated, balloon) |
| **Speed Range** | Up to **80 km/h** full frontal | Typically **10–60 km/h** |
| **Primary Standards** | ECE R94, R95, FMVSS 208 | ISO 22733, Euro NCAP AEB protocols, ISO 15622 |

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics in Near Crash Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bold Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Board Favourites)**
> - **Time-To-Collision (TTC):** The time remaining before two objects collide if their current trajectories and speeds are maintained. **TTC threshold for warning = 2.7 s**, **braking threshold = 1.5 s** (typical Euro NCAP values).
> - **Minimum Distance (d_min):** The closest spatial separation achieved between the test vehicle and target during the maneuver.
> - **Speed Reduction (Δv):** The reduction in relative velocity achieved by the active system before the potential impact.
> - **Jerk (j):** The rate of change of deceleration, measured in **m/s³**. Human comfort threshold ≈ **8.5 m/s³**.
> - **Brake Application Time (t_brake):** Time from warning issuance to measurable brake pad contact.
> - **ISO 15622 (Adaptive Cruise Control) reference speed = 120 km/h** for highway scenarios.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic of a Near Crash Test

A near-crash test is decomposed into a strict sequence of operational phases. Each phase must be measurable, reproducible, and quantifiable.

**Step 1 — Scenario Definition**
- Define the traffic conflict geometry: car-to-car rear-end, car-to-pedestrian, car-to-cyclist, lane-change near miss, or intersection conflict.
- Assign **initial subject vehicle (SV) speed**, **target speed**, **lateral offset**, and **overlap percentage**.

**Step 2 — Target Mobilization**
- Deploy a representative target: **Global Vehicle Target (GVT)**, **Electric Vehicle Target (EVT)**, **Soft Pedestrian Target (SPT)**, or **Bicycle Target (BioRider)**.
- Targets must be radar-reflective, Lidar-classifiable, and camera-recognizable to the ADAS sensors.

**Step 3 — Subject Vehicle Approach**
- The subject vehicle approaches the target at the prescribed closing speed using a **steering robot** and **pedal robot** for repeatability.
- Onboard ADAS sensors (radar, camera, LiDAR fusion) detect and classify the target.

**Step 4 — Active Intervention Phase**
- The ADAS controller computes **Time-To-Collision (TTC)**, **predicted minimum distance**, and **driver attentiveness**.
- If TTC falls below a calibrated threshold, the system issues a **warning**, applies **automatic braking**, or performs **evasive steering**.

**Step 5 — Outcome Measurement**
- The test outcome is graded by:
  - Did a collision occur? (Yes/No)
  - Final relative speed at impact (or separation achieved).
  - Warning issuance timing.
  - Maximum deceleration achieved.
  - Driver override capability.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Step**
> Each step exists because automotive OEMs must prove to regulatory bodies (Euro NCAP, NHTSA, IIHS) that their ADAS will function in **real-world edge cases**, not just in sterile laboratory crashes. A near-crash test is the controlled proxy for these edge cases.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORIZE FOR ESE**

| # | Formula / Quantity | Symbolic Expression | Standard Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Time-To-Collision** | $TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}$ | s (seconds) | Time remaining before impact if no action taken |
| 2 | **Relative Velocity** | $\Delta v = v_{SV} - v_{T}$ | m/s | Closing speed between subject and target |
| 3 | **Minimum Clearance Distance** | $d_{min} = d_{0} - v_{rel} \cdot t_{react} - \dfrac{1}{2}\,a_{max}\,t_{brake}^{2}$ | m | Closest gap achieved during intervention |
| 4 | **Required Deceleration** | $a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{rel}^{2}}{2 \cdot d_{gap}}$ | m/s² | Deceleration needed to avoid impact |
| 5 | **Deceleration achieved** | $a = \dfrac{v_{f}^{2} - v_{i}^{2}}{2 \cdot s}$ | m/s² | Actual braking performance of the system |
| 6 | **Jerk (rate of change of decel)** | $j = \dfrac{da}{dt} = \dfrac{d^{2}v}{dt^{3}}$ | m/s³ | Comfort metric; $j \le 8.5$ m/s³ typical |
| 7 | **Kinetic Energy Dissipated** | $\Delta KE = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2})$ | J | Energy removed by braking system |
| 8 | **Stopping Distance** | $s = v\,t_{react} + \dfrac{v^{2}}{2\,a_{max}}$ | m | Total distance to halt from speed v |
| 9 | **Euro NCAP AEB Speed Reduction** | $\Delta v = v_{SV,initial} - v_{SV,final}$ | m/s or km/h | Key scoring metric for AEB tests |
| 10 | **Driver Override Reaction Time** | $t_{react,human} = 0.8\text{–}1.5$ | s | Standard human response latency (ISO 21902) |
| 11 | **Perception–Reaction (PIARC)** | $t_{pr} = 2.5$ | s | Standard road-design reaction time |
| 12 | **Gap acceptance ratio** | $G = \dfrac{d_{actual}}{d_{required}}$ | dimensionless | $G \ge 1$ indicates safe gap |

> **Notation Convention:**
> $v_{SV}$ = Subject Vehicle speed, $v_{T}$ = Target speed, $d_{0}$ = initial gap, $a_{max}$ = maximum achievable deceleration, $m$ = vehicle mass, $s$ = distance covered.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Development:** Used by Bosch, Continental, ZF, and Mobileye to tune AEB intervention thresholds.
- **Regulatory Certification:** Euro NCAP's **AEB City**, **AEB Interurban**, **AEB Pedestrian**, and **AEB Cyclist** protocols are *all* near-crash test families.
- **Insurance Grading:** IIHS *Top Safety Pick+* awards require passing near-crash AEB scenarios.
- **Autonomous Vehicle Validation:** Self-driving stacks (Waymo, Cruise, Tesla FSD) are validated on millions of near-crash simulation scenarios before road deployment.
- **Academic Research:** Enables biomechanics studies on sub-injury-threshold whiplash, muscle activation, and occupant kinematics in pre-crash braking.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Time-To-Collision (TTC) Kinematic Relationship

**Problem Statement:** A subject vehicle approaches a stationary target at **60 km/h**. A pedestrian target crosses at **5 km/h** at a lateral distance of **3 m**. Derive the instantaneous TTC and the minimum required deceleration to avoid contact.

**Given:**
- $v_{SV} = 60$ km/h $= 16.67$ m/s
- $v_{T} = 5$ km/h $= 1.39$ m/s
- Initial longitudinal gap $d_0 = 25$ m
- Maximum available deceleration $a_{max} = 8$ m/s² (typical passenger car AEB)
- Human reaction time $t_{react} = 1.2$ s

**Step 1: Convert to SI units and compute relative velocity.**

$$
\Delta v = v_{SV} - v_{T} = 16.67 - 1.39 = 15.28 \text{ m/s}
$$

Here we subtract target velocity because the target is moving in a *partially* closing direction (pedestrian stepping into the path).

**Step 2: Compute initial Time-To-Collision.**

$$
TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v} = \dfrac{25}{15.28} = 1.636 \text{ s}
$$

This value is **below** the typical AEB braking threshold of 1.5–2.0 s, so the system *must intervene*.

**Step 3: Compute stopping distance required from current speed.**

$$
s_{stop} = v_{SV}\cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{SV}^{2}}{2\,a_{max}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
s_{stop} = (16.67)(1.2) + \dfrac{(16.67)^{2}}{2(8)}
$$

$$
s_{stop} = 20.004 + \dfrac{277.89}{16} = 20.004 + 17.368 = 37.37 \text{ m}
$$

**Step 4: Since the stopping distance (37.37 m) exceeds the available gap (25 m), a collision is unavoidable with pure braking.** The required deceleration for *just* stopping within 25 m is:

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{v_{SV}^{2}}{2\,(d_{0} - v_{SV}\,t_{react})} = \dfrac{(16.67)^{2}}{2\,(25 - 20.004)}
$$

$$
a_{req} = \dfrac{277.89}{2 \times 4.996} = \dfrac{277.89}{9.992} = 27.81 \text{ m/s}^{2}
$$

**Step 5: Compute residual impact speed assuming only AEB braking is applied at $a_{max}=8$ m/s².**

Distance available for braking after reaction lag:

$$
d_{brake} = d_0 - v_{SV}\cdot t_{react} = 25 - 20.004 = 4.996 \text{ m}
$$

Final velocity at impact:

$$
v_{f}^{2} = v_{SV}^{2} - 2\,a_{max}\,d_{brake} = (16.67)^{2} - 2(8)(4.996)
$$

$$
v_{f}^{2} = 277.89 - 79.94 = 197.95
$$

$$
v_{f} = \sqrt{197.95} = 14.07 \text{ m/s} = 50.65 \text{ km/h}
$$

**Step 6: Compute impact energy dissipation.**

Assuming vehicle mass $m = 1500$ kg:

$$
\Delta KE = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2}) = \dfrac{1}{2}(1500)(277.89 - 197.95)
$$

$$
\Delta KE = 750 \times 79.94 = 59{,}955 \text{ J} \approx 60 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Conclusion:** Even with maximum AEB intervention, the impact still occurs at 50.65 km/h. This scenario would **FAIL** the Euro NCAP AEB Pedestrian protocol, demonstrating why *higher closing speeds* require *evasive steering* in addition to braking.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Jerk-Limited Braking Profile (Comfort Optimization)

**Problem Statement:** A near-crash maneuver requires a deceleration ramp from 0 to **6 m/s²** over **0.4 s**. Compute the jerk, verify it is within human comfort limits, and determine the time and distance to reach full braking.

**Step 1: Define jerk as the rate of change of acceleration.**

Assuming linear acceleration ramp:

$$
j = \dfrac{\Delta a}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{6 - 0}{0.4} = 15 \text{ m/s}^{3}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with comfort threshold.**

The standard comfort threshold (from occupant biomechanics literature) is:

$$
j_{comfort} = 8.5 \text{ m/s}^{3}
$$

Since $j = 15$ m/s³ $> j_{comfort}$, the maneuver is *uncomfortable* and would cause occupant forward displacement potentially leading to **whiplash** in sub-injury collisions.

**Step 3: Determine corrected ramp time for comfort-compliant jerk.**

$$
t_{ramp,corrected} = \dfrac{a_{max}}{j_{comfort}} = \dfrac{6}{8.5} = 0.706 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 4: Distance covered during the ramp phase (trapezoidal velocity profile).**

$$
s_{ramp} = v_{i}\,t_{ramp} + \dfrac{1}{2}\,a_{max}\,t_{ramp}^{2} - \dfrac{1}{6}\,j\,t_{ramp}^{3}
$$

For $v_i = 16.67$ m/s and $a_{max} = 6$ m/s²:

$$
s_{ramp} = (16.67)(0.706) + \dfrac{1}{2}(6)(0.706)^2 - \dfrac{1}{6}(8.5)(0.706)^3
$$

$$
s_{ramp} = 11.77 + 1.495 - 0.498 = 12.77 \text{ m}
$$

This derivation is the basis of **Adaptive Brake Assist** tuning in modern vehicles.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Test Type vs. Kinematic Outcome

| Test Scenario | SV Speed | Target | Threshold Metric | Pass Criterion |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **AEB City (low speed)** | 10–30 km/h | Stationary GVT | $v_{f} = 0$ | Full stop achieved |
| **AEB Inter-Urban** | 30–80 km/h | Slow GVT (20 km/h) | $\Delta v \ge 30$ km/h | Speed reduced by ≥30 km/h |
| **AEB Pedestrian** | 20–60 km/h | Walking dummy (5 km/h) | $d_{min} \ge 0.2$ m | No contact or sub-threshold |
| **AEB Cyclist (Dooring)** | 25–45 km/h | Bicycle dummy | $v_{f} < v_{SV}/2$ | Significant speed reduction |
| **Lane Support (LSS)** | 60–80 km/h | Line markings | Lateral deviation $\le 0.3$ m | Vehicle stays in lane |
| **Evasive Maneuver (Moose Test)** | 60–90 km/h | Cones/pylons | Tip-over $a_{lat}$ | Stability maintained |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Near-Crash Test Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Test Scenario Definition] --> B[Target Mobilization System]
    B --> C[Subject Vehicle Approach]
    C --> D[Onboard Sensor Stack]
    D --> D1[Radar]
    D --> D2[Camera]
    D --> D3[LiDAR]
    D1 --> E[Sensor Fusion ECU]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    E --> F[TTC Computation]
    F --> G{TTC Threshold Check}
    G -->|TTC > 2.7 s| H[No Intervention - Monitor Only]
    G -->|1.5 < TTC < 2.7| I[FCW Warning Issued]
    G -->|TTC < 1.5| J[Full AEB Activation]
    I --> K[Driver Reaction Window]
    K --> L{Driver Response Detected}
    L -->|Yes| M[Manual Override]
    L -->|No| J
    J --> N[Brake Actuator Command]
    J --> O[Steering Actuator Command]
    N --> P[Vehicle Deceleration]
    O --> Q[Evasive Trajectory]
    P --> R[Outcome Measurement]
    Q --> R
    R --> S{Collision Avoided}
    S -->|Yes| T[PASS - Record Delta-v and d_min]
    S -->|No| U[FAIL - Record Impact Speed]
    M --> R
    H --> R
```

**Block-Level Interpretation of the Flow:**

1. **Test Scenario Definition Block** sets the geometry, speeds, and target type.
2. **Target Mobilization Block** positions the GVT / pedestrian / cyclist dummy.
3. **Sensor Stack Block** (Radar + Camera + LiDAR) detects and classifies the target.
4. **Sensor Fusion ECU Block** fuses raw sensor data into a unified object list.
5. **TTC Computation Block** continuously evaluates closing speed and predicted contact time.
6. **Threshold Check Block** is the decision tree — the heart of the ADAS logic.
7. **Brake and Steering Actuator Blocks** execute the physical intervention.
8. **Outcome Measurement Block** captures the final gap, residual speed, and any contact.

### 4.2 Euro NCAP AEB Test Rating Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Near-Crash Test Execution] --> Meas[Measure Final Velocity v_f and Impact Status]
    Meas --> Cond1{Contact Occurred?}
    Cond1 -->|No Contact| Score1[Full Score Awarded]
    Cond1 -->|Contact Below Threshold| Score2[Partial Score Based on Delta-v]
    Cond1 -->|Contact Above Threshold| Score3[Reduced Score]
    Score1 --> End[Euro NCAP Star Rating Aggregation]
    Score2 --> End
    Score3 --> End
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
> *Define Near Crash Testing. List any four active safety systems validated through this testing methodology.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Near Crash Testing is a vehicle evaluation procedure that replicates traffic scenarios where a vehicle narrowly avoids a collision or experiences a very low-severity impact, primarily used to validate Active Safety and ADAS performance. *(1 mark)*

Four active safety systems validated through near-crash testing:
1. Automatic Emergency Braking (AEB) *(0.5)*
2. Forward Collision Warning (FCW) *(0.5)*
3. Electronic Stability Control (ESC) *(0.5)*
4. Lane Keep Assist (LKA) / Lane Departure Warning *(0.5)*

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
> *What is Time-To-Collision (TTC)? Write its formula and state the typical AEB intervention threshold values for warning and braking.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
Time-To-Collision is the calculated time remaining before two objects would collide if they continue at their current relative velocity and trajectory. *(1 mark)*

$$
TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v}
$$

*(1 mark for formula)*

Typical Euro NCAP thresholds: **FCW warning issued at TTC ≈ 2.7 s**; **automatic braking initiated at TTC ≈ 1.5 s**. *(1 mark)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Question A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]**
>
> *(a)* Explain in detail the operational phases of a typical Near Crash Test. Discuss the role of surrogate targets such as the Global Vehicle Target (GVT) and Soft Pedestrian Target (SPT). *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A subject vehicle travelling at 72 km/h approaches a stationary target with an initial gap of 30 m. The AEB system has a reaction time of 0.4 s and a maximum deceleration of 7.5 m/s². Calculate:
> (i) the initial Time-To-Collision,
> (ii) the residual impact speed, and
> (iii) the energy dissipated by the braking system. Assume vehicle mass = 1400 kg. *(7 marks)*

### 5.3 Model Solutions

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Operational Phases (7 marks)

The operational phases of a near-crash test are:

1. **Scenario Definition (1 mark):** Specification of geometry (longitudinal/lateral), speeds, overlap percentage, and target type. Standardized by Euro NCAP AEB protocols.

2. **Target Mobilization (1 mark):** The target is propelled at the prescribed speed using a **mobile platform** (e.g., Platform Launcher used in Euro NCAP). The GVT must be radar/LiDAR/camera-recognizable. The SPT simulates pedestrian dielectric signature for radar.

3. **Subject Vehicle Approach (1 mark):** The test vehicle is driven (or robot-piloted) towards the target. Robotic steering and pedal actuators ensure **±0.1 km/h speed accuracy**.

4. **Active Intervention (2 marks):** The ADAS controller computes TTC, issues FCW, and applies AEB if thresholds are crossed. Brake pressure is ramped jerk-limited to ~8.5 m/s³.

5. **Outcome Measurement (2 marks):** Final relative speed, contact status, and minimum distance are measured using high-speed cameras, GPS, and onboard CAN-bus logging. The result is graded per Euro NCAP scoring matrix.

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $v_{SV} = 72$ km/h $= 20$ m/s; $d_0 = 30$ m; $t_{react} = 0.4$ s; $a_{max} = 7.5$ m/s²; $m = 1400$ kg.

**(i) Initial Time-To-Collision (2 marks):**

$$
TTC = \dfrac{\Delta d}{\Delta v} = \dfrac{30}{20 - 0} = 1.5 \text{ s}
$$

**(ii) Residual impact speed (3 marks):**

Distance remaining for braking after reaction lag:

$$
d_{brake} = d_0 - v_{SV} \cdot t_{react} = 30 - (20)(0.4) = 30 - 8 = 22 \text{ m}
$$

Applying kinematic equation $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2\,a_{max}\,d_{brake}$:

$$
v_f^{2} = (20)^{2} - 2(7.5)(22) = 400 - 330 = 70
$$

$$
v_f = \sqrt{70} = 8.367 \text{ m/s} = 30.12 \text{ km/h}
$$

**(iii) Energy dissipated (2 marks):**

$$
\Delta KE = \dfrac{1}{2}\,m\,(v_{i}^{2} - v_{f}^{2}) = \dfrac{1}{2}(1400)(400 - 70)
$$

$$
\Delta KE = 700 \times 330 = 231{,}000 \text{ J} = 231 \text{ kJ}
$$

---

> **Question B. [Alternative ESE Choice – 14 Marks]**
>
> *(a)* With neat sketches, explain the architecture of a typical Euro NCAP AEB test setup. Discuss how Time-To-Collision (TTC) and jerk are used as key performance indicators. *(7 marks)*
>
> *(b)* A vehicle travelling at 50 km/h applies AEB with a constant deceleration of 5 m/s². The driver reaction time is 1.5 s.
> (i) Compute the total stopping distance.
> (ii) If the target is at 28 m, determine the impact speed.
> (iii) Comment on whether this AEB intervention is "comfort-compliant" if the deceleration ramp-up occurs over 0.3 s. *(7 marks)*

### 5.4 Model Solution to Q.B

#### Q.B(a) — Euro NCAP AEB Architecture (7 marks)

The Euro NCAP AEB test setup consists of:

- **Test Track:** Flat, dry asphalt with µ ≥ 0.7 friction coefficient.
- **Target Carrier:** A remotely-controlled platform propels the GVT or pedestrian dummy.
- **Subject Vehicle:** Equipped with data acquisition (CAN-bus, GPS RTK, IMU).
- **DGPS Base Station:** Provides ±0.02 m positional accuracy.
- **VBOX / Oxford Technical Solutions:** Records speed, position, and time at 100 Hz.

TTC is the primary controller input. It is computed as $\Delta d / \Delta v$ in the prediction horizon of the sensor fusion module. The controller actuates braking when TTC < 1.5 s. *(2 marks)*

Jerk ($j = da/dt$) is monitored to ensure occupant comfort. ISO 2631 and biomechanics studies indicate that $j \le 8.5$ m/s³ minimizes whiplash risk. *(2 marks)*

#### Q.B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $v_{SV} = 50$ km/h $= 13.89$ m/s; $a_{max} = 5$ m/s²; $t_{react} = 1.5$ s; $d_0 = 28$ m; $t_{ramp} = 0.3$ s.

**(i) Total stopping distance (3 marks):**

$$
s = v_{SV}\cdot t_{react} + \dfrac{v_{SV}^{2}}{2\,a_{max}}
$$

$$
s = (13.89)(1.5) + \dfrac{(13.89)^{2}}{2(5)} = 20.835 + \dfrac{192.93}{10} = 20.835 + 19.293 = 40.13 \text{ m}
$$

**(ii) Impact speed at $d_0 = 28$ m (2 marks):**

Distance available for braking after reaction time:

$$
d_{brake} = 28 - 20.835 = 7.165 \text{ m}
$$

$$
v_f^{2} = v_i^{2} - 2\,a\,d_{brake} = 192.93 - 2(5)(7.165) = 192.93 - 71.65 = 121.28
$$

$$
v_f = \sqrt{121.28} = 11.01 \text{ m/s} \approx 39.64 \text{ km/h}
$$

**(iii) Comfort check (2 marks):**

$$
j = \dfrac{a_{max}}{t_{ramp}} = \dfrac{5}{0.3} = 16.67 \text{ m/s}^{3}
$$

Since $j = 16.67$ m/s³ $> j_{comfort} = 8.5$ m/s³, **the intervention is NOT comfort-compliant**. The ramp time should be at least $5/8.5 = 0.588$ s.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s:** Almost 50% of KTU students lose 1 mark by writing $v = 50$ directly into $v^2/(2a)$. Always convert.
> - **Using wrong sign conventions:** Deceleration must be entered as a *positive* quantity in $v_f^2 = v_i^2 - 2ad$ — the minus sign is already in the formula.
> - **Ignoring reaction time:** Students often compute stopping distance *without* the $v \cdot t_{react}$ term. This is the most common 1-mark deduction in Q.A(b).
> - **Mis-defining TTC:** TTC is *not* the actual time of impact — it is the *predicted* time if no intervention occurs.
> - **Skipping the comfort jerk check:** In any AEB numerical, always compute $j$ and compare to 8.5 m/s³. Examiners reward this.

---

### 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST**

- **Definition:** Near Crash Testing evaluates active safety systems in pre-impact or sub-threshold collision scenarios.
- **Primary Systems Tested:** AEB, FCW, ESC, LKA, ACC, AEB Pedestrian, AEB Cyclist.
- **TTC Formula:** $TTC = \Delta d / \Delta v$. Typical thresholds: 2.7 s (warning), 1.5 s (braking).
- **Stopping Distance Formula:** $s = v\,t_{react} + v^2/(2a_{max})$ — *always include both terms*.
- **Jerk Comfort Threshold:** $j \le 8.5$ m/s³.
- **Standard Reaction Time:** $t_{react,human} = 1.0$ to $1.5$ s; PIARC standard = 2.5 s.
- **Energy Dissipated:** $\Delta KE = \frac{1}{2} m (v_i^2 - v_f^2)$.
- **Test Targets:** GVT, EVT (Euro NCAP), Soft Pedestrian 4A/4B, BioRider cyclist.
- **Closing Speeds:** AEB City ≤ 30 km/h; AEB Inter-Urban 30–80 km/h.
- **Euro NCAP Scoring:** Based on speed reduction ($\Delta v$), contact status, and warning timing.
- **Standards to Quote:** ISO 22733, ISO 15622, ISO 11270, Euro NCAP AEB Test Protocol v3.0+.
- **Unit Conversions:** 1 km/h = 0.2778 m/s; 1 g = 9.81 m/s².
- **Examiner's Pet Question:** "Compare and contrast crash testing with near crash testing" — memorize the table from Section 1.2.
- **Numerical Trap:** When the computed stopping distance exceeds the available gap, the system has *failed* the test even if some braking occurs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vehicle to Vehicle Impact
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Vehicle to Vehicle Impact

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact** is a category of vehicular collision in which two independently moving motor vehicles come into direct mechanical contact, resulting in a sudden exchange of momentum, dissipation of kinetic energy through structural deformation, and generation of transient dynamic forces (crash pulse) on the occupants.

In the context of KTU's *Vehicle Performance and Testing* syllabus, vehicle-to-vehicle impact analysis is treated as a **one-dimensional impulsive collision problem** governed by:

1. The **Principle of Conservation of Linear Momentum** (always valid during the short impact interval because external impulse is negligible).
2. The **Coefficient of Restitution** $e$ (Newton's Experimental Law), which quantifies how "elastic" or "plastic" the impact is.

$$e = \frac{\text{Relative velocity of separation}}{\text{Relative velocity of approach}} = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}$$

where $0 \le e \le 1$ for vehicle structures (practically $0.05 \le e \le 0.3$ for modern cars due to extensive crumple zones).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Definition (verbatim flavour):** "A vehicle-to-vehicle impact is a transient dynamic event where the post-impact velocities of the two vehicles are determined by simultaneously applying conservation of momentum and the coefficient of restitution across the line of impact."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Billiards-on-the-Highway" Picture

Imagine two billiard balls colliding on a frictionless billiard table — but the balls are made of **clay** rather than ivory:

- If the clay is *fresh and soft* (high plasticity), the balls squish, stick together, and move off as a single lump. This is a **perfectly plastic impact** ($e = 0$).
- If the clay is *slightly springy* (semi-elastic), the balls deform, then push back partially, and continue moving — but at slower speeds than they approached. This is a **realistic vehicle crash** ($0 < e < 1$).
- If the clay were *perfectly rigid and springy* (ideal elastic, $e = 1$), no kinetic energy would be lost — this never happens in a real crash.

A modern car is engineered to behave as much like the *soft clay* as possible. The engineer's job is to **maximize energy absorption** in the vehicle's crumple zone so that the *occupants* decelerate gently inside a rigid survival cell — like the soft clay protecting a hard core.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world metric in use today:** The post-crash velocity change experienced by a vehicle, called **Delta-V (ΔV)**, measured in **km/h** or **m/s**, is the single best predictor of occupant injury severity. Insurance institutes (IIHS) and global NCAPs publish ΔV windows for every crash test.

## 1.3 Classification of Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impacts

| # | Impact Type | Line of Impact | Common Real Scenario |
|---|-------------|----------------|----------------------|
| 1 | **Central (Head-On) Impact** | Along the longitudinal axis of both vehicles | Two cars approaching on a two-lane road |
| 2 | **Eccentric (Offset) Head-On Impact** | Parallel but offset from the centreline | Small-overlap frontal crash |
| 3 | **Rear-End Impact** | Along the longitudinal axis of the struck vehicle | Whiplash collisions in traffic |
| 4 | **Side (T-Bone) Impact** | Perpendicular to the longitudinal axis | Intersection collisions |
| 5 | **Oblique / Angular Impact** | At an angle $\theta$ to the line of centres | Skidding or sideswipe collisions |
| 6 | **Sideswipe (Glancing) Impact** | Nearly tangential | Lane-change collisions |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU Module 3 problems, you will almost always be tested on the **Central / Head-On (collinear) case** and occasionally the **Oblique case using the Impulse-Eccentricity method**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Governing Principles

### Principle A — Conservation of Linear Momentum
During the infinitesimally short impact duration $\Delta t$, the impulsive forces at the contact surface are *internal* to the two-vehicle system. External forces (gravity, friction, aerodynamic drag) are of order $F \cdot \Delta t$ but the contact impulse $J = \int F_{contact}\, dt$ is overwhelmingly larger.

$$\boxed{\,m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2\,}$$

This gives **one scalar equation** for a 1-D collision.

### Principle B — Newton's Law of Restitution
The **coefficient of restitution** $e$ relates the relative velocity of separation to the relative velocity of approach along the line of impact:

$$\boxed{\,e = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}\,}, \qquad 0 \le e \le 1$$

This gives a **second independent scalar equation**.

Together, the two equations yield a **unique solution** for the two unknowns $v_1$ and $v_2$.

## 2.2 Closed-Form Solution (1-D Central Impact)

Solving the two simultaneous linear equations for $v_1$ and $v_2$:

$$v_1 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 + m_1 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$$

### Special Case 1 — Perfectly Plastic Impact ($e = 0$)
Both vehicles move with a common final velocity:
$$v_1 = v_2 = v_c = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$$
This is the **velocity of the centre of mass** of the system, which is conserved.

### Special Case 2 — Perfectly Elastic Impact ($e = 1$)
$$\boxed{\,v_1 = \frac{(m_1 - m_2)u_1 + 2 m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}\,}, \qquad v_2 = \frac{2 m_1 u_1 + (m_2 - m_1)u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$$
Identical to the standard 1-D elastic collision formulae.

### Special Case 3 — Vehicle 2 Initially Stationary ($u_2 = 0$)
$$v_1 = \frac{m_1 - m_2 e}{m_1 + m_2}\, u_1, \qquad v_2 = \frac{m_1 (1 + e)}{m_1 + m_2}\, u_1$$

## 2.3 Energy Lost During Impact (Crush Energy)

The most engineering-relevant quantity in crashworthiness is **kinetic energy dissipated into permanent deformation**. By computing the difference between initial and final kinetic energies:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta E = \frac{1}{2}\,\frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\,(1 - e^2)\,(u_1 - u_2)^2\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $\dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ is the **reduced mass** $\mu$ of the system. The energy loss scales with the **square of the closing speed** $(u_1 - u_2)^2$ and the factor $(1 - e^2)$. Doubling the closing speed **quadruples** the crush energy — this is why a 80 km/h crash is *four times* more destructive than a 40 km/h crash, not twice.

For the perfectly plastic case $e = 0$:
$$\Delta E_{plastic} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\,(u_1 - u_2)^2$$
which is the **maximum possible energy dissipation** (no rebound).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | $m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$ | Conservation of linear momentum | kg·m/s |
| 2 | $e = (v_2 - v_1)/(u_1 - u_2)$ | Newton's Law of Restitution | dimensionless |
| 3 | $v_1 = \dfrac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_2 e(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Post-impact velocity of vehicle 1 | m/s |
| 4 | $v_2 = \dfrac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 + m_1 e(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Post-impact velocity of vehicle 2 | m/s |
| 5 | $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Reduced mass | kg |
| 6 | $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\,(1 - e^2)\,(u_1 - u_2)^2$ | Energy lost to deformation (crush energy) | J |
| 7 | $J = m_1(v_1 - u_1) = -m_2(v_2 - u_2)$ | Impulse along the line of impact | N·s |
| 8 | $\Delta V = v_1 - u_1$ (for vehicle 1) | Velocity change — injury severity indicator | m/s |
| 9 | $v_c = (m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2)/(m_1 + m_2)$ | Common velocity for plastic impact | m/s |
| 10 | $v_{1,elastic} = \dfrac{(m_1 - m_2)u_1 + 2m_2 u_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Elastic special case (vehicle 1) | m/s |

> [!NOTE]
> All ten formulas above are fair game for KTU Part-B (14-mark) derivations. Memorise them as a single "toolkit" — any numerical problem only requires 2 or 3 of them at a time.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Crash Reconstruction** — Forensic engineers back-calculate pre-crash speeds from post-crash skid marks using $e$ and the conservation equations.
2. **Insurance Risk Modelling** — Insurers use $\Delta E$ to estimate repair cost brackets.
3. **Crumple Zone Design** — Engineers deliberately *lower* $e$ (push it toward 0) by introducing progressive buckling elements that convert kinetic energy into plastic hinge rotation.
4. **ADAS / AEB Tuning** — Autonomous Emergency Braking systems target reducing $(u_1 - u_2)^2$ — i.e., lowering closing speed by even 10 km/h halves the crush energy.
5. **NCAP Rating** — The Euro NCAP star rating system uses $\Delta V$ time-histories to score occupant protection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Closed-Form Post-Impact Velocities

We start with the two governing equations for a 1-D central impact between vehicles 1 and 2:

**Equation (i)** — Conservation of Momentum:
$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2 \tag{1}$$

**Equation (ii)** — Newton's Law of Restitution:
$$e\,(u_1 - u_2) = v_2 - v_1 \tag{2}$$

**Step 1.** Solve Eq. (1) for $v_2$:
$$m_2 v_2 = m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_1 v_1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; v_2 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_1 v_1}{m_2} \tag{3}$$

**Step 2.** Substitute Eq. (3) into Eq. (2):
$$e\,(u_1 - u_2) = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_1 v_1}{m_2} - v_1$$

**Step 3.** Multiply both sides by $m_2$ to clear the denominator:
$$e\,m_2\,(u_1 - u_2) = m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_1 v_1 - m_2 v_1$$

**Step 4.** Collect the $v_1$ terms on the right:
$$e\,m_2\,(u_1 - u_2) = m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - (m_1 + m_2)\,v_1$$

**Step 5.** Isolate $v_1$:
$$(m_1 + m_2)\,v_1 = m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - e\,m_2\,(u_1 - u_2)$$

$$\boxed{\;v_1 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}\;} \tag{4}$$

**Step 6.** By symmetry (swap indices 1 ↔ 2 and reverse the sign of the restitution term, because the relative velocity of separation is defined with vehicle 2 "leading"):

$$\boxed{\;v_2 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 + m_1 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}\;} \tag{5}$$

**Verification (sanity check):** If $e = 0$ (perfectly plastic), then both numerators reduce to the same quantity $m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2$, and both vehicles move with the common centre-of-mass velocity $v_c$. ✓

If $e = 1$ (perfectly elastic) and $u_2 = 0$, then $v_1 = (m_1 - m_2)u_1/(m_1 + m_2)$ — the standard elastic formula. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation of the Crush Energy (Energy Lost in Impact)

Initial total kinetic energy of the system:
$$T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 u_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 u_2^2$$

Final total kinetic energy of the system:
$$T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2$$

Energy lost to deformation (crush energy):
$$\Delta E = T_i - T_f$$

**Step 1.** Substitute the closed-form expressions for $v_1$ and $v_2$ (Eqs. 4 and 5). To make the algebra clean, work in the **centre-of-mass frame** where $V_{cm} = (m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2)/(m_1 + m_2)$.

In the CM frame, the initial velocities are $u_1 - V_{cm}$ and $u_2 - V_{cm}$, and the relative velocity is unchanged:
$$u_1 - u_2 = (u_1 - V_{cm}) - (u_2 - V_{cm})$$

The kinetic energy splits into $T_{cm} + T_{rel}$ where $T_{cm} = \tfrac{1}{2}(m_1 + m_2)V_{cm}^2$ is *conserved* and $T_{rel}$ depends only on $\mu$ and the relative speed:
$$T_{rel,i} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\,(u_1 - u_2)^2$$

**Step 2.** After impact, in the CM frame, the relative speed becomes $e\,(u_1 - u_2)$ (by the definition of $e$). So:
$$T_{rel,f} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\,e^2\,(u_1 - u_2)^2$$

**Step 3.** The energy loss is therefore the change in relative KE:
$$\Delta E = T_{rel,i} - T_{rel,f} = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\,(u_1 - u_2)^2 - \tfrac{1}{2}\,\mu\,e^2\,(u_1 - u_2)^2$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\frac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}\,(1 - e^2)\,(u_1 - u_2)^2\;}$$

This is the **crush energy** absorbed by the vehicle structures.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Worked in Full (KTU-style)

**Problem (KTU University Exam pattern):**
A car of mass $m_1 = 1200$ kg travelling at $u_1 = 20$ m/s collides head-on with a stationary truck of mass $m_2 = 4000$ kg. If the coefficient of restitution is $e = 0.15$, determine:

(a) The velocities of both vehicles immediately after impact.
(b) The energy lost in the collision.
(c) The impulse on each vehicle.

### (a) Post-impact velocities

$$v_1 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$$

With $u_2 = 0$:

$$v_1 = \frac{1200 \cdot 20 + 4000 \cdot 0 - 4000 \cdot 0.15 \cdot (20 - 0)}{1200 + 4000}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{24{,}000 - 12{,}000}{5200} = \frac{12{,}000}{5200} = 2.3077\;\text{m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 + m_1 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2} = \frac{24{,}000 + 1200 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 20}{5200}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{24{,}000 + 3600}{5200} = \frac{27{,}600}{5200} = 5.3077\;\text{m/s}$$

### (b) Energy lost in the collision

Initial KE:
$$T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1200 \cdot 20^2 + 0 = 240{,}000\;\text{J}$$

Final KE:
$$T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1200 \cdot (2.3077)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4000 \cdot (5.3077)^2$$

$$T_f = 600 \cdot 5.3255 + 2000 \cdot 28.1716 = 3195.3 + 56{,}343.2 = 59{,}538.5\;\text{J}$$

Energy lost:
$$\Delta E = 240{,}000 - 59{,}538.5 = 180{,}461.5\;\text{J} \approx 180.46\;\text{kJ}$$

**Cross-check using the formula:**
$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{1200 \cdot 4000}{5200} \cdot (1 - 0.15^2) \cdot 20^2$$

$$= \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 923.077 \cdot 0.9775 \cdot 400 = 180{,}461\;\text{J} \;\checkmark$$

### (c) Impulse on each vehicle

Using $J = m_1(v_1 - u_1)$:
$$J = 1200 \cdot (2.3077 - 20) = 1200 \cdot (-17.6923) = -21{,}230.8\;\text{N·s}$$

The impulse on the car is $21{,}230.8$ N·s in the direction opposite to its initial motion. The impulse on the truck is equal and opposite: $+21{,}230.8$ N·s.

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that the **truck barely changes speed** (0 → 5.3 m/s), but the **car nearly comes to rest** (20 → 2.3 m/s) and sheds ~75% of its kinetic energy. This is the classic "small car vs. heavy truck" outcome that motivates modern SUV/truck bumper-height regulations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Variants

| Problem Variant | What is given | What to find | Key equations used |
|-----------------|---------------|--------------|-------------------|
| Plastic collision ($e=0$), one vehicle stationary | $m_1, m_2, u_1$ | $v_c$, $\Delta E$ | $v_c = m_1 u_1/(m_1+m_2)$, $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu u_1^2$ |
| General $e$, both moving | $m_1, m_2, u_1, u_2, e$ | $v_1, v_2, \Delta E, J$ | All ten cheat-sheet formulae |
| Find $e$ from given post-impact data | $m_1, m_2, u_1, u_2, v_1, v_2$ | $e$ | $e = (v_2 - v_1)/(u_1 - u_2)$ |
| Find pre-impact speed from skid marks | $m_1, m_2, v_1, v_2, e$ | $u_1$ | Re-arrange Eq. (4) for $u_1$ |
| Energy absorbed given crush distance | $F$ (or $k$) and $x$ | $\Delta E$ | $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} k x^2$ (spring) or $F \cdot x$ (rigid-plastic) |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Impact Geometry — Central vs. Eccentric

The following Mermaid block renders the **functional block architecture** of a vehicle-to-vehicle impact analysis, mapping the inputs, governing equations, derived quantities, and engineering outcomes. This is the analytical flow you should draw in your KTU answer sheet.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT PARAMETERS"] --> B["Mass m1, m2"]
    A --> C["Pre-impact Velocities u1, u2"]
    A --> D["Coefficient of Restitution e"]

    B --> E["GOVERNING EQUATIONS"]
    C --> E
    D --> E

    E --> F["Momentum Conservation m1 u1 + m2 u2 = m1 v1 + m2 v2"]
    E --> G["Newton's Restitution Law e = v2-v1 / u1-u2"]

    F --> H["POST-IMPACT VELOCITIES"]
    G --> H

    H --> I["v1 formula"]
    H --> J["v2 formula"]

    H --> K["ENGINEERING DERIVED QUANTITIES"]
    H --> K

    K --> L["Crush Energy DELTA E"]
    K --> M["Impulse J"]
    K --> N["Delta-V per vehicle"]
    K --> O["Reduced Mass mu"]

    L --> P["CRASHWORTHINESS OUTPUTS"]
    M --> P
    N --> P

    P --> Q["Occupant Injury Risk"]
    P --> R["Repair Cost Estimate"]
    P --> S["NCAP Star Rating"]
    P --> T["Forensic Reconstruction"]
```

## 4.2 Classification Flow of Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact Modes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    V2V["Vehicle-to-Vehicle Impact"] --> A1["Central Head-On"]
    V2V --> A2["Offset / Small Overlap"]
    V2V --> A3["Rear-End"]
    V2V --> A4["Side T-Bone"]
    V2V --> A5["Oblique Angular"]
    V2V --> A6["Sideswipe Glancing"]

    A1 --> B1["Line of impact = longitudinal axis"]
    A4 --> B2["Line of impact = perpendicular"]
    A5 --> B3["Angle theta between axes"]
    A5 --> B4["Use Impulse-Eccentricity theorem"]
    A5 --> B5["Decompose velocities into normal and tangential components"]
```

## 4.3 Free-Body / Velocity Diagram of a Central Impact

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BEFORE["BEFORE IMPACT"]
        M1["Vehicle 1\nmass m1\nvelocity u1"]
        M2["Vehicle 2\nmass m2\nvelocity u2"]
    end

    M1 -->|"u1 > u2"| CONTACT["CONTACT INSTANT\nImpulse J exchanged\nalong line of impact"]
    M2 -->|"u2"| CONTACT

    CONTACT --> N1["Vehicle 1\nvelocity v1"]
    CONTACT --> N2["Vehicle 2\nvelocity v2"]

    subgraph AFTER["AFTER IMPACT"]
        N1
        N2
    end
```

## 4.4 Logical Decision Tree — Choosing the Right Formula

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["Given a V2V Impact Problem"] --> Q1{"Is the impact\n1-D collinear?"}

    Q1 -->|"YES"| Q2{"Is e given\nor e=0?"}
    Q1 -->|"NO"| OBL["Use Oblique Impact Method:\nDecompose velocities\nalong n-line and t-line\nApply momentum + restitution\nalong n only"]

    Q2 -->|"General e"| GEN["Use v1, v2 closed-form"]
    Q2 -->|"e = 0"| PLASTIC["Use v_c = m1 u1 + m2 u2 / m1+m2"]
    Q2 -->|"e = 1"| ELASTIC["Use elastic special case"]

    GEN --> OUT["Compute Delta E, J, Delta-V"]
    PLASTIC --> OUT
    ELASTIC --> OUT
    OBL --> OUT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this Mermaid block instead of a physical hand-drawn diagram?** A schematic of crumple-zone deformation is impossible to render textually. The block diagram above captures the **functional architecture** of the analysis: inputs → governing equations → derived quantities → engineering outputs. Examiners reward this kind of systematic flow because it shows that you understand the *engineering method*, not just the formulas.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]
**Define the coefficient of restitution for a vehicle impact. What is its typical range for a modern passenger car crash, and what does a value of $e = 0$ physically represent?**

**Model Answer:**
The coefficient of restitution $e$ is defined as the ratio of the relative velocity of separation to the relative velocity of approach along the line of impact:

$$e = \frac{v_2 - v_1}{u_1 - u_2}$$

For modern passenger car crashes, $e$ typically lies in the range **0.05 to 0.3** because extensive crumple zones absorb energy plastically. A value of **$e = 0$** represents a **perfectly plastic (inelastic) impact**, in which the two vehicles move with a common post-impact velocity equal to the velocity of the centre of mass, and the **maximum possible kinetic energy is dissipated** into permanent deformation.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]
**State and explain the principle of conservation of linear momentum as applied to a vehicle-to-vehicle impact. Why is it valid during the short impact duration?**

**Model Answer:**
The principle states that the total linear momentum of an isolated system remains constant in the absence of external impulsive forces:

$$m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$$

During the brief impact interval (typically **80–150 ms** for a car crash), the contact forces at the interface are extremely large (of the order of hundreds of kN), so the impulse $J_{contact} = \int F\,dt$ is of the order of $10^4$ N·s. By contrast, external forces such as gravity, rolling resistance, and aerodynamic drag act over the same time interval but generate impulses of only a few N·s — these are **negligible** compared to the contact impulse. Hence, the two-vehicle system can be treated as isolated, and momentum is conserved to a very high degree of accuracy.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern, Module 3 Part-B provides an internal choice. Two alternative questions are given below; the student answers **one**.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

A car of mass $m_1 = 1500$ kg moving at $u_1 = 25$ m/s collides head-on with a truck of mass $m_2 = 5000$ kg moving in the opposite direction at $u_2 = 10$ m/s. The coefficient of restitution is $e = 0.20$.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Determine the velocities of the car and the truck immediately after impact. Also find the impulse on each vehicle.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Calculate (i) the total kinetic energy before and after impact, (ii) the energy lost in the collision, and (iii) the percentage of the original kinetic energy that went into structural deformation.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.A(a)

**Given:** $m_1 = 1500$ kg, $u_1 = +25$ m/s, $m_2 = 5000$ kg, $u_2 = -10$ m/s, $e = 0.20$.

**Sign convention:** Rightward is positive. Vehicle 1 moves rightward at +25 m/s, vehicle 2 moves leftward at −10 m/s.

**Apply closed-form velocity formula:**

$$v_1 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 - m_2 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{1500 \cdot 25 + 5000 \cdot (-10) - 5000 \cdot 0.20 \cdot (25 - (-10))}{1500 + 5000}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{37{,}500 - 50{,}000 - 1000 \cdot 35}{6500} = \frac{37{,}500 - 50{,}000 - 35{,}000}{6500}$$

$$v_1 = \frac{-47{,}500}{6500} = -7.3077\;\text{m/s}$$

The negative sign means the car **rebounds backwards** (leftward) at 7.31 m/s.

$$v_2 = \frac{m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 + m_1 e\,(u_1 - u_2)}{m_1 + m_2}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{37{,}500 - 50{,}000 + 1500 \cdot 0.20 \cdot 35}{6500} = \frac{37{,}500 - 50{,}000 + 10{,}500}{6500}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{-2000}{6500} = -0.3077\;\text{m/s}$$

The truck continues leftward at a much-reduced speed of 0.31 m/s (nearly stops).

**Impulse on the car:**
$$J = m_1(v_1 - u_1) = 1500 \cdot (-7.3077 - 25) = 1500 \cdot (-32.3077) = -48{,}461.5\;\text{N·s}$$

Impulse on the truck:
$$J = m_2(v_2 - u_2) = 5000 \cdot (-0.3077 - (-10)) = 5000 \cdot 9.6923 = +48{,}461.5\;\text{N·s}\;\checkmark$$

#### Model Solution to Q.A(b)

**Initial KE:**
$$T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot 25^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 5000 \cdot 10^2 = 468{,}750 + 250{,}000 = 718{,}750\;\text{J}$$

**Final KE:**
$$T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1500 \cdot (7.3077)^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 5000 \cdot (0.3077)^2$$

$$T_f = 750 \cdot 53.4025 + 2500 \cdot 0.09468 = 40{,}051.9 + 236.7 = 40{,}288.6\;\text{J}$$

**Energy lost:**
$$\Delta E = 718{,}750 - 40{,}288.6 = 678{,}461.4\;\text{J} \approx 678.46\;\text{kJ}$$

**Cross-check using the reduced-mass formula:**

Reduced mass:
$$\mu = \frac{1500 \cdot 5000}{6500} = 1153.846\;\text{kg}$$

Closing speed:
$$u_1 - u_2 = 25 - (-10) = 35\;\text{m/s}$$

$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1153.846 \cdot (1 - 0.04) \cdot 35^2 = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1153.846 \cdot 0.96 \cdot 1225$$

$$\Delta E = 0.5 \cdot 1153.846 \cdot 1176 = 678{,}461\;\text{J} \;\checkmark$$

**Percentage of original KE lost to deformation:**
$$\%\,\text{Loss} = \frac{678{,}461}{718{,}750} \times 100\% = 94.39\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Common Mark-Loss Zones:**
> 1. **Sign convention slip:** Students often forget that $u_2$ is *negative* for a head-on collision and write $u_2 = +10$ m/s. This single error propagates into all subsequent calculations and costs **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Not verifying the sign of $v_1$:** A negative post-impact velocity physically means the car rebounds backwards. If your formula gives a positive $v_1$, you have likely used the wrong sign in the restitution term — re-check.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s:** KTU problems occasionally give speeds in km/h. Always convert: $u$ (m/s) = $u$ (km/h) ÷ 3.6.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

A car of mass $m_1 = 1000$ kg travelling at $u_1 = 15$ m/s strikes a stationary car of mass $m_2 = 1200$ kg from behind. The coefficient of restitution between the two cars is $e = 0.10$.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Find the velocities of both cars after impact, the impulse on the struck car, and the change in velocity (Delta-V) of the striking car.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Determine (i) the energy lost in the collision, (ii) the maximum compression of the front crumple zone of the striking car if the average crush force is $F_{avg} = 200$ kN, and (iii) comment on how modern crumple-zone design uses the parameter $e$ to enhance occupant safety.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a)

**Given:** $m_1 = 1000$ kg, $u_1 = +15$ m/s, $m_2 = 1200$ kg, $u_2 = 0$, $e = 0.10$.

**Apply the simplified formula for the case $u_2 = 0$:**

$$v_1 = \frac{m_1 - m_2 e}{m_1 + m_2}\, u_1 = \frac{1000 - 1200 \cdot 0.10}{2200} \cdot 15$$

$$v_1 = \frac{1000 - 120}{2200} \cdot 15 = \frac{880}{2200} \cdot 15 = 0.4 \cdot 15 = 6.0\;\text{m/s}$$

$$v_2 = \frac{m_1 (1 + e)}{m_1 + m_2}\, u_1 = \frac{1000 \cdot 1.10}{2200} \cdot 15 = 0.5 \cdot 15 = 7.5\;\text{m/s}$$

**Impulse on the struck car (vehicle 2):**
$$J = m_2(v_2 - u_2) = 1200 \cdot (7.5 - 0) = 9000\;\text{N·s}$$

**Delta-V of the striking car (vehicle 1):**
$$\Delta V_1 = v_1 - u_1 = 6.0 - 15.0 = -9.0\;\text{m/s}$$

A Delta-V of −9.0 m/s (i.e., 32.4 km/h velocity loss) is associated with **moderate-to-high injury risk** in standard injury risk curves (e.g., the Abbreviated Injury Scale).

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b)

**(i) Energy lost:**

Initial KE:
$$T_i = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1000 \cdot 15^2 = 112{,}500\;\text{J}$$

Final KE:
$$T_f = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1000 \cdot 6.0^2 + \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 1200 \cdot 7.5^2 = 18{,}000 + 33{,}750 = 51{,}750\;\text{J}$$

Energy lost:
$$\Delta E = 112{,}500 - 51{,}750 = 60{,}750\;\text{J} \approx 60.75\;\text{kJ}$$

**Cross-check using reduced-mass formula:**
$$\mu = \frac{1000 \cdot 1200}{2200} = 545.45\;\text{kg}$$

$$\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 545.45 \cdot (1 - 0.01) \cdot 15^2 = 0.5 \cdot 545.45 \cdot 0.99 \cdot 225$$

$$\Delta E = 60{,}750\;\text{J} \;\checkmark$$

**(ii) Maximum compression of the front crumple zone:**

The crush energy is absorbed by the average force $F_{avg}$ acting through the maximum crush distance $x_{max}$. Using the work-energy theorem:

$$\Delta E = F_{avg} \cdot x_{max} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; x_{max} = \frac{\Delta E}{F_{avg}}$$

$$x_{max} = \frac{60{,}750}{200{,}000} = 0.30375\;\text{m} \approx 30.4\;\text{cm}$$

This is consistent with typical real-world frontal-crush depths of 25–50 cm observed in NCAP tests.

**(iii) Engineering commentary on $e$ and crumple-zone design:**

A modern passenger car is engineered to have a *low* coefficient of restitution — typically $e \approx 0.05$ to $0.20$ — by using progressive buckling beams, honeycomb crush initiators, and de-coupling sub-frames. The lower the value of $e$, the larger the factor $(1 - e^2)$ in the crush-energy equation, meaning **more kinetic energy is absorbed by the vehicle structure** (and less is "rebound energy" transmitted back to the occupants as a jerk). The energy is dissipated through plastic deformation of the front structure, while the rear passenger cell remains rigid to maintain survival space. Thus, designers deliberately *minimize* $e$ to *maximize* crush energy absorption and occupant protection.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Q.B Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Reusing formula for both vehicles without re-deriving:** The "stationary-target" simplification only works when $u_2 = 0$. If $u_2 \ne 0$, always use the full closed-form expressions. Using the wrong formula costs **2 marks** minimum.
> 2. **Units of force in crush-distance calculation:** If $F_{avg}$ is given in kN, you **must** convert to N before using in $x_{max} = \Delta E / F_{avg}$, or your answer will be off by a factor of 1000.
> 3. **Commentary questions (part iii):** KTU examiners expect a 3–4 sentence technical answer with explicit reference to the $(1 - e^2)$ factor. A vague "crumple zones help" answer without the physics loses **2–3 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Vehicle to Vehicle Impact**

- **Two governing equations:** (i) Conservation of momentum $m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2 = m_1 v_1 + m_2 v_2$ and (ii) Newton's restitution law $e = (v_2 - v_1)/(u_1 - u_2)$. Always write both before plugging numbers.
- **Closed-form post-impact velocities:** $v_1$ has a **minus** sign in front of $m_2 e (u_1 - u_2)$; $v_2$ has a **plus** sign in front of $m_1 e (u_1 - u_2)$. Memorise the asymmetry — it is the most-tested item in KTU Part-B.
- **Reduced mass:** $\mu = m_1 m_2 / (m_1 + m_2)$. This is the "effective mass" of the impact.
- **Crush energy:** $\Delta E = \tfrac{1}{2}\mu(1 - e^2)(u_1 - u_2)^2$. Scales with the **square** of closing speed.
- **Special cases:** $e = 0$ → plastic, common final velocity $v_c = (m_1 u_1 + m_2 u_2)/(m_1 + m_2)$. $e = 1$ → elastic, use standard 1-D elastic formulas.
- **Typical $e$ for modern cars:** 0.05 to 0.3 (much less than 1) — this is the entire point of crumple zones.
- **Impulse:** $J = m_1(v_1 - u_1) = -m_2(v_2 - u_2)$; equal and opposite on the two vehicles.
- **Delta-V (ΔV):** The change in velocity of a single vehicle — direct injury-severity indicator. Higher ΔV → higher occupant injury risk.
- **Sign convention for head-on collision:** Take one direction as positive; the *opposing* vehicle's velocity is **negative**. Forgetting this is the #1 mark-loss reason.
- **Oblique impacts:** Decompose velocities into *normal* (along line of impact) and *tangential* components. Apply momentum and restitution only along the normal direction. The tangential components are *unchanged* if friction is neglected.
- **Energy dissipated = crush work = $F_{avg} \cdot x_{max}$** — the link between the impact equations and the structural deformation.
- **Real-world relevance:** Crash reconstruction, NCAP star ratings, ADAS/AEB tuning, insurance risk modelling, forensic back-calculation of pre-impact speeds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Side Impact Testing** is a standardized vehicle crashworthiness evaluation procedure designed to quantify the structural integrity of a passenger car and its ability to attenuate occupant injuries when subjected to a lateral (perpendicular-to-vehicle-longitudinal-axis) collision. The test simulates the most common real-world intersection crash scenario where the front of one vehicle strikes the side of another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PEMUT633):** Side impact testing evaluates occupant protection during a lateral collision using a Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) striking the subject vehicle, with bio-fidelic instrumented dummies positioned in the struck-side front and rear seats. The test quantifies injury levels through standardized injury metrics and ensures compliance with crashworthiness regulations such as **FMVSS 214**, **ECE R95**, **Euro NCAP**, and the Indian **AIS-096** standard.

### Regulatory Framework — The Big Four

| Standard | Issuing Body | Region | Test Mass | Impact Velocity |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| FMVSS 214 | NHTSA | USA | 1367 kg MDB | 54 km/h |
| ECE R95 | UNECE | Europe | 950 kg MDB | 50 km/h |
| Euro NCAP | Euro NCAP Consortium | Europe | 1400 kg MDB | 60 km/h |
| AIS-096 | ARAI / MoRTH | India | 950 kg MDB | 50 km/h |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Egg Between Two Spoons"

Imagine an egg placed between two spoons (one on each side). When you squeeze the spoons together:
- The egg (occupant) is squeezed between the spoons (intruding door and center console).
- A well-designed spoon (vehicle with side-impact beams, crumple zones, and energy-absorbing padding) will deform progressively, **absorbing the kinetic energy** before it reaches the egg.
- A poorly designed spoon (rigid door, no side beams) will transfer the impact force directly to the egg, **crushing it instantly**.

**Side Impact Testing is the science of designing the "spoons" to protect the "egg."**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is side impact deadlier than frontal impact?**
> Unlike the front of a car, which has ~1.0–1.5 meters of crumple zone, the side of a car has only **~15–25 cm** of crush space between the occupant and the intruding barrier. This minimal buffer means that intrusion control and energy management are critical.

---

## 1.3 Why Side Impact Testing Matters — Key Statistics

- Side impacts account for approximately **27%** of all passenger vehicle occupant fatalities in OECD countries (per WHO/IRTAD data).
- Fatality risk for belted occupants in a side impact is **2–3 times higher** than in a comparable frontal impact.
- The struck-side occupant's survival corridor is **~30 cm**, demanding millimeter-perfect structural response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Syllabus Highlights for KTU 2024:**
> 1. Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) test methodology
> 2. Anthropomorphic Test Devices (ATDs): WorldSID, ES-2, SID-IIs
> 3. Injury criteria: HIC, Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI), Rib Deflection (RDC), Pelvis Acceleration
> 4. Regulatory standards: FMVSS 214, ECE R95, Euro NCAP, AIS-096
> 5. Structural countermeasures: B-pillar reinforcement, side-impact beams, door trim padding, curtain airbags
