# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Test Setup Architecture — Component Breakdown

The side impact test rig comprises five primary subsystems, each governed by precise physical and kinematic parameters.

### A. The Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB)
The MDB is a self-propelled, deformable trolley that strikes the stationary subject vehicle (SV) at a defined velocity and perpendicular angle. Its front face is fitted with **6 rows of honeycomb aluminum blocks** that simulate the front-end stiffness of a typical striking vehicle.

- **Honeycomb blocks**: Manufactured from aluminum alloy (Al-3003), each with cell size **\approx 19.1 mm**, foil thickness **\approx 0.076 mm**, density **\approx 53 kg/m³**.
- The honeycomb crushes progressively under impact, generating a **defined force-deflection characteristic** that replicates real-world vehicle-to-vehicle engagement.

### B. The Subject Vehicle (SV) Preparation
- **Fuel system**: Drained and replaced with a water-based substitute to simulate fuel mass while eliminating fire hazard.
- **Interior trim**: Left fully assembled (production-specification).
- **Seats**: Set to mid-track, mid-height position; seat back at design angle.
- **Steering wheel**: Removed and replaced with equivalent mass only when dummy interaction is not required.

### C. Anthropomorphic Test Devices (ATDs / Dummies)
Two dummies are placed inside the SV:

| Dummy | Position | Mass | Primary Injury Measurement Regions |
|---|---|---|---|
| **ES-2 (European Side)** or **WorldSID 50th** | Front seat, struck side | ~75 kg | Thorax (ribs, spine), abdomen, pelvis, head |
| **SID-IIs (Side Impact Dummy, small)** | Rear seat, struck side | ~45 kg | Thorax, pelvis, head |

> [!NOTE]
> **ES-2 vs WorldSID**: The WorldSID (ISO/TS 21902) supersedes the older ES-2 dummy in modern regulations due to superior biofidelity in lateral thorax response.

### D. Instrumentation
- **High-speed cameras** (minimum 1000 fps) positioned orthogonal and oblique to the impact zone.
- **Accelerometers** mounted on each dummy body region (head, chest, pelvis, femur).
- **Load cells** in dummy thoracic spine, abdominal insert, and femur.
- **Potentiometers** for rib deflection measurement (6 ribs instrumented).

### E. The Track and Propulsion System
The MDB is accelerated to test velocity using a pneumatic or hydraulic catapult, then released into free flight for the final **1.5–2.0 m** before impact to ensure velocity stabilization and decouple propulsion vibration.

---

## 2.2 The Six Critical Injury Criteria (ICs)

Each dummy measurement is converted into a numerical injury criterion. These are the **non-negotiable** metrics a student must memorize for the KTU examination.

### 1. Head Injury Criterion (HIC₁₅)
$$HIC_{15} = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$$

- Where $t_2 - t_1 \le 15\,\text{ms}$ and $a(t)$ is the resultant head acceleration in $g$.
- **Limit (AIS-096 / FMVSS 214):** HIC₁₅ ≤ **1000**.
- **Limit (Euro NCAP):** HIC₁₅ ≤ **500** (more stringent).

### 2. Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI)
$$TTI = \frac{g_{R} + g_{L}}{2}$$
- $g_R$ = peak acceleration of upper thoracic spine (T1), right-side rib.
- $g_L$ = peak acceleration of upper thoracic spine, left-side rib.
- **Limit:** TTI ≤ **90 g** (ECE R95 / AIS-096).

### 3. Thoracic Rib Deflection Criterion (RDC / TRD)
$$RDC = \text{Peak deflection of any individual rib (mm)}$$
- **Limit:** RDC ≤ **44 mm** (ECE R95).
- **Limit:** Peak rib deflection ≤ **32 mm** (Euro NCAP — more stringent).

### 4. Abdominal Peak Force (APF)
$$F_{abdomen} = \text{Peak measured abdominal compression force (kN)}$$
- **Limit:** $F_{abdomen} \le$ **2.5 kN** (ECE R95).

### 5. Pelvis Peak Acceleration (PPA)
$$a_{pelvis} = \text{Peak resultant acceleration at pelvis accelerometer (g)}$$
- **Limit:** $a_{pelvis} \le$ **130 g** (AIS-096 / FMVSS 214).

### 6. Femur Compression Force (FCF)
$$F_{femur} = \text{Peak compressive load on femur load cell (kN)}$$
- **Limit:** $F_{femur} \le$ **10 kN** (ECE R95) and **6.1 kN** (FMVSS 214 knee bolster limits).

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Reference Table

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard Limit | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Head Injury Criterion | HIC₁₅ | $\left[\frac{1}{t_2-t_1}\int a\,dt\right]^{2.5}(t_2-t_1)$ | ≤ 1000 (FMVSS) | dimensionless |
| 2 | Thoracic Trauma Index | TTI | $(g_R + g_L)/2$ | ≤ 90 | g |
| 3 | Rib Deflection Criterion | RDC | Peak rib deflection | ≤ 44 (ECE), ≤ 32 (NCAP) | mm |
| 4 | Abdominal Peak Force | APF | Peak abdomen force | ≤ 2.5 | kN |
| 5 | Pelvis Peak Acceleration | PPA | Peak pelvis accel. | ≤ 130 | g |
| 6 | Femur Compression Force | FCF | Peak femur load | ≤ 10 (ECE), ≤ 6.1 (FMVSS) | kN |
| 7 | Kinetic Energy of MDB | $E_k$ | $\frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | — | J |
| 8 | MDB Momentum | $p$ | $mv$ | — | kg·m/s |
| 9 | Velocity Change of SV | $\Delta v$ | Impulse–momentum | — | m/s |
| 10 | Intrusion Velocity | $v_{int}$ | $dx/dt$ at door | — | m/s |
| 11 | Crush Energy | $E_c$ | $\int F_{crush}\,dx$ | — | J |
| 12 | Force Peak Limit | $F_{peak}$ | Structural cap | ~ 80 kN | kN |
| 13 | Door Velocity | $v_{door}$ | $\int a_{door}\,dt$ | ≤ 5 | m/s |
| 14 | Test Velocity (ECE/AIS) | $V$ | Given | 50 | km/h |
| 15 | Test Velocity (Euro NCAP) | $V$ | Given | 60 | km/h |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Aid for KTU:** "**H**ead, **T**horax, **A**bdomen, **P**elvis, **F**emur" → "**H-TAP-F**" — the five principal IC measurement regions.

---

## 2.4 The Physics of Side Impact — Energy & Momentum

The total kinetic energy of the MDB must be managed by three parallel paths:

$$E_{k,total} = E_{barrier\,deformation} + E_{SV\,crush} + E_{residual\,kinetic}$$

- **Barrier deformation (~40–50%)**: Absorbed by the honeycomb aluminum blocks.
- **SV structural crush (~35–45%)**: Absorbed by door, B-pillar, floor, roof rails.
- **Residual kinetic (~5–10%)**: Remaining velocity of MDB + SV as combined mass.

The **occupant injury potential** is governed not by total energy absorbed, but by the **rate** at which force is transmitted to the dummy — i.e., the force pulse shape.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:** A side-impact structure should ideally produce a **square-wave force pulse** (constant force during the crush phase) rather than a sharp force peak. A sharp peak translates to a high force transient on the thorax, causing rib fractures even at low average forces.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The IC limits above do not merely satisfy regulators — they translate directly to **insurance star ratings**, **fleet procurement decisions**, and **consumer safety perception**:

- **Euro NCAP 5-star**: Requires sophisticated multi-load-path door design, B-pillar hot-stamping (1500 MPa steel), curtain airbag, and seat-mounted thorax airbag.
- **Fleet sales**: Major leasing companies mandate Euro NCAP 5-star or equivalent.
- **Cost of compliance**: A full side-impact countermeasure package adds ~$200–500 per vehicle, yet reduces injury claim costs by **~40%** (per IIHS HLDI data).

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Kinetic Energy of the MDB at Impact

The fundamental first-principles quantity for any crash test is the kinetic energy of the impacting body.

**Step 1 — State the definition of kinetic energy for a translating rigid body:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the AIS-096 / ECE R95 test parameters:**

- $m = 950\,\text{kg}$ (standard MDB mass)
- $v = 50\,\text{km/h} = \frac{50 \times 1000}{3600}\,\text{m/s} = 13.89\,\text{m/s}$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical value:**

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 950 \times (13.89)^2$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 950 \times 192.93$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 91{,}640\,\text{J} \approx 91.6\,\text{kJ}}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result:**

This ~91.6 kJ is the total energy budget the SV + barrier must dissipate. For context, this is equivalent to the kinetic energy of a **2.4-tonne vehicle traveling at 28 km/h**. The structural systems must absorb this energy within ~100–120 ms without exceeding occupant IC limits.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MDB Momentum and Equivalent Impact Mass

**Step 1 — Linear momentum definition:**

$$p = m v$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$p = 950\,\text{kg} \times 13.89\,\text{m/s}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 13{,}195\,\text{kg·m/s}}$$

**Step 3 — Equivalent mass comparison (informative):**

A 1.5-tonne SUV (m_SV ≈ 1500 kg) struck laterally by this MDB, assuming a fully plastic (coefficient of restitution e = 0) collision:

$$v_{combined} = \frac{m_{MDB} v_{MDB}}{m_{MDB} + m_{SV}} = \frac{13{,}195}{950 + 1500} = 5.39\,\text{m/s} = 19.4\,\text{km/h}$$

This residual velocity (~19 km/h) is what the SV occupants effectively experience as a velocity change — a key input to the **VC (Velocity Change) injury metric**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Energy Absorbed by Door Intrusion (Detailed)

Consider the side door as a cantilever beam fixed at the hinge (front) and latched (rear), bending under a distributed transverse load $w(x)$ from the intruding barrier. We want the **work done on the door** during intrusion.

**Step 1 — Establish the force-crush characteristic.**

Experimental data from honeycomb and sheet metal tests reveals an approximately **linear stiffness characteristic** in the initial crush phase:

$$F_{crush}(x) = k \cdot x$$

where $k$ is the effective lateral stiffness of the door + B-pillar assembly (typical: $k \approx 200\text{–}400\,\text{kN/m}$).

**Step 2 — Compute the crush energy absorbed up to maximum intrusion $x_{max}$:**

$$E_{crush} = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} F_{crush}(x)\,dx = \int_{0}^{x_{max}} k x\, dx$$

$$E_{crush} = \frac{1}{2} k x_{max}^2$$

**Step 3 — Plug in realistic values:**

- $k = 300\,\text{kN/m} = 3 \times 10^5\,\text{N/m}$
- $x_{max} = 0.25\,\text{m}$ (25 cm of door intrusion — a "well-managed" crash)

$$E_{crush} = \frac{1}{2} \times (3 \times 10^5) \times (0.25)^2$$

$$E_{crush} = \frac{1}{2} \times 3 \times 10^5 \times 0.0625$$

$$\boxed{E_{crush} \approx 9{,}375\,\text{J} \approx 9.4\,\text{kJ}}$$

**Step 4 — What if the door is "softer" (lower $k$)?**

If we drop stiffness to $k = 150\,\text{kN/m}$ but allow greater intrusion $x_{max} = 0.35\,\text{m}$:

$$E_{crush}' = \frac{1}{2} \times (1.5 \times 10^5) \times (0.35)^2 = 9187\,\text{J} \approx 9.2\,\text{kJ}$$

> **Insight:** Both designs absorb similar energy (~9.2 kJ), but the second design allows 10 cm more intrusion. **This is the central engineering trade-off in side impact design:** stiffness (which limits intrusion) versus energy absorption (which limits force transmission).

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Velocity of Intruding Door (Force-Impulse Method)

The rate of door intrusion is a direct input to thoracic injury. We model the door as a rigid mass $m_d \approx 35\,\text{kg}$ struck by the door-side mass during impact.

**Step 1 — Apply the impulse-momentum theorem:**

$$\int_{0}^{t_{impact}} F(t)\,dt = m_d \cdot v_{door}$$

**Step 2 — Assume a triangular force pulse with peak $F_{peak} = 80\,\text{kN}$ and duration $\Delta t = 0.05\,\text{s}$:**

$$\bar{F} \cdot \Delta t = \frac{1}{2} \times 80{,}000 \times 0.05 = 2000\,\text{N·s}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for door velocity:**

$$v_{door} = \frac{2000}{35} = 57.1\,\text{m/s}$$

This is unrealistically high — which tells us the **rigid mass model is inadequate**. In reality, the door's structural deformation absorbs most of the impulse. We refine:

**Step 4 — Corrected model: Door as a spring-mass-damper:**

$$m_d \ddot{x} + c \dot{x} + k x = F(t)$$

Solving numerically with $c = 5000\,\text{N·s/m}$ and $k = 300{,}000\,\text{N/m}$:

$$\boxed{v_{door,peak} \approx 8\text{–}12\,\text{m/s (typical in real crash tests)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Euro NCAP Limit:** Intrusion velocity at any dummy-contact point should be **< 5 m/s** to avoid severe thoracic injury.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: HIC Calculation from Recorded Acceleration Data

A crash test records the following head acceleration time-history (resultant magnitude, sampled every 1 ms):

| Time (ms) | a (g) | Time (ms) | a (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 2 | 12 | 78 |
| 2 | 8 | 14 | 65 |
| 4 | 20 | 16 | 48 |
| 6 | 42 | 18 | 35 |
| 8 | 70 | 20 | 22 |
| 10 | 85 | 22 | 12 |

**Step 1 — Identify the 15 ms window with maximum HIC value.**

By inspection, the window from $t_1 = 6\,\text{ms}$ to $t_2 = 21\,\text{ms}$ contains the highest sustained acceleration. Use this window.

**Step 2 — Compute the average acceleration over the window:**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt \approx \frac{1}{15\,\text{ms}}\sum a_i \cdot \Delta t$$

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{15}\left[42+70+85+78+65+48+35+22+12+8+5+3+2+1+1\right] \approx \frac{477}{15} \approx 31.8\,g$$

(Note: intermediate values interpolated between tabulated points.)

**Step 3 — Compute HIC₁₅:**

$$HIC_{15} = \bar{a}^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)$$

$$HIC_{15} = (31.8)^{2.5} \times 0.015$$

$$(31.8)^{2.5} = 31.8^2 \times 31.8^{0.5} = 1011.24 \times 5.64 = 5703.4$$

$$HIC_{15} = 5703.4 \times 0.015 = 85.6$$

$$\boxed{HIC_{15} \approx 86 \ll 1000}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret:**

Head injury is well below the regulatory limit (1000), indicating that the head protection countermeasures (curtain airbag, headliner padding) are functioning effectively for this occupant.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question Paper PEMUT633]**

**Define the Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) test and list its primary components as used in side impact evaluation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) test is a standardized side impact test procedure in which a propelled deformable trolley strikes the side of a stationary subject vehicle (SV) at a defined velocity, simulating an intersection collision.

**Primary components (1 mark each — any three):**
1. **Honeycomb aluminum impact face** — Six rows of crushable aluminum honeycomb blocks (cell size ~19.1 mm) that replicate the front-end stiffness of a typical striking vehicle.
2. **Rigid backing structure** — A stiff rear frame that supports the trolley and provides the inertial mass (950 kg for ECE R95 / AIS-096; 1367 kg for FMVSS 214).
3. **Instrumentation package** — On-board accelerometers, angular rate sensors, and high-speed cameras for impact documentation.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Examination]**

**State the Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI) and Rib Deflection Criterion (RDC) with their respective regulatory limits.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI)** is the arithmetic mean of the peak accelerations recorded at the upper thoracic spine (T1) on the right-side and left-side rib load cells:

$$TTI = \frac{g_R + g_L}{2}$$

**Regulatory limit:** TTI ≤ **90 g** as per ECE R95 and AIS-096 standards. **(1.5 marks)**

**Rib Deflection Criterion (RDC)** is the peak inboard deflection of any individual instrumented rib (typically ribs 1 through 6) measured by potentiometric displacement sensors during impact.

**Regulatory limit:** RDC ≤ **44 mm** (ECE R95 / AIS-096); RDC ≤ **32 mm** under the more stringent Euro NCAP protocol. **(1.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A — Module 3 Choice Option (a)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the side impact test setup as specified under **ECE Regulation R95**, including the test apparatus, dummy positions, and test execution procedure. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define and derive the **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)**. A dummy head records a constant acceleration of $a = 35\,g$ over a 15 ms window. Compute the HIC₁₅ value and state whether this satisfies the FMVSS 214 limit. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**ECE R95 Test Setup — Step-by-Step Breakdown:**

**(i) Test Apparatus (2 marks):**

The Moving Deformable Barrier comprises:
- A rigid trolley of mass **950 ± 20 kg**, including instrumentation.
- Front face fitted with **6 deformable honeycomb aluminum elements**, each block 250 mm tall × 1500 mm wide × 150 mm deep, oriented with cells vertical to provide defined crush stiffness.
- Two **wheel assemblies** with single axle each, allowing free rolling post-impact.
- A **propulsion system** (typically hydraulic or pneumatic) capable of accelerating the MDB to **50.0 ± 0.5 km/h** before release.

**(ii) Dummy Positions (2 marks):**

- **Front seat (driver side, struck side):** ES-2 dummy (50th percentile male) seated in standard position with seat belt fastened. Positioned per H-point machine procedure.
- **Rear seat (struck side):** SID-IIs dummy (small female, 5th percentile) seated similarly.
- Both dummies instrumented per ISO/TS 21902.

**(iii) Test Execution (3 marks):**

1. Subject vehicle is parked on a level, rigid surface with fuel system drained and replaced with equivalent water mass.
2. Doors are closed (no latching force); windows are fully closed.
3. MDB is propelled along the perpendicular axis to the SV's longitudinal axis, with the **centerline of the MDB impact face aligned with the R-point of the front dummy**.
4. MDB strikes at **50 km/h** with no angular offset (±2° tolerance).
5. Data acquisition captures high-speed video, accelerations, and load cell readings from **T = −50 ms** to **T = +200 ms**.
6. Post-test: Dummy ICs computed, vehicle photographed, structural deformation analyzed.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Derivation of HIC (3.5 marks):**

The Head Injury Criterion was developed by **Gadd** (1966) and later refined by **Versace** (1971) into the HIC formulation. It integrates the resultant head acceleration over a time interval $(t_1, t_2)$ to capture the severity of the acceleration pulse.

The general **Gadd Severity Index (GSI)** is:

$$GSI = \int_{0}^{T} a^{2.5}(t)\, dt$$

where $a$ is in units of $g$ and $t$ is in seconds. Versace modified this to a sliding-window formulation:

$$HIC = \max_{t_1, t_2}\left\{ \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right\}$$

For regulatory use, the window is capped at $t_2 - t_1 \le 15\,\text{ms}$, yielding **HIC₁₅**.

**Numerical computation (3.5 marks):**

Given constant $a = 35\,g$ over the 15 ms window:

**Step 1 — Compute average acceleration:**

$$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a\,dt = 35\,g$$

**Step 2 — Apply HIC₁₅ formula:**

$$HIC_{15} = (\bar{a})^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)$$

$$HIC_{15} = (35)^{2.5} \times 0.015$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate $(35)^{2.5}$:**

$$35^{2.5} = 35^2 \times 35^{0.5} = 1225 \times 5.916 = 7247.1$$

**Step 4 — Final multiplication:**

$$HIC_{15} = 7247.1 \times 0.015 \approx 108.7$$

$$\boxed{HIC_{15} \approx 109}$$

**Step 5 — Compare with FMVSS 214 limit:**

$$HIC_{15} = 109 \ll 1000 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{PASS (well within limit)} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students frequently **omit the time-window factor** $(t_2 - t_1)$ in the HIC formula, computing only $(\bar{a})^{2.5}$ and reporting a value like "7247" — this is **worth zero marks** for the calculation. Always include the time-window multiplication. Also remember $a$ must be in $g$ units, not $\text{m/s}^2$.

---

### Question B — Module 3 Choice Option (b)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, End Semester Evaluation]**

**(a)** Describe the **six major injury criteria (ICs)** measured in side impact testing and state their respective regulatory limits. Explain why each region is measured. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The MDB has a mass of $m = 950\,\text{kg}$ and strikes the SV at $v = 50\,\text{km/h}$. Compute the kinetic energy and momentum. If 45% of this kinetic energy is absorbed by the door intrusion over a stroke of $x = 0.20\,\text{m}$, determine the **average lateral door stiffness**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**The Six Major Injury Criteria in Side Impact Testing:**

| # | Criterion | Region | Why Measured | Regulatory Limit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **HIC₁₅** | Head | To assess skull fracture and traumatic brain injury risk from acceleration pulses. | ≤ 1000 (FMVSS 214, ECE R95, AIS-096) |
| 2 | **TTI** | Upper Thorax (T1) | To quantify rib cage and thoracic spine acceleration injury risk. | ≤ 90 g (ECE R95, AIS-096) |
| 3 | **RDC** | Ribs (1–6) | To directly measure rib deflection, the primary predictor of flail chest and lung injury. | ≤ 44 mm (ECE), ≤ 32 mm (Euro NCAP) |
| 4 | **APF** | Abdomen | To assess risk of internal organ (liver, spleen, kidney) rupture from intrusion. | ≤ 2.5 kN (ECE R95) |
| 5 | **PPA** | Pelvis | To quantify risk of pelvic ring fracture from high pelvis acceleration. | ≤ 130 g (FMVSS 214, AIS-096) |
| 6 | **FCF** | Femur | To assess femur shaft fracture risk from knee bolsters or instrument panel contact. | ≤ 10 kN (ECE), ≤ 6.1 kN (FMVSS) |

**Justification (one sentence each, 1 mark):**

1. **HIC₁₅** — Head strikes are common in lateral crashes via curtain airbag interaction; brain injury has long-term disability consequences. ✓
2. **TTI** — The thoracic spine is a major injury pathway in side impact due to the lateral compression of the rib cage. ✓
3. **RDC** — Direct rib deflection measurement is the most biofidelic predictor of thoracic injury severity. ✓
4. **APF** — The abdomen is the **third-most-injured region** in side impacts (per CIREN data) due to the absence of bony protection. ✓
5. **PPA** — Pelvic fractures are life-threatening due to potential damage to major blood vessels; peak acceleration is the standard surrogate. ✓
6. **FCF** — Femur fractures impair occupant egress and increase mortality risk; compression force is a clean surrogate measurement. ✓

**Marks breakdown:** Table = 4 marks; Justifications = 3 marks.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute the kinetic energy of the MDB (2 marks):**

Convert velocity to SI units:

$$v = 50\,\text{km/h} = \frac{50 \times 1000}{3600}\,\text{m/s} = 13.89\,\text{m/s}$$

Apply $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$:

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 950 \times (13.89)^2$$

$$E_k = \frac{1}{2} \times 950 \times 192.93 = 91{,}640\,\text{J}$$

$$\boxed{E_k \approx 91.64\,\text{kJ}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the MDB momentum (1 mark):**

$$p = m v = 950 \times 13.89 = 13{,}195.5\,\text{kg·m/s}$$

$$\boxed{p \approx 13.20\,\text{kN·s}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute energy absorbed by door intrusion (2 marks):**

$$E_{door} = 0.45 \times E_k = 0.45 \times 91{,}640 = 41{,}238\,\text{J}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the average door stiffness (2 marks):**

Assuming a linear force-deflection characteristic: $F = k x$, and energy absorbed:

$$E_{door} = \int_0^{x_{max}} kx\,dx = \frac{1}{2} k x_{max}^2$$

Solve for $k$:

$$k = \frac{2 E_{door}}{x_{max}^2} = \frac{2 \times 41{,}238}{(0.20)^2} = \frac{82{,}476}{0.04}$$

$$\boxed{k \approx 2.06 \times 10^6\,\text{N/m} \approx 2060\,\text{kN/m}}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret:**

This average door stiffness of ~2060 kN/m is on the **high side** of typical passenger car values (typical range: 500–3000 kN/m). It indicates a **stiff, intrusion-resistant structure** — beneficial for limiting door intrusion, but it transmits a **higher force pulse** to the occupant thorax. A designer would balance this with energy-absorbing trim and airbag cushioning.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Question B):** A frequent mistake is computing $\bar{F} = E_{door}/x$ and then reporting $k = \bar{F}/x$ (incorrect by a factor of 2). The correct relation is $k = 2E_{door}/x^2$ for a linear spring. Marks will be deducted if the constant-of-2 is missing.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 Examination (Box Tick Guide):**

- [ ] **Side impact definition** — Lateral collision test using MDB on a stationary SV.
- [ ] **Test parameters** — ECE R95: 950 kg @ 50 km/h; FMVSS 214: 1367 kg @ 54 km/h; Euro NCAP: 1400 kg @ 60 km/h; AIS-096: 950 kg @ 50 km/h.
- [ ] **Six ICs**: HIC₁₅, TTI, RDC, APF, PPA, FCF.
- [ ] **Regulatory limits** — HIC₁₅ ≤ 1000, TTI ≤ 90 g, RDC ≤ 44 mm, APF ≤ 2.5 kN, PPA ≤ 130 g, FCF ≤ 10 kN.
- [ ] **Dummy types** — ES-2 (front), SID-IIs (rear), WorldSID (modern).
- [ ] **HIC formula** — $[\bar{a}]^{2.5} \cdot \Delta t$ with $\Delta t \le 15$ ms.
- [ ] **TTI formula** — $(g_R + g_L)/2$.
- [ ] **Kinetic energy formula** — $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$.
- [ ] **MDB kinetic energy at ECE R95 conditions** — ~91.6 kJ.
- [ ] **Door stiffness formula** — $k = 2E_{door}/x^2$ for linear F-x characteristic.
- [ ] **Honeycomb specifications** — Al-3003, cell ~19.1 mm, density ~53 kg/m³.
- [ ] **Crush space** — Side door has only ~15–25 cm of available crush space.
- [ ] **Structural countermeasures** — B-pillar hot stamping, side impact beams (boron steel), curtain airbags, thorax airbags, energy-absorbing door trim, seat-mounted airbags.
- [ ] **Engineering trade-off** — Higher stiffness → less intrusion but higher force pulse; lower stiffness → more intrusion but softer pulse.
- [ ] **Euro NCAP vs ECE R95** — Euro NCAP is more stringent on every IC limit; it also includes oblique pole test (not covered in standard MDB test).
- [ ] **AIS-096 (India)** — Indian standard adopted from ECE R95 with local modifications; enforced by MoRTH for new passenger car models.
- [ ] **Fatality statistics** — Side impacts account for ~27% of vehicle occupant fatalities.
- [ ] **Instrumentation** — Tri-axial accelerometers, load cells, potentiometers, high-speed cameras (≥1000 fps).

**Golden Equation Set (PEMUT633 — Side Impact):**
- $E_k = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$
- $p = mv$
- $HIC_{15} = \bar{a}^{2.5} \cdot \Delta t$
- $TTI = (g_R + g_L)/2$
- $E_{crush} = \frac{1}{2}kx^2$
- $k = 2E/x^2$
- $v_{combined} = \frac{m_1 v_1}{m_1 + m_2}$ (plastic collision)
- $\Delta v_{occupant} = \frac{m_{barrier} v_{barrier}}{m_{barrier} + m_{SV}}$
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Crash Test Sensor
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Crash Test Sensor

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Crash Test Sensor (Automotive Crash Sensor / Crash Discrimination Sensor):**
> A crash test sensor is a high-bandwidth, high-g-range **transducer** (typically an accelerometer, pressure transducer, or angular rate sensor) that is mounted at strategic structural and peripheral locations of a vehicle to convert the **kinematic, dynamic, or pressure signatures** generated during a vehicular collision into **measurable electrical signals**. These signals are processed by the Airbag Control Unit (ACU) / Electronic Control Unit (ECU) using **crash discrimination algorithms** to determine the **severity, direction, and type of crash event** within a decision window of **5 ms to 20 ms**, and to trigger the appropriate restraint system (airbags, pretensioners, active hood, etc.) at the precise moment dictated by the crash pulse.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the crash test sensor is studied as part of **passive safety instrumentation**, falling under the broader domain of **vehicle crashworthiness evaluation** and **restraint system deployment logic**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: The Reflex Nerve of the Human Body**
> Imagine a human hand touching a hot stove. The nerves in the skin (sensors) instantly detect the heat and pressure, send an electrical signal through the spinal cord (wiring harness) to the brain (ECU). The brain processes this in milliseconds and fires a command to the muscles (airbag inflators) to pull the hand away. A **crash test sensor works identically** — it is the "reflex nerve" of the vehicle. It does not "think"; it only **detects, quantifies, and transmits** the violent deceleration pulse so the central brain (Airbag ECU) can fire the airbag at exactly the right millisecond. If the sensor is too slow, the airbag deploys *after* the occupant's head has already hit the steering wheel. If the sensor is too sensitive, it deploys on a speed breaker, potentially causing injury and costly repair.

### Standard Reference Metrics (KTU Board-Relevant)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Crash Severity Thresholds (used globally & in Indian AIS-098 / AIS-099 / AIS-100 testing):**
> - **Deployment threshold:** 2 g to 5 g (low-speed non-deployment), **above 14–20 g** for first-stage airbag firing (frontal)
> - **Time-To-Fire (TTF):** **≤ 5 ms** for pretensioner, **≤ 10–30 ms** for driver airbag, **≤ 5 ms** for far-side airbag
> - **Sensor bandwidth:** DC to **1.5 kHz** minimum
> - **g-range of accelerometer:** ±50 g to ±500 g (depending on location — front sensors need higher range)
> - **Sampling rate of ECU:** **≥ 4 kHz** for frontal, **≥ 8 kHz** for side impact
> - **Discrimination time window:** 0 ms to **30 ms** after first contact
> - **Crash pulse integration constant:** typically uses **jerk (da/dt)** as a discriminator

### Physical Constants Used in Crash Sensor Analysis

- Standard gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
- Speed of sound in air (relevant for pressure wave side sensors): **c = 343 m/s** at 20 °C
- Density of air: **ρ = 1.204 kg/m³** (for pressure wave analysis)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Crash Test Sensors

Crash test sensors are classified by the **physical principle** they exploit and the **crash event** they are designed to detect.

**By Working Principle:**

1. **Piezoelectric Accelerometer** — uses a piezo crystal (e.g., quartz, PZT) that generates a charge when subjected to acceleration. Excellent for high-frequency crash events (> 1 kHz bandwidth).
2. **MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) Capacitive Accelerometer** — a tiny proof mass moves between capacitor plates under deceleration, changing capacitance. Dominant in modern ECUs due to low cost and CMOS compatibility.
3. **Strain Gauge Accelerometer** — a cantilever beam with bonded strain gauges flexes under acceleration, changing resistance (Wheatstone bridge).
4. **Piezoresistive Accelerometer** — uses silicon strain gauges whose resistance changes with mechanical stress.
5. **Surface Acoustic Wave (SAW) Sensor** — used in **passive/remote** keyless systems, also emerging in crash detection.
6. **Pressure / Barometric Sensor** — for **side impact** detection; senses the rapid pressure rise inside the door cavity when intruded by another vehicle.
7. **Angular Rate Sensor (Gyroscope)** — for **rollover** detection; measures roll rate (°/s).
8. **Satellite Sensor (Up-Front Sensor)** — mounted near the front of the vehicle to capture the initial crash pulse earlier than the centrally located ECU.

**By Location & Function:**

| Sensor Type | Location | Detects | Typical Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Front (primary) | Front bumper / radiator support | Frontal impact | 14–25 g |
| Up-Front Satellite | 200–400 mm behind bumper | Early frontal pulse | 4–8 g |
| Side Pressure | Inside door cavity | Side impact | Pressure rise > 20 kPa/ms |
| Side Acceleration | B-pillar / rocker | Side impact | 8–15 g lateral |
| Rollover Gyro | Center of gravity (often ECU itself) | Rollover event | 45–60° roll angle or > 100 °/s roll rate |
| Safing Sensor | Inside ECU | Confirm "real" crash vs. rough road | 2–5 g |
| Seat Occupant Sensor | Seat cushion | Occupant presence / weight | 12–47 kg discrimination |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In an ESE answer, *always* mention the **"Satellite + Safing"** dual-path architecture. The satellite sensor fires "early" (suspect event) and the safing sensor must confirm (corroborate) within a time window — this prevents inadvertent deployment on a pothole or hammer-blow to the bumper.

### 2.2 Working Principle — Stepwise Logic

**Stage 1 — Mechanical Coupling:**
The crash event imparts a **deceleration pulse (crash pulse)** to the vehicle body. The sensor housing is rigidly bolted to the body, so the internal proof mass experiences an **inertial force** opposite to the deceleration.

**Stage 2 — Transduction:**
The inertial force displaces the proof mass relative to the housing. This displacement is converted into an electrical signal:
- **Piezoelectric:** mechanical strain → charge (q = d · F)
- **Capacitive:** displacement (Δx) → capacitance change (C = εA/d, d changes)
- **Piezoresistive:** strain → resistance change (ΔR/R = G · ε)

**Stage 3 — Signal Conditioning:**
The raw millivolt signal is amplified, filtered (anti-aliasing filter at ~1.5 kHz cutoff), and digitized by an ADC inside the ECU.

**Stage 4 — Crash Discrimination Algorithm:**
The ECU executes a **real-time algorithm** that performs:
1. **Energy integration** — ∫(a · v) dt or jerk-based discrimination
2. **Velocity change computation** — Δv = ∫a dt
3. **Displacement calculation** — x = ∫∫a dt²
4. **Comparison with lookup tables** (multi-dimensional map of v₀, angle, mass, overlap)

**Stage 5 — Deployment Command:**
If all criteria are satisfied, the ECU fires the appropriate squib (pyrotechnic initiator) within **≤ 2 ms** of the decision. The airbag inflates via a **NaN₃ (sodium azide)** chemical reaction producing N₂ gas.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly relevant** for solving Module-3 numerical/analytical questions on crash test sensors and crash pulse analysis. The vertical bar symbol is rendered using `\vert` to preserve table integrity.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a = \dfrac{dv}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2 x}{dt^2}$ | Definition of acceleration (the primary signal a crash sensor measures) | m/s² |
| 2 | $F = m \cdot a$ | Newton's 2nd law — inertial force on proof mass | N |
| 3 | $g_{level} = \dfrac{a}{9.81}$ | Deceleration expressed in "g" (typical threshold: 2 g–500 g) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\Delta v = \int_{0}^{t} a(t)\,dt$ | Velocity change of vehicle during crash (velocity reduction) | m/s |
| 5 | $E_{absorbed} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | Total kinetic energy to be absorbed in crash (m = effective mass) | J |
| 6 | $E_{crush} = \int_{0}^{delta} F_{crush}\,ds$ | Energy absorbed by structural crush (s = deformation depth) | J |
| 7 | $CFC = \dfrac{a_{filtered}}{a_{original}}$ | Channel Frequency Class — SAE J211 filter (commonly CFC 60 = 100 Hz, CFC 1000 = 1 kHz) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $HIC = \left[\dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | Head Injury Criterion (occupant injury metric) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $q = d_{piezo} \cdot F$ | Charge generated by piezoelectric sensor (d₃₃ = piezoelectric charge coefficient) | C |
| 10 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d - \Delta x}$ | Capacitance of MEMS accelerometer gap; Δx from deceleration | F |
| 11 | $f_{resonant} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ | Natural frequency of accelerometer proof mass-spring system | Hz |
| 12 | $a_{range} = \pm \dfrac{F_{FS}}{k_{spring}} \cdot \omega_n^2$ | Maximum measurable acceleration (depends on proof mass & spring stiffness) | m/s² |
| 13 | $p_{side} = p_0 + \rho c \cdot v_{intrusion}$ | Acoustic pressure wave generated by side intrusion (p₀ = atmospheric) | Pa |
| 14 | $jerk = \dfrac{da}{dt}$ | Time-rate of change of acceleration; used as **primary crash discriminator** | m/s³ |
| 15 | $v_{intrusion} = \dfrac{\Delta x_{door}}{\Delta t}$ | Intrusion velocity of door (used in side crash pulse) | m/s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Restraint System Deployment Logic** — Direct input to airbag, pretensioner, and active-bonnet firing decisions.
2. **Event Data Recorder (EDR)** — Records last 5 s of pre-crash and 300 ms of post-crash data for accident reconstruction (mandatory in many countries).
3. **Crash Test Dummy (ATD) Instrumentation** — Sensors embedded in dummies (Hybrid III, THOR, WorldSID) measure biofidelic response.
4. **Pedestrian Protection** — Up-front sensors trigger active hood lift in 20–40 ms to cushion the head impact.
5. **Insurance & Legal Forensics** — EDR data used in court; AIS-099 (India), 49 CFR Part 563 (USA) make this mandatory.
6. **NCAP Star Rating** — Sensor data drives the Euro NCAP / Bharat NCAP / GSR tests.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Deceleration Pulse to Velocity Change and Displacement (Crash Pulse Integration)

A crash test sensor outputs an **acceleration time-history** $a(t)$. From this single signal, the ECU (and the KTU exam) can derive the **velocity change** and **displacement** experienced by the occupant compartment.

**Given:** $a(t)$ measured by the sensor over a crash duration of $t = 0$ to $t = t_f$.

**Step 1 — Write the fundamental kinematic relation between acceleration and velocity.**

$$
a(t) = \frac{dv(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Integrate both sides with respect to time from $t = 0$ to $t = t_f$ to obtain velocity change.**

$$
\int_{0}^{t_f} a(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{t_f} \frac{dv(t)}{dt}\,dt
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus on the right-hand side.**

$$
\int_{0}^{t_f} a(t)\,dt = v(t_f) - v(0)
$$

**Step 4 — Recognize that the left-hand side is the area under the acceleration–time curve, and the right-hand side is the velocity change $\Delta v$.**

$$
\Delta v = v(t_f) - v(0) = \int_{0}^{t_f} a(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 5 — Use the Trapezoidal Rule (commonly used in ECUs) for numerical integration.**

$$
\Delta v \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{a(t_i) + a(t_{i-1})}{2} \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})
$$

**Step 6 — Similarly, integrate again to obtain the displacement (crush) profile.**

$$
x(t) = \int_{0}^{t} v(\tau)\,d\tau = \iint_{0}^{t} a(\tau)\,d\tau\,d\tau
$$

**Step 7 — Express the final displacement in the same numerical form for engineering use.**

$$
x(t) \approx \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{j=1}^{i} \frac{a(t_j) + a(t_{j-1})}{2} \cdot (t_j - t_{j-1}) \cdot (t_i - t_{i-1})
$$

**Result — The Working Crash Pulse Equations:**

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta v = \int_{0}^{t_f} a(t)\,dt \qquad \text{and} \qquad x(t) = \iint_{0}^{t} a(\tau)\,d\tau\,d\tau\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU 2024 Board Exams:** A common 14-mark question in Module 3 is *"A frontal crash test yields the following deceleration data... Calculate the velocity change and the energy absorbed by the occupant compartment."* The above derivation is the **exact** working expected by the examiner.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example: Crash Sensor Threshold and Energy Calculation

**Problem Statement (typical KTU 14-mark style):**
> A 1500 kg passenger car traveling at 50 km/h (13.89 m/s) strikes a rigid barrier. The front crash sensor (an accelerometer) records a constant deceleration of **15 g** over a crush time of **80 ms**. Determine:
> (a) The velocity change and confirm full stop occurred.
> (b) The energy absorbed by the front structure.
> (c) The average force on the barrier.
> (d) The Head Injury Criterion (HIC) value if the resultant head acceleration pulse is approximately constant at 50 g for 3 ms.

**Given Data:**
- $m = 1500$ kg
- $v_0 = 50$ km/h = $13.89$ m/s
- $a = 15\,g = 15 \times 9.81 = 147.15$ m/s²
- $\Delta t = 80$ ms = 0.080 s
- $a_{head} = 50\,g = 490.5$ m/s², $t_2 - t_1 = 3$ ms = 0.003 s

**Solution (a) — Velocity Change:**

$$
\Delta v = a \cdot \Delta t
$$

$$
\Delta v = 147.15 \times 0.080 = 11.77 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Conversion logic:** The velocity reduces by 11.77 m/s from 13.89 m/s, leaving a residual velocity:

$$
v_{residual} = 13.89 - 11.77 = 2.12 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Confirmation:** Since $v_{residual} \neq 0$, the vehicle has **NOT** come to a complete stop in 80 ms. The crash duration is therefore longer. To fully stop:

$$
t_{stop} = \frac{v_0}{a} = \frac{13.89}{147.15} = 0.0944 \text{ s} = 94.4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Solution (b) — Energy Absorbed by the Front Structure:**

$$
E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (13.89)^2
$$

$$
E_{absorbed} = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 192.93 = 144,698 \text{ J} \approx 144.7 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Solution (c) — Average Force on the Barrier:**

$$
F_{avg} = m \cdot a = 1500 \times 147.15 = 220,725 \text{ N} \approx 220.7 \text{ kN}
$$

**Solution (d) — Head Injury Criterion (HIC):**

$$
HIC = \left[\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)
$$

Since acceleration is approximately constant at 50 g = 490.5 m/s²:

$$
\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt = 490.5 \times 0.003 = 1.4715 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt = \frac{1.4715}{0.003} = 490.5 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
HIC = [490.5]^{2.5} \times 0.003
$$

Compute $[490.5]^{2.5}$:

$$
[490.5]^{2} = 240,590.25
$$

$$
[490.5]^{0.5} = 22.147
$$

$$
[490.5]^{2.5} = 240,590.25 \times 22.147 = 5,328,745
$$

$$
HIC = 5,328,745 \times 0.003 = 15,986
$$

**Conclusion:** HIC ≈ 15,986 — this is **well above the AIS-098 threshold of HIC = 1000**, indicating a severe injury risk. (Threshold for survival ≈ 1000; threshold for AIS ≥ 4 injury ≈ 1500–2000.)

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection by Crash Event

| Crash Event | Primary Sensor | Secondary Sensor | Algorithm Discriminator | Typical Deployment Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frontal full-width rigid barrier @ 56 km/h | Up-front satellite + central ECU accelerometer | Safing sensor | $\Delta v$ over first 30 ms, jerk | ≤ 20 ms |
| Frontal offset deformable @ 64 km/h | Up-front + ECU accelerometer | Safing | Crush depth & energy at 50 ms | ≤ 25 ms |
| Side MDB (Mobile Deformable Barrier) @ 50 km/h | Side pressure sensor (door) | B-pillar accelerometer | Pressure rise rate $\geq 20$ kPa/ms | ≤ 5 ms (early) |
| Side pole impact | Side accelerometer | Pressure | Acceleration peak > 8 g in < 5 ms | ≤ 5 ms |
| Rollover (tripped / untripped) | Roll-rate gyro | Yaw-rate gyro | Roll angle > 45° in < 1 s, or vertical lift sensor | Variable (curtain airbag stays inflated 5–8 s) |
| Rear impact | Rear accelerometer (rare) | ECU accelerometer | $\Delta v$ negative (acceleration) | Pretensioner ≤ 10 ms |
| Pedestrian (head impact on hood) | Up-front + hood-contact sensor | Bumper pressure | Velocity + hood stiffness map | Active hood in 20–40 ms |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Modern Airbag Control Unit (ACU) Architecture with Crash Sensors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Front Left Crash Sensor] --> MUX
    A2[Front Right Crash Sensor] --> MUX
    A3[Up-Front Satellite Sensor] --> MUX
    A4[Side Left Pressure Sensor] --> MUX2
    A5[Side Right Pressure Sensor] --> MUX2
    A6[B-Pillar Accelerometer Left] --> MUX2
    A7[B-Pillar Accelerometer Right] --> MUX2
    A8[Roll Rate Gyro] --> MUX3
    A9[Yaw Rate Gyro] --> MUX3
    A10[Occupant Detection Sensor] --> MUX3
    A11[Safing Sensor Internal] --> MUX
    MUX --> ADC[16-bit ADC @ 4 kHz sampling]
    MUX2 --> ADC
    MUX3 --> ADC
    ADC --> DSP[DSP Core / Crash Algorithm Processor]
    DSP --> LOGIC{Discrimination Logic\nDelta-v, jerk, energy}
    LOGIC -->|FIRE| DRV1[Driver Airbag Squib Driver]
    LOGIC -->|FIRE| DRV2[Passenger Airbag Squib Driver]
    LOGIC -->|FIRE| DRV3[Side Curtain Squib Driver]
    LOGIC -->|FIRE| DRV4[Seatbelt Pretensioner Driver]
    LOGIC -->|NO FIRE| LOG[Logged to EDR / Cleared]
    DSP --> EDR[Event Data Recorder\nPre-crash 5s, Post-crash 300ms]
    DSP --> CAN[CAN Bus Output to BCM / Cluster]
    DRV1 --> BATT[Backup Capacitor\n2.5 J Reserve]
    DRV2 --> BATT
    DRV3 --> BATT
    DRV4 --> BATT
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide for KTU students:** The flow is **sensor → multiplexer → ADC → DSP → discrimination logic → squib driver → inflator.** The **backup capacitor** ensures deployment works even if the main battery is severed during the crash (a common scenario — the alternator-to-battery cable is often the first thing to break).

---

### 4.2 Functional Flow — Crash Discrimination Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0([Vehicle Ignition ON, System Armed]) --> S1[Read all sensor channels @ 4 kHz]
    S1 --> S2{Satellite sensor above\n4 g threshold?}
    S2 -->|No| S1
    S2 -->|Yes| S3[Start 30 ms integration window]
    S3 --> S4[Compute jerk = da/dt]
    S4 --> S5{jerk > 50 g per second?}
    S5 -->|No| S6[Reset, classify as pothole or curb strike]
    S6 --> S1
    S5 -->|Yes| S7[Compute Delta-v over window]
    S7 --> S8{Delta-v above 8 km/h\nand below 50 km/h?}
    S8 -->|Out of range| S9[Log event, do not fire]
    S9 --> S1
    S8 -->|In range| S10{Safing sensor confirms\nabove 2 g?}
    S10 -->|No| S11[Inhibit, possible sensor fault]
    S11 --> S1
    S10 -->|Yes| S12[Look up deployment table\nby overlap, angle, mass]
    S12 --> S13{Select deployment stage}
    S13 -->|Stage 1 low risk| S14[Fire pretensioner only, delay stage 2]
    S13 -->|Stage 2 moderate| S15[Fire pretensioner + 1st stage airbag]
    S13 -->|Stage 3 severe| S16[Fire all stages simultaneously]
    S14 --> S17([Event Logged to EDR])
    S15 --> S17
    S16 --> S17
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why two thresholds (4 g satellite + 2 g safing)?** This is the classic **dual-threshold architecture** mandated by ECE R94 and AIS-098. It virtually eliminates inadvertent deployment while keeping the response time below 20 ms.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Typical Crash Test Dummy (ATD) Sensor Instrumentation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    HEAD[Head — 3-axis Accelerometer + Angular Rate Sensor]
    NECK[Neck — Upper and Lower Load Cell]
    CHEST[Chest — 3-axis Accelerometer + Chest Deflection Potentiometer]
    LUMBAR[Lumbar Spine — Load Cell]
    FEMUR[L Femur — Load Cell]
    FEMURR[R Femur — Load Cell]
    TIBIA[L Tibia — Upper and Lower Load Cells]
    TIBIAR[R Tibia — Upper and Lower Load Cells]
    FOOTL[L Foot — 3-axis Accelerometer]
    FOOTR[R Foot — 3-axis Accelerometer]
    PELVIS[Pelvis — 3-axis Accelerometer]
    HEAD --> DAQ[Data Acquisition System\nOnboard at 20 kHz]
    NECK --> DAQ
    CHEST --> DAQ
    LUMBAR --> DAQ
    FEMUR --> DAQ
    FEMURR --> DAQ
    TIBIA --> DAQ
    TIBIAR --> DAQ
    FOOTL --> DAQ
    FOOTR --> DAQ
    PELVIS --> DAQ
    DAQ --> ONBOARD[Onboard Recording Module]
    DAQ --> WIRELESS[Wireless Telemetry to Test Rig Computer]
    ONBOARD --> POST[Post-Test Analysis HIC, NIC, Chest Deflection, Femur Load]
    WIRELESS --> POST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Biofidelic Sensors in Dummies (Hybrid III, THOR-50M, WorldSID):** Each sensor is calibrated to a specific **CFC (Channel Frequency Class)** filter (CFC 60 for head, CFC 180 for chest, CFC 600 for femur) per SAE J211 / ISO 6487.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
> *Define a crash test sensor. List the three main types of crash sensors used in modern passenger cars and state one specific function of each.*

**Model Answer:**

A crash test sensor is a transducer that converts the mechanical, kinematic, or pressure input from a vehicle collision into a measurable electrical signal, which is processed by the airbag control unit (ACU) to determine crash severity and deploy appropriate restraint systems within milliseconds.

**The three main types are:**

1. **Front Crash Sensor (Up-Front Satellite Accelerometer):** Mounted near the front of the vehicle (radiator support area) to detect the initial crash pulse early and reduce decision time for airbag deployment.

2. **Side Pressure Sensor:** Mounted inside the door cavity to detect the rapid pressure rise caused by intruding structure during a side impact — used for early firing of side/curtain airbags.

3. **Safing Sensor:** A low-g accelerometer internal to the ACU that "confirms" the event is a real crash and not a road disturbance, before the ACU fires the squib.

*(Word limit: ~80 words. Diagram may be added for full marks.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
> *What is the role of a "safing sensor" in an airbag system, and why is the dual-threshold (satellite + safing) architecture mandated by safety standards?*

**Model Answer:**

A **safing sensor** is a low-threshold (~2 g) accelerometer housed inside the ACU that must corroborate the signal from a remote satellite sensor (~4–8 g) before the airbag squib is fired. Its role is **inadvertent deployment prevention** — to ensure that a single noisy or faulty signal does not trigger the airbag.

The **dual-threshold architecture** is mandated by **ECE R94** (Europe) and **AIS-098** (India) because:
- The **satellite sensor** provides **early warning** (placed far forward of the passenger compartment) — but is exposed to potholes, kerb strikes, and hammer blows.
- The **safing sensor** provides **independent confirmation** — it is mechanically isolated inside the ACU, reducing the chance of both being triggered by the same non-crash event.
- The probability of **both** being falsely triggered simultaneously is the **product** of their individual false-trigger probabilities, making the system exponentially more reliable.

This architecture virtually eliminates inadvertent deployment while keeping total response time under 20 ms.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (ESE Style) (14 Marks Each)

> **Important KTU 2024 ESE Rule:** Each Part-B question has **Module Internal Choice** — the student must answer **one** of the two alternatives. Both alternatives below carry equal marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, S7 B.Tech Model Paper]

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the architecture of a **modern airbag control unit (ACU)**. Explain the function of the satellite sensor, safing sensor, and occupant detection sensor. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A vehicle of mass **1200 kg** travelling at **64 km/h** strikes a rigid wall. The crash sensor records a constant deceleration of **12 g** for **90 ms**, after which the vehicle rebounds with a velocity of **4 km/h** in the opposite direction. Calculate:
> 1. The velocity change during the impact phase.
> 2. The energy absorbed by the front crumple zone.
> 3. The average force exerted on the passenger compartment.
> 4. The HIC if the head accelerometer records a triangular pulse peaking at 60 g with rise time 4 ms and decay time 4 ms. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

Refer to the **Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1** for the ACU architecture.

**Architecture Description (in essay form):**
A modern ACU is a distributed system comprising:
- **Up-front satellite sensors** (one or two) placed in the front crumple zone, providing earliest possible crash pulse.
- **Side pressure sensors** inside the door cavities, and **side acceleration sensors** at the B-pillars, providing omnidirectional coverage.
- A **central ECU** containing the **safing sensor**, a high-speed 16-bit ADC (≥ 4 kHz sampling), a DSP or microcontroller running the crash algorithm, and **squib driver stages** with energy reserve capacitors.
- The **occupant detection sensor** (pressure mat or capacitive sensor in the seat) discriminates between adult, child, and empty seat, deciding whether the passenger airbag should fire and at what stage.
- A **CAN/LIN bus interface** for communication with the instrument cluster (tell-tale), Body Control Module, and the Event Data Recorder (EDR).
- **Backup capacitor** (typically 2.5 J stored) to enable squib firing even if the vehicle battery is disconnected at impact.

The **safing sensor** prevents inadvertent firing; the **occupant sensor** tailors deployment to occupant size and position; the **satellite sensors** reduce the time-to-fire by detecting the crash pulse earlier.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:**
- $m = 1200$ kg
- $v_0 = 64$ km/h = $17.78$ m/s
- $v_f = -4$ km/h = $-1.111$ m/s (negative = rebound)
- $a = 12\,g = 12 \times 9.81 = 117.72$ m/s²
- $\Delta t = 90$ ms = 0.090 s

**Step 1 — Velocity change during impact:**

$$
\Delta v = v_f - v_0 = -1.111 - 17.78 = -18.89 \text{ m/s}
$$

The magnitude of velocity change is $\vert\Delta v\vert = 18.89$ m/s.

**Step 2 — Energy absorbed by the front crumple zone:**

The kinetic energy lost is the sum of initial KE and rebound KE:

$$
E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 + \frac{1}{2} m v_f^2
$$

$$
E_{absorbed} = \frac{1}{2}(1200)(17.78)^2 + \frac{1}{2}(1200)(1.111)^2
$$

$$
E_{absorbed} = 600 \times 316.13 + 600 \times 1.234
$$

$$
E_{absorbed} = 189,675 + 740 = 190,415 \text{ J} \approx 190.4 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Average force on the passenger compartment:**

$$
F_{avg} = m \cdot a = 1200 \times 117.72 = 141,264 \text{ N} \approx 141.3 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 4 — Head Injury Criterion (HIC) for triangular pulse:**

For a triangular pulse of peak $a_{peak} = 60\,g = 588.6$ m/s² and total duration $T = 8$ ms, with $t_1 = 0$ and $t_2 = 8$ ms, the average acceleration is half the peak:

$$
a_{avg} = \frac{1}{2} \times 588.6 = 294.3 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

By the standard HIC formula (for the worst-case 36 ms window — here we use the full 8 ms pulse):

$$
HIC = \left[\frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt\right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)
$$

For a triangular pulse: $\int a\,dt = \frac{1}{2} a_{peak} \cdot T = \frac{1}{2}(588.6)(0.008) = 2.354$ m/s

$$
HIC = \left[\frac{2.354}{0.008}\right]^{2.5} (0.008) = [294.3]^{2.5} \times 0.008
$$

$$
[294.3]^{2} = 86,612.5
$$

$$
[294.3]^{0.5} = 17.155
$$

$$
[294.3]^{2.5} = 86,612.5 \times 17.155 = 1,485,950
$$

$$
HIC = 1,485,950 \times 0.008 = 11,888
$$

**Conclusion:** HIC ≈ 11,888 — this is **dangerously high** (AIS-098 limit is 1000), indicating a high probability of fatal head injury.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Internal Choice]

> **(a)** Explain the working principle of a **MEMS capacitive accelerometer** used as a crash sensor. With a neat sketch, show how deceleration causes a change in capacitance. Derive the relationship between the output voltage and the applied acceleration. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **frontal**, **side**, and **rollover** crash sensor systems in terms of (i) sensor type, (ii) location, (iii) deployment threshold, (iv) time-to-fire, and (v) injury metric used. State the relevant **AIS / ECE R** regulation for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Working Principle:**
A MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) capacitive accelerometer consists of a tiny **proof mass** suspended by flexible beams between two fixed capacitor plates. The whole structure is fabricated on a silicon chip using photolithographic processes.

When the vehicle decelerates during a crash, the proof mass (due to its inertia) tends to continue moving forward relative to the chip, displacing it by a small distance $\Delta x$ toward the rear plate (i.e., away from the front plate).

**Capacitance Change:**

If the nominal gap between the plates is $d$, the area of the plates is $A$, and the dielectric is air (permittivity $\varepsilon_0$), the nominal capacitance is:

$$
C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}
$$

When the proof mass displaces by $\Delta x$, the gap on one side becomes $(d - \Delta x)$ and on the other becomes $(d + \Delta x)$. The two capacitances become:

$$
C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d - \Delta x} \quad \text{and} \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d + \Delta x}
$$

**Differential Capacitance:**

$$
\Delta C = C_1 - C_2 = \varepsilon_0 A \left[\frac{1}{d - \Delta x} - \frac{1}{d + \Delta x}\right]
$$

$$
\Delta C = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{(d + \Delta x) - (d - \Delta x)}{d^2 - (\Delta x)^2} = \varepsilon_0 A \cdot \frac{2\Delta x}{d^2 - (\Delta x)^2}
$$

For small displacements ($\Delta x \ll d$):

$$
\boxed{\;\Delta C \approx \frac{2\varepsilon_0 A \cdot \Delta x}{d^2}\;}
$$

**Displacement due to acceleration:**

The proof mass $m_p$ is suspended by springs of stiffness $k$. Under steady deceleration $a$, the steady-state displacement is:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{m_p \cdot a}{k}
$$

**Output voltage:**

The differential capacitance is fed to a charge amplifier or switched-capacitor readout circuit. The output voltage is proportional to the capacitance change:

$$
V_{out} = \frac{V_{ref} \cdot \Delta C}{C_0} = V_{ref} \cdot \frac{2 \Delta x}{d}
$$

**Substituting** $\Delta x = m_p a / k$:

$$
\boxed{\;V_{out} = \frac{2 V_{ref}}{d} \cdot \frac{m_p}{k} \cdot a = K \cdot a\;}
$$

where $K = \dfrac{2 V_{ref} m_p}{d \cdot k}$ is the **sensitivity** of the accelerometer (typically in V/g or mV/g).

This linear relationship is why MEMS accelerometers are the **industry standard** for crash sensing.

**Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | Frontal Crash Sensor | Side Crash Sensor | Rollover Sensor |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) Sensor type | Up-front satellite MEMS accelerometer + safing accelerometer | Door pressure sensor + B-pillar accelerometer | Angular rate (yaw/roll) gyroscope + vertical accelerometer |
| (ii) Location | Front bumper / radiator support / front rail | Inside door cavity, B-pillar, rocker panel | Centre of gravity (often ECU itself) |
| (iii) Deployment threshold | 14–25 g (satellite 4–8 g for wake-up) | Pressure rise > 20 kPa/ms, OR lateral acceleration > 8 g | Roll angle > 45° within 1.2 s, OR roll rate > 100 °/s |
| (iv) Time-to-fire | ≤ 20 ms (driver airbag), ≤ 10 ms (pretensioner) | ≤ 5 ms (head curtain), ≤ 10 ms (thorax airbag) | Variable — curtain airbag stays inflated 5–8 s |
| (v) Injury metric | HIC, Chest deflection (CFC 180), Femur load (CFC 600) | Rib fracture criterion (RDC), abdominal peak force, HIC | Head excursion out of window, occupant retention |
| Regulation (India) | AIS-098 (frontal ODB), AIS-100 (FFB) | AIS-099 (side MDB), AIS-099 (side pole) | AIS-102 (rollover) |
| Regulation (Global) | ECE R94, FMVSS 208 | ECE R95, FMVSS 214 | ECE R21, FMVSS 216 |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Crash Sensor Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s.** A 64 km/h velocity **must be written as 17.78 m/s** before being used in any formula. Half the marks for a "calculate" question can be lost if unit conversion is skipped.
> 2. **Mixing up positive and negative signs.** During frontal impact, the vehicle decelerates (acceleration vector opposite to motion). A negative sign convention **must** be used; if you write only magnitudes, you will lose the "correct sign" mark.
> 3. **HIC formula is NOT a constant 1000.** The student must compute it step-by-step. Writing "HIC = 1000" without computation = 0 marks.
> 4. **Confusing "satellite" with "safing".** Many students swap these in the answer. Satellite = early warning (higher g, remote). Safing = confirmation (lower g, internal to ECU).
> 5. **Missing the regulation citation.** Any question on crash testing that asks for a standard expects **AIS-098 (India) or ECE R94 (Europe)** to be cited, not just "Euro NCAP."
> 6. **Not mentioning the dual-threshold logic** in any airbag architecture question — this is a **free 2-mark item** that students routinely miss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Crash Test Sensor (Module 3)**
>
> ✅ **Definition:** Crash test sensor = transducer that converts crash-induced mechanical/kinematic input into electrical signal for ACU.
>
> ✅ **Three primary locations:** Up-front (frontal), door/B-pillar (side), CG/center (rollover).
>
> ✅ **Two mandatory sensor types in any airbag system:** **Satellite** (early warning, ≥ 4 g) + **Safing** (confirmation, ≥ 2 g).
>
> ✅ **Dominant modern technology:** MEMS capacitive accelerometer (cheap, reliable, CMOS-compatible).
>
> ✅ **Other types:** Piezoelectric, Piezoresistive, Strain-gauge, Pressure (side), Gyroscope (rollover).
>
> ✅ **Critical ECU specs to memorize:** Sampling rate ≥ 4 kHz, Bandwidth DC–1.5 kHz, Decision window 30 ms, Backup capacitor ~2.5 J.
>
> ✅ **Key formulas to remember verbatim:**
> - $\Delta v = \int a\,dt$ (velocity change)
> - $E = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ (kinetic energy)
> - $F = ma$ (average crash force)
> - $HIC = \left[\frac{1}{\Delta t}\int a\,dt\right]^{2.5} \cdot \Delta t$ (head injury)
> - $C = \varepsilon_0 A / d$ (MEMS capacitance)
> - $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ (sensor natural frequency)
>
> ✅ **Crash severity threshold (typical):** < 8 km/h = no fire; 8–25 km/h = pretensioner + stage 1; > 25 km/h = stage 2 (full airbag).
>
> ✅ **Time-to-fire values (board-favourite):** Pretensioner ≤ 5 ms; Driver airbag ≤ 20 ms; Side airbag ≤ 5 ms; Far-side airbag ≤ 5 ms.
>
> ✅ **Indian regulations (must cite):** **AIS-098** (frontal ODB at 64 km/h, 40% overlap), **AIS-099** (side MDB at 50 km/h), **AIS-100** (full-width rigid barrier), **AIS-102** (rollover).
>
> ✅ **Global regulations:** **ECE R94** (frontal), **ECE R95** (side), **ECE R21** (interior protection), **FMVSS 208, 214, 216** (USA), **Euro NCAP** (consumer rating).
>
> ✅ **CFC filter classes (per SAE J211):** CFC 60 for head, CFC 180 for chest, CFC 600 for femur/tibia, CFC 1000 for foot/ankle.
>
> ✅ **Dummies (ATDs) used:** Hybrid III (frontal), THOR-50M (advanced frontal), WorldSID (side), Hybrid III 5th percentile female (passenger).
>
> ✅ **EDR data window:** Last 5 s pre-crash + 300 ms post-crash (per AIS-097 / 49 CFR Part 563).
>
> ✅ **Crash pulse integration output = Δv:** This single number is what determines airbag staging — it must be computed correctly.
>
> ✅ **MEMS output is linear:** $V_{out} = K \cdot a$ — derived from $C_0 = \varepsilon_0 A / d$ and $\Delta x = ma/k$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensor Mounting Positions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Sensor Mounting Positions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition
**Sensor Mounting Positions** refer to the precise spatial locations and orientation conventions prescribed for installing various transducers (accelerometers, gyroscopes, wheel-speed sensors, GPS antennas, OBD probes, and strain gauges) on a test vehicle, such that the measured data accurately represents the vehicle's true dynamic state without being corrupted by local structural resonances, thermal gradients, or electromagnetic interference.

The selection of these positions is governed by international standards — most notably **SAE J670 (Vehicle Dynamics Terminology)**, **SAE J1939 (CAN-based heavy vehicle network)**, **ISO 8855 (Road vehicles — Vehicle dynamics and road-holding ability)**, and **ISO 15037 (Road vehicles — Vehicle dynamics test methods)** — which define a standardized **right-hand Cartesian coordinate system** anchored at the vehicle's center of gravity (CG).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** Sensor mounting is the bridge between theoretical vehicle dynamics and the physical acquisition of telemetry. A wrongly placed accelerometer on a chassis rail may measure local panel vibration instead of body acceleration, leading to a **ride comfort index (RCI)** error exceeding **30 %**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "Where You Place the Stethoscope Matters"
Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. If she places her **stethoscope** on the patient's chest (over the heart), she hears the heart. If she places it on the stomach, she hears digestion. If she places it on the patient's back near the spine, she hears breathing — but it will be mixed with muscle and bone noise.

A vehicle is exactly like a patient. The **chassis** is the "skeleton," the **springs** are the "muscles," the **wheels** are the "feet," and the **engine** is the "heart." Each sensor is a stethoscope. Place the **vertical accelerometer** at the **sprung mass CG** and you measure *body bounce* (the patient's heartbeat). Place it on the **axle** and you measure *wheel hop* (the patient's footsteps). The **location fundamentally changes the signal**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Mounting positions are not chosen by accessibility or convenience — they are chosen so that the **measured degree of freedom** aligns with the **theoretical coordinate** used in the dynamic model.

### 1.3 Standardized Reference Coordinate System

| Axis (KTU Notation) | SAE J670 Designation | Positive Direction | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| **X** | Longitudinal axis | Forward from driver's heel-point | Acceleration, braking, pitch |
| **Y** | Lateral axis | To the right of the driver | Cornering, yaw, roll |
| **Z** | Vertical axis | Downward through the floor | Bounce, vertical load |

The origin is the **vehicle's CG** with the vehicle in its **design attitude** on a level surface.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Sensor Mounting Logic

The selection of a sensor mounting position follows a **five-step decision cascade**:

1. **Identify the degree of freedom (DoF) to be measured** — e.g., longitudinal acceleration $a_x$, yaw rate $\dot{\psi}$, wheel rotational speed $\omega_w$.
2. **Locate the rigid body whose motion equals the target DoF** — usually the sprung mass (body) or unsprung mass (wheel/axle).
3. **Identify the dominant resonance frequency of the candidate mounting point** — must lie **above the measurement bandwidth** (anti-aliasing principle).
4. **Verify thermal and EMC environment** — exhaust manifold, catalytic converter, and ECU wiring harnesses are thermal/EMC hot-zones.
5. **Establish mechanical rigidity** — the mounting surface should not flex more than the sensor's resolution under load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The most violated rule in student projects is **Step 3** — students place a $\pm 2 g$ accelerometer on a body panel that resonates at **18 Hz** and try to measure a body-bounce signal at the **1.2 Hz** sprung-mass mode. The result is a spectrum dominated by panel ringing, not the actual vertical motion.

### 2.2 Classification of Sensor Mounting Positions on a Test Vehicle

| Mounting Zone | Physical Location | Typical Sensors | Why Here? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Sprung Mass CG (Floor Tunnel)** | Center floor pan, near tunnel | Primary 3-axis IMU, GPS antenna | Minimum pitch/roll coupling; representative of body motion |
| **Engine Crankcase / Block** | Cylinder block near flywheel housing | TDC encoder, vibration accelerometers | Direct mechanical access; high signal-to-noise for combustion analysis |
| **Wheel Hub / Knuckle** | Non-rotating part of upright | Wheel-speed encoder, brake-temp thermocouple | Co-located with the rotating element; minimal slip |
| **Suspension Strut / Damper Body** | Upper mount of MacPherson strut | Ride-height sensor, strut potentiometer | Captures suspension travel; no wheel-rotation artefacts |
| **Steering Column** | Lower universal-joint housing | Steering-angle encoder (SAE J1939 SPN 1807) | Torsion-free region; accessible for wiring |
| **Exhaust Manifold / Pre-Cat Pipe** | 100 mm downstream of port | Lambda ($\lambda$) probe, EGT thermocouple | Required position per emissions regulations |
| **Differential Housing** | Cover bolt circle | Driveshaft RPM sensor | Rigid body, free from axle wrap |
| **Rear Axle Beam (Live Axle)** | Top of axle tube | Lateral acceleration reference for trailer testing | Captures axle wind-up and tramp |
| **Roof (above driver)** | Centroid of roof panel | GPS/GNSS antenna, roof deflection LVDT | Maximum sky visibility for satellite lock |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are the **high-frequency recalled items** for KTU ESE Module 3. Master the units and the sign conventions (SAE J670).

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\vec{a}_{\text{body}} = \vec{a}_{\text{sensor}} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}$ | Acceleration transfer from off-CG sensor to CG (rigid body) | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 2 | $a_{x,\text{CG}} = a_{x,\text{sens}} + \omega_{y}^{2}\, r_{z} + \omega_{z}^{2}\, r_{y} - \alpha_{y}\, r_{z} + \alpha_{z}\, r_{y}$ | Component form, longitudinal CG correction | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 3 | $a_{z,\text{CG}} = a_{z,\text{sens}} - \omega_{x}^{2}\, r_{y} - \omega_{y}^{2}\, r_{x} + \alpha_{x}\, r_{y} - \alpha_{y}\, r_{x}$ | Component form, vertical CG correction | $\text{m/s}^2$ |
| 4 | $v_{x} = \omega_{w}\, r_{\text{eff}}$ | Vehicle longitudinal speed from non-driven wheel | $\text{m/s}$ |
| 5 | $\omega_{\text{dyn}} = 2\pi f_{\text{nat}}$ | Relationship between natural frequency and angular rate | $\text{rad/s}$ |
| 6 | $f_{\text{sample}} \geq 2.56\, f_{\text{cutoff}}$ | Anti-aliasing sampling criterion (per ISO 6487) | $\text{Hz}$ |
| 7 | $K_{\text{isol}} = \sqrt{1 + \left(2\zeta\, r\right)^{2}} \,\big/\, \sqrt{(1-r^{2})^{2} + (2\zeta\, r)^{2}}$ | Vibration-isolator transmissibility (rigid mount = $K_{\text{isol}}=1$) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $r = f_{\text{drive}} / f_{\text{iso}}$ | Frequency ratio for isolator | dimensionless |
| 9 | $T_{\text{SAE}} = T_{\text{ISO}} \cdot \begin{pmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1 \end{pmatrix}$ | Conversion between SAE J670 and ISO 8855 axes | — |
| 10 | $h_{\text{CG}} = \dfrac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{a_{z,i} - g}{a_{x,i}}$ | Mass-centre height (height of CG) from tilt-table method | $\text{m}$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why This Matters in Real Testing

- **Homologation & Type Approval** — For ECE R13 (braking) and ECE R83 (emissions), the **accelerometer mounting on the sprung mass** is mandatory. A free accelerometer must be placed on the floor of the passenger compartment with its sensitive axis parallel to the road surface.
- **ISO 15037-1** mandates that **longitudinal and lateral accelerometers** be mounted within **$\pm 0.05\,\text{m}$** of the CG, and within **$\pm 1^{\circ}$** of the true horizontal.
- **Tyre-testing rigs** (e.g., flat-trac) place force transducers *inside* the wheel hub — a location inaccessible to in-vehicle measurement, hence the invention of **instrumented wheels**.
- **ADAS validation** (Euro NCAP AEB testing) requires **GNSS + IMU fusion** with antennas on the roof and IMU at the CG to deliver **sub-decimetre** path accuracy at **100 km/h**.

---

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Acceleration Transfer from Off-Centre Sensor to the Centre of Gravity

Consider a rigid body (the vehicle sprung mass) with a sensor mounted at a position vector $\vec{r}$ relative to the CG. The body is undergoing translation $\vec{a}_{\text{CG}}$ and rotation described by angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ and angular acceleration $\vec{\alpha}$.

**Step 1 — Position of the sensor point relative to an inertial frame:**

$$\vec{R}_{\text{sens}} = \vec{R}_{\text{CG}} + \vec{r}$$

Because $\vec{r}$ is *fixed* in the rotating body frame, its time derivative is **not** zero. Applying the transport theorem:

$$\vec{v}_{\text{sens}} = \vec{v}_{\text{CG}} + \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate again to obtain acceleration:**

Differentiate the velocity expression. The term $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$ must be differentiated as a *vector in the inertial frame*:

$$\vec{a}_{\text{sens}} = \frac{d\vec{v}_{\text{sens}}}{dt} = \vec{a}_{\text{CG}} + \frac{d\vec{\omega}}{dt} \times \vec{r} + \vec{\omega} \times \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}\bigg|_{\text{fixed}}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the transport theorem to the rotating vector $\vec{r}$:**

Since $\vec{r}$ is fixed in the body frame:

$$\frac{d\vec{r}}{dt}\bigg|_{\text{fixed}} = \vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$\vec{a}_{\text{sens}} = \vec{a}_{\text{CG}} + \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r} + \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the CG acceleration (the form the data logger actually needs):**

$$\boxed{\;\vec{a}_{\text{CG}} = \vec{a}_{\text{sens}} - \vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r} - \vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The term $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$ is the **centripetal correction**; the term $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}$ is the **Euler (tangential) correction**. Both vanish *only* when the sensor is mounted *exactly* at the CG.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style 7-Mark Sub-Question

> **Problem:** A tri-axial accelerometer is mounted on the floor of a vehicle at $r_x = -0.45\,\text{m}$ (rearward of CG), $r_y = 0.12\,\text{m}$ (right of CG), $r_z = 0.30\,\text{m}$ (above CG, since SAE Z points down). During a left-hand cornering event the sensors read: $a_{x,\text{sens}} = 1.80\,\text{m/s}^2$, $a_{y,\text{sens}} = 4.20\,\text{m/s}^2$, $a_{z,\text{sens}} = 0.20\,\text{m/s}^2$, yaw rate $\omega_{z} = 0.35\,\text{rad/s}$, yaw acceleration $\alpha_{z} = 0.10\,\text{rad/s}^2$, and roll rate $\omega_{x} = 0.05\,\text{rad/s}$. Compute the **lateral acceleration at the CG** $a_{y,\text{CG}}$. (Assume other angular rates $\omega_{y} = \alpha_{x} = \alpha_{y} \approx 0$ for simplicity.)

**Step 1 — Identify which terms in the general formula are non-zero.**

For $a_{y,\text{CG}}$ we need the **Y-component** of the centripetal and Euler terms.

$$\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) = \begin{pmatrix} \omega_x \\ 0 \\ \omega_z \end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix} \omega_x \\ 0 \\ \omega_z \end{pmatrix} \times \begin{pmatrix} r_x \\ r_y \\ r_z \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the inner cross product $\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}$:**

$$\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ \omega_x & 0 & \omega_z \\ r_x & r_y & r_z \end{vmatrix} = \hat{i}(0\cdot r_z - \omega_z\, r_y) - \hat{j}(\omega_x r_z - \omega_z r_x) + \hat{k}(\omega_x r_y - 0)$$

Numerically:

$$\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} = \hat{i}(-0.35 \cdot 0.12) - \hat{j}(0.05 \cdot 0.30 - 0.35 \cdot (-0.45)) + \hat{k}(0.05 \cdot 0.12)$$

$$\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r} = -0.0420\,\hat{i} - 0.1725\,\hat{j} + 0.0060\,\hat{k}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the outer cross product $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$:**

$$\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}) = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0.05 & 0 & 0.35 \\ -0.0420 & -0.1725 & 0.0060 \end{vmatrix}$$

$$\hat{i} \text{-component: } 0 \cdot 0.0060 - 0.35 \cdot (-0.1725) = +0.060375$$

$$\hat{j} \text{-component: } -(0.05 \cdot 0.0060 - 0.35 \cdot (-0.0420)) = -(0.0003 + 0.0147) = -0.0150$$

$$\hat{k} \text{-component: } 0.05 \cdot (-0.1725) - 0 \cdot (-0.0420) = -0.008625$$

So the centripetal term is $(+0.0604,\ -0.0150,\ -0.0086)\,\text{m/s}^2$.

**Step 4 — Compute the Euler term $\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r}$:**

Only $\alpha_{z} = 0.10\,\text{rad/s}^2$ is non-zero:

$$\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r} = \begin{vmatrix} \hat{i} & \hat{j} & \hat{k} \\ 0 & 0 & 0.10 \\ -0.45 & 0.12 & 0.30 \end{vmatrix} = \hat{i}(0) - \hat{j}(0 \cdot 0.30 - 0.10 \cdot (-0.45)) + \hat{k}(0)$$

$$\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r} = 0\,\hat{i} - 0.045\,\hat{j} + 0\,\hat{k}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the Y-component correction:**

$$a_{y,\text{CG}} = a_{y,\text{sens}} - (\vec{\alpha} \times \vec{r})_{y} - (\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r}))_{y}$$

$$a_{y,\text{CG}} = 4.20 - (-0.045) - (-0.0150)$$

$$a_{y,\text{CG}} = 4.20 + 0.045 + 0.015 = 4.26\,\text{m/s}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The lateral acceleration *at the CG* is **$4.26\,\text{m/s}^2$**, while the sensor located $0.45\,\text{m}$ behind the CG read **$4.20\,\text{m/s}^2$** — a correction of **$+0.06\,\text{m/s}^2$** ($\approx 1.4\,\%$). For braking-distance homologation, this **1.4 %** is significant.

### 3.3 Derivation: CG Height from Tilt-Table Method (used to fix IMU mounting reference)

When the vehicle is tilted through an angle $\theta$, the gravitational component along the body-fixed X-axis becomes $g \sin\theta$ and along Z becomes $g \cos\theta$. Two accelerometers mounted in the sprung mass — one along X, one along Z — record:

$$a_{x} = -g \sin\theta + a_{x,\text{true}} \quad ; \quad a_{z} = g \cos\theta$$

For a static (or low-speed) tilt test, $a_{x,\text{true}} = 0$. The height of the CG above the tilt axis is found by comparing the **measured** accelerations at two tilt angles:

$$\frac{a_{x,1} - a_{x,2}}{a_{z,1} - a_{z,2}} = \frac{-g\sin\theta_1 + g\sin\theta_2}{g\cos\theta_1 - g\cos\theta_2} = -\cot\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_1 - \theta_2}{2}\right)\cdot \tan\!\left(\dfrac{\theta_1 + \theta_2}{2}\right)$$

This quantity, when plotted against **roll/pitch angle**, gives the **CG offset** directly. Knowing the CG height tells the test engineer **exactly where to mount the primary IMU** so that the rigid-body assumption in §3.1 is satisfied to within tolerance.

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Sensor Placement Decision Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Define Target DoF"] --> B["Identify Rigid Body"]
    B --> C["Check Mount Resonance freq > Bandwidth"]
    C --> D{Resonance OK?}
    D -- No --> E["Use Vibration Isolator"]
    E --> F["Recompute Transmissibility"]
    F --> D
    D -- Yes --> G["Check Thermal / EMC Environment"]
    G --> H{Exposed to > 80 degC or EMI?}
    H -- Yes --> I["Add Heat Shield or Shielded Cable"]
    H -- No --> J["Verify Mechanical Rigidity < 0.1 mm"]
    I --> J
    J --> K{Within Tolerance?}
    K -- No --> L["Use Stiffening Plate or Honeycomb Bracket"]
    L --> J
    K -- Yes --> M["Finalize Mounting Position"]
    M --> N["Calibrate in Static and Dynamic Conditions"]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Rigid-Body Coordinate Transformation (SAE ↔ ISO)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Sensor Frame S_x_y_z"] --> B["Apply Roll-Pitch-Yaw Sequence"]
    B --> C["Translate by r_x, r_y, r_z to CG"]
    C --> D{"Target Convention?"}
    D -- "SAE J670" --> E["Use X_Fwd, Y_Right, Z_Down"]
    D -- "ISO 8855" --> F["Use X_Fwd, Y_Left, Z_Up"]
    E --> G["CG Frame Output"]
    F --> G
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Note (KTU):** The above Mermaid diagrams are not literal vehicle drawings. They are the **functional architecture** of sensor placement, which is what the examiner expects a student to draw on the answer script when a 14-mark question asks for "block diagram representation of sensor locations in a test vehicle."

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the three primary considerations that dictate the choice of an accelerometer mounting position on the sprung mass of a test vehicle.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The mounting location must lie on a **rigid portion of the sprung mass** with first natural frequency **above 100 Hz** for ride-comfort measurements (to avoid local panel resonance).
2. The mounting position should be within **$\pm 0.05\,\text{m}$** of the CG (per ISO 15037-1) so that the **centripetal correction terms** $\vec{\omega} \times (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{r})$ are negligible.
3. The orientation of the sensitive axis must be within **$\pm 1^{\circ}$** of the true vehicle-fixed axis, and the surface must be **flat, clean, and free from paint thickness variation** that would tilt the sensor.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why is a Lambda ($\lambda$) oxygen sensor mounted approximately 100 mm downstream of the exhaust port rather than inside the port itself?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. Inside the port, the gas temperature can exceed **950 °C**, exceeding the sensor's **850 °C** operational ceiling and causing **thermal shock failure**.
