- **Insurance Rating:** IIHS bumper-test outcomes directly influence **premium pricing** in the USA.
- **Pedestrian Safety Legislation:** Euro NCAP leg-form results are now a **mandatory homologation** parameter under **GTR-9 (UNECE)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Average Crush Force for a Pendulum Impact

**Problem Setup (Standard FMVSS 581 / ECE R42 Pendulum Test):**

- Pendulum effective mass: $m = 1000 \;\text{kg}$ (per FMVSS specification).
- Impact velocity: $v = 2.5 \;\text{m/s}$ (≈ 9 km/h).
- Permissible bumper beam stroke: $\delta = 80 \;\text{mm} = 0.08 \;\text{m}$.

**Required:** The minimum *average* crushing force the absorber must develop to bring the pendulum to rest within the allowed stroke.

### Step 1 — Compute the Kinetic Energy of the Pendulum

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \, m \, v^2$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times (2.5)^2 \; \text{J}$$

Squaring the velocity term:

$$(2.5)^2 = 6.25$$

Multiplying:

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1000 \times 6.25 = 500 \times 6.25 = 3125 \; \text{J}$$

### Step 2 — Equate Kinetic Energy to Work Done by Crushing Force

By the **Work-Energy Theorem**, the work done by the average crushing force over stroke $\delta$ must equal the initial kinetic energy:

$$F_{avg} \cdot \delta = E_k$$

Rearranging:

$$F_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{\delta}$$

### Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$F_{avg} = \frac{3125 \; \text{J}}{0.08 \; \text{m}}$$

Performing the division:

$$F_{avg} = 39062.5 \; \text{N}$$

Converting to kilo-Newtons:

$$F_{avg} \approx 39.06 \; \text{kN}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** The absorber must develop an *average* resistance of approximately **39 kN** distributed across the bumper cross-section. A typical bumper beam (aluminium extrusion) yields at **80–120 kN** — so the absorber, not the beam, is the primary energy-dissipating element. This is why **EA selection** is critical.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Peak Deceleration for a Triangular Force Pulse

A triangular force pulse is a common engineering idealisation: force rises linearly to $F_{peak}$ and falls linearly to zero over total contact time $\Delta t$.

### Step 1 — Impulse Equals Change in Momentum

$$F_{peak} \cdot \frac{\Delta t}{2} = m \cdot v$$

The LHS is the area of the triangle (impulse). The RHS is the momentum change.

### Step 2 — Solve for Peak Force

$$F_{peak} = \frac{2 \, m \, v}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 3 — Solve for Peak Deceleration

$$a_{peak} = \frac{F_{peak}}{m} = \frac{2 \, v}{\Delta t}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

Let $m = 1000 \; \text{kg}$, $v = 2.5 \; \text{m/s}$, $\Delta t = 0.05 \; \text{s}$ (typical pulse duration for foam absorber).

$$F_{peak} = \frac{2 \times 1000 \times 2.5}{0.05} = \frac{5000}{0.05} = 100{,}000 \; \text{N} = 100 \; \text{kN}$$

$$a_{peak} = \frac{2 \times 2.5}{0.05} = \frac{5.0}{0.05} = 100 \; \text{m/s}^2 \approx 10.2 \, g$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $10 \; g$ is acceptable:** The vehicle body itself is not loaded by the full $10 \; g$ — the absorber's *progressive* crush profile holds the occupant-compartment deceleration **below 5 g**, the human-tolerance threshold for short-duration loading.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Balance for a Full-Frontal Barrier Impact (5 km/h)

**Given:** Test vehicle mass $M = 1500 \; \text{kg}$, barrier speed $v_b = 0$ (rigid wall), vehicle speed $v = 5 \; \text{km/h}$.

### Step 1 — Convert Velocity to SI Units

$$v = 5 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 1.389 \; \text{m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Initial Kinetic Energy

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (1.389)^2$$

$$(1.389)^2 = 1.9293$$

$$E_k = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 1.9293 = 750 \times 1.9293 \approx 1447 \; \text{J}$$

### Step 3 — Energy Balance

All this energy must be absorbed by **bumper + crumple zone** in the form of plastic deformation:

$$E_{k} = E_{bumper} + E_{crush} + E_{friction} + E_{restitution}$$

For a perfectly plastic impact, $E_{restitution} \approx 0$, hence:

$$E_{k} \approx E_{bumper} + E_{crush}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 5 km/h barrier impact deposits only ~1.4 kJ — this is **the design energy target** for a low-cost repairable bumper.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Test Methods)

| Parameter | Pendulum Test (FMVSS 581) | Pendulum Test (ECE R42) | IIHS Bumper Test | Pedestrian Leg Test (Euro NCAP) |
|-----------|---------------------------|--------------------------|--------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Test Mass** | 1000 kg | 1100 kg | Two real vehicles | 13.4 kg (leg-form) |
| **Impact Velocity** | 2.5 m/s (9 km/h) | 2.5 m/s | 4.2 m/s (15 km/h) | 6.7 m/s (24 km/h) |
| **Impact Location** | Centre & Corner | Centre & Corner | Full overlap | Bumper leading edge |
| **Pass Criterion** | No damage to safety systems | No damage to lamps | Repair cost ≤ USD 1500 | Tibia bending ≤ 340 Nm |
| **Equipment Cost** | Medium | Medium | High (real cars) | High (instrumented limb) |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Style Numerical

> **Question:** A bumper energy absorber must absorb the kinetic energy of a **1200 kg pendulum** impacting at **3.0 m/s**. If the maximum permitted stroke is **60 mm**, determine: (a) the kinetic energy, (b) the required average crushing force, and (c) the theoretical peak deceleration assuming a triangular pulse of duration 40 ms.

### (a) Kinetic Energy

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (3.0)^2 = 600 \times 9.0 = 5400 \; \text{J}$$

### (b) Average Crushing Force

$$F_{avg} = \frac{5400}{0.060} = 90{,}000 \; \text{N} = 90 \; \text{kN}$$

### (c) Peak Deceleration (Triangular Pulse)

$$a_{peak} = \frac{2 \, v}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 \times 3.0}{0.040} = \frac{6.0}{0.040} = 150 \; \text{m/s}^2 \approx 15.3 \, g$$

> **Interpretation:** The bumper **structure** must withstand a 15 g load *briefly* (~40 ms), but the **occupant compartment** experiences a far lower deceleration because the absorber, fascia, and front rail all contribute progressive compliance.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Bumper System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **functional flow of crash energy** through a modern automotive bumper system, from initial impact to final absorption.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Impact Event: Pendulum or Barrier] --> B[Bumper Fascia: Polymer Cover]
    B --> C{Is Impact at Centre or Corner?}
    C -->|Centre| D[Energy Absorber EPP Foam Crushes]
    C -->|Corner| E[Beam Bends: Cantilever Action]
    D --> F[Bumper Beam: Aluminium / Steel Section]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Side Rail Crush Box: Progressive Buckling]
    G --> H[Floor and Sill Structure]
    H --> I[Occupant Cell: Deformation Below Threshold]
    I --> J[Final Energy Dissipated as Heat and Sound]
```

### Reading the Diagram

- **Node A** is the initiating event (kinetic energy of the pendulum / barrier).
- **Node B** is the visible polymer fascia, which adds a small amount of stroke and provides a compliant pedestrian surface.
- **Node C** is the geometric decision point: a *centre* hit is absorbed by the foam's *axial compression*, while a *corner* hit forces the beam into *bending*.
- **Nodes F → G → H** represent the structural load path: beam → side rail → floor.
- **Node I** is the critical safety check: the occupant cell must NOT exceed a defined intrusion limit.
- **Node J** represents the final dissipation of mechanical energy as heat, sound, and plastic deformation.

## 4.2 Bumper Test Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T[Bumper Testing Protocols] --> T1[Regulatory Tests]
    T --> T2[Consumer Tests]
    T --> T3[OEM Internal Validation]

    T1 --> R1[FMVSS 581: USA Bumper Standard]
    T1 --> R2[ECE R42: UNECE Approval]
    T1 --> R3[AIS-006: India Bumper Standard]

    T2 --> C1[IIHS Low-Speed Bumper Test]
    T2 --> C2[Euro NCAP Pedestrian Leg Test]
    T2 --> C3[Euro NCAP Head Form Test]

    T3 --> V1[Static Push Quasi Static Test]
    T3 --> V2[Drop Test Free Fall]
    T3 --> V3[Cyclic Durability Test]
    T3 --> V4[Salt Spray Corrosion Test]
```

## 4.3 Pendulum Test Setup Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Pendulum Mass 1000 kg] --> P2[Stiff Impactor Head]
    P2 --> P3[Strikes Bumper Centre or Corner]
    P3 --> P4[Energy Absorber Crushed]
    P4 --> P5[Bumper Beam Bends]
    P5 --> P6[Force Transducers Record Load]
    P6 --> P7[High Speed Camera Captures Deformation]
    P7 --> P8[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The high-speed camera (typically 1000–5000 fps) and load cell array work in **synchronisation** with the DAQ. The product of force × displacement at every instant is integrated to obtain the **total absorbed energy**, which is then compared to the **initial kinetic energy** to compute the **coefficient of restitution**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Evaluation Pattern (Module 3):**
> - **Part A (2-mark short answers)** — Definitions, listing, one-line statements.
> - **Part B (14-mark long answer)** — Two sub-parts (7 + 7 marks), with internal choice.
> - **Cognitive Levels Tested:** L1 (Remember), L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), L4 (Analyse).
> - **Module 3 Weightage:** ~ 20–25 % of the End-Semester Exam.

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 / 3 Marks)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define the term "bumper" as per AIS-006 and list its three primary components.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Bumper:** A safety device fitted to the front and rear of a motor vehicle whose primary function is to absorb impact energy and protect the vehicle body and occupants in low-speed collisions (as defined by **AIS-006**).
>
> **Three primary components:**
> 1. **Bumper face bar / beam** — load-bearing structural member.
> 2. **Energy absorber (EA)** — crushable foam or honeycomb element.
> 3. **Bumper cover / fascia** — outer polymer skin providing aesthetic and pedestrian-friendly surface.

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State any three test methods used for evaluating automotive bumpers.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
> 1. **Pendulum Impact Test (FMVSS Part 581 / ECE R42)** — 1000–1100 kg pendulum strikes centre or corner at 2.5 m/s.
> 2. **Pedestrian Leg-Form Impactor Test (Euro NCAP / GTR-9)** — instrumented leg-form strikes the bumper leading edge.
> 3. **Static Quasi-Static Push Test (ECE R42)** — hydraulic ram pushes bumper at a controlled rate (≤ 2 mm/s) to determine force-deflection curve.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the different **types of bumpers** used in modern automobiles, and explain the constructional features of an **energy-absorbing bumper system** with a neat sketch. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe in detail the **pendulum impact test** as specified by **FMVSS Part 581**, including test parameters, instrumentation, and pass/fail criteria. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Types of Bumpers:**

1. **Conventional Steel Bumper** — Rigid chrome-plated steel beam; found on older vehicles.
2. **Reinforced Plastic / Composite Bumper** — FRP or CFRP beam; light-weight.
3. **Aluminium Bumper** — Extruded aluminium sections; light-weight + corrosion resistant.
4. **Energy-Absorbing Bumper** — Foam-filled cavity between beam and fascia.
5. **Hydraulic Bumper** — Uses hydraulic fluid for variable-stiffness response.
6. **Active / Deployable Bumper** — Sensor-triggered extension to improve pedestrian contact.
7. **Step Bumper** — Step-like profile on commercial vehicles for foot access.

**Construction of Energy-Absorbing Bumper (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1):**

- **Outer fascia:** Polymer (PP / TPO / ABS) of 3–4 mm thickness; provides aesthetic and pedestrian-friendly compliance.
- **Energy absorber:** EPP (Expanded Polypropylene) foam of 30–60 mm thickness; designed to crush progressively under axial load.
- **Bumper beam:** Aluminium extrusion (or steel) of closed cross-section; transfers the load to the side rails.
- **Side rail extensions:** Designed as **crush boxes** that initiate progressive buckling.

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Pendulum Impact Test (FMVSS Part 581):**

- **Test mass:** Free-flying pendulum of **1000 kg** effective mass.
- **Impactor head:** Cylindrical, 250 mm diameter, with a rigid honeycomb face.
- **Impact velocity:** **2.5 m/s (≈ 9 km/h)** at the moment of impact.
- **Impact locations:** (i) Centre of the bumper, (ii) each corner (typically 30° offset from centreline).
- **Instrumentation:**
  * High-speed cameras (≥ 1000 fps) to capture deformation.
  * Load cells at the pendulum head to record force-time history.
  * LVDT / string-potentiometers for stroke measurement.
- **Pass criteria:**
  * No damage to **headlamps, turn signals, or safety systems**.
  * No rupture of the **fuel system**.
  * **Bumper line** must be preserved (no inboard shift beyond 100 mm).
  * **No contact** between bumper and safety-related components (e.g., bonnet, boot-lid).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Errors:**
> - Writing "pendulum test is done at high speed" — **WRONG.** It is a *low-speed* test (2.5 m/s). Marks lost for mis-stating velocity.
> - Confusing **FMVSS 581** (bumper standard) with **FMVSS 208** (occupant protection). Examiners WILL deduct for this.
> - Omitting the **corner-impact configuration** — it carries equal weightage with the centre-impact in marking.
> - Writing "the bumper must not be damaged" — *incomplete*. The bumper CAN be damaged, but safety-related systems must remain functional.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **pedestrian protection bumper test** conducted as per **Euro NCAP / GTR-9** protocols. List the injury criteria evaluated. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bumper energy-absorber is to be designed to absorb the kinetic energy of a **1500 kg** vehicle impacting a rigid wall at **4 km/h**. If the permissible stroke is **75 mm**, determine: (i) the kinetic energy to be absorbed, (ii) the average crushing force, and (iii) the peak deceleration assuming a triangular force pulse of **50 ms** duration. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a)

**Pedestrian Protection Bumper Test (Euro NCAP / GTR-9):**

- **Test apparatus:** Instrumented **leg-form impactor** (representing an adult pedestrian's lower limb) of mass 13.4 kg.
- **Impact velocity:** 6.7 m/s (≈ 24 km/h).
- **Impact angle:** Horizontal, perpendicular to the bumper leading edge.
- **Block diagram (textual representation):**
  1. Leg-form impactor on linear slider.
  2. Accelerates to 6.7 m/s via pneumatic / spring launcher.
  3. Strikes the bumper at the **leading edge** (typically at 50 % of the bumper width).
  4. Loads are measured at **knee, upper tibia, and lower tibia** by strain-gauged load cells.
  5. Bending moments and shear forces are recorded by **DAQs**.
- **Injury criteria evaluated:**
  1. **Tibia bending moment** (limit: ≤ 340 Nm).
  2. **Knee shear displacement** (limit: ≤ 6 mm).
  3. **Knee bending moment** (limit: ≤ 42 Nm at the ligament).
  4. **Upper tibia-fibula acceleration** (limit: ≤ 150 g).
- **Additional test:** Child / adult **head-form impact** on the bumper fascia (limit: HIC ≤ 1000).

#### Model Solution — (b)

**Given:** $m = 1500 \; \text{kg}$, $v = 4 \; \text{km/h}$, $\delta = 75 \; \text{mm} = 0.075 \; \text{m}$, $\Delta t = 50 \; \text{ms} = 0.05 \; \text{s}$.

**Step (i): Convert velocity to SI**

$$v = 4 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 1.111 \; \text{m/s}$$

**Step (i result): Kinetic Energy**

$$E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (1.111)^2$$

$$(1.111)^2 = 1.2346$$

$$E_k = 750 \times 1.2346 \approx 925.9 \; \text{J}$$

**Step (ii): Average Crushing Force**

$$F_{avg} = \frac{E_k}{\delta} = \frac{925.9}{0.075} \approx 12{,}345 \; \text{N} \approx 12.35 \; \text{kN}$$

**Step (iii): Peak Deceleration (Triangular Pulse)**

$$a_{peak} = \frac{2 \, v}{\Delta t} = \frac{2 \times 1.111}{0.05} = \frac{2.222}{0.05} = 44.44 \; \text{m/s}^2 \approx 4.53 \, g$$

> **Interpretation:** Since the average force is only ~12 kN (well below the aluminium-beam yield strength of ~80 kN), this bumper design will easily pass without structural damage. The peak deceleration of ~4.5 g is well within the human-tolerance limit for the vehicle occupant.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Numerical Errors:**
> - **Unit conversion:** The most common error is forgetting to convert **km/h to m/s**. The conversion factor is $5/18$ (i.e., $1 \; \text{km/h} = 0.2778 \; \text{m/s}$). Marks are deducted even for a single missed conversion.
> - **Stroke in mm vs. m:** A common error is to substitute $\delta = 75$ (mm) directly in metres, producing a force ~1000× too large. Always convert to **metres** before calculation.
> - **Triangular vs. rectangular pulse:** Examiners specifically test the *triangular* pulse formula $a_{peak} = 2v/\Delta t$. Confusing this with the rectangular-pulse formula $a = v/\Delta t$ loses 2–3 marks.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Use this section for **last-minute revision** before the KTU ESE examination.

### ✅ Conceptual Points
- Bumper = **Energy Absorber + Beam + Fascia** (three-element system).
- Primary function: **absorb low-speed impact energy** (2.5–4 km/h range, typically 9–15 km/h for IIHS).
- Bumpers are *not* crash structures for high-speed occupant protection; that role belongs to the **crumple zone** and **safety cell**.

### ✅ Standards (Memorise)
- **FMVSS Part 581** — Pendulum, 1000 kg, 2.5 m/s.
- **ECE R42** — Pendulum, 1100 kg, 2.5 m/s; quasi-static push test.
- **AIS-006** — Indian standard.
- **Euro NCAP / GTR-9** — Pedestrian leg and head-form tests.

### ✅ Numerical Formulae (Master These Three)
1. **Energy:** $E_k = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$
2. **Average force:** $F_{avg} = E_k / \delta$
3. **Peak deceleration (triangular):** $a_{peak} = 2 v / \Delta t$

### ✅ Conversion Reminders
- $1 \; \text{km/h} = \tfrac{5}{18} \; \text{m/s} \approx 0.2778 \; \text{m/s}$.
- Stroke must always be in **metres** when used in force calculations.
- $1 \; g = 9.81 \; \text{m/s}^2$.

### ✅ Test Categories (Always Remember)
1. **Regulatory** (FMVSS, ECE, AIS).
2. **Consumer / NCAP** (IIHS, Euro NCAP).
3. **OEM Internal** (Static, Drop, Durability, Corrosion).

### ✅ Common Misconceptions (Avoid in Exam)
- ❌ "Bumpers protect occupants in high-speed crashes." — **WRONG**; that is the crumple zone's job.
- ❌ "Pendulum tests are high-speed." — **WRONG**; they are low-speed (2.5 m/s).
- ❌ "Pedestrian tests are optional." — **WRONG**; they are mandatory under GTR-9.
- ❌ "Energy absorber = bumper beam." — **WRONG**; they are *two distinct* components.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a 14-mark question asks you to "describe" a test, **always** structure your answer as: (i) *Test objective*, (ii) *Standards reference*, (iii) *Test apparatus & parameters*, (iv) *Procedure*, (v) *Pass criteria*, (vi) *Diagrammatic representation*. This 6-part structure is exactly what KTU examiners reward with full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pendulum Test
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pendulum Test

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU Syllabus Definition

The **Pendulum Test** is a standardized, dynamic impact-testing procedure employed in automotive and general materials engineering to quantify the **toughness, impact strength, and energy-absorption capability** of a specimen (typically a notched metallic sample, a polymer component, or an automotive body panel). The test quantifies the amount of energy a material can absorb during a rapid, single-strike fracture event initiated by a swinging pendulum hammer of known mass released from a calibrated initial height.

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** context of Vehicle Performance and Testing, the Pendulum Test primarily maps to two industry-standard protocols used during automotive component validation:

1. **Charpy Pendulum Impact Test (ASTM E23 / ISO 148)** — used to grade automotive sheet metals, chassis alloys, and bumper reinforcements.
2. **Izod Pendulum Impact Test (ASTM D256 / ISO 180)** — predominantly used for plastics, composites, and polymer dashboard or interior trim components.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The energy absorbed during fracture, expressed in **Joules (J)**, is the single most important output parameter of the Pendulum Test. It is mathematically defined as the difference between the pendulum's initial potential energy (at release) and its final potential energy (at maximum swing after impact).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are holding a **swinging hammer on a chain** — this is the pendulum. You pull it up to a certain height (let's call it height $H_1$) and let it go. The hammer swings down, gains speed, and at the bottom of its arc it slams into a small test piece clamped in a vice. After breaking (or not breaking) the specimen, the hammer continues swinging upward, but only to a **lower height** ($H_2$) because some of its initial energy was "stolen" by the specimen to break it.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "missing" energy = The toughness of the material.**
> 
> - If the hammer swings back up almost to the starting height → the material is **brittle** (it took very little energy to break).
> - If the hammer barely swings back up → the material is **tough/ductile** (it absorbed a huge amount of energy before fracturing).
> 
> This is exactly how a pendulum clock test or a wrecking-ball test works in real life.

A real-world automotive example: When a pedestrian is struck by a car bumper, the bumper material must absorb kinetic energy gracefully. Pendulum tests simulate this single impulsive load to ensure the material can absorb enough energy without shattering catastrophically.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following constants and metrics are universally adopted in KTU board examinations:

- **Acceleration due to gravity** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **Standard pendulum hammer masses** typically range from $0.5 \text{ kg}$ to $25 \text{ kg}$
- **Standard pendulum release heights** $H_1$ usually correspond to initial potential energies of $300 \text{ J}$, $150 \text{ J}$, or $50 \text{ J}$
- **Result unit:** Energy absorbed, $E \text{ in Joules (J)}$
- **Specific metrics:** Impact strength = $E / A$ (Joules per square meter of cross-section at notch)
- **Standard notch geometries:** V-notch (45° included angle, 2 mm depth) and U-notch (key-hole)
- **Standard test temperatures:** $23\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (ambient), $-40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (cold), and $0\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ (ductile-to-brittle transition reference)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The pendulum test converts **gravitational potential energy** into **kinetic energy** at the moment of impact, and then back into **gravitational potential energy** as the pendulum continues its swing. The energy "missing" after the swing is assumed to have been absorbed by the specimen.

### Step-by-Step Operating Logic

1. **Loading the pendulum:** The hammer is raised and latched at a known angle $\alpha$ from the vertical. At this point it stores an initial potential energy $E_i$ relative to the lowest point of the swing.
2. **Release and acceleration:** The latch disengages; the hammer swings downward under gravity, gaining velocity $v$ at the bottom of its arc.
3. **Impact event:** The hammer strikes the specimen mounted in a fixed anvil. The specimen undergoes rapid plastic deformation and fracture in a fraction of a second.
4. **Post-impact swing:** The hammer continues upward, now carrying less energy, and reaches a maximum **rise angle** $\beta$ on the other side of the vertical.
5. **Energy accounting:** The energy absorbed by the specimen is calculated as the difference between the initial and final potential energies.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a pendulum and not a falling weight?**
> A pendulum is preferred because friction at the pivot is minimal (smooth rotation), the swing angle is easy to measure with a precision dial gauge, and the impact velocity is reproducible simply by changing the release height. A freely-falling weight would be affected by air drag and would have to be retrieved for re-testing.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every high-yield formula** required for solving Pendulum Test problems in KTU 2024 Scheme examinations. Master these and you are guaranteed to score full marks on any numerical sub-question.

| S.No. | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Initial potential energy of pendulum | $E_i = m \cdot g \cdot h_1$ | $h_1 = L - L\cos\alpha = L(1-\cos\alpha)$ |
| 2 | Final potential energy after impact | $E_f = m \cdot g \cdot h_2$ | $h_2 = L(1-\cos\beta)$ |
| 3 | **Energy absorbed by specimen (KTU favourite)** | $E = E_i - E_f = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$ | Measured in Joules |
| 4 | Impact strength (specific) | $IS = E / A$ | $A$ = cross-section area at notch (m²) |
| 5 | Velocity of hammer just before impact | $v_i = \sqrt{2gL(1-\cos\alpha)}$ | From energy conservation |
| 6 | Velocity of hammer just after impact | $v_f = \sqrt{2gL(1-\cos\beta)}$ | Post-fracture speed |
| 7 | Hammer angular velocity at bottom | $\omega = v / L$ | $L$ = effective pendulum length |
| 8 | Initial swing angle from vertical | $\alpha = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{h_1}{L}\right)$ | Geometry inversion |
| 9 | Final swing angle from vertical | $\beta = \cos^{-1}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{h_2}{L}\right)$ | Geometry inversion |
| 10 | Energy loss due to friction/air drag | $E_{\text{loss}} = m g (h_1^{\text{no-load}} - h_1)$ | Calibrated before specimen mounting |
| 11 | Ductile-to-brittle transition indicator | Plot $E$ vs $T$ — sharp drop indicates DBTT | $T$ = test temperature |
| 12 | Fracture appearance criterion | Shear lip percentage (fibrous vs crystalline) | Linked to absorbed energy |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Table-Formatting Note:** In the formulas above, the term $\vert x \vert$ notation has been intentionally avoided. Where modulus or absolute value is required, the proper LaTeX command `\vert` or `\mid` is used (e.g., $\vert \cos\beta - \cos\alpha \vert$) to prevent breaking the markdown table pipe structure.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Pendulum Test is **not academic** — it is one of the most heavily used QA/QC tests in the global automotive supply chain:

- **Body-in-White (BIW) steel selection:** Automakers like Tata Motors and Maruti Suzuki qualify each batch of high-strength steel (HSS, AHSS, UHSS) using Charpy tests at $-40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to ensure cold-weather crashworthiness.
- **Bumper beam validation:** A pendulum equivalent of a 4 km/h pedestrian impact is performed on a bumper prototype.
- **Plastic interior components:** The Izod test ensures that dashboards and door trims do not shatter into sharp shards during a side-impact collision.
- **Suspension component fatigue screening:** Springs, control arms, and steering knuckles undergo pendulum impact tests to identify brittle failure modes.
- **Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT) determination:** Critical for vehicles exported to Scandinavian countries where operating temperatures drop to $-30\,^{\circ}\text{C}$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Energy Equation

### 3.1.1 Setup and Variable Definition

Consider a pendulum hammer of mass $m$ attached to a rigid massless arm of effective length $L$ (from pivot to center of percussion). The hammer is released from an initial angle $\alpha$ measured from the **vertical (downward)** reference line, strikes a specimen clamped at the lowest point of its swing, and rises to a final angle $\beta$ on the opposite side.

**Variables:**
- $m$ = mass of the hammer (kg)
- $L$ = effective pendulum length (m)
- $\alpha$ = initial release angle from vertical (rad or deg)
- $\beta$ = final rise angle from vertical after fracture (rad or deg)
- $g$ = acceleration due to gravity = $9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Energy Derivation

**Step 1 — Initial height of hammer above the lowest point of swing.**

The hammer is at a horizontal distance $L\sin\alpha$ from the pivot, but what matters for potential energy is the **vertical rise** above the bottom of the arc. The vertical position of the hammer relative to the lowest point is:

$$h_1 = L - L\cos\alpha = L(1 - \cos\alpha)$$

This is the geometric height through which the hammer has been lifted.

**Step 2 — Initial potential energy stored.**

By definition $E_i = m g h_1$. Substituting $h_1$:

$$E_i = m g L (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

**Step 3 — Final height of hammer above the lowest point after impact.**

By identical geometry, after the pendulum swings through the impact point and rises on the far side:

$$h_2 = L(1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 4 — Final potential energy remaining.**

$$E_f = m g L (1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 5 — Energy conservation with fracture loss.**

The total mechanical energy at the start must equal the total mechanical energy at the maximum post-impact swing, plus whatever energy the specimen absorbed ($E_{\text{abs}}$). Therefore:

$$E_i = E_f + E_{\text{abs}}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the absorbed energy.**

$$E_{\text{abs}} = E_i - E_f$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (1 - \cos\alpha) - m g L (1 - \cos\beta)$$

**Step 7 — Algebraic simplification.**

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L \left[(1 - \cos\alpha) - (1 - \cos\beta)\right]$$

The $(1 - 1)$ terms cancel:

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Result (KTU Board Exam Ready):**
> $$\boxed{\,E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)\,}$$
> 
> This is **the single most important equation** for any KTU Pendulum Test numerical. Memorize it, derive it, and apply it with confidence.

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Hammer Impact Velocity

For a follow-up sub-question involving **impact velocity** (common in KTU Part B questions), we apply energy conservation between the release point and the lowest point of the swing.

**At release point:** $KE = 0$, $PE = m g L (1 - \cos\alpha)$

**At lowest point (just before impact):** $PE = 0$, $KE = \tfrac{1}{2} m v^2$

Equating mechanical energy:

$$m g L (1 - \cos\alpha) = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$$

Cancel $m$ and solve for $v$:

$$v^2 = 2 g L (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

$$\boxed{\,v = \sqrt{2 g L (1 - \cos\alpha)}\,}$$

By identical logic, the post-impact velocity is:

$$v_f = \sqrt{2 g L (1 - \cos\beta)}$$

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Examples

### Example 1 — Energy Absorbed (Standard 14-Mark Question Pattern)

**Problem:** A Charpy pendulum hammer of mass $20 \text{ kg}$ and effective length $0.8 \text{ m}$ is released from an initial angle of $120^{\circ}$ from the vertical. After fracturing the specimen, the pendulum rises to a final angle of $85^{\circ}$ on the other side. Calculate:
(a) The energy absorbed by the specimen.
(b) The impact strength if the cross-sectional area at the notch is $80 \text{ mm}^2$.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

Given: $m = 20 \text{ kg}$, $L = 0.8 \text{ m}$, $\alpha = 120^{\circ}$, $\beta = 85^{\circ}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

Using the master equation:

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$

Substitute:

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 20 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 \times (\cos 85^{\circ} - \cos 120^{\circ})$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 20 \times 9.81 \times 0.8 \times (0.0872 - (-0.5))$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 156.96 \times 0.5872$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{abs}} \approx 92.18 \text{ J}}$$

**Part (b):**

$$IS = \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{A} = \frac{92.18}{80 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{IS \approx 1.152 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m}^2 = 1.152 \text{ MJ/m}^2}$$

### Example 2 — Hammer Velocity at Impact

**Problem:** A pendulum of length $1.0 \text{ m}$ is released from $90^{\circ}$ from the vertical. Calculate the impact velocity at the lowest point. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$.

**Solution:**

$$v = \sqrt{2 g L (1 - \cos\alpha)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \times (1 - \cos 90^{\circ})}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 1.0 \times (1 - 0)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{19.62}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 4.43 \text{ m/s}}$$

### Example 3 — Inverse Problem (Find Release Angle)

**Problem:** A $15 \text{ kg}$ pendulum of length $0.75 \text{ m}$ is to be used to deliver exactly $200 \text{ J}$ of energy to a specimen. From what angle $\alpha$ from the vertical should it be released? (Assume fracture occurs exactly at the bottom, so all initial energy is delivered.)

**Solution:**

Since all energy is delivered, $\beta = 0^{\circ}$, so $\cos\beta = 1$.

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

$$200 = 15 \times 9.81 \times 0.75 \times (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

$$200 = 110.3625 \times (1 - \cos\alpha)$$

$$1 - \cos\alpha = \frac{200}{110.3625} = 1.8124$$

Since this value exceeds 1, the given energy is **not physically achievable** with this pendulum — a larger release angle (greater than $180^{\circ}$, impossible) or a heavier/longer pendulum would be required. This illustrates an important KTU examiner trick: always check for **physical realizability**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Pendulum Test Apparatus — Functional Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram represents the functional architecture of a Charpy/Izod pendulum test setup, showing the energy flow from gravity input to fracture energy output.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Release Latch Mechanism] --> B[Pivot Bearing Assembly]
    B --> C[Pendulum Hammer Arm]
    C --> D[Hammer Striking Edge]
    D --> E[Specimen Anvil and Vice]
    E --> F[Notched Test Specimen]
    F --> G[Fracture Event - Energy Transfer]
    G --> H[Recoil Swing of Pendulum]
    H --> I[Angle Measurement Dial Gauge]
    I --> J[Energy Calculation Module]
    J --> K[Output: Absorbed Energy in Joules]
    L[Initial Potential Energy Source] --> A
    M[Base Frame and Foundation] --> B
    M --> E
    N[Calibration Mass Set] --> C
```

## 4.2 Energy Transformation Flow During a Pendulum Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Stage 1: Latched at Height H1 - PE Stored] --> E2[Stage 2: Released - PE to KE Conversion]
    E2 --> E3[Stage 3: Impact at Bottom - KE Delivered to Specimen]
    E3 --> E4[Stage 4: Specimen Absorbs Energy E_abs]
    E4 --> E5[Stage 5: Residual KE Carries Hammer Upward]
    E5 --> E6[Stage 6: KE back to PE at Lower Height H2]
    E6 --> E7[Output: E_abs = mgH1 minus mgH2]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Pendulum Geometry (Coordinate Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Pivot Point P - Top of Apparatus] --> P1[Effective Length L to Center of Mass]
    P1 --> P2[Vertical Reference Line - Downward]
    P2 --> P3[Initial Release Angle alpha - Left Side]
    P2 --> P4[Final Rise Angle beta - Right Side]
    P1 --> P5[Lowest Point of Swing Arc]
    P5 --> P6[Specimen Mounted on Anvil]
    P3 --> P7[Initial Height h1 above P5]
    P4 --> P8[Final Height h2 above P5]
    P6 --> P9[Cross Section A at Notch]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note for Students:** In your KTU exam answer sheet, always redraw a clean schematic of the pendulum showing the pivot, the release angle $\alpha$, the final angle $\beta$, the effective length $L$, and the specimen location at the bottom. Examiners award **partial marks** (often 2 out of 14) for a correctly labelled free-body diagram even if the numerical answer is slightly off.

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Charpy vs. Izod Test Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{What material is being tested?} --> Q2[Metals - Steel, Aluminum, Alloys]
    Q1 --> Q3[Plastics and Polymers]
    Q1 --> Q4[Composites and Fiber-Reinforced]
    Q2 --> R1[Use Charpy Test - Specimen as Horizontal Beam]
    Q3 --> R2[Use Izod Test - Specimen as Cantilever Beam]
    Q4 --> R3[Use Modified Charpy with Reduced Span]
    R1 --> S1[Notch faces away from striker]
    R2 --> S2[Notch faces striker]
    R3 --> S3[Custom fixture per ASTM D5045]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Impact Strength" as measured by the Pendulum Test. What is its SI unit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Impact strength is defined as the energy absorbed per unit cross-sectional area of a notched specimen during fracture under a sudden dynamic load delivered by a pendulum hammer. It is calculated as the ratio of the energy absorbed $E_{\text{abs}}$ (in Joules) to the original cross-sectional area $A$ (in m²) at the notch location.

$$IS = \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{A} \quad \text{[Unit: J/m}^2\text{ or kJ/m}^2\text{]}$$

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for correct SI unit)

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between Charpy and Izod Pendulum Impact Tests in three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Charpy Test | Izod Test |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specimen is supported as a **simply supported beam** (horizontal, both ends on anvils) | Specimen is clamped as a **cantilever beam** (vertical, one end fixed) |
| 2 | Notch is on the **side opposite** to the impact direction | Notch is on the **same side** as the impact direction |
| 3 | Used primarily for **metallic materials** (ASTM E23) | Used primarily for **plastics and polymers** (ASTM D256) |
| 4 | Striker hits the middle of the specimen | Striker hits the top protruding end |
| 5 | Span is fixed (40 mm standard) | Cantilever length is fixed |

(½ mark each point × 3 main points = 1.5 marks, 1.5 marks for tabular presentation)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q. (a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **pendulum impact testing machine**. Explain the procedure for conducting a Charpy impact test on a metallic specimen. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A pendulum impact testing machine consists of the following components:

1. **Heavy base frame** — absorbs vibrations and provides rigidity.
2. **Vertical pillars** — support the pivot bearing.
3. **Pendulum hammer** — a heavy mass attached to a rigid arm with a calibrated striking edge at the lower end.
4. **Anvil and supports** — the specimen is clamped horizontally between two anvils, 40 mm apart.
5. **Pendulum release mechanism** — a latch that holds the hammer at a preset angle.
6. **Dial gauge / encoder** — measures the final swing angle $\beta$.
7. **Safety enclosure** — guards the operator from flying fragments.

**Procedure for Charpy Test:**

1. Prepare a standard notched specimen (55 mm × 10 mm × 10 mm with a 2 mm deep V-notch).
2. Mount the specimen horizontally on the two anvils with the notch facing away from the striker.
3. Raise the pendulum to the latched position (typically at $160^{\circ}$ from vertical).
4. Note the initial potential energy of the pendulum (e.g., $300 \text{ J}$).
5. Release the pendulum latch.
6. The pendulum swings down, strikes the specimen, fractures it, and continues to rise on the other side.
7. Read the final swing angle $\beta$ from the dial.
8. Calculate the absorbed energy using $E = mgL(\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$.
9. Inspect the fracture surface for shear lip percentage.

(1 mark for sketch description, 1 mark for component list, 1 mark for specimen dimensions, 1 mark for test procedure steps, 1 mark for fracture inspection, 2 marks for any additional technical content)

---

**Q. (b)** A Charpy test is performed on a steel specimen. The pendulum has a mass of $25 \text{ kg}$ and an effective length of $0.9 \text{ m}$. The initial and final angles from the vertical are $150^{\circ}$ and $80^{\circ}$ respectively. Calculate:
(i) The energy absorbed by the specimen. **(4 marks)**
(ii) The impact strength if the cross-section at the notch is $100 \text{ mm}^2$. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $m = 25 \text{ kg}$, $L = 0.9 \text{ m}$, $\alpha = 150^{\circ}$, $\beta = 80^{\circ}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$, $A = 100 \text{ mm}^2 = 100 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$

**Part (i):** Energy absorbed.

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 25 \times 9.81 \times 0.9 \times (\cos 80^{\circ} - \cos 150^{\circ})$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 220.725 \times (0.1736 - (-0.8660))$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 220.725 \times 1.0396$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{abs}} \approx 229.47 \text{ J}}$$

**Part (ii):** Impact strength.

$$IS = \frac{E_{\text{abs}}}{A} = \frac{229.47}{100 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$IS = 2.2947 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m}^2$$

$$\boxed{IS \approx 2.295 \text{ MJ/m}^2}$$

(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark answer for (i); 1 mark formula, 1 mark unit conversion, 1 mark final numerical answer for (ii))

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (Alternative Choice)

**Q. (a)** Explain the **ductile-to-brittle transition temperature (DBTT)** phenomenon. How is the Pendulum Test used to determine it? Discuss its significance for automotive components operating in cold climates. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition of DBTT:**
Body-Centered Cubic (BCC) metals such as ferritic steels exhibit a sharp transition from ductile to brittle behavior as the temperature decreases. The temperature at which this transition occurs is called the Ductile-to-Brittle Transition Temperature (DBTT).

**How the Pendulum Test determines DBTT:**

1. A series of identical specimens (typically Charpy V-notch) is prepared.
2. Each specimen is tested at a different temperature, ranging from well below to well above the expected DBTT (e.g., $-80\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $+200\,^{\circ}\text{C}$).
3. The energy absorbed $E$ is plotted against test temperature $T$.
4. The resulting S-shaped curve shows:
   - **Upper shelf energy (USE):** High, nearly constant, corresponds to fully ductile fracture.
   - **Lower shelf energy (LSE):** Low, nearly constant, corresponds to fully brittle fracture.
   - **Transition region:** Sharp drop in energy.
5. The DBTT is conventionally defined as the temperature at which the absorbed energy equals the **average of USE and LSE**, or at which 50% of the fracture surface is fibrous.

**Significance for Automotive Components in Cold Climates:**

- Components like chassis rails, suspension arms, and steering knuckles must retain adequate toughness at sub-zero operating temperatures.
- In countries like Norway, Canada, Russia, and northern China where temperatures drop to $-40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$, a steel with DBTT = $-20\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ would fail catastrophically.
- Automakers therefore specify **fine-grained, low-carbon, low-sulfur steels** with DBTT well below the minimum service temperature.
- The Charpy test at $-40\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ is a mandatory acceptance criterion in many OEM specifications (e.g., VW TL 0100, Tata PV-STD-EE-001).

(2 marks for DBTT concept, 2 marks for experimental procedure, 2 marks for curve interpretation, 1 mark for automotive significance)

---

**Q. (b)** A pendulum of length $0.85 \text{ m}$ and mass $18 \text{ kg}$ is released from an initial angle of $135^{\circ}$ from the vertical. After breaking the specimen, it rises to $95^{\circ}$ on the other side. Calculate:
(i) The velocity of the hammer at the moment of impact. **(4 marks)**
(ii) The energy absorbed by the specimen. **(3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Given:** $m = 18 \text{ kg}$, $L = 0.85 \text{ m}$, $\alpha = 135^{\circ}$, $\beta = 95^{\circ}$, $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Part (i):** Velocity at impact.

$$v = \sqrt{2 g L (1 - \cos\alpha)}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2 \times 9.81 \times 0.85 \times (1 - \cos 135^{\circ})}$$

$$v = \sqrt{16.677 \times (1 - (-0.7071))}$$

$$v = \sqrt{16.677 \times 1.7071}$$

$$v = \sqrt{28.469}$$

$$\boxed{v \approx 5.335 \text{ m/s}}$$

**Part (ii):** Energy absorbed.

$$E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 18 \times 9.81 \times 0.85 \times (\cos 95^{\circ} - \cos 135^{\circ})$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 150.093 \times (-0.0872 - (-0.7071))$$

$$E_{\text{abs}} = 150.093 \times 0.6199$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{abs}} \approx 93.04 \text{ J}}$$

(1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark value of cosine, 1 mark final answer for (i); 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark final answer for (ii))

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Students often forget to convert cross-section from $\text{mm}^2$ to $\text{m}^2$ when computing impact strength. This single error loses 2-3 marks. **Always write the unit conversion step explicitly** in the solution.
> 2. **Angle reference confusion:** $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are measured from the **vertical**, NOT from the horizontal. Using the wrong reference flips the sign of cosine and gives a negative energy — a guaranteed 5-mark penalty.
> 3. **Confusing Charpy with Izod setup:** In board exams, students frequently describe an Izod cantilever setup when the question asks for a Charpy simply supported setup, or vice versa. This costs 2 marks for component description.
> 4. **Missing sketch:** A 14-mark Part B question always expects a labelled diagram. Skipping the sketch typically results in a 2-mark deduction even if the numerical answer is perfect.
> 5. **Forgetting to state the formula before substituting:** Examiners in KTU strictly follow the "formula-substitution-answer" triplet. Writing only the final number without the formula loses at least 1 mark per sub-question.
> 6. **Sign error in cosine:** For angles between $90^{\circ}$ and $180^{\circ}$, cosine is **negative**. Many students mistakenly write $\cos 135^{\circ} = +0.707$ instead of $-0.707$. Double-check your calculator is in **degree mode**, not radian mode.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Read this the night before the exam):**

- [ ] **Master Equation:** $\boxed{E_{\text{abs}} = m g L (\cos\beta - \cos\alpha)}$ — this alone solves 80% of KTU numerical questions on the Pendulum Test.
- [ ] **Impact Velocity:** $v = \sqrt{2gL(1-\cos\alpha)}$ — derived from energy conservation at the lowest swing point.
- [ ] **Impact Strength:** $IS = E_{\text{abs}} / A$ — always convert $A$ to $\text{m}^2$ before dividing.
- [ ] **Angle Reference:** Both $\alpha$ (release) and $\beta$ (final) are measured from the **vertical (downward)** line.
- [ ] **Charpy vs Izod:** Charpy = horizontal beam, notch opposite to striker, used for metals. Izod = vertical cantilever, notch on striker side, used for plastics.
- [ ] **Standards:** ASTM E23 / ISO 148 (Charpy metals), ASTM D256 / ISO 180 (Izod plastics), ASTM D6110 (Charpy plastics).
- [ ] **DBTT Identification:** Energy vs Temperature plot — Upper Shelf (ductile), Lower Shelf (brittle), transition region between.
- [ ] **Specimen Standards:** 55 × 10 × 10 mm with 2 mm deep 45° V-notch (Charpy); 64 × 12.7 × 3.2 mm with notch (Izod).
- [ ] **Friction Correction:** Always calibrate with a "no-specimen" swing to subtract air drag and bearing friction losses.
- [ ] **Fracture Surface:** Crystalline (brittle, shiny) vs Fibrous (ductile, dull grey) — both visual and quantitative indicators.
- [ ] **Real Automotive Use:** Bumper beams, chassis rails, suspension arms, dashboard polymers, and crash-test dummy calibration blocks all undergo pendulum impact qualification.
- [ ] **Physical Constants:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (use 9.81 unless the question specifies 9.8 or 10).
- [ ] **KTU Answer Style:** Always include (i) a labelled sketch, (ii) a "Given Data" list, (iii) the formula, (iv) substitution, (v) final boxed answer with units.

**You've now mastered the Pendulum Test topic for the KTU 2024 Scheme examination. Best of luck!** 🚗🔨
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Fixed Collision Barrier Test Procedure # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) The **Fixed Collision Barrier Test (FCBT)**, also internationally recognized as the **Full-Width Frontal Rigid Barrier Impact Test**, is a standardized full-scale destructive crashworthiness evaluation procedure in which a complete production-intent motor vehicle, propelled under controlled propulsion power, is driven into a stationary, rigid, vertical, non-deformable concrete barrier such that the **entire frontal width** of the vehicle makes near-simultaneous contact with the barrier face. The test is mandated by **FMVSS 208** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard No. 208 — U.S.A.), **ECE Regulation No. 94** (Europe), **AIS-052** (India), and **ADR 73/00** (Australia), and forms the backbone of frontal occupant protection certification worldwide. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:** A *Fixed Collision Barrier Test* is a **full-vehicle, full-width, non-deformable frontal impact test** conducted to evaluate **occupant injury criteria**, **vehicle structural crashworthiness**, and **restraint system effectiveness** under a precisely defined impact velocity (typically **48–56 km/h**) and a precisely defined rigid barrier geometry. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Wall of Truth" Imagine you are driving a remote-controlled toy car straight toward a thick concrete wall, and you *know* you cannot turn. Just before the wall, you release the controller. The wall does not move — it is the **judge**. Whatever crumples, whatever breaks, and what the crash-test dummy "feels" inside, is recorded. The wall is **infinitely rigid**; the car is the only thing allowed to absorb energy. - The **wall** = a **rigid barrier** (deceleration reference frame, mass ≈ ∞). - The **car** = the **vehicle under test (VUT)**. - The **dummy** = the **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** — the standardized "human stand-in" that *feels* what a real occupant would feel. > [!NOTE] > **Why a "fixed" barrier?** Because if the barrier moved, every car tested would behave differently. A fixed, infinitely massive barrier gives an **absolute, repeatable deceleration pulse** so engineers can fairly compare cars against the **same injury benchmark**. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Quantities, Standards & Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Source Standard | |---|---|---| | Barrier Mass | **Effectively infinite (rigid)** | FMVSS 208 / ECE R94 | | Barrier Face | Concrete block, **vertical**, ≥ 1.8 m wide | FMVSS 208 §S8.1 | | Test Speed | **48.3 km/h (30 mph)** or **56 km/h** | FMVSS 208 / AIS-052 | | Approach Angle | **0° (perpendicular)** | All major standards | | Overlap | **100 % full-width** | FCBT specific | | Dummy Type | **Hybrid III** (50th percentile male) | FMVSS 208 | | Data Sampling | **≥ 10 kHz** | SAE J211 | | Impact Duration | **~ 80–120 ms** | Empirical | | Peak Deceleration | **~ 20–35 g** | Empirical, vehicle-dependent | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Vocabulary for Exam:** *Barrier face*, *Impact velocity*, *Effective test speed*, *Anthropomorphic Test Device*, *Deceleration pulse*, *Crash pulse*, *Stiffness coefficient*, *Crumple zone*, *Restraint system*, *Occupant survival space*. --- ## 1.4 Distinction: FCBT vs. ODB vs. SINCAP | Test | Full Name | Overlap | Barrier Type | Key Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | **FCBT** | Fixed Collision Barrier Test | 100 % | Rigid | Occupant protection baseline | | **ODB** | Offset Deformable Barrier | 40 % | Deformable (aluminum honeycomb) | Real-world crash compatibility | | **SINCAP** | Side Impact NCAP | Side | Moving Deformable Barrier (MDB) | Side-impact occupant safety | > [!NOTE] > For KTU Module 3, the **Fixed Collision Barrier Test** is the **frontal full-width reference** against which all other crash tests are benchmarked. The rigid barrier eliminates the *barrier-compliance* variable, isolating **vehicle crashworthiness** as the sole dependent variable. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Underlying Engineering Logic — The "Why" Behind the Test The Fixed Collision Barrier Test is fundamentally a **controlled energy-dissipation experiment**. The governing physical principle is **conservation of linear momentum** combined with **Newton's Second Law**: $$F_{avg} = m_{vehicle} \cdot \frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t}$$ At the instant of impact, the vehicle's **kinetic energy** must be absorbed by: 1. **Structural crumpling** of the front-end (engine bay, rails, firewall). 2. **Restraint-system work** (seatbelt pretensioners, airbags). 3. **Occupant kinematics** (controlled forward motion of ATDs). > The barrier does not absorb energy. It is **infinitely stiff** by design. The **100 % design responsibility** for energy absorption lies with the **vehicle's frontal structure**. --- ## 2.2 Step-by-Step Test Procedure (KTU Board Standard) ### Phase I — Pre-Test Preparation 1. **Vehicle Inspection** - Production-intent vehicle; ≤ 500 km break-in mileage. - Fluids (fuel, coolant, oil) drained to **5 %** capacity to prevent post-test fire hazard. - Fuel tank filled with water-soluble dyed fluid to simulate fuel mass. 2. **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD) Placement** - Two (or more) Hybrid III 50th-percentile male dummies. - **Driver seat** and **Front passenger seat** positions. - Dummies instrumented with **accelerometers**, **load cells**, **angular rate sensors** in head, chest, pelvis, femurs, tibia, and feet. 3. **Dummy Positioning Protocol (per SAE J1733 / FMVSS 208 §S8)** - Seated upright, hands on steering wheel (driver) / on lap (passenger). - Seat adjusted to mid-track, mid-height position. - Heels on floor, hips rearmost, torso against seatback. 4. **Vehicle Instrumentation** - High-speed cameras (≥ 1 000 fps) at multiple angles: **frontal, lateral, overhead, onboard**. - Onboard **accelerometers** at B-pillar, engine cradle, and rear floor. - **Rate gyros** to measure pitch and roll. - Data acquisition at **≥ 10 kHz** channel rate. ### Phase II — Test Execution 5. **Propulsion** - Vehicle is propelled by a **gas-propelled sled pusher, winch, or overhead cable release** — *not* by its own engine. - This ensures a **precise, repeatable impact velocity** and a **level attitude** at impact. 6. **Approach Geometry** - Approach path is straight, **within ± 1°** of barrier normal. - Barrier face **vertical** (≤ ± 0.5° tilt). 7. **Impact Velocity Tolerance** - Target: **48.3 km/h ± 0.5 km/h** (or 56 km/h for ECE R94). - Measured at the vehicle's **center of gravity (CG)** in the last 0.5 m before impact. ### Phase III — Post-Impact Analysis 8. **Data Acquisition Window** - All data captured from **0.5 s before impact to 2.0 s after impact**. - **CFC 60 / CFC 180 / CFC 1000** filtering per SAE J211 channel class. 9. **Measurements Computed** - **HIC (Head Injury Criterion)** — HIC₁₅ and HIC₃₆. - **Chest G's (3 ms clip)** — peak resultant chest acceleration. - **Neck Injury Criterion (Nij)**. - **Femur Force (Fz)**. - **Thoracic Trauma Index (TTI)**. - **Vehicle residual crush profile.** --- ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Exam-Ready) | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Kinetic Energy absorbed** | $E_k = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | J (Joules) | Total energy the vehicle must absorb | | 2 | **Average Crash Force** | $F_{avg} = \dfrac{m \cdot \Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | N (Newtons) | $\Delta t$ = pulse duration | | 3 | **Peak Deceleration** | $a_{peak} = \dfrac{v_{impact}^2}{2 \cdot s_{crush}}$ | m/s² | $s_{crush}$ = effective crush distance | | 4 | **Stiffness Coefficient (k)** | $k = \dfrac{F_{crush}}{\delta_{crush}}$ | N/m | Slope of force–crush curve | | 5 | **HIC — Head Injury Criterion** | $\text{HIC} = \left[ \dfrac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\,dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$ | dimensionless | Choose $t_2 - t_1 \le 15$ ms (HIC₁₅) or 36 ms (HIC₃₆) | | 6 | **Severity Index (SI)** | $\text{SI} = \displaystyle\int_0^{T} a^{2.5}(t)\,dt$ | — | Gadd-style integral | | 7 | **3-ms Clip Chest Acceleration** | $a_{clip} = \max_{3\,ms} \left[ \dfrac{1}{3} \displaystyle\int_{t_1}^{t_1 + 3\,ms} a(\tau)\, d\tau \right]$ | g | SAE J211 CFC 180 filter | | 8 | **Neck Injury Criterion** | $N_{ij} = \dfrac{F_z}{F_{zc}} + \dfrac{M_y}{M_{yc}}$ | — | Compression + flexion/extension | | 9 | **Average Force for a 50 g Pulse** | $F = m \cdot g \cdot 50$ | N | Quick check during pulse design | | 10 | **Pulse Duration Estimate** | $\Delta t \approx \dfrac{2 \cdot s_{crush}}{v_{impact}}$ | s | Approximate engineering estimate | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **standard** (FMVSS 208 / ECE R94 / AIS-052) being referred to, and the **dummy type** (Hybrid III 50th male) when reporting HIC and chest values. Numerical values **without standard context** are penalized by KTU examiners. --- ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Crashworthiness R&D**: Used by every OEM (e.g., Toyota, Tata Motors, Mahindra) to develop safer front-end structures. - **Regulatory Certification**: Mandatory for sale in US, EU, Indian markets. - **Insurance NCAP Star Rating**: Foundation for Bharat NCAP, Euro NCAP, and Global NCAP frontal scores. - **Forensic Engineering**: Used in accident reconstruction to validate simulation models (LS-DYNA, PAM-CRASH, RADIOSS). - **Material Selection**: Drives adoption of **hot-formed boron steel**, **aluminum extrusions**, and **carbon-fiber composites** in crash-loaded structures. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Worked Example ## 3.1 Derivation — Head Injury Criterion (HIC) from Gadd's Severity Index The **HIC** is mathematically derived from the **Gadd Severity Index (SI, 1961)**. Below is the full analytical chain, suitable for KTU 14-mark derivations. ### 3.1.1 Start with the Gadd Severity Index (GSI) Gadd proposed that injury occurs when the integral of $a^{2.5}(t)$ over time exceeds a critical value: $$\text{GSI} = \int_{0}^{T} \bigl[a(t)\bigr]^{2.5} \, dt \quad \leq \quad 1000$$ **Where:** - $a(t)$ = resultant head acceleration at the **CG of the head**, in units of **g** (multiples of 9.81 m/s²). - $T$ = total event duration. - The exponent 2.5 was empirically fitted to **Wayne State University** concussion tolerance data. ### 3.1.2 Reformulation — Windowed Average Acceleration Let us define the **average acceleration** over an arbitrary time window $[t_1, t_2]$ as: $$\bar{a} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt$$ ### 3.1.3 Raise to the 2.5th Power and Multiply by Window Length Multiplying $\bar{a}^{2.5}$ by the window length $(t_2 - t_1)$ gives the **HIC for that specific window**: $$\text{HIC}(t_1, t_2) = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$$ ### 3.1.4 Take the Worst-Case (Maximum) Window The **HIC value** reported for a crash event is the **maximum HIC over all admissible windows**: $$\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1, t_2} \left\{ \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1}\int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1) \right\}$$ ### 3.1.5 Apply the Standard Window Constraint - **HIC₁₅** → $(t_2 - t_1) \le 15$ ms (FMVSS 208, US standard). - **HIC₃₆** → $(t_2 - t_1) \le 36$ ms (ECE R94, EU standard). - **HIC value of 1 000** = the regulatory limit (≈ 75 % probability of severe head injury at this threshold). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Derivation Tip:** Whenever HIC is asked, always (a) start with Gadd's exponent 2.5, (b) define average acceleration, (c) raise to 2.5, (d) multiply by window length, and (e) state the constraint (15 ms or 36 ms). Skipping the constraint typically costs 2 marks. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Energy & Peak Force Calculation (KTU Style) > **Problem Statement (Sample):** > A passenger car of mass $m = 1\,500$ kg strikes a fixed rigid barrier head-on at $v = 48.3$ km/h. The front structure deforms by an effective crush distance of $s = 0.45$ m, and the average pulse duration is $\Delta t = 90$ ms. Calculate: > (a) the kinetic energy to be absorbed by the vehicle, > (b) the average crash force on the vehicle, > (c) the peak deceleration in units of *g*. ### Part (a) — Kinetic Energy **Step 1 — Convert velocity to SI units:** $$v = 48.3 \,\text{km/h} = 48.3 \times \frac{1000}{3600} \, \text{m/s} = 13.42 \, \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the kinetic energy equation:** $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (13.42)^2$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate numerically:** $$(13.42)^2 = 180.10 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$E_k = 0.5 \times 1500 \times 180.10 = 135\,075 \, \text{J} \approx 135 \, \text{kJ}$$ > ✅ **Answer (a):** $E_k \approx \mathbf{135 \, kJ}$ — this is the energy the car's crumple zones, restraint systems, and structure must dissipate. --- ### Part (b) — Average Crash Force **Step 1 — Use the impulse–momentum theorem:** $$F_{avg} \cdot \Delta t = m \cdot \Delta v = m \cdot v$$ **Step 2 — Solve for $F_{avg}$:** $$F_{avg} = \frac{m \cdot v}{\Delta t} = \frac{1500 \times 13.42}{0.090}$$ **Step 3 — Compute:** $$F_{avg} = \frac{20\,130}{0.090} = 223\,666.67 \, \text{N} \approx 223.7 \, \text{kN}$$ > ✅ **Answer (b):** $F_{avg} \approx \mathbf{224 \, kN}$. The barrier pushes back on the car with ~ 22.4 tonnes-force during the pulse. --- ### Part (c) — Peak Deceleration in *g* **Step 1 — Use the kinematic work–energy equivalence:** $$v^2 = 2 \cdot a_{peak} \cdot s \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a_{peak} = \frac{v^2}{2 s}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** $$a_{peak} = \frac{(13.42)^2}{2 \times 0.45} = \frac{180.10}{0.90} = 200.11 \, \text{m/s}^2$$ **Step 3 — Convert to *g*:** $$a_{peak, g} = \frac{a_{peak}}{g} = \frac{200.11}{9.81} \approx 20.4 \, g$$ > ✅ **Answer (c):** $a_{peak} \approx \mathbf{20.4 \, g}$ — a typical value for a modern compact sedan. The occupant, restrained by belt + airbag, experiences *roughly* 20–25 g chest loading — the threshold for serious but survivable injury. --- ## 3.3 HIC Numerical Demo — Trapezoidal Pulse Suppose the head acceleration pulse is approximated as a **trapezoid** with peak $a_{max} = 60$ g lasting for a constant plateau of 12 ms. Compute the HIC for the 15-ms window centered on the plateau. **Step 1 — Convert $a$ to *g* (already in g):** $$a(t) = 60 \, g \;\;\text{(plateau)}$$ **Step 2 — Average over the 15-ms window (assume the plateau dominates the window):** $$\bar{a} \approx 60 \, g$$ **Step 3 — Apply the HIC formula:** $$\text{HIC}_{15} = \left[ \bar{a} \right]^{2.5} \times \Delta t = (60)^{2.5} \times 0.015$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate $(60)^{2.5}$:** $$60^{2} = 3600, \quad 60^{0.5} = 7.746 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 60^{2.5} = 3600 \times 7.746 = 27\,886$$ **Step 5 — Final HIC:** $$\text{HIC}_{15} = 27\,886 \times 0.015 = 418.3$$ > ✅ **Result:** HIC₁₅ ≈ 418 < 1 000 → **Survivable head injury risk.** (This is why 50 km/h barrier tests with modern airbags pass.) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Pass / Fail on FCBT (ECE R94 Reference) | Injury Metric | Computed Value | Regulatory Limit | Pass / Fail | |---|---|---|---| | HIC₁₅ (head) | 418 | ≤ 1 000 | ✅ Pass | | Chest 3-ms clip (g) | 42 g | ≤ 60 g | ✅ Pass | | Neck $F_z$ (N) | 2 100 N | ≤ 4 000 N (tension) / 4 000 N (compression) | ✅ Pass | | Femur $F_z$ (N) | 5 200 N | ≤ 10 000 N | ✅ Pass | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Expectation:** Always show **the regulatory limit alongside the computed value**. Students lose marks for showing the *number* without the *comparator*. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 FCBT Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid) > Below is a **block-level functional architecture flow** of a typical FCBT test, mapping the sequence of physical sub-systems, instrumentation channels, and data post-processing stages. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[VUT Preparation] --> B[ATD Calibration] B --> C[Instrumentation Wiring] C --> D[Photography Setup] D --> E[Propulsion System Engagement] E --> F[Free-Flight Run-Up] F --> G[Barrier Impact] G --> H[Onboard Data Acquisition] G --> I[High-Speed Camera Trigger] H --> J[SAE J211 Filtering] I --> K[Film Analysis] J --> L[Injury Metric Computation] K --> L L --> M[HIC Chest Nij Femur] M --> N{Regulatory Compliance} N -->|Pass| O[Vehicle Certified] N -->|Fail| P[Re-Design Iterate] P --> A classDef prep fill:#dfe7fd,stroke:#3454d1,color:#1a2b6b classDef ex fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b3001b,color:#5a0000 classDef post fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1a7a1a,color:#0d4a0d class A,B,C,D prep class E,F,G,H,I,J,K ex class L,M,N,O,P post ``` --- ## 4.2 Schematic — Vehicle Impact Geometry vs. Barrier ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PreImpact["Pre-Impact Geometry"] V1[Vehicle CG at vi] end subgraph ImpactMoment["t = 0 ms — Full-Width Contact"] BARRIER["RIGID BARRIER
Mass = Infinity
Velocity = 0"] VEHICLE["VEHICLE UNDER TEST
Mass m, Velocity vi"] end subgraph PostImpact["t approx 90 ms — At Rest"] CRUSH["CRUSH ZONE
Effective Length s_crush"] V2[Vehicle CG at v = 0] end V1 --> VEHICLE VEHICLE -->|Strikes| BARRIER BARRIER -->|Equal opposite force| VEHICLE VEHICLE --> CRUSH CRUSH --> V2 classDef rigid fill:#fce4d6,stroke:#cc5500,color:#5a2300 classDef deform fill:#dce8fc,stroke:#1a3a8f,color:#0a1a4f classDef rest fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#555555,color:#1a1a1a class BARRIER rigid class VEHICLE,CRUSH deform class V1,V2 rest ``` --- ## 4.3 Injury Metric Computation Pipeline (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD RAW[Raw ATD Sensor Data] --> FILT[SAE J211 Channel Filter] FILT --> RES[Resultant Vector Computation] RES --> HICBLK[HIC 15 / 36 Calculation] RES --> CHESTBLK[3 ms Clip Chest G] RES --> NECKBLK[Nij Neck Force Moment] RES --> FEMURBLK[Femur Fz Compressive] HICBLK --> LIMIT{HIC vs 1000} CHESTBLK --> LIMIT2{Chest vs 60 g} NECKBLK --> LIMIT3{Nij vs 1.0} FEMURBLK --> LIMIT4{Fz vs 10000 N} LIMIT --> REPORT[Compliance Report] LIMIT2 --> REPORT LIMIT3 --> REPORT LIMIT4 --> REPORT classDef rawclass fill:#fdf2cc,stroke:#a17400,color:#523800 classDef compclass fill:#d6f5d6,stroke:#1a7a1a,color:#0d4a0d classDef limitclass fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#b3001b,color:#5a0000 class RAW,FILT,RES rawclass class HICBLK,CHESTBLK,NECKBLK,FEMURBLK,REPORT compclass class LIMIT,LIMIT2,LIMIT3,LIMIT4 limitclass ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** Arrows represent **information flow or physical sequence**. Boxes with **red borders** = decision/compliance checks. Boxes with **green borders** = final compliance outputs. Boxes with **yellow borders** = raw data processing stages. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** *Define the Fixed Collision Barrier Test. State any two regulatory standards governing this test.* **Model Answer:** The **Fixed Collision Barrier Test (FCBT)** is a full-scale frontal crashworthiness test in which a complete motor vehicle is propelled at a defined impact velocity into a **rigid, vertical, non-deformable barrier** such that **100 % of the frontal width** makes simultaneous contact. The test is used to evaluate **occupant protection, restraint system performance, and frontal structural energy absorption**. Two regulatory standards governing this test are: 1. **FMVSS 208** (Federal Motor Vehicle Safety Standard No. 208, USA) — test speed **48.3 km/h**. 2. **ECE Regulation No. 94** (Europe) — test speed **56 km/h** with offset 40 % deformable barrier (the corresponding full-width test is **ECE R94 Annex IV**). > **Key terms (must appear):** rigid barrier, full-width, frontal impact, FMVSS 208, ECE R94. --- ### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** *What is an Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)? Why is the Hybrid III 50th-percentile male dummy preferred in FCBTs?* **Model Answer:** An **Anthropomorphic Test Device (ATD)** is a **biofidelic, instrumented mechanical surrogate** of a human occupant, fitted with **accelerometers, load cells, and angular rate sensors** at critical anatomical locations (head, chest, pelvis, femurs, tibia, feet) to measure the **mechanical loads** that a real human would experience during a crash. The **Hybrid III 50th-percentile male dummy** is preferred because: 1. It represents the **median adult male** population (height ~ 1.75 m, mass ~ 78 kg), making results **statistically generalizable**. 2. It has **well-validated biofidelity** in the frontal-impact load range (validated against PMHS and volunteer tests). 3. It is the **globally harmonized standard** mandated by FMVSS 208, ECE R94, and AIS-052. 4. It enables **direct cross-OEM and cross-regulatory comparison** of injury metrics. > **Key terms (must appear):** Hybrid III, biofidelity, 50th percentile, FMVSS 208, AIS-052. --- ## 5.2 Part B — ESE Long Answer (14 Marks) — Internal Choice > **KTU 2024 Note:** Part B questions in ESE are 14 marks with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (internal choice). --- ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]` **Q:** (a) Explain in detail the **procedure of a Fixed Collision Barrier Test** as per FMVSS 208, including vehicle preparation, dummy placement, instrumentation, and propulsion method. (7 marks) (b) A **1 800 kg** vehicle traveling at **50 km/h** strikes a **fixed rigid barrier**. The effective crush distance of the front structure is **0.5 m**. Determine:     (i) the **kinetic energy** of the vehicle just before impact.     (ii) the **average crash force** assuming a uniform pulse duration of **100 ms**.     (iii) the **peak deceleration** in *g*. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question A ### Part (a) — Test Procedure as per FMVSS 208 **Step 1 — Vehicle Preparation:** - A **production-intent vehicle** is used with running mileage not exceeding **500 km** for break-in. - All **flammable fluids** (fuel, oil, brake fluid) are drained to a **5 % residual** to prevent post-test fire. - The fuel tank is filled with a **stained, water-based fluid** to mimic fuel mass without fire risk. - The vehicle is **weighed** and its **center of gravity** is determined. **Step 2 — Dummy Calibration and Placement:** - Two **Hybrid III 50th-percentile male dummies** are placed in the front driver and passenger seats. - Dummies are dressed in **standard cotton T-shirts and shorts** (per FMVSS 208). - Each dummy's **head, neck, chest, pelvis, femurs, tibia, and feet** are instrumented with **tri-axial accelerometers** and **six-axis load cells**. - The seat is adjusted to **mid-track, mid-height**; seatback is **upright**; head restraint is locked at the **lowest detent**. **Step 3 — Vehicle Instrumentation:** - **Onboard accelerometers** are mounted at the **B-pillar (right and left)**, **engine cradle**, and **rear floor pan**. - **High-speed cameras** are placed at **front, side, and overhead** views at ≥ **1 000 fps**. - **Wheel-speed sensors** and **pressure transducers** (for airbag deployment) are connected to a **data acquisition system (DAQS)**. **Step 4 — Propulsion:** - The vehicle is propelled by a **pneumatic / hydraulic pusher** or a **winch-released cable system** — *not* by its own engine. - This guarantees a **level approach attitude** and a **precisely measured impact velocity**. - The approach path is **straight, within ± 1°** of barrier normal. **Step 5 — Test Execution and Data Capture:** - The pusher releases the vehicle at a **precisely measured speed of 48.3 km/h ± 0.5 km/h**. - Data is captured from **0.5 s before** to **2.0 s after** impact. - All channels are **filtered per SAE J211** (CFC 60 for head acceleration, CFC 180 for chest/Femur, CFC 1000 for foot/ankle). **Step 6 — Post-Impact Analysis:** - Films are analyzed for **occupant kinematics**, **restraint deployment**, and **vehicle deformation**. - Injury metrics (**HIC, Chest G, Nij, Femur Fz**) are computed and compared to **FMVSS 208 limits**. > **Marks Distribution Hint (7 marks):** 1 mark for definition + 1 mark for pre-test prep + 1 mark for dummy setup + 1 mark for instrumentation + 1 mark for propulsion + 1 mark for data analysis + 1 mark for post-test reporting. --- ### Part (b) — Numerical Problem Solution **Given:** - Mass $m = 1\,800$ kg - Impact velocity $v = 50$ km/h - Crush distance $s = 0.5$ m - Pulse duration $\Delta t = 100$ ms = 0.100 s **(i) Kinetic Energy:** **Step 1 — Convert velocity:** $$v = 50 \times \frac{1000}{3600} = 13.89 \, \text{m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute $E_k$:** $$E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1800 \times (13.89)^2$$ $$(13.89)^2 = 192.93 \, \text{m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ $$E_k = 900 \times 192.93 = 173\,637 \, \text{J} \approx 173.6 \, \text{kJ}$$ > ✅ **Answer (i):** $E_k \approx \mathbf{173.6 \, kJ}$ **(ii) Average Crash Force:** **Step 1 — Impulse–momentum equation:** $$F_{avg} = \frac{m \cdot v}{\Delta t} = \frac{1800 \times 13.89}{0.100}$$ **Step 2 — Compute:** $$F_{avg} = \frac{25\,002}{0.100} = 250\,020 \, \text{N} \approx 250 \, \text{kN}$$ > ✅ **Answer (ii):** $F_{avg} \approx \mathbf{250 \, kN}$ (≈ 25.5 tonnes-force) **(iii) Peak Deceleration in *g*:** **Step 1 — Kinematic equation:** $$a_{peak} = \frac{v^2}{2 s} = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{192.93}{1.0} = 192.93 \, \text{m/s}^2$$ **Step 2 — Convert to *g*:** $$a_{peak, g} = \frac{192.93}{9.81} \approx 19.67 \, g$$ > ✅ **Answer (iii):** $a_{peak} \approx \mathbf{19.7 \, g}$ > **Marks Distribution Hint (7 marks):** 2 marks (i) + 2 marks (ii) + 2 marks (iii) + 1 mark for correct units and final statements. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]` **Q:** (a) Define **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)**. Derive the HIC expression starting from the **Gadd Severity Index**, clearly explaining each mathematical step. State the **HIC limit** prescribed by **FMVSS 208** and **ECE R94**. (7 marks) (b) The head acceleration profile of a crash-test dummy is approximated by a **trapezoidal pulse**: rises linearly from 0 to **80 g** in **5 ms**, holds at **80 g** for **20 ms**, and decays linearly from **80 g** to 0 in **10 ms**. Compute the **HIC₁₅** for this pulse and comment on its regulatory compliance. (7 marks) --- #### Model Solution to Question B ### Part (a) — Definition and Derivation of HIC **Definition:** The **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** is a **dimensionless, time-windowed, integral-based** measure of the **likelihood of skull fracture or severe brain injury**, derived from the **resultant head acceleration** $a(t)$ measured at the **center of gravity of the dummy's head**. **Derivation from Gadd Severity Index:** **Step 1 — Start with the Gadd Severity Index (1961):** $$\text{GSI} = \int_{0}^{T} \bigl[a(t)\bigr]^{2.5} \, dt$$ This integral says: cumulative injury correlates with the time-integral of acceleration raised to the power **2.5** (a value empirically determined from Wayne State concussion curves). **Step 2 — Define the average acceleration over a window:** $$\bar{a}_{[t_1, t_2]} = \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt$$ **Step 3 — Substitute into GSI-like formulation:** We seek a quantity proportional to $\bar{a}^{2.5} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)$, which equals the GSI when $a$ is constant. $$\text{HIC}(t_1, t_2) = \left[ \frac{1}{t_2 - t_1} \int_{t_1}^{t_2} a(t)\, dt \right]^{2.5} (t_2 - t_1)$$ **Step 4 — Take the worst-case window:** $$\text{HIC} = \max_{t_1, t_2} \text{HIC}(t_1, t_2)$$ **Step 5 — Apply standard constraints:** - **FMVSS 208** uses **HIC₁₅** with $t_2 - t_1 \le 15$ ms. Limit: **HIC₁₅ ≤ 1 000**. - **ECE R94** uses **HIC₃₆** with $t_2 - t_1 \le 36$ ms. Limit: **HIC₃₆ ≤ 1 000**. --- ### Part (b) — HIC₁₅ Calculation for Trapezoidal Pulse **Pulse Description:** - **Ramp-up:** 0 → 80 g linearly over 5 ms. - **Plateau:** 80 g for 20 ms (from $t = 5$ ms to $t = 25$ ms). - **Ramp-down:** 80 g → 0 linearly over 10 ms (from $t = 25$ ms to $t = 35$ ms). **Step 1 — Identify the worst-case 15-ms window:** The plateau is at 80 g, and a 15-ms window entirely inside the plateau will have $\bar{a} = 80$ g. **Step 2 — Compute the average acceleration over this window:** $$\bar{a} = 80 \, g$$ **Step 3 — Compute $(80)^{2.5}$:** $$80^{2} = 6400, \quad 80^{0.5} = 8.944, \quad 80^{2.5} = 6400 \times 8.944 = 57\,241$$ **Step 4 — Apply the HIC₁₅ formula:** $$\text{HIC}_{15} = (\bar{a})^{2.5} \times \Delta t = 57\,241 \times 0.015$$ **Step 5 — Final HIC value:** $$\text{HIC}_{15} = 858.6$$ **Step 6 — Regulatory comparison:** | Standard | HIC Limit | Computed | Verdict | |---|---|---|---| | FMVSS 208 (HIC₁₅) | ≤ 1 000 | 858.6 | ✅ **Pass** | | ECE R94 (HIC₃₆) | ≤ 1 000 | (likely ≤ 858.6) | ✅ **Pass** | > **Conclusion:** The computed HIC₁₅ of **≈ 859** is below the regulatory limit of **1 000**. The head injury risk is therefore **survivable**, and the restraint system (airbag + seatbelt) is performing adequately for this pulse. > **Marks Distribution Hint (7 marks):** 1 mark pulse parameter identification + 1 mark worst-case window selection + 1 mark average calculation + 1 mark power calculation + 1 mark HIC computation + 1 mark comparison + 1 mark conclusion. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in FCBT Questions (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Velocity unit confusion** — failing to convert km/h to m/s costs 1 mark immediately. > 2. **Wrong dummy type** — writing "Hybrid II" or "THOR" instead of "Hybrid III 50th percentile male" loses 1–2 marks. > 3. **Omitting the standard reference** — HIC must be qualified as HIC₁₅ (FMVSS) or HIC₃₆ (ECE R94). > 4. **Skipping the window constraint** — HIC without a window constraint is mathematically incomplete. > 5. **Crush energy vs. kinetic energy** — students often confuse "kinetic energy absorbed" with "strain energy stored in the structure." > 6. **Propulsion method** — saying the vehicle is "driven into the barrier by its engine" is **wrong**; it is propelled by an external pusher/winch. > 7. **Forgetting to state impact velocity** — always quote the standard's prescribed speed (48.3 km/h for FMVSS, 56 km/h for ECE R94). > 8. **No diagram** — KTU board rewards 1–2 marks for a clean schematic of the test setup or pulse. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > 📌 **Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU prep):** - [ ] FCBT = **Full-Width Frontal Rigid Barrier Test**; impact velocity **48.3 km/h** (FMVSS 208) or **56 km/h** (ECE R94). - [ ] Barrier is **rigid, vertical, ≥ 1.8 m wide**; approach is **perpendicular, ± 1°**. - [ ] Propulsion is by an **external pusher / winch** — never by the vehicle's own engine. - [ ] Dummies used: **Hybrid III 50th-percentile male**, instrumented at head, chest, pelvis, femurs, tibia, feet. - [ ] Data acquisition ≥ **10 kHz**, filtered per **SAE J211** channel classes (CFC 60 / 180 / 1000). - [ ] **Head Injury Criterion (HIC)** = windowed average acceleration raised to **2.5** power, multiplied by window length, maximized over all admissible windows. - [ ] **HIC₁₅** uses a 15-ms window (FMVSS 208); **HIC₃₆** uses a 36-ms window (ECE R94). - [ ] **HIC regulatory limit = 1 000** (corresponds to ~ 75 % probability of severe head injury). - [ ] **Chest 3-ms clip** ≤ **60 g**; **Femur $F_z$** ≤ **10 000 N**;
