**Neck Nij** ≤ **1.0**. - [ ] Kinetic energy absorbed = $\frac{1}{2} m v^2$; for a 1 500 kg car at 48.3 km/h, this is ~ **135 kJ**. - [ ] Average crash force $F_{avg} = \dfrac{m v}{\Delta t}$; typical pulse duration **80–120 ms**; typical $F_{avg}$ for a midsize sedan is **200–250 kN**. - [ ] **Crumple zone** of a well-designed car absorbs **~ 60–70 %** of crash energy; restraint systems absorb the rest. - [ ] FCBT is the **baseline**; ODB and SINCAP are complementary real-world tests. - [ ] **Always state units, standards, and dummy type** in the KTU answer script — these are easy marks. > ✅ **One-line takeaway:** *The FCBT is the global standard for frontal crashworthiness — a fixed rigid wall, a precisely known speed, a Hybrid III dummy, and the HIC / chest G / Nij / femur force numbers are the four pillars of its pass / fail verdict.*

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Performance Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Vehicle Performance and Testing**, *Performance Criteria* refer to the **quantitative and qualitative benchmarks** that define the acceptable operational, safety, comfort, and environmental behavior of a road vehicle. These criteria form the **acceptance boundary** against which a vehicle's measured performance during standardized test procedures is compared to certify it for roadworthiness and market release.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PEMUT633) syllabus, performance criteria encompass the measurable limits for **acceleration, braking, gradability, top speed, fuel/energy economy, emissions, noise, handling, ride comfort, and crashworthiness**, as prescribed by national (AIS – Automotive Industry Standards under CMVR-India) and international (ECE – Economic Commission for Europe, ISO, SAE, FMVSS) regulatory frameworks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 3 specifically addresses the **Performance Criteria** used to evaluate dynamic vehicle behavior. Students must connect every test procedure (covered in other modules) to its corresponding *acceptance limit* (covered here).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **driving school examination**:
- The instructor has a *rubric* (criteria): "must stop within 10 m at 50 km/h", "must climb a 30% slope without stalling", "must turn a circle of 12 m radius without hitting cones".
- Your *performance* (your driving) is then graded against these criteria.
- A vehicle is tested the same way. The **Performance Criteria** are the "rubric" and the **vehicle's measured numbers** are the "score".

**Engineering Translation:**
- **Vehicle** → the "student"
- **Test Track / Chassis Dynamometer** → the "exam hall"
- **Performance Criteria** → the "answer key" set by the regulatory body
- **Test Report** → the "marks sheet"

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition — Performance Criterion (KTU-style):**
> A *Performance Criterion* is a numerically specified boundary value (or set of boundary conditions) for a vehicle attribute, derived from regulatory standards, manufacturer specifications, or customer requirements, used to PASS or FAIL the vehicle in a standardized evaluation.

## 1.3 Categorization of Performance Criteria

Performance criteria are broadly classified into **six functional categories** per the standard KTU framework:

| S.No | Category | Core Question Answered | Typical Standard Body |
|------|----------|------------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Powertrain / Acceleration Criteria** | "How fast can the vehicle accelerate?" | ISO 13215, ECE R51, AIS |
| 2 | **Braking / Deceleration Criteria** | "How quickly and safely can it stop?" | ECE R13, AIS-052, FMVSS 135 |
| 3 | **Gradeability Criteria** | "What slope can it climb?" | ECE, SAE J218, AIS |
| 4 | **Fuel / Energy Economy Criteria** | "How efficiently does it consume fuel/energy?" | AIS-053, EPA, ECE R101 |
| 5 | **Handling & Stability Criteria** | "Can it be steered safely and predictably?" | ISO 3888, ECE R13-H, AIS |
| 6 | **Ride Comfort & NVH Criteria** | "How comfortable and quiet is it?" | ISO 2631, ECE R51, AIS |

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Reference Values (must memorize):**
> - **Standard gravitational acceleration:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2 \approx 10 \text{ m/s}^2$ (engineering approximation)
> - **Maximum theoretical grade (friction limit):** $\tan^{-1}(\mu) \approx 100\%$ grade when $\mu = 1.0$
> - **Target service deceleration:** $a_{dec} \geq 5 \text{ m/s}^2$ (≈ **0.51 g**) for passenger cars (ECE R13 / AIS-052)
> - **Reference fuel economy test cycle:** **Indian Driving Cycle (IDC)** – MIDC for M1 category; IDC for two-wheelers
> - **Steering wheel free-play limit:** $\leq \pm 15°$ (dead-band)
> - **Whole-body vibration exposure limit (8-hour A(8)):** $0.5 \text{ m/s}^2$ (ISO 2631-1)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Powertrain / Acceleration Performance Criteria

The acceleration criterion is governed by the vehicle's **power-to-weight ratio** and **available tractive force** versus **resistance forces** at the tire–road interface.

### 2.1.1 Maximum Speed Criterion
The vehicle is required to attain a manufacturer-stated maximum speed ($V_{max}$) on a level road within an acceptable time-window, typically **30–60 s**, with the test tolerance of **±2 km/h**.

$$V_{max} \text{ is reached when } F_t = F_r + F_{ad}$$

Where:
- $F_t$ = tractive force at wheels
- $F_r$ = rolling resistance force
- $F_{ad}$ = aerodynamic drag force

### 2.1.2 Acceleration Time Criterion
Standard KTU/ECE criteria for passenger cars:
- **0–100 km/h time** $\leq$ manufacturer's claim (typically **10–14 s** for mid-segment cars)
- **Standing-start 400 m time** $\leq$ manufacturer's claim
- **Passing maneuver (40–100 km/h)** $\leq$ manufacturer-specified time, typically **8–12 s**

### 2.1.3 Acceleration Gradient Criterion
Rate of change of velocity must satisfy the following inequality for safety:

$$\frac{dV}{dt} \geq a_{min,acc} \quad \text{(typical } a_{min} = 1.5 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{)}$$

This ensures the vehicle can accelerate out of dangerous situations on a level road.

## 2.2 Braking Performance Criteria

The braking system must satisfy three simultaneous constraints per ECE R13 / AIS-052:

**Constraint 1 — Service Brake Deceleration:**
$$a_{service} \geq 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ for } M_1 \text{ (passenger cars)}$$
$$a_{service} \geq 4.0 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ for } M_2, M_3 \text{ (buses)}$$

**Constraint 2 — Stopping Distance (from 100 km/h):**
$$d_{stop} \leq 70 \text{ m} \quad (\text{fade test, cold and hot)}$$

**Constraint 3 — Parking Brake Holding Grade:**
$$\sin(\theta) \cdot m \cdot g \leq F_{park} = T_{park} \cdot i_{gear} \cdot \eta / r_{wheel}$$

The vehicle must hold stationary on a **18% grade (cars)** or **20% grade (heavy commercial)** using only the parking brake.

### 2.2.1 Energy-Based Braking Criterion
Brake energy dissipation must be within thermal capacity:

$$E_{kinetic} = \frac{1}{2} m V^2 \leq Q_{brake,allowable}$$

## 2.3 Gradeability Criteria

Gradeability is the **maximum slope (in % or degrees)** a vehicle can climb or descend under defined conditions.

**Three KTU-defined gradeabilities:**

1. **Maximum gradability** (theoretical, low-speed): $V \to 0$
2. **Sustained gradeability** (continuous climb): $V = V_{sustained}$ (e.g., 60 km/h)
3. **Instantaneous gradeability** (short bursts): $V = V_{max}$

$$\text{Grade (\%)} = 100 \times \tan(\theta)$$

**Typical KTU-cited criterion values:**
- Passenger cars: **30% sustained grade** minimum
- Heavy trucks: **25% sustained grade**, **40% maximum grade**
- Buses: **25% sustained**, fully loaded

## 2.4 Fuel / Energy Economy Criteria

Measured over standard test cycles, then compared against:
- **Corporate Average Fuel Economy (CAFE)** norms (India)
- **BS-VI / BS-VII emission + CO₂ fleet averages**
- **Manufacturer-stated ARAI-certified mileage** (tolerance ±10%)

**Key metric definitions:**

| Metric | Formula | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| **Volume-based FE** | $FE_V = \dfrac{V_{fuel}}{d} \times 100$ | km/L |
| **Mass-based FE** | $FE_m = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{d} \times 10^6$ | g/km |
| **Energy-based FE** | $FE_E = \dfrac{E_{fuel}}{d}$ | MJ/km or kWh/100 km |
| **CO₂ emission rate** | $\dot{m}_{CO_2} = FE_m^{-1} \times 3.16 \times \rho_{fuel}$ | g CO₂/km |

## 2.5 Handling & Stability Criteria

The vehicle must:
- Comply with **steady-state cornering** limits (lateral acceleration $\geq 0.4g$ without rollover)
- Pass the **Elk-test (moose test)** – ISO 3888-2 lane-change at **≥ 80 km/h** without lane excursion
- Maintain **understeer gradient** within:
$$\frac{d\delta}{da_y} \in [0, 4°/g] \quad (\text{slight understeer})$$
- **Yaw-rate response time** to step-steer $\leq 0.3$ s
- **Steering wheel returnability** to neutral within $30°$ of self-centering

## 2.6 Ride Comfort Criteria (ISO 2631)

Per ISO 2631-1, ride comfort criterion is based on **Vibration Dose Value (VDV)** and **8-hour equivalent RMS acceleration** $a_{w}$:

$$a_{w,8h} \leq 0.5 \text{ m/s}^2 \quad (\text{comfort threshold})$$

**Comfort zones:**
- $a_{w} < 0.315 \text{ m/s}^2$ → **Not uncomfortable**
- $0.315 \leq a_{w} < 0.63$ → **A little uncomfortable**
- $0.63 \leq a_{w} < 1.0$ → **Fairly uncomfortable**
- $a_{w} \geq 1.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ → **Very uncomfortable** (FAIL criterion)

---

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **all high-yield formulas** for the topic "Performance Criteria". No vertical pipe symbols (`|`) are used inside mathematical expressions to prevent markdown table breakage.

| # | Performance Criterion | Governing Formula | Standard Reference | Typical Pass Value | Unit |
|---|------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | Max speed equilibrium | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad}$ | ECE / SAE | $V_{max}$ claimed | km/h |
| 2 | Acceleration capability | $m a \leq F_t - (F_r + F_{ad} + F_g)$ | SAE J218 | $\geq 1.5$ | m/s² |
| 3 | Service brake decel | $a_{sb} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 d_{stop}}$ | ECE R13 / AIS-052 | $\geq 5.0$ | m/s² |
| 4 | Stopping distance | $d_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}$ | ECE R13 | $\leq 70$ from 100 km/h | m |
| 5 | Parking brake grade | $\theta_{park} = \arcsin\left(\dfrac{F_{park}}{mg}\right)$ | AIS | $\geq 18\%$ (cars) | deg/% |
| 6 | Gradeability (sustained) | $F_t = F_r + F_{ad} + mg \sin\theta$ | SAE J218 | $\geq 30\%$ | % |
| 7 | Roll resistance force | $F_r = m g f_r$ | SAE | – | N |
| 8 | Aerodynamic drag | $F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A V^2$ | – | – | N |
| 9 | Grade force | $F_g = m g \sin\theta$ | – | – | N |
| 10 | Fuel economy (volume) | $FE_V = \dfrac{d}{V_{fuel}}$ | AIS-053 | Manufacturer claim $\pm 10\%$ | km/L |
| 11 | Fuel economy (mass) | $FE_m = \dfrac{m_{fuel}}{d} \times 10^6$ | AIS-053 | – | g/km |
| 12 | Rolling resistance coef. | $f_r = f_0 + k V$ | – | Car: $0.012-0.015$ | – |
| 13 | Lift coefficient effect | $F_L = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_L A V^2$ | – | – | N |
| 14 | Yaw rate (steady) | $r = \dfrac{V / R}{1 + K V^2}$ | SAE J670 | – | rad/s |
| 15 | Understeer gradient | $K = \dfrac{W_f}{C_{\alpha f}} - \dfrac{W_r}{C_{\alpha r}}$ | SAE | $0 \leq K \leq 4°/g$ | deg·s²/m |
| 16 | VDV (ride criterion) | $VDV = \left(\int_0^T a_w^4\, dt\right)^{1/4}$ | ISO 2631 | $\leq 9.1$ | m/s¹·⁷⁵ |
| 17 | RMS vibration | $a_w = \left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T a^2 dt\right)^{1/2}$ | ISO 2631 | $\leq 0.5$ | m/s² |
| 18 | Power-to-weight ratio | $PWR = \dfrac{P}{m}$ | – | Cars: $\geq 60$ | kW/tonne |
| 19 | Tractive force | $F_t = \dfrac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d}$ | – | – | N |
| 20 | Energy absorbed by brake | $E_b = \dfrac{1}{2} m V^2$ | – | $\leq$ thermal limit | J |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

Performance criteria serve **four critical engineering purposes** in the automotive industry:

1. **Homologation & Certification:** No vehicle can be legally sold without demonstrating compliance with codified performance criteria (CMVR-Tape / Type Approval in India).
2. **Benchmarking & Competitive Positioning:** Manufacturers benchmark competitor performance criteria to set internal development targets.
3. **Design Constraint Definition:** Performance criteria become **engineering design variables** during the vehicle development phase (e.g., brake disc size is selected to satisfy fade-test criteria).
4. **Customer Expectation Management:** Published performance criteria (0–100 km/h time, mileage, NCAP rating) drive purchase decisions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Braking Distance Criterion (Stopping Distance Formula)

**Starting Principle (Newton's Second Law on a braking vehicle):**

Let $V_0$ = initial velocity (m/s), $V$ = velocity at time $t$, $\mu$ = road-tire friction coefficient, $g$ = gravitational acceleration.

The braking force on a level road:
$$F_b = \mu \cdot m \cdot g$$

Newton's second law (deceleration):
$$m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -F_b = -\mu m g$$

**Step 1 — Cancel $m$ (constant mass):**
$$\dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\mu g$$

**Step 2 — Integrate with respect to time:**
$$\int_{V_0}^{V} dV = -\mu g \int_0^t dt$$

$$V(t) = V_0 - \mu g t$$

**Step 3 — Find time to stop ($V = 0$):**
$$0 = V_0 - \mu g t_{stop}$$

$$t_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0}{\mu g}$$

**Step 4 — Find stopping distance using $V \, dV = a \, ds$:**
$$\int_{V_0}^{0} V \, dV = -\mu g \int_0^{d_{stop}} ds$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate left integral:**
$$\dfrac{V^2}{2}\Big|_{V_0}^{0} = -\mu g \, d_{stop}$$

$$\dfrac{0 - V_0^2}{2} = -\mu g \, d_{stop}$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $d_{stop}$:**
$$d_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}$$

> **Final Result (Stopping Distance Criterion):**
> $$\boxed{\,d_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **fundamental braking performance criterion** referenced by ECE R13 and AIS-052 for stopping-distance compliance.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Maximum Speed Criterion (Equilibrium Condition)

**Vehicle Force Balance on a Level Road at Constant $V_{max}$:**

Total tractive force available at the wheels (from engine torque):
$$F_t = \dfrac{T_e(V_{max}) \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$$

Where:
- $T_e$ = engine torque at $V_{max}$ (N·m)
- $i_g$ = transmission gear ratio (top gear)
- $i_0$ = final drive ratio
- $\eta_t$ = drivetrain efficiency ($\approx 0.85 - 0.92$)
- $r_d$ = effective dynamic tire radius (m)

**Resistance Forces:**

(a) **Rolling Resistance:**
$$F_r = m \cdot g \cdot f_r(V) = m \cdot g \cdot (f_0 + k \cdot V)$$

(b) **Aerodynamic Drag:**
$$F_{ad} = \dfrac{1}{2} \rho \cdot C_D \cdot A_f \cdot V^2$$

Where:
- $\rho$ = air density ($\approx 1.225$ kg/m³ at sea level)
- $C_D$ = drag coefficient (cars: 0.25–0.35)
- $A_f$ = frontal area (m²)

**Step 1 — Equilibrium condition (zero acceleration):**
$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**
$$\dfrac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} = m g (f_0 + k V_{max}) + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V_{max}^2$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange to canonical quadratic form:**
$$\dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f \, V_{max}^2 + m g k \, V_{max} + m g f_0 - \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve the quadratic (positive root):**
$$V_{max} = \dfrac{-m g k + \sqrt{(m g k)^2 + 2 \rho C_D A_f \left(\dfrac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d} - m g f_0\right)}}{\rho C_D A_f}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Criterion:** The manufacturer-stated $V_{max}$ must be attained within **±2 km/h** of the calculated value during a **two-way average** test run on a **flat, dry, level track** per AIS/ECE procedures.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Sustained Gradeability Criterion

**Modified Force Balance on a Slope of Angle $\theta$:**

On an inclined road, an additional **grade force** acts on the vehicle:
$$F_g = m \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Updated equilibrium at sustained speed $V_s$ on grade $\theta$:**
$$F_t(V_s) = F_r(V_s) + F_{ad}(V_s) + F_g$$

**Step 1 — Substitute and isolate $\sin\theta$:**
$$\dfrac{T_e(V_s) i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d} = m g f_r \cos\theta + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V_s^2 + m g \sin\theta$$

> For moderate grades ($\theta \leq 20°$), $\cos\theta \approx 1$.

**Step 2 — Solve for $\sin\theta$:**
$$\sin\theta = \dfrac{1}{mg}\left[\dfrac{T_e i_g i_0 \eta_t}{r_d} - m g f_r - \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V_s^2\right]$$

**Step 3 — Convert to percentage:**
$$\text{Gradeability (\%)} = 100 \tan\theta \approx 100 \sin\theta \quad (\theta < 15°)$$

> **KTU Criterion:** A passenger car should achieve **sustained gradeability $\geq$ 30%** at $V_s = 60$ km/h.

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Matrix — Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem Statement (typical KTU numerical):**
> A passenger car has the following parameters:
> - Mass $m = 1500$ kg
> - Engine torque at peak $T_e = 200$ N·m
> - First gear ratio $i_g = 3.5$
> - Final drive ratio $i_0 = 3.9$
> - Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.9$
> - Tire radius $r_d = 0.32$ m
> - $C_D A_f = 0.7$ m²
> - $f_r = 0.013$
> - $\rho = 1.2$ kg/m³
> - $g = 9.81$ m/s²
>
> **Find:** (a) Maximum tractive force at peak torque, (b) Maximum gradeability at $V \to 0$, (c) Maximum speed on level road (assume $T_e \cdot \eta$ remains ~180 N·m at top gear $i_g = 0.8$).

### Solution:

**Part (a) — Tractive Force:**
$$F_t = \dfrac{T_e \cdot i_g \cdot i_0 \cdot \eta_t}{r_d}$$

$$F_t = \dfrac{200 \times 3.5 \times 3.9 \times 0.9}{0.32}$$

$$F_t = \dfrac{200 \times 3.5 \times 3.9 \times 0.9}{0.32} = \dfrac{2457}{0.32}$$

$$F_t \approx 7678 \text{ N}$$

**Part (b) — Maximum Gradeability (V → 0):**

At $V \to 0$, $F_r \to m g f_r$ and $F_{ad} \to 0$:

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{F_t - m g f_r}{mg}$$

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{7678 - (1500)(9.81)(0.013)}{(1500)(9.81)}$$

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{7678 - 191.3}{14715} = \dfrac{7486.7}{14715} \approx 0.5088$$

$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.5088) \approx 30.6°$$

$$\text{Grade}_{max} \approx 59\%$$

**Part (c) — Maximum Speed on Level Road:**

In top gear: $i_{g,top} = 0.8$, effective torque $\approx 180$ N·m.

$$F_t^{top} = \dfrac{180 \times 0.8 \times 3.9 \times 0.9}{0.32} = \dfrac{505.4}{0.32} \approx 1579 \text{ N}$$

Equilibrium: $F_t = m g f_r + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V_{max}^2$

$$1579 = 191.3 + \dfrac{1}{2}(1.2)(0.7) V_{max}^2$$

$$1387.7 = 0.42 \, V_{max}^2$$

$$V_{max}^2 = 3304 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{max} \approx 57.5 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} \approx 57.5 \text{ m/s} \approx 207 \text{ km/h}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** This $V_{max}$ of 207 km/h must be achievable within ±2 km/h tolerance during the official AIS-005 max-speed test for homologation.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Performance Criteria Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Vehicle Performance Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Powertrain Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Braking Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Gradeability Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Economy Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Handling Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Ride Comfort Criteria]
    nodeA --> nodeH[Emission Criteria]

    nodeB --> nodeB1[Max Speed]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[0 to 100 kmph Time]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Passing Ability]
    nodeB --> nodeB4[Power to Weight Ratio]

    nodeC --> nodeC1[Service Brake Decel]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Stopping Distance]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Fade Resistance]
    nodeC --> nodeC4[Parking Brake Grade]

    nodeD --> nodeD1[Max Grade]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Sustained Grade]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Instantaneous Grade]

    nodeE --> nodeE1[Fuel Economy kmpl]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Energy Efficiency]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Range per Charge]

    nodeF --> nodeF1[Steady State Cornering]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Transient Response]
    nodeF --> nodeF3[Lane Change]
    nodeF --> nodeF4[Understeer Gradient]

    nodeG --> nodeG1[Vibration Dose VDV]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[RMS Acceleration]
    nodeG --> nodeG3[Noise Level dBA]

    nodeH --> nodeH1[CO and HC Emission]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[NOx Emission]
    nodeH --> nodeH3[PM and PN]
    nodeH --> nodeH4[CO2 Fleet Average]
```

## 4.2 Vehicle Test-to-Criterion Compliance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Vehicle Under Test] --> stepB[Chassis Dyno or Track]
    stepB --> stepC[Sensor Data Acquisition]
    stepC --> stepD[Raw Performance Metrics]
    stepD --> stepE{Compare to Criterion}
    stepE -- Pass --> stepF[Record Compliance]
    stepE -- Fail --> stepG[Flag for Engineering Review]
    stepF --> stepH[Homologation Dossier]
    stepG --> stepI[Design Iteration]
    stepI --> stepB
```

## 4.3 Force Balance Block Diagram — Acceleration / Gradeability Test

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Engine Torque Te] --> B[Gearbox ig]
    B --> C[Final Drive i0]
    C --> D[Drivetrain eta_t]
    D --> E[Tractive Force Ft]
    E --> F{Force Comparison}
    F --> G[Rolling Fr]
    F --> H[Aero Drag Fad]
    F --> I[Grade Force Fg]
    F --> J[Inertia Force ma]
    G --> K[Net Acceleration]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Pass Fail Criterion]
```

## 4.4 ISO 2631 Ride Comfort Evaluation Block

```mermaid
graph TD
    rawA[Accelerometer Signals X Y Z] --> rawB[Frequency Weighting Wk Wd Wc]
    rawB --> rawC[RMS Calculation aw]
    rawC --> rawD{Compare to Criterion}
    rawD -- Less than 0.315 --> rawE[Not Uncomfortable PASS]
    rawD -- 0.315 to 0.63 --> rawF[A Little Uncomfortable PASS]
    rawD -- 0.63 to 1.0 --> rawG[Fairly Uncomfortable WARN]
    rawD -- Greater than 1.0 --> rawH[Very Uncomfortable FAIL]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3]
**Define "Performance Criterion" as applied to vehicle testing. List any four standard bodies whose regulations define these criteria.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Performance Criterion* is a quantitatively defined boundary value (or set of boundary conditions) for a vehicle attribute, prescribed by regulatory standards, manufacturer specifications, or customer expectations, used to *PASS* or *FAIL* a vehicle during standardized evaluation procedures.

**Four standard bodies regulating automotive performance criteria:**

1. **AIS** (Automotive Industry Standards, India — under CMVR)
2. **ECE** (Economic Commission for Europe — UN Regulations)
3. **SAE** (Society of Automotive Engineers — global de-facto standards)
4. **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization — global consensus)

**(Additional valid answers:** FMVSS, EPA, JIS, GB — any four.)

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 3]
**State the minimum service brake deceleration criterion for a passenger car (M1 category) as per AIS-052 / ECE R13. Also write the corresponding stopping distance limit from 100 km/h on a dry road.**

**Model Answer:**

As per **AIS-052 / ECE R13 (Service Brake Criterion)**:

- **Minimum service brake deceleration:** $a_{sb} \geq 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ (≈ **0.51 g**)
- **Maximum stopping distance from 100 km/h (≈ 27.78 m/s):** $d_{stop} \leq 70 \text{ m}$

**Quick verification:**
$$d_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 a_{sb}} = \dfrac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 5.0} \approx 77.2 \text{ m}$$

The 70 m limit accounts for reaction time and braking system lag, in addition to the pure-physics braking distance.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, Q4(a)+4(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain in detail the performance criteria used for evaluating vehicle acceleration. Derive the relationship between tractive force, resistance forces, and maximum speed of a vehicle on a level road.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition (2 marks):**
Acceleration performance criteria evaluate the vehicle's ability to increase velocity from rest (or a low speed) to a reference speed within a specified time. Standard KTU-referenced acceleration criteria include:
- 0–100 km/h time (or 0–60 mph time)
- 400 m standing-start time
- Passing maneuver (40–100 km/h) time
- Maximum speed attained
- Power-to-weight ratio (PWR ≥ 60 kW/tonne for typical mid-segment cars)

**Step 2 — Force Balance (3 marks):**
For acceleration on a level road, Newton's second law gives:
$$m a = F_t - F_r - F_{ad}$$

At maximum speed, $a = 0$:
$$F_t = F_r + F_{ad}$$

**Step 3 — Substituting the expressions (2 marks):**
$$\dfrac{T_e \, i_g \, i_0 \, \eta_t}{r_d} = m g (f_0 + k V_{max}) + \dfrac{1}{2} \rho C_D A_f V_{max}^2$$

Solving this quadratic yields the closed-form expression for $V_{max}$. The criterion is satisfied when measured $V_{max}$ falls within **±2 km/h** of the claimed value during AIS-005 homologation testing.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A vehicle has the following specifications:**
- Mass $m = 1200$ kg
- Maximum engine torque $T_{e,max} = 180$ N·m at 3500 rpm
- First gear ratio $i_{g,1} = 3.2$, Top gear $i_{g,top} = 0.85$
- Final drive $i_0 = 4.1$
- Drivetrain efficiency $\eta_t = 0.88$
- Dynamic tire radius $r_d = 0.30$ m
- $C_D A_f = 0.68$ m², $\rho = 1.225$ kg/m³, $f_r = 0.012$
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Calculate:**
1. Maximum tractive force in **first gear** (at peak torque).
2. Maximum theoretical gradeability (%) in first gear at very low speed.
3. Maximum speed on a level road in top gear (assume torque at $V_{max}$ drops to **75% of peak**, applied via top gear).

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Tractive Force in First Gear (2 marks):**
$$F_t = \dfrac{T_e \, i_g \, i_0 \, \eta_t}{r_d} = \dfrac{180 \times 3.2 \times 4.1 \times 0.88}{0.30}$$

$$F_t = \dfrac{2078.2}{0.30} \approx 6927.4 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Maximum Theoretical Gradeability (2 marks):**

At $V \to 0$: $F_r = mg f_r = 1200 \times 9.81 \times 0.012 \approx 141.3$ N

$$\sin\theta_{max} = \dfrac{F_t - F_r}{mg} = \dfrac{6927.4 - 141.3}{1200 \times 9.81} = \dfrac{6786.1}{11772} \approx 0.5765$$

$$\theta_{max} = \arcsin(0.5765) \approx 35.2°$$

$$\text{Grade}_{max} \approx 100 \tan(35.2°) \approx 70.6\%$$

**(iii) Maximum Speed on Level Road in Top Gear (3 marks):**

Effective torque in top gear at $V_{max}$: $T_{eff} = 0.75 \times 180 = 135$ N·m

$$F_t^{top} = \dfrac{135 \times 0.85 \times 4.1 \times 0.88}{0.30} = \dfrac{414.6}{0.30} \approx 1382 \text{ N}$$

Equilibrium: $F_t^{top} = F_r + F_{ad}$

$$1382 = 141.3 + \dfrac{1}{2} \times 1.225 \times 0.68 \times V_{max}^2$$

$$1240.7 = 0.4165 \, V_{max}^2$$

$$V_{max}^2 = 2978.6 \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{max} \approx 54.6 \text{ m/s}$$

$$\boxed{V_{max} \approx 196.4 \text{ km/h}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — December 2023, Module 3, Q5(a)+5(b)]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the braking performance criteria specified under AIS-052 / ECE R13 for a passenger car. Explain the concept of stopping distance, derive its expression, and compute the deceleration required to stop a vehicle from 100 km/h within 50 m.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Braking Criteria Overview (2 marks):**

AIS-052 / ECE R13 specifies three simultaneous braking criteria for $M_1$ category vehicles:

(i) **Service brake deceleration:** $\geq 5.0 \text{ m/s}^2$ on level road with no failure of brakes.
(ii) **Stopping distance:** $\leq 70$ m from 100 km/h (fade-tested condition).
(iii) **Parking brake holding grade:** $\geq 18\%$ on a level surface.

Additionally: brake-fade test (10 successive stops from 80% $V_{max}$ with engine disconnected), water-recovery test, and ABS performance test are mandated.

**Step 2 — Derivation of Stopping Distance (3 marks):**

Starting from $m \dfrac{dV}{dt} = -\mu m g$, separating variables and integrating:
$$V \, dV = -\mu g \, ds \quad \Rightarrow \quad \int_{V_0}^{0} V \, dV = -\mu g \int_0^{d_{stop}} ds$$

$$-\dfrac{V_0^2}{2} = -\mu g \, d_{stop} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{d_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

Given: $V_0 = 100$ km/h $= 27.78$ m/s, $d_{stop} \leq 50$ m.

Required deceleration:
$$a = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 d_{stop}} = \dfrac{(27.78)^2}{2 \times 50} = \dfrac{771.6}{100} \approx 7.72 \text{ m/s}^2$$

Equivalent friction coefficient:
$$\mu = \dfrac{a}{g} = \dfrac{7.72}{9.81} \approx 0.787$$

This represents a **high-performance braking** scenario (typical $\mu_{dry}$ for premium tires is 0.8–1.0).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the ISO 2631 ride-comfort performance criterion. With reference to VDV and RMS acceleration, describe the comfort zones and the threshold values.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Background (2 marks):**

ISO 2631-1 (Mechanical vibration and shock — Evaluation of human exposure to whole-body vibration) is the **de-facto international standard** for assessing ride comfort in road vehicles. It specifies two main comfort indicators:
- **$a_{w}$ (RMS frequency-weighted acceleration)** for continuous vibration.
- **VDV (Vibration Dose Value)** for transient / shock-type vibration.

**Step 2 — RMS Acceleration Criterion (2 marks):**

$$a_w = \left[\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T a_w^2(t) \, dt\right]^{1/2}$$

**Comfort zones (8-hour exposure):**
| $a_w$ Range (m/s²) | Comfort Level | Verdict |
|--------------------|----------------|---------|
| $< 0.315$ | Not uncomfortable | PASS |
| $0.315$ – $0.63$ | A little uncomfortable | PASS (marginal) |
| $0.63$ – $1.0$ | Fairly uncomfortable | FAIL (typical) |
| $\geq 1.0$ | Very uncomfortable | FAIL |

**Step 3 — Vibration Dose Value (3 marks):**

$$VDV = \left[\int_0^T a_w^4(t) \, dt\right]^{1/4}$$

- VDV is **fourth-power weighted** (more sensitive to peaks than RMS).
- It is the **preferred criterion** when the ride contains shocks (e.g., pothole strikes).
- **Acceptance criterion:** VDV $\leq 9.1$ m/s$^{1.75}$ for 8-hour exposure.
- **Health caution zone:** VDV in range **9.1 – 15** indicates **caution**; above 15 → likely **damage to health**.

**Step 4 — Frequency Weightings Applied (relevant for KTU):**
- $W_k$ — vertical seat surface (seat-back)
- $W_d$ — horizontal seat surface
- $W_c$ — vertical seat pan (most relevant for cars)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Performance Criteria Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up "gradeability" and "gradient":** Gradeability is the *capability of the vehicle*, not the road gradient. Always include vehicle parameters in the answer.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting unit conversion in stopping-distance problems:** When $V_0$ is given in km/h, students often forget to convert to m/s. Always convert $V_0$ (km/h) → $V_0/3.6$ (m/s) before applying $d = V^2/(2a)$.
> 
> 3. **Omitting drivetrain efficiency ($\eta_t$):** Tractive-force derivation MUST include $\eta_t$. Forgetting it costs **1–2 marks** consistently.
> 
> 4. **Confusing RMS and VDV:** ISO 2631 has TWO metrics — RMS is for continuous exposure, VDV is for shock/transient. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 
> 5. **Wrong sign in deceleration derivations:** When deriving stopping distance, students often write $a = +mg$ instead of $a = -\mu g$, missing the deceleration context.
> 
> 6. **Not specifying the test condition:** Performance criteria are valid ONLY under specified test conditions (dry road, level surface, $20°C$ ambient, standard tire pressure). Always state conditions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Performance Criteria"**

- [x] **Definition:** Performance criteria are *quantitative boundaries* for vehicle attributes used in pass/fail decisions.
- [x] **Six major categories:** Powertrain, Braking, Gradeability, Economy, Handling, Ride Comfort (+ Emissions).
- [x] **Stopping distance formula:** $d_{stop} = \dfrac{V_0^2}{2 \mu g}$ (memorize & derive).
- [x] **AIS-052 / ECE R13 service brake criterion:** $\geq 5$ m/s² for $M_1$ cars.
- [x] **Stopping distance limit from 100 km/h:** $\leq 70$ m.
- [x] **Parking brake grade:** $\geq 18\%$ (cars), $\geq 20\%$ (heavy vehicles).
- [x] **Sustained gradeability criterion:** $\geq 30\%$ for passenger cars at 60 km/h.
- [x] **Fuel economy metric:** Volume FE = $d/V_{fuel}$ in km/L (Indian MIDC cycle for AIS-053).
- [x] **Understeer gradient:** $K = W_f/C_{\alpha f} - W_r/C_{\alpha r}$; pass range $0 \leq K \leq 4°/g$.
- [x] **ISO 2631 ride comfort:** RMS $a_w \leq 0.5$ m/s² (8-hr); VDV $\leq 9.1$ m/s$^{1.75}$.
- [x] **Max speed formula:** Equilibrium $F_t = F_r + F_{ad}$; solve quadratic in $V$.
- [x] **Power-to-weight benchmark:** Cars $\geq 60$ kW/tonne; performance cars $\geq 100$ kW/tonne.
- [x] **Standard bodies:** AIS (India), ECE (UN/Europe), FMVSS (US), SAE (engineering), ISO (international).
- [x] **Always state test conditions:** dry road, level surface, $20°C$ ambient, standard tire pressure.
- [x] **Common pitfalls:** Unit conversion (km/h → m/s), sign of deceleration, drivetrain efficiency omission, RMS vs VDV confusion.
- [x] **Force balance template:** $F_t - F_r - F_{ad} - F_g = ma$ — universal equation for vehicle dynamics.
- [x] **Numerical tolerances:** $\pm 2$ km/h for $V_{max}$, $\pm 10\%$ for fuel economy.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Automotive Noise Criteria
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Automotive Noise Criteria

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Automotive Noise Criteria** refers to the standardized set of measurable parameters, permissible limits, and evaluation methodologies used to quantify, classify, and certify the acoustic emission characteristics of road vehicles and their components. These criteria are codified by international regulatory bodies such as **SAE (Society of Automotive Engineers)**, **ISO (International Organization for Standardization)**, **ECE (Economic Commission for Europe)**, **AIS (Automotive Industry Standards – India)**, and **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)**.

The criteria fundamentally characterize three primary acoustic domains:
1. **External (Pass-by) Noise** — Noise radiated by the vehicle into the environment, measured at a standardized distance (typically **7.5 m** from the centerline of the vehicle path under ECE R51 / ISO 362 protocols).
2. **Interior (In-Cabin) Noise** — Noise perceived by occupants, evaluated through Sound Pressure Level (SPL) measurements at the driver's ear position.
3. **Component Noise** — Noise from individual sub-systems (engine, transmission, exhaust, tires, HVAC, electrical motors).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acoustic Reference Pressure**: The threshold of human hearing is defined as $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$, which serves as the denominator in all dB-SPL calculations. This is the internationally standardized zero-reference for airborne sound.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Weight Scale" Analogy for Decibels**
> 
> Imagine noise as luggage being loaded onto a scale. A jet engine at **1 m** produces a sound pressure of about **200 Pa**, while the faintest whisper is just **0.00002 Pa**. If we used the raw Pascal scale, we would need a scale with **10 million divisions** to read both extremes — practically useless for engineering.
> 
> **The decibel (dB) compresses this enormous range** by taking the **logarithm (base 10)** of the ratio between the measured sound pressure and the reference pressure. It's like switching from a linear ruler to a logarithmic slide rule — the human ear naturally responds to stimuli in a logarithmic manner (Weber-Fechner Law), so dB units mirror human perception. **A 10 dB increase corresponds to a perceived doubling of loudness**, while a **3 dB increase corresponds to a doubling of acoustic energy**.

### 1.3 Core Acoustic Metrics at a Glance

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Equation | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | $20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | Direct SPL measurement |
| Sound Power Level | $L_W$ | $10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Source strength independent of distance |
| Sound Intensity Level | $L_I$ | $10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Directional energy flux |
| A-Weighted SPL | $L_{pA}$ | $L_p + \Delta f_A$ | Mimics human hearing response |
| Equivalent Continuous SPL | $L_{eq}$ | $10 \log_{10}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T (p^2(t)/p_0^2)\,dt\right]$ | Time-averaged exposure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reference Power**: $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$
> **Reference Intensity**: $I_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W/m}^2$
> **Speed of Sound in Air (20°C)**: $c \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$
> **Density of Air (20°C)**: $\rho_0 \approx 1.204 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Decibel System — Origin and Logic

The decibel (dB) is **one-tenth of a bel**, named in honor of **Alexander Graham Bell**. It is fundamentally a **ratio quantity**, not an absolute physical unit. The conversion from linear acoustic pressure to logarithmic dB-SPL follows a deterministic mathematical transformation:

**Step 1 — Form the Pressure Ratio**: The instantaneous acoustic pressure $p(t)$ is non-dimensionalized against the reference $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Base-10 Logarithm**: Because acoustic pressure spans **6–8 orders of magnitude**, the log transformation compresses the dynamic range into manageable integers.

**Step 3 — Multiply by 20**: This scaling factor arises from the fact that **acoustic intensity is proportional to the square of pressure** ($I \propto p^2$). Therefore:
$$\log_{10}(p^2/p_0^2) = 2 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$$

The factor 20 (rather than 10) ensures equivalence between pressure-based and intensity-based dB scales.

### 2.2 Frequency Weighting Networks — A, B, C, D, Z

The human ear is **not equally sensitive to all frequencies**. Studies by Fletcher and Munson (1933) established the **Equal Loudness Contours**, which led to standardized electronic weighting filters:

| Weighting | Application | Response Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **A-Weighting (dBA)** | General environmental noise, vehicle pass-by, occupational exposure | Heavily attenuates low frequencies (<1 kHz) and very high frequencies; correlates with perceived loudness at moderate levels |
| **B-Weighting (dBB)** | Largely obsolete; intermediate-level noise | Intermediate between A and C |
| **C-Weighting (dBC)** | Peak/impulse noise, high-level industrial noise | Nearly flat response from ~31.5 Hz to 8 kHz |
| **D-Weighting (dBD)** | Aircraft flyover noise certification | Specifically tuned to perceived aircraft noise spectra |
| **Z-Weighting (Linear)** | Scientific measurement; no weighting | Flat 0 dB at all frequencies (1 Hz to 20 kHz) |

> [!NOTE]
> **A-Weighting Correction Values ($\Delta f_A$) at 1 kHz** are normalized to **0 dB**. At **63 Hz**, the correction is approximately **−26.2 dB**, and at **8 kHz**, it is approximately **+1.1 dB**. These are tabulated in IEC 61672-1 and must be applied to broadband SPL readings for regulatory compliance.

### 2.3 Octave and Third-Octave Band Analysis

Real automotive noise is **broadband**, containing simultaneous energy across many frequencies. The **Octave Band** concept decomposes the spectrum into discrete frequency intervals using logarithmic centers.

The lower band edge $f_1$ and upper band edge $f_2$ of the $n$-th octave band are related by a constant ratio:
$$f_2 / f_1 = 2$$

For **third-octave bands**, the ratio becomes $2^{1/3} \approx 1.2599$.

**Preferred Center Frequencies (ISO / IEC)** follow the geometric series:
$$f_c(n) = 1000 \cdot 2^{n/3} \, \text{Hz}$$

This yields the standard set: **31.5, 63, 125, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 4000, 8000, 16000 Hz** (each value is $2^{1/3}$ times the previous).

### 2.4 Sound Pressure vs. Sound Power vs. Sound Intensity

These three quantities are often confused, but they measure fundamentally different physical properties:

- **Sound Pressure ($p$, Pa)**: Local time-varying pressure fluctuation. Depends on source, distance, environment, and reflections. Measured by a single microphone.
- **Sound Intensity ($\vec{I}$, W/m²)**: Vector quantity representing the time-averaged acoustic energy flux through a unit area in a specific direction. Requires a **sound intensity probe** (two-microphone arrangement, often p-p probe).
- **Sound Power ($W$, W)**: Scalar total acoustic energy emitted by a source per unit time. **Independent of distance and environment** — it is an intrinsic property of the source.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB (re 20 µPa) |
| 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level | dB (re 10⁻¹² W) |
| 3 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Sound Intensity Level | dB (re 10⁻¹² W/m²) |
| 4 | $L_{pA} = L_p + \Delta f_A(f)$ | A-Weighted SPL | dBA |
| 5 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{T}\!\int_0^T (p^2(t)/p_0^2)\,dt\right]$ | Equivalent Continuous SPL | dB |
| 6 | $L_n = L_{eq} + 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | Noise Level (N vehicles/hr) | dBA |
| 7 | $f_c(n) = 1000 \cdot 2^{n/3}$ | Octave/Third-Octave Center Frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $I = p^2/(\rho c)$ | Acoustic Intensity (plane wave) | W/m² |
| 9 | $L_{W} = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S/S_0)$ | Power-Pressure relation (reverberant) | dB |
| 10 | $\Delta L_{p} = 20 \log_{10}(r_1/r_2)$ | Inverse-Square Law (free field) | dB |
| 11 | $L_{p,\text{sum}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\sum_i 10^{L_{p,i}/10}$ | Addition of incoherent sources | dB |
| 12 | $L_{p,\text{sum}} = L_p + 3$ | Doubling of identical incoherent sources | dB |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Utility**: Automotive noise criteria form the legal backbone for vehicle type-approval (homologation). They govern market access in the EU (UNECE Regulations 51, 117), India (AIS-018, AIS-019, CMVR), USA (FMVSS 141, EPA 40 CFR §205), and Japan. Manufacturers must demonstrate compliance through certified chassis dynamometer and pass-by tests.

### 2.6 Acoustic Power and Free-Field Propagation

In a **free field** (no reflections), a point source radiates spherically. The relationship between Sound Power Level and Sound Pressure Level at distance $r$ is:
$$L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11 \, \text{dB (for spherical wave)}$$

For a **hemispherical wave** (source on a reflecting ground plane, as in real vehicle pass-by tests):
$$L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8 \, \text{dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 7.5 m?** The ECE R51 measurement distance of 7.5 m is a regulatory compromise between practical microphone placement and a distance large enough to be in the acoustic far-field of the vehicle (which is a complex multi-source geometry typically 3–5 m long).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Acoustic Pressure to dB-SPL

**Given**: A microphone measures an RMS acoustic pressure $p_{\text{rms}} = 0.632 \, \text{Pa}$ (a moderately loud sound, roughly equivalent to a busy street).

**Step 1**: Identify the reference pressure.
$$p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2**: Form the dimensionless pressure ratio.
$$\frac{p}{p_0} = \frac{0.632}{2 \times 10^{-5}} = 31600$$

**Step 3**: Apply the base-10 logarithm.
$$\log_{10}(31600) = \log_{10}(3.16 \times 10^4) = \log_{10}(3.16) + 4 = 0.4997 + 4 = 4.4997$$

**Step 4**: Multiply by 20 (pressure-squared equivalence).
$$L_p = 20 \times 4.4997 = 89.99 \approx 90 \, \text{dB}$$

**Result**: $L_p \approx 90 \, \text{dB SPL}$, consistent with a busy traffic environment.

### 3.2 Derivation: Inverse-Square Law and Distance Correction

**Given**: A motorcycle's exhaust produces $L_{p1} = 100 \, \text{dB}$ at $r_1 = 1 \, \text{m}$. Find the SPL at $r_2 = 7.5 \, \text{m}$ (ECE R51 measurement distance).

**Step 1**: Apply the inverse-square law for free-field spherical spreading.
$$\Delta L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$

**Step 2**: Substitute numerical values.
$$\Delta L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{7.5}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(0.1333)$$

**Step 3**: Evaluate the logarithm.
$$\log_{10}(0.1333) = -0.8751$$

**Step 4**: Multiply by 20.
$$\Delta L_p = 20 \times (-0.8751) = -17.5 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 5**: Add to the source SPL.
$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} + \Delta L_p = 100 - 17.5 = 82.5 \, \text{dB}$$

**Result**: At the regulatory pass-by microphone position, the SPL drops to **82.5 dB**.

### 3.3 Derivation: Addition of Incoherent Noise Sources

**Given**: A vehicle has three uncorrelated noise contributors measured at a single point:
- Engine: $L_{p1} = 80 \, \text{dB}$
- Tire/road: $L_{p2} = 78 \, \text{dB}$
- Wind: $L_{p3} = 75 \, \text{dB}$

**Step 1**: Convert each dB value to linear acoustic power ratio.
$$10^{L_{p1}/10} = 10^{80/10} = 10^{8} = 1.0 \times 10^{8}$$
$$10^{L_{p2}/10} = 10^{78/10} = 10^{7.8} = 6.31 \times 10^{7}$$
$$10^{L_{p3}/10} = 10^{75/10} = 10^{7.5} = 3.16 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 2**: Sum the linear power ratios.
$$\sum = 1.00 \times 10^{8} + 0.631 \times 10^{8} + 0.316 \times 10^{8} = 1.947 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3**: Convert back to dB.
$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(1.947 \times 10^{8}) = 10 \times (8 + \log_{10}(1.947))$$
$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \times (8 + 0.2893) = 10 \times 8.2893 = 82.89 \, \text{dB}$$

**Result**: Combined SPL $\approx 82.9$ dB. Note that the dominant engine noise contributes most of the energy; the 75 dB wind noise adds only ~0.7 dB to the total.

### 3.4 Derivation: Octave Band Center Frequencies

**Given**: Find the center frequencies of the first three third-octave bands above 1 kHz (i.e., $n = 1, 2, 3$).

**Step 1**: Apply the center-frequency formula.
$$f_c(n) = 1000 \cdot 2^{n/3} \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 2**: For $n = 1$:
$$f_c(1) = 1000 \cdot 2^{1/3} = 1000 \times 1.2599 = 1259.9 \approx 1250 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 3**: For $n = 2$:
$$f_c(2) = 1000 \cdot 2^{2/3} = 1000 \times 1.5874 = 1587.4 \approx 1600 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Step 4**: For $n = 3$:
$$f_c(3) = 1000 \cdot 2^{3/3} = 1000 \times 2.0 = 2000 \, \text{Hz}$$

**Result**: 1.25 kHz, 1.6 kHz, 2 kHz — matching the ISO preferred-frequency table.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Which Formula Applies When?

| Measurement Scenario | Applicable Formula | Key Caveat |
|---|---|---|
| Single microphone, free field | $L_p = 20\log(p/p_0)$ | Requires far-field assumption |
| Microphone at ground, hemispherical spread | $L_p = L_W - 20\log r - 8$ | Ground reflection doubles power |
| Indoor reverberant room | $L_p = L_W + 10\log(S/S_0)$ | Uses room constant method |
| Source-to-source distance correction | $\Delta L = 20\log(r_1/r_2)$ | Free-field only |
| Two incoherent sources combined | $L_{\text{sum}} = 10\log(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ | **Never** use arithmetic dB addition! |
| A-weighted measurement | $L_{pA} = L_p + \Delta f_A$ | Tabulated at each 1/3-octave center |
| Time-varying event | $L_{eq} = 10\log[(1/T)\int(p^2/p_0^2)\,dt]$ | Energy-equivalent metric |
| Traffic flow $N$ vehicles/hour | $L_n = L_{eq} + 10\log N$ | Assumes uniform source distribution |

### 3.6 Worked Example: Interior Cabin Noise Calculation

**Problem**: A passenger car's cabin is treated with sound-absorbing material that provides a Transmission Loss of **25 dB** at **1000 Hz**. The exterior SPL at this frequency is **85 dB**. The cabin volume is **$V = 2.8 \, \text{m}^3$**, and the cabin absorption area is $A = 4.5 \, \text{m}^2$ (sabin).

**Step 1**: Compute the interior SPL ignoring cabin effects.
$$L_{p,\text{int}} = L_{p,\text{ext}} - TL = 85 - 25 = 60 \, \text{dB}$$

**Step 2**: Compute the steady-state buildup using the Sabine relation (reverberant field).
$$L_{p,\text{reverb}} = L_W - 10\log(A/4)$$

**Step 3**: The total interior SPL combines direct and reverberant components.
$$L_{p,\text{total}}^2 \approx L_{p,\text{direct}}^2 + L_{p,\text{reverb}}^2$$

For this configuration, with proper volume-to-absorption ratio, the steady-state interior SPL settles at approximately **58–62 dB at the driver's ear**, which is consistent with luxury sedan benchmarks (typically 55–65 dB at 100 km/h cruise).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Automotive Noise Measurement Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Vehicle Under Test] --> B[Microphone Array Setup]
    B --> C[Sound Level Meter SLM]
    B --> D[Spectrum Analyzer FFT]
    B --> E[Sound Intensity Probe]
    C --> F[Data Acquisition System DAQ]
    D --> F
    E --> F
    F --> G[Weighting Filter Module]
    G --> H1[Output A-Weighted dBA]
    G --> H2[Output C-Weighted dBC]
    G --> H3[Output Z-Weighted Linear]
    F --> I[Octave Band Processor]
    I --> J[1/1 Octave Display]
    I --> K[1/3 Octave Display]
    F --> L[Time Analysis Module]
    L --> M[Leq Calculation]
    L --> N[Statistical LN Values]
    L --> O[Lmax and Lmin]
    F --> P[Post Processing Computer]
    P --> Q[Compliance Report ECE R51]
    P --> R[Compliance Report AIS 018]
    P --> S[NVH Optimization Database]
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: Vehicle Pass-By Noise Test Procedure (ECE R51)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Start Test Setup] --> node2[Verify Track ISO 10844]
    node2 --> node3[Calibrate Microphones]
    node3 --> node4[Position Microphones 7.5m from Path]
    node4 --> node5[Define PP Measurement Line]
    node5 --> node6[Run Vehicle at Target Gear]
    node6 --> node7[Accelerate Wide Open Throttle WOT]
    node7 --> node8[Capture Lmax at PP Line]
    node8 --> node9[Record Left and Right Side SPL]
    node9 --> node10[Average Both Microphones]
    node10 --> node11[Apply Speed Correction]
    node11 --> node12{Check Compliance with Limit}
    node12 -->|Pass| node13[Issue Type Approval]
    node12 -->|Fail| node14[Redesign or Retest]
    node14 --> node6
    node13 --> node15[End]
```

### 4.3 Hierarchy Diagram: Noise Source Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Automotive Noise Sources] --> nodeB[Powertrain Noise]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Tire Road Noise]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Aerodynamic Noise]
    nodeA --> nodeE[Ancillary Systems]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Engine Combustion]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Exhaust System]
    nodeB --> nodeB3[Transmission]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Tire Tread Impact]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Air Pumping]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Road Surface Texture]
    nodeD --> nodeD1[Wind Buffeting]
    nodeD --> nodeD2[Body Aeroacoustic]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Mirror Antenna Wake]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[HVAC Blower]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Electric Motors HV]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Brake Squeal]
```

### 4.4 Process Flow: Sound Level Meter Signal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Acoustic Pressure Wave] --> node2[Microphone Capsule 1/2 inch]
    node2 --> node3[Pre Amplifier]
    node3 --> node4[Frequency Weighting Network A B C Z]
    node4 --> node5[RMS Detector Time Weighting]
    node5 --> node6[Analog to Digital Conversion]
    node6 --> node7[Microprocessor for Leq Statistics]
    node7 --> node8[LCD Display dB SPL]
    node7 --> node9[Memory and Data Logging]
    node7 --> node10[USB and Output Interface]
```

### 4.5 Comparison Block: A, C, and Z Weighting Responses

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Input Broadband Signal] --> nodeB[Apply A Weighting]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Apply C Weighting]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Apply Z Linear Weighting]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Low Frequency Cutoff Emphasis on 1 to 5 kHz]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Nearly Flat Response Midband]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Completely Flat 0 dB Reference]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Output dBA Reading]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Output dBC Reading]
    nodeG --> nodeJ[Output dBZ or Linear Reading]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: Each block diagram models a regulatory or measurement workflow. The first maps the complete instrumentation chain. The second encodes the ECE R51 pass-by test sequence, which is the most commonly tested procedure in KTU university examinations for the subject PEMUT633.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

> Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL). A microphone measures an RMS acoustic pressure of **0.2 Pa**. Calculate the SPL in dB (reference $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$).

**Model Answer**:

Sound Pressure Level is the logarithmic measure of the RMS acoustic pressure of a sound wave, expressed in decibels relative to a standardized reference pressure of $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

**Solution:**

The SPL equation is:
$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)$$

Substituting the values:
$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{0.2}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(10000)$$

$$L_p = 20 \times 4 = 80 \, \text{dB SPL}$$

**Answer: $L_p = 80 \, \text{dB SPL}$** — a typical level for a noisy office or alarm clock at close range.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**

> Explain the significance of **A-weighting** in automotive noise measurement. Why is it preferred over linear (Z) weighting for pass-by noise certification?

**Model Answer**:

A-weighting is a frequency-filtering scheme that de-emphasizes very low and very high frequencies to which the human ear is relatively insensitive, while preserving the **1 kHz to 5 kHz** band where hearing acuity is maximum. The filter response approximates the **40-phon equal-loudness contour** of Fletcher-Munson.

**Significance in automotive testing:**

1. **Perceptual relevance**: A-weighted SPL (dBA) correlates closely with the **subjective loudness** experienced by humans, making it the regulatory metric of choice for community noise.
2. **Regulatory mandate**: ECE R51, AIS-018, ISO 362, OSHA, and EPA all specify dBA for pass-by and occupational noise compliance.
3. **Engineered filtering**: At low frequencies (e.g., 31.5 Hz), A-weighting applies approximately **−39 dB** attenuation, automatically discounting the high-amplitude infrasonic content from engine and tire sources that does not contribute to perceived loudness.
4. **Linear weighting (Z)** is reserved for **scientific measurements** where the full acoustic spectrum must be preserved without subjective bias.

### 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *Comprehensive Pass-By Noise Problem*

**Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (7 Marks)**

> A passenger car is tested for external pass-by noise on an ISO 10844 track. The measured maximum A-weighted SPL is **$L_{pA} = 74 \, \text{dBA}$** at the 7.5 m microphone. The car's test reference speed is **$v_{\text{ref}} = 50 \, \text{km/h}$**, but it actually crosses the PP line at **$v_{\text{act}} = 48 \, \text{km/h}$**. Apply the **ECE R51 speed correction** of $\Delta L = 1.5 (v_{\text{ref}} - v_{\text{act}})$, compute the corrected SPL, and check compliance with the regulatory limit of **74 dBA** for category M1 vehicles.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1**: Identify the correction formula.
$$\Delta L_p = 1.5 \times (v_{\text{ref}} - v_{\text{act}}) \, \text{dB(A)}$$

**Step 2**: Substitute the speed values.
$$\Delta L_p = 1.5 \times (50 - 48) = 1.5 \times 2 = 3.0 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

**Step 3**: Apply the correction to the measured value.
$$L_{pA,\text{corrected}} = 74 + 3 = 77 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

**Step 4**: Compare with the regulatory limit.
$$L_{pA,\text{corrected}} = 77 \, \text{dB(A)} > L_{\text{limit}} = 74 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

**Result**: The vehicle **FAILS** the ECE R51 pass-by noise test by **3 dB(A)**.

**Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] (7 Marks)**

> With the aid of a block diagram, explain the **instrumentation chain** used in a precision Sound Level Meter (SLM) for automotive pass-by noise testing. What is the role of frequency weighting in this chain?

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**

```
Microphone → Pre-amplifier → Frequency Weighting Network
→ RMS Detector (with time weighting F/S/I) → ADC
→ Microprocessor (Leq, LN statistics) → Display
```

**Functional Explanation of Each Block:**

1. **Microphone (1/2-inch free-field type)**: Converts acoustic pressure fluctuations into proportional electrical voltage. Typical sensitivity is **50 mV/Pa**.

2. **Pre-amplifier**: Provides impedance matching and initial signal conditioning. Modern SLMs include ICP/IEPE constant-current power supply.

3. **Frequency Weighting Network**: Implements A, C, or Z weighting per IEC 61672-1. The A-weighting filter, for example, applies frequency-dependent attenuation according to a standardized curve.

4. **RMS Detector with Time Weighting**: Converts the AC signal to a DC equivalent using Fast (F, 125 ms time constant), Slow (S, 1 s), or Impulse (I) characteristics.

5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)**: Typically 16- to 24-bit, sampling at 48 kHz or higher to capture full 20 kHz bandwidth.

6. **Microprocessor**: Computes Leq, Lmax, Lmin, L10, L50, L90, and other statistical descriptors in real time.

7. **Display**: Shows numerical SPL in dB and graphical bar/line plots.

**Role of Frequency Weighting**: It shapes the measured signal to match human auditory perception. For regulatory automotive tests (ECE R51, AIS-018), the **A-weighting** is mandatory because it correlates with subjective loudness. Without weighting, the meter would report raw acoustic energy, including sub-audible and ultra-sonic components that do not contribute to perceived vehicle noise nuisance.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** — *Interior Noise & Octave Band Analysis*

**Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] (7 Marks)**

> A vehicle's interior cabin is exposed to three uncorrelated noise sources at 1000 Hz: HVAC blower (65 dB), road-tire interaction (70 dB), and powertrain (60 dB). Calculate the **combined interior SPL** in dB.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1**: Convert each dB value to linear acoustic power ratio.
$$I_1 = 10^{65/10} = 10^{6.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{6}$$
$$I_2 = 10^{70/10} = 10^{7.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{7}$$
$$I_3 = 10^{60/10} = 10^{6.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 2**: Sum the linear power ratios.
$$I_{\text{total}} = 3.162 \times 10^{6} + 10.00 \times 10^{6} + 1.00 \times 10^{6} = 14.16 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3**: Convert the total back to dB.
$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(14.16 \times 10^{6}) = 10 \log_{10}(1.416 \times 10^{7})$$
$$L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \times (7 + 0.1511) = 71.51 \, \text{dB}$$

**Result**: Combined interior SPL $\approx 71.5 \, \text{dB}$ at 1000 Hz.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistake**: Students frequently perform **arithmetic dB addition** (e.g., 65 + 70 + 60 = 195 dB), which is **completely wrong**. Decibels are logarithmic; the correct approach is to **convert to linear power ratios, sum them, then convert back**. Full 2 marks are deducted for this conceptual error.

**Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model] (7 Marks)**

> Discuss the importance of **octave and third-octave band analysis** in automotive NVH engineering. How does it differ from broadband A-weighted SPL?

**Model Solution:**

**Octave Band Analysis — Concept:**

Octave band analysis decomposes a complex broadband noise signal into discrete frequency intervals, each one octave wide. The lower and upper frequencies of each band satisfy $f_2 = 2 f_1$. **Third-octave bands** subdivide each octave into three equal logarithmic intervals, giving the standard preferred center frequencies from 31.5 Hz to 16 kHz.

**Importance in Automotive NVH:**

1. **Source Identification**: Specific vehicle noise signatures reside in predictable bands:
   - Engine firing orders: 31.5 Hz to 250 Hz
   - Tire cavity resonance: 200 Hz to 300 Hz
   - Road surface texture: 1000 Hz to 5000 Hz
   - Wind buffeting: 500 Hz to 2000 Hz

2. **Targeted Mitigation**: Knowing the dominant frequency band allows engineers to design **tuned absorbers, dampers, or barriers**. For example, a 250 Hz peak from the exhaust can be addressed with a Helmholtz resonator tuned to that frequency.

3. **Regulatory Compliance**: Some standards (e.g., ISO 5131 for tractor noise) require octave-band reporting, not just broadband A-weighted SPL.

4. **Subjective Correlation**: Loudness and annoyance depend on spectral distribution, not total energy. Two vehicles with identical A-weighted SPL can have vastly different perceived acoustic quality.

**Difference from Broadband A-Weighting:**

| Parameter | Broadband dBA | Octave Band Analysis |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Single number | Spectrum (multiple bands) |
| Frequency resolution | None | 1/1 or 1/3 octave |
| Use | Compliance testing, surveys | Engineering diagnosis, design |
| A-weighting | Applied to total SPL | Applied to each band individually |
| Information content | Low | High |

**Conclusion**: A-weighted broadband SPL is suitable for **regulatory pass/fail decisions**, while octave/third-octave analysis is indispensable for **engineering design, root-cause diagnosis, and product refinement** in the automotive NVH domain.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Many students confuse the **octave band equation** $f_2/f_1 = 2$ with a constant absolute frequency difference. Always remember — **octave bands are logarithmic**, not linear. Marks are awarded specifically for stating that the **band ratio** equals 2 and for using $f_c = 1000 \cdot 2^{n/3}$ for preferred third-octave centers.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Automotive Noise Criteria**

- **Reference pressure** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$; **reference power** $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W; **reference intensity** $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **SPL equation**: $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ dB; **SWL**: $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ dB.
- **Speed of sound** at 20°C: $c = 343$ m/s; **air density**: $\rho_0 = 1.204$ kg/m³.
- **A-weighting** mimics 40-phon Fletcher-Munson contour; mandatory for **ECE R51, AIS-018, ISO 362, OSHA, EPA** compliance.
- **C-weighting** ≈ flat response, used for peak/impulse noise. **Z-weighting** is linear (0 dB at all frequencies).
- **Inverse-square law**: Doubling distance → **−6 dB**; 10× distance → **−20 dB** (free field).
- **Doubling identical incoherent sources** → +3 dB; **10 identical sources** → +10 dB.
- **Octave band**: $f_2 = 2 f_1$; **Third-octave center**: $f_c = 1000 \cdot 2^{n/3}$ Hz.
- **ECE R51 pass-by** uses **7.5 m** microphone on **ISO 10844** track surface.
- **Speed correction** (ECE R51): $\Delta L_p = 1.5 \times (v_{\text{ref}} - v_{\text{act}})$.
- **Indian AIS-018** mandates **74 dBA** for M1 category passenger cars (post-2016 norms).
- **Adding dBs**: NEVER use arithmetic addition — always convert to linear, sum, then convert back.
- **In-cabin noise target** for premium sedans: 55–65 dB at 100 km/h; economy cars: 65–72 dB.
- **Leq (Equivalent Continuous SPL)** is the **energy-averaged** SPL over a time interval $T$.
- **Statistical descriptors** $L_{10}$, $L_{50}$, $L_{90}$ represent the SPL exceeded 10%, 50%, 90% of the time.
- **Measurement distance corrections** assume far-field; near-field effects dominate within $\lambda/2\pi$ of the source.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Examination Tip**: For problems worth 7 marks or more, always include the **formula**, **substitution step with units**, **logarithm evaluation**, and a **clear conclusion sentence**. Examiners in KTU award partial marks liberally when the methodology is correct even if the final answer has an arithmetic slip.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Standard Noise Measurement Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Standard Noise Measurement Methods # MODULE 4: STANDARD NOISE MEASUREMENT METHODS ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Standard Noise Measurement Methods** refer to a rigorously codified set of internationally harmonized procedures, instrumentation specifications, environmental conditions, and data acquisition protocols used to quantify the acoustic emissions generated by road vehicles. These methods are issued by standardization bodies such as the **International Organization for Standardization (ISO)**, the **Society of Automotive Engineers (SAE)**, the **Economic Commission for Europe (ECE)** under **UN Regulation No. 51**, and the **Society of Indian Automobile Manufacturers (SIAM)** under **AIS-018** for the Indian market. For a B.Tech student of Vehicle Performance and Testing, the topic deals with two principal classes of measurement: 1. **Stationary Noise Measurement (ISO 5130 / SAE J1169):** Noise emitted by a vehicle under static, idling, or controlled steady-state RPM conditions. 2. **Dynamic Pass-by Noise Measurement (ISO 362-1 / ECE R51):** Noise emitted by a vehicle under dynamic acceleration and cruise conditions on a standardized test track. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PEMUT633):** The module emphasizes **microphone placement, weighting networks (A-weighting), instrument classification (IEC 61672 Class 1 vs Class 2), test track geometry, background noise correction (K1 and K2), and the mathematical conversion of sound pressure to sound power level.** ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy Imagine you are sitting in a quiet library and a friend whispers to you. The whisper is barely audible. Now, the same friend whispers in a crowded railway station — you cannot hear them at all. The "measurement" of the whisper hasn't changed, but the environment has. This is exactly why **standard noise measurement methods exist**: they prescribe the *exact* microphone distance, *exact* test track, *exact* background noise limits, and *exact* instrument class — so that whether you measure a car in Chennai or in Stuttgart, the **dB(A) value is comparable, repeatable, and legally defensible**. Think of the standard method as a **"photography recipe"** for sound: just as a portrait photographer fixes the lighting, lens aperture, distance to subject, and background, a noise standard fixes the **microphone height (1.2 m), microphone distance (7.5 m from the line of travel), test surface (ISO 10844), and atmospheric conditions (temperature 5–40 °C, wind < 5 m/s)**. ### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Unit | |---|---|---| | Reference Sound Pressure ($p_0$) | **20 µPa** | Pascal | | Reference Sound Power ($W_0$) | **10⁻¹²** | Watt | | Microphone Height (Pass-by) | **1.2 ± 0.05** | m | | Microphone Distance (Pass-by) | **7.5 ± 0.05** | m | | Engine Speed Range (ECE R51) | 50% of Rated RPM to 75% | RPM | | Test Track Surface | ISO 10844 | — | | Wind Speed Limit | < **5** | m/s | > [!NOTE] > The reference pressure of **20 µPa** corresponds to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz. Every decibel value reported in vehicle noise testing is referenced to this value. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Sound Pressure Level (SPL) The fundamental quantity measured by any sound level meter is the **Root Mean Square (RMS) sound pressure**, denoted as $p_{rms}$. The **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** is a logarithmic measure defined as: $$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ where $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ is the **reference sound pressure**. **Why a logarithm?** Human hearing spans a range of pressures from **20 µPa (threshold of hearing)** to **~200 Pa (threshold of pain)** — a ratio of **10⁷ : 1**. A linear scale is impractical. The decibel compresses this enormous range into a manageable 0–140 dB scale. Furthermore, human perception of loudness is approximately logarithmic (a **Weber-Fechner law** analog). ### 2.2 A-Weighting Network The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. It is most sensitive around **1 kHz–4 kHz** and less sensitive at very low and very high frequencies. To approximate the ear's response, noise meters apply a **frequency weighting filter**. The **A-weighting network** is the international standard for vehicle noise certification. The A-weighted SPL is denoted $L_{pA}$ or simply **dB(A)**. **Frequency response characteristics of A-weighting:** | Frequency (Hz) | A-Weighting Correction (dB) | |---|---| | 31.5 | −39.4 | | 63 | −26.2 | | 125 | −16.1 | | 250 | −8.6 | | 500 | −3.2 | | 1000 | 0.0 (Reference) | | 2000 | +1.2 | | 4000 | +1.0 | | 8000 | −1.1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Concept:** Regulatory limits (e.g., **74 dB(A)** for passenger cars under ECE R51) are **always quoted in dB(A)**. Reporting an unweighted dB value in an answer will be marked wrong by the examiner. ### 2.3 Sound Power Level vs. Sound Pressure Level A critical conceptual distinction in noise measurement: - **Sound Pressure Level (SPL or $L_p$):** A *local* field quantity measured at a point using a microphone. Depends on distance, environment, and geometry. - **Sound Power Level (SWL or $L_W$):** A *total* source quantity that describes the acoustic energy radiated by the source per unit time. **Independent of distance and environment.** The relationship for a source in a free field over a reflecting plane is: $$L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{S}{S_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]}$$ where: - $S$ = measurement surface area (m²) - $S_0$ = reference area = **1 m²** For a hemispherical free field, $S = 2\pi r^2$, giving: $$L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 8 \quad \text{[dB]}$$ ### 2.4 Background Noise Correction (K1 and K2) A real test site is never perfectly silent. The procedure mandates correction for background noise: | Correction | Formula | Application | |---|---|---| | **K1** (Background correction) | $K_1 = -10 \log_{10}\left(1 - 10^{-0.1 \Delta L}\right)$ | Where $\Delta L = L_{signal} - L_{background}$ | | **K2** (Environmental correction) | $K_2$ from ISO 3744 / 3746 | For semi-reverberant environments | **Rule of thumb:** The background noise should be at least **10 dB(A)** below the vehicle noise. If it is only 3 dB(A) lower, the measurement is invalid. If it is exactly 10 dB(A) lower, apply $K_1 = -0.5$ dB. If the difference is 6 dB, $K_1 = -1.3$ dB. ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Meaning | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | dB | | 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level ($W_0=10^{-12}$ W) | dB | | 3 | $L_{pA} = L_p + \Delta L_A(f)$ | A-Weighted SPL | dB(A) | | 4 | $L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S/S_0)$ | Power from Pressure (Hemispherical) | dB | | 5 | $L_{eq,T} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_p(t)/10}\, dt\right]$ | Equivalent Continuous SPL | dB | | 6 | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Addition of uncorrelated noise sources | dB | | 7 | $K_1 = -10 \log_{10}\left(1 - 10^{-0.1\Delta L}\right)$ | Background noise correction | dB | | 8 | $\Delta L = 6 - 0.0043\,(h-650)$ | Atmospheric absorption correction (K2 component) | dB | | 9 | $L_{N,NN} = L_{eq}$ | Reported pass-by result at $N_N$ reference speed | dB(A) | ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility Standard noise measurement methods are the **legal backbone** of vehicle type-approval worldwide. A manufacturer cannot sell a vehicle in the EU, India, Japan, or the US without demonstrating compliance with these standards. Furthermore, these methods are the foundation for: - **NVH (Noise, Vibration, Harshness) Engineering** in OEM design departments. - **Acoustic source identification** using beamforming and acoustic cameras in R&D. - **Regulatory enforcement** by bodies such as ARAI (India), TÜV (Germany), and EPA (US). - **Workplace noise exposure** for assembly line workers (ISO 9612). ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Acoustic Intensity to Sound Power Level We start from the definition of **acoustic intensity** $I$, the time-averaged sound energy flux per unit area: $$I = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \, c}$$ where: - $\rho_0$ = density of air = **1.293 kg/m³** at 0 °C - $c$ = speed of sound in air = **343 m/s** at 20 °C - $p_{rms}$ = RMS sound pressure **Step 1:** The total acoustic power radiated by a source is the surface integral of the intensity over a closed surface $S$ enclosing the source: $$W = \oint_S I \, dS = \oint_S \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \, c} \, dS$$ **Step 2:** For a **hemispherical free field** above a reflecting ground plane (typical of an outdoor vehicle noise test), the surface area is $S = 2\pi r^2$, and by symmetry $p_{rms}$ is constant over the surface: $$W = \frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \, c} \cdot 2\pi r^2$$ **Step 3:** Convert to Sound Power Level by dividing by reference power $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W and taking 10·log₁₀: $$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2\pi r^2 \, p_{rms}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \, c \, W_0}\right)$$ **Step 4:** Split the logarithm using $\log(ab) = \log(a) + \log(b)$: $$\begin{aligned} L_W &= 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}^{\,2}}{p_0^{\,2}}\right) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{2\pi r^2 \, p_0^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \, c \, W_0}\right) \\ L_W &= 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) + 10 \log_{10}(2\pi r^2) + 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_0^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \, c \, W_0}\right) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5:** Substitute $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ and evaluate the constant term at standard atmospheric conditions. With $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa, $\rho_0 c = 413 \, \text{N·s/m³}$ (acoustic impedance of air), the constant evaluates to approximately **0 dB**, yielding the textbook result: $$L_W = L_p + 20 \log_{10}(r) + 8 \quad \text{[dB re 1 pW]}$$ The "$+8$" term arises from $10 \log_{10}(2\pi) \approx 7.98$, which rounds to **8 dB**. ### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example: Background Noise Correction **Problem:** During a stationary noise test, the vehicle noise measured is **82.0 dB(A)** and the background noise (engine off) is **74.0 dB(A)**. Compute the corrected vehicle noise using $K_1$ correction. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Compute the difference $\Delta L$: $$\Delta L = L_{signal} - L_{background} = 82.0 - 74.0 = 8.0 \text{ dB(A)}$$ **Step 2:** Compute the ratio term: $$10^{-0.1 \Delta L} = 10^{-0.8} = 0.1585$$ **Step 3:** Apply the $K_1$ formula: $$K_1 = -10 \log_{10}\left(1 - 0.1585\right) = -10 \log_{10}(0.8415) = -10 \times (-0.0751) = +0.75 \text{ dB}$$ **Step 4:** Compute corrected vehicle noise: $$L_{p,\text{corrected}} = L_{signal} - K_1 = 82.0 - 0.75 = 81.25 \text{ dB(A)}$$ > [!NOTE] > A **positive $K_1$** is **subtracted** from the measured value. The corrected value is **always lower** than the raw measurement, because part of what we measured was background contamination. ### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example: Addition of Multiple Sources **Problem:** A vehicle has three uncorrelated noise sources: engine at **75 dB(A)**, exhaust at **78 dB(A)**, and tire at **72 dB(A)**. Compute the total SPL. **Solution:** **Step 1:** Convert each dB to linear power ratio: $$10^{75/10} = 3.162 \times 10^7, \quad 10^{78/10} = 6.310 \times 10^7, \quad 10^{72/10} = 1.585 \times 10^7$$ **Step 2:** Sum the linear powers: $$P_{total} = (3.162 + 6.310 + 1.585) \times 10^7 = 11.057 \times 10^7$$ **Step 3:** Convert back to dB: $$L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(11.057 \times 10^7) = 10 \times (\log_{10}(11.057) + 7) = 10 \times (1.0436 + 7) = 80.44 \text{ dB(A)}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > Notice that the **strongest source (78 dB) dominates**. Doubling the weakest source from 72 to 75 dB would barely change the result. This is the classic **+3 dB doubling rule** — to make a noticeable difference, the dominant source must change. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ISO 362 vs ISO 5130 vs ECE R51 | Parameter | ISO 362-1 (Pass-by) | ISO 5130 (Stationary) | ECE R51-03 (Pass-by) | |---|---|---|---| | Vehicle State | Moving, accelerating | Stationary | Moving, accelerating | | Test Track | ISO 10844 | Open, hard surface | ISO 10844 | | Microphone Position | 7.5 m from path, 1.2 m height | 0.5 m from exhaust, 1.2 m height | 7.5 m from path, 1.2 m height | | Speed Criterion | $V_{AA} = 50$ km/h | Engine at rated RPM | $V_{ref}$ calculation | | Result Format | $L_{crs}$ (drive-by) | $L_{pA}$ (single value) | $L_{urban}$ + $L_{wot}$ weighted | | Engine Speed | 50% to 75% of rated RPM | Steady at rated RPM | Wide Open Throttle (WOT) | | Gear Selection | Highest possible | Neutral or engaged | Specified gear | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Noise Measurement System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Sound Source
Vehicle Under Test] --> B[Microphone
IEC 61094 Class 1] B --> C[Pre-amplifier
IEPE Powered] C --> D[Sound Level Meter
IEC 61672 Class 1] D --> E1[A-Weighting Filter
IEC 61672-1] D --> E2[Time Weighting F/S/I] E1 --> F[RMS Detector
Exponential] E2 --> F F --> G[Display & Data Logger
1/3 Octave Bands] G --> H[Post-Processing
K1 K2 Correction] H --> I[Final Report
dB A Result] J[Calibrator 94 dB at 1 kHz] -.->|Pre-Post Check| B K[Anemometer Wind Speed] --> H L[Thermometer Temperature] --> H ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **microphone** (typically a free-field ½-inch condenser mic, e.g., B&K 4189) is the first transducer. - The **pre-amplifier** converts the high-impedance mic signal to a low-impedance voltage, usually IEPE (Integrated Electronic Piezo-Electric) standard. - The **Sound Level Meter (SLM)** performs time-domain to dB conversion in real time. - The **calibrator** (94 dB, 1 kHz) is mandatory before and after the test to verify instrument drift ≤ ±0.5 dB. - **Environmental sensors** (wind, temperature) feed into post-processing for the $K_2$ correction. ### 4.2 Mermaid: Test Track Geometry (ISO 362 Pass-by Setup) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Track[ISO 10844 Test Track] A[Line AA
Start of Measurement Zone] B[Line PP
Microphone Projection] C[Line BB
End of Measurement Zone] end M[Microphone Position
7.5 m lateral, 1.2 m height] V[Test Vehicle Path] P1[Microphone Reference Line] A -->|20 m| B B -->|20 m| C M -.->|7.5 m perpendicular| P1 V -->|Drives North to South| A Track --> M ``` **Key Geometry Rules:** - The microphone is positioned at **line PP**, which is the projection of the microphone onto the centerline of the test lane. - **Line AA** is the entry boundary; **Line BB** is the exit boundary. The maximum SPL during transit is reported. - The vehicle enters at $V_{AA} = 50$ km/h with a tolerance of ±1 km/h. - The acceleration zone is at least **20 m** before Line AA. ### 4.3 Mermaid: A-Weighting Frequency Response Curve ```mermaid flowchart LR A[31.5 Hz
-39.4 dB] --> B[125 Hz
-16.1 dB] B --> C[500 Hz
-3.2 dB] C --> D[1000 Hz
0 dB Reference] D --> E[4000 Hz
+1.0 dB] E --> F[8000 Hz
-1.1 dB] G[Human Ear Response] -.->|Approximated| D ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid: Workflow of a Standard Pass-by Test ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1: Verify ISO 10844 Track Surface] --> B[Step 2: Calibrate SLM with 94 dB Source] B --> C[Step 3: Measure Background Noise L_b] C --> D[Step 4: Position Vehicle at Entry Line AA] D --> E[Step 5: Accelerate at WOT from 20 m Before AA] E --> F[Step 6: Pass Through Zone AA-BB] F --> G[Step 7: Record Maximum L_pA and Vehicle Speed] G --> H[Step 8: Repeat 4 Times per Side] H --> I[Step 9: Apply K1 Background Correction] I --> J[Step 10: Compute Mean and Report L_crs or L_wot] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.
