<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Define **combustion noise** in an internal combustion engine. How is it different from **mechanical noise**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Combustion noise is the acoustic radiation produced by rapid, impulsive cylinder pressure fluctuations during the combustion of the air–fuel mixture. It originates from the **rate of heat release** and is transmitted via cylinder liner vibration to the engine block. (2 marks)
In contrast, mechanical noise arises from **kinematic interactions** between moving parts — piston slap, valve-train impact, gear meshing — and is present even during motoring (no combustion). (1 mark)

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> State the standard reference values for **sound pressure** and **sound power** used in acoustics, and write the dB formulas.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- Reference sound pressure: $p_0 = 20\;\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{Pa}$ (1 mark)
- Reference sound power: $W_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W}$ (1 mark)
- Formulas: $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ and $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$ (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

> **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]**
> **(a)** With a suitable block diagram, explain the **mechanism of combustion noise generation** and its **transmission paths** in a multi-cylinder SI engine. Discuss the role of **rate of pressure rise (dP/dθ)** as the dominant excitation parameter. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A 4-stroke, 4-cylinder gasoline engine produces 90 dB(A) at idle and 96 dB(A) at rated load. Calculate the **combined sound pressure level** if both events are measured simultaneously. Comment on why linear addition is **not** valid. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks

1. **Block diagram (2 marks):** Draw the chain: Fuel injection → Rapid heat release → Cylinder pressure spike → Liner vibration → Block transmission → Engine mount coupling → Cabin radiation. (Use the schematic from SECTION 4.1 as reference.)
2. **Mechanism (3 marks):** Discuss how heat release rate (HRR) creates local pressure pulses, which excite the cylinder liner at its ring-frequency (1–5 kHz). The liner's breathing mode transfers vibration to the coolant jacket and the block.
3. **Role of dP/dθ (2 marks):** Higher dP/dθ (typically > 4 bar/deg) creates impulsive loading; the radiated SPL is approximately proportional to $\log_{10}(dP/d\theta)$. Slow-burn combustion (e.g., heavily diluted mixtures) reduces combustion noise.

### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks

**Given:** $L_1 = 90$ dB(A), $L_2 = 96$ dB(A)

**Step 1:** Identify the higher level — $L_2 = 96$ dB(A). (1 mark)

**Step 2:** Compute difference — $\Delta L = 96 - 90 = 6$ dB(A). (1 mark)

**Step 3:** Compute additive correction:

$$
10 \log_{10}\!\left(1 + 10^{-6/10}\right) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(1 + 0.2512) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(1.2512) \;\approx\; 0.97 \text{ dB}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4:** Total level:

$$
L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 96 + 0.97 \;\approx\; 96.97 \;\approx\; \mathbf{97 \text{ dB(A)}}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 5:** Comment on why linear addition is not valid: Because dB is a **logarithmic scale**, doubling the number of equal sources adds only ~3 dB, not 6 dB. Human perception of loudness is logarithmic, matching the dB scale. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

> **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]**
> **(a)** Explain the **typical frequency spectrum** of combustion noise in a diesel engine. Identify the dominant frequency band and explain why the **cylinder liner ring frequency** plays a critical role. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Describe any **four engineering strategies** to mitigate combustion noise in a modern passenger-car diesel engine. For each strategy, state the **physical principle** behind the reduction. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks

1. **Spectrum overview (3 marks):** Diesel combustion noise is broadband but with a dominant peak in the **2 – 5 kHz** band, corresponding to cylinder-liner resonance. Lower frequencies (200–800 Hz) are dominated by firing-order harmonics; higher frequencies (> 5 kHz) by injector and piston-slap effects.
2. **Liner ring frequency (2 marks):** The cylinder liner behaves like a thin cylindrical shell. Its ring (breathing) mode frequency is given by $f_r = c/(2\pi R)$ where $c$ is the speed of sound in the coolant-jacket water and $R$ is the liner mean radius. For typical 80 mm bore, $f_r \approx 3$–4 kHz.
3. **Why dominant (2 marks):** Diesel premixed-burn phase produces a sharp pressure step that has high spectral content in the 2–5 kHz band — exactly coincident with the liner ring mode, leading to **resonant amplification**.

### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks

| # | Strategy | Physical Principle | Marks |
|---|----------|--------------------|-------|
| 1 | **Pilot injection** | Splits heat release into 2 smaller steps, lowering peak dP/dθ by ~30%. | 1.75 |
| 2 | **Increased EGR (15–25%)** | Dilutes charge, slows premixed burn, lowers peak pressure rate. | 1.75 |
| 3 | **Combustion chamber re-entrant design** | Promotes swirl + progressive combustion, smoothens pressure rise. | 1.75 |
| 4 | **Acoustic engine cover (mass-loaded absorber)** | Adds damping mass on radiating surfaces, reduces airborne radiation efficiency $\sigma$. | 1.75 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Adding dB arithmetically** — Always use logarithmic addition. Marks deducted for linear sums.
> 2. **Confusing $L_p$ vs $L_W$ formulas** — Pressure uses the factor **20**, power uses **10**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 20 µPa reference value** — State it explicitly in every calculation.
> 4. **Ignoring A-weighting** — Modern KTU questions are increasingly dB(A)-specific; specify the weighting.
> 5. **Vague answers on transmission paths** — Always trace the *full* path: combustion → liner → block → mount → chassis → firewall → cabin.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Combustion Noise (KTU PEMUT633, Module 4)**

- **Definition:** Combustion noise = acoustic radiation from rapid cylinder pressure fluctuations during combustion.
- **Source:** Rate of heat release $\dfrac{dQ}{d\theta}$ and rate of pressure rise $\dfrac{dp}{d\theta}$.
- **Dominant frequency band:** **1 – 5 kHz** for SI engines; **2 – 5 kHz** for diesel engines.
- **Key transmission path:** Cylinder liner (ring mode) → engine block → mounts → firewall → cabin.
- **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$.
- **Reference power:** $W_0 = 10^{-12}\,\text{W}$.
- **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ — note factor of **20**, not 10.
- **PWL formula:** $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$ — note factor of **10**.
- **Log-add rule:** Two equal sources → +3 dB. Two sources differing by 10 dB → +0.4 dB.
- **A-weighting (dB(A)):** Models human hearing; de-emphasizes low frequencies (< 500 Hz).
- **CNI:** Combustion Noise Index correlates peak cylinder pressure with radiated noise.
- **Diesel mitigation:** Pilot injection, EGR, combustion chamber shaping.
- **SI mitigation:** Knock control via spark retard, optimized ignition timing, GDI stratified charge.
- **Standard:** ISO 362 (Drive-By Noise), ISO 5130 (stationary noise), ISO 3741 (sound power).
- **Typical values:** Idling combustion noise ≈ 75–85 dB(A); WOT ≈ 95–105 dB(A).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Noise
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Noise

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Mechanical Noise** in the context of automotive engineering is defined as the undesirable sound energy radiated by the vibrating mechanical components of a vehicle, arising from dynamic forces, impacts, frictional interactions, and rotational unbalances generated within the powertrain, chassis, and running gear systems. It constitutes one of the three primary pillars of the **NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)** discipline, alongside structure-borne vibration and airborne acoustic radiation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Lock:** Mechanical noise is *not* an electrical, aerodynamic, or combustion phenomenon. It originates strictly from **solid-body mechanical actions** such as gear meshing, bearing races, belt drives, brake squeal, tire-pavement interaction (within the mechanical domain), and engine reciprocating impacts. The aerodynamic and combustion (combustion-induced mechanical) sub-classes are sometimes grouped under mechanical noise when the source is mechanically induced pressure fluctuations on a solid boundary.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine striking the side of an empty metallic bucket with a spoon. The bucket vibrates, the surrounding air molecules get pushed and pulled, and your ears perceive a **ringing sound**. In a vehicle, every rotating gear tooth, every piston slap, every brake pad press is essentially that "spoon" striking a "bucket" — except there are hundreds of such micro-strikes every second. The combined effect is the characteristic **mechanical noise signature** of a vehicle.

**Simple Analogy Table:**

| Household Analogy | Vehicle Mechanical Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Spoon striking a steel bowl | Gear tooth meshing in a gearbox |
| Hinge creaking during door swing | Bushing/bearing wear in suspension |
| Washing machine rumble during spin | Tire-road interaction noise (mechanical part) |
| Bicycle chain rattle | Timing chain / cam drive rattle |
| Coin rattling in a piggy bank | Loose accessory bracket vibration |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:** Mechanical noise is **time-domain deterministic** (gear meshing has a fixed frequency = number of teeth × RPM/60), whereas aerodynamic noise is generally **broadband and stochastic** (turbulence). Examiners love asking students to differentiate these two.

### 1.3 Standard Acoustic Metrics (Bold Constants)

The following standardized metrics govern mechanical noise quantification in KTU 2024 syllabus:

- **Sound Pressure (p)**: Instantaneous pressure fluctuation about atmospheric pressure, measured in **Pascals (Pa)**.
- **Reference Sound Pressure (p₀)**: **p₀ = 20 µPa = 20 × 10⁻⁶ Pa** (threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz).
- **Reference Sound Power (W₀)**: **W₀ = 10⁻¹² W** (10 picowatts).
- **Reference Sound Intensity (I₀)**: **I₀ = 10⁻¹² W/m²**.
- **A-weighted decibel (dBA)**: Frequency-weighted sound level mimicking human ear response (A-weighting curve, IEC 61672).

### 1.4 Sources of Mechanical Noise in a Vehicle (Classification)

Mechanical noise in an automobile is classified into the following hierarchical sources:

1. **Engine Mechanical Noise**
   - Piston slap (lateral clearance impact)
   - Valve train noise (tappet, cam follower, lash adjuster)
   - Timing chain/belt drive rattle
   - Crankshaft bearing rumble
   - Fuel injection mechanical knock (diesel)

2. **Transmission & Drivetrain Noise**
   - Gear meshing whine (gear rattle, gear whine)
   - Bearing hum (ball bearing, roller bearing defect frequencies)
   - Differential gear noise
   - Clutch release bearing squeal

3. **Brake Noise**
   - Brake squeal (1–16 kHz, friction-induced self-excited vibration)
   - Brake groan (low frequency, 100–1000 Hz)
   - Brake judder (rotational irregularity)

4. **Tire-Pavement Mechanical Interaction**
   - Tire cavity resonance
   - Tire pattern noise (impact of tread blocks)
   - Road surface roughness excitation

5. **Auxiliary Mechanical Components**
   - Water pump, oil pump bearings
   - AC compressor clutch
   - Power steering pump whine
   - Wiper motor gear noise

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Mnemonic — "PTBBA"**: **P**iston, **T**rain (valve), **B**earings, **B**rakes, **A**uxiliaries — use this to recall mechanical noise sources under exam pressure.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Acoustic Fundamentals — Why dB?

The human ear responds to sound pressure **logarithmically**, not linearly. A 1000× increase in acoustic power is perceived only as a 3× increase in loudness. The decibel scale compresses the enormous linear range (10⁻¹² W to 10² W = 14 orders of magnitude) into a manageable 0–140 dB scale.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of dB Computation

**Step 1: Sound Pressure Level (SPL)**
SPL is the logarithmic ratio of measured RMS sound pressure to the reference threshold pressure.

$$ L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]} $$

Where:
- $p_{\text{rms}}$ = root-mean-square sound pressure (Pa)
- $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ = reference pressure

**Step 2: Sound Power Level (PWL)**
PWL quantifies the total acoustic energy radiated by a source, independent of distance.

$$ L_W = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]} $$

Where:
- $W$ = radiated sound power (W)
- $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W = reference power

**Step 3: Sound Intensity Level (SIL)**

$$ L_I = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]} $$

Where intensity is acoustic power per unit area:

$$ I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^2}{\rho_0 \cdot c_0} \quad \text{[W/m}^2\text{]} $$

with $\rho_0$ = 1.2 kg/m³ (air density) and $c_0$ = 343 m/s (speed of sound at 20 °C).

**Step 4: Relationship between PWL and SPL in Free Field**

For a point source radiating spherically in a free field:

$$ L_p = L_W - 20 \cdot \log_{10}(r) - 11 \quad \text{[dB]} $$

where $r$ is the distance from source in metres, and the constant $-11$ arises from $10 \log_{10}(4\pi)$.

**Step 5: Addition of Incoherent Sound Sources**

When two independent (uncorrelated) sound sources operate simultaneously:

$$ L_{\text{total}} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right) \quad \text{[dB]} $$

**Step 6: A-Weighting Adjustment**

The A-weighted level (dBA) approximates human hearing:

$$ L_A = L_p + \Delta A(f) \quad \text{[dBA]} $$

where $\Delta A(f)$ is the A-weighting correction at frequency $f$ (negative in low and very high frequencies, near 0 dB near 1–4 kHz).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Standard Units | Domain of Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ | dB | General SPL measurement |
| 2 | Sound Power Level (PWL) | $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | dB | Source characterisation |
| 3 | Sound Intensity Level (SIL) | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | dB | Intensity probe readings |
| 4 | Acoustic Intensity | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho_0 c_0)$ | W/m² | From pressure to energy |
| 5 | Free-field SPL from PWL | $L_p = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ | dB | Outdoor propagation |
| 6 | Incoherent source addition | $L_T = 10\log_{10}(10^{L_1/10}+10^{L_2/10})$ | dB | Multiple sources |
| 7 | Coherent source addition | $L_T = 20\log_{10}(2 \cdot 10^{L/20}) = L + 6$ | dB | In-phase identical sources |
| 8 | Gear meshing frequency (GMF) | $f_{\text{GMF}} = (N \cdot n) / 60$ | Hz | Gear noise identification |
| 9 | Ball Bearing Defect Freq. | BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF (formulae below) | Hz | Bearing diagnostics |
| 10 | Wavelength | $\lambda = c_0 / f$ | m | Acoustic panel design |

Where:
- $N$ = number of teeth on gear
- $n$ = rotational speed (rpm)
- BPFO = Ball Pass Frequency Outer race = $\frac{N_b \cdot n}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$
- BPFI = Ball Pass Frequency Inner race = $\frac{N_b \cdot n}{2}\left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right)$
- BSF = Ball Spin Frequency = $\frac{D}{2d}\cdot n\left(1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2\cos^2\alpha\right)$
- $N_b$ = number of balls, $d$ = ball diameter, $D$ = pitch diameter, $\alpha$ = contact angle.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Rule:** Two identical uncorrelated sources give **+3 dB** (not +6 dB). Two identical *coherent in-phase* sources give **+6 dB**. Doubling of acoustic power = +3 dB; tenfold increase = +10 dB.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compliance & Homologation**: ISO 362 (pass-by noise), ECE R51, AIS-018 in India mandate measured SPL limits (typically ≤ 74 dBA for passenger cars at 7 m, 50 km/h).
- **Diagnostics**: A trained NVH engineer pinpoints a defective bearing by matching measured peaks with theoretical BPFO/BPFI/BSF frequencies.
- **Design Validation**: During powertrain calibration, gear meshing whine is suppressed by profile modification (lead crowning, tip relief) and by selecting optimal gear ratios that avoid GMF resonances with cabin cavity modes.
- **Customer Comfort**: Mechanical noise directly correlates with brand perceived quality — Toyota's "TNGA" and Hyundai's recent platforms have invested heavily in mechanical noise refinement.

### 2.5 Mechanical Noise Transmission Paths

Mechanical noise reaches the driver via two principal pathways:

1. **Structure-Borne Path (SBN)**: Vibration → mounts → body panels → cabin air. Treated with isolators (rubber mounts, hydraulic mounts), damping materials (constrained layer damping), and tuned mass dampers.
2. **Air-Borne Path (ABN)**: Noise → direct radiation through hood/floor/firewall openings. Treated with absorption (foams, wool), barriers (mass law: TL ∝ mass), and sealing.

The total interior SPL is the **coherent summation** of both paths, taking into account the cross-over frequency at which SBN dominates over ABN (typically 200–500 Hz for most vehicles).

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Acoustic Power to dB Scale (Worked Analytical Proof)

**Starting premise:** The decibel is defined as ten times the logarithm (base 10) of the ratio of two powers, or twenty times the logarithm of the ratio of two root-power quantities.

**Step 1: Define Power Ratio**

$$ R = \frac{W}{W_0} $$

where $W$ is the measured acoustic power and $W_0$ is the reference power.

**Step 2: Apply the definition**

$$ \text{Bel} = \log_{10}(R) $$

$$ \text{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(R) = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) $$

This is the formal definition of sound power level.

**Step 3: Convert power to pressure (RMS)**

For a plane progressive wave, intensity is proportional to the square of RMS pressure:

$$ I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho_0 \cdot c_0} $$

Since $I \propto W/A$ (power per unit area), and because $W$ is proportional to $I$, we have $W \propto p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}$.

**Step 4: Substitute into the dB definition**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_p &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{p_0^{\,2}}\right) \\
    &= 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)^{\!2} \\
    &= 10 \cdot 2 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \\
    &= 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

This proves the factor of 20 in SPL — **a high-yield derivation KTU examiners love.**

**Step 5: Physical verification**

If $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0$, then $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(1) = 0$ dB ✓ (threshold of hearing).
If $p_{\text{rms}} = 10 \cdot p_0$, then $L_p = 20$ dB ✓ (tenfold pressure increase = 100× power).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Gear Meshing Frequency

**Problem:** A 4-cylinder gasoline engine has a camshaft sprocket with 22 teeth driving a 44-tooth crankshaft sprocket. The engine runs at 3000 rpm. Compute (a) camshaft speed, (b) gear meshing frequency.

**Given:**
- $N_{\text{cam}} = 22$ teeth
- $N_{\text{crank}} = 44$ teeth
- $n_{\text{crank}} = 3000$ rpm

**Solution:**

(a) Camshaft speed (4-stroke: camshaft = ½ crankshaft):

$$ n_{\text{cam}} = \frac{N_{\text{crank}}}{N_{\text{cam}}} \cdot n_{\text{crank}} = \frac{44}{22} \cdot 3000 / 2 = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$

Wait — correction. Gear ratio from crank to cam: 44/22 = 2:1. Therefore:

$$ n_{\text{cam}} = \frac{n_{\text{crank}}}{2} = \frac{3000}{2} = 1500 \text{ rpm} $$

(b) Gear meshing frequency at camshaft:

$$ f_{\text{GMF}} = \frac{N_{\text{cam}} \cdot n_{\text{cam}}}{60} = \frac{22 \cdot 1500}{60} = 550 \text{ Hz} $$

**Result:** A clear 550 Hz tone should be observable in an FFT spectrum near the engine's front cover during steady-state operation. If a tone is observed near 1100 Hz, it is the 2nd harmonic of GMF (a known signature of mesh stiffness variation).

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 2 — SPL Calculation & Source Addition

**Problem:** Two uncorrelated noise sources individually produce SPL of 78 dB and 81 dB at a microphone position. Compute the combined SPL.

**Step 1:** Convert dB to linear power ratios.

$$
\begin{aligned}
10^{L_1/10} &= 10^{78/10} = 10^{7.8} = 6.3096 \times 10^{7} \\
10^{L_2/10} &= 10^{81/10} = 10^{8.1} = 1.2589 \times 10^{8}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** Sum the linear ratios.

$$ \Sigma = 6.3096 \times 10^{7} + 1.2589 \times 10^{8} = 1.8899 \times 10^{8} $$

**Step 3:** Convert back to dB.

$$ L_T = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(1.8899 \times 10^{8}) = 10 \cdot (8.2764) = 82.76 \text{ dB} $$

**Verification using the +3 dB doubling rule:** A 3 dB difference between sources means the louder dominates; combined ≈ louder + 3 = 81 + 3 = 84 dB. The actual 82.76 dB lies correctly between 81 and 84. ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Calculation Convention:** When two sources differ by ≥ 10 dB, the quieter is negligible — total SPL ≈ louder source. When they are equal, add exactly 3 dB.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example 3 — BPFO of a Wheel Bearing

**Problem:** A wheel bearing has 12 balls, ball diameter $d = 12$ mm, pitch diameter $D = 60$ mm, contact angle $\alpha = 0°$, and rotates at 800 rpm. Compute BPFO and the order of that frequency.

**Step 1:** Convert rpm to Hz.

$$ f_{\text{shaft}} = \frac{800}{60} = 13.33 \text{ Hz} $$

**Step 2:** Apply BPFO formula with $\cos 0° = 1$:

$$ \text{BPFO} = \frac{N_b \cdot n}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) = \frac{12 \cdot 800}{60 \cdot 2}\left(1 - \frac{12}{60}\right) $$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BPFO} &= \frac{9600}{120} \cdot \left(1 - 0.2\right) \\
            &= 80 \cdot 0.8 = 64 \text{ Hz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the order.

$$ \text{Order} = \frac{64}{13.33} = 4.8 $$

This indicates a defective **outer race** with localized spalling, since BPFO is non-integer (4.8×) and shows characteristic sidebands at $f_{\text{cage}}$ around it.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Mechanical vs. Aerodynamic Noise

| Parameter | Mechanical Noise | Aerodynamic Noise |
|---|---|---|
| Source | Solid-body impacts/vibration | Turbulent pressure fluctuations |
| Frequency character | Tonal, harmonic (line spectrum) | Broadband, stochastic |
| Dominant range | 50 Hz – 4 kHz | 1 kHz – 16 kHz |
| Speed dependence | Weak (RPM dependent) | Strong (∝ U⁵ for bluff bodies) |
| Transmission | Structure + airborne | Primarily airborne |
| Diagnostic tool | Order analysis, FFT | CPB spectra, aero-acoustic CFD |
| Reduction method | Isolation, damping, balancing | Streamlining, fairings, liners |

### 3.6 Sound Transmission Loss (Mass Law)

For a single-panel partition (used to design firewall and floor insulators):

$$ \text{TL} = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\omega \cdot m}{2 \cdot \rho_0 \cdot c_0}\right) \quad \text{[dB]} $$

where $m$ = surface density of the panel (kg/m²) and $\omega = 2\pi f$. This means **doubling the panel mass increases TL by 6 dB** (the classical mass law).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**Q1. Define Sound Pressure Level (SPL) and state the standard reference pressure used in its computation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Sound Pressure Level (SPL) is the logarithmic measure of the RMS sound pressure of an acoustic wave, expressed in decibels (dB) relative to a reference pressure. Mathematically:

$$ L_p = 20 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \text{ dB} $$

The standard reference pressure is $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa, which corresponds to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz as defined by IEC 61672-1 and ISO 389-7.

(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for reference value and standard reference.)

---

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Q2. Differentiate between A-weighted (dBA) sound level and linear (dB) sound level. Why is dBA preferred for vehicle pass-by noise measurement?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Linear dB**: Flat frequency response from 20 Hz to 20 kHz; no compensation for the human ear's sensitivity.
- **dBA**: A-weighted frequency response that emphasizes mid frequencies (1–4 kHz) where the ear is most sensitive and de-emphasizes very low and very high frequencies.

dBA is preferred for vehicle pass-by noise (per ISO 362 and AIS-018) because it correlates well with human subjective loudness perception and annoyance. Linear dB would penalise vehicles for low-frequency rumble the human ear barely perceives, while ignoring high-frequency whistle components that are highly annoying. Regulatory bodies therefore mandate dBA for fair, perceptually-relevant noise limits.

(1 mark each for differentiating definitions, 1 mark for justification.)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

**[KTU University Exam – ESE Model Question Paper – Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**(a) Explain in detail the major sources of mechanical noise in an automotive vehicle with suitable classification. Discuss how each source generates a characteristic acoustic signature.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

Mechanical noise sources in a vehicle are classified into five hierarchical categories:

**(i) Engine Mechanical Noise (2 marks)**
The reciprocating pistons produce lateral slap due to clearance between piston skirt and cylinder bore, generating low-frequency impacts at twice the firing frequency: $f_{\text{slap}} = n/60 \cdot 2$ per cylinder. Valve train noise arises from cam-tappet impact at cam profile acceleration peaks, producing tappet rattle especially during cold start with high-viscosity oil. The timing chain/belt generates high-frequency whine at meshing frequency $f = N_{\text{teeth}} \cdot n_{\text{cam}}/60$. Crankshaft bearing rumble appears as low-frequency rumble with broadband content below 500 Hz.

**(ii) Transmission Noise (1.5 marks)**
Gear meshing whine is the dominant source, governed by gear meshing frequency $f_{\text{GMF}} = N \cdot n/60$ and its harmonics. The acoustic signature is a tonal peak with sidebands at $f_{\text{shaft}} \pm f_{\text{GMF}}$ due to amplitude modulation by gear eccentricity. Bearing defects (BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF) produce impulsive tones whose frequency identifies the defect location.

**(iii) Brake Noise (1 mark)**
Brake squeal is a self-excited friction-induced vibration between 1 and 16 kHz, often correlated with the in-plane vibration modes of the disc and the out-of-plane modes of the pad. The characteristic acoustic signature is a high-pitched tonal sweep that intensifies under low deceleration, hot brakes, or specific humidity conditions.

**(iv) Tire-Pavement Mechanical Noise (1 mark)**
The interaction between tread blocks and pavement produces impact noise at $f = v/L$ where $v$ is vehicle speed and $L$ is tread block spacing. Tire cavity resonance (Helmholtz resonance at 200–250 Hz for passenger tyres) provides an amplifying acoustic cavity.

**(v) Auxiliary Components (1.5 marks)**
Accessory drives such as water pumps, alternators, AC compressors, and power steering pumps contribute tonal and broadband noise typically in the 500 Hz – 5 kHz band. Worn bearings in these units produce characteristic BPFO/BPFI tones.

> The unique **acoustic fingerprint** of each source — its dominant frequency, harmonic structure, RPM dependence, and modulation behaviour — forms the basis of NVH diagnostics using FFT and order analysis.

---

**(b) With neat sketches, describe the working principle of a sound level meter. Explain the difference between Sound Pressure Level, Sound Power Level, and Sound Intensity Level with appropriate formulae.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Sound Level Meter (SLM) Working Principle (3 marks):**

A sound level meter consists of:
1. **Microphone** (typically ½-inch free-field condenser mic, e.g., B&K 4189) that converts acoustic pressure into an electrical signal.
2. **Pre-amplifier** to boost the weak mic signal.
3. **Frequency weighting network** (A, B, C, or Z/Linear) per IEC 61672-1.
4. **RMS detector** (true RMS, with Fast/Slow/Impulse time weighting).
5. **Logarithmic amplifier** to convert linear voltage to dB.
6. **Display** (analog meter or digital readout in dB or dBA).

**Working:** Sound pressure → mic diaphragm deflection → voltage → pre-amp → weighting filter → RMS → log → dB display.

**Difference Table (4 marks):**

| Parameter | SPL ($L_p$) | PWL ($L_W$) | SIL ($L_I$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Quantity measured | Acoustic pressure at a point | Total acoustic power radiated by source | Acoustic intensity at a point |
| Reference | $p_0 = 20 \times 10^{-6}$ Pa | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| Formula | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ |
| Multiplier | 20 | 10 | 10 |
| Distance dependence | Yes (∝ 1/r in free field) | No (intrinsic to source) | Yes (∝ 1/r² in free field) |
| Use case | Driver/passenger exposure | Source rating (e.g., machine noise emission) | Sound power estimation, source localisation |

The critical distinction: PWL is a property of the **source alone**, whereas SPL and SIL are properties at a **measurement location**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) Two identical machines produce 85 dB SPL individually at a workstation. (i) Compute the combined SPL when both operate simultaneously. (ii) If a third identical machine is added, what is the new combined SPL? (iii) Comment on the practical implications of these results in industrial noise control.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Two machines combined (3 marks):**
For two **uncorrelated identical** sources, SPL rises by 3 dB:

$$ L_T = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(N) = 85 + 10 \log_{10}(2) = 85 + 3.01 = 88.01 \text{ dB} $$

**(ii) Three machines combined (2 marks):**

$$ L_T = 85 + 10 \log_{10}(3) = 85 + 4.77 = 89.77 \text{ dB} $$

**(iii) Practical implications (2 marks):**
- Adding identical machines produces diminishing acoustic returns: each doubling adds only 3 dB; ten identical machines add only 10 dB.
- Noise control by source reduction (quieter machine design) is far more effective than by simple addition of isolation booths.
- Doubling the distance from a point source reduces SPL by 6 dB (free field), so spatial separation is a powerful control technique.
- OSHA permissible exposure limit (90 dB for 8 h) is exceeded at the workstation; engineering controls (isolation, damping, quieter machines) are mandated before PPE.

---

**(b) Explain the concept of Gear Meshing Frequency (GMF) and Bearing Defect Frequencies (BPFO, BPFI, BSF). How are these used in mechanical noise diagnostics of vehicles? Support your answer with the formulae for each frequency and a sample calculation.** (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Gear Meshing Frequency (GMF) (2 marks):**
GMF is the rate at which gear teeth engage per second:

$$ f_{\text{GMF}} = \frac{N \cdot n}{60} \text{ Hz} $$

For a helical gear, the effective number of teeth includes the helix factor. GMF appears as a strong tonal peak in the spectrum; sidebands at the shaft rotation frequency indicate modulation by gear eccentricity.

**Bearing Defect Frequencies (3 marks):**
- **BPFO (Ball Pass Frequency, Outer race)** — defect on outer race:

$$ \text{BPFO} = \frac{N_b \cdot n}{2}\left(1 - \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) $$

- **BPFI (Ball Pass Frequency, Inner race)** — defect on inner race:

$$ \text{BPFI} = \frac{N_b \cdot n}{2}\left(1 + \frac{d}{D}\cos\alpha\right) $$

- **BSF (Ball Spin Frequency)** — defect on ball:

$$ \text{BSF} = \frac{D \cdot n}{2d}\left[1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 \cos^2\alpha\right] $$

where $N_b$ = number of balls, $d$ = ball diameter, $D$ = pitch diameter, $\alpha$ = contact angle, $n$ = shaft speed in rpm.

**Diagnostic Application (2 marks):**
An NVH engineer captures vibration (accelerometer) and acoustic (microphone) data during a run-up coast-down sweep. Order analysis extracts RPM-locked components. Identification of discrete peaks matching BPFO/BPFI indicates the defective race; ball defects produce BSF peaks modulated by cage frequency ($f_{\text{cage}} = f_{\text{shaft}}/2 \cdot (1 - d\cos\alpha/D)$). The bearing is then replaced preventively before catastrophic failure.

**Sample Calculation:** A bearing with $N_b = 10$, $d = 8$ mm, $D = 50$ mm, $\alpha = 15°$, $n = 1500$ rpm gives:

$$ \text{BPFO} = \frac{10 \cdot 1500}{120}\left(1 - \frac{8}{50}\cos 15°\right) = 125 \cdot (1 - 0.1545) = 105.7 \text{ Hz} $$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SPL (uses factor 20) with PWL/SIL (use factor 10).** This is the single most common error. Always ask: "Am I comparing **pressures** (→ 20) or **powers** (→ 10)?"
> 2. **Using dB value directly in linear addition.** You must convert to linear (anti-log), sum, and convert back. Marking is strict on this step.
> 3. **Forgetting the $-11$ dB correction** in free-field PWL-to-SPL conversion.
> 4. **Mixing up $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ with $20 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa.** It is 20 micro-Pascals, not 20 milli-Pascals.
> 5. **Skipping unit conversion from rpm to Hz** in GMF and bearing frequency calculations. Always divide rpm by 60.
> 6. **Not specifying A-weighting (dBA)** when discussing vehicle pass-by regulations — this is a 1-mark deduction guaranteed.
> 7. **Ignoring the coherence of sources** when summing SPL — coherent in-phase sources add 6 dB, uncorrelated add 3 dB.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Mechanical Noise**

- [ ] **SPL** = $20 \log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$; **PWL** = $10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$; **SIL** = $10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$
- [ ] Reference values: **$p_0 = 20 \,\mu\text{Pa}$**, **$W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W**, **$I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²**
- [ ] Factor 20 for pressure-squared quantities, factor 10 for power quantities — never the reverse
- [ ] Doubling of uncorrelated sources → **+3 dB**; tenfold increase → **+10 dB**
- [ ] Doubling distance from point source in free field → **−6 dB**
- [ ] Free-field relation: $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 11$
- [ ] GMF = $N \cdot n/60$ Hz; identifies gear whine diagnostics
- [ ] BPFO, BPFI, BSF formulae — commit to memory with $d$, $D$, $\alpha$, $N_b$
- [ ] Mass law: doubling panel mass → +6 dB transmission loss
- [ ] Mechanical noise is **tonal + RPM-locked**; diagnose with **order analysis**
- [ ] Aerodynamic noise is **broadband**; analyse with **CPB / octave bands**
- [ ] Two transmission paths: **structure-borne (SBN)** and **air-borne (ABN)**
- [ ] Three control strategies: **source, path, receiver** — control at source is always most effective
- [ ] Regulatory pass-by noise measured in **dBA at 7 m, 50 km/h, ISO 362** / **AIS-018**
- [ ] A-weighting penalises low and very high frequencies, mimics human ear response
- [ ] SLM architecture: mic → preamp → weighting → RMS → log amp → display
- [ ] Acoustic intensity $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho_0 c_0)$ with $\rho_0 = 1.2$ kg/m³, $c_0 = 343$ m/s

---

> **End of Notes — Mechanical Noise | PEMUT633 | KTU 2024 Scheme | Module 4**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise from Auxiliaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise from Auxiliaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Auxiliary Noise** in an automotive context is defined as the **unwanted acoustic radiation** generated by the **non-powertrain support systems** of a vehicle, which are essential for the operation, comfort, safety, and emissions control of the automobile but are not directly part of the primary combustion-to-wheel power transmission path.

In strict **NVH (Noise, Vibration, and Harshness)** engineering terminology, "auxiliaries" refers to **secondary mechanical, fluid-dynamic, and electro-mechanical subsystems** that operate either continuously or intermittently in a vehicle. They are responsible for a significant portion of the overall vehicle Sound Pressure Level (SPL) spectrum, especially during **idle, low-speed cruise, and electric-vehicle (EV) operating regimes** where the main engine noise is masked or absent.

The standard KTU-relevant auxiliaries include:

| Category | Specific Auxiliaries |
|----------|---------------------|
| **Engine-Driven** | Cooling fan, water pump, oil pump, fuel pump, power steering pump, A/C compressor, alternator, supercharger |
| **Transmission-Driven** | Gear pumps in automatic gearboxes, retarders, torque converter |
| **Brake Systems** | Disc brake squeal, drum brake groan, pad wear sensors, ABS modulators |
| **Body & Trim** | Window regulators, wiper motors, seat adjusters, door latches, sunroof mechanisms |
| **Pneumatic / Tyre** | Tire-pavement interaction (cavity noise, tread noise), aerodynamic wind noise around mirrors/wipers |
| **HVAC** | Blower fans, evaporator hiss, refrigerant expansion |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Threshold:** The reference sound pressure $p_0$ is universally defined as **$p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{Pa}$**, and the reference sound power is **$W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$**. Any auxiliary noise measurement is benchmarked against these absolute thresholds.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are sitting in a **luxury library**. The room is acoustically treated. Suddenly, the air-conditioning starts humming, a clock on the wall starts ticking, a chair creaks, and a fan starts whirring. Individually, each is harmless — but **together, they form a "noise floor"** that prevents perfect silence.

A modern vehicle is identical to this library. The **main engine** is the loudest "speaker", but when it is shut off (in hybrids/EVs) or operating at idle, the **auxiliaries become the dominant noise sources**. Even when the engine is loud, auxiliaries add a specific **tonal character** to the cabin's acoustic signature.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Orchestra Principle:** Picture an orchestra. The string section (engine) plays loudly, but you can still hear the piccolo (alternator whine) and the triangle (wiper tick). The auxiliaries are those **percussive / tonal background instruments** that engineers must individually tune so the overall mix is pleasant (low NVH rating).

### 1.3 Why Auxiliaries Matter in Vehicle Testing

In a **KTU 2024 Scheme** context, the topic falls under **pass-by noise certification** and **drive-by noise testing (ISO 362-1)**. The relevance is:
1. **Regulatory Compliance:** Auxiliary noise contributes to pass-by SPL limits.
2. **Customer Perception:** Whine from the power-steering pump is a warranty complaint.
3. **EV-Specific Concern:** With silent electric powertrains, auxiliary noise is the **dominant perceived sound source**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Auxiliary Noise Sources

Auxiliary noise sources are broadly classified based on the **physical mechanism** responsible for acoustic radiation:

#### 2.1.1 Aerodynamic / Fluid-Dynamic Noise
This is generated when a fluid (air, water, oil, refrigerant) flows turbulently through a passage.

- **Cooling Fan Noise:** Aerodynamic noise from blade-passing frequency with shroud and radiator.
- **HVAC Blower Noise:** Heater core, evaporator, and blower assembly generate broadband turbulent flow noise.
- **A/C Compressor Refrigerant Hiss:** Gas pulsation at the expansion valve and evaporator outlet.

> [!NOTE]
> The dominant frequency for fan noise is governed by the **Blade Passing Frequency (BPF)**, given by $\text{BPF} = N \times f_{\text{shaft}}$ where $N$ is the number of blades and $f_{\text{shaft}}$ is the rotational frequency in Hz.

#### 2.1.2 Mechanical / Structure-Borne Noise
Generated by the **vibration of rotating or reciprocating components**, transmitted through mounts and brackets into the body structure, which then radiates sound.

- **Alternator Whine / Bearing Noise:** Bearing race defects, brush friction, electromagnetic excitation at slot harmonics.
- **Power Steering Pump Moan:** Pressure ripple at the pump's gear-mesh frequency.
- **Wiper Motor Gear Noise:** Spur or worm gear mesh frequency and its harmonics.

#### 2.1.3 Friction-Induced Noise (Self-Excited Vibration)
Generated by the **stick-slip or flutter mechanisms** at the contact interface of two sliding surfaces.

- **Brake Squeal:** 1 kHz to 16 kHz, caused by friction-induced instability at the pad-rotor interface.
- **Wiper Blade Squeak:** Stick-slip of rubber blade on windshield glass.
- **Clutch Judder / Rattle:** Torsional and axial vibrations in the drivetrain.

#### 2.1.4 Electromagnetic Noise
Originating from **magnetic forces** in alternators, motors, and transformers.

- **Alternator Whine:** Slot harmonics at $f = 2 \times f_{\text{rotor}} \times N_{\text{slots}}$.
- **Wiper / Window Motor Cogging:** Torque ripple at low speeds.

#### 2.1.5 Tire-Pavement Interaction Noise
A complex mix of:
- **Tread Impact Noise:** Discrete frequencies from tread blocks striking the road.
- **Air Pumping Noise:** Air compression/release in the tire-road contact patch.
- **Cavity Resonance:** Helmholtz resonance of the tire's air cavity (~200–250 Hz for passenger cars).

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all the high-yield formulas** required for solving numerical and theoretical questions on auxiliary noise in the KTU ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Use |
|---|----------------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$ | Sound Pressure Level (SPL) | Measuring noise at any point with a microphone |
| 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$ | Sound Power Level (SWL) | Characterising a noise source independently of distance |
| 3 | $I = \dfrac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | Acoustic Intensity | Linking pressure to power per unit area |
| 4 | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$ | Sound Intensity Level | Field-intensity measurement |
| 5 | $L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r}{r_0}\right)\ \text{dB}$ | Inverse-Square Law (point source) | Estimating SPL at distance $r$ from source |
| 6 | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_{p,i}/10}\right)\ \text{dB}$ | Sound Pressure Level Addition | Combining multiple uncorrelated sources |
| 7 | $\text{BPF} = N_{\text{blades}} \times f_{\text{shaft}}\ \text{Hz}$ | Blade Passing Frequency | Fan and compressor tonal analysis |
| 8 | $f_{\text{gear}} = N_{\text{teeth}} \times f_{\text{shaft}}\ \text{Hz}$ | Gear Mesh Frequency | Gear whine, pump noise |
| 9 | $f_{\text{cavity}} = \dfrac{c}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{A}{V \cdot L_{\text{eff}}}}\ \text{Hz}$ | Helmholtz Cavity Resonance | Tire cavity, intake manifold resonance |
| 10 | $f_{\text{critical}} = \dfrac{c}{2\pi D}\ \text{Hz}$ | Critical Frequency of a Panel | When wavelength equals panel bending wavelength |
| 11 | $L_{\text{A,eq}} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L_A(t)/10}\,dt\right)$ | Equivalent Continuous A-weighted SPL | Time-averaged noise exposure (driver's ear) |
| 12 | $\text{RNR} = L_{\text{vehicle}} - L_{\text{background}}\ \text{dB}$ | Rated Noise Rise | Test validity check (must be $\geq$ 10 dB) |

Where:
- $p_{\text{rms}}$ = root-mean-square acoustic pressure (Pa)
- $p_0$ = reference pressure = **$20\ \mu\text{Pa}$**
- $W_0$ = reference power = **$10^{-12}$ W**
- $I_0$ = reference intensity = **$10^{-12}$ W/m²**
- $\rho c$ = characteristic impedance of air ≈ **413 Pa·s/m** at 20°C
- $c$ = speed of sound in air ≈ **343 m/s** at 20°C
- $D$ = panel stiffness parameter (m)

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **inverse-square law** is heavily tested in KTU problems involving "predict the SPL at 7.5 m if it is X dB at 1 m" — a very common Part A or Part B sub-question. The **10 dB Rule** (each 10 dB increase = perceived doubling of loudness) is a frequent theory question.

---

### 2.3 Frequency Domain Characterisation of Auxiliaries

KTU examiners frequently test the **typical frequency band** of each auxiliary. The following mapping is critical for the answer sheet:

| Auxiliary | Dominant Frequency Range | Tonal vs Broadband |
|-----------|--------------------------|---------------------|
| Cooling Fan | 200 Hz – 4 kHz | Tonal at BPF + harmonics |
| A/C Compressor | 500 Hz – 5 kHz | Tonal at discharge frequency |
| Power Steering Pump | 100 Hz – 2 kHz | Tonal at gear-mesh frequency |
| Alternator | 1 kHz – 8 kHz | Tonal at slot harmonics |
| Brake Squeal | 1 kHz – 16 kHz | Tonal (high Q) |
| Tire Cavity Noise | 200 – 250 Hz | Tonal at resonance |
| HVAC Blower | 500 Hz – 5 kHz | Broadband turbulent |
| Wiper Motor | 50 Hz – 500 Hz | Tonal + broadband |

> [!NOTE]
> **A-Weighting:** The human ear is most sensitive around 2–5 kHz. The **A-weighted filter (dBA)** applies frequency-dependent attenuation to mimic this. Always specify whether the noise level is in **dB (linear)**, **dBA**, or **dBC** in your KTU answers. Regulatory drive-by noise is reported in **dBA**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In OEM NVH laboratories:
- **Head and Torso Simulator (HATS)** measurements use binaural microphones at the driver's ear position to capture auxiliary noise.
- **Order Tracking Analysis** separates engine order noise (1st, 2nd, 3rd order) from auxiliary noise.
- **Sound Intensity Scanning** (using a two-microphone probe) is used to **localise** the dominant auxiliary source on a running vehicle.
- **Source Path Contribution (SPC)** analysis is used in **CAE** to identify the dominant airborne or structure-borne path of an auxiliary into the cabin.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Sound Pressure Level and Sound Power Level for a Point Source

For a spherically radiating point source in a free field, the acoustic intensity at radial distance $r$ is:

$$
I(r) = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 1: Express the intensity in terms of SPL using $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{2}/\rho c$:**

$$
\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{\rho c} = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for the mean-square pressure:**

$$
p_{\text{rms}}^{2} = \frac{\rho c\, W}{4\pi r^{2}}
$$

**Step 3: Take 10 log of both sides, then multiply the pressure terms appropriately:**

$$
10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{2}}{p_0^{2}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\rho c\, W}{4\pi r^{2}\, p_0^{2}}\right)
$$

**Step 4: Separate terms on the right-hand side:**

$$
L_p = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(4\pi r^{2}\right) - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\rho c\, p_0^{2}}{W_0}\right)
$$

**Step 5: Recognise that $10 \log_{10}(W/W_0) = L_W$, and combine the constant terms into $K$:**

$$
L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) + K
$$

**Step 6: For a point source radiating into a free field, $K = 10 \log_{10}(1/(4\pi)) \approx -10.99\ \text{dB}$, but for directivity $Q=2$ (hemispherical on a reflecting ground plane, as in pass-by testing), the constant is $K = -8\ \text{dB}$:**

$$
L_p = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8
$$

This is the **fundamental KTU relationship** between source power and far-field pressure for a vehicle on a hard test track.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Combining Two Auxiliary Sound Sources

A vehicle cabin receives noise from the **alternator** (84 dBA) and the **HVAC blower** (81 dBA) simultaneously. Find the combined SPL.

**Step 1: Convert dB to linear intensity ratios:**

For the alternator:

$$
\frac{I_1}{I_0} = 10^{84/10} = 2.51 \times 10^{8}
$$

For the blower:

$$
\frac{I_2}{I_0} = 10^{81/10} = 1.26 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 2: Add the linear ratios (since intensities are additive for uncorrelated sources):**

$$
\frac{I_{\text{total}}}{I_0} = 2.51 \times 10^{8} + 1.26 \times 10^{8} = 3.77 \times 10^{8}
$$

**Step 3: Take 10 log to obtain the combined SPL:**

$$
L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log_{10}(3.77 \times 10^{8})
$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the logarithm numerically:**

$$
L_{p,\text{total}} = 10\,[\log_{10}(3.77) + 8] = 10\,[0.5763 + 8] = 85.76\ \text{dBA}
$$

**Result: $L_{p,\text{total}} \approx 85.8\ \text{dBA}$.** Note that the 3 dB difference between the two sources means the louder source dominates — the combined value is only **1.8 dB higher** than the louder source alone, not 3 dB.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** When combining two SPL values:
> - If difference $\geq$ 10 dB → use the louder value as the answer.
> - If difference $\approx$ 3 dB → add **3 dB** to the louder.
> - If difference $\approx$ 0 dB → add **3 dB** to either.
> - If difference = 6 dB → add **~1 dB** to the louder.
> - If difference = 10 dB → add **~0.4 dB** to the louder.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Distance Extrapolation Using Inverse-Square Law

A cooling fan is measured to produce 78 dBA at **1 m**. What is the SPL at the driver's ear position, which is **1.8 m** from the fan?

**Step 1: Write the inverse-square law for SPL difference between two distances:**

$$
\Delta L_p = L_p(r_2) - L_p(r_1) = -20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values $r_1 = 1\ \text{m}$ and $r_2 = 1.8\ \text{m}$:**

$$
\Delta L_p = -20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.8}{1}\right)
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\Delta L_p = -20 \times 0.2553 = -5.11\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Apply the change to the original SPL:**

$$
L_p(1.8\ \text{m}) = 78 - 5.11 = 72.89\ \text{dBA}
$$

**Result: $L_p(1.8\ \text{m}) \approx 72.9\ \text{dBA}$.** The driver's ear receives a **5.1 dB lower** level than the close-field microphone position.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Auxiliary Noise — Mechanism vs. Control Strategy

| Auxiliary | Noise Mechanism | Dominant Frequency | Primary Control Strategy | KTU Weightage |
|-----------|-----------------|--------------------|---------------------------|----------------|
| Cooling Fan | Aerodynamic (blade shedding) | BPF, 200–4000 Hz | Asymmetric blade spacing, ring fan, viscous clutch, electric fan | High |
| Water Pump | Cavitation + bearing | 500–3000 Hz | Cavitation-resistant impeller, isolation mounts | Low |
| Power Steering Pump | Gear-mesh pressure ripple | 100–2000 Hz | Gerotor vs. gear, damping in pressure line | Medium |
| A/C Compressor | Reed-valve pulsation | 500–5000 Hz | Suction muffler, discharge resonator, rubber isolators | High |
| Alternator | Electromagnetic slot harmonics | 1000–8000 Hz | Skewed lamination, encapsulating cover, isolation | High |
| Brake Squeal | Friction-induced vibration | 1000–16000 Hz | Chamfered pads, damping shims, rotor damping | Very High |
| Tire Cavity | Helmholtz resonance | 200–250 Hz | Foam liner inside tire, sidewall treatment | Medium |
| Wiper Motor | Gear meshing | 50–500 Hz | Helical gears, rubber bushings | Low |
| HVAC Blower | Turbulent flow | 500–5000 Hz | Scroll housing, multi-speed control | Medium |
| Door Latch / Trim | Rattle, buzz | 50–500 Hz | Felt tapes, foam backers, gap tuning | Low |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Big Four" Auxiliaries** for KTU exams are: (1) Cooling Fan, (2) Brake Squeal, (3) Tire Cavity, and (4) A/C Compressor. Always prepare detailed notes on these four, as they appear most frequently in past papers.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Auxiliary Noise Source Map in a Modern Vehicle

The following Mermaid block provides a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** mapping the interactions of the major auxiliary noise sources with the cabin NVH system. This adapts the requirement to a readable schematic since physical drawings of the vehicle are not possible in Mermaid.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Engine Cooling Fan] --> M1[Radiator + Shroud Assembly]
    A2[Water Pump] --> M2[Engine Block Mounts]
    A3[Power Steering Pump] --> M3[Steering Rack Hose]
    A4[AC Compressor] --> M4[Condenser + Suction Line]
    A5[Alternator] --> M5[Engine Bracket + Belt Drive]
    A6[Exhaust Muffler] --> M6[Tail Pipe]
    A7[Tire Cavity] --> M7[Wheel Hub + Road]
    A8[HVAC Blower] --> M8[Dashboard Duct]
    A9[Wiper Motor] --> M9[Linkage + Wiper Arm]
    A10[Brake System] --> M10[Disc + Pad Caliper]

    M1 --> P1[Structure Borne Path via Mounts]
    M2 --> P1
    M3 --> P1
    M4 --> P1
    M5 --> P1
    M1 --> P2[Airborne Path via Radiator Aperture]
    M4 --> P2
    M6 --> P2
    M7 --> P2
    M8 --> P2

    P1 --> C1[Vehicle Body Structure]
    P2 --> C1
    A10 --> P2

    C1 --> C2[Cabin Interior Air Volume]
    C2 --> M11[Driver Ear Microphone]
    C2 --> M12[Passenger Ear Microphone]

    M11 --> R1[SPL Spectrum in dBA]
    M12 --> R1
    R1 --> R2[ISO 362 Pass By Compliance Check]
    R1 --> R3[Customer NVH Rating]
```

### 4.2 Brake Squeal Mechanism (Block-Level Flow)

Brake squeal is the most distinctive auxiliary noise in a vehicle. The mechanism can be represented as a **feedback loop**:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1[Pad Disc Friction Coefficient] --> F2[Tangential Force on Rotor]
    F2 --> F3[In Plane Mode Vibration of Disc]
    F3 --> F4[Pad Tangential Stick Slip]
    F4 --> F1
    F3 --> F5[Out of Plane Bending of Disc]
    F5 --> F6[Acoustic Radiation 1 to 16 kHz]
    F4 --> F6
    F6 --> F7[Squeal Sensation in Cabin]
```

### 4.3 HVAC Blower Noise Generation Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    B1[Blower Motor Torque] --> B2[Impeller Rotation]
    B2 --> B3[Blade Tip Vortex Shedding]
    B2 --> B4[Blade Passage Frequency BPF]
    B3 --> B5[Broadband Turbulent Noise]
    B4 --> B6[Tonal Whine]
    B5 --> B7[HVAC Duct Transmission]
    B6 --> B7
    B7 --> B8[Cabin Outlet Air Vent]
    B8 --> B9[Driver Ear SPL]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams?** The KTU examiner allocates marks for **process mapping** in NVH topics. Drawing a clear block diagram showing **Source → Path → Receiver** earns full marks in Part B answers on auxiliary noise control.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper, Q1(a)]**

**Q1. (a) Define auxiliary noise. List any four auxiliary noise sources in an automobile.**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Auxiliary noise is the unwanted acoustic radiation generated by the **non-powertrain support systems** of a vehicle, such as the cooling fan, alternator, A/C compressor, power steering pump, brake system, and HVAC blower. These systems are not part of the primary power transmission path, but they contribute significantly to the overall vehicle noise level, especially at idle and low speeds. *(3 marks)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Paper, Q1(b)]**

**Q1. (b) Distinguish between A-weighted (dBA) and linear (dB) sound pressure levels. Why is dBA used for vehicle noise certification?**
*(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The **linear SPL (dB)** is an unweighted logarithmic measure of RMS sound pressure, treating all frequencies equally. The **A-weighted SPL (dBA)** applies a frequency-dependent filter that attenuates low and very high frequencies to mimic the reduced sensitivity of the human ear. Vehicle noise (e.g., ISO 362 pass-by) is certified in **dBA** because regulatory limits are set to protect **human hearing and annoyance**, not to characterise the physical source. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2 (Module 4) — Choice A]**

**Q2. (a) Explain in detail the various sources of noise from auxiliaries in a modern automobile, with the mechanism of noise generation in each. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The auxiliaries of a vehicle are support systems that are not part of the main engine-to-wheel powertrain but contribute to the overall NVH performance. The major sources of auxiliary noise are:

1. **Cooling Fan Noise (Aerodynamic):** The fan blades rotating inside the shroud generate **blade-passing-frequency (BPF) tones**, calculated as $\text{BPF} = N \times f_{\text{shaft}}$. Turbulent flow on the blade suction side creates **broadband noise**. The interaction with the radiator matrix adds turbulent eddies. The fan is driven by a belt, and any belt slippage adds slip-grind noise. *(2 marks)*

2. **A/C Compressor Noise (Refrigerant Pulsation):** The compressor's reed valves open and close at a frequency linked to the crank, generating **gas pulsations in the suction and discharge lines**. The discharge muffler and accumulator tank are used to attenuate this. *(1 mark)*

3. **Power Steering Pump Noise (Pressure Ripple):** The internal gear or gerotor pump generates pressure ripple at the **gear-mesh frequency**, transmitted through the high-pressure hose to the steering rack. This creates a moan or whine, especially at parking speeds. *(1 mark)*

4. **Alternator Noise (Electromagnetic):** The interaction of rotor poles and stator slots produces **magnetic slot harmonics** at $f = 2 f_{\text{rotor}} N_{\text{slots}}$. The bearing noise of the alternator is also transmitted to the engine bracket. *(1 mark)*

5. **Brake Squeal (Friction-Induced Self-Excitation):** A stick-slip instability at the pad-rotor interface causes **high-frequency tonal squeal in the 1–16 kHz range**. Out-of-plane disc modes couple with pad friction to sustain the vibration. *(1 mark)*

6. **Tire Cavity Noise (Helmholtz Resonance):** The air cavity inside the tire resonates at 200–250 Hz, creating a **droning tone** that is radiated from the wheel well. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. (b) With a neat block diagram, describe the procedure for **Source-Path-Receiver** analysis of auxiliary noise in a vehicle. How does this analysis help in NVH development? (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Source-Path-Receiver (SPR) framework is a structured approach to NVH problem-solving.

1. **Source Identification:** The auxiliary is identified as the source (e.g., cooling fan). Sound intensity scanning with a two-microphone probe is used to **localise** the source on the vehicle. Order tracking separates the engine order noise from the auxiliary noise. *(2 marks)*

2. **Path Characterisation:** The path can be:
   - **Airborne path** (sound travels through air from source to receiver).
   - **Structure-borne path** (vibration travels through mounts and brackets to the body panel, which then radiates as sound).
   
   The transfer functions between the source and the receiver (e.g., driver's ear) are measured using **admittance and transmissibility tests**. *(2 marks)*

3. **Receiver Evaluation:** The receiver is the **driver's ear (or HATS)** in the cabin. The SPL is measured using a head-and-torso simulator with binaural microphones. The interior SPL is analysed in **1/3-octave bands** and A-weighted. *(1 mark)*

4. **NVH Development Utility:**
   - Enables **target setting** for each auxiliary during the design phase.
   - Supports **CAE simulation** (FEA + BEM) for virtual NVH development.
   - Helps prioritise **fixes at the source** (e.g., fan redesign) versus at the path (e.g., mounts) versus at the receiver (e.g., dash insulation). *(2 marks)*

```
SOURCE  --airborne-->  CABIN AIR  --microphone-->  EAR SPL
   \                 /
    --structure-borne--> BODY PANEL
```

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q2 (Module 4) — Choice B]**

**Q2. (a) What is brake squeal? Explain the mechanism of brake squeal generation. Discuss two methods to control brake squeal. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:** Brake squeal is a **high-frequency tonal noise (1–16 kHz)** generated by the friction-induced self-excited vibration of the brake pad-rotor interface during a braking event. It is one of the most common NVH complaints and is a typical auxiliary noise source. *(1 mark)*

**Mechanism:**
1. The brake pad is pressed against the rotating disc with a high friction coefficient.
2. Small geometric or material asymmetries cause the pad to **stick and slip** tangentially (stick-slip phenomenon).
3. This tangential force excites the **in-plane and out-of-plane vibration modes** of the disc.
4. When the friction coefficient is **negative-sloped with respect to relative velocity** (i.e., $\partial \mu / \partial v < 0$), the system becomes **unstable** and self-excited vibration occurs.
5. The disc radiates sound at its bending mode frequencies, producing the audible squeal. *(4 marks)*

**Control Methods:**
1. **Pad Chamfering and Slotted Design:** Geometric modifications of the pad face change the contact pressure distribution and break the resonance coupling. *(1 mark)*
2. **Damping Shims and Rotor Damping:** Constrained-layer damping (CLD) shims on the back of the pad, and **sintered or composite rotors**, increase damping and reduce the Q-factor of the disc modes. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. (b) The cooling fan of a vehicle is measured to produce an SPL of 82 dBA at 0.5 m from the fan. Estimate the SPL at the driver's ear located 1.5 m from the fan, assuming a free-field point source. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $L_p(r_1) = 82\ \text{dBA}$ at $r_1 = 0.5\ \text{m}$
- Distance to driver ear: $r_2 = 1.5\ \text{m}$

**Step 1: Apply the inverse-square law in dB form:**

$$
\Delta L_p = L_p(r_2) - L_p(r_1) = -20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the values:**

$$
\Delta L_p = -20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.5}{0.5}\right) = -20 \log_{10}(3)
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\Delta L_p = -20 \times 0.4771 = -9.54\ \text{dB}
$$

**Step 4: Compute the SPL at the driver's ear:**

$$
L_p(1.5\ \text{m}) = 82 - 9.54 = 72.46\ \text{dBA}
$$

**Result: $L_p(\text{ear}) \approx 72.5\ \text{dBA}$.** *(5 marks for full derivation + 2 marks for stating the assumption of free-field point source).*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Wrong formula:** Many students use $-10 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ (the intensity field formula) instead of $-20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ (the pressure field formula). For a free-field **point source**, the correct exponent is **20**, not 10.
> 2. **Ignoring the "free-field" assumption:** You MUST state that the inverse-square law applies in a free field. In a reverberant or enclosed space, the law is invalid.
> 3. **Unit confusion:** The answer is in dBA, not Pa. Always end with the dB unit explicitly.
> 4. **Forgetting reference threshold:** Forgetting to write $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ in derivations loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Noise from Auxiliaries"**

- [x] **Definition:** Unwanted acoustic radiation from non-powertrain support systems.
- [x] **Sources:** Cooling fan, A/C compressor, alternator, power steering pump, brake system, tire cavity, HVAC blower, wiper, exhaust muffler.
- [x] **Mechanisms:** Aerodynamic, mechanical (gear mesh, bearing), friction-induced (stick-slip), electromagnetic (slot harmonics), Helmholtz resonance.
- [x] **Reference Thresholds:** $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$, $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- [x] **Standard Speed of Sound:** $c = 343$ m/s at 20°C.
- [x] **Key Formulas:** $L_p = 20 \log(p/p_0)$, $L_W = 10 \log(W/W_0)$, $L_p = L_W - 20 \log(r) - 8$ (hemispherical), $\text{BPF} = N \cdot f_{\text{shaft}}$.
- [x] **Combining Two SPLs:** $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10 \log(10^{L_{p1}/10} + 10^{L_{p2}/10})$.
- [x] **Distance Rule:** Doubling distance from a point source reduces SPL by 6 dB.
- [x] **Loudness Perception:** 10 dB rise = perceived doubling of loudness; 3 dB rise = barely perceptible doubling of acoustic power.
- [x] **A-Weighting:** Used for all regulatory vehicle noise (dBA).
- [x] **ISO 362 Pass-By Test:** Standard for vehicle drive-by noise measurement.
- [x] **HATS:** Head And Torso Simulator — standard for interior SPL measurement.
- [x] **Big Four Auxiliaries** (high-weightage): Cooling Fan, Brake Squeal, Tire Cavity, A/C Compressor.
- [x] **Brake Squeal Range:** 1 kHz to 16 kHz, friction-induced.
- [x] **Tire Cavity Resonance:** ~200–250 Hz for passenger cars.
- [x] **Cooling Fan BPF:** Typically 200 Hz to 4 kHz.
- [x] **NVH Development Method:** Source-Path-Receiver (SPR) framework.
- [x] **Control Hierarchy:** Fix at Source → Fix at Path → Fix at Receiver (in that order of cost-effectiveness).
- [x] **Sound Intensity Probe:** Two-microphone probe for source localisation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wind Noises
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMUT633 - VEHICLE PERFORMANCE AND TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Wind Noises

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wind Noise (Vehicle Aeroacoustics):** Wind noise is the airborne acoustic energy radiated into the passenger cabin of an automobile as a direct consequence of the turbulent interaction between the surrounding air mass and the external surfaces of the moving vehicle body (and its protruding appendages). It is an **aerodynamic source** of interior noise and becomes the *dominant* noise contributor above vehicle speeds of approximately **120 km/h (≈ 33.3 m/s)** for sedans, and as low as **80 km/h** for SUVs and cross-over vehicles because of their larger frontal area and blunter geometry.

It is formally classified under **NVH (Noise, Vibration & Harshness)** discipline as an *exterior-flow-induced interior acoustic phenomenon* and is measured using the **A-weighted Sound Pressure Level, dB(A)**, with the microphone placed at the driver's ear position (Driver's Right Ear – DRE) per **ISO 362-1** and **SAE J1477** standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key metric used by KTU/Industry:** Overall Noise Level — **OASPL (Overall A-weighted Sound Pressure Level)** in **dB(A)**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing on a railway platform when a high-speed train passes without honking. The loud *"whoosh-rrrr"* sound you hear is **NOT** made by the train's engine — it is the air being violently displaced, separated, and re-circulated around the train's nose, sides, and pantographs. Now shrink that train down, replace it with a car at 100–150 km/h, and put your ear inside the cabin — **that is wind noise**.

A second analogy: blow across the mouth of an empty glass bottle. The whistle you hear is *vortex shedding* at the bottle's lip — exactly the same aeroacoustic phenomenon responsible for the howling at the **A-pillars** and **side mirrors** of a moving car.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Reference Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $p_{ref}$ | Reference sound pressure | **20 µPa** (20 × 10⁻⁶ Pa) |
| $W_{ref}$ | Reference sound power | **10⁻¹² W** (1 pW) |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Air density (sea level, 20 °C) | **1.204 kg/m³** |
| $c_0$ | Speed of sound in air | **343 m/s** |
| $\mu_{air}$ | Dynamic viscosity of air | **1.81 × 10⁻⁵ Pa·s** |
| $\nu_{air}$ | Kinematic viscosity of air | **1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** |

> [!TIP]
> **Why A-weighting?** The human ear does not respond equally to all frequencies — it is most sensitive around **2–5 kHz**. The **A-weighting filter** mathematically models this and is the global automotive industry standard for wind-noise reporting.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sources / Generation Mechanisms of Wind Noise (KTU-Exam-Relevant Bulleted Logic)

A moving vehicle disturbs the surrounding quiescent air in three primary ways, each of which is a **wind-noise source**:

### A. Turbulent Boundary Layer (TBL) Fluctuations
- Air *sticks* to the body surface due to viscosity, forming a thin boundary layer.
- At sufficiently high **Reynolds number** ($Re > 5 \times 10^5$ for a typical car), the layer transitions from **laminar → turbulent**.
- Turbulent eddies inside the boundary layer produce *unsteady surface pressures* that mechanically excite the glass and sheet metal, which then radiate sound inward.

### B. Flow Separation and Re-attachment
- Occurs at sharp geometric discontinuities: A-pillars, B-pillars, windshield cowl, rear window, antenna base.
- Creates **unsteady shear layers** that roll up into discrete vortices → **vortex shedding**.

### C. Bluff-Body Vortex Shedding from Appendages
- Side mirrors, roof rails, shark-fin antenna, door handles, wiper blades.
- Each acts as a **bluff body** shedding Kármán vortices at a characteristic frequency given by the **Strouhal number**.

## 2.2 The Three-Stage Transmission Path

A KTU-favourite logical flow:

1. **Generation** — aerodynamic excitation (unsteady pressure field) on outer panel.
2. **Transmission** — mechanical vibration of glass/metal panels (Structure-Borne Path).
3. **Radiation** — re-radiation of acoustic energy into cabin (Air-Borne Path).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear repeatedly in KTU ESE questions on Module 4.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SPL = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{p_{rms}}{p_{ref}}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level | **dB** |
| 2 | $SWL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_{ref}}\right)$ | Sound Power Level | **dB** |
| 3 | $L_A = SPL + \Delta_A(f)$ | A-weighted SPL | **dB(A)** |
| 4 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number — flow regime | dimensionless |
| 5 | $M = \dfrac{U}{c_0}$ | Mach number — compressibility | dimensionless |
| 6 | $St = \dfrac{f \cdot D}{U}$ | Strouhal number — vortex shedding | dimensionless (≈ 0.21 for circular cylinder) |
| 7 | $f_v = \dfrac{St \cdot U}{D}$ | Vortex-shedding frequency | **Hz** |
| 8 | $SPL \propto U^{n},\ n \in [5,\,8]$ | Lighthill's acoustic scaling law | — |
| 9 | $p_{rms}^{2} \propto \rho_{0}^{2}\,U^{8}\,L^{2}/r^{2}$ | Curle's dipole far-field pressure | Pa² |
| 10 | $L_p(\theta) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\cos^{2}\theta\right)$ | Directivity of dipole source | **dB** |

**Critical Boundary Conditions used by KTU examiners:**
- **Sub-critical Reynolds:** $Re < 2 \times 10^{5}$ → laminar vortex shedding.
- **Critical / Trans-critical:** $2 \times 10^{5} \le Re \le 5 \times 10^{5}$ → random vortex shedding (highest noise).
- **Super-critical:** $Re > 5 \times 10^{5}$ → narrower-band turbulent shedding.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cabin refinement (luxury vehicles):** Target interior SPL ≤ **65 dB(A)** at 120 km/h.
- **Electric Vehicles (EVs):** With powertrain noise absent, wind noise is the *primary* NVH complaint above 60 km/h — drives aero-acoustic optimisation.
- **Wind-tunnel testing:** OEMs use **acoustic wind tunnels** with anechoic walls to map surface pressure fluctuations using **microphone arrays** and **Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV)**.
- **Regulatory compliance:** ECE R51 (pass-by noise) and ISO 362-1 govern *exterior* noise; GM, Hyundai, and Tata Motors use proprietary **Wind Noise Quality (WNQ)** metrics for *interior* ratings.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lighthill's Eighth Power Law (Dipole Source)

This derivation explains *why* wind noise rises so steeply with vehicle speed. It is a **KTU-favourite analytical question**.

### Step-by-step analytical working

**Step 1 — Start from Lighthill's Acoustic Analogy (general form for an unbounded fluid with no external forces):**

$$\frac{\partial^{2}\rho'}{\partial t^{2}} - c_{0}^{2}\,\nabla^{2}\rho' = \frac{\partial^{2}T_{ij}}{\partial x_{i}\,\partial x_{j}}$$

Here, $\rho'$ is the acoustic density fluctuation and $T_{ij}$ is **Lighthill's stress tensor**.

**Step 2 — For aerodynamic flow over a rigid surface, the dominant noise source is the unsteady surface pressure $p_{s}(t)$ acting on the body. This is mathematically equivalent to a *distribution of acoustic dipoles* on the surface (Curle, 1955):**

$$p(\mathbf{x}, t) = \frac{1}{4\pi c_{0}} \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\int_{S}\!\frac{p_{s}(\mathbf{y}, t - \vert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\vert/c_{0})\,\cos\theta}{\vert\mathbf{x}-\mathbf{y}\vert}\,dS(\mathbf{y})$$

where $\theta$ is the angle between the surface normal and the observer direction.

**Step 3 — Dimensionally analyse the integral.** Let $L$ be the characteristic body dimension, $U$ the free-stream velocity, $\rho_{0}$ the air density, and $r$ the observer distance. The unsteady surface pressure on a bluff body scales as:

$$p_{s} \sim \rho_{0} U^{2}$$

This is **dynamic pressure**, the dominant surface pressure fluctuation amplitude.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Curle integral:**

$$p \sim \frac{1}{4\pi c_{0}\,r}\cdot\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\!\left[\rho_{0} U^{2}\cdot L^{2}\right]$$

Taking the time derivative (in flow-noise context, $\partial/\partial t \sim U/L$ because eddies convect past at speed $U$ over a length $L$):

$$\frac{\partial p_{s}}{\partial t} \sim \frac{\rho_{0} U^{3}}{L}$$

**Step 5 — Therefore the acoustic pressure amplitude scales as:**

$$p \sim \frac{\rho_{0}\,U^{3}\,L^{2}}{c_{0}\,r}$$

**Step 6 — Sound Pressure Level uses a squared ratio:**

$$SPL = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p}{p_{ref}}\right) \;\Rightarrow\; SPL \propto 20\log_{10}(U^{3})$$

$$\boxed{\;SPL \propto 60\,\log_{10}(U)\;}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to a "power law" form:**

$$SPL \propto U^{n} \quad\text{where}\quad n \approx 6 \text{ (ideal dipole)}$$

In practice, for real automotive bluff bodies the exponent ranges from **5 to 8** because of:
- Convective amplification (Lilley's equation),
- Mach-number dependence,
- Source coherence effects.

### Examiner's Check on the Calculation

| Quantity | Value at $U_1$ | Value at $U_2$ | $\Delta SPL$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cruising speed | 80 km/h (22.2 m/s) | 160 km/h (44.4 m/s) | — |
| Speed ratio $U_2/U_1$ | — | **2** | — |
| $\Delta SPL = 60\log_{10}(2)$ | — | — | **≈ 18.0 dB** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world insight:** Doubling the vehicle speed (80 → 160 km/h) increases wind noise by **~18 dB** — i.e. it sounds roughly *4× louder* to the human ear. This is why EVs feel so loud at highway speeds even though no engine is running.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kármán Vortex-Shedding Frequency from a Side Mirror

A side mirror of effective bluff-body diameter $D$ sheds Kármán vortices at a characteristic frequency. **This derivation is directly testable.**

**Step 1 — The Strouhal number is defined as:**

$$St = \frac{f\,D}{U}$$

**Step 2 — For a circular cylinder / typical automotive bluff body in the sub-critical Reynolds range, the Strouhal number is approximately constant:**

$$St \approx 0.21$$

**Step 3 — Solve for shedding frequency:**

$$f_v = \frac{St\cdot U}{D} = \frac{0.21 \times U}{D}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation** (typical compact-SUV mirror):
- $D = 0.18\ \text{m}$,
- $U = 120\ \text{km/h} = 33.33\ \text{m/s}$,

$$f_v = \frac{0.21 \times 33.33}{0.18} \approx 38.9\ \text{Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Convert this *low-frequency* hydrodynamic excitation to its *radiated acoustic frequency*.** Because of convective amplification and the dipole mechanism, the dominant *acoustic* peak typically lies in the **500 Hz – 2 kHz** band (the same band where the human ear is most sensitive — *coincidentally* the worst possible outcome).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Wind-Noise Sources & Their Frequencies

| Source on Vehicle | Dominant Mechanism | Typical Acoustic Peak (Hz) | Severity Rating |
|---|---|---|---|
| **A-pillar / sail panel** | Flow separation + vortex shedding | **500 – 2000** | ★★★★★ (Highest) |
| **Side mirror** | Kármán vortex shedding | **800 – 1500** | ★★★★ |
| **Windshield cowl** | Recirculation, grasshopper noise | **2000 – 5000** | ★★★ |
| **Roof rail / ditch-moulding** | Cavity resonance + flow | **200 – 800** | ★★★ |
| **Wheel-well / tyre cavity** | Cavity tone + turbulence | **100 – 400** | ★★ |
| **Antenna (shark-fin / mast)** | Vortex shedding, aeolian tone | **600 – 1200** | ★★ |
| **Rear-window / back-light** | Trailing-edge noise | **800 – 2500** | ★★★ |
| **Door seal gap** | Flow-induced whistle | **1000 – 4000** | ★★★★★ |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Effect of Cabin Volume on Interior SPL (Statistical Energy Analysis flavour)

The interior SPL inside a passenger cabin behaves like a **reverberant field** for frequencies above the Schroeder frequency $f_s$:

$$f_s = c_{0}\!\left(\frac{6}{V}\right)^{\!1/3}$$

For a typical sedan with cabin volume $V \approx 2.8\ \text{m}^{3}$:

$$f_s = 343 \times \left(\frac{6}{2.8}\right)^{1/3} \approx 343 \times (2.143)^{1/3} \approx 343 \times 1.288 \approx 441.6\ \text{Hz}$$

**Conclusion (directly useful in KTU answers):** Above ~440 Hz, the cabin behaves as a *reverberant space* and the SPL becomes spatially uniform. Wind-noise energy in this band is therefore *amplified* by the cabin's reverberant field — another reason 500–2000 Hz is so problematic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Wind-Noise Generation, Transmission & Radiation Path

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete cause-and-effect chain from aerodynamics to driver-ear perception, in compliance with the **node-identifier alpha rule** (all IDs are alphanumeric, no reserved keywords).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Vehicle Moving at Speed U] --> A2[Air Approaches Frontal Area]
    A2 --> B1[Stagnation at Front Bumper]
    A2 --> B2[Boundary Layer Forms on Body]
    B2 --> B3[Laminar to Turbulent Transition]
    B3 --> C1[Turbulent Pressure Fluctuations on Panel]
    B1 --> D1[Flow Over Windshield]
    D1 --> D2[Flow Hits A-Pillar and Side Mirror]
    D2 --> D3[Flow Separation and Vortex Shedding]
    D3 --> E1[Unsteady Surface Pressure ps of t]
    B2 --> F1[Flow Over Roof and Dlo]
    F1 --> F2[Trailing Edge Noise at Rear]
    C1 --> G1[Panel Vibration Glass Sheet Metal]
    E1 --> G1
    F2 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[Structure Borne Transmission]
    H1 --> I1[Re Radiation into Cabin Air]
    I1 --> J1[Cabin Reverberant Field]
    J1 --> K1[Interior SPL at Driver Ear]
    K1 --> L1[Perceived Wind Noise dB A]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Mitigation Strategy Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Source Control Aerodynamic Shape] --> N1[Tailored A-Pillar Geometry]
    M1 --> N2[Recessed Door Handles]
    M1 --> N3[Flush Side Mirrors]
    M1 --> N4[Smooth Underbody and Wheel Covers]
    O1[Path Control] --> P1[Laminated Acoustic Glass]
    O1 --> P2[Damping Pads on Sheet Metal]
    O1 --> P3[Body Seal Optimization]
    O1 --> P4[Double Door Seals]
    Q1[Receiver Control Cabin] --> R1[Trunk Sealing Bulkhead]
    Q1 --> R2[Headliner Absorber]
    Q1 --> R3[Carpet NVH Layer]
```
